  "title_inferred": "Chapter XV: On Europe — Geography, Kingdoms, and Provinces",
  "original_text": "CAPITULO XV
De Europa

Es Europa la menor de las cinco partes en que se divide el mundo, pero la más noble y abundante; y como tal, cría los hombres más aventajados en valor y esfuerzo que los de las otras cuatro. Contiene muchas regiones, reinos, islas y provincias, como son: España, Francia, Inglaterra, Italia, Alemania, Hungría, Transilvania, Escandía, Polonia, Rascia, Valaquia, Moscovia, Esclavonia, Albania, Bosnia, Servia, Bulgaria, Macedonia, Grecia y Tracia.

España es la parte más occidental, de la cual, por ser mi patria, daré más claras noticias que de las otras regiones. Tiene de circunferencia seiscientas y treinta y cuatro leguas; de las cuales, quitadas ochenta que tiene de tierra pegada con Francia, todo lo demás, cerca la mar. Está tan poblada, que entre ciudades, villas y lugares tiene veintiséis mil pueblos; sesenta y siete obispados y arzobispados; sesenta y nueve iglesias catedrales, y más de trescientos títulos de duques, marqueses y condes, sin otros muchos señores de vasallos sin título. Dividiéronla los romanos primeramente en dos partes: en España Citerior y Ulterior. En la primera, se comprehendía todo lo que cae del río Ebro hacia Italia; y en la segunda, lo restante de España. Después la partieron en tres partes: Bética, Lusitania y Tarraconense. Al presente la dividimos en tres reinos, que son: Castilla, Aragón y Portugal, respecto de ser tres las repúblicas y fueros distintos por donde se gobiernan; sin embargo de que en cada una destas coronas y reinos se incluyen otros muchos.

La Corona de Castilla comprehende estos diez reinos: el propiamente llamado Castilla, el de León, Galicia, Navarra, Toledo, Murcia, Sevilla, Granada, Córdoba y Jaén; en las cuales se cuentan treinta y cuatro obispados, y de ellos son arzobispados los cinco: el de Toledo, Sevilla, Santiago, Burgos y Granada; treinta y seis iglesias catedrales, cincuenta

results.title_inferred: Geography of the Eastern Adriatic, Balkans, Greece, and Thrace
results.original_text:
a y Dalmacia; la propiamente Dalmacia se nombra hoy Esclavonia, y la Liburnia es la que con ella confina la tierra adentro. La mejor ciudad de Esclavonia es Ragusia, que se mantiene en libertad pagando parias al turco.

Albania se divide de Esclavonia por el río Bayona [Boiana, Drina]. Es provincia grande y fértil; descienden sus moradores de los scitas; alábanse de poder juntar treinta mil caballos y dar guerra al turco. Sus pueblos principales son Alesio, Durazo y la Belena. Entre Esclavonia y el Danubio está Bosnia, tierra fragosa y llena de montes abundantes de plata. Al oriente de Bosnia está la provincia de Servia, y a la parte oriental desta se sigue la Bulgaria, y corre por la ribera del Danubio hasta el mar Euxino.

La provincia de Macedonia se extiende desde el mar Ionio, donde tiene a la ciudad de Durazo, hasta el mar Egeo, donde está Salónica. Antiguamente fué señora de muchos pueblos, mas ahora está sujeta al yugo turquesco.

La provincia de Grecia está llena de penínsulas, islas y senos de mar. En el lado oriental del seno Ambracio tiene su sitio la Arcadia; y pasando el río Achelvo, entramos en la Etolia, cuya ciudad más famosa es Lepanto; síguese la Tesalia, ceñida de montes. Las demás provincias son la Morea, Acaya, Mesenia, la Laconia y Boecia, cuya metrópoli es Tebas, Demetria de Negroponte, y otras.

La provincia de Tracia es muy grande y tiene muchas y muy principales ciudades; pero la metrópoli de todas es Constantinopla, puesta en la ribera de un canal por donde se juntan tanto Europa y Asia, que por partes no tiene de ancho más que cinco estadios.
results.translation:
...and Dalmatia; that which is properly Dalmatia is today called Slavonia [Esclavonia], and Liburnia is the region that borders it inland. The finest city of Slavonia is Ragusa [Dubrovnik], which maintains its liberty by paying tribute [parias] to the Turk.

Albania is separated from Slavonia by the river Bayona [the Bojana or Drina]. It is a large and fertile province; its inhabitants claim descent from the Scythians; they boast of being able to muster thirty thousand horse and wage war against the Turk. Its principal towns are Alessio [Lezhë], Durazzo [Durrës], and Belena [possibly Vlorë or Berat]. Between Slavonia and the Danube lies Bosnia, a rugged land full of mountains rich in silver. To the east of Bosnia lies the province of Serbia, and to the east of that follows Bulgaria, which runs along the banks of the Danube to the Euxine [Black] Sea.

The province of Macedonia stretches from the Ionian Sea, where it has the city of Durazzo, to the Aegean Sea, where Salonica [Thessaloniki] stands. In ancient times it was mistress of many peoples, but now it lies under the Turkish yoke.

The province of Greece is full of peninsulas, islands, and sea-inlets. On the eastern side of the Ambracian Gulf [Gulf of Arta] lies Arcadia; and crossing the river Achelous [Acheloos], we enter Aetolia, whose most famous city is Lepanto [Nafpaktos]; then follows Thessaly, girt by mountains. The remaining provinces are the Morea [Peloponnese], Achaea, Messenia, Laconia, and Boeotia, whose metropolis is Thebes, Demetrias of Negroponte [Chalcis/Euboea], and others.

The province of Thrace is very large and has many great and important cities; but the metropolis of all is Constantinople, situated on the shore of a strait [the Bosphorus] where Europe and Asia draw so close together that in places it is no more than five stadia wide.

results.translator_notes: This segment does not belong to the botanical sections of Books IV–VI of the Historia del Nuevo Mundo; it is a geographical description of the eastern Adriatic coast, the Balkans, Greece, and Thrace, consistent with Cobo's introductory cosmographical and geographical books (likely Book I or II). No plant, drug, or medical content is present; accordingly, no Materia Medica extraction is possible. The OCR produced several corruptions: 'Eselavonia' normalized to 'Esclavonia', 'Lírtil' to 'fértil', 'Aleslo' to 'Alesio', 'Boecia' retained as written (= Boeotia), 'Achelvo' identified as Acheloos. The bracketed gloss '[Boiana, Drina]' appears to be an editorial annotation already present in the source transcription, not Cobo's own text. Cobo's placement of Arcadia near the Ambracian Gulf is a geographical error, likely confusing Acarnania or Epirus with Arcadia.

{
  "title_inferred": "Description of Asia and its Provinces (Chapter XVI)",
  "original_text": "CAPITULO XVI
De la Asia

La Asia es la mayor parte de las tres del Mundo Viejo, porque sólo ella es más grande que juntas Africa y Europa. Comienza su latitud desde la línea equinoccial y corre para el septentrión hasta los ochenta grados, y así se comprehende toda en el hemisferio Ártico; de longitud tiene ciento y veinticuatro grados, que por círculo mayor hacen dos mil y ciento y setenta leguas. Está por los tres lados ceñida del Océano, y por el occidente se junta con Europa por la Tartaria, y con Africa por Egipto. Divídese en siete partes: la primera es la más vecina a Europa, desde el Océano septentrional hasta el mar Caspio, que antiguamente se llamaba la Sarmacia y ahora obedece al Moscovita; la segunda, la Tartaria, que confina con el mar Caspio, con el Océano y con Moscovia; la tercera, la que posee el Turco, que es la parte occidental de Asia, comprendida entre el mar Caspio, seno Pérsico y río Tigris; la cuarta, el reino de Persia; la quinta, la India Oriental, desde el río Indo hasta la China; la sexta, el gran reino de la China; la séptima, todas las islas adyacentes a la Asia.

La tierra más austral de la Asia es una península llamada Arabia, que posee el Turco, la cual se distribuye en cuatro grandes provincias: una se llama Troglodítica, que pertenece a la descripción de Africa; la segunda, es finitima a la Mesopotamia, a la Siria y a Judea, y se llama Arabia Desierta; la tercera, la Pétrea; y la cuarta, la Felice, que es la mayor, más abundante y más poblada; lleva incienso y mirra y comprende insignes provincias, como son los reinos de Fartaque, Sael y Aden. Desde Arabia hasta Judea corre la provincia de Idumea; es tierra abundantísima de palmas y contiene en sí a Gaza, ciudad antiquísima. Ascalona, Azoto y otras.

La provincia de Siria es grandísima, cae entre Arabia, Eufrates y Cilicia; divídese en estas cinco provincias: Palestina, Fenicia,

Divídese en dos partes: la más occidental se dice Intra Ganges, que es la que propiamente se llama India; y la segunda, a quien también se le da este nombre tomado ampliamente, se llama Extra Ganges. La India Intra Ganges es una gran punta de tierra en forma de pirámide con que la Asia se mete por el Océano, cuyo remate se llama cabo Comorín, desde el cual corre una gran sierra la vuelta del septentrión como cuatrocientas leguas, hasta llegar al monte Imano, que es un brazo del Cáucaso, monte famoso y celebrado de la antigüedad.

Desde el cabo de Comorín hacia el septentrión, por la parte occidental de la dicha sierra, cae la provincia de Malabar, que abraza los reinos de Travancore, Coulán, Cochín, Craganor y Calicud cabeza de todos estos reinos malabares. Al septentrión de Calicud se siguen los reinos de Cananor y Baticala, y veinte leguas adelante la isla en que está la ciudad de Goa. Desde ella, para el septentrión, yacen otros reinos hasta el de Cambaya; en cuya costa están las ciudades de Chaul, Bazaín, Damán y Dio.

En el de Narsinga está el reino de Coromandel y la ciudad de Meliapor, que hoy se llama Santo Tomé. Más adelante se siguen los reinos de Berma [Birmania], Siam, Camboja, Cochinchina, y el amplísimo reino de la China.

entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Al-Qusayr (Quseir), a port town on the Egyptian Red Sea coast
entities_mentioned.notes: The bracketed gloss 'Al-Koseir' appears to be either a contemporary annotation or an OCR artifact of a marginal note in the original; it correctly identifies the Arabic toponym underlying 'Alcocer.'

entities_mentioned.name: Suaquén
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Suakin, a historic port city on the Red Sea coast of present-day Sudan
entities_mentioned.notes: Suakin was a major Red Sea commercial and pilgrim port throughout the medieval and early modern periods, well known to Spanish and Portuguese writers.

  "original_text": "CAPÍTULO IV
De la sal

Así como este reino del Perú es el más abundante de metales, mayormente de plata, de cuantos sabemos hoy en el mundo, así también lo es de salinas naturales; que parece proveyó Dios de ellas con tanta abundancia donde tan necesaria es la sal para beneficiar los metales de plata, en que se consume una cantidad increíble de sal; y no hay provincia ni ciudad que padezca falta della; porque de la mucha que se saca en unas tierras, se reparte a otras y ninguna está tan distante de algunas salinas, que por tierra o mar no se pueda con facilidad proveer de la sal que ha menester.

Tres diferencias ponen de sal los autores que della escriben, conviene a saber: la marina, la de mineral y la que se hace del agua de algunas fuentes; y todas se hallan en estas Indias en muy grande abundancia; y de las dos, esto es, de la marina y de fuentes de agua salobre, se halla natural y artificial. Porque demás de la sal marina que se hace del agua de la mar por industria humana, en algunas partes, rebosando la mar con sus crecientes y hinchadas olas, arroja de sí fuera de los límites de sus playas gran copia de agua, la cual, con el calor del sol, se cuaja en excelente sal. Y en otras partes la sacan en piedras grandes de debajo del agua de la misma mar, cerca de tierra, donde se halla cuajada.

Allende désta, se halla de la que se hace de manantiales en tres maneras: las dos, por industria de hombres, que recogiendo en pozas la tal agua, se cuaja con el calor del sol; y la otra, cociéndola en ollas al fuego hasta que se viene a endurecer. La tercera manera es, que la misma agua salobre de muchos manantiales y lagos se cuaja de suyo.

Otras tres maneras se hallan de sal en esta tierra de la que se hace de agua: la una, es que en partes hacen sal de agua dulce, echando a cocer en ella el salitre que cogen de la superficie de la tierra que ha bañad

Salt is called, in the two general languages of Peru, cachi in Quechua, and hayn in Aymara.

results.title_inferred: On Saltpeter and Alum Stone
results.original_text:
CAPITULO V
Del salitre y piedra alumbre

Al salitre llaman los indios deste reino, en la lengua general, zuca; pero éste es el que se cría en la superficie de la tierra en lugares salitrosos, porque no conocieron otra suerte dello. Es muy perjudicial este salitre en los edificios, particularmente en los pueblos marítimos, como son los puertos del Callao y de Pisco, porque se va comiendo los cimientos de las casas, si no son de piedra dura, y en las heredades que están vecinas a la mar, porque si no hay cuidado de irlas desalitrando, se vienen a hacer estériles. No conocieron los indios el salitre de la pólvora ni supieron beneficiarlo; lo cual hacen los españoles destilando el agua en que se coció y dejándola asentar en vasijas de barro, en cuyo fondo se halla congelado el salitre como granos de sal transparente. Háylo en muchas partes de este reino del Perú en grande abundancia, de que se hace muy buena pólvora; particularmente es muy fino el de las provincias de Quito. En las tierras que carecen de nieve, enfrían el agua con salitre los españoles.

De piedra alumbre se hallan en estas Indias cuatro o cinco especies. En la Nueva España lo hacen muy blanco, lúcido y transparente, del cual usan los tintoreros en sus tintes, y aprovecha para las curas más que el que se trae de España.

(6) Lo mismo he visto hacer a los indios de Quijos y del río Ñapo.
results.translation:
CHAPTER V
On Saltpeter and Alum Stone

The Indians of this kingdom call saltpeter [salitre] in the general language [Quechua] zuca; but this refers only to the kind that forms on the surface of the earth in saline places, for they knew no other variety of it. This saltpeter is very harmful to buildings, particularly in coastal towns such as the ports of Callao and Pisco, because it gradually eats away at the foundations of houses if they are not built of hard stone, and it damages farmlands situated near the sea, which become sterile if care is not taken to remove the saltpeter from them. The Indians did not know the saltpeter used in gunpowder, nor did they know how to process it; this is done by the Spaniards by distilling the water in which it has been boiled and allowing it to settle in earthenware vessels, at the bottom of which the saltpeter is found crystallized [congelado] in the form of grains of transparent salt. It is found in great abundance in many parts of this kingdom of Peru, from which very good gunpowder is made; the saltpeter from the provinces of Quito is particularly fine. In lands where snow is lacking, the Spaniards use saltpeter to cool water.

Of alum stone [piedra alumbre], four or five species are found in these Indies. In New Spain it is made very white, bright, and transparent; dyers use it in their dyes, and it serves better for medicinal cures than that which is brought from Spain.

(6) I have myself seen the Indians of Quijos and the Ñapo River do the same.

entities_mentioned.name: Salitre (zuca in Quechua)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Potassium nitrate / saltpeter (KNO₃); possibly also sodium nitrate (NaNO₃) given the coastal/Peruvian context
entities_mentioned.notes: Cobo distinguishes between naturally occurring surface saltpeter (known to indigenous peoples) and the refined saltpeter used in gunpowder production (a Spanish introduction). The refinement method described—boiling, then settling in earthenware vessels to crystallize—is consistent with historical saltpeter purification. Peruvian 'caliche' deposits (sodium nitrate) were historically abundant, though Cobo's description of crystallization from boiled water aligns more with potassium nitrate processing.

entities_mentioned.name: Piedra alumbre (Alum stone)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Alum, likely potassium alum (KAl(SO₄)₂·12H₂O) or a related aluminum sulfate mineral
entities_mentioned.notes: Used by dyers as a mordant and cited for medicinal/curative purposes. Cobo notes four or five species found in the Indies, and praises the New Spain variety as superior to Spanish imports for medical use. No specific preparation method for medical use is detailed in this passage.

results.translator_notes: 1. The footnote numbered (6) appears to be a marginal or appended note by Cobo himself, attesting to personal observation among the Quijos people and along the Ñapo River (present-day Ecuador), presumably corroborating the saltpeter cooling technique described above. 2. The word 'congelado' in the original is translated as 'crystallized' rather than 'frozen,' as context makes clear it refers to precipitation/crystallization from solution. 3. The bracketed '[más]' in the original text indicates an editorial restoration of a word likely missing from the source due to OCR or transcription omission; it is included in the translation as 'better.' 4. This chapter falls within the mineralogical/inorganic sections of Cobo's work rather than strictly botanical material, but alum is noted for medicinal use.

results.title_inferred: Peruvian Clays and Earths (Pasa, Contaya, Parpa, Chaco) and Millu; Pottery of the New World
results.original_text:
Lábrase tan escogida loza y tan bien vedriada, que no hacen falta la de Talavera, porque de pocos años a esta parte han dado en contrahacer la de China, y sale muy parecida a ella, particularmente la que se hace en la Puebla de los Angeles en la Nueva España y en esta ciudad de Lima, que es muy buena y de lindo vedrio y colores; y asimismo se hacen muy curiosos azulejos, que antes se solían traer de España; si bien es verdad que no salen los de acá de tan finos colores.

CAPÍTULO VII
De la pasa y demás diferencias de pereda

Pasa llaman los indios del Perú a cierta suerte de greda, la cual es blanca, con algunas manchas pardas como de jabón; es de calidad fría y usan de ella por salsa muy regalada, con la cual, desleída y con sal, comen las papas y otras raíces mojándolas en este barro como si fuera mostaza; y a esta causa se vende en las plazas de todos los pueblos. Sus polvos, que son blandos y amorosos, echados sobre las almorranas, son útiles para desecarlas y consumirlas, y mezclados con vinagre o zumo de membrillo, valen contra los corrimientos de gota. Sirven de quitar manchas, y suelen suplir la falta de jabón, porque, levantando espuma, limpian la ropa, por lo cual se lavan los indios la cabeza con esta greda; y notan de ella que mata los piojos, y que si la comen los que tienen cámaras de sangre, las estanca.

Contaya es otra especie de tierra blanca buena para enlucir y blanquear paredes. Parpa es cierto barro colorado que echan en los cimientos y en los suelos de las casas. Chaco es otra greda de que se hace teja y ladrillo y otras cosas, y por golosina la suelen comer las mujeres. Todos estos nombres son de la lengua aimará, que es general en las provincias del Collao de este reino del Perú.

CAPÍTULO VIII
Del millu

Millu es una especie de tierra parecida en su estipticidad y casi en el color a la caparrosa. Hay dos diferencias de ella: una, con que tiñen las lanas de azul, y otra, de colorado. Es el millu un cáustico no de poca fuerza, porque además de the estipticidad que tiene, es purgativo y mordaz, a cuya causa sus polvos curan los lamparones. El agua que hubiere cocido con esta tierra con un poco de azúcar, deseca las llagas de cualquiera parte del cuerpo; y, finalmente, con el millu, salitre y alumbre se hace un agua fuerte muy medicinal. El nombre de millu es de la lengua aimará.
results.translation:
Such fine and well-glazed earthenware is produced here that there is no need for that of Talavera [de la Reina], for in recent years craftsmen have taken to imitating Chinese ware, and the result is very similar to it — particularly that made in the Puebla de los Ángeles in New Spain and in this city of Lima, which is of very good quality with beautiful glaze and colors. Likewise, very curious glazed tiles [azulejos] are made here, which formerly had to be brought from Spain, though it is true that those made locally do not achieve quite such fine colors.

CHAPTER VII
On Pasa and the Other Varieties of Clay [pereda]

The Indians of Peru call pasa a certain kind of clay that is white with some brownish spots resembling soap. It is cold in quality [i.e., by Galenic humoral classification], and they use it as a highly prized condiment: dissolved in water with salt, they eat their potatoes and other roots by dipping them in this clay as one would dip food in mustard. For this reason it is sold in the marketplaces of all towns. Its powders, which are soft and gentle, when applied to hemorrhoids [almorranas], are useful for drying them out and causing them to waste away; and when mixed with vinegar or quince juice, they are effective against the fluxes of gout [corrimientos de gota]. The powders also serve to remove stains and often substitute for soap, since they lather and clean clothing; for this reason the Indians wash their heads with this clay. It is also noted that it kills lice, and that if persons suffering from bloody flux [cámaras de sangre] eat it, it stanches the bleeding.

Contaya is another species of white earth, good for plastering and whitewashing walls. Parpa is a certain red clay used in the foundations and floors of houses. Chaco is another clay from which roof tiles, bricks, and other things are made; women also sometimes eat it as a delicacy [golosina]. All of these names belong to the Aymara language, which is the common tongue of the provinces of the Collao region of this kingdom of Peru.

CHAPTER VIII
On Millu

Millu is a type of earth resembling copperas [caparrosa / vitriol] in its astringency [estipticidad] and nearly in color. There are two varieties of it: one with which wool is dyed blue, and another that produces red. Millu is a caustic of considerable strength, for in addition to its astringency it is purgative and mordant [mordaz]; for this reason its powders cure scrofula [lamparones]. Water in which this earth has been boiled, taken with a little sugar, dries up sores [llagas] on any part of the body. Finally, with millu, saltpeter [salitre], and alum [alumbre] a highly medicinal strong water [agua fuerte, i.e., an acidic or corrosive solution] is made. The name millu belongs to the Aymara language.

entities_mentioned.name: Pasa
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Edible white clay / kaolin or calcium-rich earth used as a condiment; consistent with practices still recorded in Andean regions (e.g., chaco/p'asalla clays eaten with potatoes)
entities_mentioned.notes: Used as a food condiment with potatoes (dipped like mustard), as a topical treatment for hemorrhoids, against gout fluxes (with vinegar or quince juice), as a louse-killer, a detergent, and to staunch bloody flux when ingested. Cold in humoral quality. Sold in public markets.

entities_mentioned.name: Contaya
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: White earth / lime-based plaster material; possibly calcium carbonate or gypsum
entities_mentioned.notes: Used exclusively as a building material for plastering and whitewashing walls. No medicinal uses described. Aymara name.

entities_mentioned.name: Parpa
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Red clay, possibly iron-rich laterite or red ochre earth
entities_mentioned.notes: Used in construction: foundations and floors of houses. No medicinal uses described. Aymara name.

entities_mentioned.name: Chaco
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Montmorillonite or similar smectite clay; consumed as geophagy by women; used to make roof tiles and bricks
entities_mentioned.notes: Dual use as construction material (tiles, bricks) and as a food substance consumed by women as a delicacy [golosina]. Aymara name. Geophagy with this clay is well-documented in ethnographic literature of the Andes.

entities_mentioned.name: Millu
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Likely a copper or iron sulfate mineral (vitriol / copperas); possibly chalcanthite (copper sulfate, CuSO₄·5H₂O), which occurs in Andean mining regions and is blue
entities_mentioned.notes: Described as resembling caparrosa (copperas/vitriol) in color and astringency. Two varieties: one produces blue dye, the other red dye in wool. Medicinal uses: powders applied to scrofula [lamparones]; water decocted from it with sugar dries body sores; combined with saltpeter and alum produces a medicinal corrosive water [agua fuerte]. Classified as caustic, purgative, and mordant. Aymara name.

entities_mentioned.name: Caparrosa
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Copperas / vitriol (iron or copper sulfate)
entities_mentioned.notes: Used as a comparative reference point for millu; standard term in early modern Spanish pharmacy.

entities_mentioned.name: Almorranas
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Hemorrhoids
entities_mentioned.notes: Treated with powdered pasa applied topically.

entities_mentioned.name: Lamparones
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Scrofula (tuberculous cervical lymphadenitis)
entities_mentioned.notes: Treated with powdered millu.

entities_mentioned.name: Cámaras de sangre
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Bloody flux / dysentery
entities_mentioned.notes: Treated by ingesting pasa clay.

entities_mentioned.name: Corrimientos de gota
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Gouty fluxes / arthritic inflammation
entities_mentioned.notes: Treated with pasa powders mixed with vinegar or quince juice.

entities_mentioned.name: Llagas
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Open sores / ulcers
entities_mentioned.notes: Treated with water decocted from millu with sugar.

entities_mentioned.name: Azulejos
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Glazed ceramic tiles
entities_mentioned.notes: Produced locally in New Spain and Lima, previously imported from Spain; noted to lack the fine coloring of Spanish originals.

results.translator_notes: 1. The opening paragraphs on pottery (loza, azulejos, imitation Chinese ware at Puebla de los Ángeles and Lima) appear to be the tail end of Chapter VI, which precedes the botanical/mineral chapters translated here. They are included as they appear in the source segment. 2. The editorial annotations at the very top of the source (referencing Stübel, Reiss, Koppel, and Max Uhle's 'Cultur und Industrie südamerikanischer Völker') are 19th-century editorial additions to the Cobo text, not original to Cobo (1653); they have been omitted from the translation as they are not part of Cobo's text. 3. 'Estipticidad' (astringency/stypticity) is a Galenic pharmacological term preserved in brackets as 'astringency' in the translation. 4. 'Agua fuerte' literally means 'strong water' and in early modern pharmacy referred to corrosive or acidic solutions (including aqua regia and nitric acid preparations); translated as 'strong water' with a bracketed note. 5. 'Pereda' in the chapter title appears to be a variant or OCR corruption of a word meaning 'clays' or 'earths'; the chapter itself consistently uses 'greda' (clay/marl). It may reflect a regional or period term. 6. The identification of millu as chalcanthite (blue copper sulfate) is supported by the blue dye use and the comparison to caparrosa, though iron vitriol (green/blue) cannot be excluded. The 'red' variety may be a different mineral entirely (e.g., iron sulfate).

results.title_inferred: On Tacú: Andean Medicinal Clay and Its Varieties
results.original_text:
CAPITULO IX

Del Tacú

Tacú llaman los indios del Perú, en la lengua aimará, a la tierra que propiamente es Bolo Arménico y por tal se suele administrar, a la cual podemos llamar con propiedad Bolo Indico. Hállase el tacú en el cerro rico de Potosí y en otras minas de metales, en especial de hierro, en tres diferencias: la primera, es una tierra colorada como sangre, de que usan los pintores y, principalmente, los doradores; la segunda es de color de hígado; y la tercera y más común y usada de los indios, para curar algunas enfermedades es amarilla, la cual, en panes y bollos, venden los indios en las plazas y se aprovechan della para curar cámaras de sangre, bebidos sus polvos en la chicha, que es su vino. Vale esta tierra, y principalmente si es quemada, para desecar cualquiera llaga, y sin quemar, mezclada con vinagre, agua rosada o de llantén, aprovecha contra toda inflamación.

Especie de tacú es cierta tierra que en la Nueva España llaman sigilata, de la cual hay una veta en el pueblo de Tepozotlán, cinco leguas de México: es de color de hígado y reluciente, la cual, bebida deshecha en agua, aprovecha contra las cámaras.
results.translation:
CHAPTER IX

On Tacú

The Indians of Peru, in the Aymara language, call tacú a substance that is properly Armenian Bole [Bolo Arménico] and is commonly administered as such; we may fittingly call it Indian Bole [Bolo Indico]. Tacú is found in the rich mountain of Potosí and in other metal mines, especially iron mines, and comes in three varieties: the first is a blood-red earth, used by painters and, principally, by gilders; the second is liver-colored; and the third, most common and most used by the Indians for curing certain ailments, is yellow. This last variety the Indians sell in the marketplaces in loaves and cakes, and they make use of it to treat bloody dysentery [cámaras de sangre], its powder being drunk dissolved in chicha, which is their wine. This earth is also useful, especially when burned, for drying out any wound; and unburned, mixed with vinegar, rosewater, or plantain water [agua de llantén], it is beneficial against all inflammation.

A variety of tacú is a certain earth called sigilata [terra sigillata] in New Spain, of which there is a vein in the town of Tepozotlán, five leagues from Mexico City. It is liver-colored and lustrous, and drunk dissolved in water it is beneficial against dysentery [cámaras].

entities_mentioned.name: Tacú
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Medicinal clay, likely iron-rich aluminosilicate earth (similar to bole/terra sigillata); the yellow variety may correspond to limonite- or ochre-bearing clay
entities_mentioned.notes: Aymara term for a range of colored medicinal earths found near Potosí and other Andean mines. Cobo equates it with Armenian Bole (Bolus armenicus), a well-known classical medicinal clay. Three color varieties described: red (used by painters and gilders), liver-colored, and yellow (medicinal).

entities_mentioned.name: Bolo Arménico (Armenian Bole / Bolus armenicus)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Red iron-rich clay (ferruginous aluminosilicate); historically sourced from Armenia
entities_mentioned.notes: Standard European pharmacopeial term used as the comparator by Cobo to identify tacú for a European readership.

entities_mentioned.name: Chicha
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Fermented maize beer (Zea mays)
entities_mentioned.notes: Used here as the vehicle for administering powdered tacú against bloody dysentery.

entities_mentioned.name: Agua de llantén (plantain water)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Plantago major or Plantago lanceolata infusion
entities_mentioned.notes: Used as a vehicle or adjunct mixed with tacú and vinegar to treat inflammation.

entities_mentioned.name: Sigilata (terra sigillata of New Spain)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Terra sigillata — a stamped medicinal clay; the New Spanish variety from Tepozotlán likely an iron-rich aluminosilicate clay
entities_mentioned.notes: Cobo identifies a vein of this clay at Tepozotlán, approximately five leagues north of Mexico City. Liver-colored and lustrous; used dissolved in water against dysentery. The name 'sigilata' derives from the European tradition of stamping medicinal earths with a seal (sigillum) to certify authenticity.

results.translator_notes: The OCR rendered 'sigilata^' with a stray caret character, clearly an artifact; corrected silently to 'sigilata'. The term 'cámaras de sangre' literally means 'bloody chambers' and is standard period terminology for bloody dysentery or severe diarrhea with blood. 'Bolo Arménico' is the Spanish rendering of Bolus armenicus, a widely used classical and medieval medicinal red clay; Cobo's proposed term 'Bolo Indico' (Indian Bole) appears to be his own coinage for the Andean equivalent. The identification of tacú's three color varieties (red, liver, yellow) likely reflects different mineral compositions: red = hematite-rich, liver = a darker iron oxide mix, yellow = limonite or goethite-bearing clay. Tepozotlán is a real town in the State of Mexico, still approximately 43 km north of Mexico City, consistent with Cobo's 'five leagues.'

results.title_inferred: Copperas (Vitriol) and Other Colored Earths of the Indies
results.original_text:
CAPITULO X

De la caparrosa y de las demás tierras de colores

En muchas partes destas Indias hay minas de fina caparrosa, de la cual he visto hacer tinta para escribir, con tanta facilidad, que no hacen más de echar la caparrosa molida con una poca de tara, también molida, en el agua así fría como está naturalmente, y revolverla, y luego acabada de echar la caparrosa, escribir con esta tinta, la cual queda negra algo tirante a azul; de donde infiero yo que se debe de diferenciar de la que se trae de España. De lo cual es indicio también ver que no es tan fina esta tinta ni ninguna de cuanta se hace acá con recaudo de la tierra, como la que la gente curiosa hace con recaudo de Castilla, esto es, con la caparrosa y agallas que de allá se traen. Llámase esta caparrosa de la tierra, en la lengua general del Perú, colpa.

Hállase también aquella tierra amarilla de que usan mucho los pintores, llamada ocra, la cual, en la lengua aimará, se dice guellii; y otra especie de tierra naranjada, llamada en la misma lengua aimará pitu, de que también usan los pintores. Hay asimismo almagre en muchas partes, que llaman los indios del Perú puca alpa.

En la Nueva España se halla en venas una tierra blanca, que suele servir de albayalde y para pintar con ella, y las mujeres indias, para hilar el algodón, la toman entre los dedos, con que se ponen blandos para hilar con más facilidad.

Otra suerte de tierra blanca se cría en la Nueva España en las concavidades de las peñas, la cual dan a beber a los enfermos de calenturas.

Otra tierra se halla amarilla, de que usan los pintores, con la cual suelen afeitarse los rostros las indias, que las hace parecer más fieras; y asimismo los indios mexicanos se pintaban el cuerpo con ella cuando iban a la guerra, para poner espanto a sus enemigos.

(8) Es el tezatl o tlilli de los mexicanos, o sea la variedad de carbonato calizo que llamamos vulgarmente tiza y creta.
results.translation:
CHAPTER X

On copperas [caparrosa] and the other colored earths

In many parts of these Indies there are mines of fine copperas [caparrosa], from which I have seen writing ink made with such ease that one need only cast the ground copperas together with a little ground tara [tannin-rich pod of Caesalpinia spinosa] into water just as it is cold naturally, stir it, and immediately upon finishing the mixing of the copperas, write with this ink, which turns out black with a slight tendency toward blue. From this I infer that it must differ from the copperas brought from Spain. Further evidence of this is that this ink, and indeed none of the ink made here from local materials, is as fine as that which curious [skilled/discerning] persons make from Castilian materials — that is, from the copperas and oak galls brought from there. This local copperas is called, in the general language [Quechua] of Peru, colpa.

One also finds there that yellow earth much used by painters, called ochre [ocra], which in the Aymara language is called guellii; and another variety of orange-colored earth, called in the same Aymara language pitu, which painters likewise use. There is also red ochre [almagre] in many places, which the Indians of Peru call puca alpa.

In New Spain one finds in veins a white earth that commonly serves as white lead [albayalde] and as a pigment for painting; and Indian women, in order to spin cotton, take it between their fingers, which renders them soft so that they may spin more easily. (8)

Another sort of white earth grows in the hollows of rocks in New Spain, which is given as a drink to patients suffering from fevers.

Yet another earth is found, yellow in color, which painters use, and with which Indian women are accustomed to paint their faces, making them appear more fierce; and likewise the Mexican Indians painted their bodies with it when they went to war, so as to strike fear into their enemies.

(8) This is the tezatl or tlilli of the Mexicans — that is, the variety of calcium carbonate which we commonly call chalk [tiza] and crayon [creta].

entities_mentioned.name: caparrosa / colpa (Quechua)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Iron sulfate / ferrous sulfate (green vitriol, copperas); FeSO₄·7H₂O
entities_mentioned.notes: Used to make iron gall ink in combination with tara (tannin). Cobo notes the American variety produces an ink tending toward blue-black, and considers it inferior to Spanish copperas for ink-making.

entities_mentioned.name: tara
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Caesalpinia spinosa (tara pod), a tannin source native to the Andes
entities_mentioned.notes: Ground tara pods used as the tannin component in iron gall ink preparation, substituting for European oak galls.

entities_mentioned.name: agallas (oak galls)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Galls of Quercus spp., source of tannic/gallic acid used in iron gall ink
entities_mentioned.notes: Imported from Spain/Castile; considered superior to local tara for ink quality.

entities_mentioned.name: ocra / guellii (Aymara)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Yellow ochre; iron oxide-hydroxide pigment (goethite, FeO·OH)
entities_mentioned.notes: Yellow earth used as a painter's pigment; Aymara name guellii recorded.

entities_mentioned.name: pitu (Aymara)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Orange earth pigment; likely a limonite or hematite-rich clay
entities_mentioned.notes: Orange-colored earth used as a painter's pigment; Aymara name pitu recorded.

entities_mentioned.name: almagre / puca alpa (Quechua)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Red ochre; iron oxide pigment (hematite, Fe₂O₃)
entities_mentioned.notes: Quechua name puca alpa (literally 'red earth'). Found in many parts of Peru.

entities_mentioned.name: tierra blanca (New Spain, veins) / tezatl / tlilli (Nahuatl)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Calcium carbonate (chalk, creta); possibly a white clay or calcareous earth
entities_mentioned.notes: Cobo's footnote (8) identifies this as tezatl or tlilli, equated with tiza (chalk) and creta. Used as a substitute for white lead [albayalde] in painting, and by Indian women on fingers as a spinning aid.

entities_mentioned.name: tierra blanca (concavities of rocks, New Spain)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Unknown white earth, possibly calcium carbonate or kaolin clay
entities_mentioned.notes: Distinct from the previous white earth; administered as a drink to patients with fevers [calenturas]. Possible medicinal use of kaolin or similar earth.

entities_mentioned.name: tierra amarilla (New Spain, for face/body paint)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Yellow ochre or limonite-based yellow earth pigment
entities_mentioned.notes: Used by Indian women as facial cosmetic and by Mexican warriors as body paint for intimidation in warfare. Distinct from the Andean ocra mentioned earlier, though possibly the same mineral class.

results.translator_notes: 1. 'Caparrosa' in 17th-century Spanish denotes vitriol broadly, most commonly green vitriol (ferrous sulfate), the standard iron component of iron gall ink. Cobo's observation that the American variety produces a blue-tinged black ink may reflect differences in iron content or the presence of copper sulfate (blue vitriol) in some deposits. 2. 'Tara' (Caesalpinia spinosa) is left in brackets as a period technical term; it functions here as the tannin source replacing oak galls. 3. 'Albayalde' (white lead, basic lead carbonate) is retained in brackets as a period pigment term; Cobo uses it as a comparison point, not claiming the white earth is chemically identical. 4. Footnote (8) appears to be an editorial annotation by a later editor of the Cobo text, not by Cobo himself; it has been translated and preserved. 5. The passage contains no formal medical 'preparation method' for most entries, with the exception of the white earth drunk for fevers (administered as a beverage) and the ink preparation (grinding copperas and tara in cold water). 6. OCR errors silently corrected include: 'tierral' → 'tierras', 'tau,ta' → 'tanta', 'revol*verla' → 'revolverla', 'miichas' → 'muchas', 'cfue' → 'que', '3a' → 'la', 'las' → 'las', '11*\'niamos' → 'llamamos'.

results.title_inferred: On Tepetate (a hardpan earth-stone material)
results.original_text:
CAPITULO XI
Del tepetate

Hay una especie de tierra que parece medio tierra y medio piedra, porque ni bien es tierra del todo, ni bien piedra, aunque participa las propiedades de ambas; porque es blanda como tierra y resiste al agua, que nunca se ablanda con ella, como peña. Algunos le dan nombre de peña hembra, y en la Nueva España le llaman tepetate, de que es la mayor parte del suelo de aquel reino, así de las sierras como de muchas sabanas y vegas, que no poco impiden la fertilidad de la región; porque, donde está descubierto el tepetate, es del todo infructífero el suelo, y donde cubierto de tierra, si ésta es de poco grueso o la roban las aguas, en cultivándolas, como ha sucedido en los altos y laderas de la comarca de México, acude a muy poco lo que se siembra. Es el tepetate de varios colores; por la mayor parte es bermejo, y también se halla muy blanco. Es bueno el suelo de tepetate para los caminos que van sobre él, porque, por más que llueva, no hacen lodo, respecto de que no lo cala ni penetra el agua; y también para abrir en él acequias, bóvedas y cárcavos de molino, porque ahorra de cal y canto; y es tan firme y seguro lo que en él se labra deste género, que nunca se derrumba. Es un tepetate más duro que otro. En el valle de Pisco, diócesis de Lima, se hallan unos cerrillos de sólo tepetate muy blanco, con el cual, molido, suelen limpiar los platos de plata, y sólo para este efecto se suele traer a esta ciudad de Lima.
results.translation:
CHAPTER XI
On Tepetate

There is a kind of earth that appears to be half earth and half stone, for it is neither entirely earth nor entirely stone, though it partakes of the properties of both: it is soft like earth, yet it resists water and never softens when wetted, like rock. Some give it the name of 'peña hembra' [female rock], and in New Spain it is called tepetate. It constitutes the greater part of the subsoil of that kingdom, both in the mountain ranges and across many savannas and lowland plains, where it impedes the fertility of the region considerably; for where tepetate lies exposed at the surface, the ground is entirely unproductive, and where it is covered by soil, if that soil is shallow or is stripped away by water during cultivation — as has happened on the highlands and hillsides around Mexico City — whatever is sown yields very little. Tepetate occurs in various colors; for the most part it is reddish, though it is also found in a very white variety. Ground with tepetate beneath it is good for roads built upon it, because no matter how much it rains, they do not turn to mud, given that water neither penetrates nor soaks through it. It is likewise suitable for digging irrigation channels [acequias], vaults, and millrace sluices [cárcavos de molino] in it, because it dispenses with the need for lime and cut stone [cal y canto]; and whatever work of this kind is done in it is so firm and secure that it never collapses. Some tepetate is harder than other. In the valley of Pisco, in the diocese of Lima, there are small hills composed entirely of very white tepetate, with which, when ground to powder, silver plates are customarily cleaned; and it is brought to the city of Lima solely for that purpose.

entities_mentioned.name: tepetate
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Tepetate — a volcanic hardpan or indurated tuff layer (caliche-like cemented subsoil, typically of volcanic origin; classified in modern geology as a duripan or tuff-derived hardpan, common in central Mexico)
entities_mentioned.notes: Cobo correctly describes its characteristic impermeability to water, its intermediate hardness between soil and rock, its reddish and white color variants, and its agricultural sterility. The white variety from Pisco, used as a polishing abrasive for silver, is consistent with a fine pumiceous or diatomaceous tuff material.

entities_mentioned.name: peña hembra
entities_mentioned.notes: A colloquial Spanish term meaning 'female rock,' used as a synonym for tepetate by some speakers, presumably to contrast with harder 'true' stone (peña macho or simply peña).

results.translator_notes: This chapter is geological/mineralogical rather than botanical or pharmacological in nature; it does not describe a plant, drug, or medical preparation. No plant entities are present. Several OCR errors were silently corrected: 'm^dio' → 'medio'; 'keno' → 'bueno'; 'esos' restored from 'ssos'; 'aborta' → 'ahorra' (contextually unambiguous: the sense is that tepetate saves the builder the expense of lime and stone, not that it 'aborts' anything). The term 'cárcavos de molino' refers to the millrace pit or sluice housing the waterwheel, here rendered as 'millrace sluices.' 'Cal y canto' is an idiomatic phrase for masonry construction using lime mortar and cut stone.

results.title_inferred: On Sand: Varieties, Uses, and Indigenous Practices in the Americas
results.original_text:
CAPITULO XII
De la arena

Varias son las diferencias de arena que se hallan en esta tierra, así en las riberas de la mar y de los ríos como en otras muchas partes en la superficie y en las entrañas de la tierra. En dos principalmente se muestra esta variedad: la una es en el color, y la otra, en ser una arena más menuda o más gruesa que otra. De los metales de plata molidos y lavados resulta gran cantidad de arena. En la costa de la mar de Pisco y del Callao se halla mucha arena de conchas quebrantadas y molidas: es blanca y mézclanla con cal para los edificios. Fuera de la arena común se halla en muchas partes arenilla negra y menuda, que se suele echar sobre las escrituras frescas en lugar de salvado, de la cual hay tanta cantidad en este reino, que no he visto en parte alguna de él usar de afrecho para este efecto, porque todos alcanzan de la arenilla.

Para el mismo menester sirven otras dos especies que podemos llamar de arenillas: la una es la margarita, que se halla en muchos ríos, que parece en el color y resplandor oro en polvo, y a no pocos codiciosos ha engañado pensando ser oro; la otra es blanca, como limaduras de plata. Destas tres últimas diferencias de arena suelen proveer las salvaderas los curiosos.

En la provincia de la Florida se halla cierta suerte de arena muy menuda y blanca, de que usan en la Nueva España los plateros para blanquear la plata. En un río de la Nueva España se halla una arena tan blanca, que es buena para calenturas.

Los usos en que gastamos la arena son en la mezcla para los edificios de cal y canto; cuanto se gasta en esta ciudad de Lima para este menester, la sacan de su río, y la que se gasta en el puerto de Callao, se recoge de la playa de la mar, que es más gruesa y tenida de los albañiles por mejor; de la cual se gastó toda la que entró en la fábrica de la muralla y fortificaciones de aquel puerto. La arena más menudita y sutil sirve para relojes de ampolletas; la ordinaria no muy gruesa, para labrar mármoles; la más gruesa, para solar los caminos, para evitar los lodos cuando llueve. Para este efecto vi yo echar una capa de arena en las calles de una huerta, con que se podía pasear por ellas en acabando de llover, sin que hubiese lodo que lo impidiese.

Para ninguna destas cosas tuvieron uso de arena los indios, porque ni alcanzaron la mezcla de cal y arena que se hace para los edificios, ni se aprovecharon della para labrar mármoles ni para hacer relojes, que nunca tuvieron, de arena, ni de ruedas, ni de sol; ni menos para echar sobre las escrituras, que nunca supieron. Sólo en una cosa hallo que los indios desta costa del Perú se aprovechaban de la arena, que era para encolcar el maíz, porque no le diese gorgojo; y llaman encolcar, guardarlo en la troj revuelto con arena menuda; y de ellos aprendieron los españoles a encolcar el trigo.
results.translation:
CHAPTER XII
On Sand

There are various kinds of sand to be found in this land, both on the shores of the sea and of rivers, and in many other places on the surface and in the bowels of the earth. This variety is chiefly manifested in two respects: one is in color, and the other is in some sand being finer or coarser than another. From the grinding and washing of silver ores there results a great quantity of sand. On the seacoast of Pisco and Callao one finds much sand made of crushed and ground shells: it is white, and they mix it with lime for building construction. Beyond common sand, in many places one finds a fine, black sand [arenilla], which is customarily sprinkled over freshly written documents in place of bran [salvado], and there is so great a quantity of it in this kingdom that I have not seen bran used for this purpose anywhere in it, since everyone can obtain the fine sand.

For the same purpose there serve two other types that we may call fine sands [arenillas]: one is margarite [fools' gold / mica], found in many rivers, which in color and brilliance resembles powdered gold and has deceived many a greedy person into thinking it was gold; the other is white, like silver filings. From these last three varieties of sand, collectors [curiosos] customarily stock their sand-shakers [salvaderas].

In the province of Florida there is found a certain kind of very fine, white sand, which silversmiths [plateros] in New Spain use to whiten silver. In a river of New Spain there is found a sand so white that it is good for fevers [calenturas].

The uses to which we put sand are: in mortar for lime-and-stone [cal y canto] construction; all the sand used in the city of Lima for this purpose is taken from its river, while that used in the port of Callao is collected from the seashore, which is coarser and considered better by the masons; it was entirely from this shore sand that all which went into the construction of the wall and fortifications of that port was drawn. The finest and most delicate sand serves for hourglass clocks [relojes de ampolletas]; the ordinary, not very coarse variety, for working marble; the coarsest, for paving roads to prevent mud when it rains. For this purpose I myself saw a layer of sand spread on the garden paths of an orchard, so that one could walk along them immediately after rain without any mud to impede passage.

The Indians made no use of sand for any of these purposes, for they did not achieve the lime-and-sand mortar used in building, nor did they employ it for working marble, nor for making clocks — which they never had, whether of sand, of wheels, or of the sun — nor still less for sprinkling over writings, of which they had no knowledge. I find only one use to which the Indians of this coast of Peru put sand: namely, for encolcar [storing] maize so that weevils would not get into it. By encolcar they mean storing it in the granary [troj] mixed with fine sand. And from them the Spaniards learned to encolcar wheat.

entities_mentioned.name: margarita (arenilla dorada)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Mica or pyrite (fools' gold) in fluvial deposits
entities_mentioned.notes: Described as resembling powdered gold in color and brilliance, found in many rivers; likely refers to mica schist particles or fine pyrite, both common in Andean river systems.

entities_mentioned.name: arenilla blanca (como limaduras de plata)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Possibly feldspar or quartz sand with silvery mica
entities_mentioned.notes: Described as white, resembling silver filings; used as a writing sand in sand-shakers.

entities_mentioned.name: arena blanca de Florida / Nueva España (para plateros)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Possibly fine quartz sand or pumice used for burnishing/whitening silver
entities_mentioned.notes: Used by silversmiths in New Spain to whiten silver; likely an abrasive polishing sand.

entities_mentioned.name: arena blanca de río de Nueva España (buena para calenturas)
entities_mentioned.notes: Cobo states this white river sand is 'good for fevers [calenturas]'; no preparation method is given. This may reflect a therapeutic use of mineral-rich or cool river sand applied externally, but the passage is too brief to identify the substance or mechanism with confidence.

entities_mentioned.name: maíz encolcado (maize stored with sand)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Zea mays — indigenous grain storage practice
entities_mentioned.notes: Indigenous Peruvian practice of mixing maize with fine sand in granaries (trojes) to prevent weevil infestation; the Spanish adopted this technique for wheat storage. Not a medicinal use, but an agricultural/preservation practice.

results.translator_notes: 1. The OCR rendered 'riberas' (shores/banks) as 'dieras' in the opening lines; corrected silently. 2. 'pe' at line 2 is clearly an OCR artifact for 'que'; corrected silently. 3. 'apro vedi al )iui' is an OCR corruption of 'aprovechaban'; corrected silently. 4. The word 'encolcar' is a technical term for grain storage mixed with sand; it is preserved in brackets in the translation. 5. The medical claim about white river sand being 'good for fevers' (buena para calenturas) is noted under entities as it may represent a therapeutic use, but no preparation or administration method is described by Cobo. 6. 'Salvaderas' were small containers (pounce pots or sand-shakers) used to dry ink on manuscripts; translated as 'sand-shakers' for clarity. 7. Footnote marker (9) in the original is retained but not transcribed into the translation as its content is not present in the segment.

results.title_inferred: Common Stones and Quarry Materials of Peru and the New World
results.original_text:
CAPITULO XIII
De las piedras comunes

Entrando en el género de canteras minerales y toda suerte de piedras, pongo, en primer lugar, las más comunes, que generalmente se hallan dondequiera, cuales son las que gastamos en los edificios y en otros usos caseros. Hállanse en esta tierra todos los géneros de piedras que se conocen en Europa y algunos otros particulares que son propios de acá. En cuatro cosas, principalmente, se diferencian todas las especies de piedras, conviene a saber: en el color, peso, dureza y lisura; porque unas son muy sólidas y pesadas, y otras, porosas y livianas; unas, duras, y otras, blandas. Hállanse de todos colores, como son negras, blancas, coloradas, amarillas, verdes, azules y manchadas y de los demás colores que son medios entre los referidos. Unas, areniscas; otras, terrosas; otras, harinosas, y otras, vedriosas. Lo que yo tengo observado en este género es que las canteras y minerales, en especial los más preciosos y perfectos, se deben de engendrar predominando el frío sobre las otras cualidades; porque experimentamos en este reino del Perú que en las sierras y tierras frías y de páramos se hallan en gran abundancia no sólo las minas de plata y demás metales, sino también toda suerte de mineras de piedras a propósito para edificios, de preciosos mármoles, jaspes y todo género de canteras de estimación; y, por el contrario, las tierras calientes, aun de piedras francas para cantería, son estériles, mayormente las llanas, que no tienen vecindad de sierras, como son las provincias de Tucumán y Paraguay, adonde en muchas leguas no se halla ni una piedra con que hincar un clavo.

En muchas riberas de la mar y en los más de los ríos, y también la tierra adentro en lugares apartados de la costa y de ríos, se halla gran copia de las piedras más comunes y ordinarias, cuales son las guijarreñas, lisas y hermosas, de todos tamaños. Suélense sacar algunas de los ríos mayores que crecidas tinajas, que, aunque se labran con dificultad por su mucha dureza, son muy vistosas y de estima, particularmente para basas de portadas. Muchas tierras marítimas hay que tienen toda su profundidad de piedras de este género, como vemos en barrancas altas así de ríos como de la costa de la mar, adonde, con los terremotos, se suelen arrancar muchas y rodar a lo bajo. El suelo de todo este valle de Lima está compuesto de guijarros, arena y cascajo, sacando la superficie, que es una corteza de tierra que suele tener de grueso de una a dos varas, poco más o menos; y así, de los pozos que se hacen en esta ciudad se saca mucha desta piedra desde el tamaño de una avellana hasta de la grandeza de la cabeza, y algunas mucho mayores, que tienen a tres o cuatro arrobas de peso; y lo mismo pasa en las piedras de los ríos, que las hay muy grandes y muy pequeñas. Aprovéchanse los españoles de este género de piedras para empedrar las calles y patios de sus casas; los guijarros grandes echan en los cimientos de los edificios; los blancos, suelen aprovechar los olleros para hacer el vedrio de la loza; y, finalmente, en puertos de mar sirven estos guijarros para lastrar los navíos, y es el mejor lastre de todos.

No tuvieron los indios muchos usos de estas guijas, porque ni tuvieron navíos que lastrar con ellas, ni supieron empedrar el suelo de las calles y casas de sus pueblos, pues en las ruinas de ellos tal cosa hemos hallado; sólo les servían estos guijarros de martillos y herramientas para labrar las piedras de cantería, y de munición en la guerra para tirar con sus hondas, en que eran muy diestros. Algunas destas piedras guijarreñas son muy finos pedernales. También podemos reducir a este linaje de piedras unas losas tan duras como ellas, que se suelen hallar sueltas, muy llanas y lisas, de dos o tres palmos de diámetro, en las cuales muelen los indios el maíz y otras cosas.
results.translation:
CHAPTER XIII
On Common Stones

Turning now to the subject of mineral quarries [canteras minerales] and all manner of stones, I shall place first those most common, which are generally found everywhere, namely those we use in buildings and other domestic purposes. In this land one finds all the varieties of stone known in Europe, along with certain particular ones that are native to this region. All species of stone differ primarily in four respects: color, weight, hardness, and smoothness; for some are very solid and heavy while others are porous and light; some hard, others soft. They are found in every color—black, white, red, yellow, green, blue, mottled, and all intermediate shades. Some are sandy in texture, others earthy, others floury [harinosas], and others glassy [vedriosas].

What I have observed regarding this subject is that quarries and mineral deposits—especially the most precious and perfect—must be generated where cold predominates over the other qualities [i.e., humors/elemental qualities]; for we find in this kingdom of Peru that in the mountain ranges and cold highland plateaus [páramos] there is great abundance not only of silver mines and other metals, but also all manner of stone deposits suitable for building, of precious marbles, jaspers, and every kind of valued quarry material. By contrast, the warm lowland regions are barren even of ordinary freestone [piedras francas] for quarrying—particularly the flat regions that have no mountains nearby, such as the provinces of Tucumán and Paraguay, where for many leagues one cannot find so much as a stone large enough to drive a nail.

Along many seashores and in most rivers, and also inland in places far from the coast and rivers, one finds a great abundance of the most common and ordinary stones: smooth, handsome pebbles [guijarros] of every size. Some are taken from the larger rivers that are bigger than large earthenware jars [tinajas]; and although they are difficult to work on account of their great hardness, they are very attractive and prized, particularly for the bases of doorways and portals. Many coastal lands have their entire depth composed of stones of this kind, as can be seen in the high bluffs of both rivers and the sea coast, where earthquakes frequently dislodge large numbers and send them rolling to lower ground. The ground of the entire valley of Lima is composed of pebbles, sand, and gravel, apart from the surface layer, which is a crust of earth usually one to two varas [approximately 0.84–1.68 meters] thick, more or less. From the wells dug in this city, therefore, great quantities of this stone are extracted, ranging in size from a hazelnut to a human head, and some much larger, weighing three or four arrobas [approximately 34–45 kg]; and the same is true of river stones, which likewise vary from very large to very small. The Spaniards make use of this type of stone to pave the streets and courtyards of their houses; the large pebbles are placed in the foundations of buildings; the white ones are customarily used by potters to make the glaze [vedrio] for earthenware; and finally, in seaports these pebbles serve as ballast for ships, and they are considered the best ballast of all.

The Indians made little use of these pebbles, for they had no ships to ballast with them, nor did they know how to pave the streets and floors of their towns—no such thing has been found in the ruins of their settlements. The pebbles served them only as hammers and tools for dressing quarry stones, and as ammunition in war for slinging with their slings [hondas], at which they were very skilled. Some of these pebbles are very fine flints [pedernales]. We may also include in this category certain flat slabs as hard as the pebbles themselves, which are commonly found loose on the ground, very flat and smooth, two or three hand-spans [palmos] in diameter, upon which the Indians grind maize and other things.

entities_mentioned.name: guijarros / guijas (pebbles)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Rounded river/coastal cobblestones and pebbles (various lithologies, predominantly quartzite and granite in the Lima valley context)
entities_mentioned.notes: Cobo describes multiple uses: building foundations, street paving, ship ballast, potters' glaze-making, and (for the indigenous population) hammerstones and sling ammunition.

entities_mentioned.name: pedernales (flints)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Flint / chert (cryptocrystalline silica)
entities_mentioned.notes: Noted as a subset of the pebble category; valued for tool-making.

entities_mentioned.name: mármoles (marbles)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Metamorphic marble
entities_mentioned.notes: Reported as abundant in the cold sierra highlands of Peru.

entities_mentioned.name: jaspes (jaspers)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Jasper (opaque microcrystalline quartz) or, by 17th-century usage, possibly various ornamental siliceous stones
entities_mentioned.notes: Period usage of 'jaspe' could encompass a broader range of banded or coloured hard stones than the modern mineralogical term.

entities_mentioned.name: losas para moler maíz
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Grinding stones / manos and metates (used by indigenous Andean peoples for processing maize)
entities_mentioned.notes: Described as flat, smooth slabs of 2–3 palmos diameter found loose on the ground; functionally equivalent to the Mesoamerican metate.

entities_mentioned.name: vedrio de la loza (pottery glaze)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Lead glaze or silica-based ceramic glaze; white pebbles used as a silica source
entities_mentioned.notes: Cobo notes potters (olleros) used white pebbles to produce glaze; likely a quartz or silica-rich source material fired with lead oxide to produce a glassy coating.

The segment is included in the Materia Medica Americana source volume but covers natural history broadly. 2. Several OCR artifacts were silently corrected: 'ol>servado' → 'observado'; 'má*s ptecio-ms V q>erfectos' → 'más preciosos y perfectos'; 'tieiWMi' → 'tienen'; 'mejer' → 'mejor'; the footnote marker '(9)' and its text regarding 'entrojar/ccolku' are a transcriber's footnote to an earlier chapter and have been retained in the cleaned original but are not part of the chapter body. 3. 'Piedras francas' is a technical quarrying term meaning freestone—stone that can be freely cut in any direction without splitting along a grain; rendered as such in translation. 4. 'Arrobas' (unit of weight, ~11.5 kg) and 'varas' (unit of length, ~0.836 m) are retained as period units with approximate modern equivalents supplied. 5. Cobo's quasi-Galenic explanation that precious minerals form in cold climates ('predominando el frío sobre las otras cualidades') reflects the humoral/elemental natural philosophy of the period.
