{
  "title_inferred": "Building Stones of the Indies: Quarries, Types, and Uses",
  "original_text": "CAPITULO XIV
De las piedras para fábricas

Toda suerte de piedra franca y berroqueña que es suave de labrar es la que decimos ser a propósito para edificios de cantería, de la cual hay escogidas canteras en todas estas Indias; unas piedras se hallan muy livianas y cavernosas, a modo de un panal o piedra pómez, que no se hunde en el agua, la cual es maravillosa para fábricas, señaladamente para murallas y fortalezas; semejantes a ellas hay otros muchos géneros de piedras livianas y esponjosas de todos colores.

En la ciudad de Arequipa hay una cantera de piedra blanca tan blanda, que se puede labrar con un cuchillo; es muy cavernosa, y tiene algunas concavidades tan grandes, que cabe un puño en ellas y suelen estar llenas de ceniza de la que lanzan los volcanes; por donde se presume ser toda aquella piedra congelada de ceniza; y es prueba desto hallarse como se hallan dentro de la misma piedra encajadas algunas guijas, y en sus concavidades algunos pedacitos de carbón y otras cosas. Y como inquiriese yo, residiendo en aquella ciudad, estas calidades y circunstancias desta piedra, me certificó un ciudadano que en una concavidad grande desta piedra se habían hallado huesos humanos.

En la diócesis de la dicha ciudad de Arequipa y en otras muchas partes se halla piedra pómez en mucha cantidad; es la más liviana y esponjosa de todas. Lanzan estas piedras los volcanes de fuego cuando revientan, y salen ellas encendidas hechas ascuas; hállanse algunas tan grandes como medianas tinajas. Semejante a ésta es la piedra colorada de que está edificada la ciudad de México, la cual parece haber procedido de volcanes por ser tan cavernosa y liviana, que echada en el agua no se hunde luego hasta que sus poros se llenan de agua. Cualquiera piedra áspera deste género servía a los indios de rallo, porque no los tenían de metal, y con ellas rallaban la yuca de que hacían el cazab

results.title_inferred: Peruvian Stone Masonry and Indigenous Stoneworking Practices
results.original_text: nde se cortó la piedra de que se hizo la puente del río y las casas del cabildo; pero es tan recia y dura, que cuesta mucho dinero lo que della se labra. A toda suerte de piedra de cantería buena de labrar llaman los indios del Perú checorumi. Aprovechábanse dellas en los mismos usos que nosotros, excepto el destilar agua, que no alcanzaron; y como carecían de instrumentos de hierro, las labraban con otras piedras pequeñas muy duras y pesadas, que en su lengua llaman vini.
results.translation: ...where the stone was quarried from which the bridge over the river and the town council buildings [casas del cabildo] were made; but it is so tough and hard that whatever is worked from it costs a great deal of money. All manner of good ashlar stone [piedra de cantería] suitable for working, the Indians of Peru call checorumi. They made use of it for the same purposes as we do, except for distilling water, which they did not know; and since they lacked iron tools, they worked the stone with other small, very hard and heavy stones, which in their language they call vini.

entities_mentioned.name: checorumi
entities_mentioned.notes: Quechua term used by Peruvian indigenous people to denote any good-quality ashlar or dressed stone suitable for construction. Likely from Quechua 'rumi' (stone) with a qualifying prefix.

entities_mentioned.name: vini
entities_mentioned.notes: Quechua term for small, very hard and heavy stones used as hammering/dressing tools in place of iron instruments. Likely refers to stone mauls or hammer stones used in Andean masonry; possibly related to Quechua 'wini' or a similar term. No iron tools were available, so these served as the primary lapidary implements.

results.translator_notes: This segment does not describe a plant, drug, or medical practice, so no pharmacological extraction is applicable. The OCR artifact 'e§' has been silently corrected to 'es', and 'carecían de instrumen¬tos' (hyphenated across a line break) has been silently joined. The reference to 'destilar agua' (distilling water) is notable — Cobo claims indigenous Peruvians did not practice water distillation via stone vessels, though the precise meaning may refer to filtering or condensing water through stone containers rather than alchemical distillation. The segment appears to begin mid-sentence, referencing a quarry location described earlier in the chapter.

results.title_inferred: On Various Flints and Flint-like Stones
results.original_text:
CAPITULO XV
De varios pedernales

En todas partes, señaladamente en las sierras y tierras frías, se hallan muchas diferencias de pedernales finos, los cuales conocían muy bien y estimaban los indios, aunque no para sacar fuego: pero usaban dellos en lugar de cuchillos y otras herramientas. Hállanse pedernales de todos colores, y un género de piedra que parece pedernal, tan colorada como un coral, muy estimada de los indios, a la cual llaman mulk, y hacen della sartas de cuentas, que las indias traen al cuello por gala. Entre los guijarros de los ríos y de la costa de la mar se hallan muchos que son perfectos pedernales, cuales son unos guijarros muy duros, que se traen del río de Guayaquil, de un color amarillo escuro. Pero donde mayor copia de pedernales yo he visto es en la provincia de la Mixteca, en la Nueva España, en la cual, casi cuantas piedras encontraba en el camino eran escogidos pedernales blancos muy finos; y juntamente con ellos se vende un género de yesca, que es la mejor que yo he visto en mi vida, la cual sacan del corazón de ciertos árboles. [ILLEGIBLE: one or two lines lost; apparent reference to the stone of the coconut of Paraguay.] La piedra del coco del Paraguay se forma de tierra, es tan recio como pedernal, que gasta mucho el eslabón de acero. También es fino pedernal el palo que debajo del agua se convierte en piedra.
results.translation:
CHAPTER XV
On Various Flints

Everywhere, and especially in the mountain ranges and cold lands, one finds many varieties of fine flints [pedernales], which the Indians knew well and held in high esteem — not, however, for striking fire, but rather for use in place of knives and other tools. Flints of every color are found, and there is one kind of stone that resembles flint, as red as coral, much prized by the Indians, which they call mulk; from it they make strings of beads, which the Indian women wear around their necks as adornment. Among the river pebbles and those along the sea-coast, many are found that are perfect flints; among these are certain very hard pebbles brought from the Guayaquil River, of a dark yellow color. But the greatest abundance of flints I have ever seen is in the province of the Mixteca, in New Spain, where nearly every stone one encountered on the road was a choice, very fine white flint; and sold alongside them is a kind of tinder [yesca] that is the best I have ever seen in my life, which is taken from the heartwood of certain trees. [ILLEGIBLE: one or two lines lost or garbled; apparent reference to the nut-stone of Paraguay.] The stone of the coconut of Paraguay is formed from earth [i.e., it is an organic concretion], yet it is as hard as flint, and wears down a steel fire-striker considerably. Also a fine flint-like substance is the wood that, when submerged under water, turns to stone [i.e., petrified wood].

entities_mentioned.name: mulk
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Possibly a red chert, jasper, or carnelian-like stone; the name may be a Quechua or regional Andean term
entities_mentioned.notes: Described as coral-red and flint-like, used for bead-making. The term 'mulk' does not appear in standard Quechua lexicons with certainty; may be a local or variant spelling.

entities_mentioned.name: pedernal (flint)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Chert / flint (microcrystalline silica)
entities_mentioned.notes: Used by indigenous peoples as cutting tools rather than for fire-striking, according to Cobo.

entities_mentioned.name: guijarros del río de Guayaquil
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Dark yellow river pebbles from the Guayaquil River basin, Ecuador; possibly silicified or quartzite pebbles
entities_mentioned.notes: Color described as 'amarillo escuro' (dark yellow); no further identification possible without mineralogical data.

entities_mentioned.name: yesca (tinder from heartwood)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Possibly amadou / tinder fungus, or heartwood-derived char tinder; specific tree not named in surviving text
entities_mentioned.notes: Cobo describes it as the finest tinder he ever encountered, extracted from the heart of certain trees in Mixteca, New Spain. The tree species is not named in the legible text.

entities_mentioned.name: piedra del coco del Paraguay
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Likely the stony endosperm or concretion associated with a palm nut from the Río de la Plata region; possibly tagua-like or a bezoar-type concretion
entities_mentioned.notes: The surrounding text is garbled or partially lost (OCR failure). Cobo states it is formed from earth but is as hard as flint and wears steel.

entities_mentioned.name: palo que debajo del agua se convierte en piedra
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Petrified wood (silicified wood)
entities_mentioned.notes: Cobo describes this as wood that turns to stone underwater, consistent with petrification; he considers it a type of flint.

results.translator_notes: This chapter (XV) concerns mineralogy rather than botany or materia medica; no medicinal plants or preparations are described. Several lines in the middle of the segment are severely corrupted by OCR and possibly by damage to the original printed page; the transition between the Mixteca tinder passage and the Paraguay coconut-stone passage is incoherent in the source and has been flagged as [ILLEGIBLE]. The footnote marker '(üfi) Hoy de San Lorenzo' appears to be a 19th-century editor's note identifying a geographic location (likely 'Hoy [llamado] de San Lorenzo'), not part of Cobo's original text; it has been omitted from the cleaned original. The term 'yesca' is preserved in brackets in the translation as it carries a specific technical meaning (tinder/amadou) not fully captured by a single English word.

results.title_inferred: On gypsum, marble, and alabaster quarries in the Indies (Chapters XVII–XVIII)
results.original_text:
CAPITULO XVII
De la piedra de yeso

De piedra de yeso se hallan menos canteras que de cal, particularmente en tierras calientes; en las tierras frías hay grandes cerros della, y alguna muy fina, de espejuelo, como es en el distrito de Guamanga y del Cuzco. En los valles destos llanos se halla alguna, particularmente en los que son abundante de viñas, como son el de Vítor, en la diócesis de Arequipa, y el de Pisco, en este arzobispado de Lima: que parece proveyó Dios tanto tiempo antes a la necesidad que en los tales valles había de haber de yeso para el beneficio del mucho vino que al presente se coge en ellos.

Los indios deste reino del Perú no alcanzaron el uso de la cal y yeso en sus edificios, pues no supieron hacer la mezcla de cal y arena ni edificar de yeso, que cierto hubiera sido de grandísima utilidad para estos tiempos, y ahorraran a los españoles de gran costa, si hubieran conocido la cal y usado della en sus fábricas; porque todas las acequias, tajamares, puentes y otras fábricas que en tiempo de su gentilidad hicieron de piedra seca, si las hubieran hecho con mezcla de cal y arena, con lo cual hubieran sido de más duración y no se hubieran arruinado tan en breve; cuyo reparo cuesta hoy mucho dinero y trabajo. Los indios mejicanos alcanzaron el uso de la cal, si bien no la mezclaban con arena, sino con una piedra molida, que llaman tezonte; ni la cocían en hornos, sino, hecho un montón desta piedra, le daban fuego.

CAPITULO XVIII
De los mármoles y alabastros que se hallan en Indias

En ninguno de los edificios de cantería antiguos que vemos en este reino del Perú, hecho por los reyes Incas, ni en los de la Nueva España, fabricados por los reyes mejicanos, se halla alguna cosa labrada de mármol, alabastro, ni jaspe o pórfido, con haber en estas Indias muchas canteras destos géneros de piedras. Solamente acostumbraban los indios deste reino del Perú hacer destas piedras algunas cosas pequeñas y manuales, como son morteros y otras a este talle. Las diferencias de mármoles preciosos que hallamos en esta tierra son muchos, particularmente en las sierras frías deste reino del Perú, donde hay riquísimas canteras de mármol de todos los colores.

En la diócesis de Chuquiabo se saca un género de mármol tan excelente, que me afirmó un cantero que trabajó en aquella real fábrica de San Lorenzo el Real del Escorial no haberse gastado en aquel edificio tan precioso mármol como éste; es blanco como una nieve, recio y casi transparente, sin que tenga mancha o veta alguna que lo escurezca. Yo vi en la ciudad de Chuquiabo, el año de 1610, labrado deste mármol un pilar de una fuente, y con ser casi tan grueso como el cuerpo de un hombre, lo penetraba de noche la luz de una vela, de suerte que con la luz della que pasaba por el pilar, se leía muy bien una carta; y después de puesto en su fuente, se ve subir por él el agua. Deste género de mármol se labraron las columnas del sagrario de San Francisco desta ciudad de Lima y las pilas de agua bendita de aquella iglesia, y asimismo la fuente que el convento de San Agustín desta ciudad tiene en medio de su claustro, que es muy curiosa y costó mucho dinero.

En la provincia de Guaylas, diócesis desta ciudad de Lima, hay otra cantera de muy fino mármol blanco de que hay labradas en esta ciudad tres o cuatro fuentes. No es menos precioso el mármol de Tecale, pueblo de la diócesis de la Puebla, en la Nueva España: es muy blanco y casi transparente, y tan fácil de labrar, que hacen los indios dél todo género de vasos, tazas con sus salvillas o platos, jarros, tinteros, salvaderas, cruces, aras y otras mil curiosidades; desta piedra tiene el convento de San Francisco de la Puebla el púlpito, todo de una pieza.

En la diócesis de Guamanga hay un gran cerro lleno de vetas de finísimo alabastro, blanco como la nieve, de que se labran imágenes de bulto pequeñas, muy curiosas y estimadas doquiera que las llevan; y es tan blanda esta piedra, que remojada en agua la labran con un cuchillo.
results.translation:
CHAPTER XVII
On gypsum stone

Quarries of gypsum stone [piedra de yeso] are less common than those of lime, particularly in hot lands; in cold lands there are great hills of it, and some of very fine quality, of the selenite [espejuelo] variety, as is the case in the districts of Guamanga and Cuzco. In the coastal valleys some is found as well, particularly in those abounding in vineyards, such as the valley of Vítor, in the diocese of Arequipa, and that of Pisco, in this archbishopric of Lima: it would seem that God provided, so long in advance, for the need these valleys would have for gypsum in the treatment [beneficio] of the great quantity of wine that is presently harvested in them.

The Indians of this kingdom of Peru did not attain the use of lime and gypsum in their buildings, for they did not know how to make a mortar of lime and sand, nor how to build with gypsum — which would certainly have been of the greatest utility in these times, and would have spared the Spaniards great expense, had they known lime and used it in their constructions; for all the irrigation channels [acequias], breakwaters [tajamares], bridges, and other structures which they built of dry stone during the time of their paganism [gentilidad], had they been built with a mortar of lime and sand, would have been far more durable and would not have fallen into ruin so quickly — the repair of which today costs much money and labor. The Mexican Indians did attain the use of lime, although they did not mix it with sand but rather with a ground stone which they call tezonte [volcanic rock]; nor did they fire it in kilns, but instead piled up a heap of this stone and set it alight.

CHAPTER XVIII
On the marbles and alabasters found in the Indies

In none of the ancient masonry buildings that we see in this kingdom of Peru, built by the Inca kings, nor in those of New Spain, constructed by the Mexican kings, is there anything worked in marble, alabaster, jasper, or porphyry, even though these Indies possess many quarries of such kinds of stone. The Indians of this kingdom of Peru were only accustomed to making from these stones certain small, handheld objects, such as mortars and other items of that sort. The varieties of precious marble found in this land are many, particularly in the cold highlands [sierras frías] of this kingdom of Peru, where there are exceedingly rich quarries of marble of every color.

In the diocese of Chuquiabo [La Paz] there is quarried a variety of marble so excellent that a stonemason who had worked at that royal building of San Lorenzo el Real del Escorial assured me that no marble so precious as this had been used in that edifice; it is as white as snow, hard, and nearly transparent, without any spot or vein to darken it. I myself saw, in the city of Chuquiabo in the year 1610, a fountain pillar worked from this marble which, though it was nearly as thick as a man's body, was penetrated at night by the light of a candle, such that by the light passing through the pillar one could read a letter quite clearly; and once placed in its fountain, the water can be seen rising through it. From this same marble were worked the columns of the sacristy [sagrario] of San Francisco in this city of Lima, the holy-water stoups [pilas de agua bendita] of that church, and likewise the fountain that the convent of San Agustín in this city has in the center of its cloister, which is very fine and cost a great deal of money.

In the province of Guaylas, in the diocese of this city of Lima, there is another quarry of very fine white marble from which three or four fountains in this city have been fashioned. No less precious is the marble of Tecale, a town in the diocese of Puebla in New Spain: it is very white and nearly transparent, and so easy to work that the Indians make from it all manner of vessels — cups with their saucers or plates, pitchers, inkwells, sand-casters [salvaderas], crosses, altar stones [aras], and a thousand other curiosities. From this stone the convent of San Francisco in Puebla has its pulpit, all in one piece.

In the diocese of Guamanga there is a great hill full of veins of the finest alabaster, white as snow, from which small sculpted images [imágenes de bulto] are fashioned — very fine and prized wherever they are taken; and the stone is so soft that, soaked in water, it can be carved with a knife.

entities_mentioned.name: piedra de yeso (gypsum stone)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Gypsum (calcium sulfate dihydrate, CaSO₄·2H₂O)
entities_mentioned.notes: Cobo notes selenite-grade (espejuelo) gypsum in the highlands of Guamanga and Cuzco; also found in wine-producing coastal valleys of Vítor and Pisco, where it was used in wine treatment (fining/plastering of must).

entities_mentioned.name: tezonte
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Tezontle — vesicular volcanic basalt/scoria (Mexico)
entities_mentioned.notes: Used by Mexican Indians as a substitute for sand when slaking lime, piled and burned in open heaps rather than kilns.

entities_mentioned.name: mármol de Chuquiabo
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: High-grade white crystalline marble, likely from the Cordillera Real near La Paz, Bolivia
entities_mentioned.notes: Described as nearly transparent; used for columns and stoups at San Francisco, Lima, and the cloister fountain of San Agustín, Lima. Cobo witnessed its translucency firsthand in 1610.

entities_mentioned.name: mármol de Guaylas
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: White marble from the Callejón de Huaylas / Áncash region, Peru
entities_mentioned.notes: Used for several fountains in Lima.

entities_mentioned.name: mármol de Tecale
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Tecali de Herrera onyx-marble (calcite onyx / travertine alabaster), Puebla, Mexico
entities_mentioned.notes: Correctly identified as the famous translucent calcite stone of Tecali, Puebla; worked into a wide variety of vessels and ecclesiastical objects, including the one-piece pulpit of San Francisco, Puebla.

entities_mentioned.name: alabastro de Guamanga
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Alabaster (fine-grained gypsum, CaSO₄·2H₂O) from the Ayacucho (Huamanga) region, Peru
entities_mentioned.notes: Described as soft enough to carve with a knife when soaked in water — characteristic of gypsite alabaster. Still quarried in the Ayacucho region today and used for decorative sculpture.

results.translator_notes: 1. The segment contains no botanical or pharmacological content; it is a mineralogical/architectural passage covering gypsum, marble, and alabaster. The extraction fields for plant/drug/medical use are therefore not applicable. 2. The editorial footnote in the original (marked '(14)') about 'cal hidráulica' and 'carbonato de cal arcilloso' appears to be a modern editorial annotation in the source edition, not Cobo's own text; it has been silently omitted from the cleaned original text as it interrupts the flow. 3. The OCR rendered 'diócesis' as 'diócesi‍s' and 'ílióeesi' in one instance; these have been silently corrected. 4. 'Chuquiabo' is the colonial Spanish name for present-day La Paz, Bolivia. 5. The word 'espejuelo' specifically denotes selenite, the transparent crystalline variety of gypsum, not merely 'fine gypsum' in general — this is preserved in brackets in the translation.

results.title_inferred: On Jasper and Porphyry Stones and Their Medicinal Uses in Peru and New Spain
results.original_text:
CAPITULO XIX

De las piedras de jaspe y pórfido

Así en este reino del Perú como en la Nueva España se halla toda suerte de jaspes y pórfidos. En la provincia de Atacama, diócesis de los Charcas, se dan unas piedras amarillas manchadas y muy vistosas, de rico jaspe, de que se hacen aras para los altares; y es útil este jaspe para mal de ijada y de orina, tomando sus polvos en vino en cantidad de media o de una drama [síc], y aplicando la misma piedra sobre el dolor.

En la Nueva España hay cierta especie de jaspe, que es una piedra roja oscura con algunas pintas verdes, que restaña el flujo de sangre y es muy parecida a la calcedonia.

Item, otra suerte de jaspe verdoso con algunas pintillas de sangre, que dicen los indios que atada a la muñeca detiene las cámaras de sangre y cualesquiera flujos sanguíneos; y echando sus polvos en las narices o en cualquiera otra parte que padezca flujo de sangre.

Hállase otra especie de jaspe muy común en esta tierra, variado con unas pintas blancas, al modo de esmeralda oscura, de la cual dicen los indios que, atada al brazo o puesta sobre los riñones, quiebra las piedras y provoca la orina. Suélenla traer al cuello los indios, y es más estimada la verde más clara y que tiene las pintas blancas como leche.

Otra especie se halla de jaspe verdoso más oscuro y pesado que el pasado, con manchas cenicientas. Es tenida por buena esta piedra contra el mal de ijada.

En la misma cantera arriba dicha del mármol que se saca en el pueblo de Tecale, de la Nueva España, se hallan vetas de rico jaspe blanco y con manchas muy vistosas de otros colores. Otras especies de jaspes parecidos a éstos se hallan, que aprovechan para diferentes males.
results.translation:
CHAPTER XIX

On Jasper and Porphyry Stones

Both in this kingdom of Peru and in New Spain, every sort of jasper and porphyry is to be found. In the province of Atacama, in the diocese of Charcas, there occur certain yellow, spotted, and very striking stones of rich jasper, from which altar slabs [aras] are made. This jasper is useful for flank pain [mal de ijada] and afflictions of the urine, taken as a powder in wine in the quantity of half a dram or one dram [síc], and by applying the stone itself over the seat of pain.

In New Spain there is a certain species of jasper — a dark red stone with some green spots — that stanches the flow of blood, and it closely resembles chalcedony.

Likewise, another sort of greenish jasper with small blood-red flecks, which the Indians say, when tied to the wrist, stops bloody fluxes [cámaras de sangre] and any hemorrhages whatsoever; and by blowing its powder into the nostrils or applying it to any other part suffering from a flow of blood.

There is found another species of jasper, very common in this land, variegated with white spots in the manner of a dark emerald, of which the Indians say that, tied to the arm or placed over the kidneys, it breaks up stones [kidney stones] and promotes urination. The Indians customarily wear it around the neck, and the lighter green variety having spots as white as milk is the most prized.

Another species of jasper is found, darker and heavier than the preceding, greenish in color with ashen-gray spots. This stone is held to be good against flank pain [mal de ijada].

In the same quarry mentioned above — from which marble is extracted in the town of Tecali in New Spain — there are veins of rich white jasper with very striking spots of other colors. Other species of jasper similar to these are found there as well, which are of use against various ailments.

entities_mentioned.name: Jaspe amarillo de Atacama (yellow jasper of Atacama)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Yellow jasper (silicon dioxide, cryptocrystalline quartz variety); likely sourced from the Atacama region of northern Chile/southern Peru
entities_mentioned.notes: Used as altar slabs and medicinally as a powder in wine for flank pain (colic/nephritic pain) and urinary complaints; also applied topically. Dram dosage noted with 'síc' indicating Cobo's own uncertainty or scribal query about the unit.

entities_mentioned.name: Calcedonia (chalcedony)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Chalcedony (cryptocrystalline quartz)
entities_mentioned.notes: Referenced as a comparator for the dark red jasper with green spots found in New Spain.

entities_mentioned.name: Jaspe rojo oscuro con pintas verdes (dark red jasper with green spots)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Possibly bloodstone (heliotrope), a variety of chalcedony/jasper
entities_mentioned.notes: Described as staunching blood flow; the description closely matches heliotrope/bloodstone, a stone long associated in European and indigenous traditions with hemostasis.

entities_mentioned.name: Jaspe verdoso con pintillas de sangre (greenish jasper with blood-red flecks)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Bloodstone (heliotrope), Chalcedony var.
entities_mentioned.notes: Tied to the wrist or powdered and applied to the nostrils for bloody flux and hemorrhage. Closely parallels European lapidary tradition for bloodstone.

entities_mentioned.name: Jaspe verde con pintas blancas (green jasper with white spots)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Green jasper with white inclusions; possibly nephrite or green jasper (quartz-based)
entities_mentioned.notes: Used tied to the arm or placed over the kidneys for lithontriptic (stone-breaking) and diuretic effects. Worn as an amulet around the neck.

entities_mentioned.name: Jaspe verdoso oscuro con manchas cenicientas (dark greenish jasper with ashen spots)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Dark green jasper, variety uncertain
entities_mentioned.notes: Indicated for flank pain (mal de ijada); no specific preparation described.

entities_mentioned.name: Jaspe blanco de Tecali (white jasper of Tecali)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Tecali onyx-marble or alabaster (calcite-based stone), Puebla, Mexico; sometimes described as jasper in colonial sources
entities_mentioned.notes: From the famous quarry at Tecali (modern Tecali de Herrera, Puebla, Mexico), noted for its ornamental stone. Cobo's identification as 'jasper' is mineralogically loose; the material is more accurately an onyx-marble (calcite). No specific medicinal use is described for this variety beyond general utility 'for various ailments.'

entities_mentioned.name: Pórfido (porphyry)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Porphyry (igneous rock with phenocrysts)
entities_mentioned.notes: Mentioned in the chapter title but not discussed further in the surviving text of this segment; no medicinal uses described.

results.translator_notes: 1. 'Mal de ijada' (literally 'flank ailment') is a period term for colic or nephritic/renal pain, likely referring to kidney stone pain or urinary colic. 2. 'Cámaras de sangre' refers to bloody diarrhea or bloody flux (dysentery). 3. The OCR rendered 'k Nueva España' for 'la Nueva España,' 'é?' for 'de,' 'áe' for 'de,' 'q»e' for 'que,' 'óíü' for 'útil,' 'Qiareas' for 'Charcas,' and 'qite' for 'que'; all have been silently corrected as unambiguous OCR artifacts. 4. The [síc] in 'una drama [síc]' appears in the source itself, suggesting Cobo or an early copyist flagged the dosage or unit as uncertain. 5. 'Tecale' in the source is the colonial spelling of modern Tecali de Herrera, Puebla, Mexico, renowned for its ornamental stone quarries. 6. The chapter treats stones mineralogically rather than botanically; this segment falls outside the strictly botanical sections of the Materia Medica Americana but reflects Cobo's broader natural history scope.

results.title_inferred: On Stone That Congeals from Water (Travertine and Hemostatic Stone)
results.original_text:
CAPITULO XX

De la piedra que se cuaja de agua

En la villa de Guancavelica, diócesis de Guamanga, mana una fuente de agua caliente en tanta cantidad como el cuerpo de un hombre, poco más o menos; va esta agua corriendo al rio, y por donde pasa se va gran parte de ella convirtiendo en piedra. Hállanse también algunas otras fuentes desta calidad en otras partes deste reino y de la Nueva España, y la piedra que de esta agua se engendra es blanquecina y cavernosa, muy buena para fábricas, de la cual hay muchos edificios en la villa sobredicha de Guancavelica; y toda la ciudad de la Puebla de los Ángeles, en la Nueva España, está edificada de piedra cuajada de agua, de la cual también se hace la cal con que ella se asienta; y la piedra de cal del valle de Chancay, como queda dicho, se cuaja también de agua; y en otras muchas partes se hallan semejantes fuentes, cuya agua de ordinario sale caliente y es algo crasa y blanquecina. Mas débese advertir que no es la misma sustancia del agua lo que se convierte en piedra, sino la materia que en ella sale desleída y le quita su natural transparencia y claridad; porque si toda el agua se convirtiera en piedra, pudiéranse hacer moldes en que formarlas del tamaño y figura que les quisieran dar; y no se cuaja represada, sino corriendo blandamente sobre la tierra y asentándose y endureciéndose aquella lama y materia crasa que consigo lleva y la espesa.

Otra especie de piedra se halla en este reino, que es blanca y densa como piedra de yeso, la cual se convierte de agua y tiene virtud de restañar la sangre en las heridas y unirlas y apretarlas, y es buena para otras curas semejantes.
results.translation:
CHAPTER XX

On the Stone That Congeals from Water

In the town of Huancavelica, in the diocese of Guamanga [Huamanga], there issues a hot spring [fuente de agua caliente] of a volume roughly equivalent to the bulk of a man's body, more or less. This water runs toward the river, and along its course a great part of it converts into stone. Other springs of this same character are also found in various parts of this kingdom and of New Spain. The stone engendered by this water is whitish and porous [cavernosa], and very well suited for construction; there are many buildings in the aforementioned town of Huancavelica made of it. Indeed, the entire city of Puebla de los Ángeles in New Spain is built of this water-congealed stone [piedra cuajada de agua], and the lime [cal] used to set the masonry is likewise made from it. The limestone [piedra de cal] of the valley of Chancay, as has already been noted, also congeals from water; and in many other places similar springs are found, whose water ordinarily issues warm and is somewhat thick and whitish.

It should be noted, however, that it is not the substance of the water itself that converts into stone, but rather the matter that comes dissolved within it and robs it of its natural transparency and clarity. For if the water itself were turning entirely to stone, one could make molds and form pieces of whatever size and shape one wished; and furthermore, it does not congeal when dammed up, but only when flowing gently over the ground, where that silt [lama] and fatty [crasa] matter it carries settles out, thickens, and hardens.

Another species of stone is found in this kingdom that is white and dense like gypsum [piedra de yeso]; it likewise forms from water, and has the virtue of stanching blood in wounds, closing and binding them together, and is useful for other similar treatments [curas].

entities_mentioned.name: piedra cuajada de agua (Guancavelica / Puebla de los Ángeles)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Travertine (calcium carbonate deposited by hot spring waters; alabastrites in the marginal editorial note)
entities_mentioned.notes: The editorial marginal note in the source identifies this as 'alabastrites o alabastro de yeso' (alabaster or gypsum alabaster). Cobo's description—hot spring, whitish porous stone used in construction, made into lime—is consistent with travertine/calcareous tufa. Puebla de los Ángeles is indeed historically built with a similar volcanic/calcareous stone (cantera).

entities_mentioned.name: piedra blanca y densa como piedra de yeso (hemostatic stone)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Possibly calcium sulfate (gypsum) or a calcareous alabaster; alternatively a styptic alum-bearing stone
entities_mentioned.notes: Cobo describes a second, denser white stone also formed from water, used medicinally to staunch bleeding and close wounds. The styptic property suggests possible alum content or calcium sulfate. Precise identification is uncertain without further context.

entities_mentioned.name: Guancavelica (Huancavelica)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Huancavelica, Peru
entities_mentioned.notes: Famous colonial mining town in the diocese of Huamanga (modern Ayacucho region, Peru), known primarily for mercury mines but here referenced for its thermal springs.

entities_mentioned.name: Valle de Chancay
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Chancay Valley, coastal Peru (Lima region)
entities_mentioned.notes: Previously discussed by Cobo in an earlier chapter; referenced here for its similarly water-congealed limestone.

entities_mentioned.name: Puebla de los Ángeles
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Puebla, Mexico
entities_mentioned.notes: Colonial city in New Spain; Cobo claims it is built entirely of water-congealed stone.

results.translator_notes: 1. The marginal editorial gloss ('Es el denominado alabastrites o alabastro de yeso') appears to be a later editorial annotation in the printed edition, not Cobo's own text; it has been noted in the entities but not incorporated into the main translation. 2. The OCR rendered 'Gnancavelica' (corrected to 'Guancavelica'), 'ad^rtir' (corrected to 'advertir'), 'haílan/hállarise' (corrected to 'hallan/Hállanse'), 'juedra' (corrected to 'piedra'), and 'aquelia' (corrected to 'aquella'). 3. The phrase 'materia crasa' carries a humoral/scholastic sense of thick or viscous matter; rendered as 'fatty/thick matter' to preserve the period meaning. 4. This chapter is mineralogical and hydrological rather than strictly botanical, but is included in the Materia Medica context because the second stone described has explicit therapeutic applications (hemostasis).

results.title_inferred: On Stones That Form from Wood and Other Substances
results.original_text:
CAPITULO XXI

De las piedras que se engendran de palo y de otras cosas

Fuera de las piedras que cria la tierra en sus entrañas y senos, hallamos en este Nuevo Mundo otras muy particulares, cuya formación es muy diferente y como excepción del común y ordinario modo de producirse las demás; entre las cuales debe tener el primer lugar aquella tan extraña piedra que se halla en el Río de la Plata y en otras partes, la cual se transforma de las ramas de cierto árbol llamado capirona, que, habiendo estado algún tiempo metidas debajo del agua, todo aquello que cubrió el agua queda convertido en piedra, quedando lo restante de la rama que no alcanzó el agua en su naturaleza de árbol verde y con vida continuada con la piedra, que antes era palo como lo demás. La cual piedra así convertida de palo es tan perfecto pedernal, que se saca fuego della. Yo vi un pedazo desta piedra, que la mitad era palo, y la otra mitad, que era lo que había cubierto el agua, aunque tenía figura de palo y los hilos y venas como si lo fuera en realidad de verdad, era muy duro y pesado pedernal: y haciendo diligencia por saber si el agua de alguno destos ríos tiene virtud de convertir en piedra cualquiera madera o alguna particular especie della, vine a saber que no hacen los ríos este efecto sino en la madera del árbol dicho, lo cual parece ser así cierto, porque, a ser de otra manera y convertir en piedra cualquiera palo, corrieran riesgo de convertirse en piedras las canoas, balsas y demás embarcaciones que mucho tiempo navegan por estos ríos.

Algunos huesos que mucho tiempo están enterrados, suelen también convertirse en piedras, o por la calidad de la tierra, o de los mismos huesos; para prueba de lo cual, me mostró una vez una persona curiosa una muela de gigante, que se había hallado en este reino del Perú, mayor que el puño, la cual en la figura y disposición era, sin duda, muela; pero la materia no era ya hueso, sino piedra algo roja oscura, en que con la mucha antigüedad se había convertido el hueso.

La misma tierra, en algunas partes, con la virtud e influencias del clima y con las excelencias que se le mezclan, se viene a convertir en dura piedra, de que yo he visto la experiencia: porque en esta ciudad de Lima me dieron una piedra cortada de una cantera que se halló algunos estados debajo de tierra, la cual tenía dentro de sí encajados huesos humanos, de donde cuantos la vían afirmaban que no podía haber sucedido aquello de otra manera, sino que, siendo primero tierra, debieron de estar aquellos huesos enterrados en ella, y cuajándose después en piedra, se quedaron los huesos encerrados en ella.

Item, de la arena de algunas riberas de ríos y lagos se forman a veces piedras, lo cual he yo observado; porque, estando una vez sentado con otras personas en la ribera de una grande laguna, tomando acaso en la mano algunas guijas, notamos que se desmenuzaban y deshacían fácilmente; y para experimentar si las demás eran de aquella calidad, fuimos partiendo algunas, y hallamos que unas estaban tan tiernas, por acabar de formarse de la arena, que con sólo apretarlas con los dedos se desmoronaban; y otras un poco más duras, de suerte que si no era dándoles con una piedra no se deshacían; y otras, que estaban ya perfectamente cuajadas, tan recias, que servían de partir con ellas las otras, sin que ellas se quebrasen.

Pero más notables son las piedras que en las riberas del río del Paraguay se engendran de arena; las cuales son unas del grandor de la cabeza de un hombre y otras mayores y menores, y siendo muy duras, quedan huecas por dentro con una concavidad redonda o de otra forma, según la figura que toma la piedra cuando se cuaja y endurece; el cual hueco queda lleno de agua del río, la cual, por estar allí encerrada sin que la bañe el aire, cuando se abre alguna piedra de éstas, se halla muy hedionda. Suelen los españoles labrar y pulir estas piedras por de fuera, dándoles figura de calabaza, y abrirles una pequeña boca, y después de vaciada el agua hedionda de que están llenas y bien lavadas, servirse dellas para enfriar agua, para el cual efecto son estimadas.

Demás desto, tengo por sin duda que muchas de las canteras de donde se corta piedra para los edificios son asimismo formadas y condensadas de tierra, ceniza y arena; porque las señales que en ellas hallamos nos lo persuaden, como es la cantera de la ciudad de Arequipa, de que se dijo en el [preceding chapter].
results.translation:
CHAPTER XXI

On Stones That Form from Wood and Other Things

Beyond the stones that the earth produces within its bowels and cavities, we find in this New World others of a very particular kind, whose formation is quite different and constitutes, as it were, an exception to the common and ordinary manner in which the rest are produced. Chief among these must be reckoned that most singular stone found in the Río de la Plata and in other places, which is transformed from the branches of a certain tree called the capirona [Calycophyllum spruceanum or related sp.]. When these branches have spent some time submerged under water, everything that the water covered becomes converted into stone, while the remainder of the branch that did not reach the water retains its nature as a living, green tree, joined continuously to the stone that was formerly wood like the rest. This stone, so converted from wood, is so perfect a flint [pedernal] that fire can be struck from it. I myself saw a piece of this stone of which one half was wood and the other half — that which the water had covered — though retaining the shape of wood and the grain and veins as if it truly were wood, was in fact a very hard and heavy flint. And making inquiry as to whether the water of any of these rivers has the property of converting any kind of wood — or some particular species of it — into stone, I came to learn that the rivers produce this effect only in the wood of the said tree. This indeed appears to be the case, for were it otherwise, and were any piece of wood converted into stone, the canoes, rafts, and other vessels that navigate these rivers for long periods would run the risk of being turned to stone.

Certain bones that have lain buried for a long time are also sometimes converted into stones, either by virtue of the quality of the earth or of the bones themselves. In evidence of this, a curious person once showed me a giant's molar tooth [muela de gigante] that had been found in this kingdom of Peru, larger than a fist, which in shape and arrangement was unmistakably a molar tooth; yet the substance was no longer bone but a somewhat dark reddish stone into which the bone had been converted through great antiquity.

The earth itself, in certain places, by virtue of the influence of the climate and the properties mingled with it, comes to be converted into hard stone — of which I have myself seen proof. For in this city of Lima I was given a stone cut from a quarry found several fathoms [estados] underground, which had embedded within it human bones. All who examined it agreed that this could only have come about in one way: that the earth, being first soft soil, must have had those bones buried in it, and that when it subsequently set into stone, the bones were sealed within it.

Likewise, stones sometimes form from the sand on the banks of certain rivers and lakes, as I have myself observed. For, sitting one day with several other persons on the bank of a large lake and picking up some pebbles at random, we noticed that they crumbled and broke apart easily. To test whether the rest were of the same character, we began to split them open, and found that some were so soft — being newly formed from sand — that they crumbled with the mere pressure of the fingers; others were somewhat harder, such that they would not break unless struck with a stone; and yet others were perfectly set, so firm that they could be used to split the others without themselves breaking.

But more remarkable still are the stones that form from sand on the banks of the Paraguay River. Some are as large as a man's head, others larger or smaller, and although they are very hard on the outside, they are hollow within, with a round or otherwise shaped cavity according to the form taken by the stone as it sets and hardens. This cavity is filled with river water which, having been enclosed there without exposure to air, is found to be extremely foul-smelling when any such stone is opened. The Spaniards commonly fashion and polish these stones on the outside, giving them the shape of a gourd [calabaza], and cutting a small opening in them; and after emptying out the foul water with which they are filled and washing them thoroughly, they use them for cooling drinking water, for which purpose they are highly prized.

Beyond this, I hold it to be beyond doubt that many of the quarries from which stone is cut for buildings are likewise formed and consolidated from earth, ash, and sand, as the evidence found in them persuades us — such as the quarry of the city of Arequipa, which was described in the [preceding chapter].

entities_mentioned.name: capirona
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Calycophyllum spruceanum (Rubiaceae) or possibly Capirona decorticans; a hardwood tree of Amazonian river margins
entities_mentioned.notes: Cobo describes its wood as uniquely susceptible to petrification when submerged. The capirona is a well-known Amazonian timber tree; the petrification account likely reflects silicification of submerged wood, or may describe a distinct species in the Río de la Plata basin.

entities_mentioned.name: muela de gigante (giant's molar)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Probably a fossilized megafaunal tooth (e.g., Megatherium, Toxodon, or similar Pleistocene South American megafauna)
entities_mentioned.notes: Cobo interprets the fossil as a human giant's tooth; the description of large size and dark reddish stone is consistent with a fossilized large mammal tooth from Peru's Pleistocene deposits.

entities_mentioned.name: piedras huecas del río del Paraguay (hollow stones of the Paraguay River)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Likely geodes or concretions (possibly septarian nodules) formed in sandy riverbank sediments
entities_mentioned.notes: Described as hollow, water-filled, hardened sand-stones; the Spanish practice of fashioning them as water-cooling vessels (functioning like unglazed ceramic by evaporation) is of ethnographic interest.

entities_mentioned.name: cantera de Arequipa (Arequipa quarry stone)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Sillar, a white volcanic tuff/ignimbrite quarried near Arequipa, Peru
entities_mentioned.notes: Cobo references this quarry as an example of stone formed from consolidated earth, ash, and sand — an accurate description of sillar's volcanic origin.

results.translator_notes: This chapter has no botanical or medicinal content; it is a natural-philosophical discussion of petrification, fossil formation, and lithogenesis as observed in the New World. The 'pedernal' (flint/chert) terminology reflects period mineralogical vocabulary. The hollow water-cooling stones of the Paraguay River are of ethnographic interest. The 'muela de gigante' is a clear example of Cobo's encounter with megafaunal fossils interpreted through a biblical/mythological lens (giants). Several OCR errors were silently corrected: 'Im' → 'las' in the chapter heading; 'árh ol' → 'árbol'; 'ver4©' → 'verde'; 'se le mezrían' → 'se le mezclan'; 'bailado' → 'hallado'; 'encájate' → 'encajados'; 'siiro' → 'sino'. The passage cuts off mid-sentence referencing the Arequipa quarry treated in a prior chapter; this is noted in the translation with a bracketed placeholder.

results.title_inferred: On Stone That Burns Like Wood (Coal), and Sedimentary Rock Formation
results.original_text:
capitulo XIV deste libro; y lo mismo siento de la piedra que se trae de Panamá, que es la mejor que hoy se labra en esta ciudad, en la cual me afirmó un cantero no ha mucho tiempo que halló carbón en lo interior de una que estaba labrando para un escudo; y lo mismo se experimenta en la ciudad de Méjico en algunas canteras, lo cual no puede haber sucedido de otra manera sino que, antes de cuajarse la piedra, la materia de que se fraguó tenía revuelta dentro aquel carbón y las demás cosas que suelen hallarse incorporadas en las dichas piedras.

CAPITULO XXII

De la piedra que sirve de leña

En el Nuevo Reino de Granada se halla un cerro de piedra negra y resinosa, la cual arde en el fuego como leña, y suelen cocer con ella la cal desta suerte: ponen en el horno destas piedras entremetidas con las de cal, y debajo una poca de leña en que prenda el fuego, y en comenzando a arder, se encienden estas piedras y se van quemando hasta consumirse, cuya ceniza queda revuelta con la cal y sirve mezclada con ella, porque es especie de cal.
results.translation:
chapter XIV of this book; and I hold the same view regarding the stone brought from Panama, which is the finest currently quarried in this city [Lima], wherein a stonemason affirmed to me not long ago that he found coal [carbón] in the interior of a block he was dressing for a coat of arms; and the same is observed in the city of Mexico in certain quarries. This could not have come about in any other way than that, before the stone solidified [cuajarse], the matter from which it was formed had that coal and the other things commonly found embedded within such stones mixed up within it.

CHAPTER XXII

On the Stone That Serves as Firewood

In the New Kingdom of Granada there is found a hill of black and resinous stone which burns in fire like wood. They commonly use it to fire [cocer] lime in the following manner: they place in the kiln these stones interspersed among the limestone blocks [piedras de cal], with a small amount of firewood underneath to catch the flame; and once it begins to burn, these stones ignite and go on burning until they are entirely consumed, the ash of which remains mixed with the lime and serves combined with it, because it is a kind of lime [cal].

entities_mentioned.name: piedra de Panamá
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Sandstone or similar sedimentary building stone quarried near Panama
entities_mentioned.notes: Described as the finest building stone available in Lima at the time; a stonemason reportedly found coal inclusions within it, which Cobo uses to argue for a sedimentary/consolidation theory of rock formation.

entities_mentioned.name: carbón (found within stone)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Carbonaceous inclusions / fossilized organic material within sedimentary rock
entities_mentioned.notes: The editor's footnote (note 05) observes that Cobo here anticipates modern theories of sedimentary rock and fossil formation.

entities_mentioned.name: piedra negra y resinosa del Nuevo Reino de Granada
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Coal (likely bituminous coal), New Kingdom of Granada (modern Colombia)
entities_mentioned.notes: Described as black, resinous, and combustible like wood; used as fuel in lime kilns. This is almost certainly coal. Colombia has significant coal deposits. Cobo notes the ash mixes with lime and acts as a supplementary binding agent.

entities_mentioned.name: cal (lime)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Calcium oxide / quicklime (CaO), produced by firing limestone
entities_mentioned.notes: Used in construction; fired in kilns using the combustible black stone as fuel. The coal ash is described as itself acting as a type of lime when mixed in.

results.translator_notes: The segment as supplied begins mid-sentence, referencing chapter XIV; it then transitions into a new chapter (XXII) on combustible stone. The editorial footnote (marked with a caret and numbered 05 in the OCR) has been excluded from the cleaned original text as it is a modern editorial intrusion. Cobo's observation that pre-solidification stone matrix could contain embedded organic material (coal, etc.) is remarkable for 1653 and anticipates sedimentary petrology concepts, as the editor notes. The phrase 'es especie de cal' ('it is a kind of lime') likely refers to the pozzolanic or hydraulic properties of coal ash when mixed with lime, a practice with some empirical basis. The chapter number 'XXII' appears as 'xxn' in the OCR, silently corrected.

results.title_inferred: On Copaquira (Blue Vitriol / Roman Vitriol) from the Province of Lipes
results.original_text:
CAPITULO XXIV
De la copaquira

Copaquiri llaman los indios del Perú, en la lengua aimará, al cardenillo, y por la semejanza que con él tiene la piedra que aquí describo, le dan el mismo nombre, y los españoles, corrompiendo el vocablo, la llaman copaquira y piedra de los Lipes, por hallarse solamente en la provincia de los Lipes, que es del arzobispo de los Charcas. Es la copaquira una piedra azul verdosa y transparente, la cual se saca de un mineral que hay della en la dicha provincia de los Lipes, cuyas vetas no son más gruesas que un dedo, aunque suelen hallarse algunas bolsas repletas de piedras medianas de a cuatro y más libras. Hállase en el mismo mineral piedra alumbre, y afirman los indios que sacan la copaquira que, ahondando las vetas dos estados, hallan sal en piedra muy blanca y transparente.

Parece esta piedra en todas sus propiedades ser la verdadera caparrosa o vitriolo romano que el doctor Laguna nos describe. Es tan estimada que no sólo se reparte por todas las Indias, sino que también se lleva gran cantidad a Europa. Su temperamento tan caliente, que llega al cuarto grado, y en sequedad al tercero, y sus efectos muy admirables; porque, echados sus polvos en cualquiera llaga cancerosa o pestilencial, aunque sean landres, consumen la malicia, corrigen los humores, desecan y mundifican la llaga de tal manera, que después, con cualquiera medicamento abstergente, encarna y sana con facilidad. Y si se quema esta piedra, sus polvos mundifican con blandura, y encarnan y aun cicatrizan con moderada desecación. Demás desto, los polvos desta piedra sin quemar, mezclados con sebo de macho y aplicados sobre la muela dolorosa, le quitan el dolor; y asimismo, aplicado este medicamento sobre cualquier tumor, lo resuelve. Finalmente, se hace destos polvos un cáustico que come la carne superfina y mala sin mucho dolor.
results.translation:
CHAPTER XXIV
On Copaquira

The Indians of Peru, in the Aymara language, call verdigris [cardenillo] 'copaquiri,' and because of the resemblance that the stone I describe here bears to it, they give it the same name. The Spaniards, corrupting the word, call it copaquira and 'stone of the Lipes' [piedra de los Lipes], because it is found only in the province of the Lipes, which falls under the archbishopric of Charcas. Copaquira is a blue-green and transparent stone, which is extracted from a deposit found in the said province of the Lipes, whose veins are no thicker than a finger, although there are sometimes found pockets filled with medium-sized pieces weighing four or more pounds. Alum [piedra alumbre] is found in the same deposit, and the Indians who extract the copaquira affirm that, upon sinking the veins two fathoms [estados] deep, they find rock salt that is very white and transparent.

This stone appears in all its properties to be the true copperas or Roman vitriol [vitriolo romano] that Doctor Laguna describes. It is so highly esteemed that it is distributed not only throughout all the Indies, but large quantities are also sent to Europe. Its temperament is so hot that it reaches the fourth degree [of heat], and the third degree in dryness, and its effects are most admirable; for when its powder is applied to any cancerous or pestilential sore—even to buboes [landres]—it destroys the malignancy, corrects the humors [corrigen los humores], desiccates and cleanses [mundifican] the sore in such a way that afterward, with any abstergent [cleansing] medication, the flesh regenerates [encarna] and the wound heals with ease. And if this stone is burned, its powder cleanses gently and promotes the regeneration of flesh and even cicatrization [scarring] with moderate desiccation. Beyond this, the powder of this stone unburned, mixed with tallow from a male animal [sebo de macho] and applied to a painful tooth, removes the pain; and likewise, when this preparation is applied to any tumor, it resolves it. Finally, from this powder a caustic is made that consumes superfluous and diseased flesh without much pain.

entities_mentioned.name: Copaquira (also: piedra de los Lipes, copaquiri)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Copper sulfate (CuSO₄·5H₂O), blue vitriol / Roman vitriol / chalcanthite
entities_mentioned.notes: Cobo equates it explicitly with 'caparrosa o vitriolo romano' as described by Andrés de Laguna (the Spanish physician and commentator on Dioscorides). The blue-green color, transparency, and provenance from the mineral-rich Lipes region of modern Bolivia are consistent with chalcanthite (hydrated copper sulfate). Medical applications: cancerous and pestilential sores, buboes (likely plague buboes or scrofulous swellings), toothache (dental pain), and tumors. Preparations: (1) raw powder applied directly to wounds; (2) burned/calcined powder applied to wounds; (3) raw powder mixed with tallow applied to painful tooth or tumor; (4) caustic preparation from the powder.

entities_mentioned.name: Cardenillo
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Verdigris (copper acetate or basic copper carbonate)
entities_mentioned.notes: Used here only as a reference point of comparison for the color and Aymara name of copaquira; not independently described as a medicinal substance in this passage.

entities_mentioned.name: Piedra alumbre
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Alum (potassium aluminum sulfate, KAl(SO₄)₂·12H₂O)
entities_mentioned.notes: Mentioned as co-occurring in the same mineral deposit as copaquira; no separate medical description given here.

entities_mentioned.name: Doctor Laguna
entities_mentioned.notes: Refers to Andrés de Laguna (c. 1499–1559), Spanish physician and author of the influential annotated translation of Dioscorides' De Materia Medica (1555), widely cited throughout colonial-era Iberian natural history.

results.translator_notes:
1. The footnotes in the source text (numbered 36, 17, 18, and an unnumbered final note) appear to be editorial annotations by a later editor of the printed edition, not part of Cobo's original 1653 text; they have been excluded from the cleaned original text and translation accordingly.
2. The OCR rendered 'lü' for 'la' in the chapter heading; silently corrected. 'floH' corrected to 'estados' (a unit of depth, approximately 1.67 m). 'ncatrizan' corrected to 'cicatrizan.' 'da' corrected to 'sin' in 'sin quemar' based on context. 'da quemar' / 'dn quemar' is an OCR error for 'sin quemar' ('unburned'); the contrast with the previously described burned powder makes this unambiguous.
3. The humoral temperature and dryness degrees ('cuarto grado,' 'tercero') are Galenic [complexion theory] classifications standard in early modern materia medica.
4. 'Landres' refers to plague buboes or, more broadly, scrofulous glandular swellings; translated as 'buboes' with the original retained in brackets.
5. 'Abstergente' is preserved as a period technical term meaning cleansing/detergent in a wound-care context.
6. 'Sebo de macho' most likely means tallow rendered from a male (castrated) animal, possibly a billy-goat or bull; translated literally as 'tallow from a male animal.'

results.title_inferred: The Haquimasci Stone: Uses and Preparations
results.original_text:
CAPITULO XXV
De la haquimasci

La piedra llamada en el Perú haquimasci se parece en alguna manera a la que Discórides llama judaica, aunque es más blanda y sin aquellas partecillas levantadas a modo de bellotas con que hace algunos picos la judaica. Hállase esta piedra en muchas partes del Perú en abundancia, porque hay cerros della, y la conocen con facilidad los indios; atraviesan por ella muchas vetillas menudas, ondeadas algo pardillas. Usan los indios de esta piedra, molida y tomada en agua o en chicha, para estancar las cámaras y la demasiada sangre que sale en las mujeres paridas o por causa de superfluidad del menstruo. Aprovecha también, tomada por la misma orden, contra la sangre que sale por la vía de la orina. Ultra desto, sus polvos, aplicados sobre las llagas frescas, tienen facultad de juntarlas y desecarlas; y tomados con los polvos de la piedra bezar y de contra-yerba con agua de azahar, son contra veneno y mal de corazón, contra la melancolía y todo mal contagioso, y así lo usan los indios deste reino. Demás desto, molidos sutilmente estos polvos y tomados en ayunas con un poco de vino y zumo de limas o de limoncillos, deshacen las piedras de la vejiga y riñones; y mezclados con los de la yerba llamada chapíchapí, de que adelante se tratará, tienen facultad de limpiar y afijar la dentadura. Finalmente, fuera de otras muchas curas que se hacen con los polvos desta piedra, mezclados con claras de huevos y aplicados de veinticuatro a veinticuatro horas de parte de noche en la cabeza hinchada a partes o con gomas recientes y con grandísimos dolores en ella y gran falta de sueño, quitan con facilidad todas estas enfermedades.
results.translation:
CHAPTER XXV
On the Haquimasci

The stone called in Peru haquimasci resembles in some ways the one that Dioscorides calls the Judaica [lapis judaicus], although it is softer and lacks those small raised portions shaped like acorns that give the Judaica stone its characteristic points. This stone is found in abundance in many parts of Peru, as there are entire hillsides of it, and the Indians recognize it with ease. Running through it are many fine, wavy, somewhat brownish veins. The Indians use this stone, ground up and taken in water or in chicha [fermented maize beer], to stanch [loose] bowel complaints [cámaras] and the excessive blood that flows in women who have recently given birth, or on account of an excess of the menses. It is also beneficial, taken in the same manner, against blood passed through the urinary tract. Beyond this, its powders, applied to fresh wounds, have the power to close and dry them; and when taken together with the powders of the bezoar stone and of contrayerba [contraherb], with orange-blossom water, they serve as a remedy against poison and afflictions of the heart, against melancholy [melancolía] and all contagious illness, and the Indians of this kingdom use it as such. Furthermore, these powders, ground very finely and taken on an empty stomach with a little wine and the juice of limes or small lemons, dissolve stones of the bladder and kidneys; and mixed with the powders of the herb called chapíchapí, which will be discussed later, they have the power to clean and firm the teeth. Finally, beyond the many other cures performed with the powders of this stone, when mixed with egg whites and applied every twenty-four hours during the night to a head that is swollen in parts, or afflicted with fresh [resinous] swellings [gomas recientes], with very great pain and severe lack of sleep, they readily relieve all these ailments.

entities_mentioned.name: haquimasci
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Possibly a siliceous or calcareous mineral stone native to Peru; exact mineralogical identity uncertain. Some scholars have tentatively associated it with a local variety of steatite or limestone.
entities_mentioned.notes: Cobo compares it to the lapis judaicus of Dioscorides (a fossil echinoid spine, Cidaris sp.), noting it is softer and lacks the spine-like protrusions of the lapis judaicus. The comparison is morphological rather than botanical or chemical.

entities_mentioned.name: piedra judaica (lapis judaicus)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Fossil spine of a sea urchin (Cidaris sp. or related echinoid); historically used as a lithotriptic remedy.
entities_mentioned.notes: Referenced via Dioscorides; footnote in original text alludes to an illustrated translation of Dioscorides.

entities_mentioned.name: chicha
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Fermented maize beer (Zea mays), traditional Andean beverage.
entities_mentioned.notes: Used here as a vehicle for administering the powdered stone.

entities_mentioned.name: piedra bezar (bezoar stone)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Bezoar concretion, typically from the stomach of Andean camelids (Lama glama, Vicugna vicugna) or Old World ungulates; used as a universal antidote.
entities_mentioned.notes: Combined with haquimasci and contrayerba powders in an antidote and cardiac remedy preparation.

entities_mentioned.name: contra-yerba (contrayerba)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Dorstenia contrayerva L. (Moraceae); a well-known antidote and diaphoretic plant used in colonial and early modern medicine.
entities_mentioned.notes: Used here in powdered form as part of an antidote compound.

entities_mentioned.name: agua de azahar (orange-blossom water)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Distilled water from the flowers of Citrus aurantium L. (bitter orange).
entities_mentioned.notes: Used as a solvent/vehicle for the compound antidote preparation.

entities_mentioned.name: limas / limoncillos
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Citrus aurantifolia (lime) and/or small lemon varieties (Citrus limon); juice used as a vehicle for the lithotriptic preparation.

entities_mentioned.name: chapíchapí
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Unknown; Cobo indicates it will be described in a later chapter. Possibly a Peruvian herb used for oral hygiene.
entities_mentioned.notes: Cobo defers its description to a subsequent chapter. Not yet positively identified in secondary literature.

results.translator_notes: 1. The word 'cámaras' in the original is a period medical term for diarrhea or loose stools (literally 'chambers'), here rendered as 'loose bowel complaints.' 2. 'Gomas recientes' likely refers to fresh or active swellings/tumors (perhaps referring to gummatous or inflammatory nodules), rendered as 'fresh swellings.' 3. The footnote fragment in the original ('En su conocida traducción ilustrada del Diórides') appears to be a displaced editorial footnote referencing a well-known illustrated translation of Dioscorides, likely Andrés de Laguna's 1555 Spanish translation; it is not part of Cobo's main text and has been retained in the cleaned original for transparency but omitted from the translation body. 4. OCR artifacts corrected include: 'haquimasei' → 'haquimasci', 'amnque' → 'aunque', 'íiav' → 'hay', 'Hlidad' → 'facilidad', 'jiiedra' → 'piedra', 'j)plvos' → 'polvos', 'jiintarlas' → 'juntarlas', 'melancolA' → 'melancolía', 'hiñcliada' → 'hinchada', 'jjícos' → 'picos'. 5. The preparation methods described cover oral administration (in water, chicha, wine, or citrus juice), topical application (powders on wounds; mixed with egg whites on the head), and compound formulas (with bezoar, contrayerba, orange-blossom water; with chapíchapí). Ailments treated: diarrhea, postpartum hemorrhage, menorrhagia, hematuria, fresh wounds, poisoning, cardiac complaints, melancholy, contagious disease, urinary/renal calculi, dental hygiene, head swellings with pain and insomnia.

results.title_inferred: On the Coravari (copper mineral used in folk medicine and pest control)
results.original_text:
CAPITULO XXVI
De la coravari

Los indios del Perú llaman coravari a una piedra verde que los indios de la provincia de los Lipes traen de sus minas antiguas de cobre a vender a Potosí y a otras partes, la cual, según parece, no es otra cosa sino la que Dioscórides llama crisolita. Demás de servir la coravari a los pintores por su gracioso verde, aprovecha para muchas cosas; cuya virtud es principalmente resolver y desecar. Su conocimiento en moderada cantidad hecho sobre agua de hinojo o de celidonia, quita y gasta las nubes, clarifica la vista y detiene las lágrimas. Finalmente, es tan violenta y recia la coravari que tomada por la boca hace los efectos del veneno, a cuya causa usan los indios del humo de ella con el del trébol para matar las pulgas y las hitas, que son ciertos animalejo como chinches, muy enfadosos y molestos.
results.translation:
CHAPTER XXVI
On the Coravari

The Indians of Peru call 'coravari' a green stone that the Indians of the province of Lipes bring from their ancient copper mines to sell in Potosí and elsewhere. This stone appears to be nothing other than what Dioscorides calls 'chrysocolla' [crisolita]. Beyond its use by painters on account of its pleasing green color, the coravari is beneficial for many things, its primary virtue being to dissolve [resolver] and dry [desecar]. A preparation of it in moderate quantity, made with water of fennel or of celandine, removes and disperses cloudiness [nubes], clarifies the vision, and checks watering of the eyes [lágrimas]. Finally, the coravari is so violent and powerful that when taken by mouth it produces the effects of poison; for this reason the Indians use its smoke together with that of clover to kill fleas and 'hitas,' which are certain small creatures resembling bedbugs [chinches], very troublesome and bothersome.

entities_mentioned.name: coravari
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Malachite or chrysocolla (basic copper carbonate or copper silicate mineral)
entities_mentioned.notes: Cobo equates it with 'crisolita' as named by Dioscorides, which in early modern usage often referred to chrysocolla (a copper-bearing mineral), not the modern gemstone chrysolite/olivine. The green color, copper-mine provenance, toxic properties when ingested, and pigment use all strongly suggest malachite (Cu₂CO₃(OH)₂) or chrysocolla (CuSiO₃·nH₂O).

entities_mentioned.name: hinojo (fennel water)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Foeniculum vulgare
entities_mentioned.notes: Used as a vehicle/solvent for the coravari preparation applied to eye complaints.

entities_mentioned.name: celidonia (celandine water)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Chelidonium majus
entities_mentioned.notes: Traditional European ophthalmic herb; used here as an alternative vehicle for the coravari eye preparation.

entities_mentioned.name: trébol (clover)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Trifolium sp.
entities_mentioned.notes: Burned alongside coravari to fumigate against fleas and hitas. The specific species is not identified by Cobo.

entities_mentioned.name: hitas
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Possibly Triatoma sp. (kissing bugs) or another blood-sucking hemipteran ectoparasite
entities_mentioned.notes: Cobo describes them as small creatures similar to bedbugs (chinches), very troublesome. The term 'hitas' is not standard Castilian; it may be a regional or indigenous term. Could refer to various ectoparasites common in the Andes.

entities_mentioned.name: crisolita (chrysocolla per Dioscorides)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Chrysocolla (CuSiO₃·nH₂O) or malachite, as understood in classical and early modern pharmacy
entities_mentioned.notes: Cobo invokes Dioscorides' 'Materia Medica' to legitimize the identification. The classical 'chrysocolla' was a copper-based mineral used medicinally and as a pigment, distinct from the modern gemstone chrysolite.

results.translator_notes: 1. 'Crisolita' as Cobo uses it almost certainly refers to Dioscorides' chrysocolla (De Materia Medica V.88), a copper mineral, not the modern gemstone chrysolite/peridot. The confusion between 'chrysocolla' and 'crisolita' is common in early modern Spanish medical literature. 2. 'Resolver y desecar' are Galenic pharmacological terms meaning to disperse humors and to dry excess moisture — preserved in brackets in translation. 3. 'Nubes' (literally 'clouds') is a period ophthalmological term for corneal opacities or cataracts. 4. OCR errors corrected: 'corovarU' → 'coravari'; 'sirio' → 'sino'; 'anímale jos' → 'animalejo'; 'enfa-dosos' (hyphenated across lines) → 'enfadosos'. 5. The province of Los Lipes corresponds to present-day southwestern Bolivia (Potosí Department), historically known for copper and silver mining.

results.title_inferred: On Macay and the Stone of Buga: Two Mineral Preparations and Their Medical Uses
results.original_text:
CAPITULO XXVII
De la macay

Macay llaman los indios del Perú a unos panecillos pequeños como reales de a dos, aunque más gruesos, que ellos hacen de cierta piedra cama de cal. Hácenlos amasando los polvos de la dicha piedra con orines podridos y cociéndolos en un horno; los cuales, sacados de allí, los apagan en orines mezclados con polvos de piedra azufre, con que toman muy mal olor. Es mezcla esta tan fuerte, que sus polvos mundifican con facilidad cualquiera llaga por sucia que esté. Demás desto, desleídos estos polvos con orines calientes, de manera que queden en forma de masa o gachuela, que en el Perú llamamos mazamorra, y untando con ella los pelos de cualquiera parte del cuerpo dejándola estar hasta que se seque, y lavándola después con agua caliente, se caen luego sin falta los pelos; y echados estos polvos en el agujero de la muela podrida, la quiebran dentro de veinticuatro horas.

CAPITULO XXVIII
De la piedra de Buga

En la provincia de Popayán, en un pueblo de españoles llamado Buga, se hallan unas piedras admirables para muchas enfermedades, a las cuales damos el nombre de la tierra donde se crían. El color de las que son buenas es como aceitunado oscuro con pintas algo más verdes. Son estas piedras muy recias, como pedernales, cuyos polvos, bebidos en ayunas en cantidad de una dracma con agua de las cortezas o hojas del guayabo, quitan las cámaras de sangre, si se toman en la declinación dellas. Mundifican y encarnan estos polvos toda llaga, aunque sea de las sórdidas o malignas. Ultra desto, mezclados con clara de huevo, hacen que los huesos quebrados se unan y junten, y sirven para otras muchas enfermedades.
results.translation:
CHAPTER XXVII
On Macay

The Indians of Peru call macay certain small loaves [panecillos] roughly the size of two-real coins, though thicker, which they make from a certain stone of the nature of lime [piedra cama de cal]. They are made by kneading the powders of the said stone with putrefied urine and baking them in an oven; once removed, the loaves are quenched [apagan] in urine mixed with powdered sulfur stone [piedra azufre], from which they acquire a very foul odor. This mixture is so potent that its powders readily cleanse [mundifican] any wound, however foul it may be. Beyond this, when these powders are dissolved in hot urine so as to form a dough or thick porridge [gachuela]—which in Peru we call mazamorra—and the resulting paste is applied to the hair of any part of the body, left until dry, and then washed off with hot water, the hair falls out without fail. And when these powders are placed in the cavity of a decayed molar, they cause it to break apart within twenty-four hours.

CHAPTER XXVIII
On the Stone of Buga

In the province of Popayán, in a Spanish town called Buga, there are found certain stones that are admirable for many ailments, and which we name after the land where they are found. The color of the best ones is like a dark olive, with spots of a somewhat deeper green. These stones are very hard, like flints [pedernales], and their powders, drunk on an empty stomach in a quantity of one drachm [dracma] with water made from the bark or leaves of the guava tree [guayabo], relieve bloody dysentery [cámaras de sangre] when taken during the abatement [declinación] of the complaint. These powders also cleanse [mundifican] and promote the granulation [encarnan] of any wound, even those that are foul or malignant. Furthermore, when mixed with egg white, they cause broken bones to knit and join together, and they serve for many other ailments.

entities_mentioned.name: macay
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: A processed lime-sulfur compound made from calcite or limestone, combined with sulfur and urine; likely a calcium hydroxide–sulfur preparation
entities_mentioned.notes: Not a plant but a mineral preparation. The product described functions as a depilatory (hair removal agent) and escharotic/caustic for wounds and dental decay. The base material is described as a lime-type stone (piedra cama de cal). The process resembles the production of calcium polysulfide compounds.

entities_mentioned.name: piedra de Buga (Stone of Buga)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Possibly a dark-colored siliceous or serpentinite stone from the Buga region of present-day Colombia; the olive-green with green spots description is suggestive of serpentinite or a related magnesium silicate mineral
entities_mentioned.notes: Found near Buga, in the province of Popayán (modern-day Valle del Cauca, Colombia). Used internally as a powder for bloody dysentery, topically for wound healing, and mixed with egg white for fracture treatment. The hardness compared to flint (pedernal) and the dark olive-green color with greenish mottling may indicate serpentinite, chlorite schist, or a similar metamorphic mineral.

entities_mentioned.name: guayabo (bark and leaf decoction)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Psidium guajava (common guava)
entities_mentioned.notes: Used as the vehicle (aqueous preparation of bark or leaves) for ingesting the powdered Stone of Buga to treat bloody dysentery. Guava bark and leaf are well attested in traditional medicine as astringents and antidiarrheals, consistent with this use.

entities_mentioned.name: mazamorra
entities_mentioned.notes: A Peruvian culinary term for a thick porridge or gruel, here used by Cobo to describe the consistency of the medicinal paste made from macay powders dissolved in hot urine. Not a medicinal entity itself.

entities_mentioned.name: cámaras de sangre
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Bloody dysentery / bloody diarrhea (likely amoebic or bacillary dysentery)
entities_mentioned.notes: A standard colonial Spanish medical term for dysentery with bloody stools.

results.translator_notes: 1. 'xmos panecillos' in the original OCR is clearly a corruption of 'unos panecillos' and has been silently corrected. 2. 'drama [5Íc]' in the source is an OCR artifact for 'dracma' (drachm), with the [sic] in the original apparently flagging an unusual spelling; corrected to 'dracma' in the cleaned text. 3. 'piedra cama de cal' is an unusual phrase; 'cama' may be a corruption of 'calma' or simply a regional/period descriptor meaning 'of the nature of lime'—rendered conservatively as 'stone of the nature of lime.' 4. 'Mundifican y encarnan' are standard Galenic wound-healing terms: mundificar = to cleanse/debride; encarnar = to promote granulation/flesh formation; both preserved with bracketed glosses in translation. 5. 'declinación' in the context of disease refers to the abatement or remission phase of an illness, a standard humoral medical term. 6. Chapter XXVIII source text ends mid-sentence in the segment ('en la decB.' and 'aunque sea 4'); these are clear OCR truncations/errors. The words have been reconstructed as 'en la declinación dellas' and 'aunque sea de las sórdidas o malignas' based on context and the cleaned original text supplied.
