results.translator_notes:
1. MEDICINAL USE EXTRACTED: Plant: batata / camote (Ipomoea batatas). Ailment: infestation by niguas (chigoe fleas, Tunga penetrans). Preparation: fresh green leaf of the batata or camote, pounded (majada) and mixed with unsalted lard (unto sin sal). Application: applied topically over the site of the nigua. Effect claimed: kills the parasite and relieves pain and irritation. 
2. The OCR rendered several words poorly (e.g., 'tamLién' for 'también', 'pefpieña' for 'pequeña', 'nui-clio' for 'mucho', 'íjue' for 'que', 'xma' for 'una', 'las' for 'losa', 'dof?' for 'dos', 'veiu-te' for 'veinte', 'estima' partially corrupted, 'apkH' for 'apichu')—all silently corrected in the cleaned original text as the intended words are unambiguous from context. 
3. 'apichu' (Quechua) and 'tuctuca' (Aymara) are the indigenous Andean names Cobo records; modern Quechua forms include 'apichu' or 'kumara'. 
4. 'camuesa' is a specific variety of apple (possibly Malus domestica 'Camuesa'), used here as a simile for the finest of a category. 
5. 'unto sin sal' (unsalted lard/fat) was a common pharmaceutical vehicle in 17th-century Iberian and colonial medicine. 
6. The passage 'Sácanse de una mata dos y catorce destas raíces' is somewhat ambiguous—it likely means 'between two and fourteen roots,' rendered accordingly.

results.title_inferred: The Liren Root (Chapter IX)
results.original_text:
CAPITULO IX
Del liren

Llámanse lirenes, en la lengua de la isla Española, unas raíces del tamaño de dátiles, y algo mayores o como perillas cermeñas, que en lo exterior son pardas y la cáscara vellosa y delgada, que luego que se cuecen se quita fácilmente, y por de dentro son blancas, las cuales, cocidas, son de buen sabor. Están estas raicillas asidas de unas vaguetas delgadas como las batatas; la planta es pequeña y extiende sus ramas sobre la tierra como la de la batata, a la cual es tan parecida, que parece particular especie della.
results.translation:
CHAPTER IX
Of the Liren

In the language of the island of Hispaniola, the name 'lirenes' is given to certain roots the size of dates, and somewhat larger, or like small bergamot pears [perillas cermeñas], which on the outside are brownish [pardas], with a soft, thin, hairy skin [cáscara vellosa y delgada] that comes off easily once they are cooked; and on the inside they are white, and when cooked they are of good flavor. These little roots are attached to thin runners [vaguetas delgadas] like those of the sweet potato [batatas]; the plant is small and spreads its branches over the ground like the sweet potato plant, to which it is so similar that it appears to be a particular variety [especie] of it.

entities_mentioned.name: liren
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Calathea allouia (Guinea arrowroot / llerén)
entities_mentioned.notes: A tuberous root crop native to the Caribbean and South America, known in modern Spanish as 'llerén' or 'topinambur caribeño.' The Taíno name from Hispaniola is preserved here. Cobo's description of the thin runners, the hairy brownish skin, and the white interior that cooks well all match Calathea allouia closely.

entities_mentioned.name: batata
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Ipomoea batatas (sweet potato)
entities_mentioned.notes: Used by Cobo as a morphological reference point for the growth habit and root attachment of the liren plant.

results.translator_notes: The source text is heavily corrupted by OCR errors (e.g., 'k isla Española,' 'tam&', 'rillas cermeñas,' 'deka4,' 'Idanca*»,' 'l>tteii sato,' 'vfi guetas,' 'hatatm*,' 'sib,' '<inc pt- rece'). All corrections are treated as unambiguous given context and period usage. No medical application or preparation method is described in this segment; the chapter is purely descriptive/botanical. The phrase 'perillas cermeñas' refers to a small pear variety (bergamot or warden pear) used as a size/shape comparison.

results.title_inferred: The Achira Plant: Description and Medical Use
results.original_text:
CAPITULO X
De la achira

En la lengua quichua o general del Perú se llama achira una planta cuya raíz sirve de mantenimiento a los indios, la cual crece cuatro codos en alto; echa unas varas tan gruesas como un dedo de la mano, derechas, sin que de ellas brote rama, algo parecidas a las cañas, por tener a trechos nudos y estar envueltas en la hoja antes que ella se extienda y aparte del vástago, como lo están las cañas; son macizas y correosas. La hoja es tan grande, que tiene más de dos palmos de largo, y por donde más, uno de ancho, tiesa, lisa y toda llena de venas o rayas algo relevadas, que del lomo salen a las orillas. Echa en el remate unas florecitas coloradas, agradables a la vista, mas de ningún olor, y junto a ellas unas cabezuelas o capullos, que, cuando verdes, se parecen al madroño, y en secándose, tienen dentro la semilla, que son unos granos o bolillas muy redondas, negras, duras, y lisas, del tamaño de garbanzos, de las cuales se suelen hacer rosarios. La raíz es larga y blanca como nabo, mas no tan tierna, aunque en lo demás se parece a él. Aplícase esta raíz para el mal de la del monte.
results.translation:
CHAPTER X
On the Achira

In the Quechua language, the general tongue of Peru, a plant is called achira whose root serves as sustenance for the Indians. It grows four cubits [roughly six feet] in height; it sends up stalks as thick as a finger of the hand, straight, without any branches sprouting from them, somewhat resembling canes [reeds], as they have nodes at intervals and are sheathed within the leaf before it spreads and separates from the stalk, as canes are; they are solid and tough. The leaf is so large that it measures more than two spans [palmos] in length and, at its widest, one span across; it is stiff, smooth, and entirely covered with veins or somewhat raised ridges running from the midrib out to the edges. At the top it produces small red flowers, pleasant to the eye but entirely without scent, and alongside them small heads or buds which, when green, resemble the strawberry tree [madroño, Arbutus unedo], and upon drying, contain within them seeds consisting of very round, black, hard, and smooth grains, the size of chickpeas, from which rosaries are commonly made. The root is long and white like a turnip, though not as tender, though in other respects it resembles one. This root is applied [medicinally] for the illness known as 'mal de la del monte' [the disease of the forest / a specific ailment of the wilderness, possibly a dermatological or parasitic condition].

entities_mentioned.name: achira
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Canna indica (or closely related Canna edulis / Canna discolor)
entities_mentioned.notes: Achira (Quechua) is a well-attested common name for Canna indica and Canna edulis in the Andean region. The description matches closely: the tall, unbranched, reed-like stems with sheathing leaves, red flowers, hard round black seeds used as rosary beads, and edible starchy rhizome. Canna edulis (achira) is specifically cultivated for its edible rhizome.

entities_mentioned.name: madroño
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Arbutus unedo (strawberry tree)
entities_mentioned.notes: Cobo uses madroño as a comparative reference for the appearance of the green seed capsules. He may be referring to the European Arbutus unedo familiar to Spanish readers, or possibly a New World analogue used loosely for comparison.

results.translator_notes: 1. 'Mal de la del monte' is an ambiguous phrase; literally 'the sickness of the one of the forest/wilderness.' It may refer to a specific folk-recognized condition such as leishmaniasis (uta), a tropical ulcer, or another forest-associated disease. The phrasing is elliptical and may reflect an OCR drop of a word (e.g., 'mal de la [llaga] del monte' — 'sickness of the forest sore/ulcer'). Marked as a medical use but identification of the condition is uncertain. 2. The phrase 'macizas y correosas' (rendered 'solid and tough') describes the stalks' texture. 3. 'Palmo' is a Spanish unit of approximately 8–9 cm; 'codo' (cubit) approximately 45–50 cm, so four cubits is roughly 1.8–2 m, consistent with Canna. 4. OCR artifact 'ñudos' silently corrected to 'nudos'; 'V correosas' corrected to 'y correosas'; 'm parece' corrected to 'se parece.'

results.title_inferred: The Racacha plant and its description
results.original_text:
CAPITULO XI
De la racacha

En la lengua quichua deste reino se llama racacha una planta cuya raíz es comestible y muy parecida a la achira; la mata es de tres palmos de alto, muy copada y poblada de hojas, las cuales son de un verde oscuro y todas inmediatamente de la raíz. El pezón dellas o tallo es de un codo de largo, tan grueso como un mediano junco, liso y con unas pequeñas rayas o venas coloradas a lo largo; es todo hueco como un cañón, y en su remate nacen tres hojas, cada una de tres o cuatro puntas hendidas más largas que las de las ortigas. Echa un tallo de cuatro codos de alto, tan grueso como un dedo de la mano, redondo, liso, correoso y de color verde oscuro que tira a morado, en cuya cumbre se produce la semilla a la manera que el perejil y culantro, la cual es muy parecida a la del hinojo.
results.translation:
CHAPTER XI
Of the Racacha

In the Quechua language of this kingdom, the name racacha is given to a plant whose root is edible and very similar to the achira [Canna edulis]. The bush stands three hand-spans [palmos] tall, very full-crowned and densely leafed, the leaves being dark green and all springing directly from the root. Their petiole or stalk is one cubit [codo] in length, as thick as a medium-sized rush [junco], smooth, and marked along its length with small reddish streaks or veins; it is entirely hollow like a tube, and at its tip grow three leaves, each with three or four deeply cut points longer than those of nettles. The plant sends up a flowering stalk four cubits tall, as thick as a finger of the hand, round, smooth, tough [correoso], and dark green shading toward purple, at whose summit the seed is produced in the manner of parsley and coriander, the seed itself being very similar to that of fennel.

entities_mentioned.name: racacha
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Arracacia xanthorrhiza (white carrot / arracacha)
entities_mentioned.notes: An Andean root vegetable in the family Apiaceae. Cobo's comparison of the seed to fennel, parsley, and coriander is consistent with its umbelliferous family. The Quechua name 'racacha' survives in modern Andean Spanish.

entities_mentioned.name: achira
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Canna edulis (edible canna)
entities_mentioned.notes: Cobo compares the racacha root to the achira root for reference to the reader. Achira is a well-known Andean starchy root crop.

entities_mentioned.name: perejil
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Petroselinum crispum (parsley)
entities_mentioned.notes: Used as a comparative reference for the seed umbel structure.

entities_mentioned.name: culantro
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Coriandrum sativum (coriander/cilantro)
entities_mentioned.notes: Used as a comparative reference for the seed umbel structure. Note: in colonial Latin American usage 'culantro' typically refers to Coriandrum sativum, not Eryngium foetidum.

entities_mentioned.name: hinojo
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Foeniculum vulgare (fennel)
entities_mentioned.notes: Cobo compares the racacha seed to fennel seed, consistent with both being Apiaceae.

results.translator_notes: Several OCR corruptions were silently corrected: 'achUm' → 'achira'; 'Biiiy' → 'muy'; 'iuacü' → 'junco'; 'biseco' → 'hueco'; 'j)e-rejil' → 'perejil'. The chapter contains botanical description only and no explicit medicinal use, preparation method, or ailment treated; accordingly no pharmacological extraction was performed. The measurement terms 'palmo' (hand-span, approx. 20 cm) and 'codo' (cubit, approx. 42 cm) are preserved in translation with bracketed originals.

results.title_inferred: On the Maní (Peanut)
results.original_text:
CAPITULO XII
Del maní

El maní es una raíz muy diferente de todas las demás de Indias; la mata és baja y muy aparrada con la tierra; produce muchos tallos y hojas, de manera que se viene a hacer muy cerrada. Son los tallos de dos tercias de largo, y son más los que se extienden por el suelo que los que suben hacia arriba; son redondos y tan gruesos como juncos, de color rojo, pero no lisos, sino algo vellosos y con mucha hoja, la cual, en talle y grandor, se parece a la del lentisco, salvo que es más delgada y un poco mayor y de un verde oscuro. La fruta desta mata son unas raicitas cada una del tamaño del dedo meñique, algo más corta, con una cascarilla blanquecina muy arrugada y tan delgada y sutil, que apretada ligeramente entre los dedos se quiebra; dentro de ella tiene cada raíz dos o tres pepitas muy parecidas en todo a los piñones, cubiertas de un hollejico rojo muy sutil, como el de la almendra, que, quitado, queda la pepita muy blanca como piñón mondado, la cual se divide en dos partes como la haba. Cómese esta raíz por fruta regalada y de muy buen sabor, cocida y tostada; pero, comida cruda, causa dolores de cabeza, vagidos y jaqueca. Hácense della muy buenos turrones, confitura y otros regalos. El modo como esta planta produce su fruto es asido a unas venillas delgadas o barbas como la batata, y para desenterrarlo, se arranca la mata, en la cual salen asidas muchas destas raicillas de maní, aunque muchas más quedan soterradas, las cuales se sacan cavando toda la tierra alrededor.

Las zorras son muy golosas desta fruta, la cual se comen escarbando la tierra y desenterrándola. La leche del maní, que se saca como la de las almendras, sirve para almendradas, y, mezclada con la que se saca de las pepitas de melón o calabaza, agrava blandamente el cerebro y causa sueño en los faltos de él; y si a la almendrada, en lugar de azúcar se le echa miel de abejas, es contra la ictericia y purgazón de riñones. Llámase maní esta raíz en la lengua de la isla Española; los mexicanos le llaman cacaguate, y los indios peruanos, inchic, en la lengua quichua, y ckocopa, en la aimará.
results.translation:
CHAPTER XII
On the Maní

The maní is a root very different from all others in the Indies; the plant is low-growing and spreads close to the ground, producing many stems and leaves, so that it becomes very dense. The stems are two-thirds of a vara [roughly 18 inches] in length, and more of them extend along the ground than rise upward; they are round and as thick as rushes, reddish in color, not smooth but somewhat hairy, and well-leafed, the leaves resembling in shape and size those of the mastic tree [lentisco, Pistacia lentiscus], save that they are thinner, slightly larger, and of a dark green. The fruit of this plant consists of small root-pods, each one about the size of the little finger, somewhat shorter, with a whitish, very wrinkled shell so thin and fine that if lightly pressed between the fingers it breaks; inside each pod there are two or three seeds [pepitas] very similar in every respect to pine nuts [piñones], covered with a very fine reddish skin [hollejico] like that of the almond, which when removed leaves the seed very white, like a peeled pine nut, and which divides into two parts like a bean. This root is eaten as a choice and very flavorful fruit, both boiled and roasted; but eaten raw it causes headaches, dizziness, and migraine. Very fine nougats [turrones], confections, and other delicacies are made from it. The manner in which this plant bears its fruit is by attachment to thin little veins or filaments like those of the sweet potato [batata], and in order to dig it up one uproots the plant, from which many of these small maní roots come up attached, though many more remain buried underground and must be recovered by digging all the surrounding earth.

Foxes are very fond of this fruit, which they eat by scratching the earth and digging it up. The milk of the maní, extracted in the manner of almond milk, serves for almond drinks [almendradas], and when mixed with the milk extracted from melon or squash seeds, it gently burdens [agrava] the brain and induces sleep in those who lack it; and if to this almond drink honey from bees is added in place of sugar, it is [efficacious] against jaundice and purging of the kidneys [purgazón de riñones]. This root is called maní in the language of the island of Hispaniola; the Mexicans call it cacaguate [cacahuate]; and the Peruvian Indians call it inchic in the Quechua tongue, and ckocopa in Aymara.

entities_mentioned.name: maní / cacaguate / inchic / ckocopa
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Arachis hypogaea (peanut / groundnut)
entities_mentioned.notes: Cobo provides multiple indigenous names: maní (Taíno/Hispaniola), cacaguate (Nahuatl, modern cacahuate), inchic (Quechua), ckocopa (Aymara). The botanical description—underground pods, two-lobed seeds, spreading hairy stems—is consistent with Arachis hypogaea. OCR had 'j^epitas' (corrected to 'pepitas'), 'pifiones' (corrected to 'piñones'), 'hollé jico' (corrected to 'hollejico'), 'vagui¬dos' (corrected to 'vagidos'), and 'cav'ando' (corrected to 'cavando').

entities_mentioned.name: lentisco
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Pistacia lentiscus (mastic tree)
entities_mentioned.notes: Used as a comparator for leaf shape and size; a plant familiar to Cobo's Spanish readership.

entities_mentioned.name: batata
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Ipomoea batatas (sweet potato)
entities_mentioned.notes: Mentioned only by analogy, to describe the thread-like filaments attaching the pods to the root system.

results.translator_notes: Medical/pharmacological extractions: (1) Raw maní — causes headaches, dizziness, and migraine; not recommended for consumption in this state. (2) Maní milk mixed with melon or squash seed milk — preparation: expressed like almond milk, combined with expressed melon/squash seed liquid; indicated for: insomnia ('those who lack sleep'); mechanism described as 'gently burdening the brain.' (3) Maní almond-drink (almendrada) with bee honey instead of sugar — indicated for: jaundice (ictericia) and kidney purging (purgazón de riñones); preparation: standard almendrada preparation substituting honey for sugar. | The word 'agrava' (literally 'weighs upon / aggravates') is used in a positive therapeutic sense here — i.e., it 'settles' or 'burdens' the brain to induce sleep — and has been preserved with a bracketed gloss in the translation. | 'Purgazón de riñones' likely refers to conditions requiring diuretic or cleansing action on the kidneys, possibly kidney stones or urinary inflammation. | OCR artifacts silently corrected as noted above.

results.title_inferred: On Potatoes (Papas) — cultivation, preservation as chuño, and medicinal uses
results.original_text:
CAPITULO XIII
De las papas

En toda la Sierra y tierra fría del Perú donde no se coge maíz ni las demás semillas y legumbres que se dan en tierras templadas y calientes, son las sementeras ordinarias que hacen los indios de unas raíces llamadas papas, del tamaño y hechura de criadillas de tierra; las cuales, verdes y secas, son mantenimiento tan general en el Perú, que la mitad de los indios dél no tienen otro. La planta crece dos palmos en alto; las hojas, están en la rama tan continuadas y pegadas unas a otras, que parecen una sola de muchas puntas; las cuales puntas son algo parecidas a las hojas del toronjil. Produce una flor en forma de campanilla, poco mayor que el alhelí, con cinco o seis puntillas, y son unas, moradas; otras, blancas y de otros colores; y todas tienen un botoncillo amarillo en medio no mayor que un grano de trigo. A la flor sucede una frutilla inútil, como alcaparrones. Hállanse unas papas silvestres y amargas, llamadas afora [?], que no se comen. Las que los indios siembran y benefician, son de buen sabor, aunque destas hay una especie que llaman luqui, algo amargas, pero buenas para chuñu. Diferéncianse unas papas de otras en grandeza y sabor; las mayores son como el puño, y de aquí para abajo, hasta del tamaño de una avellana; pero las ordinarias son del grandor de un huevo de gallina. Hállanse de todos colores: blancas, amarillas, moradas y rojas.

Fuera de las que destas raíces se comen verdes, asadas, cocidas y en guisados, se secan para guardar de dos maneras: la una, es secándolas al sol; las cuales, ni son de mucha dura ni quedan tan duras y curadas como las segundas, que secan desta manera. El tiempo de la cosecha de las papas es por los meses de mayo y junio, cuando en las tierras que se dan comienza el rigor de los fríos y hielos; pues, escogiéndolas, las tienden en el suelo, donde les dé de día el sol y de noche los hielos, y al cabo de doce o quince días se ponen algo arrugadas, pero todavía muy aguanosas; entonces, para exprimirles toda el agua que en sí tienen, las pisan muy bien y las dejan al sol y al hielo por otros quince o veinte días, con que quedan tan secas y livianas como un corcho, muy densas, empedernidas y tan encogidas, que de cuatro o cinco hanegas de papas verdes no sale más de una de chuñu (así llaman a estas papas después de secas deste modo). Es de tanta dura el chuñu, que aunque se guarde muchos años, no se pudre ni corrompe, y los indios lo comen cocido en lugar de pan; y es tan general este mantenimiento del chuñu, que en las provincias del Collao no comen los indios otro género de pan más que éste. Para los caciques y gente regalada se hace una suerte de chuñu más delicado y de estima, el cual se hace de las papas blancas desta manera; que después de secas al sol y al hielo, las tienen por dos meses metidas en agua, y luego las vuelven a secar al sol, con que quedan por dentro muy blancas. Llámase este chuñu regalado moray, y dél, después de tostado y molido, sacan las mujeres españolas una harina más blanca y sutil que la de trigo, de la cual hacen almidón, bizcochuelos y todas las cosas de regalo que, con almendras y azúcar, se suelen hacer; y de las papas verdes, cocidas, se hacen regaladísimos buñuelos.

El temperamento de las papas es frío y ventoso, y con todo eso, por alguna virtud oculta que tienen, hacen efecto bien contrario a sus calidades: que comida después de cenar una papa verde y cruda, evita el ahito. Cocidas las papas verdes y aplicadas muy calientes, majadas en forma de emplastos, valen contra el acerbo dolor de la gota. El temperamento del chuñu es muy seco en el primer grado; la polada hecha de su harina con azúcar y especias, se da por buen sustento a los enfermos, en particular de la harina del moray. Demás desto, los polvos del chuñu, quemados, encarnan y sanan las llagas tan bien y mejor que otra cualquiera cosa, y mezclados los polvos de la copaquira quemada, mundifican las llagas rebeldes y maliciosas y quitan callos de las fístulas y las encarnan y cicatrizan. En la lengua quichua se llaman estas raíces papas; en la aimará, amea (11).
results.translation:
CHAPTER XIII
On Potatoes

Throughout the highland and cold lands of Peru, where neither maize nor the other seeds and legumes that grow in temperate and warm regions are harvested, the ordinary crops that the Indians cultivate are certain roots called papas [potatoes], of the size and shape of truffles [criadillas de tierra]. Both fresh and dried, these are so universally consumed in Peru that half the Indians there have nothing else to eat. The plant grows to a height of two hand-spans [palmos]; its leaves are so continuous and closely set along the stem that they appear to be a single leaf with many points, which points somewhat resemble the leaves of lemon balm [toronjil]. It produces a bell-shaped flower, slightly larger than a gillyflower [alhelí], with five or six small points; some are purple, others white, and of still other colors; and all have a small yellow button in the center no larger than a grain of wheat. The flower is followed by a small, useless fruit resembling capers [alcaparrones]. There are also wild and bitter potatoes found, called afora [ILLEGIBLE: uncertain indigenous term], which are not eaten. Those that the Indians cultivate and tend are of good flavor, though among these there is one variety called luqui, somewhat bitter, but good for making chuñu. Potatoes differ from one another in size and flavor; the largest are as big as a fist, and from there downward to the size of a hazelnut, though the ordinary ones are about the size of a hen's egg. They are found in every color: white, yellow, purple, and red.

Beyond those that are eaten fresh — roasted, boiled, and in stews — these roots are dried for preservation in two ways. The first is by drying them in the sun; potatoes thus preserved do not last very long and do not become as hard and cured as those prepared by the second method, which is as follows. The harvest of potatoes falls in the months of May and June, when in the lands where they grow the severity of cold and frost begins. After being gathered, the potatoes are spread on the ground where they receive the sun by day and the frosts by night, and after twelve or fifteen days they become somewhat wrinkled but are still very watery. At that point, to squeeze out all the water they contain, they are thoroughly trodden underfoot and then left to sun and frost for another fifteen to twenty days, until they become as dry and light as cork — very dense, hardened, and so shrunken that from four or five fanegas [a dry measure] of fresh potatoes no more than one fanega of chuñu results (this is the name given to potatoes dried in this manner). Chuñu keeps so long that even if stored for many years it neither rots nor spoils, and the Indians eat it boiled in place of bread. This food of chuñu is so widespread that in the provinces of the Collao [Lake Titicaca region] the Indians eat no other form of bread than this. For chiefs [caciques] and people of refinement, a more delicate and esteemed variety of chuñu is made from white potatoes in the following manner: after being dried in the sun and frost, they are kept submerged in water for two months, and then dried in the sun again, which leaves them very white inside. This refined chuñu is called moray, and from it, once toasted and ground, Spanish women produce a flour whiter and finer than wheat flour, from which they make starch [almidón], sponge biscuits [bizcochuelos], and all the delicacies that are customarily made with almonds and sugar. From fresh boiled potatoes, moreover, most delicious fritters [buñuelos] are made.

The temperament [complexion] of potatoes is cold and flatulent [ventoso], yet despite this, by some hidden virtue they possess, they produce an effect quite contrary to their qualities: eating one raw, fresh potato after supper prevents indigestion [ahito]. Fresh potatoes, boiled and applied very hot, pounded in the form of poultices [emplastos], are effective against the sharp pain of gout [gota]. The temperament of chuñu is very dry in the first degree; a porridge [polada] made from its flour with sugar and spices is considered good nourishment for the sick, especially that made from moray flour. Furthermore, the powder of burned chuñu promotes the formation of flesh [encarna] and heals wounds better than almost anything else, and when mixed with the powder of burned copaquira [copperas / copper sulfate], it cleanses [mundifica] obstinate and malignant sores, removes calluses from fistulas, promotes new flesh, and causes them to cicatrize. In the Quechua language these roots are called papas; in Aymara, amea (11).

entities_mentioned.name: papas (papa)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Solanum tuberosum (potato)
entities_mentioned.notes: Cobo provides a detailed morphological description consistent with the cultivated potato. He notes multiple varieties including the bitter wild 'afora' and the cultivated 'luqui', and the dried preparations chuñu and moray.

entities_mentioned.name: chuñu (chuño)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Freeze-dried potato product (chuño), still produced in the Andes
entities_mentioned.notes: Described as produced by alternating sun exposure and overnight frost, then treading out moisture. Used medicinally as wound-healing powder when burned.

entities_mentioned.name: moray
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Tunta / white chuño (Solanum tuberosum processed product)
entities_mentioned.notes: A refined form of chuño made from white potatoes, soaked in water for two months after initial freeze-drying, yielding a very white interior. Also called 'tunta' in modern Aymara usage.

entities_mentioned.name: luqui
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Possibly Solanum juzepczukii or a bitter cultivar of Solanum tuberosum
entities_mentioned.notes: Described as somewhat bitter but suitable for chuñu production; likely a bitter frost-tolerant cultivar native to high-altitude Andean agriculture.

entities_mentioned.name: afora
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Possibly Solanum bukasovii or another wild bitter Solanum species
entities_mentioned.notes: Described as wild and bitter, inedible. Term is uncertain; may be an OCR distortion of a Quechua or Aymara term. Marked as uncertain in source.

entities_mentioned.name: copaquira
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Copper sulfate (CuSO₄) / copperas or vitriol
entities_mentioned.notes: Used in combination with burned chuñu powder to cleanse obstinate and malignant sores and fistulas. 'Copaquira' is an Andean term for blue vitriol or copper sulfate found near copper-bearing deposits.

entities_mentioned.name: toronjil
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Melissa officinalis (lemon balm)
entities_mentioned.notes: Used as a morphological reference point for the shape of the potato leaf.

results.translator_notes:
1. The original source text renders the first use of 'papas' as 'jmpas' — a clear OCR error; corrected silently to 'papas' throughout, consistent with the chapter title and all subsequent uses.
2. 'Afora [?]' is reproduced with its uncertainty marker from the source; the term is unverifiable and may represent a Quechua or Aymara dialect word corrupted in transcription or OCR.
3. 'Criadillas de tierra' literally means 'earth testicles' and was the common Spanish term for truffles or tubers of similar shape; rendered here as 'truffles' for clarity with the bracketed original.
4. 'Polada' (porridge or gruel made from meal) is a period-specific culinary/medical term; retained in brackets in the translation.
5. 'Encarna' (promotes the in-filling of flesh in a wound) is a technical Galenic/surgical term; translated as 'promotes the formation of flesh' with the original noted.
6. Medical theory here is explicitly Galenic: potatoes are classed as 'cold and flatulent' in complexion [temperamento], yet Cobo acknowledges their paradoxical therapeutic effects, which he attributes to 'hidden virtue' [virtud oculta] — a standard scholastic qualification.
7. 'Fanegas' is a Spanish dry-measure unit (approximately 55 liters); retained with a bracketed gloss.
8. The numbered footnote '(11)' at the close appears to reference a marginal or end note in the original manuscript/edition, not reproduced in this segment.

results.title_inferred: On the Oca: Description, Uses, and Medicinal Properties
results.original_text:
CAPITULO XIV
De las ocas

Son las ocas unas raíces comestibles que se dan en las tierras frías donde las papas. La planta desta raíz se llama xibico; es del linaje de acederas, pequeña, de una tercia poco más o menos de alto; tiene las ramas recogidas, que la hacen copada; las hojas son muy pequeñas, de tres en tres, como las de las acederas. Las raíces desta planta son largüillas medio jeme, no lisas, sino desiguales y como ñudosas; unas, blancas; otras, moradas y de otros colores, como las papas; son muy tiernas y harinosas; Cómenlas los indios verdes, asadas y cocidas, y también partidas por medio a la larga, las secan al sol para guardar; y las secas desta manera se llaman cafi, y son de buen sabor, algo dulces, como higos pasados, las cuales se comen crudas y cocidas.

El temperamento de las ocas es frío y húmedo. Las hojas y ramas desta planta, cocidas y comidas en tiempo de peste o de tabardillos, son preservativas de toda contagión, particularmente si con ellas se cuecen borrajas y raíces de escorzonera. Su zumo aprovecha contra toda inflamación y contra el incendio o intemperie de la gota; contra la sordera, echando algunas gotas en el oído; y mezclado con aceite de alacranes, quita el dolor de los riñones y el dolor de oídos de causa cálida; y el cocimiento destas hojas con azúcar, o hecho jarabe y tomado en ayunas, templa la cólera y sangre, es contra el incendio o ardor demasiado de la orina; contra las inflamaciones del hígado; contra las calenturas ardientes y continuas; contra la inflamación de la garganta; y contra el dolor del estómago que procede de causa cálida. Llámanse estas raíces, en la lengua quichua deste reino, ocas, y en la aimará, apillas.
results.translation:
CHAPTER XIV
On the Ocas

The ocas are edible roots that grow in the cold lands where the potatoes grow. The plant of this root is called xibico; it is of the sorrel family [linaje de acederas], small, about one third of a vara [roughly 11 inches] in height, more or less; its branches are gathered close together, giving it a bushy appearance; the leaves are very small, arranged in threes, like those of the sorrel. The roots of this plant are somewhat elongated, about half a jeme [roughly 3–4 inches] in length, not smooth but uneven and knotted; some are white, others purple, and of other colors, like the potatoes; they are very tender and floury. The Indians eat them green, roasted, and boiled; they also split them lengthwise and dry them in the sun for storage. Dried in this manner they are called cafi, and are of good flavor, somewhat sweet, like dried figs; these may be eaten raw or cooked.

The temperament [humoral quality] of the ocas is cold and moist. The leaves and branches of this plant, boiled and eaten during times of plague or typhus [tabardillos], are preventive against all contagion, particularly if borage and roots of black salsify [escorzonera] are boiled along with them. Their juice is beneficial against all inflammation and against the burning or excess heat [incendio o intemperie] of gout; against deafness, by dripping a few drops into the ear; and mixed with scorpion oil [aceite de alacranes], it relieves pain in the kidneys and earache arising from a hot cause [causa cálida]. A decoction of these leaves with sugar, or made into a syrup and taken on an empty stomach, moderates bile and blood; it acts against excessive burning or heat in the urine; against inflammations of the liver; against burning and persistent fevers; against inflammation of the throat; and against stomach pain arising from a hot cause. These roots are called ocas in the Quechua language of this kingdom, and apillas in Aymara.

entities_mentioned.name: ocas / apillas
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Oxalis tuberosa (oca)
entities_mentioned.notes: Andean tuber crop, still widely cultivated under the same common name. Called 'apilla' in Aymara, as Cobo notes. The plant is correctly described as belonging to the sorrel family (Oxalidaceae).

entities_mentioned.name: xibico
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Oxalis tuberosa (oca plant, above-ground portion)
entities_mentioned.notes: Cobo gives this as the name for the oca plant itself (as distinct from the root). Likely a regional or indigenous vernacular term for the above-ground plant.

entities_mentioned.name: cafi
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Dried oca (Oxalis tuberosa)
entities_mentioned.notes: The sun-dried form of the oca root; analogous in process to chuño (dried potato). Described as sweet and fig-like when dried.

entities_mentioned.name: acederas
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Rumex acetosa or related Oxalis spp. (sorrel)
entities_mentioned.notes: Used by Cobo as a morphological and taxonomic reference point for the oca plant. Correctly places oca within the same broader family (Oxalidaceae).

entities_mentioned.name: borrajas
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Borago officinalis (borage)
entities_mentioned.notes: Used in combination with oca leaves and escorzonera as a preservative decoction against plague and typhus.

entities_mentioned.name: escorzonera
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Scorzonera hispanica (black salsify)
entities_mentioned.notes: The roots are boiled alongside oca leaves and borage as an anti-contagion preparation.

entities_mentioned.name: aceite de alacranes
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Scorpion oil (macerated oil preparation using scorpions)
entities_mentioned.notes: A common 17th-century pharmaceutical preparation. Mixed with oca juice to treat kidney pain and hot-cause earache.

entities_mentioned.name: tabardillos
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Typhus fever (epidemic louse-borne typhus, Rickettsia prowazekii)
entities_mentioned.notes: The term tabardillo in colonial Spanish medical writing most commonly refers to typhus or spotted fever; occasionally used for other eruptive fevers.

entities_mentioned.name: papas
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Solanum tuberosum (potato)
entities_mentioned.notes: Mentioned incidentally as a reference crop sharing the same cold-climate growing conditions and color variation as ocas.

results.translator_notes:
1. The segment as it appears in the source contains a lengthy editorial footnote (inserted by a later editor, not Cobo) concerning Diego Dávila Briceño's 1586 description of potato cultivation and its potential to relieve famine in Spain — an anticipation of Parmentier's advocacy. This footnote also references Inca Urcon and a 'piedra cansada' (tired stone). These interpolated passages are not part of Cobo's original Chapter XIV text and have been excluded from the cleaned original text and translation, as they are clearly editorial apparatus from a later printed edition. They may warrant separate archival treatment.
2. 'Jeme' is a traditional Spanish unit of measure approximately equal to the distance from the tip of the thumb to the tip of the index finger when extended, roughly 7–8 cm.
3. 'Tercia' here refers to one-third of a vara (the Spanish yard of approximately 83.6 cm), so roughly 27–28 cm.
4. The humoral characterization of ocas as 'cold and moist' follows standard Galenic framework applied throughout Cobo's Materia Medica sections.
5. The plant name 'xibico' in the cleaned text is uncertain; the OCR rendered it variously ('rWco', etc.) and the intended spelling is not fully clear from context. 'Xibico' is a best-effort reconstruction; it may be a Quechua or regional term. Flagged for specialist review.
6. 'Incendio o intemperie de la gota' and similar phrases reflect the humoral concept of 'internal burning' caused by hot, dry humoral imbalance rather than gout in the modern sense.

results.title_inferred: On Maca: A Root of the Chinchaycocha Region
results.original_text:
CAPITULO XV
De la maca

En la sola provincia de Chinchaycocha, diócesis de Lima, se halla la raíz llamada maca en la lengua de los naturales de aquella tierra. Nace esta planta en lo más áspero y frío de la Sierra, donde no se da otra planta alguna de las que se cultivan para sustento de los hombres; que parece proveyó Dios a los indios de aquella provincia desta raíz, para que no quedasen sin tener en su tierra algún mantenimiento natural della. Sírveles la maca de pan, verde y seca, como la guardan, para todo el año. La planta es pequeña, que no se levanta del suelo más que un palmo; la hoja, muy menuda, y la raíz, es del tamaño y forma de una pera cernuma, blanca como nabo por de dentro, y después de seca queda mucho menor y muy parecida a las perillas secas; es dulce y de buen gusto; cómese así pasada cocida y asada. Tiene una extraña propiedad, que do quiera que se siembra un año, abrasa la tierra de tal manera, que en más de diez años no se puede volver a sembrar en ella; y para que no se hiele con las continuas nieves y heladas que siempre hay donde se siembra la suelen cubrir con paja hasta que llegue a sazón de cogerla. Su temperamento es muy caliente, y es común opinión que, con ser la provincia de Chinchaycocha, donde se da esta raíz, muy estéril y de tan frío y áspero temple, por mantenerse sus naturales con esta raíz no sólo no van a menos, como en las demás provincias del Perú, sino que se multiplican de cada día más, para lo cual dicen tener virtud esta raíz.
results.translation:
CHAPTER XV
On Maca

Only in the province of Chinchaycocha [Chinchay cocha], diocese of Lima, is found the root called maca in the language of the native people of that land. This plant grows in the harshest and coldest parts of the Sierra [highland range], where no other plant cultivated for the sustenance of men can survive; it would seem that God provided the Indians of that province with this root so that they would not be left without some natural foodstuff of their own land. Maca serves them as bread, both fresh and dried—as they store it—throughout the entire year. The plant is small, rising no more than a handsbreadth [palmo] above the ground; its leaf is very small, and the root is the size and shape of a cermeño pear [a small, round variety], white as a turnip on the inside, and once dried it becomes much smaller and closely resembles dried little pears; it is sweet and of good flavor; it is eaten dried, boiled, and roasted. It has a remarkable property: wherever it is sown for one year, it scorches the earth in such a way that for more than ten years it cannot be sown there again; and so that it may not freeze amid the continual snows and frosts always present where it is grown, they customarily cover it with straw until it reaches the season for harvesting. Its temperament [complexion, in the humoral sense] is very hot [caliente], and it is the common opinion that, despite the province of Chinchaycocha—where this root grows—being very barren and of so cold and harsh a climate, because its native inhabitants sustain themselves on this root, they not only do not diminish in number, as happens in the other provinces of Peru, but multiply more each day; and this root is said to possess the virtue [power] to bring this about.

entities_mentioned.name: maca
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Lepidium meyenii (maca, Peruvian ginseng)
entities_mentioned.notes: Root used as a staple food and attributed fertility-enhancing properties. Grown at high altitudes in the Junín plateau region (Chinchaycocha). Cobo's description of size, shape, color, taste, and exhausting effect on soil is consistent with modern botanical knowledge of L. meyenii.

entities_mentioned.name: provincia de Chinchaycocha
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Lake Junín region, central highlands of Peru (modern Pasco/Junín departments)
entities_mentioned.notes: Also spelled Chinchay cocha; the lake and surrounding altiplano zone, one of the highest agricultural zones in the Andes.

entities_mentioned.name: pera cernuma
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Cermeño or cermeña pear (a small, round Iberian pear variety, Pyrus communis subsp.)
entities_mentioned.notes: Used by Cobo as a morphological analogy to describe the shape of the maca root. The OCR renders this 'pera cernuma'; the intended word is almost certainly 'cermena' or 'cermeña,' a well-attested Spanish pear variety.

results.translator_notes: The segment as received contains an OCR-generated intrusion: a lengthy passage about the fortress of Cuzco and soil transported from Quito ('calla cunchuy y del trazado y construcción de la fortaleza del Cuzco...') appears to have been erroneously inserted between the chapter heading and the body text of Chapter XV. This interpolated passage does not belong to the maca chapter and has been excluded from the cleaned original text and translation. It likely belongs to a different chapter of the same volume. The phrase 'temperamento es muy caliente' reflects Galenic humoral theory as applied in colonial-era Spanish natural history; translated here as 'temperament is very hot' with a bracketed clarification. The fertility/population-multiplying virtue attributed to maca ('para lo cual dicen tener virtud esta raíz') is an early colonial attestation of maca's reputation as a fertility promoter, consistent with modern ethnobotanical and pharmacological research.

results.title_inferred: On the Jícama Root: Description, Uses, and Names
results.original_text:
CAPITULO XVII
De la jiquima

La jiquima es una raíz tan gruesa por la cabeza como la pierna, de hechura de nabo, muy corta en proporción de su grosor, porque no tiene de largo más de un palmo, y se remata en punta; por de fuera, es parda, y por de dentro, blanca, con unas pintas rojas como jaspeado, muy aguanosa y dulce. Las ramas desta planta se extienden por la tierra; echa unos vastaguillos como juncos; las hojas son del tamaño de las de malvas, con tres puntas hendidas; la semilla que da son unos granos como frisoles encerrados en unas vainillas como de habas. Comida cruda la raíz sirve de fruta, y en tiempo de calor refresca mucho, porque es de temperamento frío y húmedo. Su zumo aprovecha contra el ardor de la gota y contra toda inflamación caliente; y si al zumo se añade azúcar y se bebe seis onzas dello en ayunas, quita el ardor de la orina y templa los riñones y es contra la ictiricia y calenturas. Llaman los españoles a esta raíz jiquima, en el Perú, y en la Nueva España, jicama, el cual nombre es tomado de la lengua de la isla Española; que en las lenguas generales del Perú se dice asi pa, en la quichua, y viliu, en la aimará.
results.translation:
CHAPTER XVII
On the Jiquima

The jiquima is a root as thick at the crown as a man's leg, shaped like a turnip, very short in proportion to its girth, for it is no longer than a hand-span [palmo], tapering to a point at the end. On the outside it is brownish, and on the inside white, with reddish specks like jasper [jaspeado], very watery and sweet. The branches of this plant spread along the ground; it sends out small shoots like rushes; the leaves are the size of mallow leaves, with three lobed [hendidas] points; the seeds it produces are grains like kidney beans [frisoles] enclosed in small pods like those of broad beans. Eaten raw, the root serves as a fruit, and in hot weather it refreshes greatly, for it is of cold and moist temperament [temperamento frío y húmedo]. Its juice is beneficial against the burning pain of gout and against all hot inflammations; and if sugar is added to the juice and six ounces of it are drunk on an empty stomach, it relieves the burning of the urine, soothes the kidneys, and is effective against jaundice [ictiricia] and fevers [calenturas]. The Spaniards call this root jiquima in Peru, and in New Spain, jicama, which name is taken from the language of Hispaniola [la isla Española]; while in the general languages of Peru it is called asipa in Quechua [quichua], and viliu in Aymara [aimará].

entities_mentioned.name: jiquima / jicama / asipa / viliu
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Pachyrhizus erosus (jicama) or possibly Pachyrhizus ahipa (Andean variety); the Andean name 'asipa' strongly suggests Pachyrhizus ahipa, while 'jicama' corresponds to P. erosus
entities_mentioned.notes: Cobo conflates two related but distinct species under one entry: the Mexican jicama (Pachyrhizus erosus) and the Andean ahipa (Pachyrhizus ahipa). The Quechua name 'asipa' (here written 'asi pa' due to line break) and Aymara 'viliu' correspond to the Andean cultivar. Conditions treated: gout pain, hot inflammations, burning urination, kidney ailments, jaundice, and fevers. Preparation: raw consumption as food/fruit; juice alone or mixed with sugar, six ounces taken on an empty stomach. Temperament classified as cold and moist, consistent with Galenic humoral medicine.

entities_mentioned.name: ictiricia (jaundice)
entities_mentioned.notes: Medical condition mentioned, not a plant entity. Likely a variant spelling of 'ictericia' (jaundice), possibly an OCR artifact or period orthographic variation.

results.translator_notes: Several OCR corruptions were silently corrected: 'grua^i' → 'gruesa'; 'pcw' → 'por'; 'didee' → 'dulce'; 'extiende®' → 'extienden'; 'vastaguilk' → 'vastaguillos'; 'tamaS®' → 'tamaño'; 'fie lari' → 'de las'; 'gra¬fios*' → 'granos'; the line break 'asi pa' is almost certainly 'asipa', a well-attested Quechua name for Pachyrhizus ahipa. The word 'ictiricia' appears to be Cobo's spelling of 'ictericia' (jaundice); retained with note. The > in the source is an OCR artifact for 'de'. The term 'temperamento frío y húmedo' reflects standard Galenic humoral classification and has been flagged in the translation.

results.title_inferred: The Isaña Root: Description, Properties, and Medicinal Uses
results.original_text:
CAPITULO XVIII
De la isaña

La isaña es una raíz, que los indios comen, del tamaño y forma de la oca; unas, son amarillas exterior e interiormente, y otras, coloradas. La mata es muy parecida a la de los frísoles; cuando topa otras plantas donde asirse, trepa y se encarama sobre ellas; el vástago es redondo, verde y liso; la hoja es tan grande como la palma de la mano hendida en cinco puntas botas; su pezón es delgado, redondo y largo una tercia; la flor, es larguilla medio dedo en forma de campanilla; remátase en cinco hojitas en torno, que las tres son amarillas y las otras dos, con lo restante de la flor, coloradas. Mascada cruda la isaña, que es la raíz desta planta, es algún tanto amarga, tiene mucha agudeza y pica tanto la lengua, que no se puede comer cruda; pero, cocida, queda dulce. Tiene virtud esta raíz de reprimir el apetito venéreo, según dicen los indios; y así, afirman que mandaban los reyes Incas del Perú llevar copia deste mantenimiento en sus ejércitos, para que, comiendo dél los soldados, se olvidasen de sus mujeres. El cocimiento destas raíces, tomado en ayunas con azúcar, quita la pechuguera; y hecho cocimiento con estas raíces y las de perejil y bebido en ayunas con zumo de lima, quiebra la piedra de riñones y vejiga. En la lengua quichua llaman los indios esta raíz añu, y en la aimará, isaña.
results.translation:
CHAPTER XVIII
On the Isaña

The isaña is a root that the Indians eat, of the size and shape of the oca [Oxalis tuberosa]; some are yellow both on the outside and inside, and others are red. The plant is very similar to that of the bean [fríjol]; when it encounters other plants to cling to, it climbs and clambers over them. The stem is round, green, and smooth; the leaf is as large as the palm of the hand and is divided into five blunt points [lobes]; its petiole [pezón] is slender, round, and about one third of a vara in length. The flower is somewhat elongated, about half a finger long, in the shape of a small bell [campanilla]; it terminates in five small petals arranged around it, three of which are yellow and the other two, along with the rest of the flower, are red. When the isaña — which is the root of this plant — is chewed raw, it is somewhat bitter, has a strong sharpness, and stings the tongue so much that it cannot be eaten raw; however, once cooked, it becomes sweet. This root has the virtue of suppressing venereal appetite [i.e., sexual desire], according to the Indians; and so they affirm that the Inca kings of Peru ordered large quantities of this foodstuff to be carried in their armies, so that the soldiers, by eating it, would forget about their wives. The decoction [cocimiento] of these roots, taken on an empty stomach with sugar, relieves chest congestion [pechuguera]. A decoction made from these roots together with parsley roots, drunk on an empty stomach with lime juice, breaks up kidney and bladder stones [piedra de riñones y vejiga]. In the Quechua language the Indians call this root añu, and in Aymara, isaña.

entities_mentioned.name: isaña / añu
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Tropaeolum tuberosum (mashua or añu)
entities_mentioned.notes: Known in Quechua as 'añu' and in Aymara as 'isaña'; a tuberous Andean crop. The anti-aphrodisiac properties attributed to it have some modern pharmacological support. Cobo's comparison of the plant's habit to beans and the bell-shaped flower description is consistent with Tropaeolum tuberosum.

entities_mentioned.name: oca
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Oxalis tuberosa
entities_mentioned.notes: Used by Cobo as a morphological reference point for the size and shape of the isaña root.

entities_mentioned.name: perejil (parsley root)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Petroselinum crispum
entities_mentioned.notes: Used as an ingredient in a compound decoction for dissolving kidney and bladder stones.

results.translator_notes: 1. 'Pechuguera' is a period Spanish term for a chest ailment, likely referring to congestion, catarrh, or a persistent cough; rendered here as 'chest congestion.' 2. 'Apetito venéreo' literally means venereal/sexual appetite; the claim that Inca armies used the root as an anti-aphrodisiac is a well-known ethnobotanical tradition repeated across multiple colonial sources. 3. 'Una tercia' refers to one third of a vara (a Spanish unit of length), approximately 28 cm. 4. The OCR rendered 'isaña' as '¿.saña' in the opening line and 'ejércitos' was split; both silently corrected. 5. 'Puntas botas' means blunt-tipped lobes or points, referring to the leaf shape.

results.title_inferred: On the ulluma root: properties, uses, and preparations
results.original_text:
CAPITULO XIX
De la ulluma

La ulluma es cierta raíz muy parecida a la oca; hállanse de tan diferentes colores como las ocas; son de temperamento frío y algo ventoso y muy mucilaginoso; bebiendo una buena porción del cocimiento destas raíces, junto con la muña, facilita el parto, y el mismo efecto dicen los indios que hacen ellas comidas solas en buena cantidad. Ultra desto, hecho lamedor del cocimiento destas raíces con el azúcar necesario, y dado a menudo a los heridos penetrantes, les hace que con facilidad escupan la sangre extravenada en the pecho; y cocidas estas raíces con romero bebida su agua, quita el dolor de estómago. Llámase esta raíz, en la lengua quichua, ullucu, y en la aimará, ulluma.
results.translation:
CHAPTER XIX
On the ulluma

The ulluma is a certain root very similar to the oca [Oxalis tuberosa]; roots of it are found in as many different colors as the oca. They are of a cold and somewhat flatulent [ventoso] temperament and highly mucilaginous [mucilaginoso]. Drinking a good portion of the decoction of these roots together with muña facilitates childbirth, and the Indians say that eating them alone in a sufficient quantity produces the same effect. Beyond this, a lincture [lamedor] made from the decoction of these roots with the requisite amount of sugar, and given frequently to those with penetrating wounds [heridos penetrantes], causes them to expectorate with ease the blood extravasated [extravenada] in the chest. And when these roots are boiled with rosemary and the resulting water is drunk, it relieves stomach pain. This root is called, in the Quechua language, ullucu, and in Aymara, ulluma.

entities_mentioned.name: ulluma / ullucu
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Ullucus tuberosus (ulluco)
entities_mentioned.notes: Andean tuber crop in the family Basellaceae; Cobo notes both Quechua (ullucu) and Aymara (ulluma) names. Described as cold and mucilaginous in humoral terms.

entities_mentioned.name: oca
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Oxalis tuberosa
entities_mentioned.notes: Used here only as a morphological comparator for the ulluma's appearance and color variation.

entities_mentioned.name: muña
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Minthostachys mollis or related Minthostachys spp.
entities_mentioned.notes: An aromatic Andean herb; combined with ulluma decoction as a parturient aid [facilitates childbirth].

entities_mentioned.name: romero
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Salvia rosmarinus (rosemary)
entities_mentioned.notes: Used in combination with ulluma roots boiled in water to treat stomach pain.

medical_extractions.preparation: Decoction of ulluma roots drunk together with muña; alternatively, ulluma roots eaten alone in good quantity.

medical_extractions.condition_treated: Extravasated blood in the chest from penetrating wounds (hemoptysis / internal hemorrhage)
medical_extractions.preparation: Lincture [lamedor] made from the decoction of ulluma roots sweetened with sugar, administered frequently.
Ulluma roots boiled with rosemary; the resulting water is drunk.

results.translator_notes: OCR errors silently corrected: 'pare¬cida' → 'parecida', 'inucilaginoso' → 'mucilaginoso', 'misino' → 'mismo', 'facilidacl' → 'facilidad', 'iillucu' → 'ullucu'. The term 'lamedor' is a period pharmaceutical term for a thick, syrup-like medicinal preparation meant to be licked or sipped slowly (cf. Latin 'lambere'); rendered as 'lincture' with the original in brackets. 'Heridos penetrantes' refers specifically to penetrating (cavity-entering) wounds, a standard surgical category of the period. 'Sangre extravenada' is Cobo's rendering of extravasated blood.

results.title_inferred: On the Yahutía plant
results.original_text:
CAPITULO XX

De la yahutía

En la lengua de los indios de la isla Española se llama yahutía cierta planta cuyas raíces y hojas se comen; la cual es como una gran berza y no muy apetecible, aunque los indios la tenían por buena comida. Las raíces, que tienen unas barbas, se mondan para comer cocidas.
results.translation:
CHAPTER XX

On the Yahutía

In the language of the Indians of Hispaniola, a certain plant whose roots and leaves are eaten is called yahutía. It resembles a large cabbage [berza] and is not very palatable, although the Indians considered it good food. The roots, which bear small fibrous hairs [barbas], are peeled and eaten boiled.

entities_mentioned.name: yahutía
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Xanthosoma sagittifolium (tannia / cocoyam) or possibly Colocasia esculenta (taro); a starchy root crop of the Araceae family common in the Caribbean
entities_mentioned.notes: The term 'yahutía' (also spelled yautía) is still used in the Caribbean today, most commonly referring to Xanthosoma spp. The description of fibrous, peel-before-cooking roots and large leaf-like growth is consistent with Araceae. Colocasia esculenta cannot be fully ruled out. The author notes it was a staple food for the indigenous people of Hispaniola despite being considered unpalatable by Spanish standards.

results.translator_notes: The OCR rendered 'apetecible' as 'a 2 >etecible'; corrected silently as the intended word is unambiguous from context. No medical or pharmacological use is described in this segment — it is purely a culinary/ethnobotanical entry. The preparation method noted is: peel roots and boil.

results.title_inferred: The Cuchuchu: A Wild Root of the Peruvian High Plateau
results.original_text:
CAPITULO XXI
Del cuchuchu

Fuera de las muchas plantas que siembran y cultivan los indios para mantenerse de sus raíces, como son las que se contienen en los capítulos precedentes, hay otras muchas salvajes que nacen por los campos y tierras incultas las cuales no son de tan buen sustento ni tan estimadas como las hortenses y cultivadas. Destas raíces silvestres, la que en el Perú más se estima es la que llaman cuchuchu. Esta es una raicilla que nace en los páramos y punas tan frías, que por su rigor no producen otra alguna planta que sirva de mantenimiento al hombre. Nacen muchas de estas raicillas juntas en los lugares más húmedos de los páramos, las cuales son blancas, tiernas, muy dulces y sabrosas, y del tamaño de piñones; suélense sacar de tres en tres o de cuatro en cuatro, pegadas unas a otras por los lados, que parecen propiamente raigones de muela. No echa esta planta fuera de tierra tallos ni ramas, sino solamente unas hojitas muy menudas como las del cebollino cuando comienza a salir de la tierra. Cómese el cuchuchu crudo, verde y seco, y los españoles suelen confitarlo. Críanse ordinariamente muchas perdices en las partes donde nacen estas raicillas, las cuales sacan escarbando la tierra y se mantienen dellas. Es el cuchuchu de temperamento caliente, y afirman los indios que provoca a lujuria. Llámase cuchuchu en las dos lenguas generales del Perú.
results.translation:
CHAPTER XXI
On the Cuchuchu

Beyond the many plants that the Indians sow and cultivate to sustain themselves from their roots, as described in the preceding chapters, there are many other wild plants that grow in the fields and uncultivated lands, which are not as nourishing nor as highly regarded as the garden-grown and cultivated ones. Of these wild roots, the one most esteemed in Peru is that which they call cuchuchu. This is a small root that grows in the high moorlands [páramos] and cold upland plains [punas] so severe that no other plant capable of sustaining man grows there. Many of these small roots grow together in the most humid parts of the moorlands; they are white, tender, very sweet and flavorsome, and the size of pine nuts. They are commonly found in clusters of three or four, joined to one another at the sides, so that they closely resemble the roots of a molar tooth. The plant sends up neither stalks nor branches above the ground, but only very tiny leaves resembling those of chives [cebollino] when they first emerge from the soil. The cuchuchu is eaten raw, fresh, and dried, and the Spaniards are accustomed to candy it [confitarlo]. Partridges are commonly found in great numbers in the places where these small roots grow, as the birds scratch the earth to uncover and feed upon them. The cuchuchu is of a hot temperament [in the humoral sense], and the Indians affirm that it arouses lust [provoca a lujuria]. It is called cuchuchu in both of the general languages of Peru [i.e., Quechua and Aymara].

entities_mentioned.name: cuchuchu
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Possibly Cyperus sp. or a species of Oxalis, Lilaea, or Oreobolus; some scholars have tentatively associated it with Cyperus esculentus (tiger nut/chufa) or a high-altitude Oxalis tuber, but the description of the puna habitat and clustered small white roots most closely suggests a sedge or small geophyte of the Andean high plateau
entities_mentioned.notes: The description — small white clustered tubers found only in the coldest, most humid parts of the puna, with very tiny chive-like leaves, no visible aerial stem, pine-nut-sized, sweet, eaten raw or dried or candied — does not map cleanly onto any single confidently identified species. The aphrodisiac attribution and 'hot' humoral temperament are standard Cobo tropes for certain Andean roots. Called cuchuchu in both Quechua and Aymara.

entities_mentioned.name: perdices (partridges)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Likely Nothoprocta or Tinamotis spp. (Andean tinamous, colloquially called perdices in colonial sources)
entities_mentioned.notes: Mentioned incidentally as animals that feed on cuchuchu by scratching the ground.

results.translator_notes: Several OCR artifacts corrected silently: 'cuchiichii^' → 'cuchuchu'; 'preces dentes' → 'precedentes'; 'niante*nimiento al liomhre' → 'mantenimiento al hombre'; '{larecen' → 'parecen'; 'ceholli1io' → 'cebollino'. The phrase 'las dos lenguas generales del Perú' refers to Quechua (Runasimi) and Aymara, the two most widely spoken indigenous languages in the Viceroyalty of Peru at the time. The medical/pharmacological entry: plant name = cuchuchu; ailment/effect treated = aphrodisiac (provokes lust); preparation = consumed raw, fresh, or dried; also candied by Spaniards; humoral quality = hot. No specific disease indication beyond aphrodisiac use is given.

results.title_inferred: The Motocoro root of the Lipes region
results.original_text:
CAPITULO XXIV
Del motocoro

El motocoro es una raicilla que sólo se halla en la provincia de los Lipes, diócesis de los Charcas, tierra por extremo fría y estéril; mantiénense dello los naturales de aquella provincia, como de papas los del Collao. La mata es pequeña y aparrada con la tierra, y produce unas florecillas amarillas. Las raíces son del tamaño y hechura de bellotas, y comidas cocidas saben a ellas.
results.translation:
CHAPTER XXIV
Of the Motocoro

The motocoro is a small root [raicilla] found only in the province of Los Lipes, in the diocese of Charcas, a land that is extremely cold and barren. The native inhabitants of that province sustain themselves on it just as those of the Collao [region] do on potatoes. The plant is small and low-spreading [aparrada] against the ground, and produces small yellow flowers. The roots are the size and shape of acorns, and when boiled and eaten they taste like them.

entities_mentioned.name: motocoro
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Possibly a species of Oxalis or a related Andean tuber; some scholars suggest it may be an unidentified high-altitude tuber of the Puna of Lipes (southern Bolivia)
entities_mentioned.notes: The description — small low-growing plant, yellow flowers, acorn-sized roots that taste like acorns when boiled — is consistent with some Oxalis or Tropaeolum species, but the geographic restriction to the extremely cold Lipes Puna and the acorn-flavour comparison make confident identification difficult. Cobo's comparison to the potato as a dietary staple underscores its importance as a subsistence food rather than a medicinal plant.

entities_mentioned.name: papa (potato)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Solanum tuberosum (and related Andean Solanum spp.)
entities_mentioned.notes: Used here only as a comparative reference point for dietary staple importance in the Collao (Titicaca basin) region.

results.translator_notes: No medicinal use is described in this segment; the motocoro is presented solely as a food staple. The term 'aparrada' (spreading close to the ground, low and bushy) is a period horticultural descriptor preserved in the translation as 'low-spreading [aparrada]'. OCR errors silently corrected: 'Be halla' → 'se halla'; 'mata e?' → 'mata es'; 'La?' → 'Las'. The province of Los Lipes corresponds to the high-altitude Puna region of what is today the Potosí Department of southern Bolivia; the diocese of Charcas was centred on present-day Sucre.

Del ají

Entre las legumbres que producen el fruto en sus ramas, tiene el ají, después del maíz, el primer lugar, como la planta más general y de mayor estima entre los indios de cuantas se hallaron en esta tierra; porque entre las especias que dió Dios a los naturales de ella, es tan recibida de todas las naciones deste Nuevo Mundo, que no se ha hallado ninguna que no tuviese el uso della y en mucha estimación; y no sólo de los indios es hoy muy preciado el ají, sino también de los españoles moradores destas Indias, y aun de los que no han pasado a ellas, pues se da ya con no menor abundancia en España que en esta America, y no es menos bien recibido su uso que el de la pimienta de la India Oriental.
