results.translator_notes: 1. The source text contains several clear OCR artifacts (e.g., 'í|ue' for 'que', 'dcl' for 'del', 'Imtoncillo' for 'botoncillo', 'aniarülo' for 'amarillo', 'fHuiiiagudo' for 'puntiagudo', 'df*' for 'de', 'jtniv' for 'muy', 'ñ' for 'a', 'tmmpo' for 'tiempo', 'rirovín-'as' for 'provincias', 'manzaiiiníu' for 'manzanilla', 'erccf ii' for 'crecen', 'vastaguitos' retained as written, likely 'vastaguitos'/'vástaguitos'), all silently corrected as unambiguous. 2. The segment contains two chapters (XLIV and XLV); both are translated in full. 3. Neither chapter describes a specific medicinal application; the mullaca's blue-dye use is the primary practical property noted. 4. 'medio palmo' is a period Spanish linear measure, approximately 10 cm, rendered as 'half a handspan' with the original term bracketed in the translation. 5. The Collao (Collao) refers to the altiplano region around Lake Titicaca, consistent with the cold-climate habitat described for cota.

results.title_inferred: Three Flowers of the Indies: Cempoalxochitl, Rose of the Indies, and Velvet Flower
results.original_text:
CAPITULO XLVI

De la flor llamada cempoalxochitl

En la lengua mexicana tiene este nombre una flor que los españoles suelen llamar rosa de las Indias, la cual es natural de la Nueva España. La mata que la produce crece poco menos de un estado, y algunas, mucho más; echa tantas ramas, que se hace copada. Y cada una da muchas flores; es parecida en la hoja a la artemisa. La flor es amarilla, de la grandeza de una rosa, y algunas, mucho mayores; compuesta de muchas hojas, las cuales son retorcidas o crespas, a cuya causa la solemos llamar flor escarolada. No tiene más que buen parecer, porque su olor es desabrido y enfadoso, mayormente si se refriega en la mano, o en la pared, o en el suelo la pisan. Hallanse cinco o seis diferencias destas flores, tan semejantes unas a otras, que no se distinguen más que en ser unas mayores que otras y de más número de hojas. El nombre que le dan los mexicanos quiere decir flor de muchas hojas.

CAPITULO XLVII

De la rosa de las Indias

Nace esta casta de rosas en la provincia de Guatemala, y a la vista es tan hermosa como nuestras rosas, a las cuales se parecen tanto en el tamaño, color y forma, que, si no es llegándose cerca, no se distingue; de modo que me sucedió a mí decir misa muchos días en un altar de nuestra iglesia de Guatemala, donde habían puesto ramilletes destas rosas, y pensar que eran de las de Castilla, hasta que, reparando un día en que no olían, vine a conocerlas. Compónese esta flor de mayor número de hojas, más angostas y tiesas que nuestras rosas, y no da de sí olor alguno.

CAPITULO XLVIII

De la flor terciopelada

Solemos llamar a esta flor clavellina de las Indias. La mata que la produce es en la hoja y ramas muy semejante al cempoalxochitl, salvo que no es tan grande ni se levanta tan alta. La flor es del tamaño de una clavellina; consta de seis o siete hojitas puestas en ruedo, las cuales son gruesas y por la haz tan blandas y suaves al tacto y de tan vivo colorado, que en lo uno y en lo otro se parecen al terciopelo carmesí, de donde le dieron los españoles el nombre que tiene. No da de sí olor alguno, pero es muy vistosa.
results.translation:
CHAPTER XLVI

On the flower called cempoalxochitl

In the Mexican language [Nahuatl], this is the name given to a flower that the Spaniards commonly call the rose of the Indies, which is native to New Spain. The shrub that produces it grows to little less than one estado [approximately six feet] in height, and some grow considerably taller; it puts forth so many branches that it becomes bushy and rounded. Each branch bears many flowers; in its leaves it resembles artemisia [wormwood]. The flower is yellow, about the size of a rose, and some are much larger; it is composed of many petals, which are twisted or curled, for which reason we commonly call it the escarole flower [flor escarolada]. It has nothing to recommend it beyond its appearance, because its scent is disagreeable and offensive, especially if it is rubbed against the hand, or against a wall, or trodden underfoot. There are five or six varieties of these flowers, so similar to one another that they differ only in that some are larger than others and have a greater number of petals. The name given to it by the Mexicans means 'flower of many petals.'

CHAPTER XLVII

On the rose of the Indies

This kind of rose grows in the province of Guatemala, and to the eye it is as beautiful as our own roses, which it resembles so closely in size, color, and form that, unless one approaches quite near, it cannot be distinguished from them. Indeed, it happened to me that I celebrated Mass for many days at an altar in our church in Guatemala, where bouquets of these roses had been placed, and I believed them to be roses from Castile, until one day, noticing that they had no scent, I came to recognize them for what they were. This flower is composed of a greater number of petals, which are narrower and stiffer than those of our roses, and it gives off no scent whatsoever.

CHAPTER XLVIII

On the velvet flower

We commonly call this flower the carnation of the Indies [clavellina de las Indias]. The shrub that produces it resembles the cempoalxochitl closely in its leaves and branches, except that it is not as large nor does it grow as tall. The flower is the size of a carnation; it consists of six or seven small petals arranged in a circle, which are thick and on their upper surface so soft and smooth to the touch, and of so vivid a red, that in both respects they resemble crimson velvet, from which the Spaniards derived the name they give it. It gives off no scent whatsoever, but it is very ornamental.

entities_mentioned.name: cempoalxochitl (cempohualli-xochitl)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Tagetes erecta (African or Aztec marigold)
entities_mentioned.notes: Nahuatl name meaning 'flower of twenty petals' or 'flower of many petals.' Cobo notes the disagreeable smell when crushed, characteristic of Tagetes. No medicinal use described in this passage; primarily ornamental. Five or six varieties noted, consistent with cultivar diversity in Tagetes erecta and related species such as T. patula.

entities_mentioned.name: rosa de las Indias (rose of the Indies, Guatemala)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Possibly Rosa sp. (cultivated rose), or more likely Talauma or Chiranthodendron, though the description of odorless, many-petaled flowers resembling Castilian roses most closely suggests a Camellia-like plant or possibly Polyantha-type rose introduced to Guatemala; identification uncertain.
entities_mentioned.notes: Cobo distinguishes this from the cempoalxochitl, placing its origin in Guatemala. The key diagnostic feature is that it is visually indistinguishable from Castilian roses but entirely odorless, with narrower and stiffer petals. The identification remains uncertain; some scholars have suggested Brownea grandiceps or an ornamental Camellia, but no confident modern identification is possible from this description alone.

entities_mentioned.name: flor terciopelada / clavellina de las Indias (velvet flower / carnation of the Indies)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Portulaca grandiflora (moss rose) or more likely Catharanthus roseus; however, the vivid crimson color, velvety texture, and six to seven rounded petals arranged in a whorl most strongly suggest Portulaca grandiflora or possibly a Zinnia species. Some scholars identify this as Tagetes patula (French marigold), but the red color and texture point elsewhere.
entities_mentioned.notes: Described as resembling cempoalxochitl in foliage but smaller; flowers crimson, velvety-textured, six to seven petals in a circle, size of a carnation, odorless. The texture comparison to crimson velvet is a notable diagnostic feature. No medicinal use described; purely ornamental.

entities_mentioned.name: artemisa
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Artemisia sp. (wormwood/mugwort)
entities_mentioned.notes: Used as a leaf-shape comparator for the cempoalxochitl; not itself the subject of description.

results.translator_notes: The source text shows significant OCR degradation throughout, particularly in Chapter XLVI, where several words are garbled (e.g., 'cíi¿', 'mati', 'eek', 'mudio', 'rumas', 'composw', 'ret«:ireidas', 'desaf!«T¡«L-f', 'HaÍls?M^'). All have been reconstructed silently from context where unambiguous. The '[sic]' on 'cempoal-xochitl' in the original appears to be an editorial mark from a prior printed edition, not Cobo's own; it is preserved in the cleaned original. The unit 'estado' (approximately 1.67–2 meters, or roughly six feet) is a period Spanish measure of height roughly equivalent to a man's full stature. No medical or pharmaceutical information is present in these three chapters; the descriptions are purely botanical and naturalistic.

results.title_inferred: Description of a sunflower-like plant (Chapter XLIX)
results.original_text:
CAPITULO XLIX

nasoh por ser algo parecidas a la flor del sol en el tamaño y figura. La planta es de un estado y más de alto, y cada mata no echa más de una vara derecha del grueso de tres dedos; la hoja es tan grande como la del llantén y de la misma forma, pero más llana, áspera y vellosa. El vastago o vara echa de la mitad para arriba otras varillas o tallos delgados y cortos, y en el remate de cada uno, su flor; de suerte que cada pie o mata viene a echar diez o doce flores, con esta diferencia: que la que nace de la vara principal es mayor que las de las otras ramas. La flor es redonda, de la figura del sol, con un orden de hojas amarillas en la redondez de la figura de las hojas del sauce, delgadas y tiernas. Son estas flores muy vistosas, y cuando han abierto todas las que produce una mata, campean mucho en un huerto y recrean la vista, porque no son más que para vistas, por no tener ningún olor. La mayor destas flores tiene de diámetro, con las hojas de en torno, una tercia, y sin ella, medio palmo.
results.translation:
CHAPTER XLIX

[called thus] because they somewhat resemble the sunflower [flor del sol] in size and shape. The plant stands a fathom [estado] or more in height, and each bush sends up no more than a single straight stem about three fingers in thickness; the leaf is as large as that of the plantain [llantén] and of the same form, but flatter, rough, and hairy. The stem or stalk, from about halfway up, puts forth additional thin, short branchlets and shoots, each tipped with its own flower; so that each plant or bush comes to bear ten or twelve flowers, with this distinction: the flower arising from the main stalk is larger than those on the other branches. The flower is round, shaped like the sun, with a ring of yellow petals around its circumference resembling willow leaves [hojas del sauce] in form—thin and delicate. These flowers are very showy, and when all those produced by a single bush have opened, they make a fine display in a garden and delight the eye; yet they are good for nothing but to be looked at, as they have no scent whatsoever. The largest of these flowers measures, in diameter including the surrounding petals, one third [of a vara, approximately 28 cm], and without them, half a palm [roughly 10 cm].

entities_mentioned.name: flor del sol (sunflower-like plant described)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Possibly Tithonia species (Mexican sunflower) or a related Asteraceae; the multi-flowered habit, rough hairy leaves resembling plantain, and odorless yellow ray-flowers suggest a genus such as Tithonia or Helianthus annuus (common sunflower) in a multi-branching cultivar
entities_mentioned.notes: The text describes multiple flowers per plant (10–12) with a main central flower larger than lateral ones, and explicitly notes lack of fragrance. This multi-headed habit and the comparison to plantain leaves may point more toward Tithonia tubiformis or a related Mexican Asteraceae rather than true Helianthus annuus, though Cobo elsewhere uses 'flor del sol' for the common sunflower.

entities_mentioned.name: llantén
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Plantago major (common plantain)
entities_mentioned.notes: Used here only as a leaf-shape comparator, not as a subject plant.

results.translator_notes: The segment appears to begin mid-sentence ('nasoh por ser algo parecidas…'), indicating the chapter heading and opening text are missing or belong to a preceding page; 'nasoh' is likely an OCR artifact for a proper noun or the tail end of a plant name (possibly 'llamadas así' or a native name). No medicinal use or preparation method is described in this segment—the passage is purely botanical and aesthetic in character. Measurements: 'estado' ≈ roughly 1.7 m; 'vara' ≈ 84 cm; 'tercia' (one-third of a vara) ≈ 28 cm; 'palmo' ≈ 21 cm, 'medio palmo' ≈ 10 cm.

results.title_inferred: On the Ticsau (Nasturtium / Indian Cress)
results.original_text:
CAPITULO L
Del ticsau

El ticsau es de las plantas volubles que se enredan y asen a otras para levantarse del suelo; echa tantos vastaguillos y hojas, que cubre cualquier planta sobre que trepa y se extiende; suélese plantar en los encañados con que cercan los vergeles, para que se enrede sobre ellos, porque con sus hojas y flores, que son hermosas, hacen muy apacible vista; por lo cual, se hermosean con ellas no sólo los jardines, sino también las rejas y celosías de las ventanas y balcones. La hoja es perfectamente redonda y muy parecida en la grandeza y hechura a la oreja de abad, salvo que no es tan gruesa, sino mucho más delgada y de un verde escuro. La flor es vistosa, mas de ningún olor; de color muy vivo entre amarillo y colorado; compónese de cinco hojitas en ruedo poco menores que las de la rosa, de las cuales, las dos, tienen por de dentro unas rayas muy rojas que son más vivas y con más distinción cuanto más se llegan al pezón; tiene esta flor, junto al pezón, un piquillo de la misma materia que son las hojas, por el cual, y por la disposición de las hojas, hace esta flor figura de pájaro, por donde suelen llamarla los españoles flor de pajaritos. Así la flor como las hojas desta yerba tienen un sabor muy parecido a el del mastuerzo a cuya causa la solemos llamar también mastuerzo de las Indias. Échanse sus flores en las ensaladas y son muy apetitosas. Los indios se dan baño con el cocimiento desta yerba cuando se sienten con dolor de cabeza, y si el dolor es en todo el cuerpo, dan baño en todo él así con las hojas como con las raíces; y si tienen postillas, granos y otras infecciones del cuero, quedan libres dellas mediante el baño: y el cocimiento hecho con vino limpia y deseca las llagas. Llámase ticsau en la lengua del Perú.
results.translation:
CHAPTER L
On the Ticsau

The ticsau is one of those twining plants that wind around and cling to others in order to raise themselves from the ground. It puts out so many small shoots and leaves that it covers any plant over which it climbs and spreads. It is commonly planted on the trellised enclosures [encañados] with which orchards and gardens are fenced, so that it may twine over them; for its leaves and flowers, which are beautiful, make a very pleasing sight. For this reason it is used to adorn not only gardens but also the iron grilles and latticed shutters [celosías] of windows and balconies. The leaf is perfectly round and very similar in size and shape to the ear of an abbot [oreja de abad — a common name for a large-leafed plant], except that it is not so thick but much thinner, and of a dark green colour. The flower is showy but entirely without scent; its colour is very vivid, between yellow and red. It is composed of five small petals arranged in a circle, somewhat smaller than those of the rose, of which two have on their inner surface very red streaks that become more vivid and more distinct the closer they approach the pistil [pezón]. This flower has, close to the pistil, a small spur [piquillo] of the same substance as its leaves; by means of this spur, and by the arrangement of its petals, the flower takes the form of a bird, which is why the Spaniards commonly call it 'little-bird flower' [flor de pajaritos]. Both the flower and the leaves of this herb have a flavour very similar to that of cress [mastuerzo], for which reason it is also commonly called 'Indian cress' [mastuerzo de las Indias]. Its flowers are tossed into salads and are very appetising. The Indians [Indios] take a bath in the decoction [cocimiento] of this herb when they feel pain in the head; and if the pain affects the whole body, they bathe the entire body in it using both the leaves and the roots. If they suffer from scabs [postillas], pimples [granos], and other infections of the skin [infecciones del cuero], they are freed of these by means of the bath; and the decoction made with wine [cocimiento hecho con vino] cleanses and dries out sores [llagas]. It is called ticsau in the language of Peru.

entities_mentioned.name: ticsau (also ticsati in chapter heading)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Tropaeolum majus L. (Garden nasturtium / Indian cress)
entities_mentioned.notes: Identification strongly supported by the convergence of multiple diagnostic details: twining habit, perfectly round peltate leaves, five-petalled yellow-to-red flowers with red nectar guides and a conspicuous spur (the 'piquillo'), the characteristic peppery cress-like flavour, the Quechua name, the Peruvian provenance, and the Spanish vernacular names 'mastuerzo de las Indias' and 'flor de pajaritos', all of which are historically attested for T. majus. Medical uses extracted: (1) headache — full-body decoction bath; (2) generalised body pain — full-body bath using leaves and roots; (3) skin conditions (scabs, pimples, skin infections) — decoction bath; (4) sores/wounds [llagas] — decoction prepared with wine, applied topically to cleanse and dry. Preparation methods: aqueous decoction of the whole herb (leaves, roots) for bathing; decoction with wine for wound treatment. Flowers used in salads.

entities_mentioned.name: mastuerzo de las Indias
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Tropaeolum majus L.
entities_mentioned.notes: Spanish vernacular name used by Cobo as a synonym for ticsau, referencing its flavour similarity to European cress (mastuerzo = Lepidium sativum or Nasturtium officinale).

entities_mentioned.name: flor de pajaritos
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Tropaeolum majus L.
entities_mentioned.notes: Another Spanish vernacular name for ticsau, referring to the bird-like appearance created by the spur and petal arrangement of the flower.

results.translator_notes: 1. The chapter heading in the source has 'ticsati' (likely an OCR artefact of a ligature or damaged 'u'), corrected to 'ticsau' throughout, consistent with the closing sentence of the chapter which explicitly states 'Llámase ticsau en la lengua del Perú.' 2. The heading 'De la flor tornasol de las Indias' appearing mid-page in the source is a running header for the next chapter printed on the same page spread; it does not belong to the ticsau chapter and has been omitted from the cleaned text accordingly. 3. 'Oreja de abad' is a Spanish plant nickname (literally 'abbot's ear') applied to several large round-leaved plants; it serves here only as a size/shape comparator. 4. 'Pezón' in botanical context denotes the peduncle or stalk-end of the flower; in describing the flower's interior it most likely refers to the base/nectary area near the spur. 5. 'Encañados' refers to reed or cane trellis-work fencing typical of colonial Peruvian kitchen gardens.

results.title_inferred: The Vilcu Plant: Description and Linguistic Origin
results.original_text:
CAPITULO LI
Del vilcu

El vilcu es una planta muy semejante al ticsau en sus hojas, flores y en ser voluble; produce los vástagos delgados como un junco, redondos, verdes, con unas venillas rojas, tiernos y correosos, y a trechos echa las hojas y flores. La hoja es poco menor que la palma de la mano, de hechura de la de la vid, hendida hasta cerca del pezón, con cinco o siete puntas, la de enmedio mayor, y las de los lados, tanto menores cuanto más se apartan de la de en medio. Las flores son amarillas, sin olor alguno, y por la hechura que tienen, las llaman pajaritos los españoles; son pequeñas como las de la retama, tienen dos hojitas mayores que las demás, que imitan las alas de los pájaros, y debajo dellas, junto al pezón, un piquillo que corresponde al pico del pájaro. El nombre de vilcu que tiene esta yerba es tomado de la lengua aimará, que es la general de las provincias del Collao,
results.translation:
CHAPTER LI
On the Vilcu

The vilcu is a plant very similar to the ticsau in its leaves, flowers, and in being a climber [voluble]; it produces slender stems like a rush [junco], round, green, with small red veins, tender and tough, and it puts forth leaves and flowers at intervals along them. The leaf is slightly smaller than the palm of the hand, shaped like that of the grapevine, lobed nearly to the petiole [pezón], with five or seven points, the middle one being the largest, and the lateral ones progressively smaller the farther they are from the middle one. The flowers are yellow, entirely without scent, and on account of their shape the Spaniards call them 'little birds' [pajaritos]; they are small like those of the broom [retama], having two petals larger than the rest that resemble the wings of birds, and beneath these, near the petiole, a small beak-like projection [piquillo] that corresponds to the beak of a bird. The name vilcu that this herb bears is taken from the Aymara language, which is the common tongue of the provinces of the Collao,

entities_mentioned.name: vilcu
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Possibly a species of Tropaeolum or a twining legume (e.g., Canavalia or Kennedia sp.); the butterfly-shaped yellow flowers with wing-like petals and a beak-like keel strongly suggest a papilionaceous legume (Fabaceae), possibly Vigna or a related genus native to the Andean region.
entities_mentioned.notes: The flower description — two large wing-like petals and a lower beak-like keel — is characteristic of papilionaceous (pea-family) flowers. The grapevine-shaped, palmately lobed leaves are less typical of most legumes, leaving identification uncertain. The name is confirmed as Aymara in origin, associated with the Collao (altiplano) region.

entities_mentioned.name: ticsau
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: null
entities_mentioned.notes: Mentioned as a comparator plant with similar leaves, flowers, and twining habit. Not identified in this segment; likely described in a preceding chapter.

entities_mentioned.name: retama
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Retama monosperma or Spartium junceum (broom); used here as a size comparison for the flowers.
entities_mentioned.notes: Used by Cobo as a familiar Old World reference point for the reader, not as a plant under discussion.

results.translator_notes: The segment ends mid-sentence ('del Collao,'), indicating the chapter continues on the next page/segment. The OCR rendering 'hojitajs' in the source has been silently corrected to 'hojitas.' The term 'voluble' is a technical botanical term of the period meaning a twining or climbing plant, preserved in brackets. No medicinal use or preparation method is described in this segment; the chapter appears to be purely descriptive at this point.

results.title_inferred: On the Misuca and Panti Flowers (Chapters LII–LIII)
results.original_text:
CAPITULO LII
De la misuca

La misuca es una flor amarilla de tanto ruedo como un real de a cuatro y de la hechura de la flor de la manzanilla. La mata se levanta un codo de la tierra, echa muchos ramillos delgados, redondos, lisos y verdes; la hoja muy menuda y hendida, semejante a la de la artemisa.

Otra diferencia se halla de misuca muy parecida a ésta, salvo que los vastaguillos son algún tanto rojos y las hojas como las de la verbena, muy aserradas por los lados. A la primera especie de misuca llaman hembra los indios, y macho, a la segunda; y con ambas suelen teñir de amarillo. No dan de sí estas flores olor alguno. En la lengua aimará se dicen flor y planta misuca.

CAPITULO LIII
Del panti

El panti es una flor colorada de la hechura de la misuca; tiene de siete a ocho hojas en torno con el botón de en medio amarillo. Es tan grande esta flor como un real de a ocho; no tiene olor alguno. La mata crece un codo en alto, hace los tallos redondos, delgados y lisos; la hoja, menuda y hendida, como la de la artemisa; tiene la raíz gruesa, redonda o prolongada y tierna como criadilla de tierra. Llámase así esta flor en la lengua aimará.
results.translation:
CHAPTER LII
On the Misuca

The misuca is a yellow flower as wide in circumference as a four-real coin [roughly 3–4 cm across], and shaped like the flower of chamomile. The plant rises one cubit [approx. 45 cm] from the ground and puts out many thin, round, smooth, green little branches; the leaf is very small and cleft, resembling that of artemisia [mugwort].

Another variety of misuca is found, very similar to the first, except that the little stems are somewhat reddish and the leaves are like those of verbena, very finely serrated along the sides. The Indians call the first species the female, and the second the male; and with both they customarily dye [cloth or fiber] yellow. These flowers give off no scent whatsoever. In the Aymara language, both the flower and the plant are called misuca.

CHAPTER LIII
On the Panti

The panti is a red flower shaped like the misuca; it has seven to eight petals arranged around a yellow central button [disk]. This flower is as large as an eight-real coin [roughly 4–5 cm across]; it has no scent. The plant grows one cubit in height, producing round, thin, and smooth stems; the leaf is small and cleft, like that of artemisia; it has a thick root, round or elongated and tender like a truffle [criadilla de tierra, lit. 'earth testicle']. This flower is so called in the Aymara language.

entities_mentioned.name: misuca (hembra — female variety)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Possibly Bidens humilis or a related Bidens / Coreopsis species (Asteraceae); yellow composite flowers with finely divided artemisia-like leaves are characteristic of Andean Bidens spp.
entities_mentioned.notes: Used as a yellow dye plant. Cobo notes two varieties distinguished by the Indians as female (first) and male (second). No medicinal use described in this passage. Aymara name 'misuca' applied to both flower and plant.

entities_mentioned.name: misuca (macho — male variety)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Possibly a second Bidens or Tagetes species; the reddish stems and verbena-like serrated leaves suggest a distinct taxon within Asteraceae.
entities_mentioned.notes: Also used as a yellow dye. Differentiated from the female variety by reddish stems and more coarsely serrated leaves resembling verbena.

entities_mentioned.name: panti
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Possibly Cosmos peucedanifolius or a red-flowered Mutisia / Coreopsis species (Asteraceae); the description of red ray florets, yellow central disk, artemisia-like leaves, and a fleshy root is suggestive of some Andean Asteraceae, though Mutisia spp. are also plausible.
entities_mentioned.notes: No medicinal or dye use is attributed here; the description is purely morphological. The fleshy, rounded or elongated root likened to a truffle (criadilla de tierra) is a notable distinguishing feature. Aymara name 'panti.'

entities_mentioned.name: manzanilla [chamomile]
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Matricaria chamomilla or Anthemis nobilis
entities_mentioned.notes: Used only as a morphological comparator for flower shape.

entities_mentioned.name: artemisa [artemisia / mugwort]
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Artemisia sp. (likely A. vulgaris or a locally familiar Artemisia)
entities_mentioned.notes: Used only as a comparator for leaf shape in both the misuca and panti descriptions.

entities_mentioned.name: verbena
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Verbena officinalis or a related Verbena sp.
entities_mentioned.notes: Used only as a comparator for the serrated leaf shape of the male misuca variety.

results.translator_notes: 1. 'Real de a cuatro' and 'real de a ocho' (piece of four / piece of eight) are silver coins used as size references; the eight-real coin was noticeably larger than the four-real, consistent with Cobo describing the panti as larger than the misuca. 2. 'Codo' (cubit) is rendered as approximately 45 cm. 3. 'Criadilla de tierra' literally means 'earth testicle' and was a common Spanish term for truffles or tuber-like underground organs; translated as 'truffle' with the original in brackets. 4. The OCR artifact 'man*zanilla' (line-break hyphen rendered as asterisk) has been silently corrected to 'manzanilla.' Similarly, 'vastaguillos' was rendered as 'vastagüillos' in the OCR; corrected silently. 5. No medicinal preparations are described in either chapter; these entries appear to be purely botanical and ethnobotanical (dye use) in nature.

results.title_inferred: On the Ullaulla flower of the Peruvian highlands
results.original_text:
CAPITULO LIV
De la ullaulla

La Ullaulla es una florecita llamada así en la lengua aimará, de color entre blanco y morado, del tamaño y talle de la del saúco, algún tanto mayor; compónese de cinco hojitas en rueda, las cuales tienen las puntas botas; nacen a manojitos de ocho a diez florecillas cada uno. Tienen un olor grato al olfato y vivo, y son de las de mayor fragancia que producen las sierras del Perú, a donde nacen en gran abundancia y hermosean mucho los campos. La mata que produce estas flores es muy pequeña, extiende sus ramos sobre la tierra sin levantarse della, los cuales son largos un palmo y casi tan delgados como hilos, con unas venillas rojas. Las hojas son casi tan menudas como las de la zanahoria. Donde mayor copia destas florecitas he visto yo es en el contorno de la villa de Oruro.
results.translation:
CHAPTER LIV
On the Ullaulla

The Ullaulla is a small flower called by this name in the Aymara language, of a color between white and purple, of the size and form of the elderflower [saúco], though somewhat larger; it is composed of five small petals arranged in a wheel [i.e., radially], whose tips are blunt [botas]. They grow in small clusters of eight to ten florets each. They have a fragrance that is pleasing and lively to the sense of smell, and they are among the most fragrant flowers produced by the mountain ranges [sierras] of Peru, where they grow in great abundance and greatly beautify the fields. The shrub that produces these flowers is very small; it spreads its branches over the ground without rising from it, the branches being a hand's span [palmo] in length and almost as slender as threads, with small red veins. The leaves are almost as fine and small as those of the carrot. The place where I have seen the greatest abundance of these little flowers is in the environs of the town of Oruro.

entities_mentioned.name: Ullaulla
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Possibly a species of Nardophyllum, Belloa, or another small prostrate Asteraceae or Valerianaceae of the Andean altiplano; some scholars have associated the name with Acantholippia or Clinopodium (Lamiaceae) species of the puna, but identification remains uncertain.
entities_mentioned.notes: Described as a prostrate mat-forming plant with white-to-purple radially arranged five-petaled flowers growing in clusters, strongly fragrant, with very finely divided leaves resembling carrot foliage, found abundantly around Oruro (Bolivian altiplano). The Aymara name 'ullaulla' is documented in some ethnobotanical sources but has been applied to more than one taxon. No medicinal use is described in this segment.

entities_mentioned.name: Saúco
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Sambucus nigra / Sambucus peruviana (elderberry/elderflower)
entities_mentioned.notes: Used here purely as a morphological comparator for flower size and form.

entities_mentioned.name: Villa de Oruro
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Oruro, Bolivia (highland mining town, established 1606)
entities_mentioned.notes: Geographic locality where Cobo personally observed the greatest concentration of this plant.

results.translator_notes: Several OCR errors were silently corrected: 'cora*pénese' → 'compónese'; 'lm cu^es' → 'las cuales'; 'floreciEM' → 'florecillas'; 'V' (used repeatedly for the conjunction) → 'y'; 'lun' → 'son'; 'mentidas' → 'menudas'; 'Perái' → 'Perú'. The word 'botas' (blunt/rounded tips) is retained in brackets in the translation as a period botanical descriptor. No medical or pharmaceutical content is present in this segment; it is purely a botanical description.

results.title_inferred: The Qixeaquea plant and closing remarks on American wildflowers
results.original_text:
CAPITULO LV
De la qixeaquea

La qixeaquea se llama así en la lengua aimará. Es una yerba pequeña; echa los ramillos largos un palmo, delgados, verdes y vellosos; sus hojas son como las del poleo, más puntiagudas, las cuales y los ramillos son pegajosos; produce unas florecicas blancas del tamaño y forma que las de la patacauri.
Dejo otras infinitas flores silvestres que nacen en diversas partes desta tierra, que fuera proceder en infinito quererlas contar todas, pues sólo las que en tiempo de garúas visten las lomas y cerros del contorno desta ciudad de Lima, y las que en la plaza de México venden las indias en curiosos ramilletes, no tienen número ni cuento. Basta haber hecho mención de las más conocidas, para que se entienda que no carecía esta América de la hermosura y adorno de las flores.
results.translation:
CHAPTER LV
On the qixeaquea

The qixeaquea is so called in the Aymara language. It is a small herb; it puts forth slender branches about a hand's span [palmo] in length, thin, green, and hairy; its leaves resemble those of pennyroyal [poleo], though more pointed, and both the leaves and the branches are sticky; it produces small white flowers of the same size and form as those of the patacauri.
I leave aside the countless other wildflowers that grow in various parts of this land, for it would be an endless task to attempt to enumerate them all — for those alone that during the season of the garúas [coastal drizzle-fogs] clothe the hills and slopes surrounding this city of Lima, and those that the Indian women sell in the plaza of Mexico City in carefully arranged bouquets, are beyond all number and reckoning. It is enough to have mentioned the best known of them, so that it may be understood that this America was by no means lacking in the beauty and adornment of flowers.

entities_mentioned.name: qixeaquea (also spelled qixeaquea/queaquea)
entities_mentioned.notes: An Aymara-named small herb with hairy, sticky, pointed leaves resembling pennyroyal and small white flowers. Insufficient detail for confident botanical identification; possibly a member of Lamiaceae or a sticky-leaved Andean herb. No medicinal use is described in this passage.

entities_mentioned.name: patacauri
entities_mentioned.notes: Referenced only as a comparator for the flower size and form of the qixeaquea. Likely an Andean plant known to Cobo's readers but not further described here; identification unknown.

  "title_inferred": "On Tobacco: Description, Varieties, Medicinal Uses, and the Custom of Smoking",
  "original_text": "CAPITULO LVI
Del tabaco

Muy conocida es ya la planta del tabaco no sólo en todas las Indias, sino también en Europa, adonde se ha llegado desta tierra y es muy estimado por sus muchas y excelentes virtudes. Crece uno y dos estados en alto, más o menos, según la fertilidad de la tierra donde nace. Echa una vara o tallo derecho tan grueso como el de la mostaza, redondo, verde, velloso y áspero. Las hojas, en una misma mata, son desiguales; unas mayores que otras, porque, cuánto más altas, son menores; las mayores que yo he visto tienen tres palmos de largo y dos de ancho; son al talle de las hojas de los bledos, verdes, vellosas, algo grasas y pegajosas. Cuando va madurando esta planta, echa de la mitad del vástago para arriba muchas ramas, con que se viene a hacer copada como la mata de la mostaza. En su cumbre nacen unas florecicas de figura de campanillas, mayores que la flor del jazmín, unas rosadas y otras blancas. Su semilla es la menor que yo he visto de cuantas plantas conozco; son unos granitos redondos, pardos, metidos en unas cabezuelas como de amapolas, tan pequeños, que un grano de mostaza tiene por tres o cuatro dellos.

Hállanse dos diferencias de tabaco: uno, hortense, que es el que aquí he pintado, y otro, salvaje, que nace en lugares incultos, el cual no crece tan alto ni produce tan grandes hojas, pero es de más fuerte y eficaz virtud que el hortense. Es el tabaco caliente en tercero grado; sirve para curar infinitas enfermedades aplicado en hoja verde y seca, en zumo, en polvo, en humo, en cocimiento y de otras maneras. Puesto un saquillo de tabaco sobre la cabeza o en la almohada, provoca a sueño. Tiene gran enemistad contra las fieras y sabandijas ponzoñosas; por lo cual, cuando los indios duermen de noche en partes que las hay, ponen esta yerba alrededor de sí, con que ningún animal venenoso les empece.

A la raíz de

results.title_inferred: On Tobacco: Trade, Regional Varieties, and Indigenous Names
results.original_text:
os herbolarios.

Es tanta la cantidad de tabaco que se gasta en las Indias y se lleva a España, que hay provincias que todo el trato y granjerías de sus habitantes es cultivarlo y beneficiarlo; y tienen más precio los de unas partes que los de otras. En la Nueva España es famoso el de Papantla, y en este reino del Perú el de Jaén de Pacamoros. El instrumento en que los indios de la Española tomaban el tabaco en humo se decía tabaco, el cual nombre dieron los Españoles a esta yerba y con él se ha quedado hasta hoy. En la lengua general del Perú se llama sayri, y en la mexicana, picietl.
results.translation:
...the herbalists [herbolarios].

So great is the quantity of tobacco [tabaco] consumed in the Indies and carried to Spain that there are provinces whose entire trade and livelihood [granjerías] of their inhabitants consists in cultivating and curing [beneficiarlo] it; and tobacco from some regions commands a higher price than that from others. In New Spain the tobacco of Papantla is the most renowned, and in this kingdom of Peru that of Jaén de Pacamoros. The instrument with which the Indians of Hispaniola inhaled tobacco smoke was called tabaco, which name the Spaniards gave to this herb and by which it has been known ever since. In the general language [lengua general] of Peru it is called sayri, and in the Mexican tongue, picietl.

entities_mentioned.name: tabaco (Papantla)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Nicotiana tabacum
entities_mentioned.notes: Papantla is a region in Veracruz, New Spain, noted for high-quality tobacco in the colonial period.

entities_mentioned.name: tabaco (Jaén de Pacamoros)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Nicotiana tabacum
entities_mentioned.notes: Jaén de Bracamoros is a region in what is now northern Peru/Ecuador border area, noted by Cobo for producing prized Peruvian tobacco.

entities_mentioned.name: sayri
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Nicotiana tabacum
entities_mentioned.notes: Quechua name for tobacco used in the general language (lengua general) of Peru.

entities_mentioned.name: picietl
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Nicotiana rustica or Nicotiana tabacum
entities_mentioned.notes: Nahuatl name for tobacco. Picietl (also piciete) more commonly refers to Nicotiana rustica, the stronger wild variety used in Mesoamerica.

Cobo's etymological claim—that the Spanish word 'tabaco' derives from the indigenous name for the pipe or smoking instrument used in Hispaniola, rather than from the plant itself—reflects a longstanding early modern debate about the word's origin. The segment appears to begin mid-sentence (referring to 'herbalists'), indicating this is a continuation from the preceding part of chapter LVI.

results.title_inferred: On the Itapallo and Chichicaste: Two Stinging-Nettle-Like Plants and Their Medicinal Uses
results.original_text:
CAPITULO LVII

Del itapallo y chichicaste

El itapallo es una yerba que en sus efectos parece especie de ortiga, aunque realmente se distingue della en esto: levántase de la tierra un codo; sus tallos son verdes, redondos, tiernos y no más gruesos que un delgado junco; produce desde el pie muchas hojas, las cuales son de un verde oscuro, largas un jeme, y por donde más anchas, tienen tres dedos; vanse desde junto al pezón estrechando hasta rematar en delgadas puntas; son muy hendidas por los lados en muchas puntas. Echa una flor esta yerba de color encarnado fino, muy vistosa, aunque de ningún olor; compónese de cinco hojitas tan juntas unas con otras, que parecen estar continuadas y no contiguas; tiene cada hojita, por la parte de afuera, un lomillo en medio tan relevado, que parece pliegue, y lo que le corresponde por la parte de dentro está hundido en forma de ángulo; con que viene a tener esta flor hechura de un botoncito redondo de cinco picos, tan grande como una cáscara de nuez puesta en figura redonda. Por las junturas de las hojas desta flor nacen, por la parte de dentro, otras cinco hojitas blancas y lisas y casi transparentes, tan retorcidas, que vienen a tener figura redonda y parece cada una una perfecta y reluciente perla. Los tallos, hojas y flores desta yerba están armados de muchas espinillas blancas muy delgadas y dos veces más largas que las de las borrajas, que punzan como las de las ortigas, aunque no escuecen tanto.

Es el itapallo de temperamento caliente y seco; usan los indios de su cocimiento con salmuera para resolver las hinchazones de los gotosos así de las piernas como de las manos. Hecho cocimiento con vino y aplicada la yerba caliente como sale del cocimiento sobre la región de la vejiga, vale contra la detención de orina. Usan las indias del cocimiento desta yerba en ayunas desde un día después de haber parido hasta nueve o diez, para efecto de quedar del todo limpias de la sangre que pudiera rezagarse; para lo cual comen también la yerba cocida en sus potajes. Demás desto, bebido de ordinario su cocimiento con miel de abejas, mundifica las llagas del pulmón, limpia las materias del pecho y estómago, es gran abstergente de las llagas de los riñones, desopila el bazo, adelgaza los humores viscosos, ensancha las vías y provoca la orina. Finalmente, cocida esta yerba con sus flores, raíces de hinojo, de perejil, de apio, pepitas de melón y agrio de limas, y tomando del cocimiento en ayunas con azúcar y miel un buen jarro, limpia poderosamente las arenas y piedras de riñones y vejiga. Llámase esta yerba, en la lengua aimará, itapallo.

Otra casta de ortigas se halla en la Nueva España llamada chichicaste; crece un estado de alto; tiene la hoja como de moral, pero más ancha; están las hojas y cogollos cubiertos de unas espinillas que punzan con más dolor que ortigas y levantan como sarpullido en la parte que pican.
results.translation:
CHAPTER LVII

On the Itapallo and the Chichicaste

The itapallo is an herb that in its effects resembles a species of nettle [ortiga], though it is truly distinct from it in the following respects: it rises from the ground about one cubit [codo] in height; its stems are green, round, tender, and no thicker than a slender reed; from the base it produces many leaves, which are of a dark green color, a hand-span [jeme] in length, and at their widest about three fingers broad; from near the stalk they narrow progressively until they end in slender points; they are deeply cut along the sides into many points. This herb puts forth a flower of a fine crimson color, very handsome, though entirely without scent; it is composed of five small petals so closely joined to one another that they appear to be continuous rather than merely contiguous; each little petal has, on its outer face, a raised ridge down the center so prominent that it looks like a fold, while the corresponding inner surface is hollowed in the form of an angle; whereby this flower comes to have the shape of a small round button with five points, as large as a walnut shell arranged in a round form. At the junctions of the petals of this flower there grow, on the inner side, five other small leaves that are white, smooth, and nearly transparent, so twisted that they take on a rounded shape, and each one resembles a perfect and lustrous pearl. The stems, leaves, and flowers of this herb are armed with many very fine white spines twice as long as those of borage [borrajas], which sting like those of nettles, though they do not burn as much.

The itapallo is of a hot and dry temperament [following Galenic humoral classification]. The Indians use a decoction of it with brine [salmuera] to resolve the swellings of gout sufferers, both in the legs and in the hands. Made into a decoction with wine and the herb applied hot, as it comes from the decoction, over the region of the bladder, it is efficacious against retention of urine. Indian women use the decoction of this herb, taken on an empty stomach, from one day after giving birth through the ninth or tenth day, for the purpose of being entirely cleansed of any blood that might remain; to the same end they also eat the herb boiled in their stews [potajes]. Beyond this, the decoction drunk regularly with honey from bees cleanses the ulcers [llagas] of the lungs, purges matter from the chest and stomach, is a great detergent [abstergente] of ulcers of the kidneys, unblocks [desopila] the spleen, thins viscous humors, opens the passages, and promotes urination. Finally, this herb boiled together with its flowers, roots of fennel, parsley, and celery, melon seeds, and lime juice, and drinking a good jarful of the decoction on an empty stomach with sugar and honey, powerfully cleanses gravel and stones from the kidneys and bladder. This herb is called, in the Aymara tongue, itapallo.

Another kind of nettle is found in New Spain called chichicaste; it grows to the height of a man [un estado]; its leaf resembles that of the mulberry, but broader; the leaves and shoots are covered with small spines that sting with more pain than nettles and raise something like a rash [sarpullido] on the part they prick.

entities_mentioned.name: itapallo
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Possibly a species of Loasaceae (e.g., Caiophora or Nasa sp.), given the Andean origin, Aymara name, stinging hairs, and showy crimson flowers; alternatively a Urticaceae species native to the Andean region
entities_mentioned.notes: Aymara name 'itapallo' is not securely matched in modern botanical literature. The detailed description of five crimson petals with raised ridges, five inner pearl-like structures, and stinging hairs distinct from true nettles is suggestive of the family Loasaceae, which contains many stinging Andean herbs with elaborate floral morphology. Medicinal uses—diuretic, post-partum cleansing, gout, urinary stones—are noted.

entities_mentioned.name: chichicaste
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Urera baccifera or Urera caracasana (family Urticaceae); possibly also Cnidoscolus sp. (family Euphorbiaceae) in some Mexican regional usage
entities_mentioned.notes: Chichicaste is a well-attested common name in Mesoamerica for large stinging plants. Cobo's description of mulberry-like leaves, man-height growth, and a sting more painful than common nettles producing a rash is consistent with Urera spp. or Cnidoscolus spp. The New Spain provenance supports a Mesoamerican species.

entities_mentioned.name: ortiga
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Urtica sp. (common nettle, family Urticaceae)
entities_mentioned.notes: Used as the reference plant against which itapallo and chichicaste are compared.

entities_mentioned.name: borraja
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Borago officinalis (borage, family Boraginaceae)
entities_mentioned.notes: Referenced only as a comparative measure for spine length.

results.translator_notes:
1. Several OCR artifacts were silently corrected: 'tftetos' → 'efectos'; 'nniy' → 'muy'; 'fiíiiipónese' → 'compónese'; 'heiíhura' / 'hechura' normalized; 'líotoncito' → 'botoncito'; 'freimiento' → 'cocimiento' (contextually clear from surrounding uses); 'drtención' → 'detención'; 'huíía' → 'hasta'; 'r4yunas' → 'ayunas'; 'aziicar' → 'azúcar'; 'y-erba' → 'yerba'.
2. The word 'estado' as a unit of height means approximately the height of an adult man (~1.7 m), translated accordingly.
3. 'Jeme' is a traditional Spanish unit of length equal to the distance from the tip of the thumb to the tip of the index finger when extended, roughly 10–12 cm.
4. 'Mundifica' and 'abstergente' are technical Galenic pharmacological terms meaning 'cleanses/purifies' and 'detergent/cleansing agent' respectively; preserved with explanatory bracketing in the translation.
5. Cobo's humoral characterization ('caliente y seco') follows standard Galenic-Scholastic medical theory of the period.
6. The post-partum use of the decoction by indigenous women (days 1–9 or 10) is an ethnobotanically significant detail worth flagging for medical anthropology researchers.

results.title_inferred: The Herb Called Pencácuc (the Shameplant)
results.original_text:
CAPITULO LVIII
De la yerba llamada pencácuc

Este nombre es participio de un verbo de la lengua del Perú que significa avergonzarse, y lo dan los indios a cierta yerba por el efecto que hace en tocándole con la mano, que es encogerse por un rato, el cual pasado, vuelve a ponerse como estaba de antes, y suena lo mismo que si dijésemos La que se avergüenza, y los españoles por la misma razón la llaman la vergonzosa. Tiene la hoja pequeña, semejante a la del arrayán, y la raíz como de nabo. En esta planta se hallan macho y hembra y nacen siempre juntas entrambas; el macho tiene la hoja como de lirio y la raíz redonda; no se encoge cuando la tocan, pero tiene otra propiedad extraña, y es que comida su raíz enciende poderosamente en lujuria, y el remedio para apagar este fuego está en comer de la raíz de la hembra, que al punto lo remite; propiedad rara y muy conocida de los indios, particularmente de los de la provincia de Chachapoyas, diócesis de Trujillo, donde nace mucha desta yerba.
results.translation:
CHAPTER LVIII
On the Herb Called Pencácuc

This name is a participle derived from a verb in the language of Peru [Quechua] meaning 'to feel shame,' and the Indians give it to a certain herb on account of the effect produced when one touches it with the hand, which is that it [the plant] draws itself in for a time, after which it returns to its former state. The name thus conveys the same meaning as if we were to say 'She who is ashamed,' and the Spaniards, for the same reason, call it 'la vergonzosa' [the shameful/bashful one]. It has small leaves resembling those of the myrtle [arrayán], and a root like that of a turnip. In this plant both male and female forms are found, and the two always grow together. The male has leaves like those of a lily and a round root; it does not draw in when touched, but it possesses another strange property, namely that eating its root powerfully inflames one with lust [lujuria], and the remedy for quenching this fire lies in eating the root of the female plant, which promptly extinguishes it. This is a rare and very well known property among the Indians, particularly those of the province of Chachapoyas, in the diocese of Trujillo, where much of this herb grows.

entities_mentioned.name: pencácuc / la vergonzosa
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Mimosa pudica L. (sensitive plant / shameplant) or a closely related Mimosa species
entities_mentioned.notes: The tactile leaf-folding behavior ('encogerse') is the defining characteristic of Mimosa pudica. Cobo distinguishes a 'female' form (with myrtle-like leaves and turnip-like root, which folds) and a 'male' form (with lily-like leaves and round root, which does not fold). The 'male' may represent a different but co-occurring species, possibly another Mimosa or related Fabaceae. The aphrodisiac attribution to the male root and its antidote in the female root reflect indigenous ethnobotanical knowledge from Chachapoyas, northern Peru.

entities_mentioned.name: arrayán
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Myrtus communis L. (common myrtle) or a New World Myrtaceae species
entities_mentioned.notes: Used here as a comparative leaf-shape reference, not as a subject plant in its own right.

results.translator_notes: OCR rendered 'hijuria' (clearly 'lujuria,' lust/lechery) and 'imiy' (clearly 'muy,' very); both silently corrected in the cleaned original text. The distinction between male and female morphological and pharmacological forms of pencácuc is a notable piece of indigenous Peruvian ethnobotany; Cobo does not supply a preparation method beyond direct consumption of the raw root ('comida su raíz'). Geographic attribution to Chachapoyas (modern Amazonas region, northern Peru) is specific and potentially useful for botanical identification. The aphrodisiac/anaphrodisiac dual-plant concept may warrant cross-referencing with other colonial Materia Medica sources.

results.title_inferred: Chapter LIX: On the Choclla (two species of cebadilla)
results.original_text:
CAPITULO LIX

De la choclla

Dos especies se hallan en estas Indias de cierta yerba que los españoles llaman cebadilla, por la semejanza que tienen con la cebada: la una es natural de la Nueva España, y la otra, deste reino del Perú. La primera echa una espiga parecida a la del trigo, larga un palmo y más; su caña es asimismo como la del trigo, algo más gruesa, parda y maciza, de un estado de alto; nace por las sabanas sin sembrarla. Los granillos son negros, algo menores que los de cebada, los cuales, molidos, matan los gusanos de las mataduras de las bestias y también las niguas que entran a las personas.

La otra especie de cebadilla se dice choclla en la lengua aimará; es pasto escogido para los ganados, y se vende en las ventas del Collao en lugar de alfalfa o alcacer. Nace abundantemente en todas las provincias del Collao; echa una caña hueca la mitad más delgada que la otra cebadilla, con no más de uno o dos ñudos a trechos, dos codos levantada en alto, con muy pocas hojas, que son parecidas a las de la cebada.

Desde una tercia antes de su remate produce a trechos hasta la cumbre cuatro o cinco manojillos de espigas, y en cada uno, solas, cuatro o cinco, las cuales nacen de la caña en unos hilillos casi tan delgados como cerdas, de cuatro dedos de largo, en cuyo remate están las espigas con una docena de granos cada una, los cuales se parecen algo a los de la cebada, pero no están tan llenos como ellos. Puesta esta yerba sobre las heridas frescas las une y sana maravillosamente, y su zumo, mezclado con miel rosada, mundifica blanda y suavemente las llagas: y añadiendo a esto un poco de agua de llantén, aprovecha para las llagas de los ojos, porque las mundifica, encarna y cicatriza.
results.translation:
CHAPTER LIX

On the Choclla

Two species are found in these Indies of a certain herb that the Spaniards call cebadilla [little barley], on account of its resemblance to barley. One is native to New Spain and the other to this kingdom of Peru. The first puts forth a spike resembling that of wheat, a handspan or more in length; its stalk is likewise similar to that of wheat, somewhat thicker, brownish, and solid, about a man's height tall; it grows wild in the savannas without being sown. The grains are black, somewhat smaller than those of barley, and when ground they kill the worms [maggots] in the sores [saddle-galls] of beasts of burden, and likewise the chigoe fleas [niguas] that burrow into persons.

The other species of cebadilla is called choclla in the Aymara language; it is a prized fodder for livestock, and is sold at the inns [ventas] of the Collao region in place of alfalfa or green barley [alcacer]. It grows abundantly throughout all the provinces of the Collao; it puts forth a hollow stalk half again as slender as the other cebadilla, with no more than one or two nodes spaced along it, rising two cubits in height, with very few leaves, which resemble those of barley.

Starting about a third of the way below its tip, it produces at intervals up to its crown four or five small clusters of spikes, each cluster bearing only four or five spikes, which emerge from the stalk on little threads nearly as thin as bristles, four fingers in length, at whose ends are the spikes, each bearing about a dozen grains, which somewhat resemble those of barley but are not as full as them. When this herb is applied to fresh wounds it joins and heals them wonderfully; and its juice, mixed with rose honey [miel rosada], cleanses ulcers [llagas] gently and smoothly; and with the addition of a little plantain water [agua de llantén], it is beneficial for ulcers of the eyes, because it cleanses them, promotes the growth of new flesh, and causes them to cicatrize [scar over].

entities_mentioned.name: cebadilla (New Spain species)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Schoenocaulon officinale (sabadilla / sabadilla lily, Liliaceae/Melanthiaceae)
entities_mentioned.notes: The description of black grains used ground to kill maggots and chigoe fleas is consistent with sabadilla, a well-known insecticidal plant from Mexico. Conditions treated: maggots in animal wounds (mataduras); chigoe flea infestations in humans (niguas). Preparation: grains ground and applied topically.

entities_mentioned.name: choclla / cebadilla (Peru / Collao species)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Bromus catharticus or a related Andean grass species (Poaceae); possibly Festuca or Bromus sp.
entities_mentioned.notes: The Aymara name choclla and the description as a pasture grass of the Collao (Altiplano) with hollow, slender stalks and small clusters of spikes suggest a native Andean grass used as fodder. Conditions treated: fresh wounds (external application); ulcers/sores (topical); eye ulcers (topical with rose honey and plantain water). Preparation: (1) herb laid directly on fresh wounds; (2) juice mixed with rose honey applied to ulcers; (3) juice with rose honey and plantain water applied to eye ulcers.

entities_mentioned.name: miel rosada
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Rose honey (honey infused with rose petals — a standard Galenic pharmaceutical preparation)
entities_mentioned.notes: Standard compounding agent in early modern Spanish and colonial pharmacy.

entities_mentioned.name: agua de llantén
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Plantago major or P. lanceolata distillate/infusion (plantain water)
entities_mentioned.notes: Commonly used in colonial and European medicine as an eye and wound wash.

entities_mentioned.name: niguas
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Tunga penetrans (chigoe flea / jigger)
entities_mentioned.notes: Parasitic flea that burrows under human skin, especially the feet. Mentioned as a condition treated by the ground grains of the New Spain cebadilla.

results.translator_notes: 1. 'cades' in the source OCR ('los cudes') is almost certainly a misread of 'cuales' — corrected silently to 'los cuales.' 2. 'mataduras' refers specifically to saddle-galls or chafing wounds on pack animals; translated as 'sores' with clarification. 3. 'un estado de alto' is a period height measure roughly equivalent to a man's full height (~1.7–1.8 m). 4. 'alcacer' is young/green barley harvested for fodder, distinct from mature grain barley. 5. 'mundifica, encarna y cicatriza' is a technical triad from Galenic wound-healing theory: cleansing, granulation (flesh formation), and cicatrization; rendered in English with bracketed clarifications. 6. The final lines of the source show apparent printing/OCR artifacts ('íl', 'I'') which are page-end decorative or catchword remnants and carry no text content; omitted from translation.

results.title_inferred: The Guachanca plant: description, identification, and medicinal uses as a purgative
results.original_text:
CAPITULO LX

De la guachanca

Por parecerse las raíces desta yerba a las papas, las llaman los españoles popillas de purga. Crece la guachanca un palmo; hace los ramos tan delgados y más que el pezón de la hoja de la vid, redondos, lisos y tiernos, con pocas hojas hasta la cumbre, en que echa tres o cuatro juntas, las cuales, en el tamaño y talle, son semejantes a las del trébol. Su flor es tan pequeña como la del trébol o mostaza, a la cual sucede la semilla, que son unos granillos triangulares, tan grandes como los del culantro. Las raíces son como criadillas de tierra, blancas y con cáscara delgada y parda. Así de la raíz desta planta como de sus ramas partidas, brota un licor blanco como leche, parecido al que sale de la leche-trezna. Hay desta planta macho y hembra; ésta produce muchos tallos verdes con flor blanca que tira a colorada, y el macho echa su flor colorada. La raíz desta planta es una purga muy usada en todo el Perú. La hembra es más blanda en el purgar, menos bascosa y violenta. Administrase molida de media hasta una dracma en agua de cebada o de llantén, o en azúcar rosado o miel rosada, y el efecto que hace es purgar la melancolía, flema y cólera adusta. Suelen los indios tomar esta purga, en la cantidad que la experiencia les ha mostrado, en una escudilla de chicha, que es su vino; y luego, sin más guarda ni recato, se echan el vientre al sol y purgan muy bien y con facilidad. En la ciudad de Guánuco, diócesis de Lima, hacen cierta conserva destas raíces como la de membrillo, que, por gran regalo, se lleva a todas las partes deste reino; porque, sin dar vómitos ni bascas, purga maravillosamente. Demás desto, las ayudas hechas con el cocimiento destas raíces y hojas, con aceite, sal y azúcar, aprovechan contra los dolores de vientre e ijada y matan las lombrices. Llámase guachanca en la lengua aimará.
results.translation:
CHAPTER LX

On the Guachanca

Because the roots of this herb resemble potatoes [papas], the Spaniards call it 'purgative little potato' [popillas de purga]. The guachanca grows to a hand's breadth [palmo] in height; it produces branches as slender as, or more so than, the stalk of a grape leaf, round, smooth, and tender, with few leaves up to the top, where it puts forth three or four together, which in size and shape resemble those of clover. Its flower is as small as that of clover or mustard, followed by seeds that are small triangular granules, about the size of coriander seeds. The roots are like truffles [criadillas de tierra], white, with a thin, brownish skin. Both from the root of this plant and from its broken branches there flows a white milky sap, similar to that of the spurge [leche-trezna, i.e., Euphorbia]. There are male and female forms of this plant; the female produces many green stems with a white flower tending toward red, while the male bears a red flower. The root of this plant is a purgative widely used throughout Peru. The female is milder in its purging action, less nauseating and violent. It is administered ground, in a dose of half to one drachm [dracma], in barley water or plantain water, or in rose sugar or rose honey; and its effect is to purge melancholy [black bile], phlegm, and adust choler [cólera adusta]. The Indians are accustomed to take this purge, in whatever quantity experience has shown them to be appropriate, in a bowl of chicha, which is their wine; and then, with no further precaution or care, they expose their belly to the sun and purge very well and easily. In the city of Huánuco, diocese of Lima, a certain preserve is made from these roots in the manner of quince paste [conserva de membrillo], which is sent as a prized gift to all parts of this kingdom, because it purges wonderfully without causing vomiting or nausea. Furthermore, enemas [ayudas] prepared with the decoction of these roots and leaves, combined with oil, salt, and sugar, are beneficial against pains of the belly and flank [ijada], and kill intestinal worms [lombrices]. It is called guachanca in the Aymara language.

entities_mentioned.name: guachanca / popillas de purga
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Possibly Euphorbia huanchahama or another Euphorbiaceae species; some scholars suggest a species of Jatropha or Croton native to the Andes. The milky latex, purgative action, and tuberous roots are consistent with Euphorbiaceae.
entities_mentioned.notes: The Aymara name 'guachanca' and the described milky latex strongly suggest Euphorbiaceae. The tuberous root resembling a small potato and the male/female distinction complicate precise identification. Requires further botanical verification against Andean flora.

entities_mentioned.name: leche-trezna
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Euphorbia sp. (likely Euphorbia lathyris or a related New World spurge)
entities_mentioned.notes: Used here as a comparative reference for the milky latex. 'Lechetrezna' is the common Spanish name for various Euphorbia species.

entities_mentioned.name: chicha
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Fermented maize beer (Zea mays)
entities_mentioned.notes: Traditional Andean fermented beverage used here as a vehicle for administering the purgative.

entities_mentioned.name: conserva de guachanca (quince-style preserve)
entities_mentioned.notes: A pharmaceutical preparation made in Huánuco noted for its mild purgative effect without emesis; no modern equivalent identified.

results.translator_notes: 1. The source text has significant OCR damage throughout, particularly along the right margin; reconstructions are made where context is unambiguous (e.g., 'popillas' for 'po. i pillas', 'palmo' for 'palmo m I', 'tallos' for 'taBos I', 'flema' for 'fle-ma', 'indios' for 'infliaf', etc.). 2. 'dracma [sic]' appears in the source as 'drama [sic]'; this is a clear OCR/scribal error for 'dracma' (drachm), a standard apothecary weight. 3. 'cólera adusta' is a humoral medicine term referring to 'burnt' or adust choler, the most dangerous form of yellow bile; it is preserved in brackets in the translation for technical accuracy. 4. 'Ijada' refers to the flank or side, often used to describe kidney or colic pain. 5. 'Ayudas' in the medical context of the period refers specifically to enemas or clysters. 6. The city name 'Guannco' in the OCR is clearly 'Guánuco' (modern Huánuco, Peru).

results.title_inferred: On the Cunturire root (Contrayerba)
results.original_text:
CAPITULO LXI

De la Cunturire

Los indios del Perú llaman cunturire a la raíz nombrada de los españoles contrayerba. Nace esta yerba en las provincias de Tomina y Mizque; echa un ramillo pequeño con cuatro o cinco hojas cada uno, las cuales son tan largas como las del naranjo, pero más angostas y de más escuro verde. Sus raíces son pequeñas, menores que un dedo de la mano, ñudosas y muy semejantes al jengibre; son de temperamento caliente y seco en el segundo grado, de olor aromático, algún tanto amargas y mordicantes; valen contra todo veneno, a cuya causa las tienen los indios y españoles por la triaca del Perú. En tiempo de enfermedades contagiosas las echan en las tinajas del agua que han de beber, con algunos clavos y piedra bezar. Los polvos destas raíces son abstergentes o mundificantes de las llagas, y dados por la boca con los de la piedra bezar en agua de azahar o de escorzonera y un poco de triaca toledana, son contra todo veneno, alegran el corazón y quitan la melancolía y tristezas; y mezclados con triaca, cebollas y ajos, y aplicados sobre las mordeduras de víboras o de otros animales emponzoñados, quitan la fuerza del veneno y aseguran la parte. Los indios de la provincia de Mizque llaman a esta yerba huanluqui.
results.translation:
CHAPTER LXI

On the Cunturire

The Indians of Peru call cunturire the root known among the Spaniards as contrayerba [counterherb / antidote herb]. This plant grows in the provinces of Tomina and Mizque; it puts forth small branches bearing four or five leaves each, which are as long as those of the orange tree, but narrower and of a darker green. Its roots are small, no larger than a finger of the hand, knotted, and very similar to ginger; they are of a hot and dry temperament in the second degree [humoral classification], of aromatic scent, somewhat bitter and mordant [biting to the taste]; they are effective against all poisons, for which reason both the Indians and the Spaniards regard them as the theriac [universal antidote] of Peru. During times of contagious illness, they cast them into the water jars from which people are to drink, together with some cloves and bezoar stone. The powders of these roots are abstergent and cleansing [mundificant] of wounds; and when given by mouth together with powder of bezoar stone in orange-blossom water or scorzonera water and a little Toledan theriac, they serve against all poisons, gladden the heart, and dispel melancholy and sadness. Mixed with theriac, onions, and garlic, and applied over the bites of vipers or other venomous creatures, they draw out the force of the poison and secure [protect] the affected part. The Indians of the province of Mizque call this herb huanluqui.

entities_mentioned.name: Cunturire / Contrayerba / Huanluqui
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Dorstenia contrayerva (Moraceae) or a closely related Dorstenia species
entities_mentioned.notes: The name 'contrayerba' was applied to several antidote plants in colonial Latin America; the most commonly cited candidate is Dorstenia contrayerva. The knotted, ginger-like roots and aromatic, bitter taste are consistent with this identification. The Quechua name 'cunturire' and the Mizque regional name 'huanluqui' are both noted by Cobo.

entities_mentioned.name: Piedra bezar (Bezoar stone)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Concretions (gastroliths) from the gastrointestinal tracts of Andean camelids or other animals, used as antidotes
entities_mentioned.notes: A standard component of colonial antidote formulations; combined here with contrayerba powder in oral and topical preparations.

entities_mentioned.name: Triaca toledana (Toledan theriac)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Theriac/Theriaca — a complex polypharmaceutical antidote of Galenic tradition, produced in Toledo
entities_mentioned.notes: A preparation descended from classical 'Theriaca Andromachi,' widely used in Spanish colonial medicine as a universal antidote and alexipharmic.

entities_mentioned.name: Agua de azahar (Orange-blossom water)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Distilled water of Citrus aurantium blossoms
entities_mentioned.notes: Used here as a medicinal vehicle (menstruum) for oral administration of antidote powder.

entities_mentioned.name: Agua de escorzonera (Scorzonera water)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Decoction or distillate of Scorzonera hispanica (black salsify), also used as an alexipharmic
entities_mentioned.notes: Scorzonera was itself regarded as an antidote plant in early modern European medicine; its water served as a vehicle here.

results.translator_notes:
1. OCR rendered 'contrayerba' as 'cmtrayerba' — silently corrected. 'cunturire' in the chapter heading appeared as 'CUTI tur ir e' — corrected to 'Cunturire' from context. 'piedra' followed by a line break and capitalized 'Los' strongly suggests 'piedra bezar' (bezoar stone) with 'bezar' lost to OCR; restored in the cleaned text. The final indigenous name appeared as 'hqmluquL' — corrected to 'huanluqui' based on phonological plausibility and manuscript context.
2. Humoral temperature characterization ('hot and dry in the second degree') follows standard Galenic pharmacological grading.
3. The dual nomenclature (cunturire vs. huanluqui) may reflect different ethnic or linguistic groups in the Mizque/Tomina region (likely Quechua vs. a local language).
4. The passage contains three distinct preparation/application methods: (a) prophylactic infusion in drinking water with cloves and bezoar; (b) oral powder compound with bezoar, orange-blossom water, scorzonera water, and Toledan theriac; (c) topical poultice with theriac, onion, and garlic for venomous bites.

results.title_inferred: On the Guaki and Chapichapi plants: medicinal uses
results.original_text:
CAPITULO LXII
De la guaki

Llaman guahi los indios peruanos a una raíz muy conocida y estimada dellos; es muy amarga y de profundo olor no enfadoso; su temperamento es caliente y seco. Es tan parecida esta yerba a la que Dioscórides llama aristoquia luenga, que no parece distinguirse della. Usan los indios de su raíz para muchas enfermedades; con ella, mascada, se refriegan cualquiera parte del cuerpo dolorosa. Su cocimiento resuelve toda ventosidad, limpia el vientre y da calor a las partes frías, y bebido de ordinario, quita la ictericia; y hecho el cocimiento con la raíz desta yerba, palo santo, polipodio y algunas hojas de sen, bebido de ordinario, quita cualquiera dolor de las junturas y aprovecha contra toda pasión de bubas; y haciendo untura con sus polvos mezclados con enjundia de gallina, se alargan los nervios encogidos; y el cocimiento desta raíz con mostaza, eneldo, romero y miel de abejas, tomado tibio en ayunas, vale contra el dolor de ijada, contra la cólica y contra los azogados.

CAPITULO LXIII
De la chapichapi

Esta es una planta pequeña que produce unas ramillas cortas tendidas por el suelo, con muchas hojas menudas y unas flores olorosas entre blancas y moradas; su raíz es tan larga como un dedo de la mano, colorada y de sustancia estíptica y confortante, de temperamento caliente y seco. Tiene virtud esta raíz de constipar o apretar, a cuya causa su zumo, aplicado sobre las heridas recientes, las junta y une; y la raíz traída de ordinario en la boca, aprieta, conserva y limpia la dentadura; y el mismo efecto y con mayor fuerza hace su cocimiento con vino y con las hojas de coca, de molle y de pincopinco. El agua desta raíz, si hierve con pincopinco, bebida de ordinario, conforta el estómago, estanca la demasiada sangre menstrua y la que sale por la vía de la orina; y hecho lamedor del cocimiento que haya hervido bien con las dichas cosas y tomado a menudo, estanca la vena rota del pecho y las cámaras de sangre. Los polvos de esta raíz aprovechan en todos los lavatorios y baños estípticos. Allende de lo dicho, se aprovechan della los indios en la tintura de las lanas coloradas.
results.translation:
CHAPTER LXII
On the Guaki

The Peruvian Indians call by the name guahi a root that is very well known and esteemed among them. It is very bitter and has a deep but not unpleasant smell; its temperament [complexion] is hot and dry. This herb so closely resembles what Dioscorides calls aristolochia longa [long birthwort] that it seems indistinguishable from it. The Indians use its root for many ailments; when chewed, they rub it on any painful part of the body. Its decoction [cocimiento] dispels all flatulence [ventosidad], cleanses the bowels, and warms cold parts of the body; drunk regularly, it cures jaundice [ictericia]. A decoction made from the root of this herb together with guaiacum [palo santo], polypody [polipodio], and some senna leaves [hojas de sen], drunk regularly, relieves all pain of the joints and is beneficial against the disease of buboes [pasión de bubas; i.e., syphilitic swellings]. An ointment [untura] made from its powder mixed with chicken fat [enjundia de gallina] lengthens and loosens contracted sinews [nervios encogidos]. A decoction of this root with mustard, dill, rosemary, and honey, taken warm on an empty stomach, is effective against flank pain [dolor de ijada], colic, and those afflicted by mercury poisoning [los azogados].

CHAPTER LXIII
On the Chapichapi

This is a small plant that puts forth short little branches spread along the ground, with many small leaves and fragrant flowers ranging from white to purple. Its root is about as long as a finger, reddish in color, and of an astringent [estíptica] and strengthening substance, with a hot and dry temperament. This root has the virtue of binding and tightening [constipar o apretar]; for this reason, its juice applied to fresh wounds draws and closes them together. Carrying the root habitually in the mouth tightens, preserves, and cleans the teeth; and a decoction of it with wine and with the leaves of coca [coca], molle [molle], and pincopinco [pincopinco] achieves the same effect with greater force. The water of this root, boiled with pincopinco and drunk regularly, fortifies the stomach, arrests excessive menstrual bleeding, and stops blood passed through the urinary tract. A syrup [lamedor] made from the decoction well boiled with the aforementioned ingredients, taken frequently, arrests hemorrhage from a ruptured vessel in the chest [vena rota del pecho] and bloody dysentery [cámaras de sangre]. The powder of this root is also useful in all astringent washes [lavatorios] and baths. Beyond what has been said, the Indians also make use of it in dyeing red wool.

entities_mentioned.name: guaki (guahi)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Aristolochia species, possibly Aristolochia bridgesii or a closely related South American birthwort
entities_mentioned.notes: Cobo explicitly equates it with Dioscorides' aristolochia longa (long birthwort). Conditions treated: pain (topical, by chewing and rubbing), flatulence, constipation, jaundice, joint pain, syphilitic buboes, contracted sinews, flank pain, colic, mercury poisoning. Preparations: chewed root (topical rub), decoction drunk regularly, ointment with chicken fat, compound decoction with guaiacum/polypody/senna, compound decoction with mustard/dill/rosemary/honey taken warm on empty stomach.

entities_mentioned.name: palo santo
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Guaiacum officinale or Guaiacum sanctum (guaiacum / lignum vitae)
entities_mentioned.notes: Used in compound decoction for joint pain and syphilis.

entities_mentioned.name: polipodio
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Polypodium calaguala or related Andean polypody fern
entities_mentioned.notes: Used in compound decoction for joint pain and syphilis.

entities_mentioned.name: sen (hojas de sen)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Senna alexandrina (senna leaves)
entities_mentioned.notes: Used in compound decoction; known laxative and purgative.

entities_mentioned.name: chapichapi
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Possibly Monnina species or another Andean astringent-rooted plant; identification uncertain
entities_mentioned.notes: Described as a small ground-creeping plant with small leaves and white-to-purple fragrant flowers; red astringent root about a finger's length. Conditions treated: fresh wounds (juice topical), dental care (chewing root), excessive menstrual bleeding, hematuria, chest hemorrhage, bloody dysentery, stomach weakness. Also used as a red wool dye. Preparations: fresh juice (topical), root chewed, compound decoction with wine/coca/molle/pincopinco, decoction with pincopinco drunk regularly, syrup (lamedor), powder for astringent washes and baths.

entities_mentioned.name: coca
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Erythroxylum coca
entities_mentioned.notes: Leaves used in compound decoction with chapichapi.

entities_mentioned.name: molle
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Schinus molle (Peruvian peppertree)
entities_mentioned.notes: Leaves used in compound decoction with chapichapi.

entities_mentioned.name: pincopinco
