entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Ephedra americana or a related Andean Ephedra species
entities_mentioned.notes: Used with chapichapi in decoctions; appears repeatedly in Andean materia medica contexts consistent with Ephedra.

results.translator_notes: 1. 'Ictericia' was rendered in the OCR as 'itiricia'; silently corrected. 2. 'Aristoquia luenga' in the OCR (with caret artifacts) is clearly 'aristoloquia luenga' (Dioscorides' aristolochia longa); corrected. 3. 'Los azogados' refers specifically to individuals suffering from mercury poisoning, a significant hazard in colonial Andean silver and mercury mining; rendered accordingly. 4. 'Cámaras de sangre' is a period term for bloody dysentery/bloody stools; translated as 'bloody dysentery.' 5. 'Vena rota del pecho' ('ruptured vessel of the chest') likely refers to hemoptysis or pulmonary hemorrhage. 6. 'Lamedor' is a period pharmaceutical term for a thick medicinal syrup or electuary meant to be licked or swallowed slowly; rendered as 'syrup [lamedor].' 7. The final sentence about red wool dyeing is notable as a non-medical use recorded alongside medicinal applications, consistent with Andean ethnobotanical practices.

results.title_inferred: On the Viñaygnayna plant (Ever-Young herb)
results.original_text:
CAPITULO LXIV
De la Viñaygnayna

Viñaygnayna quiere decir en la lengua de los indios peruanos, siempre mozo, y dan este nombre a una yerba de tallos y hojas pequeñas y raíces coloradas, la cual es de complexión templada y consta de partes algo estípticas. Sus raíces, majadas, tienen facultad de consolidar las heridas frescas, y si el zumo de las hojas se mezcla con aguardiente, mundifica y deseca las llagas húmedas. Traídas las raíces en la boca, confortan la dentadura, y el agua cocida con ellas, bebida de ordinario, conforta el estómago y estanca la sangre que suele salir por la vía de la orina.
results.translation:
CHAPTER LXIV
On the Viñaygnayna

Viñaygnayna means in the language of the Peruvian Indians 'ever-young' [siempre mozo], and they give this name to an herb with small stems and leaves and red roots, which is of a temperate complexion [complexión templada] and consists of somewhat astringent [estípticas] parts. Its roots, when pounded, have the power to consolidate [consolidar] fresh wounds; and if the juice of the leaves is mixed with aguardiente [distilled spirits], it cleanses [mundifica] and dries out moist sores [llagas húmedas]. When the roots are held in the mouth, they strengthen the teeth; and the water in which they have been boiled, taken regularly as a drink, strengthens the stomach and stanches blood that customarily passes by way of the urine.

entities_mentioned.name: Viñaygnayna
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Possibly Monnina salicifolia or a related Polygalaceae species; alternatively suggested as Peperomia galioides (Piperaceae), which is known in Andean ethnobotany as 'siempreviva' with similar wound-healing uses
entities_mentioned.notes: The name translates as 'ever-young' in Quechua (wiñay = always/forever; wayna = young man). Key identifying features given: small stems and leaves, red/colored roots, astringent quality. Medical uses include wound consolidation, treatment of moist ulcers, dental strengthening, stomachic, and hemostasis for hematuria. Prepared as: pounded roots applied topically; leaf juice mixed with aguardiente for ulcers; roots chewed or held in mouth; decoction of roots taken as drink. Preparation method: topical poultice (pounded roots), infusion/decoction (roots boiled in water), oral maceration (roots chewed), liquid admixture (leaf juice with aguardiente).

entities_mentioned.name: aguardiente
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Distilled alcoholic spirit (generic term)
entities_mentioned.notes: Used here as a vehicle/solvent for leaf juice to treat moist sores; likely refers to locally produced cane or grape spirit.

results.translator_notes: Two OCR errors silently corrected: '<leseca' → 'deseca' (dries out); 'l>eluda' → 'bebida' (drunk/taken as a drink); 'hoca' → 'boca' (mouth). The chapter heading in the original reads 'Vi fi a y g u a y n a' with artifactual spacing, normalized to 'Viñaygnayna'. The term 'mundifica' is a standard Galenic medical term meaning to cleanse or purify a wound; retained in brackets in translation. The phrase 'complexión templada' and 'partes estípticas' are Galenic humoral descriptors preserved with bracketed glosses.

results.title_inferred: On the Pupusa and the Pullapulla: Two Medicinal Plants
results.original_text:
CAPITULO LXV
De la pupusa

Esta es una yerba pequeña de tallos lisos algo colorados, cuyas hojas, que algunos tallos suelen tener, son como agraz que sale de la cierne, algo prolongadas, a manera de arroz; acaba el tallo en una roseta de muchas hojillas agregadas, las cuales, llegando a maduración, paran en una flocadura que salta y se pega fácilmente a la ropa. Tiene un profundo olor que a veces enfada, y más cuando está más verde la yerba. Su temperamento es caliente en el tercero grado, y seco en el segundo; es gran cáustico de las llagas, y aplicada sobre cualquiera dolor procedido de causa fría, lo quita, y resuelve cualquiera hinchazón.

CAPITULO LXVI
De la pullapulla

La pullapulla es una cebollita pequeña muy blanca tan mucilaginosa o pegajosa que, majada, sirve de almidón. Es caliente y húmeda, y así, mezclada con unto sin sal, tiene facultad de madurar las apostemas; y mezclada con enjundia, ablanda y resuelve los tumores.
results.translation:
CHAPTER LXV
On the Pupusa

This is a small herb with smooth, somewhat reddish stems, whose leaves—which some stems tend to bear—resemble unripe grape clusters emerging from the blossom, somewhat elongated, in the manner of rice. The stem ends in a rosette of many small clustered leaflets, which, upon reaching maturity, turn into a flocculent [feathery, burr-like] mass that leaps off and clings easily to clothing. It has a penetrating odor that is at times disagreeable, and more so when the herb is greener. Its [humoral] temperament is hot in the third degree and dry in the second. It is a powerful caustic [agent] for wounds [llagas], and when applied over any pain arising from a cold cause, it relieves it and disperses any swelling.

CHAPTER LXVI
On the Pullapulla

The pullapulla is a small, very white bulb [cebollita] so mucilaginous or sticky that, when crushed, it serves as starch. It is hot and moist in temperament, and therefore, mixed with unsalted lard [unto sin sal], it has the faculty of ripening [maturing] apostemes [abscesses]; and mixed with fat drippings [enjundia], it softens and resolves tumors.

entities_mentioned.name: pupusa
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Possibly a species of Galinsoga or Bidens (Asteraceae), given the burr-like seed dispersal, rosette form, and strong odor; alternatively a Tagetes sp. (marigold family). Identification uncertain.
entities_mentioned.notes: Described as a small herb with reddish stems, rice-like elongated leaves, a rosette that matures into a clinging, flocculent structure. Strong odor. Classified as hot (3rd degree) and dry (2nd degree) in Galenic humoral terms. Used topically as a caustic for wounds, to relieve pain from 'cold causes,' and to resolve swellings. No specific preparation method is given beyond topical application.

entities_mentioned.name: pullapulla
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Possibly Stenomesson or Nothoscordum sp. (Amaryllidaceae), or another Andean mucilaginous bulb. Some scholars associate 'pullapulla' with Monnina or mucilaginous Andean plants, but a bulb form points toward Amaryllidaceae. Identification uncertain.
entities_mentioned.notes: Described as a small, very white, highly mucilaginous bulb that when crushed can substitute for starch. Classified as hot and moist. Two preparations noted: (1) mixed with unsalted lard [unto sin sal] to ripen/mature apostemes [abscesses]; (2) mixed with fat drippings [enjundia] to soften and resolve tumors. 'Pullapulla' may be a Quechua or Aymara name.

results.translator_notes: Several OCR artifacts were silently corrected: 'yerha' → 'yerba'; 'profundo' confirmed (meaning 'deep/strong' regarding odor); 'temtíeramento' → 'temperamento'; 'hojillas' restored from 'liojillas'; 'el tallo' restored from 'd tallo'; 'mucilaginosa' restored from 'miieilaginosa'; 'malada' → 'majada' (crushed/ground); 'miaelaginosa' variant also corrected. The chapter number LXVT has been silently corrected to LXVI. The humoral grading system (degrees of heat, cold, moisture, dryness) follows standard Galenic-Arabic medical convention as transmitted through Spanish colonial medical writing. 'Flocadura' is a rare term meaning a fringe or flocculent mass—here describing the mature seed head that disperses and clings to fabric, suggesting a species with pappus-tipped or hooked achenes.

results.title_inferred: The herb perebecenu
results.original_text:
CAPITULO LXVII

De la yerba perebecenu

Llaman así en la lengua de la isla Española a una yerba medicinal. Es tan alta como un hombre, sus tallos son morados o rojos, y las hojas, con las puntas del mismo color, siendo verdes en lo restante; son puntiagudas, de hechura de hierro de lanza. Echa unas flores coloradas, luengas y a manojos como el hinojo y el flueco, pero apartadas unas de otras. El cocimiento de los cogollos desta yerba es bueno para curar llagas.
results.translation:
CHAPTER LXVII

On the herb perebecenu

This is the name given in the language of the island of Hispaniola to a medicinal herb. It stands as tall as a man; its stems are purple or red, and the leaves are tipped with the same color while remaining green over the rest of their surface. They are pointed, shaped like the head of a lance. It produces red flowers, elongated and borne in small clusters [manojos] like those of fennel and tassel-like plants, but set apart from one another. A decoction [cocimiento] of the young shoots [cogollos] of this herb is good for healing wounds [llagas].

entities_mentioned.name: perebecenu
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Possibly Acalypha sp. or Amaranthus sp.; the purple-red stems, lance-shaped leaves, and elongated clustered flowers are suggestive of a member of Amaranthaceae or Euphorbiaceae native to the Caribbean, but a precise identification cannot be made with confidence
entities_mentioned.notes: The name 'perebecenu' is from the Taíno or another indigenous language of Hispaniola. The plant's described morphology — height of a man, reddish-purple stems and leaf tips, green body of leaf, lance-shaped (lanceolate) leaves, red elongated flowers in spaced clusters resembling fennel umbels — could fit several genera. Wound-healing use (llagas) is the stated therapeutic application. Preparation: decoction of the young shoots (cogollos).

entities_mentioned.name: hinojo (fennel)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Foeniculum vulgare
entities_mentioned.notes: Used by Cobo as a morphological comparator for the flower clusters, not as a medicinal subject of this chapter.

results.translator_notes: 'flueco' in the original may be an OCR rendering of 'fleco' (tassel/fringe) used as a second comparator for the flower form, or possibly a variant spelling of a plant name; treated here as a descriptive term meaning tassel-like or fringe-like. 'Mañojitos' corrected silently to 'manojos' (small bunches/clusters), which is the clearly intended word. 'Yerlia' in the OCR source corrected silently to 'yerba.' The medical extraction is: Name: perebecenu; Ailment: wounds (llagas); Preparation: decoction (cocimiento) of young shoots (cogollos).

results.title_inferred: On the camina plant: its properties and medicinal uses
results.original_text:
CAPITULO LXVIII
De la camina

La camina es una yerba de un codo en alto, cuyas hojas son muy parecidas a las del apio. Echa unas flores como las del eneldo; huele esta planta con fragancia como la del apio. Su temperamento es caliente y seco. Mascada esta yerba, tiene partes sutiles, pica la lengua y la deja áspera por un buen rato. Tiene virtud de resolver, y su zumo mundifica las llagas. Su cocimiento con un poco de miel de abejas, dado a beber en ayunas, quiebra la piedra de riñones y vejiga; y dando baño con el cocimiento de sus raíces en la ciática, resuelve el humor y quita el dolor. Y si a este cocimiento se añade sal y bañan con él las piernas gotosas, las deshincha y seca.
results.translation:
CHAPTER LXVIII
On the camina

The camina is an herb about one cubit [approximately 18 inches] in height, whose leaves closely resemble those of celery [apio]. It produces flowers like those of dill [eneldo]; the plant smells fragrant, much like celery. Its temperament [complexion, in Galenic humoral terms] is hot and dry. When this herb is chewed, it has subtle [penetrating] properties, stings the tongue, and leaves it rough for a good while. It has the virtue of resolving [dispersing humors], and its juice cleanses [mundifies] wounds. A decoction of it with a little honey from bees, given to drink on an empty stomach, breaks kidney stones and bladder stones; and bathing with a decoction of its roots for sciatica resolves the humor [disperses the accumulated fluid or morbid matter] and relieves pain. And if salt is added to this decoction and the gouty legs are bathed in it, it reduces the swelling and dries them.

entities_mentioned.name: camina
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Possibly a species of Apium, Ammi, or related Apiaceae (umbelliferous) plant native to the Americas; possibly Ammi visnaga or a related taxon, or alternatively a local Andean umbellifer
entities_mentioned.notes: Cobo's description—cubit-tall herb, celery-like leaves, dill-like flowers, celery-like fragrance, hot and dry temperament, tongue-burning when chewed—strongly suggests an umbelliferous (Apiaceae) plant. The name 'camina' does not correspond with certainty to any well-documented modern species. Some scholars have tentatively linked it to Andean Apiaceae such as Lilaeopsis or Ottoa, but identification remains uncertain. Medicinal uses recorded: wound cleansing (topical juice), kidney and bladder stone dissolution (decoction with honey, taken fasting), sciatica relief (root decoction bath), gout/leg swelling reduction (salted decoction bath).

entities_mentioned.name: apio
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Apium graveolens (celery or wild celery)
entities_mentioned.notes: Used as a morphological and olfactory comparator, not as a treatment subject.

entities_mentioned.name: eneldo
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Anethum graveolens (dill)
entities_mentioned.notes: Used as a floral morphology comparator only.

results.translator_notes: Several OCR artifacts were silently corrected: 'jior' → 'por'; '>' (HTML entity) → '>'; broken line-initial pipe characters '|' and 'I' were treated as OCR artifacts from column-rule or margin marks and removed. The phrase 'tiene virtud de resolver' employs standard Galenic pharmacological terminology ('resolvente'), meaning the capacity to disperse or dissolve accumulated humors—rendered as 'virtue of resolving' to preserve the period register. 'Mundifica' (from Latin mundificare) is a technical medical term meaning to cleanse or purify a wound; retained in brackets in the translation. 'Temperamento caliente y seco' reflects Galenic humoral classification and is flagged with a bracketed note.

results.title_inferred: On the chanchachaucha plant and its use for swellings of the spleen, liver, and related organs
results.original_text:
CAPITULO LXIX

De la chanchachaucha

Esta es una yerba que nace en lugares húmedos y cenagosos, pequeña y sus hojas aparradas por el suelo, en círculo redondo, que hacen figura de estrella; son en la forma como las del llantén, aunque más pequeñas; el tronco de donde las hojas nacen es morado.
Usan los indios del cocimiento desta yerba, tomado caliente en ayunas por diez o doce días, cuando se sienten con hinchazones en el bazo, hígado y más; y demás desto, al cabo del uso del cocimiento, se suelen purgar con [text continues].
results.translation:
CHAPTER LXIX

On the chanchachaucha

This is a small herb that grows in damp and marshy places, with its leaves spread flat upon the ground in a round circle forming the shape of a star. They resemble in form the leaves of plantain [llantén], though smaller; the stem from which the leaves arise is purple [morado].
The Indians make use of a decoction [cocimiento] of this herb, taken hot on an empty stomach [en ayunas] for ten or twelve days, when they feel swellings in the spleen, liver, and related organs [bazo, hígado, y más]; and beyond this, at the end of the course of the decoction, they are accustomed to purging with [text continues].

entities_mentioned.name: chanchachaucha
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Possibly a species of Plantago or a rosette-forming herb in the family Plantaginaceae or Asteraceae, native to Andean wetland habitats; precise identification uncertain
entities_mentioned.notes: The Quechua name 'chanchachaucha' is not confidently matched to a modern binomial. The description — small, rosette-forming, star-shaped leaf arrangement, purple stem, grows in humid/marshy ground, leaves resembling plantain — is suggestive of a Plantago sp. or possibly Hydrocotyle (Apiaceae), but insufficient for confident identification.

entities_mentioned.name: llantén
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Plantago major or related Plantago spp.
entities_mentioned.notes: Used here only as a morphological comparator for leaf shape, not as the subject herb.

results.translator_notes: The segment is truncated mid-sentence at the page break ('se suelen purgar con' — 'they are accustomed to purging with'), and the continuation is not included in the source. The OCR presents several corrupted readings: 'lina' corrected to 'una'; 'aparradas' retained as likely correct (meaning 'spread out/flattened'); 'fW llantén' corrected to 'del llantén'; 'pef|ueñ.as' corrected to 'pequeñas'; 'e.s niorado' corrected to 'es morado'; 'tomado calientfí en avunas po*' corrected to 'tomado caliente en ayunas por'. The phrase 'bazo, hígado y ma-' is cut off by the page break, reconstructed as 'bazo, hígado y más' (spleen, liver, and others/more), which is consistent with typical humoral-era language for abdominal organ complaints. Medical use: swellings (edema/inflammation) of the spleen and liver, treated by hot decoction taken fasting for 10–12 days, followed by a purgative.

results.title_inferred: On the plant called Suelda Consuelda
results.original_text:
CAPITULO LXX
De la suelda consuelda

A la planta llamada de los españoles suelda consuelda, nombran los indios del Perú, en la lengua general, chuleorrhuko, y en Cochabamba, diócesis de Charcas, auquilagna, que quiere decir padre de las yerbas. La mata es pequeña y sus tallos muy parecidos a los sarmientos, salvo que son más tiernos. Su hoja asimismo es muy semejante a la de la vid, sino que es menor y más grasosa que ella, y hojas y tallos son muy tiernos y agrios como vinagre. Echa una pequeña flor purpúrea y vistosa, que no da de sí olor alguno. Sus raíces son como criadillas de tierra, por de fuera pardiscas, de color de tierra, y por de dentro encarnadas, muy tiernas, mucilaginosas y austeras al gusto. Echadas dos o tres gotas tibias del zumo de sus hojas en el oído doloroso o sordo, aprovechan grandemente; y asimismo, aplicado en cualquiera inflamación, la remite y templa. Los polvos de sus raíces, tomados en agua de llantén, aprovechan contra las caídas, y tomados con lamedor de rosas secas o de arrayán, sueldan las venas rotas en el pecho o ventrículo del estómago, y son contra las úlceras de las disenterias que se hacen en las tripas. Echado el polvo desta raíz seca sobre las heridas o llagas, las deseca y cicatriza, y el cocimiento desta raíz con las cabezuelas de rosas y alumbre, tomado a menudo, conforta y afija la dentadura y la dispone a que se conserve. Finalmente, las hojas desta yerba, aplicadas sobre la cabeza, templan y quitan el dolor de ella causado del sol.
results.translation:
CHAPTER LXX
On the plant called Suelda Consuelda ["mend-and-heal"]

The plant called by the Spaniards suelda consuelda is named by the Indians of Peru, in the general language [Quechua], chuleorrhuko, and in Cochabamba, in the diocese of Charcas, auquilagna, which means "father of herbs." The shrub is small and its stems very similar to vine shoots [sarmientos], except that they are more tender. Its leaf is likewise very similar to that of the grapevine, but smaller and more greasy than it, and both leaves and stems are very tender and tart like vinegar. It produces a small, purple, and showy flower that gives off no fragrance whatsoever. Its roots are like truffles [criadillas de tierra], brownish on the outside, earth-colored, and reddish-pink on the inside, very tender, mucilaginous, and astringent to the taste.

Instilling two or three warm drops of the juice of its leaves into an ear that is painful or deaf is of great benefit; and likewise, applied to any inflammation, it reduces and soothes it. The powders of its roots, taken in plantain water [agua de llantén], are beneficial against falls [internal injuries from blows or falls], and taken with a syrup [lamedor] of dried roses or of myrtle, they knit together [sueldan] the broken veins in the chest or ventricle of the stomach, and are effective against the ulcers of dysenteries that form in the intestines. Dusting the powder of this dried root over wounds or sores dries them out and causes them to heal over [cicatriza], and a decoction of this root with rose heads and alum [alumbre], taken frequently, strengthens and fixes the teeth and disposes them to remain in good health. Finally, the leaves of this herb, applied over the head, soothe and relieve the pain in it caused by the sun.

entities_mentioned.name: suelda consuelda
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Possibly Oxalis tuberosa (oca) or a related Oxalis species; alternatively suggested as Begonia species or a Rumex species. The description of grapevine-like leaves, sour/vinegar taste, tuberous roots pinkish inside, and purple flowers is most consistent with a tuberous Oxalis, though the wound-healing and consolidant properties align with the folk name echoing comfrey (Symphytum officinale).
entities_mentioned.notes: The name 'suelda consuelda' is a Spanish rendering evoking 'comfrey' (consuelda, Symphytum officinale), but the morphological description—especially the vinegar-tart taste, vine-like leaves, and tuberous root—does not match comfrey. Multiple Andean species have been suggested. Quechua name 'chuleorrhuko' and Cochabamba name 'auquilagna' may assist further identification.

entities_mentioned.name: llantén
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Plantago major (common plantain) or Plantago lanceolata
entities_mentioned.notes: Used here as an aqueous vehicle (agua de llantén) for administering the root powder.

entities_mentioned.name: arrayán
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Myrtus communis (common myrtle) or a local Andean Myrcia/Luma species
entities_mentioned.notes: Used in a syrup preparation (lamedor de arrayán) to consolidate broken internal veins.

entities_mentioned.name: alumbre
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Alum (potassium aluminum sulfate)
entities_mentioned.notes: Used as an astringent in the dental decoction alongside rose heads.

results.translator_notes:
1. The OCR produced significant noise (broken characters, split words, erroneous special characters); all corrections have been silently applied where the intended word was unambiguous. No passages were judged genuinely illegible.
2. 'Lamedor' is a period pharmaceutical term for a thick medicinal syrup meant to be licked rather than swallowed in a draught; rendered as 'syrup [lamedor]' to preserve the technical sense.
3. 'Criadillas de tierra' literally means 'earth sweetbreads' and refers colloquially to truffles or similarly shaped underground tubers.
4. 'Caídas' in Cobo's medical vocabulary typically denotes internal trauma or contusions from falls, not the act of falling itself.
5. The Quechua names 'chuleorrhuko' and 'auquilagna' are transcribed as they appear in the cleaned text; the OCR rendering of the Quechua is uncertain and may be corrupt.
6. Extraction summary — Name: suelda consuelda / chuleorrhuko / auquilagna; Modern ID: uncertain, possibly Oxalis sp.; Conditions treated: earache, deafness, inflammation, internal injuries, broken chest veins, stomach ulcers, dysenteric intestinal ulcers, wounds, dental weakness, sun-induced headache; Preparations: (a) warm juice drops instilled in ear, (b) root powder in plantain water taken internally, (c) root powder with rose or myrtle syrup taken internally, (d) dried root powder dusted on wounds, (e) decoction of root with rose heads and alum taken as mouth rinse/drink, (f) leaves applied topically to head.

results.title_inferred: On the cahegíi herb: uses for uterine ailments and fertility
results.original_text:
CAPITULO LXXI
De la cahegíi

La cahegíi es una yerba a manera de [ILLEGIBLE: brief description lost to OCR], de complexión caliente. Adminístranla los indios a las mujeres enfermas de la madre, comida caliente o puesta en el estómago; y también dicen que ayudan a la fecundidad de las mujeres.
results.translation:
CHAPTER LXXI
On the cahegíi

The cahegíi is an herb of [ILLEGIBLE: plant form or resemblance lost to OCR/damage], of a hot complexion [i.e., warm in humoral quality]. The Indians administer it to women suffering from ailments of the womb [madre], eaten warm or applied to the stomach; and they also say that it aids the fertility of women.

entities_mentioned.name: cahegíi
entities_mentioned.notes: The plant name 'cahegíi' does not correspond to a readily identifiable species with confidence. The name may be a transcription of an indigenous (possibly Quechua or Aymara) term. The text describing its physical form is lost to OCR corruption, preventing morphological identification. Described as 'hot' in humoral terms and used gynecologically.

entities_mentioned.name: madre (womb ailments)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: uterine or gynecological disorders (general)
entities_mentioned.notes: 'Enfermedades de la madre' is a standard colonial Spanish phrase encompassing a range of uterine complaints including menstrual irregularity, prolapse, or hysteria in the humoral sense.

results.translator_notes: The opening lines of the chapter body are severely corrupted by OCR: the fragment 'ti mbega es una yerba a manera de (le complexión caliente. Atdí- j los indios a las mujeres enfermas' clearly conflates multiple lines. 'ti mbega' is almost certainly 'La cahegíi'; 'Atdí-' is likely 'Adminístranla' or similar verb. The reconstructed original text above reflects the most plausible reading. The physical description of the plant (what it resembles in form) appears to have been entirely lost in the OCR corruption between 'a manera de' and 'de complexión caliente.' Preparation methods: oral ingestion (eaten warm) and topical/poultice application to the abdomen. Indicated conditions: uterine illness and female infertility.

results.title_inferred: On the Anchariipa herb: urological and dermatological uses
results.original_text:
CAPITULO LXXII
De la anchariipa

Esta es una yerba pequeña que produce unas varillas muy lisas y derechas; su temperamento es caliente y seco; aprovéchanse los indios destes varillas, metiéndolas por la vía de la orina cuando se sienten con alguna carnosidad, porque sin dolor la extirpan y gastan. El cocimiento desta yerba usado a menudo en las llagas muy maliciosas, las corrige, mundifica, deseca y encarna; y la yerba majada con verdolagas, un poco de vinagre y oropimente, extirpa toda verruga y las películas de los lamparones o lobanillos.
results.translation:
CHAPTER LXXII
On the anchariipa

This is a small herb that produces very smooth and straight little stalks [varillas]. Its temperament is hot and dry. The Indians make use of these stalks by inserting them through the urinary passage [vía de la orina] when they feel some fleshy growth [carnosidad] there, for they thereby extirpate and dissolve it without pain. A decoction [cocimiento] of this herb, used frequently on very malignant sores [llagas muy maliciosas], corrects, cleanses [mundifica], dries, and promotes the growth of new flesh [encarna]. And the herb pounded together with purslane [verdolagas], a little vinegar, and orpiment [oropimente], extirpates all warts [verrugas] and the films or membranes of scrofulous swellings [lamparones] or cysts [lobanillos].

entities_mentioned.name: anchariipa
entities_mentioned.notes: An Andean herb described as small with smooth, straight stalks; Quechua or regional vernacular name. Not confidently matched to a modern species. The straight smooth stalks used as urethral sounds suggest a rush or reed-like plant, possibly a species of Juncus or Equisetum, but this is highly speculative. Thermal qualities described as hot and dry per Galenic humoral schema. Treated conditions: urethral carnosidad (possibly urethral stricture or polyp), malignant ulcers, warts, scrofula, and cysts. Preparations: (1) physical insertion of stalks as a urethral sound/dilator; (2) decoction applied topically to wounds; (3) poultice of pounded herb with purslane, vinegar, and orpiment for warts and scrofulous swellings.

entities_mentioned.name: verdolagas
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Portulaca oleracea (common purslane)
entities_mentioned.notes: Used as an ingredient in a topical preparation for warts and scrofulous swellings.

entities_mentioned.name: oropimente
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Orpiment (arsenic trisulfide, As₂S₃)
entities_mentioned.notes: A mineral pigment and caustic agent used in early modern medicine as an escharotic to remove warts and skin growths. Combined here with anchariipa and purslane in a topical poultice.

results.translator_notes: 1. 'Carnosidad' in the urological context almost certainly refers to a fleshy obstruction or growth within the urethra (e.g., urethral caruncle, polyp, or stricture tissue); the use of the plant's smooth straight stalks as urethral sounds or bougies is a notable description of an Indigenous medical technique. 2. OCR had 'herruga' (corrected to 'verruga' — wart) and 'oropiniiente' (corrected to 'oropimente' — orpiment); both corrections are unambiguous in context. 3. 'Lamparones' typically denotes scrofula (tuberculous cervical lymphadenitis) in colonial Spanish medical literature; 'lobanillos' refers to sebaceous cysts or similar subcutaneous lumps. 4. 'Mundifica' is a standard Galenic/pharmaceutical term meaning to cleanse a wound of morbid matter; retained in brackets as it carries specific period meaning.

results.title_inferred: On the Chuquicañilla plant: uses for headache, fever, asthma, and gout
results.original_text:
CAPITULO LXXIII
De la chuquicañilla

La chuquicañilla es una yerba pequeñuela, espinosa, que se levanta del suelo como cuatro dedos y echa de sí un olor que no enfada. Su temperamento es caliente y seco. Usan de su sahumerio los indios para dolores de cabeza y calenturas, y dan su cocimiento para que suden con él los tales enfermos y expelen la calentura, porque es esta yerba muy provocativa de sudor. Aprovecha su cocimiento caliente y con azúcar a los asmáticos y a los que tienen el pecho cerrado; y cocida esta yerba con molle, tola, y mucha sal, dando con ello baño, deseca las piernas gotosas e hinchadas.
results.translation:
CHAPTER LXXIII
On the Chuquicañilla

The chuquicañilla is a small, thorny herb that rises from the ground about four fingers' height and gives off an odor that is not unpleasant. Its temperament [complexion] is hot and dry. The Indians use its smoke [fumigation] for headaches and fevers, and they give its decoction so that the sick may sweat with it and expel the fever, because this herb is a very powerful promoter of perspiration. Its decoction, taken hot and with sugar, benefits asthmatics and those with a constricted chest [tight-chested]; and when this herb is boiled together with molle, tola, and a large quantity of salt and used as a bath, it dries out gouty and swollen legs.

entities_mentioned.name: chuquicañilla
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Possibly Chuquiraga spinosa (Asteraceae), a small spiny Andean herb; some sources also associate the name with species of Gutierrezia or Haplopappus
entities_mentioned.notes: The name 'chuquicañilla' is attested in Andean ethnobotany but is applied variously across sources. The description—small, spiny, four fingers tall, not unpleasant odor, hot and dry temperament, strongly diaphoretic—is consistent with several Andean Asteraceae shrublets. Confident genus-level identification is not possible from this description alone.

entities_mentioned.name: molle
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Schinus molle (Anacardiaceae), Peruvian pepper tree
entities_mentioned.notes: Molle is consistently identified as Schinus molle throughout colonial Peruvian sources and is well-attested in Cobo's own work.

entities_mentioned.name: tola
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Parastrephia lepidophylla or related Parastrephia spp. (Asteraceae); also sometimes Baccharis spp.
entities_mentioned.notes: 'Tola' is a widely used Andean name for several resinous shrubs used as fuel and medicine; Parastrephia lepidophylla is the most commonly cited referent in the southern Andes.

results.translator_notes: Multiple OCR errors silently corrected: 'chiiqiiicanlla' → 'chuquicañilla'; 'peque-miela' → 'pequeñuela'; 'uue' → 'que'; 'callieiite' → 'caliente'; 'sahume-rio' → 'sahumerio'; 'y^erba' → 'yerba'; 'expelan' → 'expelen' (standardized); '})año' → 'baño'. The medical properties extracted are: (1) Name: chuquicañilla. (2) Modern ID: uncertain, possibly Chuquiraga spinosa or related Asteraceae. (3) Conditions treated: headache, fever, asthma/chest congestion, gout, swollen legs. (4) Preparation methods: (a) fumigation/smoke inhalation for headache and fever; (b) decoction administered hot as a diaphoretic to expel fever; (c) hot decoction with sugar for asthma and chest complaints; (d) bath prepared by boiling with molle, tola, and salt, applied to gouty and swollen legs.

results.title_inferred: On the Guariconca herb: uses, preparations, and etymology
results.original_text:
CAPITULO LXXIV
De la guariconca

Esta es una yerba cabelluda de color blanco y de temperamento caliente y húmedo. Usan los indios de su cocimiento cuando les aflige la tos, tomándolo caliente en cantidad con azúcar en ayunas y después de cenar. Facilita el escupir en los que tienen dolor de costado, y principalmente si de su cocimiento se hace lamedor y se toma a menudo; y tomado este cocimiento con miel de abejas o hecho lamedor dél, es contra el asma y limpia las llagas de los pulmones. Cocida esta yerba con culantrillo de pozo y hecho lamedor con azúcar, facilita el pecho y desarraiga la tos antigua. Quariconca quiere decir, en la lengua del Perú, pescuezo de vicuña.
results.translation:
CHAPTER LXXIV
On the Guariconca

This is a hairy herb, white in color, and of a hot and moist temperament [complexion]. The Indians use its decoction when they are afflicted by a cough, taking it hot and in quantity with sugar, on an empty stomach and after supper. It facilitates expectoration in those suffering from pleurisy [dolor de costado, lit. 'side pain'], and especially if a syrup [lamedor] is made from its decoction and taken frequently. Taken as a decoction with honey, or made into a syrup thereof, it is effective against asthma and cleanses the sores [ulcers] of the lungs. Boiled together with maidenhair fern [culantrillo de pozo] and made into a syrup with sugar, it eases the chest and eradicates a chronic cough. Quariconca means, in the language of Peru [Quechua], 'neck of the vicuña.'

entities_mentioned.name: guariconca (also spelled quariconca)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Possibly a species of Perezia or another Andean composite herb; the name and description do not permit confident identification
entities_mentioned.notes: Described as hairy, white-colored, hot and moist in temperament. Quechua name means 'neck of the vicuña,' possibly referring to the plant's long or slender appearance. Used for cough, pleurisy, asthma, and pulmonary ulcers.

entities_mentioned.name: culantrillo de pozo
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Adiantum capillus-veneris (common maidenhair fern) or a related Adiantum species
entities_mentioned.notes: A well-attested colonial Spanish name for maidenhair fern, commonly used in combination preparations for respiratory complaints.

entities_mentioned.name: vicuña
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Vicugna vicugna (Andean camelid)
entities_mentioned.notes: Mentioned only in the etymological gloss of the plant's Quechua name.

results.translator_notes: The term 'lamedor' (lit. 'licker') denotes a thick medicinal syrup meant to be licked or sipped slowly, equivalent to an electuary or linctus; it has been rendered as 'syrup [lamedor]' on first occurrence and 'syrup' thereafter. 'Dolor de costado' classically refers to pleurisy or a painful chest condition, here rendered as 'pleurisy' with the original phrase noted. 'Llagas de los pulmones' (lit. 'sores/wounds of the lungs') likely refers to pulmonary ulcers or tubercular lesions in period medical understanding. The OCR error 'giiaric onc a' in the chapter heading has been silently corrected to 'guariconca'. The dash-like break mid-word ('coci¬miento', 'cos¬tado') is a line-break hyphenation artifact, silently corrected throughout.

results.title_inferred: On the hacagua guaní and the hampeani — two medicinal herbs of Peru
results.original_text:
CAPITULO LXXV

De la hacagua guaní

Esta es una yerba de dos palmos en alto; tiene las hojas como las del lirio, algo más angostas, la flor purpúrea; las raíces son en gran cantidad y como los dedos de la mano y como zanahorias. Su temperamento es caliente y seco; el zumo de su raíz, echado en el oído, aprovecha para la sordera, y tomado en la boca, quita cualquiera dolor de muelas. El cocimiento destas raíces, tomado en ayunas, es contra la detención de orina, provoca el menstruo y facilita el escupir en los dolores de costado; y las raíces majadas y aplicadas calientes sobre la gota, aplacan el dolor della.

CAPITULO LXXVI

De la hampeani

La hampeani es una yerba pequeña que nace entre peñas y en lugares húmedos; tiene las hojas muy parecidas a las de la pimpinela, las cuales se aparran por el suelo. Su raíz, gruesa y colorada; produce una flor colorada, aunque hay otra que echa la flor naranjada, a la cual llaman los indios hembra, y a ésta, macho; y désta, por tener más satisfacción que de la hembra, se aprovechan de ordinario en sus enfermedades. Es esta yerba caliente y seca; cuando los indios padecen cámaras de sangre, comen de sus raíces cocidas, y además desto, conforta el estómago. El cocimiento dellas y de sus hojas, según dicen los indios, conforta el corazón, la vista y es contra las melancolías.

En la peste de viruelas que hubo este reino del Perú por los años de 1591 y 1592, de que murió gran número de indios, un hombre muy versado en el conocimiento de plantas y cosas naturales, echó a mucha desta yerba en tinajas del agua, y asimismo la esparció por toda su casa, con lo cual se libraron todos los della, que eran muchos, de la peste, habiendo muerto muchos de sus vecinos; por donde se creyó ser esta yerba contra la peste y males contagiosos.
results.translation:
CHAPTER LXXV

On the hacagua guaní

This is an herb reaching two palms [roughly 40 cm] in height; its leaves resemble those of the lily [lirio], though somewhat narrower, and its flower is purple. The roots are plentiful, shaped like the fingers of a hand and like carrots. Its temperament [complexion, in Galenic terms] is hot and dry. The juice of its root, dropped into the ear, is beneficial for deafness; and taken in the mouth, it relieves any toothache. A decoction [cocimiento] of these roots, taken on an empty stomach, counteracts urinary retention, provokes menstruation, and eases expectoration in pleuritic pains [dolores de costado]; and the roots, pounded and applied warm over the gout, relieve its pain.

CHAPTER LXXVI

On the hampeani

The hampeani is a small herb that grows among rocks and in damp places; its leaves closely resemble those of burnet [pimpinela], and they spread along the ground. Its root is thick and reddish; it bears a red flower, though there is another variety that produces an orange flower, which the Indians call the female, while this one they call the male. This latter variety is generally preferred for treating ailments, as it is considered more reliable than the female. This herb is hot and dry in temperament. When the Indians suffer from bloody flux [cámaras de sangre, i.e., dysentery], they eat its roots boiled; moreover, it strengthens the stomach. A decoction of the roots and leaves, according to the Indians, fortifies the heart, improves the sight, and is effective against melancholy.

During the smallpox epidemic that struck this kingdom of Peru in the years 1591 and 1592, in which a great number of Indians died, a man highly versed in the knowledge of plants and natural things placed large quantities of this herb in jars of water and likewise spread it throughout his entire house. By this means all the members of his household, who were many, were spared from the epidemic, even as many of his neighbors perished. It was therefore held to be established that this herb is efficacious against plague and contagious diseases.

entities_mentioned.name: hacagua guaní
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Possibly a species of Werneria, Sisyrinchium, or another Andean iris-like herb (Iridaceae); the purple flower and carrot-like roots suggest Sisyrinchium or related genus
entities_mentioned.notes: The description — lily-like but narrower leaves, purple flower, abundant finger- or carrot-shaped roots — is consistent with several Andean genera. The name does not appear in standard colonial herbals with certainty. Galenic temperament: hot and dry.

entities_mentioned.name: hampeani
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Possibly Oenothera or Oxalis species, or perhaps a Lamiaceae; some scholars have tentatively linked Andean 'hampeani' to Clinopodium or Satureja species
entities_mentioned.notes: Two varieties distinguished by the Indians as 'male' (red flower, preferred medicinally) and 'female' (orange flower). Grows among rocks in humid places; leaves resemble burnet (pimpinela = Sanguisorba). The 1591–1592 smallpox plague anecdote is a notable historical datum. Galenic temperament: hot and dry.

entities_mentioned.name: pimpinela
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Sanguisorba minor (burnet) or Pimpinella anisum; used here only as a morphological comparator for leaf shape
entities_mentioned.notes: In colonial Spanish botanical writing, 'pimpinela' typically refers to Sanguisorba minor (lesser burnet) when describing leaf shape.

results.translator_notes:
Several OCR artifacts were silently corrected: 'cociráiento' → 'cocimiento'; 'aiin-aue' → 'aunque'; 'lada' line break → 'naranjada'; 'drsto' → 'desto'; 'esparció' reconstructed from 'espai- ció'; 'muchos' reconstructed from 'machos' in the plague passage (context clearly requires 'muchos' = 'many'); 'se creyó' reconstructed from 'se ció'. The smallpox epidemic of 1591–1592 in Peru is historically attested and provides a useful anchor for dating Cobo's sources. The male/female plant distinction reflects standard indigenous Andean ethnobotanical categorization. Medical extractions for both chapters are embedded in the translation; see below for structured summary:

HACACUA GUANÍ — Ailments: deafness, toothache, urinary retention, amenorrhea, pleurisy, gout. Preparations: (1) raw root juice instilled in ear; (2) raw root juice held in mouth; (3) decoction of roots taken fasting; (4) pounded roots applied warm topically.

HAMPEANI — Ailments: bloody dysentery, weak stomach, melancholy, poor vision, smallpox/contagious disease. Preparations: (1) boiled roots eaten; (2) decoction of roots and leaves drunk; (3) herb placed in water jars and spread about the house (prophylactic/fumigatory use).

results.title_inferred: On the Anocara plant (wild cress of the Indies)
results.original_text:
CAPITULO LXXVII
De la anocara

Este nombre anocara, en la lengua aimará, significa el perro, y dando los indios a cierta yerba tan semejante a nuestro mastuerzo en la figura y en el gusto cálido y mordicante, que la llaman los españoles mastuerzo silvestre. Nace en tierras frías y calientes; en las primeras no crece tan vicioso como en las segundas; su hoja es pequeña, larguilla y muy hendida, con pequeñas puntas, como la del mastuerzo; echa muchos tallos, todos por la parte más alta, llenos alrededor de una simiente muy menudica, que, metida en sus hollejuelos, tiene hechura de lentejas, aunque es mucho menor.

Hanse visto con esta yerba maravillosos efectos en heridas, y particularmente si se aplica a la primera cura para detener la sangre. Curan también con ella los indios las cámaras de sangre y otras enfermedades, entre los cuales tiene muchos nombres, porque en unas partes la llaman chichira, y en otras, cubicula. En las dos lenguas generales del Perú se dice, sipi, en la quichua, y anocara en la aimará.
results.translation:
CHAPTER LXXVII
On the Anocara

The name anocara, in the Aymara language, means 'the dog,' and the Indians give it to a certain herb so resembling our garden cress [mastuerzo] in form and in its hot, biting taste that the Spaniards call it wild cress [mastuerzo silvestre]. It grows in both cold and warm lands; in the former it does not grow as vigorously as in the latter. Its leaf is small, somewhat elongated, and deeply cut, with small points, like that of garden cress. It puts forth many stalks, all of them, along the upper portions, thickly covered with a very fine seed which, enclosed in its little husks [hollejuelos], has the shape of lentils, though it is much smaller.

Marvelous effects have been observed with this herb in the treatment of wounds, and particularly when applied at the first dressing [primera cura] to stop the bleeding. The Indians also use it to treat bloody flux [cámaras de sangre] and other ailments. Among them it has many names, for in some places they call it chichira, and in others, cubicula. In the two general languages of Peru it is called sipi in Quechua, and anocara in Aymara.

entities_mentioned.name: anocara / sipi / chichira / cubicula / mastuerzo silvestre
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Lepidium chichicara Desv. (or a closely related Andean Lepidium species, family Brassicaceae); possibly also confused with Lepidium bipinnatifidum
entities_mentioned.notes: Cobo's description — deeply lobed leaves resembling garden cress (Lepidium sativum), hot and pungent taste, small lentil-shaped seeds in husks, growth in both cold and warm Andean zones — strongly suggests a native Andean Lepidium. The Quechua name 'sipi' and the wound-healing and antidysenteric uses are consistent with ethnohistorical records of Lepidium chichicara. The Aymara name 'anocara' literally meaning 'dog' may reflect a folk taxonomy distinguishing it as the wild or inferior counterpart of cultivated cress.
entities_mentioned.preparation_method: Applied topically to wounds at first dressing to stop bleeding; also used internally (preparation not specified) for bloody flux.

entities_mentioned.name: mastuerzo / mastuerzo silvestre
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Lepidium sativum L. (garden cress) used as comparative reference
entities_mentioned.notes: Used by Cobo as a morphological and organoleptic reference point familiar to Spanish readers; not itself the subject of the chapter.

results.translator_notes: 'Cámaras de sangre' is the standard 17th-century Iberian and colonial medical term for bloody dysentery or bloody diarrhea. 'Hollejuelos' are the small seed husks or silicles, consistent with Brassicaceae fruit morphology. The word rendered as 'cubicula' in the original OCR is uncertain; it may be a proper regional indigenous name corrupted by the scribe or typesetter — flagged but not emended. 'Vicioso' in colonial botanical usage means 'luxuriantly' or 'vigorously' (growing), not 'viciously.' The OCR in the source was heavily degraded with line-end hyphenation artifacts and character substitutions; these have been silently corrected throughout the cleaned original text.

results.title_inferred: The Haratuc plant: properties and medicinal uses
results.original_text:
CAPITULO LXXVIII
Del haratuc

Esta es una yerba áspera, algo olorosa y aparrada en la tierra; echa unas flores blancas que, secas, parecen flocaduras y saltan en tocándolas; es de virtud caliente y húmeda, y yerba pectoral. Usan della los indios para muchas enfermedades, en particular en los dolores de costado, dando en ayunas y de parte de noche una buena porción de su cocimiento con miel de abejas y aplicando la misma yerba caliente y rociada con vino sobre el dolor. Vale también su cocimiento contra los dolores de estómago, contra todo género de opilación y contra la detención de orina.
results.translation:
CHAPTER LXXVIII
On the haratuc

This is a rough herb [yerba], somewhat fragrant and spreading low upon the ground; it bears white flowers which, when dry, resemble tassels [flocaduras] and spring apart when touched. It is of hot and moist virtue [virtud caliente y húmeda] and is a pectoral herb [yerba pectoral]. The Indians use it for many ailments, particularly for pains in the side [dolores de costado], administering a generous portion of its decoction [cocimiento] with honey on an empty stomach and at night, while also applying the herb itself, warmed and sprinkled with wine, directly over the area of pain. Its decoction is likewise effective against stomach pains, against all manner of obstruction [opilación], and against retention of urine.

entities_mentioned.name: haratuc
entities_mentioned.notes: Name appears to be Quechua in origin. The description—rough, spreading, fragrant, white flowers that burst when dry—is insufficient for confident modern identification. Some Andean ethnobotanical sources have tentatively associated similar plant names with species in the genera Clinopodium or Satureja (Lamiaceae), but this remains speculative. Further archival research recommended.

entities_mentioned.name: miel de abejas
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: honey (Apis mellifera or native Meliponini species)
entities_mentioned.notes: Used as a vehicle for the decoction, administered fasting and at night.

results.translator_notes: Several OCR errors were silently corrected: 'morte' → 'algo' (contextually clear from the phrase 'algo olorosa'); 'recen flocaduras' represents a line-break corruption of 'parecen flocaduras'; 'avunas' → 'ayunas'; '^ro de opilación' → 'género de opilación'. The word 'aparrada' is retained as a period term meaning spreading or sprawling close to the ground (from 'aparrarse'). 'Dolores de costado' likely refers to pleuritic or intercostal pain rather than a modern clinical diagnosis. The humoral characterisation 'caliente y húmeda' reflects Galenic medical theory still current in 17th-century colonial medicine. Medical uses extracted: (1) pleuritic/side pain — oral decoction with honey on empty stomach at night + topical warm herb sprinkled with wine; (2) stomach pain — oral decoction; (3) obstruction [opilación] — oral decoction; (4) urinary retention — oral decoction.

results.title_inferred: On the Harmico plant
results.original_text:
CAPITULO LXXIX
Del harmico

El harmico es una yerba semejante a la salvia, cálida y de olor aromático; cómenla cruda los indios muy de ordinario, porque dicen los preserva de criar lombrices; mezclada con ají y yeso desbecho en agua, lo beben en ayanas caliente contra la tos y pechugera; majadas las hojas y aplicadas sobre las heridas frescas, las sueldan y restañan la sangre; y su cocimiento, tomado de ordinario, sana las llagas de la boca y de los pulmones.
results.translation:
CHAPTER LXXIX
On the Harmico

The harmico is an herb resembling sage [salvia], warm [cálida] in temperament and of aromatic smell. The Indians eat it raw very commonly, because they say it prevents them from breeding worms [lombrices]. Mixed with chili pepper [ají] and gypsum [yeso] dissolved in water, they drink it warm in the mornings [ayanas] against cough and chest ailments [pechugera]. The leaves, pounded and applied over fresh wounds, close them and staunch the blood. And its decoction [cocimiento], taken regularly, heals sores [llagas] of the mouth and of the lungs.

entities_mentioned.name: harmico
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Possibly Chenopodium ambrosioides (epazote / wormseed) or a related Chenopodium species, given the anti-parasitic (anthelmintic) use, aromatic quality, and resemblance to sage
entities_mentioned.notes: The name 'harmico' is not widely attested in standard colonial botanical literature; the anthelmintic use and aromatic character are strongly consistent with Chenopodium ambrosioides, but the sage-like description could also suggest a Salvia or Lippia species. Identification remains tentative.

entities_mentioned.name: ají
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Capsicum annuum or related Capsicum species (chili pepper)
entities_mentioned.notes: Standard colonial term for New World chili peppers.

entities_mentioned.name: yeso
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Gypsum (calcium sulfate dihydrate)
entities_mentioned.notes: Used here as a medicinal adjuvant dissolved in water; gypsum had various uses in colonial-era medicine.

medical_uses.notes: Prophylactic use; Indians consumed it raw regularly as a preventive

medical_uses.condition: Cough and chest ailments (tos y pechugera)
medical_uses.preparation: Mixed with chili pepper and gypsum dissolved in water; drunk warm in the mornings

medical_uses.notes: Described as closing wounds and staunching blood (vulnerary and hemostatic use)

medical_uses.condition: Sores of the mouth and lungs (llagas de la boca y de los pulmones)
medical_uses.notes: Lung 'sores' likely refers to tuberculosis or similar pulmonary ulceration in period understanding

results.translator_notes: Several OCR corruptions were silently corrected: 'lia salvia' → 'a la salvia'; '«¿menla' → 'cómenla'; 'etiar lombrices' → 'criar lombrices'; 'yo desbecho' → 'yeso desbecho'; 'ajanas' → 'ayanas' (mornings, from Quechua); 'pechugera' is a period term for chest/respiratory affliction, retained in brackets. The word 'ayanas' (or 'ayunas') may alternatively mean 'on an empty stomach / fasting' rather than 'mornings'; both readings are plausible in context. The schema was extended with a 'medical_uses' array to fully satisfy extraction rule 3, as the segment describes multiple distinct conditions, preparations, and ailments.

results.title_inferred: On the Higuana plant: properties, wound treatment, and poisonous use
results.original_text:
CAPITULO LXXX

De la higuana

La higuana es una planta muy pareja a la yerba de la estrella; son sus hojas como las del apio: echa una flor marina en forma de estrella; sus raíces son largas y delgadas. Es yerba de temperamento caliente, a cuya causa, el zumo della es un cáustico fuerte que abrasa las partes do llega y puesto en moderada cantidad mundifica las llagas viejas con presteza, aunque estén muy sucias y hediondas. Usaban los indios de los polvos desta yerba en su bebida para matar a sus enemigos.
results.translation:
CHAPTER LXXX

On the Higuana

The higuana is a plant very similar to the herb of the star [yerba de la estrella]; its leaves resemble those of celery [apio]; it puts forth a sea-like [marina] flower in the shape of a star; its roots are long and slender. It is an herb of hot temperament [temperamento caliente], by reason of which its juice [zumo] is a powerful caustic [cáustico fuerte] that burns the parts it touches; yet applied in moderate quantity it cleanses [mundifica] old wounds [llagas viejas] with speed, even when they are very foul and fetid. The Indians were accustomed to use the powders of this herb mixed into drink in order to poison their enemies.

entities_mentioned.name: higuana
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Possibly a species of Ranunculus or another caustic-juiced Andean herb; some sources tentatively associate it with plants in the Apiaceae family given the celery-like leaf description, but identification remains uncertain.
entities_mentioned.notes: The star-shaped flower, celery-like leaves, long thin roots, and strongly caustic juice are the key distinguishing features noted by Cobo. The name 'higuana' does not map cleanly to a well-attested modern botanical taxon.

entities_mentioned.name: yerba de la estrella
entities_mentioned.notes: Referenced only as a comparator plant; not described independently in this passage.

entities_mentioned.name: apio
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Apium graveolens (celery) or a related Apiaceae species used as a morphological reference.
entities_mentioned.notes: Used here as a leaf-shape comparator, not as a subject plant.

medical_extraction.condition_treated: Old, foul, and fetid wounds (llagas viejas muy sucias y hediondas)
medical_extraction.preparation_method: Juice (zumo) applied topically in moderate quantity as a caustic cleanser; powders (polvos) reportedly used by indigenous peoples as a poison administered in drink.

results.translator_notes: Several OCR errors were silently corrected: 'bajas' → 'hojas' (leaves), 'mariha' → 'marina' (sea-like / marine), 'zaino' → 'zumo' (juice), 'iasa' → 'abrasa' (burns), 'do' is retained as archaic Spanish for 'donde' (where), '%as' → 'llagas' (wounds), 'ia' → 'estén' (are), 'Amigos' → 'enemigos' (enemies; clearly a scanning inversion given context). The word 'marina' describing the flower is unusual and may mean 'sea-like' in color or form, or could be a corruption of another adjective; it is translated literally. The poisoning use ('matar a sus enemigos') is a notable ethnobotanical detail recorded without moral commentary by Cobo.

results.title_inferred: On the hopahopa (aquatic lentil-like plant)
results.original_text:
CAPITULO LXXXI
De la hopahopa.

Llaman hopahopa los indios a ciertas lentejuelas que se dan encima del agua encharcada y en los totorales o juncales; son algo coloradas y de complexión templada, con muy poca estipticidad; majada esta yerba y puesta en la muela que duele, quita el dolor; y asimismo, puesta sobre las quebraduras o fracturas de huesos con un poco de sal y polvos de muña, las restaura; y su cocimiento con vino y alumbre, tomado a menudo, sana las llagas de la boca y afija la dentadura.
results.translation:
CHAPTER LXXXI
On the hopahopa.

The Indians call hopahopa certain small lentil-like [plants] that grow on the surface of stagnant water and in reed beds or rush beds [totorales o juncales]. They are somewhat reddish and of a temperate complexion [temperament], with very little astringency [estipticidad]. This herb, ground [majada] and applied to the aching molar, relieves the pain; and likewise, applied over fractures or broken bones together with a little salt and powdered muña, it restores them. Its decoction taken with wine and alum [alumbre], drunk frequently, heals mouth sores [llagas de la boca] and firms the teeth.

entities_mentioned.name: hopahopa
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Possibly Lemna sp. (duckweed) or a related aquatic floating plant (Lemnaceae family); the description of small reddish lentil-like bodies floating on stagnant water in Andean reed beds is consistent with duckweed species.
entities_mentioned.notes: The description — small, lentil-shaped, reddish, floating on still water near totora reed beds — points strongly to a Lemnaceae (duckweed) species, possibly Lemna minuta or Wolffia sp. native to Andean wetlands. The name 'hopahopa' appears to be Quechua in origin. Medical uses extracted: (1) toothache — ground herb applied directly to affected tooth; (2) bone fractures — ground herb with salt and muña powder applied topically; (3) mouth sores and loose teeth — decoction with wine and alum taken orally as a frequent wash/drink.

entities_mentioned.name: muña
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Minthostachys mollis or related Minthostachys spp. (Andean mint-like aromatic herb, Lamiaceae)
entities_mentioned.notes: Muña is a well-documented Andean medicinal herb frequently cited by Cobo and others; used here in powdered form combined with hopahopa for treating bone fractures.

entities_mentioned.name: alumbre
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Alum (potassium aluminium sulfate, KAl(SO₄)₂·12H₂O)
entities_mentioned.notes: A classical astringent [estiпtico] used in European and colonial medicine; here combined with wine and hopahopa decoction for oral use against mouth sores and loose teeth.

results.translator_notes: The word 'afija' (from 'afijar' / 'afianzar') meaning to fix, secure, or firm — here applied to teeth — is an archaic usage preserved in brackets context as 'firms the teeth.' The term 'estipticidad' (astringency/stypticity) reflects humoral medical terminology of the period. 'Totorales' refers specifically to stands of Schoenoplectus tatora (totora reed), characteristic of Andean highland wetlands such as Lake Titicaca, giving useful geographic/ecological context for the plant's habitat. The OCR artifact 'juncales' (for 'juncales', rush beds) has been silently corrected; the line-break hyphen in 'junca¬les' was an OCR artifact.

results.title_inferred: On the Herb of Santa María
results.original_text:
CAPITULO LXXXIV
De la yerba de Santa María

Nace esta yerba en la costa de la mar del Sur de la Nueva España y allí le dan este nombre; crece un estado en alto; su hoja es de forma de corazón y mayor que la palma de la mano; echa unos botoncillos largos y delgados, muy semejantes a los canelones de disciplina. Tiene su hoja olor y sabor de hinojo, y puesta en la cabeza, es buena para el dolor della causado del sol.
results.translation:
CHAPTER LXXXIV
On the Herb of Santa María

This herb grows on the coast of the South Sea [Pacific coast] of New Spain, where it is given this name. It grows to the height of one estado [approximately one fathom, or the height of a man]; its leaf is heart-shaped and larger than the palm of the hand. It puts forth long, slender little buds very similar to the lashes [tails] of a discipline whip [flagellant's scourge]. Its leaf has the smell and taste of fennel, and when placed on the head it is good for headache caused by the sun.

entities_mentioned.name: yerba de Santa María
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Possibly Teloxys graveolens (formerly Chenopodium graveolens) or a related epazote-family species; the fennel-like scent and coastal Pacific Mexico habitat are suggestive, though a precise match remains uncertain
entities_mentioned.notes: The name 'yerba de Santa María' was applied to multiple unrelated plants in colonial sources. The heart-shaped leaves larger than a palm, slender elongated flower buds resembling a whip's tails, and strong fennel-like scent are key diagnostic details. The coastal New Spain (Pacific coast) provenance and topical use for sun-induced headache are also noted.

entities_mentioned.name: hinojo
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Foeniculum vulgare (fennel)
entities_mentioned.notes: Used here as a comparative aromatic reference, not as the subject plant.

results.translator_notes: 1. 'Un estado' is a unit of measure roughly equal to the height of a man (approx. 1.6–1.8 m or ~6 ft). 2. 'Canelones de disciplina' refers to the knotted or braided cords of a flagellant's penitential whip (discipline), used here as a simile for the long, thin flower buds. The OCR rendered 'dieipliiia' which is clearly 'disciplina'; corrected silently. 3. 'La mar del Sur' is the colonial Spanish term for the Pacific Ocean. 4. The medical application is topical: leaves applied directly to the head to relieve sun-induced headache — no preparation method beyond direct application is described. 5. 'ía' in the original OCR is clearly 'la'; corrected silently.

results.title_inferred: On the Congona plant
results.original_text:
CAPITULO LXXXV
De la congona

La congona es una yerba muy parecida a la siempreviva; crece media vara en alto, poco más o menos; hace sus ramas tan gruesas como un junco mediano, son verdes, tiernas y pobladas de hojas, que es lo que en esta planta se estima; es esta hoja semejante a la de la verdolaga, un poco mayor y dos veces más gruesa que ella, verde, lisa, tierna y muy aguanosa. Las calidades que tiene porque se precia, es por ser olorosa y que, mascada, tiene un sabor agudo y picante parecido a el de la nuez moscada o clavos de comer, y que deja la boca olorosa por un rato, como si se hubiera tenido en ella alguna de las especias referidas. Suelen echarlas en las cazoletas olorosas que se aderezán para los perfumadores, y sirven también para otros usos.
results.translation:
CHAPTER LXXXV
On the Congona

The congona is an herb very similar to the houseleek [siempreviva]; it grows to about half a vara [approximately 16–17 inches] in height, more or less. Its branches are as thick as a medium-sized rush [junco], and are green, tender, and densely covered with leaves, which are the most prized part of the plant. The leaf resembles that of purslane [verdolaga], being somewhat larger and twice as thick, green, smooth, tender, and very succulent. The qualities for which it is esteemed are its fragrance and the fact that, when chewed, it has a sharp and pungent flavor similar to that of nutmeg or cloves, and it leaves the mouth fragrant for a while, as if one had held some of the aforementioned spices in it. The leaves are commonly added to the fragrant brazier-pans [cazoletas olorosas] prepared for perfume-burners [perfumadores], and they also serve other purposes.

entities_mentioned.name: congona
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Peperomia inaequalifolia or Peperomia congona (family Piperaceae)
entities_mentioned.notes: Congona is a well-known Andean Peperomia species, widely recognized in Peruvian ethnobotany. The description of thick, succulent, aromatic leaves with a spicy, clove-like taste when chewed is consistent with several Peperomia spp., most commonly identified as Peperomia congona or closely related taxa.

entities_mentioned.name: siempreviva
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Sempervivum tectorum or a related succulent (family Crassulaceae); possibly used loosely for similar-looking succulents
entities_mentioned.notes: Used here as a morphological comparison, not as a subject plant. In colonial Iberian botanical writing, 'siempreviva' typically referred to houseleek.

entities_mentioned.name: verdolaga
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Portulaca oleracea (common purslane, family Portulacaceae)
entities_mentioned.notes: Used here as a leaf-shape comparison only.

results.translator_notes: No ailment or medical preparation is described in this segment; the chapter focuses on the plant's morphology, sensory properties, and aromatic/cosmetic uses (perfume braziers). The OCR artifact 'qu<*' has been silently corrected to 'que'. The unit 'media vara' is approximately 41–42 cm (roughly 16–17 inches) based on the Castilian vara of ~83 cm. 'Cazoletas olorosas' refers to small aromatic burner-pans or censers used for fumigation and perfumery, a common domestic practice in the colonial period.

results.title_inferred: On the Chaneorama plant
results.original_text:
CAPITULO LXXXVI

De la chaneorama

Esta es una yerba espinosa, cuyos tallos crecen un codo en alto, y son más delgados que el dedo meñique, redondos, macizos, de un verde blanquecino, cubiertos de un corto y delgado vello, que los hace algún tanto ásperos. Las hojas son largas una tercia y anchas un dedo, aserradas por los lados, armadas de espinas cortas, delgadas y agudas, que punzan. En el remate de los tallos echa esta yerba unas flores blancas, diez o doce juntas en cada uno, menores que las de la manzanilla. En la lengua aimará se dice así esta yerba.
results.translation:
CHAPTER LXXXVI

On the Chaneorama

This is a thorny herb whose stems grow to the height of one cubit [approximately 18 inches], and are thinner than the little finger, round, solid, of a whitish green color, covered in a short, fine down that makes them somewhat rough to the touch. The leaves are one-third of a vara [approximately 11 inches] in length and one finger's width wide, serrated along the edges, and armed with short, thin, sharp spines that prick. At the tips of the stems this herb produces white flowers, ten or twelve together on each stem, smaller than those of chamomile [manzanilla]. In the Aymara language this herb is called by this name.

entities_mentioned.name: chaneorama
entities_mentioned.notes: Aymara-language name for a spiny herb found in the Andean region. The description — spiny, whitish-green stems with fine hairs, serrated leaves armed with short sharp spines, and small white composite-like flowers in clusters of 10–12 smaller than chamomile — is suggestive of a member of the Asteraceae or possibly Solanaceae family, but no confident modern identification can be made without further corroborating sources. No medicinal use is described in this segment.

entities_mentioned.name: manzanilla
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Matricaria chamomilla (chamomile) or a local Andean analogue
entities_mentioned.notes: Used here as a comparative size reference for the flowers of chaneorama, not as a subject of description itself. In colonial Peruvian contexts 'manzanilla' could refer to European chamomile or to Andean species with similar appearance.

results.translator_notes: The source text contains several apparent OCR errors corrected silently: 'cha neorama' → 'chaneorama' (chapter title split by line break artifact); 'La' → 'Las' (before 'hojas'); 'm dedo' → 'un dedo'; 'diex' → 'diez'. The measurement 'una tercia' refers to one-third of a vara (a Spanish unit of approximately 33 inches), thus roughly 11 inches. No medical or preparation information is present in this segment.

results.title_inferred: The Cacahuara Plant: Description and Use in Setting Fractures
results.original_text:
CAPITULO LXXXVII
De la cacahuara

Esta es una yerba que produce desde el suelo sus hojas, que son poco más angostas que un dedo y largas dos palmos, por la haz lisas y verdes, y por el envés, blanquecinas y vellosas. Echa un tallo de un codo de alto, tan grueso como un dedo, cubierto de un vello blanco, con muchas flores amarillas en el remate, del tamaño y talle de las de la cerraja. Es yerba caliente y muy útil para soldar cualesquiera quebraduras. Llámase en la lengua aimará racahuara.
results.translation:
CHAPTER LXXXVII
On the Cacahuara

This is an herb that produces its leaves from the ground; they are a little narrower than a finger and two spans [palmos] long, smooth and green on the upper face [haz], and whitish and hairy on the underside [envés]. It sends up a stalk one cubit [codo] high, as thick as a finger, covered in white down, with many yellow flowers at the tip, of the size and form of those of the sowthistle [cerraja]. It is a hot herb [yerba caliente] and very useful for knitting [soldar] any fractures [quebraduras]. In the Aymara language it is called racahuara.

entities_mentioned.name: cacahuara / racahuara
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Possibly a species within Senecio or a related Asteraceae genus native to the Andean highlands; the yellow composite flowers resembling sowthistle (Sonchus spp.) and the whitish-hairy underside of leaves are consistent with several Andean Senecioneae
entities_mentioned.notes: The Aymara name 'racahuara' may assist further identification. The description of basal, strap-like leaves that are green above and white-tomentose beneath, with a stalk bearing yellow capitulate flowers similar to cerraja (Sonchus oleraceus), is broadly consistent with Senecio or Pseudognaphalium species common in Andean flora.

entities_mentioned.name: cerraja
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Sonchus oleraceus (common sowthistle)
entities_mentioned.notes: Used here as a morphological reference point for the flower size and shape, not as the plant under description.

results.translator_notes: OCR rendered 'xin dedo' in the source; this is clearly 'un dedo' (one finger's width) and has been silently corrected. The phrase 'yerba caliente' reflects the Galenic humoral classification of the plant as 'hot' in temperament. 'Soldar quebraduras' literally means 'to weld/solder fractures' — i.e., to promote the knitting of broken bones — and likely refers to use as a poultice or compress applied externally, though Cobo does not specify the preparation method in this passage. Medical extraction: Name: cacahuara / racahuara; Ailment treated: bone fractures; Preparation method: not specified; Humoral quality: hot [caliente].

results.title_inferred: On the Latatata plant and its use in treating bone fractures
results.original_text:
CAPITULO LXXXVIII
De la latatata

Esta es una yerba muy pequeña, que, echando muchos ramillos, se hace muy espesa y aparrada con la tierra. Son los ramillos delgados como hilos y no más largos que dos o tres dedos; las hojitas menuditas, semejantes a las del romero, la mitad menores, más delgadas y más tiernas. Echa unas florecitas blancas cada una por sí, que son las más pequeñas que yo he visto en otra yerba, porque son menores que las del trébol. Con esta yerba majada suelen curar los indios las quebraduras de huesos. El nombre que tiene es de la lengua aimará.
results.translation:
CHAPTER LXXXVIII
On the latatata

This is a very small herb which, putting forth many small branches, becomes very dense and spreading close to the ground. The branches are thin as threads and no longer than two or three fingers; the leaves are very tiny, resembling those of rosemary, half the size, more slender, and more tender. It produces small white flowers, each separately, which are the smallest I have ever seen on any herb, being even smaller than those of clover. With this herb, pounded, the Indians are accustomed to treat broken bones [fractures]. The name it bears is from the Aymara language.

entities_mentioned.name: latatata
entities_mentioned.notes: A small, dense, ground-hugging herb with thread-like branches, tiny rosemary-like leaves, and minute white flowers smaller than clover blossoms. Name is of Aymara origin. Used pounded as a poultice or topical application for bone fractures. Insufficient detail for confident modern identification; possibly a small-leaved Andean herb in the families Caryophyllaceae or Asteraceae.

results.translator_notes: Several words in the source were corrupted by OCR: 'hoj^^' corrected to 'hojitas', 'mh' to 'más', 'hlanesi')' to 'blancas', 'peque^ñas' to 'pequeñas', 'trélMíl' to 'trébol', and 'lenfi* aimará' to 'lengua aimará'. All corrections are unambiguous in context. The plant name 'latatata' (also sometimes rendered 'latatata' or 'llatallata' in other sources) is Aymara. The medical use — pounded herb applied to broken bones — suggests a poultice preparation. No dosage or internal use is described.

results.title_inferred: On the Acana plant: properties, preparations, and medical uses
results.original_text:
CAPITULO LXXXIX

De la acana

La acana crece un codo de alto; en las ramas y hojas es muy parecida a la alfalfa; tiene flor amarilla del talle de la de la manzanilla; nace entre peñas y es yerba pectoral, más amarga al gusto que la acíbar; es caliente y seca en el segundo grado. Comen los indios con ella la coca. Su cocimiento con un poco de alumbre es bueno para las llagas de la boca; y tomado caliente en ayunas con alfeñique, aprovecha contra la tos y asma y ablanda y limpia el pecho y el estómago. Allende desto, aplicado su cocimiento al rostro, quita las pecas dél, y si se hace con vino, quita las nubes de los ojos.
results.translation:
CHAPTER LXXXIX

On the Acana

The acana grows to a cubit [approximately 18 inches] in height; in its branches and leaves it closely resembles alfalfa; it bears a yellow flower of the shape of that of camomile; it grows among rocks and is a pectoral herb [i.e., beneficial to the chest and lungs], more bitter to the taste than aloes [acíbar]; it is hot and dry in the second degree [following Galenic humoral classification]. The Indians eat coca with it. A decoction of it with a little alum is good for sores of the mouth; and taken warm on an empty stomach with sugar candy [alfeñique], it is beneficial against cough and asthma, and softens and cleanses the chest and stomach. Beyond this, the decoction applied to the face removes freckles from it, and if prepared with wine, it removes cataracts [nubes, lit. 'clouds'] from the eyes.

entities_mentioned.name: acana
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Possibly a species of Arenaria, Paronychia, or another Andean saxicolous herb; identification uncertain
entities_mentioned.notes: Described as growing among rocks, resembling alfalfa in habit, with yellow camomile-like flowers, and extremely bitter. The name 'acana' does not map cleanly to a well-documented modern taxon in standard references for Cobo's flora. Could also be a vernacular name for a species of Senecio or Hypericum given the yellow flower and bitter taste.

entities_mentioned.name: alfalfa
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Medicago sativa
entities_mentioned.notes: Used here as a morphological comparator for the acana's habit, not as a medicinal subject itself.

entities_mentioned.name: manzanilla
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Matricaria chamomilla (camomile)
entities_mentioned.notes: Used as a comparator for flower shape.

entities_mentioned.name: acíbar
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Aloe vera or related Aloe spp. (the dried bitter juice/latex)
entities_mentioned.notes: Used as a benchmark of bitterness; a standard reference in Spanish colonial and European pharmacopoeia.

entities_mentioned.name: alumbre (alum)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Potassium alum, KAl(SO₄)₂·12H₂O
entities_mentioned.notes: Used as an astringent additive in the decoction for mouth sores.

entities_mentioned.name: alfeñique
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Sugar candy / pulled sugar confection
entities_mentioned.notes: A sweetener/vehicle for the oral preparation; derived from Arabic al-fanīd. Common in colonial Spanish medical preparations.

entities_mentioned.name: coca
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Erythroxylum coca
entities_mentioned.notes: Mentioned incidentally as a foodstuff chewed by Indigenous people together with acana; not a subject of the medical description in this chapter.

results.translator_notes: The opening word of the chapter body was rendered 'La mana' in the OCR source, which is clearly a misread of 'La acana' — silently corrected. Several other OCR artifacts were corrected silently (e.g., 'V' → 'y', 'verba' → 'yerba', 'gusta' → 'gusto', 'k boca' → 'la boca', 'fila' → 'ella', 'd segundo' → 'el segundo', 'f! estómago' → 'el estómago', 'T «i' → 'y si', 'flf' → 'de'). The phrase 'nubes de los ojos' literally means 'clouds of the eyes' and refers to corneal opacities or cataracts in period medical usage.
