The Galenic qualification 'caliente y seca en el segundo grado' situates the plant in the standard four-degree humoral scale widely used in colonial pharmacological writing.

results.title_inferred: On the Copana plant and its uses
results.original_text:
CAPITULO XC
De la copana

La copana es una yerba que produce las hojas cortas, espinosas y aparradas por el suelo; sus raíces son unas pequeñas cebollas mucilaginosas por extremo, de temperamento caliente y húmedo. Úsan dellas las indias para aderezarse y engomarse el cabello. Majadas estas cebollas con malvas y unto sin sal, valen para madurar cualquiera apostema.
results.translation:
CHAPTER XC
On the copana

The copana is an herb that produces short, spiny leaves that spread low along the ground; its roots are small bulbs that are extremely mucilaginous, of a hot and moist temperament [in the Galenic sense]. The Indian women use them to dress and set their hair. These bulbs, pounded together with mallows and lard without salt, are efficacious for ripening [i.e., bringing to a head] any abscess [apostema].

entities_mentioned.name: copana
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Possibly a species of Oxalis or a bulb-bearing Andean herb; insufficient detail to identify with confidence
entities_mentioned.notes: Described as a ground-hugging, spiny-leaved herb with small mucilaginous bulbs. The mucilaginous, bulbous roots and low-spreading spiny leaves may suggest a member of the Oxalidaceae or possibly Hypoxidaceae, but no firm identification can be made from this description alone.

entities_mentioned.name: malvas
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Malva sylvestris or related Malva spp. (common mallow)
entities_mentioned.notes: Used in a topical preparation combined with copana bulbs and unsalted lard to mature abscesses; consistent with mallow's well-documented emollient and drawing properties in early modern medicine.

entities_mentioned.name: unto sin sal
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Unsalted animal lard (rendered fat)
entities_mentioned.notes: A standard vehicle in colonial and early modern European topical preparations; the absence of salt was considered important to avoid irritation and promote ripening of apostemas.

results.translator_notes: The chapter heading in the source reads 'De la c o p and' — clearly an OCR error; the intended text is 'De la copana,' as confirmed by the body of the passage. The term 'apostema' is retained in brackets in the translation as it refers specifically to the Galenic/humoral concept of an abscess or suppurating swelling that must be 'ripened' (matured) before it can be resolved. The phrase 'temperamento caliente y húmedo' reflects standard Galenic humoral categorisation and has been preserved with a bracketed gloss. 'Aparradas por el suelo' means spreading or prostrate along the ground, describing the plant's growth habit.

results.title_inferred: The Espinco plant: properties, preparations, and uses
results.original_text:
CAPITULO XCI
Del espinco

Esta es una yerba olorosa y tan parecida al trébol, que sin duda es especie suya; su hoja y flores son como las del trébol, salvo que no se levanta de la tierra y tienen unas espinillas redondas como medecillas. Es caliente y húmeda yerba, y della hacen los indios una pasta de que forman unas cuentas negras muy olorosas, que, ensartadas, las traen al cuello. Los polvos desta yerba, mezclados con polvos de incienso y dados en vino, hacen no sentir los tormentos por rigurosos que sean. Afirman los indios que su pasta hecha con salvia y resina de molle, puesta sobre el ombligo, hace fecundas las mujeres estériles; y que trayendo consigo las dichas cuentas, van seguros de las víboras y animales ponzoñosos. Llámase espinco en la lengua general del Perú.
results.translation:
CHAPTER XCI
On the Espinco

This is a fragrant herb so similar to clover that it is undoubtedly a species thereof. Its leaf and flowers are like those of clover, except that it does not rise from the ground, and it bears small round spines like tiny medals [medecillas]. It is a warm and moist herb, and from it the Indians make a paste with which they form very fragrant black beads that, when strung, they wear around the neck. The powders of this herb, mixed with powders of incense [frankincense] and given in wine, render even the most severe torments unfelt. The Indians assert that its paste, made with sage and resin of molle and placed upon the navel, makes barren women fertile; and that by carrying the aforesaid beads on one's person, one is kept safe from vipers and venomous animals. It is called espinco in the general language [Quechua] of Peru.

entities_mentioned.name: espinco
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Acmella (Spilanthes) or possibly Astragalus species; some sources suggest Gomphrena meyeniana or a related Andean clover-like herb
entities_mentioned.notes: Described as clover-like, prostrate, aromatic, with small round spiny structures. Called 'espinco' in Quechua (Lengua General del Perú). The identification is uncertain; the description has been tentatively linked to Acmella or certain Andean legumes, but no consensus exists.

entities_mentioned.name: trébol (clover)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Trifolium sp.
entities_mentioned.notes: Used as a morphological comparator for the espinco plant.

entities_mentioned.name: incienso (incense / frankincense)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Boswellia sacra or a locally sourced resinous incense
entities_mentioned.notes: Used in powdered form, mixed with espinco powder and wine as an analgesic preparation.

entities_mentioned.name: salvia (sage)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Salvia officinalis or a local Salvia sp.
entities_mentioned.notes: Combined with espinco paste and molle resin in a topical fertility treatment applied to the navel.

entities_mentioned.name: molle
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Schinus molle (Peruvian pepper tree)
entities_mentioned.notes: The resin of molle is used as a component in the topical fertility paste. Schinus molle is well attested throughout Cobo's work and widely identified in colonial Andean sources.

results.translator_notes: Several OCR errors were silently corrected: 'pare­cía' → 'parecida'; 'wa' → 'suya'; 'hopa' → 'hoja'; 'íJtbol' → 'trébol' (twice); 'tseira' → 'tierra'; 'ctiiio niedecillas' → 'como medecillas'; 'pata' → 'pasta'; 'cuentas ne-' with OCR gap → 'cuentas negras'. The phrase 'medecillas' (diminutive, possibly 'medallas' [little medals] or a dialectal term for small disc-like structures) is retained as 'medecillas' in the cleaned text with a bracketed note in translation. The analgesic use (powders in wine to suppress sensation of torment) may reflect use in judicial torture contexts, a motif appearing in other colonial botanical sources. The fertility application (paste on the navel) is a notable ethnobotanical claim. The protective use of beaded amulets against snakebite is a widespread Andean belief recorded in multiple colonial sources.

results.title_inferred: On the Lacrataruca plant
results.original_text:
CAPITULO XCII
De la lacrataruca

Llaman los indios lacrataruca, que en la lengua del Perú significa lengua de venado, a una yerba que nace en lugares sombríos; sus hojas son de un palmo de largo y dedo y medio de ancho; están por el envés como aljofaradas y algunas como manchadas. Es yerba estíptica al gusto, de temperamento templado; su cocimiento hecho con la indivia y bebido de ordinario, vale contra la itiricia y flema salada, y contra las obstrucciones del hígado y bazo y mal color del rostro; los polvos mezclados con miel de abejas hacen crecer los pelos.
results.translation:
CHAPTER XCII
On the Lacrataruca

The Indians call lacrataruca — which in the language of Peru [Quechua] means 'deer's tongue' — an herb that grows in shady places. Its leaves are a hand's span [palmo] in length and a finger and a half in width; on the underside they appear as if beaded with small pearls [aljofaradas] and some are as if spotted. It is an herb that is astringent [estíptica] to the taste, of temperate temperament [in the Galenic sense]. Its decoction, made together with endive [indivia] and taken regularly as a drink, is effective against jaundice [itiricia] and salt phlegm [flema salada], and against obstructions of the liver and spleen, and poor complexion [mal color del rostro]. Its powder, mixed with honey, promotes the growth of hair.

entities_mentioned.name: lacrataruca
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Possibly a species of Elaphoglossum (tongue ferns) or Ophioglossum sp.; the Quechua name meaning 'deer's tongue' and the description of shady habitat, dotted/pearl-like undersides, and palmate leaves are consistent with certain ferns or broad-leaved shade herbs of the Andes
entities_mentioned.notes: The name 'lengua de venado' (deer's tongue) in Quechua contexts has been applied to several unrelated plants in colonial sources. The pearly or spotted undersides may suggest a fern with sori, or a plant with glandular dots. Modern identification remains uncertain without further corroboration.

entities_mentioned.name: indivia
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Cichorium endivia (endive) or Cichorium intybus (chicory)
entities_mentioned.notes: Standard colonial Spanish term for endive/chicory, widely used in Galenic medicine of the period as a hepatic remedy.

results.translator_notes: 'Aljofaradas' refers to a surface resembling scattered small pearls (aljófar = seed pearl), likely describing sori or glandular dots on the leaf underside. 'Itiricia' is the period spelling of ictericia (jaundice). 'Flema salada' is a Galenic humoral term referring to a salt or acrid phlegmatic humor, associated with skin conditions and digestive complaints. 'Temperamento templado' reflects Galenic humoralism: the plant is neither excessively hot, cold, wet, nor dry. The preparation methods extracted are: (1) decoction with endive, taken as an oral beverage for jaundice, salt phlegm, liver/spleen obstructions, and poor complexion; (2) powdered herb mixed with honey, applied (presumably topically) to promote hair growth.

results.title_inferred: On the quinta-Laura plant and its medicinal uses
results.original_text:
CAPITULO XCIV
De la quinta-Laura

La quinta-Laura es una yerba muy parecida a la romaza; tiene las hojas largas de un palmo y anchas como dos dedos, y las raíces tan gruesas como un dedo. El cocimiento destas raíces y hojas, tomado en ayunas, ablanda el vientre del que está estreñido. El agua de las hojas, sacada por la alquitara, bebida en ayunas con un poco de canela, vale contra los dolores de estómago, y tomada con azúcar, es contra la cólera y contra la ictericia. Las hojas majadas y hecho dellas un emplasto con harina de maíz y sal, deshincha el vientre y piernas de los hidrópicos; y la semilla desta yerba, tostada, tomada en vino tinto en cantidad de una drama, estanca las cámaras de sangre.
results.translation:
CHAPTER XCIV
On the quinta-Laura

The quinta-Laura is an herb very similar to dock [romaza]; its leaves are a hand-span long and two fingers wide, and its roots are as thick as a finger. A decoction [cocimiento] of these roots and leaves, taken on an empty stomach, loosens the bowels of one who is constipated. The water of the leaves, extracted by alembic [alquitara], drunk on an empty stomach with a little cinnamon, is effective against stomach pains; and taken with sugar, it is used against bile disorders [cólera] and against jaundice [ictericia]. The leaves, pounded and made into a poultice [emplasto] with maize flour and salt, reduces the swelling of the belly and legs of those suffering from dropsy [hidrópicos]; and the seed of this herb, toasted and taken in red wine in the quantity of one dram [drama], stanches bloody flux [cámaras de sangre].

entities_mentioned.name: quinta-Laura
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Possibly a Rumex species (dock) or a related Polygonaceae; some scholars suggest Rumex cuneifolius or a related South American dock
entities_mentioned.notes: Cobo explicitly compares it to 'romaza' (dock, Rumex sp.), suggesting a close morphological resemblance. The specific New World species remains uncertain. The name 'quinta-Laura' does not correspond to any well-established modern taxon.

entities_mentioned.name: romaza
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Rumex sp. (dock)
entities_mentioned.notes: Standard Spanish vernacular name for dock plants of the genus Rumex, used here as a morphological comparator.

entities_mentioned.name: canela
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Cinnamomum verum or Cinnamomum sp. (cinnamon)
entities_mentioned.notes: Used as an adjuvant in the distilled leaf-water preparation for stomach pain.

medical_extractable.preparation: Decoction of roots and leaves, taken on an empty stomach
Water distilled from the leaves by alembic, drunk on an empty stomach with a little cinnamon
Distilled leaf water taken with sugar
Leaves pounded and made into a poultice with maize flour and salt, applied externally
Seeds toasted and taken in red wine, dose of one dram

results.translator_notes: The word 'ictericia' appears in the source as 'itiricia,' a common period variant/OCR conflation, silently corrected. 'Cámaras de sangre' is a period term for bloody flux or dysentery. 'Alquitara' is an alembic or still used for distillation. 'Drama' refers to the apothecary's dram as a unit of weight. The header '196 OBRAS DEL PADRE' is a page/volume marker and has been excluded from the translated body text.

results.title_inferred: On the Saltica herb
results.original_text:
CAPITULO XCV

De la saltica

Esta es una yerba pequeñuela de color pardo, fea a la vista, cuyos tallos acaban en una flor más blanca, pequeña y desflocada y entrapada, así ella como sus pequeñuelas hojas, de una sustancia como algodón o blanda lana, y todas las hojas, aparradas por el suelo. Es caliente y seca y muy amarga y de un olor penetrativo y enfadoso. Toman los indios su cocimiento en ayunas, cuando se sienten con alguna opilación en el estómago, hígado y bazo. Así mismo provoca la orina, quita el dolor del estómago, de ijada y riñones, y es contra la cólica; demás desto, haciendo del cocimiento lamedor con azúcar y tomado a menudo, es contra la tos y limpia el pecho de las materias así de dolores de costado como de heridas penetrantes.
results.translation:
CHAPTER XCV

On the Saltica

This is a small herb of brownish color, unpleasant to look upon, whose stems end in a rather white, small, ragged and tattered flower [desflocada y entrapada], as are its tiny leaves, which are of a substance like cotton or soft wool, with all the leaves spread close to the ground. It is hot and dry [in the Galenic sense], very bitter, and possessed of a penetrating and disagreeable smell. The Indians take its decoction on an empty stomach when they feel some obstruction [opilación] in the stomach, liver, or spleen. Likewise, it promotes urination, relieves pain in the stomach, flanks [ijada], and kidneys, and is effective against colic. Moreover, by making a lozenge [lamedor] from its decoction with sugar, taken frequently, it is effective against coughs and cleanses the chest of matter arising both from pleurisy [dolores de costado] and from penetrating wounds.

entities_mentioned.name: saltica
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Possibly a species of Gnaphalium or Achyrocline (family Asteraceae), given the cottony/woolly texture of leaves and flowers and Andean provenance; alternatively a Gamochaeta or related genus
entities_mentioned.notes: The description of small brownish herb with white, ragged, cottony flowers and leaves spread close to the ground is consistent with woolly-leaved Asteraceae common in Andean flora. 'Saltica' does not correspond to a well-documented colonial name in standard botanical indices; confident modern identification is not possible from description alone.

preparation_methods: Decoction taken on an empty stomach (for obstruction, pain, colic, diuretic effect)
Decoction combined with sugar to make a lozenge/electuary [lamedor], taken frequently (for cough and chest complaints)

results.translator_notes: The term 'lamedor' denotes a thick syrup or electuary meant to be licked or swallowed slowly, a standard early modern pharmaceutical preparation. 'Opilación' refers to Galenic obstruction of the organs. 'Ijada' denotes flank or side pain, associated with the kidneys or spleen. 'Dolores de costado' typically indicates pleurisy or intercostal pain in colonial medical usage. The OCR ligature 'co¬ lor' and similar line-break hyphens have been silently corrected in the original_text field.

results.title_inferred: On the Tinyatinja and Tulma plants: descriptions, temperaments, and medicinal uses
results.original_text:
CAPITULO XCVI

De la tinyatinja

Esta es una yerba de un codo en alto, la cual está verde todo el año; pica la lengua, si la gustan, y su temperamento es caliente. Vale su cocimiento contra el dolor de ijada, y particularmente si sobre el dolor se aplican las hojas calientes rociadas con vino; y asimismo aplicadas por esta orden, quitan todo dolor de causa fría.

CAPITULO XCVII

De la tulma

Es la tulma una yerba de hojas menudas, las ramillas un poco coloradas y aparradas con la tierra, y echa una flor morada y tiene las raíces coloradas. Es de temperamento caliente y seco y muy provocativa de sudor. Su cocimiento, bebido de ordinario, aprovecha contra la detención de orina, quita el dolor de ijada y purga el estómago, vientre y la madre de las mujeres reciemparidas; es contra el pasmo, perlesía y miembros encogidos. Sus hojas majadas sueldan y secan las heridas frescas.
results.translation:
CHAPTER XCVI

On the Tinyatinja

This is an herb about one cubit [approximately 18 inches] in height, which remains green throughout the year; it stings the tongue if tasted, and its temperament [complexion, in humoral terms] is hot. Its decoction is effective against pain of the flank [dolor de ijada: pain in the side or kidney/flank region], and particularly if the warm leaves, sprinkled with wine, are applied directly over the site of the pain; applied in this manner, they likewise relieve all pain arising from a cold cause [i.e., pain of cold humoral origin].

CHAPTER XCVII

On the Tulma

The tulma is an herb with small leaves; its little branches are somewhat reddish and spread close to the ground; it produces a purple flower and has reddish roots. Its temperament is hot and dry, and it is highly provocative of sweat [diaphoretic]. Its decoction, taken regularly as a drink, is beneficial against urinary retention, relieves flank pain, and purges the stomach, bowels, and womb [la madre] of recently delivered women [reciemparidas]; it is also used against spasms [pasmo], palsy [perlesía], and contracted or shrunken limbs. Its leaves, pounded, close and dry fresh wounds.

entities_mentioned.name: tinyatinja
entities_mentioned.notes: Described as a perennial herb about one cubit tall, with a pungent/stinging taste and hot humoral temperament. Used in decoction and as a topical warm-leaf poultice with wine for flank/kidney pain and pain of cold origin. The name appears to be Quechua or a regional Andean vernacular; no confident modern botanical identification has been established.

entities_mentioned.name: tulma
entities_mentioned.notes: Described as a low-growing, spreading herb with small leaves, reddish stems and roots, and a purple flower. Hot and dry temperament; strongly diaphoretic. Used internally (decoction) for urinary retention, flank pain, postpartum uterine purging, spasms, palsy, and contracted limbs; used externally (pounded leaves) to close and dry fresh wounds. Likely an Andean species; name appears Quechua in origin but confident modern identification is not possible without further botanical detail.

results.translator_notes: 1. 'Dolor de ijada' is consistently rendered as 'flank pain'; it conventionally referred in early modern Spanish medical writing to pain in the side, flank, or kidney region, often associated with kidney stones or colic. 2. 'La madre' literally means 'the womb/uterus' and is here translated as 'womb.' 3. 'Pasmo' is a broad early modern term for spasm, tetanus, or convulsive/contractile conditions; 'perlesía' denotes palsy or paralysis. 4. 'Aparradas con la tierra' means the branches spread out flat against or very close to the ground (a prostrate or decumbent growth habit). 5. OCR artifacts corrected silently: 'CAPITOL XGVII' → 'CAPITULO XCVII'; 'mny' → 'muy'; 'sueldan j secan las herida frescas' → 'sueldan y secan las heridas frescas.' 6. Asterisks in the original OCR output (used as period substitutes) have been replaced with full stops.

results.title_inferred: On the Talquina plant: properties and medicinal uses
results.original_text:
CAPITULO XCVIII
De la talquina

La talquina se levanta un codo de la tierra; tiene las hojas algo blanquecinas y mucilaginosas; echa flores amarillas y unas vainillas con la semilla, que es como garbancillos. Es caliente y húmeda esta yerba, y majada y aplicada en emplasto en las apostemas abiertas y heridas dolorosas, mitiga el dolor y dispone la parte a que críe buenas materias; y aplicada con miel rosada en las úlceras, las mundifica y encarna. Majada con malvavisco y aplicada en forma de emplasto, resuelve todo tumor.
results.translation:
CHAPTER XCVIII
On the Talquina

The talquina rises one cubit [approximately 18 inches] from the ground; its leaves are somewhat whitish and mucilaginous; it bears yellow flowers and small pods containing seeds that resemble small chickpeas. This herb is hot and moist in temperament [humoural classification], and when pounded and applied as a poultice [emplasto] to open abscesses [apostemas] and painful wounds, it relieves pain and prepares the affected part to generate healthy matter [buenas materias]. Applied with rose honey [miel rosada] to ulcers, it cleanses [mundifica] and promotes the growth of new flesh [encarna]. Pounded together with marshmallow and applied as a poultice, it resolves all swelling [tumor].

entities_mentioned.name: talquina
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Possibly a species of Sida or Abutilon (family Malvaceae), given the mucilaginous leaves, yellow flowers, and pod-like fruits; alternatively a Crotalaria sp. (Fabaceae) given the chickpea-like seeds in pods
entities_mentioned.notes: The combination of mucilaginous whitish leaves and yellow flowers suggests Malvaceae, but the seed description ('like small chickpeas' in pods) is more consistent with Fabaceae. The name 'talquina' does not correspond unambiguously to a well-documented species in standard botanical literature on colonial Peru. Further manuscript comparison recommended.

entities_mentioned.name: malvavisco
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Althaea officinalis (marshmallow)
entities_mentioned.notes: Standard early modern Spanish term for marshmallow, used throughout colonial medical literature. Commonly combined with other plants in poultice preparations as an emollient and anti-inflammatory agent.

entities_mentioned.name: miel rosada
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Rose honey (a pharmaceutical preparation of honey infused with rose petals)
entities_mentioned.notes: A standard preparation in Galenic pharmacy, used topically to cleanse and promote healing of ulcers.

results.translator_notes: The source text contains several OCR artifacts: 'tulquina' in the chapter heading corrected to 'talquina' per the chapter title and internal consistency; 'k' corrected to 'la'; 'j' corrected to 'y'; 'htimeda' corrected to 'húmeda'; 'áolc^' corrected to 'dolor'; 'buenai' corrected to 'buenas'; 'tod&' corrected to 'toda'. The humoural quality description ('caliente y húmeda') reflects Galenic medical theory standard in 17th-century Iberian and colonial medicine. 'Apostemas' refers to abscesses or purulent swellings. 'Mundifica y encarna' is technical therapeutic vocabulary: mundification (cleansing of an ulcer) and incarnation (promotion of new flesh growth).

results.title_inferred: On the Anocarazapallo, or Bitter Cucumber
results.original_text:
CAPITULO XCIX

Del anocar a zapallo

Anocarazapallo es tanto en la lengua del Perú como decir zapallo o calabaza de perro; así llaman los indios a cierta planta que los españoles nombran cohombro amargo, por parecerse al que trae Dioscórides en su tratado. Enjuagándose con su cocimiento, mitiga el dolor de la dentadura. Echado el zumo de las hojas tibio en el oído doloroso, le quita el dolor. Las hojas cocidas en vinagre y aplicadas en forma de emplasto, quitan el dolor agudo de la gota; el cocimiento de las hojas quita las señales del rostro, y su zumo, dado a los hidrópicos, en ayunas, desde tres onzas hasta cuatro, con un poco de vino, les disminuye el agua del vientre.
results.translation:
CHAPTER XCIX

On the Anocarazapallo

'Anocarazapallo' means in the language of Peru something like 'zapallo [gourd] or dog's calabash'; this is what the Indians call a certain plant that the Spaniards name 'bitter cucumber' [cohombro amargo], on account of its resemblance to the one described by Dioscorides in his treatise. Rinsing the mouth with its decoction alleviates toothache. The juice of the leaves, applied warm into a painful ear, relieves the pain. The leaves, boiled in vinegar and applied as a poultice [emplasto], relieve the acute pain of gout. The decoction of the leaves removes blemishes [señales] from the face. Its juice, given to dropsical patients [hidrópicos] on an empty stomach, in doses of three to four ounces with a little wine, reduces the accumulation of water in the belly.

entities_mentioned.name: Anocarazapallo / cohombro amargo (bitter cucumber)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Probably Cucumis anguria (West Indian gherkin) or more likely Ecballium elaterium (squirting cucumber), the latter being the 'bitter cucumber' of Dioscorides widely referenced in early modern herbals
entities_mentioned.notes: Cobo explicitly links this plant to the 'bitter cucumber' (cohombro amargo) described by Dioscorides (De Materia Medica, Book IV), which most classical and Renaissance authorities identify as Ecballium elaterium. However, the Peruvian indigenous name and New World context introduce ambiguity; it may be a local cucurbit perceived as analogous rather than identical to the Old World species.

entities_mentioned.name: Dioscórides
entities_mentioned.notes: Reference to the Greek physician Pedanius Dioscorides (1st century AD) and his De Materia Medica, the standard pharmacological authority of the period.

results.translator_notes: Several OCR corruptions were silently corrected: 'lenpt' → 'lengua'; 'calabm' → 'calabaza'; 'ce-hombro amargo^' → 'cohombro amargo'; 'qs?' → 'que'; 'mm' → 'zumo'; 'dolorcm' → 'doloroso'; 'es»plasto' → 'emplasto'; 'te' (×2) → 'los' and 'les'; 'omu' → 'onzas'. The medical uses described—decoction for toothache, warm leaf juice for earache, vinegar poultice for gout, facial blemish removal, and diuretic/dropsical treatment—closely parallel the therapeutic properties attributed to Ecballium elaterium in Dioscorides and in Andrés Laguna's influential 1555 Spanish commentary on Dioscorides, which Cobo likely consulted.

results.title_inferred: On the Chamico plant
results.original_text:
CAPITULO C

Del chamico

La yerba que los indios llaman chamico crece un codo, poco más o menos; tiene las hojas mayores algo que los dedos, con unas puntas a los lados; echa una flor blanca de hechura de campanilla, pequeña, que se convierte en unas cabezuelas del tamaño de las adormideras, cubiertas de agudas espinas, en cuya cavidad se encierra la semilla, que es como de rábano, la cual dicen que es tan fría como el apio. Tomado su cocimiento, adormece los sentidos. Usan los indios dél para embriagarse, y si se toma mucha cantidad, saca de sentido a una persona de manera que, teniendo los ojos abiertos, no ve ni conoce. Suélense hacer grandes males con esta bebida; y aún no ha mucho tiempo que sucedió en este reino, que yendo camino un conocido mío con otro compañero, éste, para robarle, le dio a beber chamico, con que el paciente salió de juicio y estuvo tan furioso, que desnudo, en camisa, se iba a echar en un río. Agarráronlo como a loco y lo detuvieron, y estuvo desta suerte sin volver en sí dos días. El zumo de las hojas desta yerba, mezclado con unas gotas de vinagre y aplicado sobre el hígado y espinazo, quita la intemperancia cálida y es contra las fiebres ardientes; y el cocimiento de las mismas hojas, bebido de ordinario, es contra la calentura continua.
results.translation:
CHAPTER C

On the Chamico

The herb that the Indians call chamico grows to about one cubit [approximately 18 inches] in height, more or less. Its leaves are somewhat larger than one's fingers, with pointed tips along the sides. It produces a small white flower in the shape of a little bell, which turns into small heads the size of poppy capsules, covered with sharp spines, within whose cavity the seed is enclosed; the seed resembles that of the radish, and is said to be as cooling [in humoral temperament] as celery. A decoction of it taken internally dulls the senses. The Indians use it to intoxicate themselves, and if a large quantity is consumed, it deprives a person of their senses in such a way that, though their eyes remain open, they neither see nor recognize anything. Great harms are commonly wrought by means of this drink; and indeed it was not long ago that there occurred in this kingdom an incident involving an acquaintance of mine who was traveling with a companion: the companion, in order to rob him, gave him chamico to drink, whereupon the victim lost his mind and became so frenzied that, stripped to his shirt, he set out to throw himself into a river. He was seized as one would seize a madman and was restrained, and he remained in this state without recovering his senses for two days. The juice of the leaves of this herb, mixed with a few drops of vinegar and applied externally over the liver and spine, removes excessive heat [intemperancia cálida] and is effective against burning fevers; and the decoction of the same leaves, taken regularly as a drink, is effective against continuous fever.

entities_mentioned.name: chamico
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Datura stramonium or Datura ferox (Jimsonweed / thornapple, family Solanaceae)
entities_mentioned.notes: All described features—bell-shaped white flower, spiny seed capsules, seeds resembling radish seed, powerful hallucinogenic and deliriant effects (open eyes yet unseeing, frenzy, prolonged unconsciousness)—are consistent with Datura spp. D. ferox is particularly common in the Andean region. The plant was widely documented in colonial sources under this Quechua-derived name. Cobo's humoral characterization of the seed as 'cold' aligns with standard 17th-century Galenic frameworks applied to New World plants.

entities_mentioned.name: apio
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Apium graveolens (celery or wild celery)
entities_mentioned.notes: Used here as a comparative humoral reference point: celery was considered 'cold' in Galenic medicine, serving as a benchmark for chamico's cooling temperament.

entities_mentioned.name: adormideras
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Papaver somniferum (opium poppy)
entities_mentioned.notes: Mentioned only as a size comparison for chamico's seed capsules, not as an independently treated entity.

results.translator_notes: 1. OCR errors silently corrected: 'mico' → 'chamico' (first line, truncated by scan); 'j Wedos' → 'los dedos'; 'cxibiertas' → 'cubiertas'; 'cxial' → 'cual'; 'ÍEtrioso' → 'furioso'; 'bolas' → 'hojas' (in 'cocimiento de las mismas bolas'); 'edentura' → 'calentura'. 2. The phrase 'intemperance cálida' [intemperancia cálida] is a standard Galenic humoral term meaning an excess of the hot quality in a bodily organ; rendered as 'excessive heat' in translation with the original bracketed. 3. The anecdotal account of criminal use of chamico as a stupefying agent for robbery is a recurrent trope in colonial Peruvian literature and has ethnobotanical significance regarding Datura's documented use as a soporific poison. 4. Three distinct medical uses are recorded: (a) oral decoction as a psychoactive/narcotic; (b) leaf juice mixed with vinegar applied topically to liver and spine for febrile inflammatory conditions; (c) oral decoction for continuous (hectic) fever.

results.title_inferred: The Herb of Puebla: A Poisonous Plant Causing Fatal Frenzy in Animals
results.original_text:
CAPITULO CI
De la yerba de la Puebla

En la Puebla de los Angeles nace una yerba bien conocida en toda la Nueva España con nombre de yerba de la Puebla; es de hechura de zacate, vellosa, y echa una florecilla amarilla. Es yerba muy ponzoñosa, y tiene tal propiedad, que en comiéndola cualquier animal parte de carrera con gran furia, y así cesa de correr y saltar hasta caer muerto. Usan della para matar lobos y perros, dándoles a comer carne que haya estado en adobo hecho desta yerba, y en comiéndola el perro, da a correr sin cesar hasta que, molido de correr y brincar, muere. Y se ha experimentado, que lo que principalmente mata a los animales es el molerse ellos corriendo y brincando; y en confirmación desto,

me contó una persona, que comió de la carne inficionada con esta yerba un perro suyo acaso, al cual tomó y amarró muy bien de manera que no pudiese correr ni brincar; y que desta manera, no embargante que el perro hacía fuerza por soltarse, no murió, sino que digirió la ponzoña; mas pelóse todo.
results.translation:
CHAPTER CI
Of the Herb of Puebla

In the city of Puebla de los Ángeles there grows an herb well known throughout all of New Spain by the name of yerba de la Puebla [herb of Puebla]. It resembles a grass [zacate], is hairy, and bears a small yellow flower. It is a highly poisonous herb, and has such a property that when any animal eats it, it immediately sets off running with great fury, and does not cease running and leaping until it falls dead. It is used for killing wolves and dogs by giving them meat that has been soaked in a marinade [adobo] made from this herb; and upon eating it, the dog begins to run without stopping until, worn out from running and leaping, it dies. It has been observed through experience that what chiefly kills the animals is the physical exhaustion they bring upon themselves through running and jumping. In confirmation of this,

a person told me that one of his dogs happened to eat meat contaminated with this herb, whereupon he seized the animal and tied it up very securely so that it could neither run nor leap; and that in this manner, notwithstanding the dog's straining to break free, it did not die, but instead digested the poison—though it lost all its hair.

entities_mentioned.name: yerba de la Puebla
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Possibly Karwinskia humboldtiana (tullidora/coyotillo) or a species of Astragalus (locoweed); the described neurological frenzy and exhaustion-driven death also suggest possible relation to Datura or Senecio species. The yellow flower and grass-like form may point toward a Senecio or related Asteraceae genus.
entities_mentioned.notes: The symptom profile—uncontrollable running and leaping until death by exhaustion, with survival possible if movement is physically restrained—is unusual and does not map cleanly onto any single well-documented species. The detail that restrained animals survived but lost all hair is a distinctive pharmacological clue. Locoweed (Astragalus spp.) causes erratic behavior in livestock but not typically this acute presentation. Further archival and ethnobotanical research is warranted.

results.translator_notes: Several OCR errors were silently corrected: 'wba' → 'yerba', 'bechxira' → 'hechura', 'zacate^' → 'zacate', 'íiíí' → 'así', 'Muerto. Usan' (erroneous capitalization mid-sentence context resolved), 'pxi-diése' → 'pudiese', 'Aneando' → 'brincando'. The word 'adobo' is retained in brackets in the translation as it refers to a specific marinade/steeping preparation that may have technical relevance to the method of poison delivery. The anecdote about the restrained dog is a remarkably early empirical observation suggesting the poison's lethality is partly or wholly mediated by physical exertion rather than direct toxicity alone — an observation Cobo presents as experimentally confirmed ('se ha experimentado').

results.title_inferred: Spider-herb and Bead-herb: Two Peruvian Plants from the Lima Region
results.original_text:
CAPITULO CII

De la yerba de la araña

Han puesto a esta yerba los españoles este nombre, tomado del efecto que hace, que es curar las picaduras de arañas ponzoñosas. Nace en lugares incultos, y en este valle de Lima se halla mucha junto al puerto del Callao; es muy parecida en el tamaño, ramos y hojas al tomillo, sólo que no tiene olor alguno. Aplícase a la picadura de araña desta manera: hácese della cocimiento, con él lavan muy bien la parte donde está la picadura, apretándola para que salga la materia, y luego echan en ella los polvos desta misma yerba, con que se ven cada día maravillosas curas.

CAPITULO CIII

De la yerba de las cuentas

Una particular especie de yerba nace en esta tierra, que tomando el nombre del fruto que lleva, la llaman comúnmente yerba de las cuentas. Crece como dos o tres palmos y echa pocas hojas, las cuales son largas y angostas, algo parecidas a las de la caña dulce cuando comienza a nacer. En la cumbre de su tallo echa unos granos pardos o cenicientos, muy duros, lisos y relucientes, del tamaño de garbanzos, un poquito mayores, salvo que no son redondos perfectamente, sino algo ahusados, a los cuales llama el vulgo lágrimas de Moisés; dellos, horadándolos, se suelen hacer rosarios para los muchachos y gente ruda, porque no son de alguna estima.
results.translation:
CHAPTER CII

On the spider-herb [yerba de la araña]

The Spaniards gave this herb its name, derived from the effect it produces, which is to cure the bites of venomous spiders. It grows in uncultivated places, and in this valley of Lima it is found in great abundance near the port of Callao. It closely resembles thyme in size, branching, and leaves, except that it has no scent whatsoever. It is applied to a spider bite in the following manner: a decoction [cocimiento] is made from it, with which the affected area is washed very thoroughly, while pressing it so that the matter [venom/pus] is expelled; the powdered form of this same herb is then applied to the wound, and by this means one witnesses remarkable cures on a daily basis.

CHAPTER CIII

On the bead-herb [yerba de las cuentas]

A particular species of herb grows in this land which, taking its name from the fruit it bears, is commonly called the bead-herb [yerba de las cuentas]. It grows to about two or three hand-spans [palmos] in height and produces few leaves, which are long and narrow, somewhat resembling those of sugarcane when it first begins to sprout. At the top of its stalk it bears grains that are brownish or ash-grey, very hard, smooth, and lustrous, roughly the size of chickpeas or slightly larger, though not perfectly round but somewhat spindle-shaped; these the common people call 'Moses' tears' [lágrimas de Moisés]. By piercing them, rosaries are commonly made for children and simple folk, as the beads are not held in any great esteem.

entities_mentioned.name: yerba de la araña
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Possibly a species within Chenopodium, Alternanthera, or a small-leaved coastal herb of the Lima littoral; the thyme-like description without scent and coastal Lima habitat may suggest Alternanthera halimifolia or a related Amaranthaceae, but confident identification is not possible
entities_mentioned.notes: Described as: thyme-like in size, branching, and leaves; scentless; grows in uncultivated ground near Callao, Lima. Used exclusively against venomous spider bites. Preparation: (1) decoction used as a wash on the bite site with expression of venom/matter; (2) dried powder of the same herb applied topically to the wound. Ailment treated: bites of venomous spiders.

entities_mentioned.name: yerba de las cuentas / lágrimas de Moisés
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Coix lacryma-jobi (Job's tears, Poaceae)
entities_mentioned.notes: The description of hard, smooth, lustrous, ash-grey to brownish, ovoid-spindle-shaped grains borne at the top of the stalk, on a grass-like plant with long narrow leaves resembling young sugarcane, is a classic description of Coix lacryma-jobi. The folk name 'lágrimas de Moisés' (Moses' tears) is a regional variant of the more widely used 'lágrimas de Job' (Job's tears). No medicinal use is attributed in this passage; the grains are noted only as material for rosaries and children's beads.

results.translator_notes: 1. 'CAPITULO cm' in the OCR source is a misread of 'CAPITULO CIII' (Roman numeral III rendered as 'm'); silently corrected. 2. 'caña dxilce' is an OCR error for 'caña dulce' (sugarcane); silently corrected. 3. 'nn poquito' is an OCR error for 'un poquito'; silently corrected. 4. 'BERruBE' in the page footer is an OCR error for 'BERNABE'; noted but not part of the translated body text. 5. The word 'materia' in the spider-bite passage carries the dual sense of 'venom' and 'suppurative matter' common in early modern medical writing; rendered with both possibilities in brackets. 6. 'palmo' (hand-span) was a variable colonial unit of roughly 20–21 cm; retained as 'hand-span' with the original in brackets.

results.title_inferred: On the herb called Y
results.original_text:
CAPITULO CIV
De la yerba dicha Y

En muchas partes de las Indias, especialmente en las islas de Barlovento, nace una yerba que los indios de la isla Española llamaban Y. Hace una rama luenga, y levántase como la correhuela o la yedra, y tiene la hoja casi de aquella hechura; la cual es gran pasto para el ganado de cerda y lo engorda mucho, y le es tan provechosa como la bellota en España. También es yerba medicinal, y en algunas partes se purgan con ella.
results.translation:
CHAPTER CIV
On the herb called Y

In many parts of the Indies, especially in the Windward Islands [islas de Barlovento], there grows a herb which the Indians of Hispaniola called Y. It sends out a long branch and climbs like bindweed [correhuela] or ivy [yedra], and its leaf is nearly the same shape as theirs. This plant is excellent fodder for swine and fattens them greatly, being as beneficial to them as acorns are in Spain. It is also a medicinal herb, and in some places it is used as a purgative.

entities_mentioned.name: Y (herb)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Ipomoea batatas or a related Ipomoea sp. (sweet potato family / morning glory family, Convolvulaceae)
entities_mentioned.notes: The climbing habit, ivy-like leaf shape, use as swine fodder, and purgative properties are consistent with various Ipomoea species common to the Caribbean. The name 'Y' is an extremely brief indigenous Taíno designation recorded by Cobo; without further corroborating sources the identification remains uncertain. Some Ipomoea species have latex with purgative resin glycosides.

entities_mentioned.name: correhuela
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Convolvulus arvensis (field bindweed)
entities_mentioned.notes: Used by Cobo as a comparative morphological reference familiar to Spanish readers.

entities_mentioned.name: yedra
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Hedera helix (common ivy)
entities_mentioned.notes: Used alongside correhuela as a secondary morphological comparison for the leaf shape and climbing habit.

medical_botanical_extraction.preparation_method: Not specified; the text states only that 'in some places they purge with it' [en algunas partes se purgan con ella]
medical_botanical_extraction.additional_uses: Fodder for swine; described as fattening livestock comparably to acorns in Spain

results.translator_notes: The chapter title 'De Ja yerba dicha Y' in the source is an OCR artifact; corrected silently to 'De la yerba dicha Y'. The single-letter indigenous name 'Y' is unusual and may represent a Taíno word truncated by OCR or manuscript damage, though Cobo does elsewhere record very short indigenous plant names. 'Islas de Barlovento' refers to the Windward Islands of the Caribbean. 'Ganado de cerda' literally means 'bristle livestock,' i.e., pigs/swine.

results.title_inferred: The Herb of the Knuckle (Yerba del Ñudillo)
results.original_text:
CAPITULO CV
De la yerba del ñudillo

La yerba del ñudillo se llama así porque todo el tallo es muy ñudoso; crece un estado y echa muchos tallos no más gruesos que la caña del trigo. Su hoja es del talle y grandeza de la del alhelí y con ella curan las heridas. Nace esta yerba en lugares húmedos, y tallos, y hojas es muy tierna y pasto del ganado.
results.translator_notes: The word 'ñudoso' (knotty/jointed) is the basis for the common name 'yerba del ñudillo' (herb of the little knot or knuckle). 'Un estado' is a unit of height roughly equivalent to a man's height (~1.7 m / ~5.5 ft). 'Alhelí' refers to the wallflower (Erysimum/Cheiranthus cheiri), used here as a leaf-size comparison. The phrase 'tallos, y hojas es muy tierna [sic]' is grammatically inconsistent in the original (plural subject with singular adjective), retained and marked [sic] in the original but rendered naturally in translation. Modern identification is tentative; the description of a jointed-stemmed, moisture-loving herb used for wound healing in the Americas is consistent with several Polygonum or Persicaria species (knotweeds), though certainty is low.
results.translation:
CHAPTER CV
On the herb of the knuckle [yerba del ñudillo]

The herb of the knuckle [yerba del ñudillo] is so called because the entire stem is very knotty [jointed]; it grows to the height of a man and puts out many stalks no thicker than the stem of a wheat plant. Its leaf is of the shape and size of that of the wallflower [alhelí], and with it they treat wounds. This herb grows in moist places; its stalks and leaves are very tender and serve as fodder for livestock.

entities_mentioned.name: yerba del ñudillo
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Possibly Polygonum or Persicaria sp. (knotweed/smartweed family, Polygonaceae)
entities_mentioned.notes: The common name ('knotty herb') and description of jointed stems, moist habitat, tender leaves used as animal fodder, and wound-healing application are consistent with members of Polygonaceae, particularly Polygonum or Persicaria. However, several other jointed-stemmed New World herbs could fit this description.

entities_mentioned.name: alhelí
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Erysimum cheiri (wallflower) or Matthiola incana (stock); used here as a morphological reference, not a medicinal entity
entities_mentioned.notes: Used by Cobo as a leaf-shape and size comparator. 'Alhelí' in 17th-century Spanish colonial usage most commonly referred to the wallflower (Cheiranthus / Erysimum cheiri), though the term was sometimes applied loosely to Matthiola incana or related plants.

entities_mentioned.name: heridas (wounds)
entities_mentioned.notes: Condition treated: wounds (external). Preparation method: not specified; implied topical application of the leaf ('con ella curan las heridas' — 'with it they treat wounds'). No further detail on preparation (poultice, decoction, etc.) is given in this passage.

results.title_inferred: On Zacate and Acamalote (New Spain Fodder Grasses)
results.original_text:
CAPITULO CVII

Del zacate

La yerba que en la Nueva España corresponde al hicho es el zacate, que si bien es nombre genérico para toda suerte de yerba silvestre, con todo eso, se aplica a cierta especie della, que es más general en aquel reino, de que están cubiertos los campos y sabanas, particularmente de la Tierra caliente, y se sustentan los ganados y bestias de carga. Crece el zacate dos o tres codos en alto, de modo que da en los estribos a los caminantes; y para que con la humedad que tiene del rocío no moje los pies a los que caminan, usan de estribos de palo, cerrados por delante, y de botas untadas con ule. Nace muy espesa esta yerba, y es parecida su hoja a la de la caña dulce cuando pequeña. Queman estos pastos cada año en pasando las aguas, como en el Perú el hicho, para que nazca nueva yerba. Hay deste zacate diferentes especies; de el que es más delgado y blando, hinchen los arrieros las enjalmas, porque aprieta bien y es muy liviano.

CAPITULO CVIII

Del acamalote

Es el acamalote una yerba tan parecida a la planta del maíz en el talle, caña y hoja, que vista en los campos engaña a muchos. No produce semilla alguna; véndese en los pueblos de la Nueva España como el zacate, y con él engordan mucho las bestias.
results.translation:
CHAPTER CVII

On Zacate

The grass that in New Spain corresponds to the hicho [of Peru] is the zacate. Although this is a generic name for all manner of wild grass, it is nonetheless applied to a particular species thereof, which is the most widespread in that kingdom, covering the fields and savannas—particularly in the Tierra Caliente [hot lowlands]—and upon which livestock and pack animals are sustained. Zacate grows two or three cubits [roughly three to four feet] in height, such that it reaches the stirrups of travelers; and so that the moisture it carries from the dew does not wet the feet of those who pass through it, they use wooden stirrups closed at the front, and boots greased with ule [natural rubber]. This grass grows very densely, and its leaf resembles that of the sugarcane when young. These pastures are burned every year after the rainy season, just as the hicho is burned in Peru, so that new grass may sprout. There are different species of this zacate; of the variety that is thinner and softer, muleteers stuff their pack-saddle pads [enjalmas], because it packs down well and is very lightweight.

CHAPTER CVIII

On Acamalote

The acamalote is a grass so similar to the maize plant in its stature, stalk, and leaf, that when seen in the fields it deceives many people. It produces no seed whatsoever; it is sold in the towns of New Spain just as zacate is, and with it beasts of burden are greatly fattened.

entities_mentioned.name: zacate
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Likely refers broadly to grasses of the genera Paspalum, Andropogon, or Hyparrhenia (e.g., Hyparrhenia rufa, jaraguá grass), or possibly Paspalum spp.; the term is generic in Mesoamerican Spanish but here denotes a dominant savanna grass of the hot lowlands
entities_mentioned.notes: Cobo explicitly notes 'zacate' is a generic term applied to a specific dominant species. The description of height (2–3 cubits), dense growth, leaf resembling young sugarcane, and use in the Tierra Caliente is consistent with several tall tropical grasses. No medicinal use described; primary use is as forage and packing material.

entities_mentioned.name: hicho
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Stipa ichu (Ruiz & Pav.) Kunth — ichu or puna grass of the Andean highlands
entities_mentioned.notes: Used by Cobo as a comparative reference point; described in an earlier chapter. The annual burning practice mirrors Andean ichu management.

entities_mentioned.name: ule
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Castilla elastica Sessé — natural rubber (hule/ule), from the ule tree of Mesoamerica
entities_mentioned.notes: Used here as a waterproofing agent for boots, not in a medicinal context.

entities_mentioned.name: acamalote
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Possibly Echinochloa colona (L.) Link or a related barnyard grass; some scholars associate 'acamalote' with Paspalum fasciculatum Willd. or related large-leafed aquatic/semi-aquatic grasses
entities_mentioned.notes: Described as maize-like in stalk, leaf, and overall form but producing no seed. Sold as fodder in New Spain towns. The name may also be applied to water hyacinth relatives in some regions, but Cobo's description here fits a terrestrial fodder grass.

results.translator_notes: The OCR rendered 'corresponde' as 'cörresponde' and 'zacate' with a caret symbol on first mention; these have been silently corrected. 'Codos' (cubits) is approximately 16–18 inches each, so two to three cubits equals roughly 32–54 inches. 'Enjalmas' are pack-saddle pads or stuffed saddle cushions used on mules and donkeys; the term is preserved in brackets in the cleaned text. No medicinal preparations appear in this segment; both chapters concern agronomy and animal husbandry rather than materia medica proper.

results.title_inferred: Shrubs Found in the Indies of the Same Species as Those of Spain
results.original_text:
CAPITULO PRIMERO

De las matas que se hallaron en estas Indias de una misma especie con las de España

En el segundo grado de plantas se comprehenden todas aquellas los latinos llaman frutex y el Calepino romancea mata; porque, aunque este nombre es común para toda suerte de plantas, ya está apropiado para significar solas aquéllas que tienen mayor parentesco con los árboles que con las yerbas, así por ser de tan larga vida, como ellos, como por constar de materia más sólida, recia y leñosa que todas las yerbas cadañales, que, dado una vez su fruto, mueren. Habiendo, pues, de tratar en este libro de todas las plantas desta segunda clase naturales deste Nuevo Mundo, doy principio en este capítulo por las que se hallaron acá semejantes en especie a las naturales de España y conocidas en ella.

De las cuales, la primera y más noble es la vid. Esta se halló salvaje en muchas partes, como es en las islas de Barlovento y en algunas provincias de la Nueva España; dado que no la había en todo este reino del Perú, y así no tiene nombre en ninguna lengua de las naturales dél; ni en las tierras que de suyo nace fue jamás conocida ni cultivada de los indios. El fruto que lleva son unas uvillas pequeñas, negras, muy silvestres y agrias, cuya acerbidad fue sin duda causa de que los indios no hiciesen caso desta planta para trasponerla en sus huertas y domesticarla; y por haber traído consigo los españoles de las vides domésticas de Europa, tampoco ellos se han aplicado a cultivar y hacer hortenses éstas de las Indias, con que se han quedado tan salvajes e inútiles como antes.

La planta deste género más útil y general que nace en toda esta tierra es la del algodón, que los indios del Perú llaman, en la lengua quichua, utcu, y en la aimará, quela. Hay tres o cuatro suertes dello, y de todas se halla silvestre, el cual se diferencia del doméstico y hortense en ser las matas más bajas y menores los capullos. El más fino algodón de cuantos yo he visto por acá es una especie dél que nace en la provincia de Chachapoyas, diócesis de Trujillo, el cual es tan blando y delicado que parece fina seda. Lábrase en todas las Indias gran cantidad de ropa y lienzo de algodón, porque lo más de los indios no se visten de otra cosa; y los españoles también se aprovechan dello en muchos usos; y en especial, en esta mar del Sur las velas de todos los navios que navegan en él son de lona o lienzo de algodón; y en todas las Indias se gasta grandísima cantidad en pábilo para velas de cera y sebo, el cual en esta tierra no se hace de otra cosa y es mucho mejor que de lino y cáñamo.

La higuerilla de infierno nace dondequiera, mayormente en las tierras templadas. En algunos valles destos Llanos del Perú suelen sacar aceite de su semilla para que arda en las lámparas de las iglesias; pero esto sólo en pueblos de indios lo he visto hacer, que en las iglesias de españoles, con aceite de olivas se alimentan las lámparas.

Los madroños se hallan en la Nueva España, y donde yo los vi fue en la provincia de la Mixteca. No es su fruto tan crecida como la de los madroños de España ni se hace caso della. En todo este reino del Perú no los hay; ni aun pienso que en toda esta América los hay.

En dondequiera nacen las matas siguientes, que todas son silvestres: zarzamora, aulagas y carrizo; déste hacen esteras para cubrir las casas humildes donde no llueve; y en muchas partes las casas de los indios no son de otros materiales, que más propiamente se pueden llamar chozas.
results.translation:
CHAPTER ONE

On the shrubs found in these Indies of the same species as those of Spain

The second rank of plants comprises all those which the Latins call frutex and which Calepino's dictionary renders in the vernacular as mata [shrub]; for although this term is common to all sorts of plants, it has by now been appropriated to signify solely those which have a greater kinship with trees than with herbs — both by virtue of their long life, like trees, and because they consist of a more solid, tough, and woody substance than all annual herbs [yerbas cadañales], which, once they have borne their fruit, die. Having, then, to treat in this book of all plants of this second class native to this New World, I begin in this chapter with those found here to be of the same species as the plants native to Spain and known there.

Of these, the first and most noble is the vine. This was found growing wild in many places, such as the Windward Islands [islas de Barlovento] and in certain provinces of New Spain; though it was entirely absent from this realm of Peru, and thus has no name in any of the native languages of that land; nor in the lands where it grows of its own accord was it ever known or cultivated by the Indians. The fruit it bears consists of small, black, very wild and bitter little grapes, whose astringency was doubtless the reason the Indians paid no attention to the plant in order to transplant it into their gardens and domesticate it; and since the Spaniards brought with them the cultivated vines of Europe, they likewise have not applied themselves to cultivating and making garden plants of these wild ones of the Indies, so that they have remained as wild and useless as before.

The plant of this genus that is most useful and widespread throughout this land is cotton, which the Indians of Peru call, in the Quechua tongue, utcu, and in Aymara, quela. There are three or four varieties of it, and of all of them a wild form exists, which differs from the domestic and garden variety in that the shrubs are lower and the bolls smaller. The finest cotton of any I have seen here is a species that grows in the province of Chachapoyas, in the diocese of Trujillo, which is so soft and delicate that it resembles fine silk. A great quantity of cloth and cotton linen is produced throughout the Indies, because the greater part of the Indians clothe themselves in nothing else; and the Spaniards also make use of it in many ways. In particular, on this South Sea [Pacific Ocean] the sails of all the ships that sail upon it are made of canvas or cotton linen; and throughout the Indies a very great quantity is consumed as wicking for wax and tallow candles, which in this land are made of nothing else and are much superior to those of flax and hemp.

The castor-oil plant [higuerilla de infierno] grows everywhere, particularly in temperate lands. In certain valleys of the coastal plains [Llanos] of Peru it is customary to press oil from its seed to burn in the lamps of churches; but I have only seen this done in Indian villages, for in Spanish churches the lamps are fed with olive oil.

Strawberry trees [madroños] are found in New Spain; where I saw them was in the province of the Mixteca. Their fruit is not as large as that of the strawberry trees of Spain, and no particular use is made of it. Throughout this realm of Peru there are none; nor do I think they exist anywhere in this America.

The following shrubs grow everywhere and are all wild: blackberry [zarzamora], gorse [aulagas], and reed [carrizo]; from the last, mats are made to cover humble houses in places where it does not rain; and in many places the houses of the Indians are built of no other materials, so that they may more properly be called huts [chozas].

entities_mentioned.name: vid (grape vine)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Vitis spp. (likely Vitis caribaea or related wild American species)
entities_mentioned.notes: Cobo notes wild vines found in the Windward Islands and New Spain, but absent from Peru. Wild fruit described as small, black, and very sour. Not cultivated by indigenous peoples. No Quechua or Aymara name recorded.

entities_mentioned.name: algodón / utcu (Quechua) / quela (Aymara)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Gossypium barbadense or Gossypium hirsutum (American cotton)
entities_mentioned.notes: Three or four varieties described, both wild and cultivated. Wild form distinguished by lower shrubs and smaller bolls. Finest variety from Chachapoyas, diocese of Trujillo. Used for cloth, sail canvas, and candle wicking. No medical use noted in this passage.

entities_mentioned.name: higuerilla de infierno (castor-oil plant)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Ricinus communis
entities_mentioned.notes: Oil extracted from seeds used to fuel church lamps in Indian villages in the coastal valleys (Llanos) of Peru. No medicinal preparation described in this passage, though the name and use are consistent with Ricinus communis. Spanish churches preferred olive oil.

entities_mentioned.name: madroño (strawberry tree)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Arbutus xalapensis (Mexican strawberry tree, New World analogue of Arbutus unedo)
entities_mentioned.notes: Found in New Spain, specifically the Mixteca region. Fruit described as smaller and less notable than the Spanish variety. Absent from Peru. Cobo doubts it exists anywhere in the Americas — this is an error, as Arbutus xalapensis is native to Mexico.

entities_mentioned.name: zarzamora (blackberry)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Rubus spp.
entities_mentioned.notes: Described as wild and widespread. No specific use noted beyond being named.

entities_mentioned.name: aulagas (gorse / furze)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Ulex spp. or possibly a local thorny shrub identified by analogy with Spanish gorse
entities_mentioned.notes: The term 'aulagas' in Spain refers to Ulex europaeus; its use here may denote a morphologically similar New World shrub. No medicinal or utilitarian use noted.

entities_mentioned.name: carrizo (reed)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Phragmites australis or Arundo donax
entities_mentioned.notes: Used to make mats (esteras) for roofing humble houses in arid regions of the Indies. Also used as the primary building material for indigenous dwellings (chozas) in many areas.

results.translator_notes: 1. 'Cadañales' (glossed by Cobo's original editor as 'cadañales o cadañeras') denotes annual or short-lived herbaceous plants that die after fruiting — rendered here as 'annual herbs.' 2. 'Calepino' refers to Ambrogio Calepino's famous polyglot dictionary, widely used in early modern Europe; 'frutex' is the Latin botanical term for a shrub. 3. 'Islas de Barlovento' = the Windward Islands of the Caribbean. 4. 'Mar del Sur' = the Pacific Ocean (South Sea), the standard 17th-century Spanish designation. 5. The OCR rendered 'suyo' as 'ímyo,' 'doméstico' as 'dom^tke,' 'pábilo' as 'pábifo,' 'Nueva' as 'Nucft,' 'fruto' as 'ír^,' 'madroños' as 'madroS«®,' and 'pienso' as 'píjÉ^^so' — all silently corrected as unambiguous. 6. The sentence 'ni aun pienso que en toda esta América los hay' is historically inaccurate; Arbutus xalapensis is native to Mexico, which Cobo had presumably not encountered in the Mixteca region in sufficient abundance to generalize.

De las tunas

En todas estas Indias, así en temples fríos como calientes, nace un linaje de plantas que generalmente los españoles llaman cardones, el cual nombre abraza muchas especies dellos, que unos se dan sólo en tierras frías y otros en calientes y templadas. Convienen todas estas matas entre sí en que ni bien son yerbas, ni árboles, ni aun parecen matas, aunque realmente lo son, porque de todas las plantas de Europa se diferencian notablemente. Viven muchos años, en que difieren de las yerbas; no producen ramas ni hojas, sino unos trozos redondos o gruesas pencas encaramadas e injeridas unas sobre otras; son tiernas y aguanosas, como zabilas, pepinos o calabazas; cortadas, destilan un humor pegajoso como el de la zabila, en que muestran no convenir con los árboles y matas. Están de alto abajo pobladas de agudísimas espinas, mayores que otras, conforme las diferencias de cardones que las producen. results.title_inferred: The Cochineal Cactus and the Cultivation of Cochineal Dye
results.original_text:
CAPITULO III

De la tuna de la grana

Criase la grana en cierta especie de tunas, que es una planta parecida a las demás deste género, salvo que tiene flores y más delgadas hojas y sin las espinas que las otras, sino tan pequeñillas como vello, pues apenas se echan de ver, y unas tunas muy chiquillas y coloradas, que no se comen. Plantan y cultivan esta mata los indios de la Nueva España para la granjería de la grana, y donde hay más heredades della es en la diócesis de Guajaca. El modo de criarse la grana es admirable: no nace igualmente todos los años ni tampoco la crían todas las pencas de una mata: son unos gusanillos colorados, cuyo principio es unas manchuelas blancas que se ven en la hoja de la manera de un polvito de harina que allí hubiese caído acaso; y debajo deste vello blanco se va criando el gusano cubierto con él, desdel tamaño de un grano de mostaza hasta de la grandeza de un garbanzo o frísol; es por las espaldas redondo, ceñido de unas raítas muy sutiles, que lo rodean en torno, y por el vientre es chato; crecen pegados a la hoja, y unas veces proceden naturalmente y otras por industria y arte, deslizando a cierto tiempo a las tunas la semilla del año pasado. Vienen a tener sazón en la Nueva España por cuaresma; y después de cogidos y secos al sol quedan como granos de pimienta. A este color de grana llaman cochinilla en la Nueva España, y en este reino del Perú, magno.
results.translation:
CHAPTER III

On the Cochineal Cactus [tuna de la grana]

Cochineal [grana] is produced on a certain species of prickly pear [tuna], which is a plant resembling the others of its kind, except that it has smaller and more slender pads [hojas] and lacks the spines the others have — or rather, its spines are so tiny that they are no more than fine hairs, scarcely visible — and it bears very small, reddish fruits [tunas] that are not eaten. The Indians of New Spain plant and cultivate this shrub for the trade in cochineal, and the area where the greatest number of plantations [heredades] of it are found is in the diocese of Oaxaca [Guajaca]. The manner in which the cochineal is produced is remarkable: it does not appear equally every year, nor does every pad of a single plant yield it. The insects are tiny reddish grubs, which begin as small white spots seen on the leaf, much as if a little dusting of flour had happened to fall there; and beneath this white fluff the grub grows, covered by it, from the size of a mustard seed up to the size of a chickpea or bean. On the back it is rounded, girdled by very fine little stripes encircling it all around, and on the underside it is flat. They grow attached to the pad, and they arise sometimes naturally and at other times by human industry and art, by transferring the seed [semilla] of the previous year to the cactus pads at a certain season. In New Spain they reach maturity around Lent [cuaresma]; and after being harvested and dried in the sun they remain like grains of pepper. This dye color [color de grana] is called cochinilla in New Spain, and in this kingdom of Peru, magno.

entities_mentioned.name: tuna de la grana
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Opuntia cochenillifera (cochineal cactus / nopal)
entities_mentioned.notes: The spineless or near-spineless cactus cultivated specifically as a host for cochineal insects, distinct from edible nopal species.

entities_mentioned.name: grana / cochinilla / magno
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Dactylopius coccus (cochineal insect)
entities_mentioned.notes: 'Cochinilla' was the term used in New Spain (Mexico); 'magno' was the Peruvian term according to Cobo. The insect was harvested as a crimson dye. The editorial footnote credits Cobo as the most precise early describer of this insect and notes that José de Acosta had already revealed the true animal nature of the drug in his 'Historia Natural y Moral de las Indias' (1590).
