The passage contains no direct medical or pharmaceutical application; cochineal here is treated as a dye commodity, not a medicinal preparation.

results.title_inferred: On Cardones (Columnar and Other Cacti): Species, Uses, and Medicinal Applications
results.original_text:
CAPITULO IV

De los cardones

Al otro genero de matas espinosas que no echan hojas anchas llamamos, generalmente, cardones, en que se comprehenden mucha mayor multiplicidad y variedad de especies que en el linaje de las tunas. Difieren primeramente los cardones en el tamaño, porque los más crecidos son tan altos como cipreses, y los menores no se levantan del suelo. Destos pequeñuelos hay unos delgados como el dedo pulgar, que se extienden sobre la tierra, y otros, que son los menores de todos, nacen en forma de estrella, no tienen espinas, y los comen las bestias; y por ser ellos muy aguanosos, no han menester beber los animales que los comen. Y entre estos dos extremos se hallan de diferente altura.

Demás desto, unos son estériles y otros frutíferos, y destos, unos dan fruta muy sabrosa y delicada; otros, grosera y desabrida, y algunos, del todo inútil, por no ser comestible. Unos son redondos y acanalados como hachas de muchos pábilos; otros, labrada su corteza de un galano escamado; otros, con anchas y largas pencas, y todos están armados de agudas espinas, y unos las tienen mayores que otros. Tratar de cada especie de por sí, sería cosa larga y prolija, porque según la diversidad que dellos he visto, creo pasan de cincuenta. Reducirélas a algunos géneros conforme la mayor similitud que en ellas hay, dando a cada una su capítulo; y de las castas de algunos cardones que hubiere que decir algo en particular, lo haré en capítulo aparte.

Todo género de cardones es bueno para cercar heredades, porque con sus agudas pilas defienden la entrada a los ganados. De algunas castas dellos se hace cola para blanquear las paredes; la cual se hace echando a cocer algunos trozos tiernos, y el agua en que cocieron queda glutinosa y a propósito para el efecto dicho. Item, el zumo de cierto cardón es útil para soldar quebraduras. De otra especie de los grandes que tiene cinco esquinas, se saca por alquitara un agua muy útil para curar la gola, dando baños con ella tibia; y para destilarla, se han de picar los cogollos tiernos para echarlos en la alquitara; y el agua que se destila se guarda en redomas de vidrio para muchos días.

Finalmente, no ha muchos años que se halló en este reino del Perú arbitrio para sacar brea destos cardones, y de hecho se ha sacado alguna que yo he visto, y es muy buena para todos los usos en que sirve la pez, y en particular para pegar cosas quebradas, pues no ha mucho que vi yo a un cantero que pegó con esta brea una piedra grande que se le quebró labrándola.
results.translation:
CHAPTER IV

On the Cardones [Columnar Cacti]

The other genus of thorny shrubs that do not put forth broad leaves we call, generally, cardones [columnar or tree cacti], which encompass a far greater multiplicity and variety of species than does the lineage of the tunas [prickly pears]. The cardones differ first of all in size, because the tallest among them are as high as cypress trees, while the smallest do not rise from the ground. Among these small ones, some are as slender as the thumb and spread across the earth; others, the very smallest of all, grow in the shape of a star, have no spines, and are eaten by livestock; and because they are very watery, the animals that eat them have no need to drink. Between these two extremes there are specimens of varying height.

Beyond this, some are sterile and others fruit-bearing; of the latter, some yield fruit that is very flavorsome and delicate, others coarse and insipid, and some entirely useless because it is not edible. Some are round and ribbed [acanalados] like candles with many wicks; others have their bark worked into a handsome scaly pattern; others have broad and long paddles [pencas], and all are armed with sharp spines, some having larger ones than others. To treat each species individually would be a long and tedious undertaking, for, given the diversity I have observed among them, I believe they number more than fifty. I shall reduce them to a few genera according to their greatest similarities, devoting a chapter to each; and for those varieties of cardones about which there is something particular to say, I shall do so in a separate chapter.

Every kind of cardón is good for fencing estates, because their sharp points [pilas] prevent livestock from entering. From certain varieties of them a glue [cola] is made for whitewashing walls, which is prepared by boiling some tender pieces, whereupon the water in which they were cooked becomes glutinous and suitable for the said purpose. Likewise, the sap [zumo] of a certain cardón is useful for mending [soldar] fractures [quebraduras]. From another species among the large ones — which has five angles [esquinas] — a water is drawn off by alembic [alquitara] that is very useful for treating the throat [gola], by bathing [the affected part] with it while warm; and to distil it, the tender growing tips [cogollos] must be chopped and placed in the alembic; the distilled water is then stored in glass flasks [redomas] for many days.

Finally, not many years ago a method was discovered in this kingdom of Peru for extracting pitch [brea] from these cardones, and indeed some has been obtained, which I have myself seen; it is very good for all the uses to which pitch [pez] is put, and in particular for mending broken things — for not long ago I saw a stonemason mend with this pitch a large stone that had broken as he was dressing it.

entities_mentioned.name: cardones
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Various columnar and other cacti of the family Cactaceae, likely including Cereus, Echinopsis (syn. Trichocereus), Browningia, and related genera native to South America
entities_mentioned.notes: Cobo uses 'cardones' as a broad vernacular umbrella term for non-Opuntia cacti. The species with five angles (cinco esquinas) used medicinally for throat ailments may correspond to a Cereus or Neoraimondia species. The prostrate star-shaped spineless form may refer to a ground-level cactus such as Lobivia or Echinopsis in a juvenile form.

entities_mentioned.name: tunas
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Opuntia spp. (prickly pears), family Cactaceae
entities_mentioned.notes: Used here as a contrasting reference group to the cardones; the tuna / Opuntia is treated separately by Cobo in preceding chapters.

entities_mentioned.name: aguacolla quizca
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Possibly Echinopsis pachanoi (San Pedro cactus) or a related columnar cactus; the Quechua name is noted in Betanzos's footnote
entities_mentioned.notes: Named only in the editorial footnote citing Juan de Betanzos. Used by Inca Yupanqui's order as a mucilaginous plaster admixture for walls. The name 'aguacolla' may relate to 'aguacolla' / huaranhuay but the quizca element means 'thorn' in Quechua, suggesting a spiny cactus.

results.translator_notes:
1. The bulk of the footnote material in the source text (the long editorial commentary on cochineal/Coccus cacti, Llano y Zapata, Ruyschen, León Pinelo, Herrera, etc.) appears to be an editorial annotation by a later editor of Cobo's text, not Cobo's own prose. It has been omitted from the translated main text but its presence is noted here; it concerns the history of cochineal identification and trade and is editorially interpolated from the prior chapter context.
2. 'Soldar quebraduras' is rendered 'mending fractures'; in context this likely refers to fractured bones (i.e., a bone-setting application) rather than broken objects, given the medical register, though it could also mean mending cracked vessels. The ambiguity is preserved.
3. 'Alquitara' is the Spanish adaptation of Arabic al-qiṭāra, meaning a still or alembic; retained in brackets.
4. 'Cola para blanquear las paredes' — the glutinous decoction used as a size or binder for whitewash — is a well-attested use of mucilaginous cactus tissue in Andean construction, corroborated by the Betanzos footnote.
5. OCR errors silently corrected include: 'ijiie' → 'que', 'cipre-les' → 'cipreses', 'imelo' → 'suelo', 'eiÚos' → 'ellos', 'c'^ales' → 'cuales', 'i-inViiíe' → 'animal' (context: 'cosa animal y no vegetal'), 'dc^' → 'de'.

results.title_inferred: The Queylla: Large Columnar Cacti of the Americas
results.original_text:
CAPITULO V
De la queylla

Es la queylla el género de cardones más crecidos de todos, en que se incluyen cinco o seis diferencias dellos, que son todos aquellos que secos son tan recios y fuertes, que sirve su madera para fábricas y otros usos. Son todos los desta clase verdes o pardiscos, y sacando el corazón, que es palo duro, lo demás es tierno y aguanoso como calabaza verde, que es la cáscara y corteza, la cual tiene de grueso de tres a cuatro dedos. Son esquinados, con una honda y angosta canal entre una y otra esquina. Unos tienen más esquinas y lomos que otros; algunos llegan a tener veinte, y de ahí van bajando. Por sobre estas esquinas y partes relevadas están llenos de agudas espinas, que nacen a modo de estrellas, desde seis hasta doce juntas en cada una; con que está tan armado el cardón, que por ninguna parte se le puede echar mano. Las mayores púas son como agujas de arrieros, otras como alesnas y menores hasta del tamaño de pequeños alfileres. Son pardas o blanquecinas, causan gran dolor cuando punzan, y enconan la parte que hieren.

Son estos cardones parejos, tan gruesos por su cumbre y medio como por el pie; algunos crecen derechos de solo un mástil; otros, a un estado o dos de la tierra echan dos o tres a modo de ramas, de la misma groseza que el primero en que se encaraman. Los mayores son tan altos, gruesos y derechos como un ciprés, y hállanse algunos tan gruesos, que no los pueden abrazar dos hombres; déstos nacen muchos en la provincia de los Lipes, diócesis de La Charcas; sácanse dellos grandes vigas y asiérranse tablas anchas para enmaderamientos de casas. Su madera está toda agujereada a manera de red, y así, las tablas que se hacen, quedan llenas de agujerillos, que son largos y angostos, del tamaño de piñones, y pasan de una parte a otra, que parece cada tabla una red o celosía. Otras castas hay de cardones grandes, que no tienen estos agujerillos, sino que su madera es sólida.

De la madera destos cardones, por ser recia, correosa y liviana, hacen en algunas partes los fustes de las sillas. Mas, es de advertir, que toda madera de cardón se ha de labrar cuando está verde, porque entonces está blanda y se corta y labra sin trabajo; pero después de seca, se pone dura como un hueso y no se puede labrar sino con muy grande dificultad.

Las pitahayas más dulces, olorosas y regaladas son las que llevan estos cardones grandes, cuales son las de las provincias de los Lipes y de Venezuela. Son del tamaño de naranjas, la cáscara tierna como la de la tuna, la carne muy zumosa, llena de unos pequeños granillos que se comen sin quebrantarlos: éstas son blancas; y otras hay coloradas de dentro y fuera, y quien las come echa la orina como sangre: no son tan regaladas estas pitahayas coloradas como las blancas.

Otros cardones hay altos y derechos como una pica y tan gruesos como la pantorrilla, y aun como el muslo; dan unas pitahayas del tamaño de nueces, poco menos sabrosas que las primeras. Otros son infrutíferos; nacen éstos en toda la Sierra del Perú y crecen de dos a tres estados en alto tan gruesos como el cuerpo de un muchacho; de los cuales se sacan unas varas largas y delgadas como el brazo, con que se maderan casas pajizas y chozas.
results.translation:
CHAPTER V
On the Queylla

The queylla is the genus of cardones [columnar cacti] that grows largest of all, encompassing five or six distinct varieties, all of which, when dried, are so tough and strong that their wood serves for construction and other uses. All of those in this class are green or brownish, and removing the core—which is hard wood—the remainder is tender and watery like a green gourd, consisting of the rind and outer bark, which is three to four finger-widths thick. They are ribbed, with a deep and narrow channel between one rib and the next. Some have more ribs and ridges than others; some reach as many as twenty, and from there the number decreases. Along these ribs and raised portions they are covered with sharp spines that grow in star-like clusters of six to twelve together at each point, so that the cardón is so well armored that it cannot be seized from any direction. The largest thorns are like packers' needles [agujas de arrieros], others like awls [alesnas], and the smallest are no bigger than small pins. They are brown or whitish, cause great pain when they prick, and inflame [enconan] the part they wound.

These cardones are uniform in girth, as thick at the top and middle as at the base; some grow straight as a single mast; others, at one or two fathoms [estados] from the ground, put out two or three branches of the same thickness as the main stem they spring from. The largest are as tall, thick, and straight as a cypress, and some are found so thick that two men cannot encircle them with their arms; many of these grow in the province of Los Lipes, in the diocese of La Charcas; from them are drawn large beams, and wide planks are sawn for the timber-framing of houses. Their wood is pierced throughout in the manner of a net, and so the planks made from it are left full of small holes, long and narrow, the size of pine seeds [piñones], passing from one side to the other, so that each plank resembles a net or latticework [celosía]. There are other kinds of large cardones that do not have these little holes; rather, their wood is solid.

From the wood of these cardones, because it is tough, flexible, and light, saddle-trees [fustes de las sillas] are made in some places. It should be noted, however, that all cardón wood must be worked while it is still green, because at that stage it is soft and can be cut and shaped without difficulty; but once dry, it becomes as hard as bone and can only be worked with very great effort.

The sweetest, most fragrant, and most delicious pitahayas [dragon fruit] are those borne by these large cardones, such as those from the provinces of Los Lipes and Venezuela. They are the size of oranges, with a rind as tender as that of the tuna [prickly pear], the flesh very juicy, filled with small seeds that are eaten without crushing them. These are white; and there are others that are red inside and out, and whoever eats them passes urine like blood [i.e., causes red-colored urine]; these red pitahayas are not as delicious as the white ones.

There are other cardones, tall and straight as a pike and as thick as a calf or even a thigh; these bear pitahayas the size of walnuts, somewhat less flavorful than the first. Others are fruitless; these grow throughout the highlands [Sierra] of Peru and reach two to three fathoms in height, as thick as a boy's body; from these are obtained long, slender poles the thickness of an arm, with which thatched houses [casas pajizas] and huts are framed.

entities_mentioned.name: queylla
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Echinopsis atacamensis (syn. Trichocereus atacamensis) or related large columnar cacti of the Andean highlands (e.g., Browningia, Neoraimondia, or Cereus spp.); the lattice-wood description is particularly consistent with Echinopsis/Trichocereus species
entities_mentioned.notes: Quechua/Aymara term 'queylla' or 'quehua' is associated with large Andean columnar cacti. The latticed or reticulate woody skeleton is characteristic of Echinopsis atacamensis (cardón del Valle) and relatives. Cobo notes five or six varieties under this grouping.

entities_mentioned.name: cardón
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: General term for large columnar cacti; likely encompasses Echinopsis atacamensis, Cereus spp., Neoraimondia arequipensis, and others depending on region
entities_mentioned.notes: Spanish colonial generic term for large columnar cacti of the Americas; not a single species.

entities_mentioned.name: pitahaya (blanca)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Selenicereus undatus (white-fleshed dragon fruit) or fruit of large Hylocereus/Echinopsis spp.
entities_mentioned.notes: Cobo distinguishes white and red pitahayas. The white variety is described as more delicious; the red variety is noted to color the urine red, a well-documented harmless effect of betalain pigments in red Hylocereus spp. (e.g., Hylocereus costaricensis).

entities_mentioned.name: pitahaya (colorada)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Hylocereus costaricensis or Hylocereus polyrhizus (red-fleshed dragon fruit)
entities_mentioned.notes: The observation that eating the red pitahaya causes the urine to appear like blood (pseudohematuria from betacyanin pigments) is medically accurate and consistent with red Hylocereus species.

entities_mentioned.name: tuna
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Opuntia ficus-indica (prickly pear)
entities_mentioned.notes: Used here as a comparative reference for the texture of the pitahaya's rind.

results.translator_notes: 1. 'Estado' is a unit of measurement approximately equal to one fathom or the height of a man (~1.7 m). 2. 'Fustes de las sillas' refers to the wooden saddle-tree frame, not chair furniture. 3. The red-urine effect of the colored pitahaya (beeturia-like reaction) is correctly observed by Cobo and is caused by betalain pigments, not blood. 4. 'Enconan' (to inflame or infect a wound) is a period medical term preserved in brackets in translation. 5. The province of 'Los Lipes' corresponds to the Lípez region of modern southwestern Bolivia. 6. OCR errors silently corrected include: 'gn^^sos' → 'gruesos', 'tii' → 'de', 'th' → 'de', 'I í^' → 'La', 'x\m' → 'una', 'aaa' → 'una', 'ago-jerillos' normalized, 'liviama' → 'liviana', 'regaladas' preserved, 'colorad*^' → 'coloradas', 'l^^' → 'las', 'derecha' → 'derechos', 'h' → 'las', 'nuecf^^' → 'nueces', 'primer^' → 'primeras', 'éstos' normalized, 'df' → 'de', 'ga-itas' → 'casas'. 7. No medical preparations are described in this segment; the chapter is primarily botanical and economic in focus.

results.title_inferred: The Avacollay and Other Columnar Cacti
results.original_text:
CAPITULO VI
Del avacollay y otros cardones

El cardón llamado avacollay es el mayor de los de esta segunda clase, en que se ponen todos los demás que son verdes, espinosos, acanalados y que no dan madera de provecho; y deben de pasar de diez o doce suertes dellos. El avacollay crece unas veces levantado y derecho, y otras, tendido por el suelo, que no parece sino culebra verde. Tiene de cuatro a seis esquinas; en la hechura se parece a un cirio de muchos pábulos. Es tan grueso como el brazo y largo desde uno hasta dos estados. Echa en su cumbre una flor blanca sin olor alguno, dos o tres veces mayor que una azucena, la cual comen los indios cocida. Son muchas las diferencias de cardones que se hallan entre el avacollay y el menor de todos, que es aquel linaje de cardoncillos poco más gruesos que el dedo pulgar, de que ya queda dicho; éstos llevan unas pitahayas del tamaño de madroños, cuyo zumo he visto dar a los héticos y dicen ser provechoso para esta enfermedad.

De las castas de cardones deste capítulo y clase unos son frutíferos y otros, no. Hállase cierta especie dellos más delgados y con menores y mayor número de esquinas que los primeros, de los cuales nacen tantos sobre un mismo pie y tronco, que viene a hacerse un árbol muy copado. Las diferencias de flores que producen estos cardones son muchas, que todas convienen en carecer de olor. Es muy digno de reparar en estos cardones, y que yo lo he ponderado no pocas veces, que nacen de ordinario entre piedras y riscos y no pocas veces sobre las mismas peñas; y lo que más acrecienta la admiración es que también suelen nacer en partes donde nunca llueve, como en estos Llanos del Perú; de suerte que sin tener jamás riego del cielo ni de la tierra, y estando en suelo seco de cascajo y peñas, sin rastro de humedad, estén ellos tan verdes y aguanosos como un pepino, pues, en dándoles una cuchillada, sale dellos mucha agua.
results.translation:
CHAPTER VI
On the Avacollay and Other Columnar Cacti [cardones]

The columnar cactus [cardón] called avacollay is the largest of the second class, which includes all those that are green, spiny, ribbed, and that yield no serviceable timber; there must be more than ten or twelve varieties of them. The avacollay sometimes grows upright and erect, and at other times sprawls along the ground, looking for all the world like a green serpent. It has four to six ridges [esquinas]; in its shape it resembles a large candle with many wicks [pábulos]. It is as thick as an arm and between one and two fathoms [estados] in length. At its crown it puts forth a white flower entirely without scent, two or three times larger than a white lily [azucena], which the Indians eat cooked. There are many varieties of columnar cacti [cardones] between the avacollay and the smallest of all — that kind of small cactus [cardoncillo] barely thicker than a thumb, already mentioned — the latter bearing pitahayas the size of strawberry-tree berries [madroños], the juice of which I have seen given to consumptive patients [héticos], and which is said to be beneficial for that disease.

Among the varieties of columnar cacti treated in this chapter and class, some are fruit-bearing and others are not. There is found a certain species of them that is more slender and has smaller but more numerous ridges than the first; so many of these grow from a single base and trunk that the whole forms a very densely branching tree. The variety of flowers these cacti produce is great, yet all agree in having no scent. What is very worthy of note concerning these cacti — and I have reflected upon it no small number of times — is that they ordinarily grow among stones and crags, and not infrequently upon bare rock faces. What adds still more to the wonder is that they also tend to grow in places where it never rains, as in the coastal plains [Llanos] of Peru; so that, never receiving moisture from sky or soil, and standing in dry ground of gravel and rock without any trace of humidity, they remain as green and full of water as a cucumber — for when one slashes into them, a great deal of water flows out.

entities_mentioned.name: avacollay
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Probably Browningia candelaris or a large Echinopsis/Trichocereus species (e.g., Echinopsis atacamensis or Neoraimondia arequipensis)
entities_mentioned.notes: Described as the largest of the ribbed, spiny, non-timber cacti; grows both erect and prostrate; 4–6 ribs; as thick as an arm; 1–2 fathoms long; large white odorless flower eaten cooked by indigenous people. Quechua name 'avacollay' (also spelled 'waqollay') is attested in Andean sources for large columnar cacti.

entities_mentioned.name: cardoncillo (small cactus, thumb-thick)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Possibly a small Opuntia or slender Cereus/Echinopsis species; insufficient detail for confident identification
entities_mentioned.notes: Previously described in an earlier chapter; bears pitahaya fruits the size of madroño berries.

entities_mentioned.name: pitahaya (fruit of the small cardoncillo)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Fruit of a columnar or climbing cactus, genus Selenicereus, Hylocereus, or related Cactaceae; in Andean context possibly Echinopsis sp.
entities_mentioned.notes: Described as the size of madroño (Arbutus unedo) berries. The juice was administered to 'héticos' (consumptive patients). Medical use extracted below.
entities_mentioned.condition_treated: Hectic fever / pulmonary consumption (hética — likely tuberculosis or advanced wasting disease)
entities_mentioned.preparation: Juice (zumo) of the pitahaya fruit, given to drink to consumptive patients

entities_mentioned.name: cardones (general class of columnar cacti)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Family Cactaceae, tribe Cereeae; multiple genera including Echinopsis, Browningia, Neoraimondia, Weberbauerocereus, etc.
entities_mentioned.notes: Cobo describes over ten or twelve varieties in this class: ribbed, spiny, green, non-timber. Notes their remarkable ability to grow without rain in the coastal Llanos of Peru and to retain water internally.

results.translator_notes: 1. 'Estado' is a Spanish unit of length approximately equal to a fathom (~1.67–1.80 m), so 'uno hasta dos estados' = roughly 5–12 feet. 2. 'Pábulos' (wicks/flames) — OCR rendered 'páblos'; corrected to 'pábulos' (pl. of pábulo, used here in the sense of wicks or flames of a candelabra-style candle), consistent with the candle/cirio metaphor for columnar cacti. 3. 'Héticos' refers to patients suffering from 'hética' — hectic fever associated with consumption/tuberculosis, a standard early modern medical category. 4. 'Madroño' = Arbutus unedo, the strawberry tree; its berries are used as a size reference for the pitahaya fruits. 5. 'Llanos del Perú' refers to the hyperarid Pacific coastal desert strip of Peru, not the Amazonian llanos. 6. The OCR hyphenated 'avacoU-iav' in the source; silently corrected to 'avacollay' throughout.

results.title_inferred: On the Achuma Cactus and the Cardón of Erysipelas
results.original_text:
CAPITULO VII
De la achuma

La achuma es cierta especie de cardón de los del segundo género; crece un estado de alto y a veces más; es tan grueso como la pierna, cuadrado y de color de zabila; produce unas pitahayas pequeñas y dulces. Es ésta una planta con que el demonio tenía engañados a los indios del Perú en su gentilidad; de la cual usaban para sus embustes y supersticiones. Bebido el zumo della, saca de sentido de manera que quedan los que lo beben como muertos, y aun se ha visto morir algunos por causa de la mucha frialdad que el cerebro recibe. Transportados con esta bebida los indios, soñaban mil disparates y los creían como si fueran verdades. Es de temperamento frío en el tercero grado y húmedo en el segundo; aprovecha su zumo contra las intemperies cálidas, contra el ardor de los riñones; y bebido en poca cantidad, es bueno contra las calenturas largas, contra la itiricia y ardor de orina.

CAPITULO VIII
Del cardón de la isípula

A esta especie de cardón le damos nombre del efecto que hace, mas el vulgo lo llama muérete por mí. Es muy medicinal y a esta causa lo suelen algunas personas criar en sus casas; crece de uno a dos estados en alto; el tronco es grueso como el brazo, del cual van saliendo en lugar de ramas muchos trozos, y déstos, unos se van encaramando en otros; y todos estos trozos o ramos están armados de unas espinillas del tamaño de alfileres pequeños, y junto a las espinas echa en lugar de hojas infinitos picos o pezoncillos muy semejantes en el tamaño y forma a las vainillas verdes en que los rábanos producen la semilla, sólo que son más tiernos y aguanosos. Con esta planta se cura la isípula y el calor del hígado, mojada y aplicada sobre la parte en que está el mal.
results.translation:
CHAPTER VII
On the Achuma

The achuma is a certain species of cardón [columnar cactus] of the second genus; it grows to the height of one estado [approximately 1.7 meters] and sometimes more; it is as thick as a man's leg, four-sided [ribbed], and the color of aloe [zabila]. It produces small, sweet pitahayas [cactus fruits]. This is a plant by which the Devil had deceived the Indians of Peru in their heathendom, and which they used in their trickery and superstitions. When its juice is drunk, it deprives one of one's senses in such a way that those who drink it are left as if dead; and some have even been seen to die from the great coldness that the brain receives. Transported by this drink, the Indians would dream a thousand absurdities and believed them as if they were truths. Its temperament [complexion] is cold in the third degree and moist in the second; its juice is beneficial against hot intemperances [excessive heat conditions], against burning of the kidneys; and drunk in small quantity, it is good against prolonged fevers, against jaundice [itiricia], and burning of urine.

CHAPTER VIII
On the Cardón of Erysipelas

We give this species of cardón its name from the effect it produces, though common people call it muérete por mí ["die for me"]. It is highly medicinal, and for this reason some people are accustomed to growing it in their homes. It grows from one to two estados in height; the trunk is as thick as an arm, from which, in place of branches, many segments grow, and some of these climb upon others. All of these segments or branches are armed with small spines the size of tiny pins, and alongside the spines, in place of leaves, it puts forth countless tiny tips or little nipples [pezoncillos] very similar in size and shape to the green pods in which radishes produce their seed, except that they are more tender and watery. With this plant, erysipelas [isípula] and heat of the liver are treated, by wetting it and applying it over the afflicted part.

entities_mentioned.name: achuma
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Echinopsis pachanoi (San Pedro cactus) or possibly Trichocereus spp.
entities_mentioned.notes: A columnar cactus used as a psychoactive ritual drink among Andean peoples, containing mescaline. Cobo describes it as four-sided (ribbed), which is consistent with E. pachanoi. Footnote in source notes that the Peruvian and Chilean creole verb 'chumarse' (to get drunk) likely derives from this plant's name. Medical use: treatment of kidney inflammation, prolonged fevers, jaundice, and dysuria. Preparation: juice expressed from the cactus and drunk. Cold/moist in humoral theory (3rd/2nd degree). Condition treated: hot intemperances, kidney burning, prolonged fevers, jaundice, burning urine.

entities_mentioned.name: cardón de la isípula / muérete por mí
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Possibly a Rhipsalis, Mammillaria, or related jointed/segmented cactus species; identification uncertain
entities_mentioned.notes: Named for its therapeutic effect against erysipelas (isípula). Common name 'muérete por mí' ('die for me'). Described as having small pin-like spines and small fleshy nipple-like protrusions resembling green radish seedpods. Condition treated: erysipelas (skin infection/inflammation) and liver heat. Preparation: the plant is wetted and applied topically over the affected area.

entities_mentioned.name: zabila
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Aloe vera or related Aloe species
entities_mentioned.notes: Used here as a color reference for the achuma cactus, not as a medicinal subject in this passage.

entities_mentioned.name: pitahaya
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Fruit of various columnar cacti, likely Hylocereus or Stenocereus spp.
entities_mentioned.notes: Used here descriptively to identify the fruit of the achuma; said to be small and sweet.

results.translator_notes: 1. 'Estado' is a unit of height roughly equivalent to a man's full stature (~1.65–1.75 m); retained in brackets. 2. 'Itiricia' is an archaic Spanish form of 'ictericia' (jaundice); translated accordingly. 3. 'Isípula' is the archaic/folk spelling of 'erisipela' (erysipelas), a bacterial skin infection; retained in brackets in translation. 4. 'Temperamento frío en el tercero grado y húmedo en el segundo' reflects Galenic humoral medicine; translated literally with a bracketed gloss. 5. The footnote in the source (marked as note 5) explains the etymology of the creole verb 'chumarse'; it has been preserved in the cleaned original text as a note but is not part of Cobo's main narrative. 6. The segment ends mid-page, suggesting the chapter on the cardón de la isípula continues beyond this excerpt.

results.title_inferred: On Several Other Cardones (Cacti): Pullapulla and Hachacana
results.original_text:
CAPITULO IX
De los demás cardones

De las muchas diferencias de cardones que yo he visto, no deben de quedar más que tres o cuatro, mas no dudo sino que habrá otros de que yo no tenga noticias. Uno de los más pequeños es el que se dice pullapulla, el cual no se levanta de la tierra más que, cuando mucho, un jeme. Hácese una mata redonda algo ahusada, que no parece sino un montoncillo de peras o de tunas puestas muy juntas con orden y concierto, las coronillas hacia arriba; porque, en lugar de ramas o pencas, produce esta planta unos pezoncilos o trocillos del tamaño y figura de tunas o de peras larguillas, los cuales van naciendo unos de otros no por la cumbre, sino por los lados, hasta formar la mata de la figura dicha; quedando los de en medio un poco más levantados que los de la redonda. Los primeros de abajo van con el tiempo engrosando y perdiendo la forma que al principio tenían. Cuando comienza a nacer cada una destas penquillas, produce una flor de tan grande ruedo como una pequeña rosa, aunque no tiene tantas hojas; son estas flores, unas, purpúreas y, otras, naranjadas, sin olor alguno. Suelen los indios comer esta frutilla sin embargo de que es desabrida y nada apetitosa. Está toda esta mata muy armada de agudas espinas, y ella es de una sustancia verde, tierna y muy aguanosa, como los demás cardones. Nacen de ordinario en tierras muy frías, y suelen en muchas partes nacer sobre las paredes de tierra y ser buena guarda y defensa para que no salten por ellas.

Otras matas deste género vemos por los páramos deste reino cubiertas de un vello blanco a modo de algodón, en tanta cantidad, que parece cada mata un vellón de lana blanca tendida en el suelo o amontonada. El nombre de pullapulla es de la lengua aimará.

De la misma lengua es el nombre de hachacana, que dan los indios a otra especie de cardón, el cual no se levanta de la tierra ni echa ramas ni hojas; nace de la forma que la pullapulla: asoma sobre la tierra un tallo o cabeza de figura de pepino grande tan grueso como el puño y mayor, y sólo se levanta cuatro dedos, poco más o menos; todo acanalado a lo largo a manera de melón, con muchas canales más hundidas y juntas que las del melón; y nacen en cada mata muchos destos tallos o picos, unos pegados a otros, con que la mata se pone redonda; la cual está por todas partes armada, como un erizo, de muchas espinas, como los demás cardones, si bien las espinas déste son en mucha cantidad, muy delgadas y larguillas. Es esta planta de color verde tirante a rojo, muy tierna y viscosa como pencas de zábila. Echa una flor colorada del tamaño de una clavellina, sin olor alguno. A la flor sucede una frutilla silvestre tan grande como un huevo de paloma, agria y nada apetecible, que es especie de pitahaya. Llámase esta mata, en la lengua aimará, hachacana; su fruto, sancayu, que suelen comer los indios, y la flor, añapancu.
results.translation:
CHAPTER IX
On the Other Cardones [cacti]

Of the many varieties of cardones [columnar or globose cacti] that I have seen, there remain no more than three or four to describe, though I do not doubt that there are others of which I have no knowledge. One of the smallest is the one called pullapulla, which rises from the ground no more than, at most, one span [jeme: the distance from the tip of the thumb to the tip of the index finger, roughly 7–8 cm]. It forms a round, somewhat tapering clump that looks like nothing so much as a little pile of pears or prickly-pear pads [tunas] arranged very close together in orderly fashion, their crowns pointing upward; for instead of branches or broad leaves [pencas], this plant produces small stems or joints [pezoncilos o trocillos] the size and shape of prickly-pear pads or longish pears, which grow one from another not from the top but from the sides, until they form the clump in the shape described, with those in the middle standing somewhat higher than those around the edge. The lowest joints gradually thicken over time and lose the shape they had at first. When each of these small pads begins to sprout, it produces a flower as wide as a small rose, though with fewer petals; some of these flowers are purple and others orange, entirely without fragrance. The Indians are accustomed to eating this small fruit, even though it is insipid and altogether unappetizing. The entire clump is heavily armed with sharp spines, and its substance is green, tender, and very watery, like that of the other cardones. They commonly grow in very cold lands, and in many places they grow on earthen walls, serving as a good guard and defense against anyone climbing over them.

Other clumps of this kind are seen on the high moorlands [páramos] of this kingdom covered in a white down resembling cotton, so abundantly that each clump looks like a fleece of white wool spread out or heaped upon the ground. The name pullapulla is from the Aymara language.

Also from the same language comes the name hachacana, which the Indians give to another species of cardón, one that does not rise from the ground, nor does it put forth branches or leaves. It grows in the same manner as the pullapulla: a stem or head shaped like a large cucumber, as thick as a fist or larger, emerges from the soil and rises only about four finger-widths, more or less. It is entirely ribbed lengthwise in the manner of a melon, with many furrows deeper and closer together than those of a melon, and many of these stems or knobs grow from a single clump, joined one to another, so that the clump becomes rounded. It is armed on all sides, like a hedgehog, with numerous spines, as are the other cardones, though the spines of this one are very numerous, very thin, and rather long. The plant is greenish tending toward red in color, very tender and viscous like the pads of aloe [zábila]. It bears a red flower the size of a carnation [clavellina], entirely without scent. After the flower comes a small wild fruit as large as a pigeon's egg, sour and wholly unappealing, which is a kind of pitahaya [dragon fruit cactus]. This clump is called, in the Aymara language, hachacana; its fruit is called sancayu, which the Indians are accustomed to eat; and the flower is called añapancu.

entities_mentioned.name: pullapulla
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Probably a species of Austrocylindropuntia or Cumulopuntia (formerly Opuntia), likely Cumulopuntia sphaerica or a closely related globose/clustering Andean cactus
entities_mentioned.notes: Aymara name. Described as a very low-growing, clustering cactus forming rounded mounds resembling piled pear-shaped pads; purple or orange flowers; watery interior; grows in very cold, high-altitude lands. Some specimens noted with white woolly covering, possibly suggesting Oreocereus or a woolly Cumulopuntia species. Fruit eaten by indigenous people despite being bland.

entities_mentioned.name: hachacana
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Probably Echinopsis maximiliana (syn. Trichocereus maximilianus) or a related low-growing Echinopsis/Lobivia species; alternatively Echinopsis ancistrophora; the fruit called 'sancayu' aligns with known Echinopsis edible fruits
entities_mentioned.notes: Aymara name. Low, clustering, ribbed globose cactus resembling a large cucumber, deeply furrowed, armed with many thin long spines; greenish-red color; viscous like aloe; red flower the size of a carnation; egg-sized sour fruit (called sancayu) described as a type of pitahaya; flower called añapancu. Sancayu is still a common name in the Andes for edible Echinopsis fruits.

entities_mentioned.name: sancayu
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Edible fruit of Echinopsis spp. (commonly Echinopsis maximiliana or related species); sancayo/sancayu remains a market name in Peru and Bolivia
entities_mentioned.notes: Aymara name for the fruit of hachacana. Described as sour, pigeon-egg-sized, and a type of pitahaya. Eaten by indigenous people.

entities_mentioned.name: añapancu
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: null
entities_mentioned.notes: Aymara name for the flower of the hachacana cactus. No independent modern botanical referent identified; the name does not appear in standard taxonomic literature.

entities_mentioned.name: pitahaya
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: General Andean/colonial Spanish term for cacti bearing edible fruits, broadly applicable to Stenocereus, Hylocereus, and Echinopsis spp.
entities_mentioned.notes: Used here as a category term to classify the hachacana's fruit, not a specific species name.

results.translator_notes: 1. 'Jeme' is a traditional Spanish hand measure (span from thumb tip to index finger tip, approximately 7–8 cm); retained in brackets. 2. 'Pencas' refers to the broad, fleshy pads or leaves of cacti and succulents; retained as a period-technical term and translated contextually. 3. 'Zábila' is the colonial Spanish form of 'sábila,' i.e., aloe (Aloe vera or related species); rendered as 'aloe [zábila].' 4. 'Clavellina' is a small carnation or pink (Dianthus caryophyllus or similar); used here purely as a size reference. 5. OCR errors silently corrected include: 'pezonc^s' → 'pezoncilos,' 'fornítr' → 'formar,' 'figura d^cha' → 'figura dicha,' 'príncinío' → 'principio,' 'peípiefia' → 'pequeña,' 'aunada' → 'armada' (contextually certain given the following 'de agudas espinas'), 'aña^ pancu' → 'añapancu,' 'apele- cible' → 'apetecible,' 'aimari' → 'aimará.' 6. 'Páramos' retained in translation as 'high moorlands' with the Spanish term noted; these are the high-altitude Andean grasslands above the treeline. 7. No medicinal preparations or treatments are described in this segment; Cobo here provides purely botanical and ethnobotanical description.

results.title_inferred: The Mutuy plant: description, uses, and medicinal preparations
results.original_text:
CAPITULO X

Del mutuy

El mutuy es una mata que crece poco más de un estado en alto; echa muchas ramas a la redonda y muy juntas, con que se hace muy ancha y copada; sus ramas son rojas, mayormente hacia los pimpollos; produce las hojas como el lentisco, de dos en dos, en ramillos largos de un palmo, y se parecen en el talle y tamaño a las del lentisco, salvo que son más tiernas, más delgadas y un verde más escuro. Echa esta planta muchas flores amarillas de ningún olor, pequeñas, de cuatro o cinco hojitas, que hacen tanto ruedo como una clavellina; cúbrese toda la mata destas flores, que la hermosean mucho, y donde no la abrasa el hielo, conserva todo el año sus hojas y flores. Produce unas vainillas tan largas y anchas como un dedo, muy delgadas y enjutas, con diez o doce pepitas cada una, como las de algarroba, sólo que no son tan llanas, sino larguillas y de un color algo aceitunado. Los botoncillos de las flores, antes de abrirse, son amarillos y redondos, algún tanto ovados, del tamaño de pequeñas alcaparras, a las cuales se parecen solamente en la grandeza y por eso los llaman los españoles alcaparras de las Indias, y los aderezan y preparan para comer en ensaladas, como las alcaparras de España; aunque los indios suelen comerlas cocidas en sus guisos.

Es comida que la abraza el estómago de buena gana y que despierta el apetito y repara los vómitos; para cuyo efecto, y de confortar al estómago, suelen aplicar estas alcaparras majadas y mezcladas con miel de abejas, nuez moscada, canela, almáciga, y polvos de yerba buena; y hacen en el estómago muy buen efecto; y el cocimiento de las hojas y flores desta planta, bebido de ordinario, desopila el estómago y bazo. Llámase, en la lengua quichua, mutuy, y en la aimará, mutii.
results.translation:
CHAPTER X

On the Mutuy

The mutuy is a shrub that grows to little more than one estado [approximately the height of a man, roughly 1.7 m] in height; it sends out many branches all around, growing very close together, which makes it very wide and bushy. Its branches are red, especially toward the new shoots. It produces leaves like those of the mastic tree [lentisco], in pairs, on long sprigs of about a hand-span, and they resemble those of the mastic in shape and size, except that they are more tender, thinner, and of a darker green. This plant bears many small yellow flowers with no fragrance, composed of four or five petals, spreading about as wide as a carnation [clavellina]; the entire shrub becomes covered with these flowers, which greatly beautify it, and where it is not scorched by frost, it retains its leaves and flowers throughout the year. It produces small pods as long and wide as a finger, very thin and dry, each containing ten or twelve seeds, like those of the carob, except that they are not so flat, but rather longish and of a somewhat olive color. The flower buds, before they open, are yellow and round, somewhat oval, the size of small capers, to which they bear resemblance only in size, and for this reason the Spaniards call them 'capers of the Indies' [alcaparras de las Indias]. They are seasoned and prepared for eating in salads, just as capers are in Spain; though the Indians usually eat them cooked in their stews.

It is a food that the stomach readily accepts, and it awakens the appetite and relieves vomiting. For this purpose, and to fortify the stomach, these 'capers' are commonly applied crushed and mixed with honey, nutmeg, cinnamon, mastic [almáciga], and powdered mint [yerba buena]; and they produce a very good effect on the stomach. The decoction of the leaves and flowers of this plant, drunk regularly, unblocks [desopila] the stomach and spleen. In the Quechua language it is called mutuy, and in Aymara, mutii.

entities_mentioned.name: mutuy (also mutii in Aymara)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Senna multiglandulosa (syn. Cassia tomentosa) or closely related Senna species (Fabaceae)
entities_mentioned.notes: A shrubby legume native to the Andean region. The description of yellow flowers, pinnate leaves, and long dry seed pods with olive-colored seeds, along with the Quechua name 'mutuy,' aligns well with Senna multiglandulosa, commonly known in the Andes as mutuy or mutu. Some sources also identify it as Cassia hookeriana or related species.

entities_mentioned.name: lentisco
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Pistacia lentiscus (mastic tree)
entities_mentioned.notes: Used as a comparative reference for leaf shape and size, not a subject plant.

entities_mentioned.name: almáciga
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Pistacia lentiscus resin (mastic resin)
entities_mentioned.notes: Used as an ingredient in the medicinal preparation for the stomach.

entities_mentioned.name: yerba buena
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Mentha spicata or Mentha × piperita (spearmint / peppermint)
entities_mentioned.notes: A common culinary and medicinal herb in colonial Spanish contexts; used in powdered form in the stomach preparation.

results.translator_notes: Several OCR errors were silently corrected: 'miiehay' → 'muchas'; 'Im' → 'los'; 'rf' → 'el'; 'lioji-' → 'hoji-' (hojitas); 'velo' → 'hielo' (frost — contextually unambiguous); '%aimllas' → 'vainillas'; 'ísaado' → 'aceitunado'; 'ite,' → 'dos,'; 'pifa' → 'para'; 'giú-' (truncated at line break) → 'guisos'; 'jscícladas' → 'mezcladas'; 'ija buena' → 'yerba buena'; 'efecto; y' → 'buen efecto; y'. The term 'desopila' (unblocks, removes obstructions) is a standard humoral-medical term of the period indicating removal of blockages in the organs. 'Un estado' as a height measure is glossed in the translation. The botanical section on medicinal use contains extractable data as follows — Name: mutuy / mutii; Modern ID: Senna multiglandulosa (high confidence); Ailments treated: loss of appetite, vomiting, gastric weakness, obstruction of the stomach and spleen; Preparation methods: (1) flower buds crushed and mixed with honey, nutmeg, cinnamon, mastic resin, and powdered mint — applied for stomach complaints; (2) decoction of leaves and flowers drunk regularly for unblocking stomach and spleen.

results.title_inferred: The Suana plant (Andean saffron)
results.original_text:
CAPITULO XI
De la suana

La suana es una mata de dos codos de alto, que echa muchos pimpollos o ramas muy derechas; la hoja es como la del As[phodelus], salvo que es dos tantos más ancha. Produce una flor blanca de forma de campanilla, como azucena, y muchas raíces delgadas, cuyas cortezas tiñen de [color] como azafrán, por lo cual la [usan] y venden por todo el Perú, y [usan] de azafrán en los guisados, dado que no se sabe hagan otro efecto más que darles color. Por nacer esta planta en las provincias de los Andes, la llaman los españoles azafrán de los Andes; pero los indios de la provincia de la Recasa [Larecaja], diócesis de Chuquiabo, que habitan la tierra yanca de aquel distrito, donde esta planta nace, la llaman suana.
results.translation:
CHAPTER XI
On the Suana

The suana is a shrub [mata] two cubits tall, which puts forth many shoots or very straight branches. The leaf resembles that of the asphodel, except that it is twice as wide. It produces a white flower in the shape of a little bell [campanilla], like a lily [azucena], and many thin roots whose bark [cortezas] dyes [things] the color of saffron. For this reason it is used and sold throughout Peru, and used in place of saffron in stews [guisados], although it is not known to produce any effect beyond imparting color. Because this plant grows in the Andean provinces, the Spaniards call it 'saffron of the Andes' [azafrán de los Andes]; but the Indians of the province of Larecaja, in the diocese of Chuquiabo [La Paz], who inhabit the lowland [tierra yanca] of that district where this plant grows, call it suana.

entities_mentioned.name: suana / azafrán de los Andes
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Escobedia grandiflora (family Orobanchaceae / formerly Scrophulariaceae), a root parasite whose bark yields a yellow-orange dye used as a saffron substitute; sometimes also identified as Escobedia scabrifolia
entities_mentioned.notes: The description of bell-shaped white flowers, straight shoots, thin roots whose bark yields a saffron-colored dye, and origin in the yungas (tierra yanca) of Larecaja (Bolivia) is consistent with Escobedia grandiflora or a closely related species. The plant is still sold in Andean markets today as 'azafrán de los Andes' or 'palillo' for coloring food.

entities_mentioned.name: Larecaja (Recasa in text)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Larecaja Province, La Paz Department, Bolivia
entities_mentioned.notes: The OCR rendering 'Recasa' is clearly a corruption of 'Larecaja,' which Cobo names elsewhere consistently. Chuquiabo is colonial La Paz.

results.translator_notes: The chapter heading in the OCR reads 'De la suana' but the opening sentence erroneously renders the plant name as 'mana' — almost certainly an OCR error for 'suana,' consistent with the chapter title and the closing sentence. The word 'yanca' (also spelled 'yunca' or 'yungas') refers to the warm lowland valleys on the eastern Andean slopes; it is preserved here in its colonial form. The reference plant for the leaf shape appears to be asphodel (Asphodelus), which has been silently expanded from the truncated OCR 'As.' No medicinal application beyond food coloring is attributed to this plant by Cobo.
