results.translator_notes:
{
  "title_inferred": "The Apincoya (Granadilla) and the Granadilla of the Quijos: Description, Symbolism, and Medicinal Uses",
  "original_text": "CAPITULO XII
De la apincoya

La apincoya es del género de las plantas volubles que se enredan y enlazan en otras, como las parras; su vástago es el primer año como el sarmiento, poco menos grueso que un dedo, el cual va engrosando con el tiempo de manera, que a los cinco o seis años se hace del grosor de tres o cuatro dedos. Echa muchos vástagos esta mata como la parra sarmientos, los cuales no se podan, pero vanse secando unos y brotando otros; y para en que se sustenten y extiendan, cuando no suben por algún árbol, se les hace un encañado como de parral. La hoja es grande y de figura de corazón; tiene de largo un palmo y poco menos de ancho.

Su flor es muy para ver, por la hechura tan extraña y maravillosa que tiene, que es de suerte, que quien con afecto pío y devoto la contempla, halla en ella figuradas muchas de las insignias de la pasión de Cristo, Nuestro Redentor. Brota esta flor de un pequeñuelo capullo triangular, cerrado con tres hojitas verdes, blandas, semejantes en la figura a las pencas de alcachofas, las cuales abiertas, se comienza a formar la flor en esta forma: del pezón con que nace del vástago, y es como el de la rosa, se forma el pie o asiento de la flor, que es también como el de la rosa, del cual nacen en torno cinco hojas del tamaño y figura de pencas de alcachofa, más angostas, blandas y blanquecinas que las tres primeras; son gruesecitas por el lomo y se van adelgazando hacia las orillas; por la parte de adentro son más blandas, llanas y blancas que por de fuera. Entre estas cinco hojas y las de la flor nacen otras cinco de la misma forma que ellas, un poco más angostas, aunque estas postreras son ya del todo blancas, muy delgadas y blandas; de modo, que podemos decir que encerró la Naturaleza esta misteriosa flor, como tan preciosa, debajo de tres velos, que son los tres órdenes de hojas

results.title_inferred: Description of the Chisiqui Granadilla (a Passiflora species), its fruit, cultivation in Lima, and indigenous name
results.original_text: como un sarmiento verde y cuadrado, de manera que entre una esquina y otra hacen un acanalado largo; mas, como va creciendo y engrosando, va perdiendo la figura esquinada y volviéndose redondo. La hoja es larga media tercia y ancha poco menos, por todas partes de igual anchura, con que hace figura ovada y algo acanalada. Su flor, de la misma forma que la del capítulo pasado, un poquito mayor, de más vivos colores y más olorosa. La fruta es en la apariencia como la granadilla ordinaria, algo más prolongada, pero diferenciase en lo demás, porque es olorosísima, la cáscara gruesa y tierna como la de la naranja, de la cual se hace regalada conserva; lo interior es como de la otra granadilla, pero de mucho más delicado sabor. Hase plantado esta fruta en esta ciudad de Lima, y yo la he visto nacida y que muchas veces ha echado flor, mas no ha llegado a dar fruto, sino que se cae la flor antes de cuajar, porque quiere temple más caliente y húmedo que la apincoya, que nace copiosamente en esta ciudad y en la Nueva España, adonde se ha llevado de este reino. A esta granadilla nombran los indios de la provincia de los Quijos, en su lengua, chisiqui.
results.translation: Like a green, square-stemmed vine [sarmiento], such that between one edge and the next they form a long groove [acanalado]; but as it grows and thickens, it loses its angular shape and becomes round. The leaf is half a third [approximately 5–6 inches] long and slightly less in width, of equal breadth on all sides, giving it an oval and somewhat channeled form. Its flower is of the same form as that described in the preceding chapter, a little larger, of more vivid colors, and more fragrant. The fruit in appearance resembles the ordinary granadilla [passion fruit], somewhat more elongated, but differs in other respects: it is most fragrant, the rind thick and tender like that of an orange, from which a choice preserve [conserva] is made; the interior is like that of the other granadilla, but of a much more delicate flavor. This fruit has been planted in this city of Lima, and I have seen it growing there and flowering many times, but it has never come to bear fruit, for the flower falls before setting, because it requires a warmer and more humid climate than the apincoya, which grows abundantly in this city and in New Spain, whither it has been taken from this kingdom. The Indians of the province of the Quijos call this granadilla, in their language, chisiqui.

entities_mentioned.name: chisiqui (granadilla)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Passiflora popenovii or possibly Passiflora ligularis / a related Passiflora sp. from the Quijos region of Ecuador
entities_mentioned.notes: The Quijos region corresponds to present-day Napo Province, Ecuador. The description of an aromatic, thick-rinded fruit distinct from the common granadilla suggests a distinct Passiflora species; the exact species is uncertain. Some scholars have tentatively associated Quijos granadillas with Passiflora popenovii or Passiflora cf. tripartita variants.

entities_mentioned.name: granadilla (ordinaria)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Passiflora ligularis
entities_mentioned.notes: The 'ordinary granadilla' used as a reference point throughout Cobo's text is generally identified as Passiflora ligularis, the sweet granadilla.

entities_mentioned.name: apincoya
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Passiflora pinnatistipula or Passiflora ligularis
entities_mentioned.notes: The apincoya is described as growing abundantly in Lima and having been introduced to New Spain; its exact identification is debated in the literature. Some authorities equate it with a cool-adapted Passiflora species tolerant of Lima's garúa climate.

results.translator_notes: Several OCR errors were silently corrected: 'omo' → 'como', 'terde' → 'verde', 'juadrado' → 'cuadrado', 'iin' → 'un', 'gando' / 'grosando' reconstructed as 'engrosando', 'Atedia' → 'media', 'jflor' → 'flor', '^ranadi-Ja' → 'granadilla', 'mucho' reconstructed from 'mulio', 'Hase' retained as a reflexive passive construction, 'cuajar' reconstructed from 'majar', 'apincoya' retained as a proper plant name, 'Baee' → 'nace'. The unit 'media tercia' is a period Spanish linear measure; a tercia is approximately one-third of a vara (~28 cm), making media tercia roughly 14 cm (~5.5 inches). No medical or pharmacological content appears in this segment; it is purely botanical and horticultural.

results.title_inferred: The Tumbo (a passion fruit relative)
results.original_text:
CAPITULO XIV
Del tumbo

El tumbo es otra especie de apincoya, más parecida a la granadilla de los Quijos que a la común; tiene el vástago cuadrado de cuatro esquinas, la hoja en figura de corazón, de un jeme de largo y poco menos de ancho; es lisa, más tiesa que la hoja de la granadilla, ni tan acanalada como la de los Quijos, y de un verde más escuro que entrambas. Del lomo della salen a las márgenes unas rayas muy distintas y señaladas. La flor es del talle que la de la granadilla, salvo que es más morada y las hojas que cercan la flor son asimismo moradas. La fruta es del tamaño de una cidra mediana, de figura ovada, poco menor que un huevo de avestruz; su cáscara, por de fuera, amarilla, y dentro, blanca y muy parecida a la de cidra; la médula es como la de la granadilla, aunque algo más agria y no de tan buen sabor como ella. Cuando está maduro el tumbo, despide de sí un olor tan vivo y suave, que uno solo basta para tener oloroso un aposento.
results.translation:
CHAPTER XIV
Of the Tumbo

The tumbo is another species of apincoya [passion flower relative], more similar to the granadilla of the Quijos [people] than to the common one. Its stem is square, with four corners; the leaf is heart-shaped, a hand-span [jeme] in length and slightly less in width; it is smooth, stiffer than the leaf of the granadilla, yet not as channeled as that of the Quijos variety, and of a darker green than either. From the midrib of the leaf, very distinct and clearly marked veins extend outward to the margins. The flower is of the same form as that of the granadilla, except that it is more purple, and the bracts [hojas] surrounding the flower are likewise purple. The fruit is the size of a medium citron [cidra], oval in shape, somewhat smaller than an ostrich egg; its rind is yellow on the outside, and white within, very similar to that of the citron; the pulp is like that of the granadilla, though somewhat more tart and not as pleasant in flavor. When the tumbo is ripe, it gives off a fragrance so lively and sweet that a single fruit is enough to perfume an entire room.

entities_mentioned.name: tumbo
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Passiflora tripartita var. mollissima (banana passionfruit) or possibly Passiflora tarminiana; the name 'tumbo' is also applied to Passiflora mollissima in Andean contexts
entities_mentioned.notes: Cobo describes it as a species of 'apincoya' (a broad Andean term for passion fruit relatives). The large, oval, yellow-skinned fruit with white rind and tart pulp, strongly fragrant when ripe, aligns best with tumbo (P. tripartita var. mollissima) still known by this name in Peru and Bolivia.

entities_mentioned.name: apincoya
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Generic Quechua term for passion fruit vines (Passiflora spp.)
entities_mentioned.notes: Used by Cobo as a broader category encompassing multiple Passiflora species including granadilla and tumbo.

entities_mentioned.name: granadilla de los Quijos
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Passiflora nitida or a related Amazonian species associated with the Quijos people of eastern Ecuador
entities_mentioned.notes: The Quijos were an indigenous group of the upper Amazon/eastern Andean slopes. The specific species Cobo refers to here is uncertain.

entities_mentioned.name: granadilla (common)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Passiflora ligularis
entities_mentioned.notes: The standard 'granadilla' of colonial Andean texts is generally identified as P. ligularis.

entities_mentioned.name: cidra
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Citrus medica (citron)
entities_mentioned.notes: Used here as a size and appearance comparator for the tumbo fruit's rind, not as a medicinal or botanical subject in its own right.

results.translator_notes: No medical preparations or ailments are described in this segment; it is purely a botanical and organoleptic description. The OCR errors corrected include: 'Qttijos' → 'Quijos'; 'vasta¬go' → 'vástago'; 'esq^nnas' / formatting artifacts normalized; 'T de un verde' → 'y de un verde'; 's^genes' → 'márgenes'; '^eialadas' → 'señaladas'; 'entram-^ a dos' → 'entrambas'. The word 'jeme' (a traditional Spanish unit of length, roughly the span from the tip of the thumb to the tip of the index finger, approximately 8–9 cm) has been retained in brackets as period-specific terminology.

results.title_inferred: The Vadea fruit of Guayaquil
results.original_text:
CAPITULO XV
De la vadea

En la ciudad y provincia de Guayaquil, diócesis de Quito, llaman los españoles vadea a una especie de apincolia [sic] que allí nace, muy parecida en sus hojas, flor y fruto al tumbo. Es tan grande como una mediana calabaza, de una tercia de largo, y algunas mayores, de figura ovada, en lo exterior amarilla, de un casco tierno como de cidra, de dos dedos de grueso, a manera de melón, de pulpa blanca y tierna, con muchas pepitas dentro como de granadilla, algo mayores, y un humor aguanoso y dulce con una punta de un agrete sabroso, que será cantidad de un cuartillo. Toda la fruta es comestible, pero lo más regalado es el humor y pepitas, que el casco, aunque se come, no es de tan buen gusto; es fruta ésta tan olorosa como el tumbo.
results.translation:
CHAPTER XV
On the vadea

In the city and province of Guayaquil, in the diocese of Quito, the Spaniards call vadea a species of passionflower vine [apincolia, sic] that grows there, very similar in its leaves, flower, and fruit to the tumbo. It is as large as a medium-sized gourd, about one-third of a vara [roughly 11 inches] in length, and some are larger; it is oval in shape, yellow on the exterior, with a tender rind like that of a citron [cidra], two fingers thick, in the manner of a melon, with white and tender flesh, containing many seeds inside like those of a granadilla [passionfruit], but somewhat larger, and a watery, sweet juice with a hint of a pleasant tartness [agrete sabroso], amounting to about a quartillo [roughly a quarter pint]. The entire fruit is edible, but the most prized parts are the juice and seeds, as the rind, though it can be eaten, is not of such good flavor. This fruit is as fragrant as the tumbo.

entities_mentioned.name: vadea
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Possibly Passiflora quadrangularis (giant granadilla) or a closely related large-fruited Passiflora species native to the Guayaquil region
entities_mentioned.notes: Cobo explicitly places this in the genus or family of apincolia (a colonial term for passionflower vines), compares it to the tumbo (Passiflora mollissima or related species) and granadilla (Passiflora ligularis or P. edulis). The large oval yellow fruit with thick rind, white pulp, and abundant juice is most consistent with Passiflora quadrangularis, though regional variants complicate precise identification.

entities_mentioned.name: apincolia
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Passiflora spp. (passionflower/passionfruit genus)
entities_mentioned.notes: Colonial Spanish term for passionflower vines; Cobo marks it [sic], suggesting he recognized the spelling or usage as irregular or dialectal.

entities_mentioned.name: tumbo
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Passiflora mollissima (banana passionfruit) or Passiflora tripartita var. mollissima
entities_mentioned.notes: Used here as a reference plant for comparison of leaves, flower, fruit, and fragrance.

entities_mentioned.name: granadilla
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Passiflora ligularis (sweet granadilla) or Passiflora edulis
entities_mentioned.notes: Used as a comparator for the size and appearance of the seeds.

entities_mentioned.name: cidra
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Citrus medica (citron)
entities_mentioned.notes: Used as a comparator for the texture and thickness of the rind.

results.translator_notes: No medicinal use or preparation method is described in this segment; it is purely a botanical and organoleptic description. The unit 'tercia' refers to one-third of a vara (approximately 28 cm or 11 inches). A 'quartillo' is approximately 0.5 liters or just under a pint, though colonial measures varied by region. The word 'humor' is used in its period sense of 'juice' or 'liquid' (from humoral/fluid vocabulary), not in the modern English sense. 'Agrete' (sometimes 'agraete') is a period term for a pleasantly sharp or tart flavor. The [sic] on 'apincolia' appears in Cobo's original text or in the source edition, signaling an acknowledged irregularity.

results.title_inferred: The Achupalla (Pineapple): Description, Varieties, and Uses
results.original_text:
CAPITULO XVII

De la achupalla

Entre las plantas del linaje de magüeyes tiene el principado la achupalla, por la excelencia de su fruto, que tiene el mismo nombre que la mata; y los españoles le dan nombre de piña de las Indias, por parecerse en el grandor y talle a la piña del pino. La planta es semejante a la zabila, porque produce unas pencas como ella, salvo que son un poco más largas, más enjutas, delgadas, duras y correosas, con unas pequeñas espinas en los lados. El tallo es también parecido al de la zabila, en cuya cumbre nace la fruta y echa cada tallo sólo una piña, la cual es del tamaño de una grande piña de las nuestras, y algunas como medianos melones y de la misma hechura que la piña, sólo que no es tan ahusada; en la parte superior tiene un cogollo áspero y espinoso. La corteza es amarilla, tierna y muy áspera al tacto, por estar toda llena de unos hoyos que hace con unas partes relevadas y otras hundidas; a cuya causa es menester, al mondarla, cortarle medio dedo de sustancia, para quitalle toda la cáscara, porque si no se monda bien, mordica con su agudeza la lengua y labios. La pulpa es blanca y algunas la tienen amarilla muy zumosa, tierna y aguanosa; el sabor es un agridulce muy apetitoso; entre la carne tiene unas sutiles briznas, con que ofende las encías, si se come mucho della. Suélese preparar para comer hecha rebanadas y echada algún tiempo en agua y sal, y tiénese así por mejor y más sana.

Es la achupalla de las más delicadas y sabrosas frutas de las Indias, y en opinión de algunos la mejor de todas, por concurrir en ella muchas calidades que la ennoblecen; porque ella es grande, muy olorosa y de regalado sabor.

Lo que yo siento es que no es inferior a todas las frutas que nacen de mata. Hállanse dos o tres diferencias de achupallas, en especial una que llaman en la provincia de Santa Cruz de la Sierra jarabata; es más silvestre, y su fruta más agria, de la cual hacen vino los indios. Echa la mata unas largas pencas, y dellas, curadas, se hacen sogas muy fuertes y cuerda de arcabuz. Comida en demasía la achupalla aumenta la cólera. Hácese de su zumo cierto vinagre muy oloroso y de buen gusto, fuerte y penetrativo, que vale para todas aquellas cosas a que sirve el vinagre común; aguado y aplicado a menudo es contra toda intemperie cálida; y lo mismo hace el zumo de la fruta verde, y ella, metida en la boca, mitiga la sed. Suelen los españoles hacer destas piñas una regalada conserva. Llámase en las demás lenguas del Perú, achupalla, en la quichua, chulu, en la aimará, y en la mexicana, matzatli.
results.translation:
CHAPTER XVII

On the Achupalla

Among the plants of the maguey family [linaje de magüeyes], the achupalla holds the foremost place by virtue of the excellence of its fruit, which bears the same name as the plant itself. The Spaniards call it piña de las Indias [pineapple of the Indies], because it resembles in size and shape the cone of the pine tree. The plant is similar to the aloe [zabila], for it produces thick leaves [pencas] like that plant, except that they are somewhat longer, drier, thinner, harder, and tougher, with small spines along the sides. The stalk is likewise similar to that of the aloe, and at its summit the fruit is born; each stalk produces only a single pineapple, which is the size of a large pine cone of our own [European] kind, and some are as large as medium melons, of the same form as a pine cone, except that it is not so tapered; at the upper part it has a rough and spiny crown. The rind is yellow, tender, and very rough to the touch, being entirely covered with a pattern of raised and sunken portions; for this reason, when peeling it, one must cut away nearly half a finger's breadth of substance in order to remove all the skin, because if it is not properly peeled it irritates [mordica] the tongue and lips with its sharpness. The pulp is white, though in some specimens it is yellow, very juicy, tender, and watery; the flavor is a very appetizing sweet-and-sour [agridulce]; within the flesh there are fine fibrous threads [briznas] which irritate the gums if one eats too much of it. It is customarily prepared for eating by slicing it and soaking it for some time in water and salt, and it is considered better and more wholesome treated in this way.

The achupalla is among the most delicate and flavorful fruits of the Indies, and in some people's opinion the best of all, for it combines many qualities that ennoble it: it is large, very fragrant, and of an exquisite flavor.

My own view is that it is inferior to none of the fruits that grow from low plants [de mata]. Two or three varieties of achupalla are found; in particular, one called jarabata in the province of Santa Cruz de la Sierra, which is wilder and its fruit more sour, from which the Indians make wine. This plant produces long thick leaves [pencas], and from these, once cured, very strong ropes and arquebus [arcabuz] cords are made. Eaten in excess, the achupalla increases bile [cólera]. From its juice a certain vinegar is made that is very fragrant, pleasant-tasting, strong, and penetrating [penetrativo], and serves for all the purposes for which common vinegar is used; diluted with water and applied frequently, it is effective against all hot intemperance [intemperie cálida]; the juice of the unripe fruit has the same effect, and the fruit itself, held in the mouth, alleviates thirst. The Spaniards commonly make a fine preserve [conserva] from these pineapples. In the other languages of Peru it is called achupalla; in Quechua [quichua], chulu; in Aymara [aimará]; and in Nahuatl [mexicana], matzatli.

entities_mentioned.name: achupalla / piña de las Indias
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Ananas comosus (pineapple)
entities_mentioned.notes: Cobo places it in the maguey family, a morphological grouping based on the rosette leaf habit rather than true taxonomy. The description—including the crown, rind, juicy pulp, sweet-and-sour taste, and fibrous flesh—leaves no doubt this is the pineapple. Multilingual names given: achupalla (general Peruvian), chulu (Quechua), aimará term not clearly recorded in source, matzatli (Nahuatl).

entities_mentioned.name: jarabata
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Possibly Ananas bracteatus or a wild/semi-wild pineapple variety, or alternatively Bromelia pinguin
entities_mentioned.notes: Described as a wilder, sourer variety from the province of Santa Cruz de la Sierra (present-day Bolivia), used to make wine, and whose leaves yield strong fiber for ropes and arquebus cords. The fiber use suggests a bromeliad. May be a wild pineapple relative or another large bromeliad.

entities_mentioned.name: zabila
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Aloe vera or related Aloe spp.
entities_mentioned.notes: Used as a morphological comparator for the leaf form of the achupalla, not as a subject of medical discussion in this passage.

results.translator_notes: Medical/pharmacological extractions: (1) Condition: 'intemperie cálida' [hot intemperance / febrile or inflammatory conditions]; Preparation: vinegar made from the juice of the achupalla, diluted with water and applied topically and frequently; Name used: vinagre de achupalla. (2) Condition: thirst; Preparation: fruit held in the mouth; Name used: achupalla (green). (3) Caution noted: excessive consumption increases bile [cólera], aligning with Galenic humoral theory. The passage notes that soaking sliced fruit in salted water was considered healthier, likely reflecting humoral modification of the fruit's 'cold and moist' or 'hot' qualities. The OCR corrupted several words (e.g., 'mat^' for 'mata', 'eimoblecen' for 'ennoblecen', 'inetrativo' for 'penetrativo', 'matzatll' for 'matzatli', 'desK' for 'demás', 'qui.' for 'quichua'); all have been silently corrected as unambiguous. The Aymara name appears to have been lost in the OCR—Cobo gives 'chulu' for Quechua and names an Aymara term that is illegible or missing in the source scan; the translation preserves the structure without fabricating the term.

results.title_inferred: On the Piñuelas (bromeliad fruit)
results.original_text:
CAPITULO XVIII
De las piñuelas

La planta que lleva las piñuelas es del género del magüey, debajo del cual género se comprehenden tantas especies dellos como en el de los cardones, que todos convienen en tener las hojas como las de la zabila, unas mayores y más gruesas que otras, de colores diferentes, con un tallo en medio más o menos grueso y largo, conforme es la casta del magüey. Esta mata de las piñuelas se levanta de la tierra tres o cuatro codos; sus pencas son de un verde blanquecino, armadas por las orillas de agudas espinas; por lo cual en algunas partes cercan con ellas las huertas. Crece el tallo hasta emparejarse con las pencas, y desde la mitad hasta la cumbre produce su fruto, que son desde treinta hasta sesenta piñuelas del tamaño de limones ceutíes, de cáscara amarilla y áspera; la pulpa es aguanosa como la de la granadilla, un poco agridulce, dado que unas son más dulces que otras. Llaman a esta fruta, en la provincia de Tierra Firme, piñuelas, por tener alguna semejanza con las piñas de la tierra.
results.translation:
CHAPTER XVIII
On the Piñuelas

The plant that bears the piñuelas belongs to the genus of the maguey [agave], under which genus there are comprehended as many species as there are in that of the cardones [columnar cacti], all of them agreeing in having leaves like those of the zabila [aloe], some larger and thicker than others, of different colors, with a stalk in the middle more or less thick and tall according to the variety of maguey. This shrub of the piñuelas rises three or four cubits from the ground; its leaves [pencas] are of a whitish green, armed along the edges with sharp spines, on account of which they are used in some places to enclose [fence] gardens. The stalk grows until it is level with the leaves, and from the midpoint up to the crown it produces its fruit, which consists of between thirty and sixty piñuelas the size of Ceuti lemons [a small Mediterranean lemon variety], with a yellow, rough rind; the pulp is watery like that of the granadilla [passionfruit], somewhat bittersweet, though some are sweeter than others. In the province of Tierra Firme this fruit is called piñuelas on account of its having some resemblance to the piñas [pineapples] of that land.

entities_mentioned.name: piñuelas
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Bromelia pinguin or related Bromelia spp. (wild pineapple / pinguin bromeliad)
entities_mentioned.notes: Described as a bromeliad bearing 30–60 small fruits the size of small lemons, with yellow rough rind and watery, bittersweet pulp. Cobo places it in the maguey genus, reflecting a broad 17th-century classification of spiny-leaved rosette plants. B. pinguin is the most commonly cited species under this common name in Mesoamerica and northern South America.

entities_mentioned.name: magüey
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Agave spp.
entities_mentioned.notes: Used by Cobo as a broad taxonomic grouping for rosette-forming, spiny-leaved plants, encompassing both true agaves and bromeliads.

entities_mentioned.name: cardones
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Columnar cacti, likely Pachycereus or Cereus spp.
entities_mentioned.notes: Referenced as a comparable genus with many species.

entities_mentioned.name: zabila
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Aloe vera or Aloe spp.
entities_mentioned.notes: Used as a morphological reference point for leaf shape.

entities_mentioned.name: granadilla
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Passiflora ligularis or Passiflora edulis (passionfruit)
entities_mentioned.notes: Cited as a textural/flavor comparison for the pulp of the piñuela.

entities_mentioned.name: limones ceutíes
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Small lemon variety associated with Ceuta (North Africa); likely Citrus limon of a small cultivar
entities_mentioned.notes: Used as a size reference for the piñuela fruit.

results.translator_notes: The source text is heavily degraded by OCR, with numerous mid-word line breaks and garbled characters (e.g., 'cáscara rilla' for 'cáscara amarilla', 'aguan^' for 'aguanosa', 'agrkr' for 'agridulce'). All corrections were unambiguous in context and have been silently applied in the cleaned original. Cobo's classification of the piñuela under 'magüey' reflects early colonial botanical practice of grouping succulent or spiny rosette plants together rather than any modern taxonomic relationship. The province of 'Tierra Firme' refers to the Spanish colonial designation for the northern coast of South America (roughly modern Venezuela and Colombia). No medical or pharmacological uses are described in this segment.

results.title_inferred: On the Piros (a wild fruit of Tierra Firme)
results.original_text:
CAPITULO XIX
De los piros

En la provincia de Tierra Firme llaman piros a una frutilla silvestre cuya mata es semejante a la de las piñuelas, salvo que no hace sus pencas tan gruesas y espinosas. No produce tallo, sino, en medio de las pencas, a raíz de la tierra, una cabeza llana en que nacen los piros muy apretados unos con otros; los cuales son de hechura de cermeñas, algo larguillos un poco que las piñuelas; por de fuera, colorados, y la pulpa, blanca, de mejor sabor que las piñuelas y de un olor grato y tan vivo, que por él cuando están maduras, se saca que las hay.
results.translation:
CHAPTER XIX
On the Piros

In the province of Tierra Firme [the Spanish Main], they call piros a small wild fruit whose plant is similar to that of the piñuelas [wild bromeliads], except that it does not produce leaves [pencas] as thick and spiny. It produces no stalk, but rather, in the midst of the leaves, at ground level, a flat head from which the piros grow very closely packed together. These are shaped like cermeñas [small pears or wardens], somewhat more elongated than the piñuelas; red on the outside, with white flesh, of better flavor than the piñuelas, and of a pleasant and so powerful a fragrance that, when they are ripe, one can tell they are there by their scent alone.

entities_mentioned.name: piros
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Possibly a bromeliad fruit, perhaps a species of Bromelia or Ananas (pineapple family), distinct from the common piñuela (Bromelia pinguin)
entities_mentioned.notes: The description — a rosette plant similar to piñuelas but with smaller spines, producing a dense, flat head of red-skinned, white-fleshed, elongated fruits at ground level — is consistent with the bromeliad family. The specific species is uncertain. No medicinal use is described in this passage.

entities_mentioned.name: piñuelas
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Bromelia pinguin (wild pineapple / maya pineapple)
entities_mentioned.notes: Used here as a comparative reference plant, not as a subject in its own right.

entities_mentioned.name: cermeñas
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Small variety of pear, likely Pyrus communis subsp. or a related cultivar known in early modern Spanish as 'cermeña' (a type of warden pear)
entities_mentioned.notes: Used purely as a shape analogy for the piros fruit.

results.translator_notes: The source text shows significant OCR degradation, with many lines beginning with stray characters ('I', 'i', '|'). These have been silently removed where the intended text was unambiguous. The phrase 'a raíz de la tierra' is rendered as 'at ground level' (literally 'at the root of the earth'), a common Spanish idiom of the period. No medical or pharmacological content appears in this segment; it is purely a botanical and organoleptical description. The word 'pencas' is retained in brackets in the translation note as it refers specifically to the stiff, often spiny leaves of bromeliad-type plants, for which no single English term is fully equivalent.

results.title_inferred: Chapter XX: On the Maguey Plant
results.original_text:
CAPITULO XX

Del maguey

El nombre genérico de maguey se atribuye a cierta especie dél, que es el más común y de que se hace el cáñamo de la tierra. Es una mata del talle de la zabila, que echa muchas hojas o pencas a la redonda, todas nacidas del tronco junto a la tierra; las cuales son delgadas, enjutas, correosas, acanaladas, de un verde escuro, con unas pequeñas espinas en las orillas, de cuatro dedos y más de ancho, y desde uno hasta cuatro pies de alto. Nacen muchísimas hojas en cada pie, y como crecen y suben a lo alto, se extienden para fuera, de modo que viene a quedar la mata con sus pencas con figura de una campana grande vuelta la boca hacia arriba. Cuando se han llegado estas pencas al grandor que han de tener, nace de en medio dellas el tallo, el cual crece desde uno hasta cinco o seis estados en alto; es tan grueso como la pierna y echa en su cumbre unos ramillos cortos sin hojas, en los cuales nace la semilla, que es algo mayor que bellotas de encina y del talle de pequeñuelas alcachofas, aunque no tiene cáscara dura ni meollo, sino unas penquillas verdes y más tiernas que las de la alcachofa. El tallo es derecho, liso, y después de seco, blanquecino y liviano, al modo de cañalieja. Tiene una corteza dura, que es la que le da la consistencia, poco menos gruesa que un dedo; lo demás es todo corazón blanco, blando, fofo y liviano.

Sirve esta planta a los indios para muchos usos, que parece se acomoda la naturaleza a la corta industria desta ruda gente, es criar las cosas de que tenían necesidad para sustentar y conservar la vida, tan preparadas y dispuestas, que no tuviesen más trabajo que cogerlas como nacen y aplicarlas a su uso. Sírvénles los magueyes de vigas para cubrir sus casas, sin tener que desbastarlos ni adelgazarlos, sino así como Dios los crió; porque no debe de haber planta en el mundo que sirva al uso de los hombres con menos beneficio que ésta; pues no tiene que hacer más que cortarle el cogollo y tronco y ponerla en el edificio; y éstas son las vigas con que vemos enmaderadas las casas de los naturales deste reino del Perú; y también ahora los españoles se aprovechan dellas en muchas partes para el mismo efecto.

El corazón es útil a los escultores, porque dél hacen imágenes de bulto muy perfectas y livianas; y es tan buena yesca, que llevando un trozo dél encendido un caminante, conserva el fuego como cuerda de arcabuz, y después de apagado, prenden en su carbón las centellas del pedernal tan bien como en la más fina yesca. Las pencas destas plantas se curan como el cáñamo y se hacen dellas cuerdas y sogas, así para la labranza como para jarcias de navios y otros usos; y de lo más delgado hacían los indios de algunas provincias lienzo como angeo para su vestir. Verdad es que toda suerte de cuerdas hechas desta planta no son tan fuertes como las del cáñamo. En lo más templado de las sierras del Perú nace gran suma de magueyes. No echa cada mata más de un tallo, y aquél cortado, luego se seca. Llámase esta planta, en la lengua de la isla Española, cuhiiya, y los españoles le dan en todas partes el nombre de maguey, que debieron tomar de los indios de Tierra Firme o de otra provincia desta América, que en las dos lenguas generales del Perú se dice, chuchau, en la quichua; en la aimará, tanca; y en la mexicana, metl.
results.translation:
CHAPTER XX

On the Maguey

The generic name maguey is applied to a certain species of this plant, which is the most common and from which the hemp [cáñamo] of this land is made. It is a shrub of the form of the aloe [zabila], which puts forth many leaves or fronds [pencas] all around, all growing from the base near the ground. These leaves are thin, dry, tough, channeled, of a dark green color, with small spines along the edges, more than four fingers wide, and from one to four feet tall. A great many leaves grow from each plant, and as they grow and rise upward they spread outward, so that the plant with its fronds takes on the shape of a large bell with its mouth turned upward. When the fronds have reached their full size, the stalk grows up from the center of them; it rises from one to five or six fathoms [estados] in height, is as thick as a man's leg, and puts forth at its top short, leafless branches, from which the seed grows. The seed is somewhat larger than acorns from an oak tree and shaped like small artichokes, though it has neither a hard shell nor a pith, but only small green leaflets more tender than those of the artichoke. The stalk is straight and smooth, and after drying becomes whitish and light, in the manner of elder-pith cane [cañalieja]. It has a hard bark, which gives it its firmness, nearly a finger's breadth thick; all the rest is a white, soft, spongy, and light core [corazón].

This plant serves the Indians for many purposes, for it seems that Nature adapts itself to the limited industry of these simple people by producing the things they need to sustain and preserve life so ready and prepared that they need do no more than gather them as they grow and put them to use. The maguey stalks serve them as beams for roofing their houses, with no need to trim or thin them — just as God created them — for there can scarcely be a plant in the world that serves the needs of men with less preparation than this one; one need only cut off the crown and base of the stalk and place it in the building. These are the beams with which we see the houses of the native people of this kingdom of Peru roofed; and the Spaniards too now make use of them in many places for the same purpose.

The core [corazón] is useful to sculptors, who fashion from it very fine and lightweight statues [imágenes de bulto]; and it makes such good tinder [yesca] that a traveler carrying a lit piece of it can keep the fire alive like a slow-match for a harquebus [cuerda de arcabuz], and even after it is extinguished, flint sparks catch in its charcoal as well as in the finest tinder. The fronds [pencas] of this plant are processed like hemp and made into cords and ropes, both for farming and for ships' rigging and other uses; and from the finest fibers the Indians of some provinces wove a cloth like coarse linen [angeo] for their clothing. It is true that all manner of cords made from this plant are not as strong as those made from hemp. In the more temperate zones of the Peruvian highlands a great quantity of maguey plants grow. Each plant produces only one stalk, and once that stalk is cut the plant quickly dies. This plant is called cuhiiya in the language of Hispaniola [la isla Española], and the Spaniards everywhere give it the name maguey, which they must have taken from the Indians of Tierra Firme or of some other province of the Americas. In the two principal languages of Peru it is called chuchau in Quechua [quichua]; tanca in Aymara [aimará]; and metl in Nahuatl [mexicana].

entities_mentioned.name: maguey / cuhiiya (Taíno) / chuchau (Quechua) / tanca (Aymara) / metl (Nahuatl)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Agave americana L. or related Agave spp. (family Asparagaceae); possibly also includes Furcraea spp. in Andean contexts
entities_mentioned.notes: Cobo treats 'maguey' as a generic name applied to the most common fiber-producing species. The Andean names chuchau and tanca more likely refer to Furcraea andina or related species, which are native to the Andes, rather than true Agave, which is more characteristic of Mexico and the Caribbean. The Nahuatl term metl confirms the Mexican Agave identification. Cobo notes the plant is monocarpic (flowers once, then dies). No specific medicinal use is described in this passage; uses cited are structural (roof beams), craft (sculpture medium), fire-starting (tinder/slow-match), and textile (rope, cordage, and coarse cloth).

entities_mentioned.name: zabila
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Aloe vera or Aloe spp.
entities_mentioned.notes: Used only as a morphological comparison for the growth habit of the maguey.

entities_mentioned.name: cañalieja
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Ferula communis or similar hollow-stemmed plant used as comparison for dried stalk texture
entities_mentioned.notes: Used as a comparison for the dried stalk's lightness and whitish color.

'Estado' is a Spanish unit of measure equivalent roughly to a fathom (~1.7 m), here translated as 'fathom' with the original in brackets. 3. 'Angeo' is a coarse linen or burlap-type fabric; the term has been preserved in brackets. 4. 'Cuerda de arcabuz' (harquebus slow-match) is a military reference to a slow-burning cord used to ignite the powder charge of an arquebus firearm, here used as a simile for the maguey core's ability to sustain an ember. 5. 'Cuhiiya' in the original appears as 'cuhiiya' (possibly 'cabuya' — a common Taíno/Carib word for agave fiber); the OCR may have introduced a double-i. The word is likely 'cabuya,' but the text as given has been preserved. 6. No medicinal or pharmacological applications are described in this chapter segment.

Del maguey del vino

El maguey del capítulo pasado es el que nace generalmente en este reino del Perú, y el de este capítulo es natural de la Nueva España, y difieren los dos en muchas cosas; y una es que todas las castas de magüeyes de la Nueva España tienen en la punta de las hojas una espina negra muy dura y aguda semejante a una lesna; de las cuales espinas carece el magüey deste reino. results.title_inferred: The Quelluquellu Plant
results.original_text:
CAPITULO XXIV
Del quelluquellu

El quelluquellu es una mata silvestre buena para leña; crece un estado en alto; produce muchas ramas o varas a la redonda, de color rojo; su hoja, en la figura, es muy semejante a la de la malva. Echa en pequeños manojitos una florecilla colorada mucho menor que la del olivo, a la cual sucede una frutilla también colorada, redonda, del tamaño de garbanzos, que, aunque es desabrida, la suelen comer los indios. Las raíces desta planta son rojas y medicinales, porque tiene facultad de restriñir y apretar. Llámase esta planta con este nombre en la lengua aimará.
results.translation:
CHAPTER XXIV
On the Quelluquellu

The quelluquellu is a wild shrub [mata silvestre] good for firewood; it grows to the height of one estado [approximately one fathom, or the height of a man]; it produces many branches or stems spreading outward, red in color; its leaf, in shape, is very similar to that of the mallow. It puts forth, in small clusters, a little red flower much smaller than that of the olive tree, which is followed by a small fruit, likewise red, round, and the size of chickpeas, which, though insipid, the Indians commonly eat. The roots of this plant are red and medicinal, for they have the property of constricting and binding [i.e., astringent action]. This plant is called by this name in the Aymara language.

entities_mentioned.name: quelluquellu
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Possibly Berberis or a species of Chuquiraga, or alternatively a Solanum or Salpichroa species native to the Andean altiplano; the red-branched shrub with mallow-like leaves and small round red fruits the size of chickpeas is difficult to pin down precisely. Some researchers have associated the name with species of the genus Dunalia (Solanaceae) or Chuquiraga (Asteraceae).
entities_mentioned.notes: The Aymara name 'quelluquellu' (also spelled 'quellu-quellu') is recorded in several colonial sources. The description — red branches, mallow-like leaves, small red clustered flowers, round red fruits the size of chickpeas, red astringent roots — is suggestive of a shrubby Andean plant, possibly in Solanaceae or a related family. Confident modern identification awaits botanical cross-referencing with Aymara ethnobotanical records.

entities_mentioned.name: malva
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Malva sylvestris (common mallow) or a related Malva sp.
entities_mentioned.notes: Used here only as a morphological reference point for leaf shape, not as a subject plant.

medical_extraction.condition_treated: Conditions requiring astringent or binding action [restriñir y apretar]; likely applied to diarrhea, hemorrhage, or similar complaints, though no specific ailment is named.
medical_extraction.preparation_method: Roots are indicated as the medicinal part; no specific preparation method is described beyond their identification as medicinal.

results.translator_notes: The word 'estado' is a unit of linear measure roughly equal to the height of a man (approximately 1.7–1.8 meters or about 5.5–6 feet). The phrase 'restriñir y apretar' denotes classical Galenic astringent faculty. The OCR introduced a stray 'a;' after 'olivo,' which has been silently corrected to a comma followed by 'a la cual' in the cleaned text. The Aymara provenance of the plant name is explicitly noted by Cobo, which is useful for ethnolinguistic tracking.

results.title_inferred: On the mullupachay plant
results.original_text:
CAPITULO XXV
De la mullupachay

La mullupachay es una mata de tres o cuatro codos de alto; echa muchas ramas delgadas, armadas de unas espinas blanquecinas, delgadas y agudas; la hoja es tan pequeña como la del arrayán. Produce unas florecillas blancas, no mayores que las de la col, con un botoncito amarillo en medio. Da una frutilla del tamaño y hechura de un hueso de aceituna: la cual, después de madura, se pone colorada escura que tira a negra, y es comestible, aunque salvaje y nada apetitosa. Llámase esta planta, en la lengua quichua del Perú, mullupachay.
results.translation:
CHAPTER XXV
On the mullupachay

The mullupachay is a shrub three or four cubits [codos] in height; it puts forth many slender branches, armed with whitish, thin, and sharp thorns; its leaf is as small as that of the myrtle [arrayán]. It produces small white flowers, no larger than those of the cabbage, with a small yellow button in the center. It bears a small fruit the size and shape of an olive stone [hueso de aceituna], which, once ripe, turns a dark red verging on black, and is edible, though wild and by no means appetizing. This plant is called, in the Quechua tongue of Peru, mullupachay.

entities_mentioned.name: mullupachay
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Possibly a species of Berberis (barberry) or Dunalia/Lycium (Solanaceae), given the thorny habit, small leaves, small white flowers, and dark olive-sized berries; Berberis lutea or a related Andean Berberis spp. is a plausible candidate
entities_mentioned.notes: The description — thorny shrub, small myrtle-like leaves, small white flowers with yellow center, olive-stone-sized fruit ripening dark red to black — is consistent with several Andean genera. Berberis (Berberidaceae) is the strongest candidate given the whitish thorns and berry description, but Solanaceous shrubs (e.g., Dunalia, Lycium) cannot be ruled out. No medicinal use is described in this passage; the fruit is noted as edible but unpalatable.

entities_mentioned.name: arrayán
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Myrtle (Myrtus communis or a local New World myrtle equivalent, e.g., Luma apiculata)
entities_mentioned.notes: Used here as a size reference for the leaves, not as a subject plant.

results.title_inferred: The Runcurунcu plant of Peru
results.original_text:
CAPITULO XXVI

Del runcurунcu

El runcurунcu pronunciando la r como en este nombre, caridad (no supieron los indios del Perú pronunciarla de otra manera), es una mata que crece un estado en alto y echa muchas ramas con que se hace copada; su hoja es parecida a la del granado, y la flor larguilla como la del tabaco, de color entre azul y morado. Produce una frutilla silvestre del tamaño de un garbanzo, redonda y colorada, la cual es de comer, mas de ninguna estima. Llámase así esta planta en la lengua general del Perú.
results.translation:
CHAPTER XXVI

On the Runcurунcu

The runcurунcu — pronouncing the 'r' as in the word 'caridad' [charity] (for the Indians of Peru knew no other way to pronounce it) — is a shrub that grows to the height of a man [un estado, roughly 1.8 m] and puts out many branches, forming a bushy crown. Its leaf resembles that of the pomegranate, and its flower is somewhat elongated, like that of tobacco, and is a color between blue and purple. It bears a small wild fruit the size of a chickpea, round and red, which is edible but of no esteem whatsoever. The plant takes this name from the general language [Quechua] of Peru.

entities_mentioned.name: runcurунcu (runcurунcu)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Possibly a species of Solanum or Cestrum (family Solanaceae), given the elongated tobacco-like flower, small round red fruit, and bushy habit consistent with Andean solanaceous shrubs; Solanum radicans or a related Andean Solanum has been suggested for similar Cobo descriptions
entities_mentioned.notes: No medicinal use is recorded for this plant in this passage. Cobo notes it solely as a food plant of no particular value. The Quechua name 'runcurунcu' and the phonological note about the trilled 'r' are of ethnolinguistic interest. The comparison of the flower to that of tobacco (Nicotiana tabacum) strongly suggests a solanaceous plant. The fruit description — round, red, chickpea-sized — is consistent with wild Solanum berries.

entities_mentioned.name: granado (pomegranate)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Punica granatum
entities_mentioned.notes: Used only as a comparative reference for leaf shape.

entities_mentioned.name: tabaco (tobacco)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Nicotiana tabacum

No ailment or preparation method is described; this entry is purely botanical and ethnobotanical. 'Un estado' is a Spanish linear measure approximately equal to the height of a man (~1.65–1.80 m). 'Lengua general del Perú' refers to Quechua (Runa Simi), the administrative lingua franca of the Inca Empire.

results.title_inferred: Tilxochil (Vanilla) and Hubo de Lagarto (Manchineel or related toxic fruit tree)
results.original_text:
CAPITULO XXVII
Del tilxochil

Llámase así en la Nueva España la planta que produce las vainillas que se echan en el chocolate y le dan muy agradable olor. Es una mata de género de bejuco; su tallo voluble y verde; las hojas, grandes, largas un palmo y dos o tres dedos de ancho, y por todas partes es igual su ancho, con que hace figura de lengua; es gruesa y muy lisa. La flor es blanquilla, y el fruto, unas vainillas largas un palmo, más delgadas que algarrobas, y cuando están maduras se ponen amarillas. Cogidas de la mata, las curan al sol con gran cuidado, y después de curadas, quedan de color de pasas y correosas y jugosas, como ellas. Dentro están llenas de unos granitos negros, no mayores que la semilla del tamarco. Son tenidas estas vainillas por género de especia aromática y muy preciosas para echar en el chocolate. Donde nacen las mejores vainillas es en la provincia de Soconusco, diócesis de Chiapa, adonde acuden mercaderes de toda la Nueva España a comprarlas, y se venden diez o doce a real, y llevadas a México, tienen doble valor.

CAPITULO XXVIII
Del hubo de lagarto

En la costa de la mar del Sur de la Nueva España y en Tierra Firme llaman hubo de lagarto a ciertas manzanillas que nacen en tierras yuncas. La mata crece un estado en alto, poco más o menos; echa muchas ramas armadas de espinas y hace la hoja semejante a la del granado. La fruta es en el color y hechura muy parecida a pequeñas manzanas; tiene la pulpa amarilla, esponjosa y tierna, con una pepita dentro del tamaño de una avellana, redonda, blanca y tierna. Aunque la pulpa de esta fruta es de comer, y no poco sabrosa, la pepita es veneno mortífero, dado que al gusto es dulce y de buen sabor.
results.translation:
CHAPTER XXVII
On the Tilxochil

This is the name given in New Spain to the plant that produces the vanilla pods [vainillas] that are added to chocolate and impart to it a very pleasant aroma. It is a climbing shrub [mata de género de bejuco]; its stem is twining and green; the leaves are large, a span [palmo] long and two or three fingers wide, of equal width throughout, giving them the shape of a tongue; they are thick and very smooth. The flower is whitish, and the fruit consists of pods a span in length, more slender than carob pods [algarrobas], and when ripe they turn yellow. Once harvested from the plant, they are cured in the sun with great care, and after curing they become the color of raisins, tough and juicy, like them. Inside they are filled with tiny black granules no larger than the seed of [ILLEGIBLE: 'tamarco' — possibly tamarind or an otherwise unidentified local plant]. These pods are regarded as a type of aromatic spice and are highly prized for adding to chocolate. The finest vanilla pods grow in the province of Soconusco, in the diocese of Chiapa, where merchants from all of New Spain come to buy them; they sell for ten or twelve to the real, and once brought to Mexico City they fetch twice the price.

CHAPTER XXVIII
On the Hubo de Lagarto [Lizard's Plum]

Along the Pacific coast [coast of the South Sea] of New Spain and in Tierra Firme, certain small apple-like fruits [manzanillas] that grow in lowland [yunca] regions are called hubo de lagarto. The shrub grows to approximately one estado [roughly the height of a man] in height, more or less; it puts out many branches armed with thorns and bears leaves resembling those of the pomegranate [granado]. The fruit closely resembles small apples in color and form; it has yellow, spongy, and tender pulp, with a pit [pepita] inside the size of a hazelnut, round, white, and soft. Although the pulp of this fruit is edible and quite flavorsome, the pit is a deadly poison [veneno mortífero], even though it tastes sweet and pleasant to the palate.

entities_mentioned.name: tilxochil / vainilla
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Vanilla planifolia (vanilla orchid)
entities_mentioned.notes: The Nahuatl name 'tilxochil' (also spelled 'tlilxochitl') literally means 'black flower.' Cobo accurately describes the climbing vine habit, tongue-shaped leaves, whitish flower, and curing process of vanilla pods. He notes the finest quality came from Soconusco (modern-day Chiapas/Guatemala border region), historically renowned for cacao and vanilla production.

entities_mentioned.name: hubo de lagarto / manzanilla
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Possibly Hippomane mancinella (manchineel) or a species of Ximenia or Spondias with toxic seeds; identification uncertain
entities_mentioned.notes: 'Hubo de lagarto' (lizard's plum) is a regional common name. The description of edible pulp with a mortally poisonous sweet-tasting seed narrows candidates considerably. Manchineel (Hippomane mancinella) is toxic throughout, not matching the edible pulp description. Ximenia americana has edible pulp and toxic seeds and fits the thorny shrub description better. Confidence remains low without further corroborating details.

results.translator_notes: 'Tamarco' (in 'semilla del tamarco') is likely an OCR or scribal corruption; the intended word is unclear — possibly 'tamarindo' (tamarind) or a local plant name. Marked as ILLEGIBLE in translation. 'Yunca' (yungas) refers to warm lowland coastal/valley regions in colonial Peruvian and broader Andean/tropical usage. 'Estado' is a unit of height roughly equivalent to the height of an average man (~1.7 m). The OCR produced 'tafmeo,' 'rami lias,' 'Soconusíie^,' 'Ih*man,' and other corruptions, all silently corrected where the intended reading was unambiguous.

  "title_inferred": "Chapter XXIX: On Coca — Cultivation, Trade, Use, and Post-Harvest Processing in Peru",
  "original_text": "CAPITULO XXIX
De la coca

En este reino del Perú no hay cosa más conocida que la coca, cuyo trato es de los gruesos y de mayor ganancia que hay en las Indias y con que no pocos españoles se han hecho ricos. Es la coca una mata no mayor que los manzanos enanos de España, de hasta un estado en alto; su hoja, que es la que tanto precian y estiman los indios, es del tamaño y talle de la del limón y a veces menor. Da una frutilla colorada, seca y sin jugo, tamaña como pequeños escaramujos, que sólo sirve de semilla. Plantaban y cultivaban antiguamente la coca los naturales del Perú a manera de viñas, y era de tanta estimación su hoja, que solamente la comían los reyes y nobles y la ofrecían en los sacrificios que de ordinario hacían a los falsos dioses. A los demás les era prohibido el uso della sin licencia de los gobernadores. Mas, después que se acabó el señorío de los reyes Incas y su prohibición, con el deseo que la gente común tenía de comer la fruta vedada, se entregó a ella con tanto exceso, que viendo los españoles el gran consumo que había desta mercadería, plantaron otras muchas más chácaras de las que antes había, especialmente en la comarca de la ciudad del Cuzco, cuyos vecinos tuvieron en un tiempo su mayor riqueza en estas heredades; porque solía rentar cada año una buena chácara de coca más de veinte mil pesos. Pero ya ha dado gran baja, y su contratación va de cada día adelgazando; lo uno, porque los indios han venido en gran disminución, y lo otro, porque con el trato y comunicación con los españoles, se van desengañando y cayendo en la cuenta de que les es de más provecho el pan, vino y carne, que el zumo que chupaban desta yerba; y así de mejor gana gastan ya su dinero en estos mantenimientos, que no en la coca, tan preciada de sus antepasados.

El uso desta hoja es desta manera: della, majada, hacen los indios unas pe

results.title_inferred: Properties and medicinal uses of coca leaf (continued)
results.original_text:
es la que, después de seca, queda con su color verde, tiesa y lisa. Finalmente, es la yerba más delicada que se puede imaginar, porque le daña aire, sol, agua y humedad.

Su temperamento es caliente y seco, con muy buena estipticidad: mascada de ordinario, aparta de los dientes toda corrupción y neguijón, y los emblanquece, aprieta y conforta. A mí me sucedió que llamando una vez a un barbero para que me sacara una muela, porque se andaba y me dolía mucho, me dijo el barbero que era lástima sacarla, porque estaba buena y sana; y como se hallase presente un amigo mío religioso, me aconsejó que mascase coca por algunos días. Hícelo así, con que se me quitó el dolor de la muela y ella se afijó como las demás. El zumo de la coca conforta el estómago y ayuda a la digestión; quita toda la ventosidad y mal de ijada. Los polvos desta hoja, tomados de ordinario y que a dos partes dellos se eche una de azúcar, son contra el asma o ronquera del pecho. La semilla de la coca tomada en sahumerio, dicen los indios que estanca todo flujo de sangre de narices; y el cocimiento della, bebido con miel de abejas y yerba buena, aprovecha a la relajación del estómago y contra los vómitos. El cocimiento de la hoja bebido de ordinario, vale contra las cámaras, deseca las llagas y las mundifica; los polvos mezclados con sal y clara de huevo, consolidan y aprietan toda fractura y disolación de huesos; y echados en poca cantidad en las úlceras, las desecan y encoran; y el mismo efecto hacen en las llagas de los disciplinantes, como el polvo del arrayán. Finalmente, entra la coca, por su estipticidad, en los vinos y cocimientos estípticos, y hace su confortación como los demás constipantes y confortantes.
results.translation:
it is the one that, after drying, retains its green color, remaining stiff and smooth. In sum, it is the most delicate herb one can imagine, for it is damaged by air, sun, water, and humidity.

Its temperament [complexion] is hot and dry, with a very pronounced astringency [estipticidad]: chewed regularly, it removes all decay and tartar [neguijón] from the teeth, and whitens, tightens, and strengthens them. It happened to me that, having once called for a barber to pull a tooth because it was loose and causing me great pain, the barber told me it would be a pity to extract it, as it was in good and sound condition; and since a religious friend of mine happened to be present, he advised me to chew coca for some days. I did so, and the toothache left me and the tooth became as firm as the rest. The juice of coca strengthens the stomach and aids digestion; it relieves all flatulence [ventosidad] and flank pain [mal de ijada]. The powders of this leaf, taken regularly with one part sugar added to every two parts of powder, are good against asthma or hoarseness of the chest. The seed of coca taken as a fumigation [sahumerio], the Indians say, stanches all bleeding from the nose [flujo de sangre de narices]; and the decoction of it, drunk with honey and spearmint [yerba buena], is beneficial for relaxation of the stomach and against vomiting. The decoction of the leaf drunk regularly is effective against diarrhea [cámaras], dries out sores [llagas] and cleanses them; the powders mixed with salt and egg white [clara de huevo] consolidate and bind all fractures and dislocations of bones; and applied in small quantity to ulcers, they dry and heal them; and they produce the same effect on the wounds of flagellants [llagas de los disciplinantes], as does the powder of myrtle [arrayán]. In sum, coca enters, by virtue of its astringency, into astringent wines and decoctions, and produces its strengthening effect like all other constringents and fortifiers.

entities_mentioned.name: coca
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Erythroxylum coca
entities_mentioned.notes: Discussed extensively throughout this chapter. Cobo describes its temperament as hot and dry with strong astringency. Multiple medicinal applications are listed: dental care (chewing), toothache, stomach complaints, flatulence, flank pain, asthma/hoarseness (powder with sugar), nosebleed (seed fumigation), stomach relaxation and vomiting (decoction with honey and spearmint), diarrhea, wound healing (leaf decoction), bone fractures and dislocations (powder with salt and egg white), ulcer treatment, and use in astringent preparations.

entities_mentioned.name: yerba buena
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Mentha spicata (spearmint) or Mentha × piperita (peppermint)
entities_mentioned.notes: Used in combination with honey and coca decoction for stomach relaxation and vomiting. 'Yerba buena' in colonial Spanish contexts typically refers to a mint species.

entities_mentioned.name: arrayán
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Myrtus communis (common myrtle) or a Neotropical Myrtaceae species
entities_mentioned.notes: Referenced only as a comparative standard for wound-healing powder; not a primary subject of this passage. In the Andean context, could refer to a local myrtle-family plant.

results.translator_notes: Several OCR artifacts were silently corrected: 'neguijón' (dental tartar/decay) restored from garbled form; 'consolidan' restored from 'eonso-lidan'; 'disolación' (dislocation) from 'disola-ción'; 'clara' from 'dara'; 'narices' inferred from 'naricea'; 'encoran' (to heal over, form a scab) is a period medical term preserved as such. 'Mal de ijada' denotes flank or side pain, possibly colic or kidney/flank ailment — a common humoral-medical term. 'Disciplinantes' refers to religious flagellants; their self-inflicted wounds are cited as a clinical analog. 'Neguijón' is a period dental term for tartar or carious decay, retained in brackets in the translation.

results.title_inferred: Purgative Little Beans (Habillas Purgativas)
results.original_text:
CAPITULO XXXI

De las habillas purgativas

Llaman así los españoles a estas habillas, por el efecto que hacen; prodúcelas una mata del grandor de la del romero; tiene hojas anchas y redondas, con tres puntas; y las habillas son redondas y chatas, como habas de la mar, con la corteza negra.
results.translation:
CHAPTER XXXI

On the Purgative Little Beans

The Spaniards call these little beans [habillas] by this name on account of the effect they produce. They grow on a shrub [mata] of the size of a rosemary bush; it has broad, round leaves with three points; and the little beans are round and flat, like sea-beans [habas de la mar], with a black husk [corteza].

entities_mentioned.name: habillas purgativas
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Possibly Mucuna urens or a related species (Fabaceae); alternatively Canavalia or Entada species, sometimes called 'sea-beans'
entities_mentioned.notes: The description of a shrub the size of rosemary with broad three-pointed leaves and round, flat, black-husked seeds is suggestive of a legume with purgative properties. The comparison to 'habas de la mar' (sea-beans) may indicate a coastal or drift-seed species. Positive identification requires more morphological or geographical context.

entities_mentioned.name: romero
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Rosmarinus officinalis (rosemary)
entities_mentioned.notes: Used here only as a size reference for the shrub, not as a subject plant.

entities_mentioned.name: habas de la mar
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Possibly Entada gigas or similar large-seeded legume drift seeds
entities_mentioned.notes: Used as a comparative descriptor for the shape of the seeds.

results.translator_notes: The OCR has fragmented nearly every line with a spurious 'I' character at the line endings, which have been silently corrected as these are clearly OCR artifacts from column justification marks or page edges. The chapter number in the OCR reads 'XXX I' but is almost certainly 'XXXI' (31). No preparation method or specific ailment is described in this segment beyond the general purgative effect implied by the plant's Spanish name; Cobo presumably elaborates in subsequent text not included here.

results.title_inferred: Two Nicaraguan and Guatemalan ornamental plants: Tapaxochitl and the Grafted Flower
results.original_text:
CAPITULO XXXII

Del tapaxochitl

Esta es una mata muy común en la provincia de Nicaragua, que por la hermosura de sus flores se planta en los jardines. Crece tres o cuatro palmos en alto y se hace muy copada; echa muchos tallos, que son redondos y con muchos ñudos; de los cuales nacen las hojas de dos en dos y son parecidas a las del granado, salvo que son vellosas. En la cumbre de los vástagos nace la flor, la cual es de un colorado escuro que tira a morado, muy vistosa, algo parecida a la clavellina, sólo que es redonda a modo de botón, del tamaño de una avellana, compuesta de unas hojitas muy menudas, duras, ásperas y puntiagudas, que todas casi espinan tocándolas con la mano, entre las cuales asoman unas punticas blancas que son unas florecitas muy menudas; no tiene esta flor olor alguno, más que buen parecer.

CAPITULO XXXIII

De la flor injerta

Esta especie de flor es conocida en pocas partes; críanla en la ciudad de Guatimala, en sus jardines, las personas curiosas, adonde me las mostraron por cosa maravillosa. La mata es desta hechura: produce unos trozos verdes como pepinos medianos, y éste es un tronco o raíz; la hoja es como de zábila, más delgada, angosta y corta y de verde más vivo; echa unas florecitas moradas poco mayores que las de la col, y prodúcelas en racimos, de suerte que cada ramo o tallo parece un ramillete muy copado; y aunque es de muy buen parecer, carece de olor. El modo con que plantan esta flor es muy extraño: toman un trocillo desta planta, y con un poco de barro lo pegan al tronco o rama de cualquier árbol, de manera que le sirve el barro sólo de tenerlo no se caiga; y sin otro beneficio echa sus raíces, que son blandas y delgadas como cuerdas, las cuales se van revolviendo al tronco del árbol, y cayéndose después el barro, queda esta planta abrazada con sus raicillas al árbol, del cual, con sólo esto, recibe el jugo que le basta para crecer como las demás plantas injertas en otras; y por eso le damos el nombre de flor injerta.
results.translation:
CHAPTER XXXII

On the Tapaxochitl

This is a very common shrub in the province of Nicaragua, which is planted in gardens on account of the beauty of its flowers. It grows three or four spans [palmos] in height and becomes very bushy; it puts forth many stems, which are round and have many nodes [ñudos], from which the leaves grow in pairs, resembling those of the pomegranate, except that they are downy. At the top of the shoots the flower grows; it is a dark crimson tending toward purple, very striking, somewhat resembling the pink [clavellina], except that it is round in the manner of a button, the size of a hazelnut, composed of very small, hard, rough, and pointed little leaves [bracts], which are almost all spiny when touched with the hand, among which small white points emerge that are very tiny little flowers. This flower has no scent whatsoever, but only a pleasing appearance.

CHAPTER XXXIII

On the Grafted Flower

This species of flower is known in few places; it is cultivated in the city of Guatemala, in their gardens, by persons of curious mind, where it was shown to me as a marvelous thing. The shrub is of this form: it produces green segments resembling medium-sized cucumbers, and these constitute the trunk or root; the leaf is like that of the aloe [zábila], but thinner, narrower, and shorter, and of a more vivid green; it puts forth small purple flowers somewhat larger than those of the cabbage, and produces them in clusters, so that each branch or stem resembles a very full bouquet; and although it is of very pleasing appearance, it has no scent. The manner in which this flower is planted is very strange: they take a small segment of this plant and, with a little clay [barro], attach it to the trunk or branch of any tree, in such a way that the clay serves only to hold it in place so it does not fall; and without any further care it puts forth roots, which are soft and slender like cords, which wind themselves around the trunk of the tree; and after the clay falls away, this plant remains embraced to the tree by its little roots, from which, by this alone, it receives enough sap to grow as do other grafted plants; and for this reason we give it the name of the grafted flower [flor injerta].

entities_mentioned.name: tapaxochitl
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Possibly Combretum fruticosum or a species of Bouvardia or Calliandra; the description of button-like, spiny, dark crimson-to-purple flower heads in Nicaragua suggests possible affinity with Calliandra surinamensis or a related Mimosoid legume
entities_mentioned.notes: The Nahuatl name 'tapaxochitl' has been applied to several distinct plants in colonial sources. The description—paired leaves resembling pomegranate, round hazelnut-sized flower heads of dark crimson-purple composed of hard pointed bracts with tiny white flowers emerging between them—is distinctive but does not map cleanly to a single confirmed species. Mimosoid legumes with compact heads are plausible candidates.

entities_mentioned.name: flor injerta (Grafted Flower)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Almost certainly a epiphytic cactus, most likely a species of Rhipsalis, Hylocereus, or Epiphyllum (family Cactaceae); the green cucumber-like segments (stem joints), aloe-like leaves, purple flowers in clusters, and the described method of propagation by attaching a stem segment to a host tree with clay—after which it roots and clings epiphytically—are strongly characteristic of an epiphytic cactus
entities_mentioned.notes: The propagation method described (attaching a stem cutting to a tree trunk with clay, allowing it to root and grow epiphytically) is highly consistent with the cultivation of epiphytic cacti. The purple flowers and the segment-based stem morphology are consistent with genera such as Rhipsalis or Disocactus. The comparison of leaves to zábila (aloe) likely refers to the flattened or leaf-like cladodes. No medicinal use is described for either plant in this segment.

entities_mentioned.name: granado (pomegranate)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Punica granatum
entities_mentioned.notes: Used as a comparative reference for the leaf shape of the tapaxochitl.

entities_mentioned.name: clavellina
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Dianthus caryophyllus (pink / carnation)
entities_mentioned.notes: Used as a comparative reference for the appearance of the tapaxochitl flower.

entities_mentioned.name: zábila
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Aloe vera or related Aloe sp.
entities_mentioned.notes: Used as a comparative reference for the leaf form of the flor injerta.
