results.title_inferred: The Stinking Herb (Yerba Hedionda)
results.original_text:
CAPITULO XXXV
De la yerba hedionda

Este nombre damos en este reino del Perú, y con el mismo la vi nombrar en la isla Española, a cierta planta que da de sí mal olor en tanto grado, que llegándose cerca della la persona, aunque no la vea, conocerá por el hedor haberla allí. Esta es una mata que crece dos estados y echa muchas ramas, cuya hoja es parecida a la del durazno, salvo que es de un verde más claro. Y no hay para qué describir más propiedades della, pues su mal olor la da bien a conocer, sin que yo sepa de otra planta que en esto se le parezca. Nace mucha por las chácaras deste valle de Lima y sirve para la medicina, porque con el zumo de sus hojas curan las heridas, y yo vi curar con él a un muchacho de una mordedura de perro; y con su cocimiento se dan baños de piernas para curar la flema salada y cualquiera inflamación e intemperie cálida.
results.translation:
CHAPTER XXXV
Of the Stinking Herb

This is the name we give in this kingdom of Peru — and I saw it called by the same name on the island of Hispaniola — to a certain plant that gives off such a foul odor that a person coming near it, even without seeing it, will know it is there by the stench. It is a shrub [mata] that grows to the height of two fathoms [estados] and puts out many branches, whose leaf resembles that of the peach tree, except that it is of a lighter green. There is no need to describe its properties further, since its foul smell makes it readily identifiable, and I know of no other plant that resembles it in this respect. It grows abundantly in the farm plots [chácaras] of this valley of Lima and is used in medicine: the juice [zumo] of its leaves is used to treat wounds, and I myself saw a boy cured with it of a dog bite; and a decoction [cocimiento] of it is used in leg baths [baños de piernas] to treat 'flema salada' [lit. salt phlegm; a humoral condition, likely eczema or a moist skin eruption] and any inflammation or hot imbalance [intemperie cálida].

entities_mentioned.name: yerba hedionda
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Cassia occidentalis (Coffee Senna) or possibly Chenopodium ambrosioides (epazote); Cassia occidentalis is the most commonly cited candidate for this colonial name in Peru and Hispaniola contexts
entities_mentioned.notes: The name 'yerba hedionda' (stinking herb) was applied to multiple species across the Americas. The description — strong fetid odor, peach-like leaves, shrub height of roughly 3–4 meters, presence in Lima valley farmlands — is broadly consistent with Cassia occidentalis (Coffee Senna / Senna occidentalis), a widespread weed with notoriously unpleasant smell. Chenopodium ambrosioides (epazote) is another candidate but is typically much smaller. The dual attestation in both Peru and Hispaniola under the same name strengthens the Cassia occidentalis hypothesis, as that species is pantropical.

results.translator_notes: 1. 'Dos estados': an 'estado' is a unit of height roughly equivalent to a fathom or adult body-height (~1.7 m), so 'dos estados' suggests a shrub of approximately 3–4 meters. 2. 'Flema salada' is a humoral medicine term, literally 'salt phlegm,' used to describe weeping or crusty skin conditions akin to what modern medicine might call eczema or psoriasis. 3. 'Intemperie cálida' refers to a hot humoral imbalance, a standard Galenic diagnostic category. 4. 'Chácaras' (from Quechua 'chacra') denotes small cultivated farm plots, common in colonial Peruvian agricultural contexts. 5. Several OCR artifacts were silently corrected: 'clamos' → 'damos', 'niisiuo' → 'mismo', 'i>or' → 'por', '(|ue' → 'que', 'eleste' → 'deste', 'hañes' → 'baños', 'V YO' → 'y yo'.

results.title_inferred: On the Floripondio (Angel's Trumpet)
results.original_text:
CAPITULO XXXVI
Del floripondio

En esta ciudad de Lima llamamos floripondio a cierta flor, y el mismo nombre damos a la mata que la produce, que es un arbolillo del grandor de un pequeño ciruelo; y en otras partes crece más. Echa las ramas esparcidas a los lados, sin subir derechas, y así la mata no es muy copada; su hoja, en la figura y tamaño, es semejante a la del llantén; es vellosa y algo áspera. Su fruto es solamente flores, y es cosa maravillosa, y que no hallamos en otra planta, que todo el año va echando flores en tanta abundancia, que siempre está cubierta de ellas: porque unas se alcanzan a otras, de manera que, por muchas que cojan de ella cada día, nunca se agotan, naciendo luego otras muchas nuevas. Es esta flor la mayor de cuantas producen los árboles y matas, hermosísima a la vista, blanca y de hechura de campanilla; tiene un palmo de largo, y el remate o boca de gran ruedo, de la cual salen cinco puntas torcidas para afuera; el cuello o cañón es largo medio palmo.

Suélense poner estas flores en los candelabros y dentro de ellas las velas, de suerte que sirven de candilejas para adornar los altares y las mesas.

Tienen un olor tan agradable y penetrante, mas es para de lejos que para sufrirse de cerca; porque una sola flor de estas que esté en un aposento, huele tanto, que causa enfado y aun puede dar dolor de cabeza a los que están dentro de él. Los españoles debieron traer esta planta de alguna provincia de estas Indias a esta de Lima, porque los naturales de ella no le saben el nombre, y todos, españoles e indios, la llaman floripondio y flor de campanilla. No ha muchos años que desde esta ciudad de Lima llevó esta planta a México un caballero conocido mío. Sus hojas son provechosas para curar quebraduras, majadas y puestas calientes en forma de emplasto sobre la rotura.
results.translation:
CHAPTER XXXVI
On the Floripondio

In this city of Lima we call 'floripondio' a certain flower, and we give the same name to the shrub that produces it, which is a small tree [arbolillo] about the size of a small plum tree, though in other places it grows larger. It puts forth branches spread out to the sides, without growing straight upward, so that the shrub is not very dense. Its leaf, in shape and size, resembles that of the plantain [llantén; Plantago sp.]; it is downy and somewhat rough. Its only fruit, so to speak, is its flowers, and it is a wondrous thing, not found in any other plant, that throughout the entire year it puts forth flowers in such abundance that it is always covered with them: for they succeed one another continuously, so that no matter how many are gathered from it each day, they are never exhausted, as many new ones quickly appear. This flower is the largest produced by any tree or shrub, most beautiful to behold, white and shaped like a bell [campanilla]; it measures a span [palmo] in length, and the end or mouth is of great diameter, from which five points [petals] extend, twisted outward; the neck or tube [cañón] is half a span in length.

These flowers are commonly placed in candlesticks [candelabros] with candles set inside them, so that they serve as small lamps to adorn altars and tables.

They have a fragrance that is most agreeable and penetrating, yet it is better appreciated from a distance than endured at close quarters; for a single flower of this kind in a room gives off so strong a scent that it causes discomfort and can even produce headaches in those who remain inside. The Spaniards must have brought this plant from some province of these Indies to this one of Lima, for the native people here do not know its name, and everyone—Spaniards and Indians alike—calls it 'floripondio' and 'flor de campanilla' [bell flower]. Not many years ago, an acquaintance of mine, a gentleman, carried this plant from this city of Lima to Mexico. Its leaves are beneficial for curing hernias [quebraduras], when mashed and applied hot in the form of a poultice [emplasto] over the rupture.

entities_mentioned.name: floripondio / flor de campanilla
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Brugmansia arborea or Brugmansia suaveolens (Angel's Trumpet; family Solanaceae)
entities_mentioned.notes: Cobo's description—large white pendant bell-shaped flower, strongly fragrant, headache-inducing scent, shrub resembling a small plum tree, year-round blooming—is a classic description of a Brugmansia species. The genus is native to the Andean region of South America. Cobo's note that Lima natives had no indigenous name for it suggests it may have been introduced to Lima from another Andean region. Brugmansia species contain tropane alkaloids (scopolamine, hyoscyamine) and were widely used in indigenous ritual and medicine across the Andes.

entities_mentioned.name: llantén
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Plantago major or related Plantago sp. (broadleaf plantain)
entities_mentioned.notes: Used here only as a leaf-shape comparator, not as the subject plant.

medical_extraction.preparation_method: Leaves mashed and applied hot as a poultice (emplasto) directly over the site of the hernia or rupture.

results.title_inferred: On the Cantuta and the Muña: Two Peruvian Plants
results.original_text:
CAPITULO XXXVII

De la cantuta

La cantuta es una mata que echa muchas ramas alrededor, con que se viene a hacer copada; crece comúnmente dos estados; está muy poblada de hojas, la cual es como la del arrayán, algún tanto mayor y más delgada y tierna. No produce otro fruto esta planta más que flores del mismo nombre, las cuales son purpúreas de color encendido y otras amarillas; las mejores y de más agradable parecer son las primeras. Es el pezón o cuellecito hueco, del grosor del pezón de la clavellina, un poco más largo, el cual y las hojas de la flor son de una misma sustancia, muy sutil y delgada y de un mismo color. Junto al remate o boca de la flor se abre el pezón en cinco o seis hojitas en rueda, que hacen tan grande copa como la de la clavellina. Nacen de en medio cinco o seis vastaguillos muy delgados, dentro de la flor, con unos pequeños hilacillos en el remate. No tiene olor alguno esta flor, más que buen parecer.

La mata es muy vistosa cuando se cubre de flores, y los ramilletes que de ella se hacen parecen de clavellinas. Tiene esta flor entre las demás del Perú el lugar que la clavellina entre las flores de Europa. En la lengua quichua se llama cantut, y en la aimará, cantilpo. También la suelen llamar los indios flor del Inca, porque la estimaban mucho los reyes Incas.

CAPITULO XXXVIII

De la muña

La muña es una planta que parece medio entre poleo y orégano, por lo cual la llaman los españoles en este reino del Perú, poleo silvestre, dado que es de distinta especie. Crece de dos a cuatro codos en alto; echa muchas ramas derechas, delgadas, cuadradas, como las de la yerba buena, y algo rojas; las cuales están muy pobladas de hojas, que son menores que las del orégano y de un verde más claro. Entre el nacimiento de las hojas y el tallo, en las coyunturas o nudos dél, produce muchas flores blancas muy pequeñas, semejantes en la forma a la flor de la col, sino que son mucho menores. Es esta mata la de más aguda y penetrativa fragancia de cuantas he topado en estas Indias, la cual se asemeja mucho a la del poleo, y por ser tan aguda, suele causar fastidio. Nace tanta copia de muña en las provincias del Collao, que los días festivos y solemnes la esparcen los indios en sus iglesias en lugar de juncia, con que se ponen muy olorosas.

Es la muña caliente y seca en el tercero grado, y con su agudeza mordica y enciende notablemente la lengua. Majadas sus hojas y aplicadas con un poco de sal, resuelven los tumores; y si a esto se añaden claras de huevos, curan las fracturas o quebraduras de huesos. Su cocimiento con salmuera hincha [i.e., alivia] los pies gotosos; majadas las hojas con vino, sal y arrope, y aplicadas en forma de emplasto, quitan el dolor de ijada por agudo que sea; y su cocimiento, bebido de ordinario con miel de abejas, limpia la flema del pecho y llagas del pulmón y las materias y llagas de los riñones y vejiga, y vale contra la detención de orina. Llámase esta mata, en la lengua quichua, muña, y en la aimará, tiene dos nombres, que son: coa y huaycha.
results.translation:
CHAPTER XXXVII

On the Cantuta

The cantuta is a shrub that puts forth many branches all around, giving it a bushy, rounded crown. It commonly grows to about two fathoms [estados] in height and is thickly set with leaves resembling those of myrtle [arrayán], though somewhat larger, more slender, and more tender. This plant produces no other fruit than the flowers that share its name; some of these are purple of a vivid, glowing hue, and others are yellow—the purple ones being the finer and more pleasing in appearance. The stem or little neck [cuellecito] is hollow, about the thickness of a carnation's [clavellina's] stem and somewhat longer; both it and the petals of the flower are of the same substance, very fine, thin, and uniform in color. Near the tip or mouth of the flower, the stem opens into five or six small leaflets arranged in a wheel, forming a cup as large as that of a carnation. From the center spring five or six very slender little stalks, within the flower, each tipped with small filaments [hilacillos]. This flower has no fragrance whatsoever, its merit being solely in its beauty.

The shrub is a very handsome sight when covered in bloom, and the posies made from it resemble those of carnations. Among all the flowers of Peru, this one holds the place that the carnation holds among the flowers of Europe. In the Quechua tongue it is called cantut, and in Aymara, cantilpo. The Indians also commonly call it the Flower of the Inca [flor del Inca], because the Inca kings held it in great esteem.

CHAPTER XXXVIII

On the Muña

The muña is a plant that seems midway between pennyroyal [poleo] and oregano, on account of which the Spaniards in this kingdom of Peru call it wild pennyroyal [poleo silvestre], although it is a distinct species. It grows from two to four cubits [codos] in height; it puts forth many straight, slender, square-stemmed branches, similar to those of spearmint [yerba buena] and somewhat reddish; these are thickly covered with leaves that are smaller than those of oregano and of a lighter green. In the axils of the leaves and along the joints or nodes of the stalk, it produces many very small white flowers, similar in form to those of the cabbage, though much smaller. This shrub has the sharpest and most penetrating fragrance of any I have encountered in these Indies; it closely resembles that of pennyroyal and, being so pungent, is apt to cause discomfort. So abundantly does muña grow in the provinces of the Collao that on feast days and solemn occasions the Indians scatter it on the floors of their churches in place of rushes [juncia], whereby the churches become very fragrant.

The muña is hot and dry in the third degree, and by virtue of its sharpness it notably bites and inflames the tongue. The leaves, pounded and applied with a little salt, resolve tumors; and if egg whites are added to this, the preparation cures fractures or breaks of bones. A decoction of it with brine relieves gouty feet; the leaves pounded with wine, salt, and grape syrup [arrope] and applied as a plaster remove flank pain [dolor de ijada], however acute; and its decoction, drunk regularly with honey, cleanses phlegm from the chest, heals sores of the lung, and clears matter and sores from the kidneys and bladder, and it is effective against retention of urine. This shrub is called muña in the Quechua tongue, and in Aymara it has two names: coa and huaycha.

entities_mentioned.name: cantuta / cantut (Quechua) / cantilpo (Aymara) / flor del Inca
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Cantua buxifolia Juss. ex Lam. (family Polemoniaceae)
entities_mentioned.notes: National flower of Peru and Bolivia. Cobo's description of the hollow tubular corolla opening into five or six lobes, the color variants (purple and yellow), and the Quechua name cantut all correspond precisely to Cantua buxifolia. No medicinal uses are attributed to the cantuta in this passage; it is treated purely as an ornamental. Cobo's comparison to the carnation (clavellina) is a recurring analogy used to convey the flower's prestige to European readers.

entities_mentioned.name: muña / coa / huaycha (Aymara)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Minthostachys mollis (Kunth) Griseb. or closely related Minthostachys spp. (family Lamiaceae); possibly also Satureja boliviana
entities_mentioned.notes: Andean aromatic herb of the mint family, still widely used in traditional Andean medicine under the name muña. Cobo's description of square stems, small white flowers resembling those of cabbage (i.e., cruciform-like in miniature), and an intensely pungent pennyroyal-like scent matches Minthostachys mollis well. Multiple medicinal applications are given (see below). The humoral assessment as 'hot and dry in the third degree' follows Galenic pharmaceutical convention.
entities_mentioned.ailment_treated: tumors (external); bone fractures; gout (feet); flank/side pain (ijada, likely renal colic or pleurisy); chest phlegm; pulmonary sores; kidney and bladder sores/matter; urinary retention
entities_mentioned.preparation_method: 1. Leaves pounded with salt → poultice for tumors. 2. Leaves pounded with salt and egg whites → poultice for bone fractures. 3. Decoction with brine → topical for gouty feet. 4. Leaves pounded with wine, salt, and grape syrup (arrope) → plaster for flank pain. 5. Decoction drunk regularly with honey → internal remedy for chest phlegm, lung sores, kidney/bladder complaints, and urinary retention.

entities_mentioned.name: arrayán
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Myrtus communis L. (common myrtle) or Andean myrtle relatives (Luma, Myrcianthes spp.)
entities_mentioned.notes: Used only as a comparative leaf-shape reference for the cantuta.

entities_mentioned.name: clavellina
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Dianthus caryophyllus L. (carnation / clove pink)
entities_mentioned.notes: Used only as a comparative morphological and aesthetic reference for the cantuta flower.

entities_mentioned.name: poleo (silvestre)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Mentha pulegium L. (pennyroyal); the 'wild' form here refers to muña itself
entities_mentioned.notes: Cited for olfactory comparison to muña.

results.translator_notes: 1. Several passages in the original are damaged by OCR: 'echa » / chas ramas' = 'echa muchas ramas'; 'crecé comúnmente fe / estados' = 'crece comúnmente dos estados'; 'algún tsñU / mayor' = 'algún tanto mayor'; 'de más a^s- / dable' = 'de más agradable'; 'Es ^ / pezón' = 'Es el pezón'; 'un poco » / largo' = 'un poco más largo'; 'de en medio (lA / seis' = 'de en medio cinco o seis'; 'pequeños / cilios' = 'pequeños hilacillos'; 'buen pareo??' = 'buen parecer'; 'fa mata' = 'la mata'; 'la llaman los in¬ / dios' reconstructed from context; 'cantilpo' reconstructed from 'cantil- / m' (likely 'cantilpo' or 'cantilpu' in Aymara sources—flagged as uncertain); 'Majada??' = 'Majadas' (pounded, fem. plural past participle). 2. The word 'hincha' in the phrase 'hincha los pies gotosos' is problematic: literally 'swells,' which is the opposite of the expected therapeutic effect. This may be a scribal/print error for 'alivia' (relieves) or 'deshincha' (reduces swelling); I have translated with a bracketed clarification. 3. 'Dolor de ijada' refers to severe side/flank pain and most likely denotes renal colic or pleuritic pain in this context. 4. 'Arrope' is a thick grape-must syrup, a common excipient in Iberian and colonial pharmacy. 5. The humoral grading 'caliente y seca en el tercero grado' follows the standard Galenic four-degree scale.

results.title_inferred: The Incavisa Plant
results.original_text:
CAPITULO XXXIX
De la incavisa

La incavisa es una mata que se levanta de la tierra tres o cuatro codos; echa muchas varas derechas tan gruesas como un dedo, correosas y algún tanto rojas; sus hojas son semejantes a las del sauce, salvo que son algo más cortas, angostas, tiernas, más gruesas y aserradas. Produce esta planta muchas flores juntas, las cuales son muy parecidas en el tamaño y figura a la flor de la manzanilla; aunque tiene las hojas que ciñen el botoncillo amarillas, un poco mayorcitas y no tan juntas. Tiene esta flor olor grato y agradable parecer, a la cual, en secándose, le sucede un velo delgado o flocadura, que se la lleva el viento. Llámase esta planta y su flor, en la lengua aimará, incavisa.
results.translation:
CHAPTER XXXIX
On the Incavisa

The incavisa is a shrub that rises three or four cubits [roughly 4.5–6 feet] from the ground; it puts out many straight stems as thick as a finger, tough and somewhat reddish; its leaves resemble those of the willow, except that they are somewhat shorter, narrow, tender, thicker, and serrated. This plant produces many flowers together, which are very similar in size and shape to the chamomile flower; although the petals [literally: leaves] that encircle the little button are yellow, slightly larger, and not as closely set. This flower has a pleasant fragrance and an agreeable appearance; once it dries, it is succeeded by a thin veil or downy tuft [flocadura] that is carried away by the wind. This plant and its flower are called, in the Aymara language, incavisa.

entities_mentioned.name: incavisa
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Possibly a species of Baccharis or Mutisia (Asteraceae), given the composite/daisy-like flowers with a pappus and willow-like leaves; Baccharis genistelloides or a related Andean Baccharis spp. are plausible candidates
entities_mentioned.notes: The description of willow-like serrated leaves, chamomile-resembling yellow-centered composite flowers, and a wind-dispersed pappus ('flocadura') strongly suggests a member of the Asteraceae family native to the Andean altiplano. The Aymara name 'incavisa' has not been definitively matched to a modern taxon in standard references. No medicinal use is described in this passage.

entities_mentioned.name: manzanilla (chamomile)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Matricaria chamomilla or Anthemis nobilis
entities_mentioned.notes: Used here only as a morphological comparator for the incavisa flower, not as a subject in its own right.

results.translator_notes: The OCR rendered 'hotoncillo' (clearly 'botoncillo,' diminutive of 'botón,' meaning 'little button' or floral disc) which has been silently corrected. No medicinal preparations or ailments are described in this segment; the chapter is purely botanical and descriptive. The term 'flocadura' (a downy, fibrous fringe or tuft) refers to the pappus of a composite flower and has been retained in brackets as a period technical term. 'Codo' (cubit) is approximately 16–18 inches, giving the plant a height of roughly 4–6 feet.

results.title_inferred: The Hitija Plant
results.original_text:
CAPITULO XL
De la hitija

Esta es una mata de dos o tres codos de alto; echa muchas ramas delgadas y muy juntas, con que se hace copada; y en la cumbre dellas muchas hojas de un verde alegre, las cuales se parecen a las de la albahaca en el tamaño y hechura, sólo que son más puntiagudas y algo pegajosas. Produce esta planta en manojitos unas florecillas blancas a la manera de la madreselva, sino que son tan pequeñas, que no tienen más que el pezón o botoncillo, que es poco mayor que un piñón; del cual, por la parte alta, salen unas hojitas blancas, retorcidas, cortas y tan menudas como delgados hilos. No tiene esta flor ningún olor, más que agradable parecer. Llámase esta flor con este nombre en la lengua aimará.
results.translation:
CHAPTER XL
On the hitija

This is a shrub [mata] two or three cubits [codos] in height; it puts forth many slender branches growing very close together, which give it a bushy, rounded crown [copada]; and at the tops of these branches are many leaves of a bright, cheerful green, which resemble those of basil [albahaca] in size and form, except that they are more pointed and somewhat sticky. This plant produces, in small clusters, tiny white flowers after the fashion of honeysuckle [madreselva], save that they are so small they consist of nothing more than a little stalk or button [pezón o botoncillo] scarcely larger than a pine nut [piñón]; from the upper part of which emerge small white leaflets, twisted, short, and as fine as slender threads. This flower has no scent whatsoever, beyond a pleasing appearance. This flower takes its name in the Aymara language.

entities_mentioned.name: hitija
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Possibly a species within Baccharis or a related Asteraceae shrub native to the Andean altiplano, given the Aymara name and physical description; alternatively a Gnaphalium or Lucilia species
entities_mentioned.notes: The Aymara name 'hitija' (or variant spellings) has not been firmly matched to a modern binomial in available references. The description of sticky, basil-like leaves, clustered tiny white flowers with thread-like petals, and no fragrance is consistent with several Andean Asteraceae genera. No medicinal use or preparation is described in this passage.

entities_mentioned.name: albahaca
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Ocimum basilicum (common basil)
entities_mentioned.notes: Used here purely as a morphological comparator for leaf size and shape.

entities_mentioned.name: madreselva
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Lonicera spp. (honeysuckle)
entities_mentioned.notes: Used here as a morphological comparator for flower form.

results.translator_notes: Several OCR errors silently corrected: 'hitija' for 'hitija' (consistent throughout), 'co¬dos' (hyphenated line-break) resolved to 'codos', 'hotoncillo' corrected to 'botoncillo' (clearly intended diminutive of 'botón'), and 'pifión' corrected to 'piñón'. No medicinal application, ailment, or preparation method is described in this segment; it is purely a morphological description. The passage closes by noting that both the plant and its flower share the name 'hitija' in the Aymara language, suggesting the Aymara name properly refers to the flower.

results.title_inferred: The Sopo (Tola) shrub: description, habitat, and medicinal uses
results.original_text:
CAPITULO XLI
Del sopo

Esta es una mata que crece tres o cuatro codos de alto; sus ramas son de color pardisco; no produce hojas, sino que en su cumbre echa muchos ramillos delgados que unos nacen de otros, verdes y no más gruesos que hilo de acarreto; no son lisos, sino ásperos o escamosos, a causa de unas puntillas que nacen por todo el tallo unas sobre otras, como escamas, que son más verdes que lo que se descubre del tallo, que es muy poco y más blanquecino. Nace esta mata en los páramos muy fríos, de la cual, comiendo las vicuñas, dicen los indios que crían las piedras bezares; no echa flor ni fruto alguno; es muy buena leña, y se quema de ordinario en las provincias del Collao, y en la villa del Potosí cuecen con ella el pan. Es caliente y algo estíptica, muy pegajosa, y tiene virtud de soldar y apretar; y así, su baño hecho con orines y sal o con salmuera, resuelve los tumores de las piernas de los gotosos; sus hojas o cogollos verdes majados y aplicados sobre las heridas sangrientas, las juntan y desecan; su polvo con sal y claras de huevos junta los huesos quebrados. Aunque en la lengua aimará se llama esta planta sopo, en algunas provincias donde se habla la misma lengua la suelen llamar tola.
results.translation:
CHAPTER XLI
On the Sopo

This is a shrub that grows three or four cubits [codos] tall; its branches are brownish in color; it does not produce leaves, but at its crown it puts forth many thin small branchlets that spring one from another, green and no thicker than pack-thread [hilo de acarreto]; they are not smooth but rough or scaly, owing to small points that arise all along the stem one above another like scales, which are greener than the exposed portion of the stem, which is very little and rather whitish. This shrub grows in the very cold high moorlands [páramos], and the Indians say that vicuñas grazing upon it thereby produce bezoar stones [piedras bezares]; it bears neither flower nor any fruit; it makes excellent firewood and is commonly burned in the provinces of the Collao, and in the town of Potosí bread is baked with it. It is warm [caliente] and somewhat astringent [estíptica] in quality, very sticky, and has the virtue of binding and tightening; accordingly, a bath made from it with urine and salt, or with brine, resolves the swellings of the legs in gout sufferers; its green leaves or young shoots, pounded and applied to bleeding wounds, close and dry them; its powder mixed with salt and egg whites knits broken bones. Although in the Aymara language this plant is called sopo, in some provinces where the same language is spoken it is commonly called tola.

entities_mentioned.name: sopo / tola
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Parastrephia lepidophylla or a closely related Parastrephia / Baccharis species (Asteraceae); 'tola' is the widespread Andean vernacular name applied to several resinous shrubs of the high puna
entities_mentioned.notes: The description—leafless, scale-like branchlets, resinous/sticky, grows on cold Andean páramos, used as fuel in the Collao and Potosí—fits well with Parastrephia lepidophylla (ch'illca tola) or possibly Baccharis incarum. The association with vicuña bezoar production is a recurring ethnomedical belief in Andean sources.

entities_mentioned.name: piedras bezares (bezoar stones)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Bezoar calculi found in the stomachs of camelids (vicuña, llama)
entities_mentioned.notes: Highly valued in 17th-century European and colonial pharmacy as a universal antidote; Cobo here records the indigenous belief that consumption of sopo/tola causes their formation in vicuñas.

entities_mentioned.name: vicuña
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Vicugna vicugna

conditions_treated: gout (tumores/swellings of the legs in gout sufferers)

preparations.ingredients: Sopo plant boiled with urine and salt, or with brine
preparations.application: External bath for gouty leg swellings

preparations.ingredients: Fresh green leaves or young shoots (cogollos), pounded raw
preparations.application: Applied directly to bleeding wounds to close and dry them

preparations.ingredients: Powdered plant mixed with salt and egg whites

medical_extraction.humoral_qualities: Warm [caliente], somewhat astringent [estíptica], very sticky/resinous; virtue of binding and consolidating

results.translator_notes: 1. 'eh.' in the source before 'su cumbre' is an OCR artifact for 'en'; corrected silently. 2. 'hilo de acarreto' (pack-thread or coarse carrying-cord) rendered as 'pack-thread' to preserve the period technical sense. 3. 'Collao' (also Qullasuyyu / Collaosuyu) refers to the altiplano region around Lake Titicaca. 4. The medical extraction fields are provided outside the strict schema as supplementary structured data; they can be integrated into 'translator_notes' or a custom schema extension as needed. 5. 'estíptica' is retained in brackets in the translation as it carries both astringent and styptic senses relevant to the therapeutic claims made.

results.title_inferred: Cañahuasopo — a rosemary-like shrub of the Collao region
results.original_text:
CAPITULO XLII

Del cañahuasopo

El cañahuasopo es una mata tan parecida al romero en sus ramas y hojas, que fácilmente se engañara cualquiera teniéndolo por tal; sólo se diferencia en que sus hojas son un poco más cortas que las del romero y de un verde más blanquecino. Produce una flor pequeña y amarilla como la de la ruda; levántase de la tierra esta planta dos o tres codos; mascadas sus hojas son tan amargas y picantes como las del romero; sus ramas, junto al pie, tienen un color negro, que parecen chamuscadas. Nace esta planta en las tierras del Collao en grande abundancia, donde más comúnmente sirve de leña, y llámase así en la lengua aimará.
results.translation:
CHAPTER XLII

On the Cañahuasopo

The cañahuasopo is a shrub so closely resembling rosemary in its branches and leaves that anyone might easily mistake it for such; it differs only in that its leaves are somewhat shorter than those of rosemary and of a more whitish green. It produces a small yellow flower like that of rue [ruda]; the plant rises two or three cubits [codos] from the ground; when its leaves are chewed they are as bitter and pungent as those of rosemary; its branches, near the base, are of a black colour, so that they appear scorched. This plant grows in great abundance in the lands of the Collao [the Altiplano region around Lake Titicaca], where it is most commonly used as firewood, and it is called by this name in the Aymara language.

entities_mentioned.name: cañahuasopo
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Posibly Parastrephia lepidophylla or a related Asteraceae shrub (e.g., Baccharis or Parastrephia spp.) native to the Andean Altiplano; the rosemary-like habit, whitish-green foliage, small yellow flowers, and use as firewood in the Collao are consistent with these genera
entities_mentioned.notes: The Aymara name is not widely attested in modern botanical literature under this exact spelling; 'tola' shrubs (Parastrephia, Fabiana, Baccharis) are the dominant fuel-source shrubs of the Collao region and share the described characteristics. Confident identification would require additional sources.

entities_mentioned.name: romero
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Salvia rosmarinus (formerly Rosmarinus officinalis) — rosemary
entities_mentioned.notes: Used as a comparative reference plant, not described as a native Andean species here.

entities_mentioned.name: ruda
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Ruta graveolens — common rue
entities_mentioned.notes: Referenced only for flower-colour comparison.

results.translator_notes: The source text contains several OCR artefacts: 'cañahu^sopo' (chapter heading), 'bojas' for 'hojas', 'BUS' for 'sus', 'mot' for 'más', and a line break mid-sentence obscuring 'sirve de leña' — all silently corrected as unambiguous. No medical or pharmaceutical use is described; the plant's primary noted utility is as fuel. The term 'codo' (cubit, approximately 42–48 cm) is preserved in brackets. 'Collao' refers to the broad altiplano territory around Lake Titicaca, inhabited by Aymara speakers.

results.title_inferred: On the Tola Plant (Chapter XLIII)
results.original_text:
Capítulo XLIII

De la tola

Los que dan al sopo nombre de tok, llaman a esta planta, tola menor, a diferencia de la otra; y ésta es una mata que nace en regiones frías, de uno a cuatro codos en alto, muy poblada de ramas y hojas, que la hacen tan copada como una hermosa mata de albahaca. Son las hojas de un verde oscuro, del tamaño de las del arrayán, las cuales, comenzando muy angostas en el pecíolo, se van ensanchando hasta el cabo y se rematan en tres punticas, la de en medio un poco más larga que las de los lados. Echa en la cumbre de sus tallos muchos botoncillos no mayores que guisantes pequeños, de que salen unas florecitas blanquecinas de unas hojillas tan cortas y menudas, que no parecen sino delgados hilos que asoman por el remate del botón. Dan de sí estas flores un olor muy grato, semejante al de la madreselva. Es la tola planta caliente con algo de humedad, muy amarga y mucilaginosa; porque, echándola en el fuego, en calentándose sus hojas, se pegan como si estuvieran untadas con miel. Tienen virtud de soldar los huesos quebrados, y majadas sus hojas en unto sin sal, hacen dos efectos en los apostemas, porque, o maduran, si hallan disposición de calor, o resuelven, si naturaleza se inclina a ello. Llámase esta mata, en la lengua aimará, tola.
results.translation:
Chapter XLIII

On the Tola

Those who give the name 'tok' to the sopo call this plant 'lesser tola,' to distinguish it from the other; and this is a shrub that grows in cold regions, from one to four cubits [codos] in height, very densely covered in branches and leaves, which make it as full and bushy as a fine clump of basil [albahaca]. Its leaves are a dark green, the size of those of the myrtle [arrayán], which, beginning very narrow at the petiole [pecíolo], gradually widen toward the tip and terminate in three small points, the middle one being slightly longer than those on either side. At the tops of its stalks it produces many small buds no larger than small peas, from which emerge tiny whitish flowers with such short and fine little petals that they appear as nothing more than slender threads protruding from the tip of the bud. These flowers give off a very pleasant fragrance, resembling that of honeysuckle [madreselva]. The tola is a plant warm [caliente] in quality with some moisture [humedad], very bitter and mucilaginous; for when thrown into fire, as its leaves heat up, they become sticky as if smeared with honey. They have the virtue of knitting [soldar] broken bones, and when the leaves are pounded in unsalted lard [unto sin sal], they produce two effects on abscesses [apostemas]: they either bring them to a head [maduran], if there is a disposition toward heat, or they resolve [resuelven] them, if nature inclines that way. This shrub is called, in the Aymara tongue, tola.

entities_mentioned.name: tola (tola menor)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Parastrephia lepidophylla or a closely related species of Parastrephia / Baccharis (Asteraceae); 'tola' is a general Andean term applied to several resinous high-altitude shrubs
entities_mentioned.notes: Cobo distinguishes this 'lesser tola' from another plant he calls 'sopo' or 'tok.' The description of sticky, resinous leaves that adhere when heated, the high-altitude cold habitat, and the Aymara name 'tola' are consistent with Parastrephia spp. or Baccharis spp. of the Andean puna. Multiple species share this common name.

The medical/humoral terms 'caliente' (warm), 'humedad' (moist), 'maduran' (to ripen/suppurate), and 'resuelven' (to resolve/disperse) are Galenic in framework and have been preserved with their Spanish originals in brackets. 'Unto sin sal' (unsalted lard) is the standard vehicle for topical preparations in this corpus. The therapeutic claim of bone-knitting (soldar huesos quebrados) is noteworthy and likely reflects indigenous Aymara medical knowledge. The identity of 'sopo/tok' mentioned at the opening as a related or contrasted plant is unclear from this segment alone and would require cross-reference with other chapters.

results.title_inferred: Chapter XLIV: On the Chilca (two species)
results.original_text:
CAPITULO XLIV

De la chilca

Esta es una mata muy conocida de los indios y españoles por sus buenos efectos; nace en gran cantidad en los Llanos del Perú, en las orillas de ríos, y sirve de leña. Crece un estado poco más o menos; echa muchas ramas delgadas y derechas; sus hojas son larguillas, tiernas y delgadas, muy parecidas a las del sauce, y son un poco resinosas. Es la chilca de temperamento caliente y húmedo; usan della los indios aplicándola caliente contra todo dolor de frío, y para este efecto la tuestan con canela y rocían con vino o aguardiente. Quita y resuelve toda ventosidad y es contra la ijada, aplicada por la misma orden sobre el dolor; puesta en la cabeza, quita el dolor della, y los mismos efectos hace su cocimiento; con el cual, dando baños a los cuartanarios, los deja libres, y a los que no pueden dormir, causa sueño; y si al cocimiento le añaden hojas de mollé y bastante sal, deseca y enjuga las piernas de los gotosos. Las hojas, majadas y aplicadas en las heridas frescas, las secan y juntan. Llámase esta mata, en la lengua general del Perú, chilca.

Otra especie se halla de chilca que crece de uno a dos estados, y si se arrima a otros árboles, trepa por ellos hasta su cumbre. Echa muchísimos vástagos y ramas, con que se extiende y espesa, de modo que cierra y condensa las cercas de las huertas cuando nace en ellas. Las ramas son unas varillas lisas como un dedo, muy largas, verdes y fofas. En los pimpollos echa unos racimos de florecillas blancas, olorosas como la madreselva. No es mayor cada florecilla que un garbanzo, compuesta de unos hilillos delgados. La hoja es del tamaño de la del granado, un poquito más ancha, y las más crecidas serán como de limón ceutí; es de color verde claro, gruesa, tierna y con unas pestañas en torno. Las hojas de los cogollos son tan pegajosas como las del abrojo, que parece están untadas con miel. Hay en esta planta macho y hembra; el macho lleva flor, y la hembra, fruto; y ésta tiene las hojas más verdes, la cual llaman los indios, llaca chilca.
results.translation:
CHAPTER XLIV

On the Chilca

This is a shrub very well known to the Indians and Spaniards alike for its beneficial effects. It grows in great abundance in the coastal plains [Llanos] of Peru, on the banks of rivers, and serves as firewood. It grows to about one estado [roughly six feet] in height, more or less; it puts forth many thin, straight branches; its leaves are longish, tender, and slender, very similar to those of the willow, and are somewhat resinous. The chilca is of a hot and moist temperament [in the humoral sense]; the Indians use it, applying it warm against all pain caused by cold, and for this purpose they toast it with cinnamon and sprinkle it with wine or brandy [aguardiente]. It removes and dispels all flatulence [ventosidad] and is [used] against flank pain [ijada], applied in the same manner over the site of pain; placed on the head, it relieves headache, and its decoction produces the same effects; with this decoction, giving baths to those suffering from quartan fever [cuartanarios], it frees them of it, and in those who cannot sleep it induces sleep; and if leaves of molle and a good quantity of salt are added to the decoction, it dries and purges the legs of those suffering from gout. The leaves, bruised and applied to fresh wounds, dry and close them. This shrub is called, in the general language [Quechua] of Peru, chilca.

Another species of chilca is found that grows to between one and two estados in height, and if it leans against other trees, it climbs them to their very tops. It puts forth very many shoots and branches, spreading and thickening in such a way that it closes off and fills the fences of kitchen gardens when it grows in them. The branches are smooth rods about as thick as a finger, very long, green, and pithy. On the young shoots it produces clusters of small white flowers, fragrant like honeysuckle. Each little flower is no larger than a chickpea, made up of thin filaments. The leaf is the size of that of the pomegranate, a little wider, and the largest will be about the size of a Ceuta lemon leaf; it is light green in colour, thick, tender, and fringed with small cilia [pestañas] all around. The leaves of the young shoots are as sticky as those of the thistle [abrojo], seeming as though they were smeared with honey. This plant has a male and a female: the male bears flowers and the female bears fruit; the female has greener leaves, and the Indians call it llaca chilca.

entities_mentioned.name: chilca (first species)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Baccharis salicifolia (Ruiz & Pav.) Pers., or possibly Baccharis latifolia — willow-leaved baccharis; family Asteraceae
entities_mentioned.notes: Description of willow-like, resinous leaves, riverside habitat, and hot/moist humoral classification fits B. salicifolia well. Multiple Baccharis spp. share the vernacular name 'chilca' across the Andes.
conditions_treated: gout (combined with molle leaves and salt, as a bath)
preparation_methods: Toasted with cinnamon and sprinkled with wine or aguardiente, applied warm to site of pain
Decoction used as a bath for quartan fever, insomnia, and gout
Leaves bruised and applied directly to fresh wounds

entities_mentioned.name: chilca (second species) / llaca chilca
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Possibly Baccharis floribunda Kunth or another climbing/sprawling Baccharis sp.; alternatively Eupatorium sp. — family Asteraceae
entities_mentioned.notes: Climbing or scrambling habit, honeysuckle-scented white flowers, sticky young leaves, and dioecious character (male/female plants) noted. The name 'llaca chilca' (llaca = thin/lean in Quechua) may indicate the female form. No medicinal use is described for this species in the text.

entities_mentioned.name: mollé / molle
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Schinus molle L. (Peruvian pepper tree); family Anacardiaceae
entities_mentioned.notes: Mentioned as an additive to the chilca decoction bath for treating gout.
preparation_methods: Leaves added to chilca decoction with salt, used as a bath

results.translator_notes:
1. The chapter heading 'De la cínica' in the OCR source is a clear misread of 'De la chilca'; corrected silently per rule 2.
2. 'pastillas en torno' (OCR) is almost certainly 'pestañas en torno' (fringe of cilia/eyelash-like margins); corrected silently as the botanical description demands it.
3. 'abrojo' (OCR reads 'azúz') is the best candidate for the sticky-leaved comparison plant; abrojo (thistle/burr) fits the context of stickiness.
4. 'un estado' is a traditional Spanish linear measure approximately equal to the height of a man (c. 1.67–1.8 m / ~6 ft).
5. 'Cuartanarios' refers to sufferers of quartan (four-day cycle) malaria or ague.
6. 'Ijada' denotes pain in the flank or side, corresponding to conditions such as colic or nephralgia in period usage.
7. 'Lengua general del Perú' refers to Quechua (Runasimi), the lingua franca of the Inca empire and early colonial Peru.
8. The sex distinction (macho/hembra) with the hembra called 'llaca chilca' likely reflects folk taxonomic observation of a dioecious or functionally dioecious species.

results.title_inferred: On the Hisachalahua plant
results.original_text:
CAPITULO XLV

De la hisachalahua

La hisachalahua es una mata que se levanta un codo en alto; echa muchas ramas delgadas y ñudosas; su hoja es pequeña como la del poleo, algún tanto más larga y puntiaguda, tan gruesa como la de la verdolaga, aunque no tan tierna y lisa. Así las hojas como los cogollos desta planta están cubiertos de un vello muy cortico, delgado y blanco, con que sus ramas tienen un color blanquecino, como polvoreadas con ceniza. En su cumbre produce unos botoncillos prolongados poco mayores que piñones, puntiagudos y del mismo color que las hojas, por cuyas puntas salen unas florecillas algo rojas, que no parecen sino manojitos cortos de hilos, que, abriéndose, paran en un vello o flocadura delgada, que salta con el viento. Huelen un poco las ramas y hojas desta planta; la cual, en la lengua aimará, se dice hisachalahua.
results.translation:
CHAPTER XLV

On the Hisachalahua

The hisachalahua is a shrub that rises about one cubit [approximately 18 inches] in height; it puts forth many slender and knotty branches; its leaf is small like that of pennyroyal [poleo], somewhat longer and more pointed, as thick as that of purslane [verdolaga], though not as tender and smooth. Both the leaves and the young shoots [cogollos] of this plant are covered with a very short, fine, and white down, which gives its branches a whitish color, as though dusted with ash. At its summit it produces small elongated buds slightly larger than pine nuts [piñones], pointed and of the same color as the leaves, from whose tips emerge small flowers somewhat reddish in color, which look like nothing so much as short little bundles of threads, which, upon opening, resolve into a fine down or flocking [flocadura] that is carried off by the wind. The branches and leaves of this plant have a faint scent; it is called hisachalahua in the Aymara language.

entities_mentioned.name: hisachalahua
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Possibly a species within Baccharis (Asteraceae), given the whitish downy coating, thread-like florets that disperse as pappus, small knotty branches, and Andean Aymara provenance; perhaps Baccharis genistelloides or a related Andean Baccharis sp.
entities_mentioned.notes: The description of whitish downy branches, small pointed leaves resembling pennyroyal, elongated buds, reddish thread-like flowers resolving into wind-blown pappus, and faint scent is consistent with several Baccharis species native to the Andean altiplano. No medicinal use is described in this passage. The Aymara name 'hisachalahua' has not been definitively matched to a modern taxon in standard references.

entities_mentioned.name: poleo
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Mentha pulegium (pennyroyal) or a local Andean analogue
entities_mentioned.notes: Used here as a comparative reference for leaf size, not as the subject plant.

entities_mentioned.name: verdolaga
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Portulaca oleracea (common purslane)
entities_mentioned.notes: Used here as a comparative reference for leaf thickness, not as the subject plant.

results.translator_notes: Several OCR errors were silently corrected: 'ba hisachalahua' → 'La hisachalahua' (sentence-initial capital lost); 'jíiñones' → 'piñones'; 'floca¬dura' reconstructed as 'flocadura' (a period term for flocking or fiber-like down). No medicinal preparation or therapeutic use is described in this chapter; the passage is purely botanical and descriptive. The plant name is given explicitly as Aymara. The 'codo' (cubit) is a period unit of roughly 18 inches (45 cm).

results.title_inferred: The Paucarcancha Plant
results.original_text:
CAPITULO XLVI
De la paucarcancha

Esta es una mata de hasta un estado de alto; produce muchas ramas leñosas de color pardo, cuyos cogollos están muy poblados de hojas, la cual es pequeña, menor que la del arrayán, muy tiesa, lisa, acanalada y en la punta una espinilla delgada y dura con que se ponen estas hojas tan espinosas como las de la coscoja. En la cumbre y remate de cada tallo nace una flor colorada tan larga como la mitad de un dedo de la mano y poco más delgada que él; la cual se compone de muchas hojitas que salen juntas del pezón, tan angostas como el grueso de un grano de cebada, y como van llegando al medio y centro de la flor, van siendo más delgadas. Son duras, tiesas, ásperas y casi espinosas. Las flores que produce cada mata son muchas, y aunque no huelen nada, la hermosean mucho con su buen parecer. Con ellas se suelen enramar las calles y arcos en las fiestas de procesiones solemnes. Llámase esta mata, en la lengua general del Perú, paucarcancha.

OBRAS DEL PADRE BERNABE COBO
results.translation:
CHAPTER XLVI
On the Paucarcancha

This is a shrub [mata] of up to one estado [approximately 1.7 meters, or roughly the height of a man] tall; it produces many woody branches of a brownish color, whose shoot-tips [cogollos] are very densely furnished with leaves. The leaf is small—smaller than that of the myrtle [arrayán]—very stiff, smooth, channeled, and terminating at the tip in a thin, hard spine, which renders these leaves as prickly as those of the kermes oak [coscoja]. At the crown and apex of each stem there grows a red flower as long as half a finger of the hand and only slightly thinner than the finger itself; this flower is composed of many small leaflets [hojitas, i.e., petals or tepals] that emerge together from the stalk [pezón], as narrow as the thickness of a grain of barley, and as they approach the middle and center of the flower, they become progressively more slender. They are hard, stiff, rough, and almost spine-like. Each shrub produces many flowers, and although they have no fragrance whatsoever, they lend the plant great beauty by their handsome appearance. They are commonly used to garland the streets and arches during the festivities of solemn processions. This shrub is called, in the general language [lengua general, i.e., Quechua] of Peru, paucarcancha.

entities_mentioned.name: paucarcancha
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Possibly a species of Chuquiraga (e.g., Chuquiraga spinosa, Asteraceae), a spiny Andean shrub with prominent red flowers; alternatively a Puya or related genus, but Chuquiraga is the strongest candidate given the description of stiff spine-tipped leaves and red terminal flowers
entities_mentioned.notes: The Quechua name 'paucarcancha' (roughly 'enclosure of flowers') and the description—woody shrub with spine-tipped leaves resembling kermes oak, large solitary red terminal flowers with narrow, stiff, almost spiny petals, used decoratively in processions—aligns well with Chuquiraga spinosa or a closely related Chuquiraga sp. No medicinal use is described in this passage; the plant is noted solely for its ornamental and ceremonial use.

entities_mentioned.name: arrayán
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Myrtle (Myrtus communis or local Andean Myrtaceae species)
entities_mentioned.notes: Used as a size-reference comparator for the leaf, not described independently.

entities_mentioned.name: coscoja
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Kermes oak (Quercus coccifera, Fagaceae)
entities_mentioned.notes: Used as a tactile/morphological comparator for the spiny character of the leaves, not described independently.

results.translator_notes: No medicinal preparation or ailment is described in this segment; the chapter is purely botanical and ethnographic, noting the plant's ceremonial decorative use during religious processions. The OCR error 'dxira' (in 'espinilla delgada y dxira') has been silently corrected to 'dura' (hard/firm), which is unambiguous in context. 'Un estado' is a Spanish linear measure roughly equivalent to the height of a man (~1.67–1.80 m). 'Lengua general del Perú' is Cobo's standard term for Quechua.

results.title_inferred: Two Peruvian shrubs: the Ujuta and the Urcu-urcu
results.original_text:
CAPITULO XLVII

De la ujuta

Los indios del Perú llaman ujuta al calzado que usan, y dan el mismo nombre a una flor, por tener, como tiene, la figura del dicho calzado. La mata que la produce crece dos o tres codos y echa muchas ramas o varas delgadas, pardas, redondas, lisas y correosas. Su hoja se parece a la del almendro, salvo que es un poco menor y más tierna. Es la flor amarilla de muy fino color, de vistoso parecer, aunque sin olor; la cual no tiene más de una hoja en figura de una calabacita, poco más corta que un dedo, que por donde más gruesa es como la yema del dedo pulgar, y va adelgazando hacia el pezón, quedando hueca de dentro. Los botoncillos desta flor, antes de abrirse, parecen alcaparras. Cúbrese la mata destas flores con que se pone muy vistosa. Llámase ujuta en la lengua quichua, y en la aimará, staca-staca.

CAPITULO XLVIII

Del urcu-urcu

Esta es una mata que se levanta de la tierra dos o tres codos; echa muy pocas varas muy apartadas unas de otras; son no más gruesas que un delgado junco, redondo, lisas, moradas y sin nudos, porque no nacen unas de otras ni producen cogollos si no es en la cumbre; están muy pobladas de hojas, cuyas puntas se apartan muy poco del vástago. La hoja tan larga como la de la mata de la azucena y algo más, pero mucho más angosta, recia, lisa, correosa, por de dentro algo blanquecina, sin hacer lomo en medio, y a lo largo llena de unas venillas blancas. Produce en el remate de sus ramas, a manojos o racimos, una flor encarnada, que tiene la figura del pezón de la clavellina; compónese de muchas hojitas largas, tan juntas unas de otras por las puntas como por su nacimiento, con que la flor no es más gruesa ni de más ruedo en el remate que por junto al pezón; y siendo toda la hoja desta flor encarnada, tiene la punta verde. Salen de en medio della unos vastaguillos del mismo color, tan delgados como hilos, con unos botoncillos en los remates menores que pepitas de ají. No huele cosa esta flor, solamente recrea la vista. Llámase, en la lengua aimará, urcu-urcu.
results.translation:
CHAPTER XLVII

Of the Ujuta

The Indians of Peru call ujuta the footwear [sandal] they use, and they give the same name to a flower because it has, as indeed it does, the shape of said footwear. The shrub that produces it grows two or three cubits tall and puts out many branches or thin stems, brownish, round, smooth, and tough [correosas]. Its leaf resembles that of the almond tree, except that it is somewhat smaller and more tender. The flower is yellow of a very fine color, of handsome appearance, though without fragrance; it has no more than a single petal [hoja] in the shape of a small gourd, a little shorter than a finger, thickest where it is about as wide as the ball of the thumb, tapering toward the stalk [pezón], and hollow within. The little buds of this flower, before opening, resemble capers. The shrub becomes covered with these flowers, making it very showy. It is called ujuta in the Quechua tongue, and in Aymara, staca-staca.

CHAPTER XLVIII

Of the Urcu-urcu

This is a shrub that rises two or three cubits from the ground; it puts out very few stems, widely spaced from one another; they are no thicker than a slender reed, round, smooth, purple, and without nodes, for they do not branch from one another nor do they produce shoots except at the top; they are thickly set with leaves whose tips extend very little from the stem. The leaf is as long as, or a little longer than, the leaf of the lily plant [azucena], but much narrower, stiff, smooth, tough [correosa], somewhat whitish on the inner surface, without a midrib [lomo], and running lengthwise full of small white veins. At the tip of its branches it produces, in clusters or bunches, a scarlet flower having the shape of the calyx-tip [pezón] of the carnation [clavellina]; it is composed of many long narrow petals [hojitas], as closely joined at their tips as at their bases, so that the flower is no wider and no broader at the tip than near the stalk; and although the whole petal of this flower is scarlet, the tip is green. From the center of it emerge small filaments [vastaguillos] of the same color, as thin as threads, with tiny buds at their ends smaller than pepper seeds [pepitas de ají]. This flower has no fragrance whatsoever; it pleases only the eye. It is called, in the Aymara tongue, urcu-urcu.

entities_mentioned.name: ujuta (also: staca-staca in Aymara)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Calceolaria species (likely Calceolaria pinnata or related Calceolaria sp.)
entities_mentioned.notes: The Quechua name 'ujuta' means sandal, given because the flower resembles a sandal or small slipper — consistent with the slipper-shaped flowers of genus Calceolaria. The hollow, single-petaled, gourd-shaped yellow flower description strongly suggests this genus. No medicinal use is described in this passage.

entities_mentioned.name: urcu-urcu (Aymara name)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Puya species or possibly Fascicularia / Bromelia sp. (family Bromeliaceae), or alternatively a Galtonia / Scilla relative; description also consistent with Puya (Bromeliaceae)
entities_mentioned.notes: The description of a rosette shrub with long, stiff, leathery, whitish-veined leaves resembling those of a lily (azucena) and scarlet clustered flowers with protruding filaments is suggestive of a bromeliad, possibly Puya or Fascicularia. The purple, smooth, jointless stems are unusual. Identification remains uncertain. No medicinal use described.

results.translator_notes: The chapter heading in the OCR source reads 'Di? la iijuia' — this is an OCR corruption of 'De la ujuta,' corrected silently. 'Staca-staca' appears in the original as 'staca^staca'; the caret is interpreted as a hyphen or repetition marker and rendered as 'staca-staca.' The word 'urcu-urcu' appears as 'urcfi-urcn' and 'urcfi-urcn' in the OCR; the intended form 'urcu-urcu' is unambiguous from context and Aymara linguistic convention. The term 'hoja' used for the flower of ujuta literally means 'leaf' but here functions as 'petal' in a botanically idiosyncratic way typical of Cobo's descriptive style. No medicinal or pharmaceutical use is described for either plant in this segment.

results.title_inferred: The Pucatica Plant
results.original_text:
CAPITULO XLIX

De la pucatica

La pucatica es una mata que crece un estado; echa la hoja mediana con tres puntas y una flor encarnada de color muy vivo, vistosa y de buen olor; la cual es de figura de campanilla, cuya boca tiene tanto ruedo como un real de a ocho. Llámase así en la lengua de los indios peruanos.
results.translation:
CHAPTER XLIX

On the pucatica

The pucatica is a shrub that grows to the height of a man [un estado]; it puts forth medium-sized leaves with three points and a vivid crimson flower, showy and of pleasant fragrance; this flower is bell-shaped [campanilla], its mouth being as wide across as a piece-of-eight [real de a ocho]. It is so called in the language of the Peruvian Indians.

entities_mentioned.name: pucatica
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Possibly a species of Ipomoea or Mutisia (Asteraceae), or potentially a Passiflora species; the bell-shaped crimson flower and trilobed leaf suggest a morning glory or related Convolvulaceae, but identification remains uncertain
entities_mentioned.notes: The name 'pucatica' likely derives from Quechua 'puka' (red/crimson), consistent with the described flower color. No medicinal use or preparation method is described in this passage. The diameter comparison to a real de a ocho (Spanish silver eight-real coin, approximately 38–40 mm) gives a useful measure of flower size.

results.translator_notes: No ailment, preparation method, or medicinal application is described in this segment; it is purely a morphological description. The OCR artifact 'coi?' in the original has been silently corrected to 'con' (with), as the context makes this unambiguous. 'Un estado' is a period unit of height roughly equivalent to the stature of a man (~1.65–1.80 m). 'Lengua' (language) was corrected from OCR 'lengiu'.

results.title_inferred: The Sunchu plant: description, properties, and uses against snake venom
results.original_text:
CAPITULO L
Del sunchu

El sunchu es un linaje de flores amarillas, que abraza algunas diferencias dellas muy parecidas todas entre sí en su figura, que es del talle de la flor de la manzanilla, y en la forma de la planta, que es una mata de un estado de alto, aromática y resinosa, la cual echa unas varas delgadas, redondas y algo rojas. Sus hojas son semejantes a las de la yerba-mora, y tocadas con la lengua, son abstersas o estípticas, las cuales y las varas o ramas están cubiertas de un vello corto y áspero. Difieren entre sí los sunchos en crecer unos más que otros y en hacer la hoja algo desemejante, porque algunos la tienen mayor que la de la yerba-mora y con unas puntas, como la de las malvas. La flor no tiene olor alguno; es tan grande como un real de a ocho, con un orden de hojas pequeñas en torno, y en medio un botón llano o chato, compuesto de unas hojitas muy menudas, que también son amarillas como el resto de la flor; cada hoja de las de su redondez es del grandor y figura de una almendra, con dos raítas [sic] o canales pequeñas a lo largo, que la dividen en dos partes iguales.

Es el sunchu caliente en segundo grado. Descubiertas sus raíces y dando en ellas algunas sajaduras, dan de sí cierta resina o goma que los indios estiman en mucho, porque les sirve, traída en la boca o cocida en vino, para afijar y blanquear la dentadura. El polvo de la resina aglutina las heridas frescas, y el mismo efecto hacen también el zumo de las raíces y sus hojas majadas. Valen asimismo las raíces majadas contra las punturas de víboras y de otros animales ponzoñosos; y notan los indios dellas acerca de cuán provechosas sean contra todo veneno, que un animalejo, que, por ser parecido en el talle y ánimo de acometer al hurón, lo llaman así los españoles, cuando se encuentra con las víboras, las acomete con sobrado ánimo; y si en la pelea acaso la víbora le pica, se aparta della con gran presteza y busca el sunchu, que con su natural instinto conoce, y descubriendo con sus uñas la raíz, la chupa y come y se revuelca sobre ella; con que cobra tanto ánimo, por verse libre de la ponzoña, que vuelve a buscar la víbora, y todas las veces que se siente herido, hace el mismo remedio.

Entre muchas suertes que hay de sunchos es muy particular el que se da en el valle de Cochabamba, diócesis de los Charcas, llamado por su virtud yerba de la víbora, porque ha mostrado la experiencia ser tan poderosa y eficaz su virtud, que picando en cualquiera parte del cuerpo alguna de las víboras que matan en veinticuatro horas, bebido el zumo deste sunchu, se repara el daño en un momento. Nace esta planta en tierras frías y llámase sunchu en la lengua quichua, y pinahua en la aimara.
results.translation:
CHAPTER L
On the Sunchu

The sunchu is a genus of yellow-flowered plants, encompassing several varieties that are all very similar to one another in appearance: the shape of the flower resembles that of chamomile [manzanilla], and the plant itself is a shrub about one fathom [estado] in height, aromatic and resinous, which puts forth slender, round, and somewhat reddish stems. Its leaves are similar to those of nightshade [yerba-mora], and when touched to the tongue they prove abstersive [cleansing] or astringent [estípticas]. Both the leaves and the stems are covered with a short, rough down. The various sunchos differ from one another in height and in the shape of their leaves, for some have leaves larger than those of nightshade and bearing small points like those of the mallow. The flower has no scent whatsoever; it is as large as a piece-of-eight [real de a ocho], with a single row of small petals arranged around the edge and, in the center, a flat or flattened disc composed of very tiny florets, which are likewise yellow like the rest of the flower. Each of the outer petals is the size and shape of an almond, with two small grooves or channels running lengthwise that divide it into two equal parts.

The sunchu is hot [caliente] in the second degree [according to Galenic humoral classification]. When its roots are uncovered and several incisions are made in them, they exude a certain resin or gum that the Indians prize highly, for it serves—either held in the mouth or boiled in wine—to fix and whiten the teeth. The powdered resin closes [agglutinates] fresh wounds, and the juice of the roots and the crushed leaves produce the same effect. The crushed roots are likewise effective against the bites of vipers and other venomous animals. The Indians observe, regarding how beneficial the roots are against all poisons, that a small animal which the Spaniards call a hurón [ferret] on account of its similar build and aggressive nature, when it encounters vipers, attacks them with great boldness; and if in the fight a viper happens to bite it, the animal withdraws very quickly and seeks out the sunchu, which by natural instinct it recognizes; and uncovering the root with its claws, it sucks and eats it and rolls upon it, whereby it regains such vigor—finding itself freed from the venom—that it goes back to seek out the viper, and every time it feels itself bitten it performs the same remedy.

Among the many varieties of sunchu, a particularly noteworthy one grows in the valley of Cochabamba, in the diocese of Charcas, called by virtue of its properties yerba de la víbora [viper's herb], for experience has shown its power to be so strong and efficacious that when one of the vipers that kills within twenty-four hours bites any part of the body, drinking the juice of this sunchu repairs the harm in an instant. This plant grows in cold lands and is called sunchu in the Quechua language, and pinahua in Aymara.

entities_mentioned.name: sunchu / pinahua
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Possibly Viguiera (Helianthus-allied) or Flourensia spp. (Asteraceae); some sources tentatively identify Andean 'sunchu' with Viguiera australis or related composites; the 'yerba de la víbora' variety from Cochabamba may be a distinct taxon within the same genus
entities_mentioned.notes: The yellow composite flower resembling chamomile, resinous aromatic shrub about 1.5–2 m tall with reddish stems, and Andean distribution are consistent with several genera in Asteraceae (e.g., Viguiera, Flourensia, or possibly Baccharis). The Quechua name 'sunchu' and Aymara 'pinahua' are attested in colonial sources. Definitive identification requires comparison with herbarium specimens from Cochabamba.

entities_mentioned.name: yerba-mora
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Solanum nigrum (black nightshade)
entities_mentioned.notes: Standard colonial Spanish name for Solanum nigrum; used here as a leaf-shape reference.

entities_mentioned.name: manzanilla
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Matricaria chamomilla (chamomile) or related Anthemis spp.
entities_mentioned.notes: Used as a morphological comparator for the flower form.

entities_mentioned.name: malvas
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Malva spp. (mallow)
entities_mentioned.notes: Used as a leaf-shape comparator for lobed/pointed leaf margins.

entities_mentioned.name: hurón
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Possibly a mustelid or procyonid native to South America, perhaps Galictis cuja (lesser grison) or Mustela frenata (long-tailed weasel)
entities_mentioned.notes: Cobo uses the Spanish word for 'ferret' (hurón) by analogy; the animal described—known to seek out the sunchu root after a snakebite—is likely the lesser grison (Galictis cuja), which is native to Andean regions and noted in other colonial sources for snake-fighting behavior.

entities_mentioned.name: yerba de la víbora
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: A variety of sunchu from Cochabamba; specific taxon uncertain
entities_mentioned.notes: This is presented as a distinct and especially potent variety of sunchu, used specifically as an antidote for lethal viper bites. The name 'yerba de la víbora' may correspond to several Andean plants with reputed antivenomous properties.

results.translator_notes: 1. 'Estado' is a unit of height roughly equivalent to a fathom or the height of a man (~1.5–1.8 m); translated as 'fathom' with the original in brackets. 2. 'Caliente en segundo grado' is a standard Galenic humoral classification indicating the plant's temperament; preserved in translation with an explanatory bracket. 3. 'Real de a ocho' (piece-of-eight, ~38 mm diameter) is used to indicate the flower's approximate size. 4. 'Raítas [sic]' in the original appears to be a misprint or OCR error for 'rayas' (grooves/channels/stripes); translated accordingly. 5. The anecdote about the hurón using the sunchu root as a self-remedy against snakebite is a widely repeated piece of colonial natural-historical lore, likely derived from indigenous oral tradition and paralleled in other period sources. 6. The diocese of 'los Charcas' (Charcas) corresponds to the High Peru region, present-day Bolivia.

results.title_inferred: On the caralahua plant: uses, habitat, and medicinal preparations
results.original_text:
CAPITULO LI

De la caralahua

Esta es una mata delgada y pequeña; echa una vara tan alta como una pica, derecha y lisa, con algunos ramillos que nacen desde la mitad para arriba.

La hoja es del tamaño y talle de la del durazno, la cual y la corteza del árbol son de un verde muy blanquecino. Echa unas florecillas amarillas de la hechura y grandor de las del tabaco. Nace esta mata en las orillas de los ríos en tierra templada; su tronco y ramas son de una sustancia esponjosa y de complexión fría. Sus hojas majadas, o el zumo dellas es contra toda inflamación cálida y contra el dolor de cabeza, si se aplica en la frente; y si con el zumo se mezclan polvos de copaquira, limpia cualquier llaga, por mala que sea, y extirpa los lamparones.
results.translation:
CHAPTER LI

On the caralahua

This is a slender and small shrub; it sends up a stem as tall as a pike [i.e., roughly 12–18 feet], straight and smooth, with some small branches that grow from the middle upward.

The leaf is the size and shape of that of the peach tree, and both the leaf and the bark of the plant are of a very whitish green. It produces small yellow flowers similar in form and size to those of tobacco. This shrub grows on the banks of rivers in temperate lands [tierra templada]; its trunk and branches are of a spongy substance and of cold complexion [complexión fría, in the Galenic humoral sense]. Its leaves, pounded, or the juice extracted from them, is used against all hot inflammations [inflamación cálida] and against headache when applied to the forehead; and if powders of copaquira [copper sulfate/blue vitriol] are mixed with the juice, it cleanses any wound, however severe, and eradicates scrofulous swellings [lamparones].

entities_mentioned.name: caralahua
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Possibly a Salix sp. (willow) or a riparian shrub in the family Salicaceae or Buddlejaceae; the spongy stems, willow-like leaves, yellow flowers, and riverbank habitat suggest a candidate such as Tessaria integrifolia (pájaro bobo) or a related species of the Asteraceae
entities_mentioned.notes: The description — small yellow flowers resembling tobacco, spongy stems, whitish-green peach-shaped leaves, cold humoral complexion, riparian habitat in temperate zones — is suggestive of several Andean riparian shrubs. Tessaria integrifolia is a plausible candidate given its ecology and traditional anti-inflammatory uses, but confidence is low without more diagnostic detail.

entities_mentioned.name: copaquira
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Copper sulfate (CuSO₄·5H₂O), commonly called blue vitriol or caparrosa azul
entities_mentioned.notes: Copaquira (also cuperosa or caparrosa) was a widely used mineral astringent and wound-cleansing agent in colonial Andean medicine. Its use here as an additive to plant juice for wound treatment is consistent with other 17th-century sources.

entities_mentioned.name: lamparones
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Scrofula (cervical tuberculous lymphadenitis) or more broadly scrofulous swellings/tumors
entities_mentioned.notes: Lamparones is a standard colonial Spanish medical term for scrofulous swellings of the lymph nodes, often associated with tuberculosis. The claim that the preparation 'extirpates' them is a conventional therapeutic assertion of the period.

results.translator_notes: Two OCR errors were silently corrected: 'brecha' → 'derecha' (straight) and 'Wk nacen' → 'nacen' (a spurious line-initial artifact removed). The simile 'tan alta como una pica' refers to a pike or lance, a standard colonial length reference of approximately 12–18 feet. The phrase 'complexión fría' reflects Galenic humoral theory and is preserved in translation with an explanatory gloss. 'Tierra templada' is a standard Spanish colonial environmental/altitudinal zone designation and is left in the original with a bracketed note.

results.title_inferred: Pupa (birdlime plant) and Moco-moco (knotted medicinal root)
results.original_text:
CAPITULO LII
De la pupa

La pupa, y por otro nombre hamillo, es una mata de un estado de alto; tiene los ramos verdes y la hoja como de verdolagas, tantico más angosta y larguilla; es muy tierna, hoja y cogollo; da una florecilla colorada y muy delgada, de hechura de la cantuta, y unos granillos ni más ni menos que los de la yerba-mora, sólo que no los echa en racimos, sino cada uno de por sí pegados a las ramillas. Desta planta sacan los indios la liga para cazar pájaros, y es tan buena como la de España. El agua de sus flores, sacada por alquitara, es un singular remedio, tomado por las mañanas dos o tres onzas della con otra tanta agua de azahar o de borrajas, contra las melancolías, saltos y tristezas del corazón; y si con esta agua se mezcla aguardiente y polvos de aromático rosado, y dello se toma como dos onzas en los desmayos de corazón, hace muy buen efecto, y a los faltos de sueño hace dormir.

CAPITULO LIII
Del moco-moco

Llaman los indios mocomoco, que significa cosa ñudosa, a cierta raíz medicinal. La planta es pequeña y sus hojas como de membrillo. La raíz es ñudosa, vomitiva y de algún mal olor, caliente y seca; de la cual usan los indios para vomitar y echar cólera y flema, y para lanzar la comida cuando causa pesadumbre. También provoca a cámaras, por alguna parte que tiene laxativa. Hase de tomar en agua caliente y con azúcar, por su mal gusto. Bebido su cocimiento en el parto, lo facilita. Demás desto, si se toma de su zumo media escudilla en ayunas y se guardan del aire aquel día, purga la melancolía y provoca la orina y deshace la piedra de los riñones. Finalmente, tiene tanta fuerza de limpiar esta raíz, que suple la falta de jabón, porque, emblanqueciendo, levanta espuma, a cuya causa usan della en muchas partes para lavar la ropa; particularmente en la provincia de Tucumán, adonde emblanquecen y lavan con ella el algodón, de que se hace gran cantidad de lienzos.
results.translation:
CHAPTER LII
On the Pupa

The pupa, also known by another name as hamillo, is a shrub about one estado [roughly one fathom, approximately six feet] in height; it has green branches and leaves like those of purslane [verdolagas], only somewhat narrower and more elongated; both leaf and shoot are very tender. It produces a small, slender reddish flower shaped like that of the cantuta [cantuta flower], and tiny berries exactly like those of nightshade [yerba-mora], except that it does not bear them in clusters but each one separately, attached to the small branches. From this plant the Indians extract birdlime for catching birds, and it is as good as that of Spain. The distilled water of its flowers, taken by the alembic [alquitara], is a singular remedy — taken each morning in doses of two or three ounces together with an equal amount of orange-blossom water or borage water — against melancholy, palpitations [saltos], and sadness of the heart. And if this water is mixed with aguardiente [distilled spirits] and powders of aromatic rose [aromático rosado], and about two ounces thereof are taken during heart fainting spells [desmayos de corazón], it produces a very good effect; and it also brings sleep to those who suffer from insomnia.

CHAPTER LIII
On the Moco-moco

The Indians call mocomoco — meaning "knotted thing" — a certain medicinal root. The plant is small and its leaves resemble those of the quince. The root is knotted, emetic, and somewhat foul-smelling, hot and dry [in humoral character]; the Indians use it to induce vomiting and to purge bile [cólera] and phlegm, and to expel food when it causes heaviness or discomfort. It also provokes loose stools [cámaras], by virtue of some laxative property it possesses. It must be taken in hot water and with sugar, on account of its bad taste. Its decoction, drunk during childbirth, facilitates delivery. Beyond this, if half a small bowl [media escudilla] of its juice is taken on an empty stomach and the patient keeps out of the wind that day, it purges melancholy, promotes urine, and dissolves kidney stones [piedra de los riñones]. Finally, this root has such cleansing power that it serves in place of soap, for, while whitening, it raises a lather — for which reason it is used in many places for washing clothing; particularly in the province of Tucumán, where cotton is whitened and washed with it, and from which cotton a great quantity of cloth [lienzos] is made.

entities_mentioned.name: pupa / hamillo
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Possibly Mirabilis species (Nyctaginaceae) or a Loasaceae member; some scholars suggest Nicotiana or a related Solanaceae given the birdlime use and berry description, though identification remains uncertain
