entities_mentioned.notes: The birdlime extraction, small red flower described as resembling cantuta, and berries similar to nightshade (Solanum nigrum) suggest a possible Solanaceae affinity, but 'hamillo' and 'pupa' have not been definitively resolved in the literature. Treated conditions: melancholy, heart palpitations, fainting, insomnia. Preparation: flowers distilled by alembic; resulting hydrosol taken with orange-blossom or borage water; also combined with aguardiente and powdered aromatic rose.

entities_mentioned.name: cantuta
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Cantua buxifolia (Polemoniaceae)
entities_mentioned.notes: Used here only as a morphological comparator for flower shape; the sacred flower of the Incas.

entities_mentioned.name: yerba-mora
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Solanum nigrum (black nightshade)
entities_mentioned.notes: Used here only as a comparator for berry size and appearance.

entities_mentioned.name: verdolagas
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Portulaca oleracea (common purslane)
entities_mentioned.notes: Used here only as a leaf-shape comparator.

entities_mentioned.name: moco-moco
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Possibly Smilax species (Smilacaceae) or Chondrodendron/Cissampelos species; the saponin-rich, knotted-root description and use in Tucumán for washing cotton suggests a saponin-containing plant such as Saponaria or a related genus, or possibly Soapwort (Saponaria officinalis) analog native to South America
entities_mentioned.notes: The name 'mocomoco' (Quechua: 'knotted/nodular thing') and the range of uses — emetic, laxative, parturient, diuretic, lithotriptic, and soap substitute — are notable. The soap-substitute function strongly implies saponins. Treated conditions: need to vomit, excess bile and phlegm, digestive heaviness, constipation, difficult childbirth, melancholy, urinary retention, kidney stones. Preparation: taken in hot water with sugar; decoction drunk during labor; juice taken as half a bowl on an empty stomach; also used directly to wash clothing and whiten cotton.

results.translator_notes: 1. 'Estado' is a unit of height approximately equal to a man's height (~6 feet / ~1.7 m). 2. 'Alquitara' denotes an alembic or still, indicating distillation was a known technique among colonial-era practitioners. 3. 'Aromático rosado' likely refers to a compound powdered preparation based on rose and aromatic spices used in period pharmacy. 4. 'Cámaras' in this context means loose stools or diarrhea, a standard humoral-medical term. 5. 'Pesadumbre' (rendered 'heaviness/discomfort') in a digestive context means a feeling of oppression or indigestion. 6. OCR error 'pesadumtre' silently corrected to 'pesadumbre'; 'falla' corrected to 'falta'; 'rinones' corrected to 'riñones'. 7. The province of Tucumán referenced is the colonial-era jurisdiction in present-day northwestern Argentina, much larger than the modern province.

results.title_inferred: The Putu-puru plant: identification, appearance, and medicinal uses
results.original_text:
CAPITULO LIV
Del putu-puru

Putu-puru, en la lengua del Perú, es tanto como decir bolsillas; llaman así los indios a una mata pequeña que todo el año está verde y florida; produce una flor amarilla semejante a una bolsilla con sus cerraderos. Mezclado el polvo desta flor seca con ungüento cetrino, quita las manchas y paños del rostro y las señales de las heridas.

original_language_terms.gloss: citrine/yellow ointment, a standard galenic preparation

results.translation:
CHAPTER LIV
On the Putu-puru

'Putu-puru,' in the language of Peru [Quechua], means something like 'little purses' [bolsillas]; this is what the Indians call a small shrub that remains green and in flower throughout the entire year. It produces a yellow flower resembling a little purse with its drawstrings [cerraderos]. The powder of this dried flower, mixed with citrine ointment [ungüento cetrino], removes blemishes, discolorations [paños], and spots on the face, as well as the marks left by wounds.

entities_mentioned.name: Putu-puru
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Possibly Calceolaria species (family Calceolariaceae), whose slipper- or pouch-shaped yellow flowers closely match the 'bolsilla' description; alternatively a Crotalaria or Cassia species has been proposed by some scholars
entities_mentioned.notes: The Quechua name meaning 'little purses' and the description of a small, ever-green and ever-flowering shrub with yellow pouch-like flowers is strongly suggestive of Calceolaria (calceolaria = little shoe/purse in Latin), a large Andean genus. However, without further morphological detail a definitive identification cannot be made.

entities_mentioned.name: ungüento cetrino
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Citrine ointment — a standard Galenic/European pharmaceutical preparation, typically composed of mercury oxide or lead compounds in a fat base, used as a skin remedy
entities_mentioned.notes: This is a conventional European medicinal ointment of the period, not a New World preparation; its combination with the native plant powder represents the colonial-era blending of Galenic and indigenous pharmacopeias.

condition_treated: skin discolorations (paños — likely melasma or similar hyperpigmentation)
scarring or marks left by wounds
medical_extraction.preparation_method: Flowers are dried and ground to a powder, then mixed with citrine ointment [ungüento cetrino] and applied topically to the face or affected area.

results.translator_notes: The OCR rendered the chapter heading and plant name inconsistently (e.g., 'puT u~pu T u', 'PuTU-puru^'); these have been silently normalized to 'putu-puru' throughout. The term 'paños' is a period dermatological term referring to dark patches or discolorations of the skin (analogous to modern melasma); it has been translated as 'discolorations' with the original retained in brackets. 'Cerraderos' (literally 'closures' or 'drawstrings') describes the structural feature of the flower that resembles the tie or clasp of a small purse, reinforcing the Calceolaria identification. The phrase 'ungüento cetrino' should be understood within the Galenic pharmacological tradition, not as a native Andean remedy.

results.title_inferred: The Chacalia plant: uses for bone fractures, wound healing, and hair loss
results.original_text:
CAPITULO LV
De la chacalia

La chacalia es una mata pequeña, cuyas hojas son como las del lentisco, pegajosas y de temperamento caliente. Usan dellas los indios aplicándolas majadas y tostadas en cazuela y rociadas con vino, en cualquiera quebradura de hueso; y las mismas hojas majadas o su zumo, sueldan las heridas frescas. Dando baño en la cabeza con su cocimiento, le quita el dolor, y tiene virtud de afijar el cabello que por alguna mala calidad o flaqueza se va cayendo.
results.translation:
CHAPTER LV
On the Chacalia

The chacalia is a small shrub whose leaves resemble those of the mastic tree [lentisco], being sticky and of a hot temperament [caliente]. The Indians use them by applying the leaves pounded and toasted in an earthenware pan [cazuela] and sprinkled with wine to any fracture of the bone [quebradura de hueso]; and the same leaves when pounded, or their juice, knit together [sueldan] fresh wounds. Applied as a wash to the head in the form of a decoction [cocimiento], it relieves pain, and it has the virtue of fixing [afijar] hair that is falling out due to some poor quality or weakness of the scalp.

entities_mentioned.name: chacalia
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Possibly Dodonaea viscosa (hopbush/chacalia), a widespread resinous shrub common in tropical and subtropical Americas; the description of sticky leaves and hot temperament is consistent with this species.
entities_mentioned.notes: The name 'chacalia' and the description of sticky, mastic-like leaves strongly suggest Dodonaea viscosa, which is known ethnobotanically across Latin America for wound healing and pain relief. Alternative identification as a species of Burseraceae or Anacardiaceae cannot be excluded.

entities_mentioned.name: lentisco
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Pistacia lentiscus (mastic tree)
entities_mentioned.notes: Used here as a comparative reference point familiar to Spanish readers, not as a plant described in its own right.

results.translator_notes: The chapter heading in the source reads 'De la eh ac alia' — an obvious OCR split of 'De la chacalia'; silently corrected in the cleaned original text. The word 'afijar' (to fix, to fasten) is an archaic/period spelling of 'afirmar' or 'fijar' in this context, retained in brackets in the translation. 'Temperamento caliente' reflects the Galenic humoral framework standard in colonial Materia Medica. Three distinct therapeutic applications are described: (1) fracture treatment — pounded and toasted leaves in a pan, sprinkled with wine, applied topically; (2) wound healing — pounded leaves or expressed juice applied directly to fresh wounds; (3) headache and alopecia — decoction used as a head wash.

results.title_inferred: Three Andean medicinal plants: Casigua, Cilla-cilla, and Maycha
results.original_text:
CAPITULO LVI

De la casigua

Esta es una mata pequeña semejante al romero así en las ramillas delgadas como en el tamaño de las hojas, son algo puntiagudas, espinosas y más gruesas; si se tratan entre las manos, son algo pegajosas y dulces al principio, pero al fin de mascarlas, dejan algún género de amargo. Es caliente y seca; su cocimiento con culantrillo y anís es contra la tos; y con mayor fuerza hace su efecto si del cocimiento se hace lamedor y se toma a menudo.

CAPITULO LVII

De la cilla-cilla

Los indios de la provincia de los Charcas llaman cilla-cilla a una mata y a la purga que della se hace; a la cual los españoles nombran madre Rolán, porque así se decía una mujer que la puso en práctica. Es una mata que en tierras fáciles crece mucho; sus hojas se parecen a las del olivo, son por de dentro verdes y por de fuera blanquecinas, vellosas y agregadas unas a otras; cuyo temperamento es muy caliente y seco. Tienen las hojas virtud de purgar la cólera y melancolía. Tomadas molidas en agua o en vino aguado. Dase también esta purga a los que tienen grandes opilaciones, así en el estómago y hígado como en el bazo, y a los enfermos de buhas. Cocidas las cáscaras de su tronco con orozúz y miel, dada una escudilla de su cocimiento caliente en ayunas con miel y azúcar, purga la flema del pecho, es contra el asma y limpia los pulmones.

CAPITULO LVIII

De la maycha

Esta es una planta pequeña, caliente y muy aperitiva, a cuya causa mandan su cocimiento los indios cuando se sienten con algunas opilaciones; fuera de que mundifica y limpia los riñones y vejiga y es contra la detención de orina. Demás desto resuelve maravillosamente toda hinchazón de piernas, y este mismo efecto hace si se aplica caliente y rociada con vino.
results.translation:
CHAPTER LVI

On the casigua

This is a small shrub resembling rosemary both in its slender little branches and in the size of its leaves; the leaves are somewhat pointed, thorny, and rather thick. If handled between the fingers, they are somewhat sticky and sweet at first, but after chewing them they leave a certain bitterness in the mouth. It is hot and dry in temperament [in the Galenic sense]. A decoction of it made with maidenhair fern [culantrillo] and anise is used against cough; and it takes effect with greater force if a syrup [lamedor, a lick-medicine] is prepared from the decoction and taken frequently.

CHAPTER LVII

On the cilla-cilla

The Indians of the province of Charcas call cilla-cilla both the shrub itself and the purgative preparation made from it. The Spaniards name it 'madre Rolán' [Mother Rolán], after a woman of that name who first put it into practice. It is a shrub that grows abundantly in fertile, open soils. Its leaves resemble those of the olive tree; they are green on the inner side and whitish and woolly on the outer side, and they grow clustered together. Its temperament is very hot and dry. The leaves have the virtue of purging choler [yellow bile] and melancholy [black bile], taken ground in water or in diluted wine. This purgative is also given to those who suffer from great obstructions [opilaciones] in the stomach, liver, and spleen, and to those afflicted with syphilis [buhas]. The bark of the trunk, boiled with licorice [orozúz] and honey, and given as a warm cupful on an empty stomach with honey and sugar, purges phlegm from the chest, acts against asthma, and cleanses the lungs.

CHAPTER LVIII

On the maycha

This is a small plant, hot and strongly aperitive [opening/deobstruent], for which reason the Indians prescribe a decoction of it whenever they feel themselves afflicted with obstructions. Beyond that, it cleanses and purifies the kidneys and bladder and is used against retention of urine. Furthermore, it wonderfully resolves all swelling of the legs, and the same effect is achieved if it is applied warm and sprinkled with wine.

entities_mentioned.name: casigua
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Possibly a species of Baccharis or a related Asteraceae shrub native to the Andes
entities_mentioned.notes: Described as rosemary-like in appearance; hot and dry in temperament; used for cough in combination with maidenhair fern and anise. Precise identification requires further botanical context.

entities_mentioned.name: culantrillo
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Adiantum capillus-veneris (maidenhair fern) or related Adiantum spp.
entities_mentioned.notes: Common name in colonial Spanish medical literature for maidenhair fern, used here as an adjunct in the cough decoction.

entities_mentioned.name: cilla-cilla (also called madre Rolán)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Possibly Senecio or Baccharis spp. (Asteraceae); some scholars suggest Calea or related genera
entities_mentioned.notes: Purgative shrub from the Charcas province (modern Bolivia/NW Argentina). Named 'madre Rolán' by Spaniards after a woman who popularized its use. Olive-like leaves, whitish and woolly beneath. Treats bilious and melancholic disorders, obstructions of stomach/liver/spleen, syphilis, asthma, and pulmonary phlegm.

entities_mentioned.name: orozúz
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Glycyrrhiza glabra (licorice root)
entities_mentioned.notes: Standard ingredient in colonial pharmacy; used here to compound the bark decoction of cilla-cilla.

entities_mentioned.name: maycha
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Possibly Senecio graveolens or another Andean Senecio spp.; some identifications suggest Tagetes or Muña (Minthostachys mollis)
entities_mentioned.notes: Small Andean plant described as hot and strongly aperitive; used by indigenous peoples for obstructions, kidney and bladder complaints, urinary retention, and leg swelling (applied topically with wine and taken internally as a decoction).

results.translator_notes: Several passages were reconstructed from damaged or OCR-corrupted text. 'Lamedor' (literally 'licker') is a specific galenic pharmaceutical form—a thick syrup taken by licking from a spoon—rendered here as 'syrup [lamedor, a lick-medicine]' to preserve the technical term. 'Buhas' is consistently used in colonial sources to denote syphilis. 'Opilaciones' (obstructions/blockages in the humoral system) is retained in brackets throughout. 'Temperamento caliente y seco' reflects Galenic humoral classification. The phrase 'en tierras fáciles' is interpreted as 'in fertile, easy-working soils' (i.e., loamy or well-tilled ground). The name 'madre Rolán' suggests an early colonial empiric or curandera who disseminated the use of cilla-cilla among Spanish settlers; this is a rare instance in Cobo of a named female practitioner. The original chapter numbering uses Roman numerals LVI, LVII, LVIII; OCR rendered 'LVII' as 'LVn' and 'LVIII' as 'LVin'—corrected silently.

results.title_inferred: On the Mangapaqui plant: properties and medicinal uses
results.original_text:
CAPITULO LIX
De la mangapaqui

La mangapaqui es una mata de dos palmos de alto; las hojas y ramas son casi como las del orégano, algo desabridas. Echa por flores unas rosetas pequeñas, que, abiertas, vienen a quedar en una flecadura o vello blanco y sutil, fácilmente salta y se pega a la ropa; da de sí un olor no enfadoso. Su temperamento es caliente; mascada o comida la yerba, tiene facultad de quitar el mal olor de la boca. Su cocimiento, tomado caliente en ayunas, es contra la detención de orina, desopila el hígado y bazo y quita el dolor de ijada. Dando baño y sudor con esta planta en todo el cuerpo, quita los dolores de las juntas y el pasmo y alarga los nervios encogidos. El zumo de sus hojas, echado tibio en el oído doloroso por causa fría, lo templa y sana.
results.translation:
CHAPTER LIX
On the Mangapaqui

The mangapaqui is a shrub two palms [palmos] in height; its leaves and branches are nearly like those of oregano, somewhat insipid in flavor. It produces small rosette flowers which, when open, become a delicate white fringe or down that easily detaches and clings to clothing; the plant gives off a not unpleasant odor. Its temperament [i.e., humoral quality] is hot [caliente]. Chewed or eaten as a herb, it has the faculty of removing bad breath [mal olor de la boca]. A decoction [cocimiento] of it, taken warm on an empty stomach, is effective against urinary retention [detención de orina], clears obstructions of the liver and spleen [desopila el hígado y bazo], and relieves flank pain [dolor de ijada]. Applied as a bath and sweat treatment over the whole body, it relieves joint pains [dolores de las juntas], spasms [pasmo], and relaxes contracted sinews [nervios encogidos]. The juice [zumo] of its leaves, poured lukewarm into an ear that is painful due to a cold cause [causa fría], soothes and heals it.

entities_mentioned.name: mangapaqui
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Possibly a species of Baccharis or Gnaphalium (Asteraceae), given the cottony white pappus that clings to clothing and the oregano-like foliage described; alternatively a Pseudognaphalium sp. (cudweed). Precise identification uncertain.
entities_mentioned.notes: The description of small flowers opening into a white cottony down (pappus) that adheres to fabric is strongly suggestive of the Asteraceae family. The Quechua name 'mangapaqui' has not been firmly tied to a modern taxon in the standard literature consulted. Gnaphalium or Pseudognaphalium spp. (wira-wira group) are plausible candidates used medicinally in Andean contexts.

entities_mentioned.name: orégano (comparison plant)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Origanum vulgare or Lippia graveolens (New World oregano)
entities_mentioned.notes: Mentioned only as a morphological comparator for leaf and branch form, not as a subject of medical description.

results.translator_notes: Several OCR errors were silently corrected: 'pimos' → 'palmos'; 'desahriáas' → 'desabridas'; 'pergeñas' → 'pequeñas'; 'qpiedar' → 'quedar'; 'flocadura' → 'flecadura'; 'bigado' → 'hígado'; 'hazo' → 'bazo'; 'tóo' → 'baño'; 'juntos' → 'juntas'; '^tcofddos' → 'encogidos'; 'ím' → 'fría'. The phrase 'dolor de ijada' refers to pain in the flank or side (hypochondriac region), often associated with kidney or urinary complaints in period usage. 'Pasmo' in colonial medical Spanish denotes spasm, lockjaw, or tetanic-type muscular rigidity. 'Causa fría' reflects humoral etiology: earache attributed to cold humors.

results.title_inferred: The Rata-rata cactus and its medicinal uses
results.original_text:
CAPITULO LX
De la rata-rata

Llaman los indios peruanos rata-rata a una suerte de espino o cardón que tiene las hojas o pencas del grosor de un dedo y anchas como la palma de la mano, de color verde. Su temperamento es frío y húmedo. Echan de sí estas pencas, al partirlas, buena cantidad de jumo viscoso, que sirve de repelente para las inflamaciones, y mezclado con trementina, tiene virtud de atraer las espinas hincadas.
results.translation:
CHAPTER LX
Concerning the rata-rata

The Peruvian Indians give the name rata-rata to a kind of thorn-plant or cactus [cardón] whose leaves or pads [pencas] are as thick as a finger and as wide as the palm of the hand, and green in color. Its temperament [complexion] is cold and moist. When these pads are split open, they yield a good quantity of viscous juice [jugo viscoso], which serves as a repellent [repelente] against inflammations; and when mixed with turpentine [trementina], it has the virtue of drawing out embedded thorns or splinters.

entities_mentioned.name: rata-rata
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Possibly a species of Opuntia (prickly pear cactus) or a related flat-padded cactus in the family Cactaceae, native to Peru
entities_mentioned.notes: The description of flat, finger-thick pads ('pencas') and viscous mucilaginous sap is consistent with Opuntia spp. or possibly Tephrocactus/Austrocylindropuntia spp. The common name 'rata-rata' is not securely matched to a modern taxon. The 'cold and moist' temperament is a Galenic humoral classification.

entities_mentioned.name: trementina
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Turpentine (resinous exudate, likely from Pinus or Schinus species in the Andean context)
entities_mentioned.notes: Used here as a vehicle or adjuvant to enhance the drawing (maturative/extractive) properties of the preparation.

results.translator_notes: Several OCR errors were silently corrected: 'r ata^r ata' → 'rata-rata'; 'Mí suerte' → 'una suerte'; 'ieae' → 'tiene'; 'm. dedo' → 'un dedo'; 'isano' → 'mano'; 'U es' → 'to es' (temperamento); 'feias' → 'pencas'; 'tmm viscoso' → 'jumo viscoso' (jugo viscoso); 'm. h®' → 'para las'; 'Sementina' → 'trementina'; 'cw hincadas' → 'espinas hincadas'. The medical use data: condition treated = inflammations and embedded thorns/splinters; preparation = raw split pad juice applied topically as a repellent for inflammation; pad juice mixed with turpentine to extract embedded splinters or thorns. The Galenic 'cold and moist' temperament ascribed to the plant justifies its use as an anti-inflammatory.

results.title_inferred: The Añaguaya shrub of the Collao region
results.original_text:
CAPITULO LXI
De la añaguaya

La añaguaya es una mata espinosa a la canilla; levántase de la tierra de uno a dos codos; la hoja es de la forma que la de la sabina y algo más menuda; las espinas son menores que las del naranjo, derechas, agudas y blanquiscas. Da una florecita amarilla de ningún olor, del talle que la de la retama, un poquito menor. Nace esta planta en las provincias del Collao, en tierra muy fría, y sólo sirve de leña.
results.translation:
CHAPTER LXI
On the añaguaya

The añaguaya is a thorny shrub of slender [cane-like] form; it rises from the ground one to two cubits [roughly 18 to 36 inches]; the leaf is shaped like that of the savin [juniper] and somewhat smaller; the thorns are smaller than those of the orange tree, straight, sharp, and whitish. It produces a small yellow flower of no scent, in shape like that of the broom plant [retama], though a little smaller. This plant grows in the provinces of the Collao [the altiplano region around Lake Titicaca], in very cold country, and serves only as firewood.

entities_mentioned.name: añaguaya
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Possibly a species within Adesmia (Fabaceae), such as Adesmia spinosissima or a related thorny high-altitude legume shrub native to the Andean altiplano; the yellow retama-like flowers and spiny habit are consistent with this genus
entities_mentioned.notes: The description — spiny shrub, yellow broom-like flowers, found in the cold altiplano of the Collao — is suggestive of Adesmia spp. (commonly called 'añahuaya' or 'lampaya' in Andean vernacular), though certainty is not possible from description alone. No medicinal use is recorded; Cobo states explicitly it is used only as fuel.

entities_mentioned.name: sabina
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Juniperus sabina (savin juniper)
entities_mentioned.notes: Used by Cobo as a leaf-shape reference familiar to Spanish readers.

entities_mentioned.name: retama
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Retama sphaerocarpa or Spartium junceum (broom)
entities_mentioned.notes: Used by Cobo as a flower-shape reference; the yellow pea-family flower is the key comparator.

results.translator_notes: Two apparent OCR errors were silently corrected: 'ea' → 'es' (verb 'to be') and 'la canlla' → 'a la canilla' (meaning 'of slender/cane-like build'). The phrase 'a la canilla' is an idiomatic physical descriptor common in period botanical writing. No medical or pharmacological information is present in this segment; the plant is described solely as a fuel source. The Collao (also spelled Qollao) refers to the high altiplano around Lake Titicaca, shared today by Peru and Bolivia.

results.title_inferred: On the Calcas (Pajaro-bobo) and the Quisca-quisca
results.original_text:
CAPITULO LXII

De la calcas

La calcas es una mata que los españoles llaman pájaro-bobo. Nace en las orillas de los ríos y lugares húmedos con grande espesura; de cada mata sale una vara derecha, que desde el suelo produce muchos ramillos y no hace copa en su cumbre. Las más crecidas son tan gruesas como el brazo y altas desde uno hasta cuatro estados. Su hoja es algo más larga que la del olivo y dos veces más ancha que ella; comienza desde el pezón angosta, y hacia el remate tiene su mayor anchura y unas punticas por las orillas. Así la hoja como la corteza son de un verde blanquecino. Echa en su cumbre unas florecillas coloradas, redondas como botoncillos, compuestas de unos hilicos sutiles, las cuales, después de secas, quedan rojas y como tostadas. En todas las tierras marítimas del Perú, y en especial en este valle de Lima, nace gran copia destas matas, cuya leña se quema en los hornos desta ciudad de Lima.

CAPITULO LXIII

De la quisca-quisca

Llaman los indios del Perú quisca-quisca, que es tanto como decir planta espinosa, a una mata que crece de uno a dos estados; produce muchas ramas delgadas, con que se hace muy espesa, las cuales tiene muy pobladas de hojas y de agudas espinas del mismo color y sustancia que las ramas. La hoja es muy pequeñita, como las puntas de las del romero, y muy semejante a la hoja del tomillo. Echa unas florecillas amarillas tan pequeñas como las de los berros, y entre las hojas y espinas unos granillos negros cada uno de por sí, no mayores que granos de culantro y muy aguanosos, cuyo zumo tiñe de fino morado. En la lengua quichua se dice esta planta quisca-quisca, y en la aimará, huacra-huacra.
results.translation:
CHAPTER LXII

On the Calcas

The calcas is a shrub that the Spaniards call pájaro-bobo ["foolish bird"]. It grows on riverbanks and in damp places, forming dense thickets; from each shrub rises a single straight stem which, from the ground upward, puts forth many small branches and does not form a crown at its top. The largest specimens are as thick as a man's arm and range in height from one to four fathoms [estados]. Its leaf is somewhat longer than that of the olive and twice as broad; it begins narrow at the stalk and reaches its greatest width toward the tip, which has small points along the edges. Both the leaf and the bark are of a whitish green. At its crown it produces small red flowers, round like little buttons, composed of fine threadlike filaments; once dried, these remain red and appear as if toasted. Throughout all the coastal lands of Peru, and especially in this valley of Lima, great quantities of these shrubs grow, and their wood is burned in the ovens of this city of Lima.

CHAPTER LXIII

On the Quisca-quisca

The Indians of Peru call quisca-quisca — meaning, in effect, "spiny plant" — a shrub that grows to between one and two fathoms in height. It produces many slender branches, making it very dense, and these are thickly set with leaves and sharp thorns of the same color and substance as the branches. The leaf is very small, like the tips of rosemary leaves, and closely resembles the leaf of thyme. It bears tiny yellow flowers no larger than those of watercress, and among the leaves and thorns it produces small black berries, each separate from the others, no larger than coriander seeds and very juicy; their juice dyes [cloth] a fine purple. In the Quechua language this plant is called quisca-quisca, and in Aymara, huacra-huacra.

entities_mentioned.name: calcas / pájaro-bobo
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Tessaria integrifolia (Ruiz & Pav.) DC. (Asteraceae) — commonly known as pájaro-bobo in Peru; a riparian shrub typical of Peruvian coastal river margins
entities_mentioned.notes: Cobo's description of habitat (riverbanks, humid places), growth form (single straight stem with lateral branches, no crown), whitish-green leaves broader than olive, and small red button-like composite flowers aligns well with Tessaria integrifolia. The wood was used as fuel in Lima's ovens, consistent with known ethnobotanical records.

entities_mentioned.name: quisca-quisca / huacra-huacra
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Possibly Berberis lutea Ruiz & Pav. or another Berberis sp. (Berberidaceae); alternatively a species of Lycium (Solanaceae) or Adesmia (Fabaceae). The purple-dyeing juice from small black berries and spiny habit are suggestive of Berberis.
entities_mentioned.notes: The Quechua name quisca-quisca ("very spiny") is used for multiple spiny Andean shrubs. The yellow flowers, small rosemary-like leaves, and purple-staining black berries could fit Berberis lutea or related barberry species, which are known dye plants. Identification remains uncertain without further voucher data.

results.translator_notes: 1. The word 'estado' (pl. 'estados') is a Spanish unit of height roughly equivalent to a fathom or the height of a man (~1.67 m); translated as 'fathom' for period-appropriate flavor, with the original term retained in brackets in the cleaned text. 2. OCR errors silently corrected include: 'miichos' → 'muchos', 'hotoncillos' → 'botoncillos', 'hlanquecino' → 'blanquecino', 'tino' → 'uno', 'aima-rá' (split across line) → 'aimará', 'CAPITLTLO' → 'CAPITULO'. 3. No medicinal or pharmaceutical use is described for either plant in this segment; the calcas is noted only as a fuel source. 4. The term 'culantro' (coriander) used as a size reference for the quisca-quisca berries is standard colonial Spanish botanical vocabulary.

results.title_inferred: On the Olincana plant and its toxic properties
results.original_text:
CAPITULO LXIV

De la olincana

Esta es una mata de medio a un estado de alto; nace en lugares secos y pedregosos; echa en torno de sí muchas ramas, no derechas, sino torcidas; hace su tronco junto a la tierra tan grueso como el muslo, y aun como el cuerpo de un hombre. Las ramas van adelgazando hacia la punta, las cuales están desnudas de hojas, de color de carne y como arrugadas; al remate de cada rama nacen tres o cuatro hojas juntas con la flor en medio. Es la hoja de tres puntas hendidas hasta cerca del pezón, de manera que forman un pie de ave; cada punta es del tamaño y hechura que la hoja de los bledos, algo mayorcita la de en medio que las de los lados; el pezón liso y delgado como el de la hoja de la vid, aunque no tan largo. Cuando estas hojas están tiernas, son moradas, y luego hojas y pezón se vuelven de un color verdinegro como la hoja de la berenjena. La flor que produce esta planta es colorada fina como la del granado, salvo que es menor y del talle que la del alhelí. A la flor sucede una frutilla seca y de ningún provecho, del tamaño y hechura de una aceituna, maciza, y cuando madura, de color morado. Es notablemente ponzoñosa esta mata; sajando sus ramas destila un licor como leche, que, cayendo en cualquiera parte del cuerpo, la inflama y levanta ampollas. Majadas las ramas y echadas en los remansos y charcos de los ríos, matan al pescado. No es buena para leña, por ser de sustancia aguanosa y fofa, y cuando se quema da de sí muy [ILLEGIBLE: adjective describing bad odor, word cut off in source] olor.
results.translation:
CHAPTER LXIV

On the Olincana

This is a shrub [mata] of half to one estado [approximately 4–7 feet] in height; it grows in dry, stony places; it puts forth many branches around itself, not straight but twisted; its trunk near the ground becomes as thick as a thigh, or even as thick as a man's body. The branches taper toward their tips, and are bare of leaves, flesh-colored and somewhat wrinkled in appearance; at the end of each branch grow three or four leaves together with the flower in the middle. The leaf has three points cleft nearly to the petiole [pezón], so that they form a bird's foot; each point is the size and shape of the leaf of the bledo [Amaranthus spp.], the middle point somewhat larger than those on the sides; the petiole smooth and slender like that of a vine leaf, though not as long. When these leaves are tender, they are purple, and thereafter both leaves and petiole turn a dark greenish-black color like the leaf of the eggplant. The flower produced by this plant is a bright red, like that of the pomegranate, except that it is smaller and of the form of the wallflower [alhelí]. After the flower comes a small dry fruit of no use whatsoever, the size and shape of an olive, solid, and when ripe, purple in color. This shrub is notably poisonous; when its branches are cut, they exude a milky liquid which, falling on any part of the body, inflames it and raises blisters. When the branches are pounded and cast into the still pools and puddles of rivers, they kill the fish. It is not good for firewood, being of a watery and spongy substance, and when burned it gives off a very [ILLEGIBLE: adjective describing bad odor] smell.

entities_mentioned.name: olincana
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Possibly a Euphorbia species (family Euphorbiaceae), given the milky caustic latex, succulent wrinkled stems, arid habitat, and fish-stunning properties; alternatively proposed by the footnote as 'Villuama' or 'Ullucum', which may point to an Andean taxon. Some features also suggest Jatropha or a related genus.
entities_mentioned.notes: Cobo's own footnote (note 7) expresses doubt about the form of the name and speculatively proposes 'Villuama' or 'Ullucum' as alternatives. The caustic milky sap, fish-killing properties, and succulent leafless branches are strongly characteristic of the Euphorbiaceae (e.g., Euphorbia or Jatropha), common in dry Andean and coastal Peruvian zones. No medical preparation is described; the plant is treated as a poison and curiosity rather than a remedy.

entities_mentioned.name: bledos
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Amaranthus spp. (amaranth), used here only as a morphological comparator for leaf shape.
entities_mentioned.notes: Reference is purely comparative/descriptive.

results.translator_notes: 1. 'Estado' is a Spanish unit of length roughly equivalent to a man's height (~1.7–1.8 m); rendered as 'estado' with gloss. 2. 'Pezón' in botanical context means petiole (leaf stalk); rendered accordingly. 3. 'Alhelí' typically refers to the wallflower (Erysimum / Cheiranthus cheiri) in colonial Spanish botanical writing; retained with gloss. 4. The final adjective describing the smell of the burning wood is cut off in the source (OCR/print truncation); marked as ILLEGIBLE. The context strongly implies an unpleasant odor (e.g., 'muy mal olor'). 5. 'Majadas' (past participle of majar) means pounded or crushed; rendered as 'pounded.' 6. No medical preparation or therapeutic use is described in this passage; the plant is presented exclusively as a toxic and ecologically harmful species. 7. Footnote 7 in the original is Cobo's own editorial uncertainty about the plant name; preserved and paraphrased in the entity notes.

results.title_inferred: On the Pinco-Pinco Plant: Properties and Medical Uses
results.original_text:
CAPITULO LXV
Del pinco-pinco

Esta es una mata de tres o cuatro codos en alto; echa muchas varillas juntas delgadas y parejas como las de la retama; son macizas, leñosas y desnudas de hojas, y aunque lisas, de trecho a trecho ñudosas. Nace por los suelos una frutilla colorada del tamaño de garbanzos, con dos granitos negros dentro poco menores que los de la linaza. Es muy estimada esta planta en el Perú por sus muchos y maravillosos efectos. Es caliente y seca con estipticidad manifiesta. Majadas sus hojas o ramas y puestas sobre las heridas frescas, las juntan, desecan y sanan. Sus polvos, revueltos con los de alumbre, tienen fuerza de secar toda llaga. El agua de pinco-pinco, cocida con el guayacán, y bebida de ordinario, es contra los dolores de bubas. Mascada esta yerba y traída de ordinario en la boca, aprieta maravillosamente la dentadura. Mezclados sus polvos con sal y claras de huevos, sueldan las fracturas de los huesos; el agua desta planta, habiendo cocido bien y estando almacigada, bebida de ordinario, suelda las venas rotas del pecho, aprovecha contra las disenterias y contra el flujo de sangre que suele salir por la orina, conforta el estómago flaco, da calor y ganas de comer, hace buena digestión, consume las flemas y evita los corrimientos de la gota.
results.translation:
CHAPTER LXV
On the Pinco-Pinco

This is a shrub three or four cubits [codos] in height; it puts forth many slender and even stems together, like those of the broom plant [retama]; they are solid, woody, and bare of leaves, and although smooth, knotted at intervals. At ground level it produces a small red fruit the size of chickpeas, containing two small black seeds inside, somewhat smaller than those of flaxseed. This plant is highly esteemed in Peru for its many and marvelous effects. It is hot and dry with a manifest astringency [estipticidad]. Its leaves or branches, pounded and applied to fresh wounds, draw them together, dry them, and heal them. Its powders, mixed with those of alum, have the power to dry out any sore or ulcer. The water of pinco-pinco, decocted with guaiacum [guayacán] and drunk regularly, is used against the pains of the pox [bubas, i.e., syphilis]. This herb, chewed and kept habitually in the mouth, wonderfully tightens the teeth [dentadura]. Its powders, mixed with salt and egg whites, knit fractures of the bones; the water of this plant, having been well boiled and being resinous [almacigada], drunk regularly, knits broken vessels [veins] of the chest, is beneficial against dysenteries and against the flux of blood that tends to pass in the urine, strengthens a weak stomach, provides warmth and appetite, promotes good digestion, consumes phlegms [flemas], and prevents the fluxes [corrimientos] of gout.

entities_mentioned.name: pinco-pinco
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Ephedra americana (or a closely related Andean Ephedra species, possibly Ephedra rupestris)
entities_mentioned.notes: The description of leafless, jointed, rush-like woody stems with small red berry-like fruit containing two dark seeds is highly characteristic of Ephedra spp. Common in the Andean highlands of Peru. The astringent, hemostatic, and tonic properties attributed are consistent with Ephedra alkaloids and tannins.

entities_mentioned.name: retama
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Spartium junceum (Spanish broom) or a related broom species; used here only as a morphological comparator
entities_mentioned.notes: Referenced only as a visual comparison for the stem habit of pinco-pinco.

entities_mentioned.name: guayacán
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Guaiacum officinale or Guaiacum sanctum (lignum vitae / guaiac wood)
entities_mentioned.notes: Guaiacum was the canonical European and colonial remedy for syphilis (bubas) in the 16th–17th centuries. Here used in combination decoction with pinco-pinco for the same purpose.

entities_mentioned.name: alumbre
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Alum (potassium aluminium sulfate)
entities_mentioned.notes: A common mineral astringent used in wound care and ulcer treatment throughout the period.

entities_mentioned.name: bubas
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Syphilis (Treponema pallidum infection); the term 'bubas' was the standard colonial Spanish term for the disease
entities_mentioned.notes: Specifically referenced as the indication for the combined guayacán and pinco-pinco decoction.

results.translator_notes: Several OCR errors were silently corrected: 'cuaLm' → 'cuatro'; 'varilla' (line break) → 'varillas'; 'des.' + 'nudas' → 'desnudas'; 'ét' → 'de'; 'cW tro' → 'dentro'; 'linm' → 'linaza'; 'efectc^' → 'efectos'; 'ma¬ nifiesta' → 'manifiesta'; 'fae- za' → 'fuerza'; 'piim- pinco' → 'pinco-pinco'; 'bebi& de' → 'bebida de'; 'ife' → 'de'; '&' → 'de'; 'maravilk* sámente' → 'maravillosamente'; 'la dentaelura' → 'la dentadura'; 'm' → 'sus'; 'sad* dan' → 'sueldan'; 'el apa' → 'el agua'; 'bien j' → 'bien y'; 'ordiaar rio' → 'ordinario'; 'pecb®' → 'pecho'; 'tm tra' → 'contra'; 'por' (implied) confirmed by sense; 'h' → 'da'; 'buena i* gestión' → 'buena digestión'; 'evita te corrimientos' → 'evita los corrimientos'. The term 'almacigada' (describing the decoction) likely means 'made resinous/thickened by reduction' rather than containing mastic (almáciga), though the latter is also possible; the sense of a concentrated decoction is preferred in context. 'Venas rotas del pecho' (literally 'broken veins of the chest') likely refers to haemoptysis or internal thoracic haemorrhage rather than discrete anatomical vessels.

results.title_inferred: Three Plants: Mío (Poisonous Shrub), Flor de la Trinidad (Tiger Flower), and Ñuñunya (Andean Berry)
results.original_text:
CAPITULO LXVI
Del mío

En las provincias de Tucumán y Paraguay nace una mata que los indios llaman mío, que es como decir yerba ponzoñosa, la cual se parece mucho en las hojas y altor al romero; es de tal calidad, que si los caballos la comen, al punto se comienzan a hinchar y dentro de un cuarto de hora mueren sin remedio. A cuya causa, los que caminan por donde hay esta planta, el remedio que hacen es untar con ella los hocicos de los caballos, y con esto no la comen.

CAPITULO LXVII
De la flor de la Trinidad

De las flores naturales de Indias, la más vistosa que hay, a juicio de muchos, es la que llamamos de la Trinidad. La mata de que nace es alta dos o tres palmos; echa las hojas muy parecidas a las de la palma cuando muy pequeña antes que haga tronco; por las cuales a lo largo discurren unos nervios. El vastago en que la flor nace es semejante al del lirio en el tamaño, es muy verde, redondo y liso. La flor tiene un pezoncillo, con que está asida al tallo, a cuatro o cinco dedos de largo, delgado y liso. La flor se forma de tres hojas grandes, que cada una tiene de largo cuatro dedos; están puestas en triángulo perfecto, desta suerte: desde el pezón a la mitad están juntas, y por la parte de dentro cóncavas, de manera que todas tres juntas forman una como media bola, del tamaño de media lima, aunque no con tan perfecta redondez, por la concavidad que cada hoja hace, que se distinguen como tres cáscaras de nueces pegadas; desde la mitad hasta la punta se apartan estas hojas, abriéndose hacia afuera, y se van estrechando hasta rematar cada una en una punta; y estas tres puntas vienen a quedar en igual distancia unas de otras, de suerte que de una punta a otra hay distancia de medio jeme. La sustancia destas hojas es tan delgada y delicada como la de las hojas de la rosa. El color es vario, por de fuera entre amarillo y colorado, que inclina más a amarillo, y por de dentro, desde la mitad por donde se juntan hasta la punta, es finísimo colorado; la otra mitad que forma la concavidad sobredicha, es de unas vistosísimas manchas de amarillo y colorado, como piel de tigre. Dentro destas tres grandes hojas nacen otras tres menores, iguales en la sustancia a las primeras y en proceder en forma triangular; pero de tal modo, que su nacimiento de cada una es en la juntura de las grandes, y así proceden entremetidas con ellas. El color destas segundas hojas es todo jaspeado como el sobredicho. De en medio desta flor nace un vastaguillo como el de la azucena, largo casi cuatro dedos, poco más delgado que el pezón de la misma flor; es liso y de un encarnado claro, y en la cumbre remata en seis hilitos que nacen dél con tres como botoncillos alrededor más larguillos y delgados que granos de cebada, cubiertos de un polvito como oro molido. Ella es flor hermosísima y fuera más estimada si fuera olorosa, que no lo es; y cogida por la mañana, a la tarde está ya marchita. Hásele dado el nombre que tiene por su composición de ternos de hojas y botoncillos, la cual ha poco tiempo que se trajo a este reino del Perú. En la Nueva España, de donde debe de ser natural, la llaman los indios oceloxochitl, que quiere decir flor de tigre. La raíz es como de puerro y buena de comer.

CAPITULO LXVIII
De la ñuñunya

La ñuñunya es una mata de poco más de un estado de alto. Echa muchas ramas; su hoja es algo parecida a la del durazno; es larga de más de medio palmo, angosta y muy acanalada. Produce una frutilla en pequeños racimos como cerezas, salvo que no es tan redonda, sino algún tanto larguilla y ahusada, la cual, después de madura se pone de un colorado muy escuro; es muy aguanosa y su zumo colorado, con el cual se pintan el rostro las indias de la provincia del Collao. Tiene dentro unas pepitas del tamaño de semilla de rábano, sólo que no son redondas, sino chatas. La flor desta planta es morada, no mayor que la flor de la col, y se parece a la de la yerba-mora. En las dos lenguas generales del Perú se llama esta planta, ñuñunya en la quichua, y ñuñumayu en la aimará.
results.translation:
CHAPTER LXVI
Of the Mío

In the provinces of Tucumán and Paraguay there grows a shrub that the Indians call mío, which is as much as to say 'poisonous herb' [yerba ponzoñosa]. It closely resembles rosemary in its leaves and height. It is of such a nature that if horses eat it, they immediately begin to swell and within a quarter of an hour they die without remedy. For this reason, those who travel through areas where this plant grows resort to rubbing it on the muzzles of their horses, which prevents the animals from eating it.

CHAPTER LXVII
Of the Flor de la Trinidad [Flower of the Trinity]

Among the natural flowers of the Indies, the most spectacular, in the judgment of many, is the one we call the Flor de la Trinidad. The shrub from which it grows stands two or three palms [spans] tall; it sends out leaves very similar to those of the palm tree when it is very small, before it has formed a trunk, through which veins run lengthwise. The stem on which the flower grows is similar in size to that of the lily; it is very green, round, and smooth. The flower has a small stalk by which it is attached to the stem, four or five fingers in length, slender and smooth. The flower is formed of three large petals [hojas, lit. 'leaves'], each measuring four fingers in length; they are arranged in a perfect triangle, in this fashion: from the stalk to the midpoint they are joined together and concave on the inner side, so that all three together form a kind of half-sphere, the size of half a lime, though not perfectly round on account of the concavity that each petal makes—they appear like three walnut shells joined together. From the midpoint to the tip, these petals separate, opening outward, and narrow until each one ends in a point; these three points remain at equal distances from one another, such that from one point to the next the distance is half a jeme [roughly half a hand-span]. The substance of these petals is as thin and delicate as that of rose petals. The color is varied: on the outside it ranges between yellow and red, inclining more toward yellow; on the inside, from the midpoint where the petals join to the tip, it is a very fine red; and the other half that forms the concavity described above bears very striking mottled patches of yellow and red, like a tiger's skin. Within these three large petals grow three smaller ones, equal in substance to the first and likewise arranged in triangular form, but positioned so that each one originates at the junction of two of the large petals and thus proceeds interleaved among them. The color of these second petals is entirely mottled as described above. From the center of this flower rises a small stem like that of a lily, nearly four fingers long, slightly slimmer than the flower's own stalk; it is smooth and of a light crimson, and at the top it terminates in six small filaments [threads] that issue from it, with three small button-like [knob-like] structures around them, longer and more slender than barleycorns, covered in a fine dust like powdered gold [i.e., pollen]. It is a most beautiful flower and would be even more prized if it were fragrant, which it is not; and picked in the morning, by afternoon it has already wilted. It has been given its name on account of its composition of sets of three petals and knobs [botoncillos]. It was only recently brought to this kingdom of Peru. In New Spain, from which it must originate, the Indians call it oceloxochitl, meaning 'tiger flower.' The root is like that of a leek and is good to eat.

CHAPTER LXVIII
Of the Ñuñunya

The ñuñunya is a shrub somewhat more than one estado [roughly six feet] in height. It puts out many branches; its leaf is somewhat similar to that of the peach tree; it is more than half a palm [span] long, narrow, and deeply channeled [grooved]. It produces a small fruit in small clusters like cherries, except that it is not as round, but somewhat elongated and tapering; after ripening it turns a very dark red. It is very watery, and its red juice is used by the Indian women of the province of the Collao to paint their faces. Inside it contains small seeds about the size of radish seeds, except that they are not round but flat. The flower of this plant is purple, no larger than a cabbage flower, and resembles that of the black nightshade [yerba-mora]. In the two general languages of Peru this plant is called ñuñunya in Quechua, and ñuñumayu in Aymara.

entities_mentioned.name: mío (yerba ponzoñosa)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Solanum glaucophyllum or possibly Coriaria ruscifolia ( 'duraznillo negro'); the description of rapid fatal swelling in horses is consistent with several toxic Andean shrubs, but Coriaria ruscifolia is a strong candidate given its distribution in Tucumán/Paraguay and known equine toxicity
entities_mentioned.notes: Cobo notes the name means 'poisonous herb.' The described toxic effect—rapid swelling and death within 15 minutes in horses—and the rosemary-like appearance are the main identifying features. No medicinal preparation described; the only use noted is as an olfactory deterrent applied to horse muzzles to prevent consumption.

entities_mentioned.name: flor de la Trinidad / oceloxochitl
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Tigridia pavonia (tiger flower, Iridaceae)
entities_mentioned.notes: The Nahuatl name oceloxochitl ('tiger flower'), the triangular arrangement of large and small tepals, the tiger-spotted coloration, and the description of a leek-like edible bulb are all hallmarks of Tigridia pavonia. Cobo notes it was recently introduced to Peru from New Spain. No medicinal use described; the root is noted as edible.

entities_mentioned.name: ñuñunya (Quechua) / ñuñumayu (Aymara)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Probably Jaltomata procumbens or a related Solanaceae; possibly Physalis peruviana or a Cestrum species. The purple flower resembling yerba-mora (Solanum nigrum) and the dark red juicy berry suggest a Solanaceous plant from the Andean Collao region.
entities_mentioned.notes: The plant is described as roughly 6 feet tall with peach-like channeled leaves, small dark red elongated berry-like fruits in clusters, flat radish-seed-sized seeds, and purple flowers resembling black nightshade. The red juice was used cosmetically by Collao women to paint their faces. No medicinal preparation described beyond the cosmetic use of the fruit juice.

entities_mentioned.name: yerba-mora
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Solanum nigrum (black nightshade)
entities_mentioned.notes: Used by Cobo as a comparator for the flower of the ñuñunya; standard colonial Spanish name for black nightshade.

The passage beginning 'HISTORIA DEL NUEVO MUNDO 227' is a page header and has been omitted from the cleaned text. The word 'jeme' (a unit of measure from the tip of the thumb to the tip of the index finger when extended, roughly 4 inches) is retained in translation with a gloss. 'Estado' (a unit of height roughly equal to a man's height, ~6 feet) similarly glossed.

results.title_inferred: The Fire Plant
results.original_text:
CAPITULO LXIX

De la planta del fuego

Esta especie de mata nace en la Nueva España, y yo la he visto en las huertas de México. Es un arbolillo de uno o dos estados de alto, mal poblado de hoja, la cual es espinosa, grande y de hechura de la de los bledos: hoja, tronco y ramas son de un color rojo como de fuego, lo cual y el efecto que obra le dan el nombre que tiene; porque si una persona la toca con la mano o con alguna parte de su cuerpo desnuda, como brazo o pierna, le causa un ardor terrible, y la parte tocada se inflama y pone encendida como si tuviera mal de Sán Lázaro; a cuya causa huyen todos de tocar esta mata.
results.translation:
CHAPTER LXIX

On the Fire Plant

This species of shrub grows in New Spain, and I have myself seen it in the gardens of Mexico [City]. It is a small tree of one or two fathoms [estados] in height, sparsely leaved, the leaves being spiny, large, and shaped like those of the bledo [pigweed/amaranth]. The leaf, trunk, and branches are all of a fiery red color, and it is from this appearance and from the effect it produces that the plant takes its name; for if a person touches it with the hand or with any bare part of the body, such as an arm or a leg, it causes a terrible burning sensation, and the part touched becomes inflamed and reddened as though afflicted with Saint Lazarus's disease [mal de San Lázaro, i.e., leprosy or a leprous-like skin condition]. For this reason, everyone avoids touching this shrub.

entities_mentioned.name: planta del fuego (fire plant)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Cnidoscolus stimulosus or possibly Cnidoscolus multilobus (stinging nettletree / mala mujer), a urticating shrub in the family Euphorbiaceae native to Mexico and Central America
entities_mentioned.notes: The description of fiery red coloration throughout the plant, spiny large leaves resembling amaranth (bledos), and a severe inflammatory skin reaction upon contact is strongly suggestive of a Cnidoscolus species, commonly called 'mala mujer' in Mexico. Some scholars have also proposed Jatropha urens. The reddish coloration detail is unusual and may reflect the specific variety or seasonal state observed.

entities_mentioned.name: bledos
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Amaranthus spp. (amaranth / pigweed)
entities_mentioned.notes: Used here as a morphological reference point for leaf shape; 'bledo' was the standard colonial Spanish term for amaranth.

entities_mentioned.name: mal de San Lázaro
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Leprosy (Hansen's disease) or, more broadly, severe inflammatory/leprous skin conditions
entities_mentioned.notes: A standard colonial-era term for leprosy or leprosy-like conditions, named after Saint Lazarus, patron saint of lepers. Cobo uses it here as a simile to describe the inflammatory redness caused by contact with the plant, not as a literal diagnosis.

results.translator_notes: No medical preparation or treatment is described in this segment; Cobo's account is purely observational and cautionary. The word 'estados' (plural of 'estado') was a Spanish unit of height roughly equivalent to a fathom or adult body-length (~1.7 m), so the shrub is described as approximately 1.7–3.4 m tall. The OCR hyphenation artifact 'Nue¬va España' has been silently corrected to 'Nueva España'. 'Mal de Sán Lázaro' accent on 'Sán' appears to be a typographic artifact of the original printing; normalized to 'San Lázaro' in the cleaned text.

results.title_inferred: The Ataranca Plant
results.original_text:
CAPITULO LXX
De la ataranca

Esta es una planta muy parecida al runcú-Tuncu. Levántase de la tierra como un estado; produce muchas ramas todas llenas de unas espinas blanquecinas tan grandes como las del naranjo; su hoja es algo semejante a la del granado, sólo que es un poco más angosta. En la lengua quichua se llama esta mata ataranca.
results.translation:
CHAPTER LXX
Of the Ataranca

This is a plant very similar to the runcú-tuncu. It rises from the ground to about the height of a man [un estado]; it produces many branches all covered with whitish thorns as large as those of the orange tree; its leaf is somewhat similar to that of the pomegranate, except that it is a little narrower. In the Quechua language this shrub is called ataranca.

entities_mentioned.name: ataranca (ataránca)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Possibly a species within Berberis or Lycium (family Berberidaceae or Solanaceae), given the described spines and narrow leaves in an Andean context; alternatively a Schinus or Teline species. Insufficient detail for confident assignment.
entities_mentioned.notes: Quechua name 'ataranca' has been tentatively associated with spiny Andean shrubs. The comparison to pomegranate (Punica granatum) leaves and orange (Citrus sinensis) thorns suggests a thorny shrub of moderate height (~1.7–1.8 m, i.e., 'un estado'). No medicinal use is described in this segment.

entities_mentioned.name: runcú-Tuncu (runcúTuncu)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Unknown; presumably another Andean shrub described elsewhere in Cobo's work.
entities_mentioned.notes: Used as a morphological comparator for the ataranca; not otherwise described in this segment. OCR likely split 'runcúTuncu' with a line break; reconstructed as a single compound Quechua name.

entities_mentioned.name: naranjo (orange tree)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Citrus sinensis
entities_mentioned.notes: Cited only as a size reference for the thorns of the ataranca.

entities_mentioned.name: granado (pomegranate)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Punica granatum
entities_mentioned.notes: Cited only as a leaf-shape comparator; ataranca leaves described as slightly narrower.

results.translator_notes: No medicinal or pharmacological information is provided in this segment; it is purely a morphological description. 'Un estado' is a period Spanish unit of measurement roughly equivalent to a man's height (~1.7 m). OCR rendered 'ataranca' as 'atar anca' in the chapter heading and 'runcU'Tuncu' with spurious apostrophe and capital; both have been silently corrected in the cleaned original text. The Quechua spelling 'quichua' is preserved as Cobo's period usage.

De la jareta

La jareta es una planta tan peregrina, que ni parece mata ni árbol, aunque arde y sirve de leña; ni tampoco puede comprehenderse debajo del género de las yerbas, porque solamente es una mancha verde que nace en los páramos y tierras muy frías.
