results.title_inferred: The Vine of Poisonous Pine-Nuts
results.original_text:
CAPITULO LXXV
Del bejuco de piñones ponzoñosos

En la costa de la Mar del Sur de la Nueva España nace una especie de bejuco que produce unos piñones ponzoñosos, los cuales echa en racimos y son del tamaño de nuestros piñones; están cubiertos con una cascarilla como de bellota, más delgada, correosa y puntiaguda, la cual, en secándose, se abre; el color del piñón, después de seco y mondado, es de color entre pardo y morado. Aprovéchanse desta fruta ponzoñosa los ganaderos de la diócesis de Guadalajara para matar los lobos, que son muchos los que se crían en aquella provincia; y el modo que tienen en matarlos es éste: muelen uno o dos almudes destos piñones, y toman una res o bestia vieja e inútil, y viva le van haciendo muchas heridas y llenándolas de aquella harina; y dejada un día desta suerte la res emponzoñada, muere luego y se hincha; a la cual acuden la noche siguiente los lobos, coyotes y zorras, y cuantos comen della, mueren. Suelen poner una cabra colgada junto a la res emponzoñada, para que sirva de reclamo, y a sus voces acuden las fieras. También vi en México que echaban destos piñones en los aposentos, y decían que morían los ratones que comían dellos; mas yo no hice la experiencia.
results.translation:
CHAPTER LXXV
On the Vine of Poisonous Pine-Nuts

On the coast of the South Sea [Pacific coast] of New Spain there grows a species of vine [bejuco] that produces certain poisonous pine-nuts [piñones ponzoñosos], which it bears in clusters and which are the size of our pine-nuts [i.e., European stone pine seeds]. They are covered with a small shell like that of an acorn, though thinner, leathery, and pointed, which splits open upon drying. The color of the pine-nut, once dried and shelled, is between brown and purple. The ranchers [ganaderos] of the diocese of Guadalajara make use of this poisonous fruit to kill wolves, of which a great many are raised [i.e., breed] in that province; and the method they employ to kill them is as follows: they grind one or two almudes [a dry measure, roughly 4–8 liters] of these pine-nuts, then take an old and useless head of cattle or beast of burden, and while it is still alive they make many wounds in it and fill them with that ground meal [harina]. After the poisoned animal has been left in this condition for a day it soon dies and swells up; the following night wolves, coyotes, and foxes gather around it, and however many eat of it die. They are accustomed to hang a goat near the poisoned carcass to serve as a lure [reclamo], and the wild animals are drawn by its cries. I also saw in Mexico City that people scattered these pine-nuts in their rooms, saying that the mice that ate of them would die; but I did not myself put this to the test.

entities_mentioned.name: bejuco de piñones ponzoñosos
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Jatropha curcas (physic nut) or possibly Hura crepitans (sandbox tree); the cluster-fruiting, acorn-like capsule description and Pacific coast provenance suggest Jatropha curcas most strongly, though Ricinus communis (castor bean) is also plausible
entities_mentioned.notes: Cobo describes a vine (bejuco) bearing clustered seeds in thin, pointed, leathery capsules that split when dry, with brown-to-purple seeds used as a rodenticide and carnivore poison. Jatropha curcas seeds contain curcin and phorbol esters consistent with the described toxicity. The 'vine' description is unusual for Jatropha, which is shrubby, so Luffa or another cucurbit poisonous species cannot be fully excluded, though none fits as well toxicologically.

entities_mentioned.name: lobos
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Canis lupus baileyi (Mexican gray wolf)
entities_mentioned.notes: Described as numerous in the Guadalajara diocese in the 17th century.

entities_mentioned.name: coyotes
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Canis latrans

entities_mentioned.name: zorras
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Urocyon cinereoargenteus (gray fox) or Vulpes macrotis (kit fox), both present in western Mexico

results.translator_notes: Several OCR errors silently corrected: 'culnertos' → 'cubiertos'; 'liellota' → 'bellota'; 'Aprovéclianse' → 'Aprovéchanse'; 'cpie' → 'que'; 'empozonada' (second instance) → 'emponzoñada'; 'luce' → 'hice'. The unit 'almud' (pl. almudes) is a traditional Spanish dry measure varying regionally between roughly 4 and 8 liters; it is left in the original with a bracketed explanation. 'Reclamo' is preserved in brackets as a period hunting term for a live decoy animal used to attract prey. The passage contains no human medical application; the poisonous preparation is used exclusively for pest/predator control (wolves, coyotes, foxes, and mice).

results.title_inferred: The Enepil plant
results.original_text:
CAPITULO LXXVII
Del enepil

Esta es una mata voluble, de género de bejuco; produce la hoja como la yedra, y echa unas vainas largas un palmo, anchas de dos a tres dedos y gruesas poco más de un dedo, las cuales están llenas de semillas, que son muy telicas como alas de mariposa; y de fuera es tan áspera la cáscara de estas vainas, por las muchas puntillas agudas que tiene, que puede servir de alhucema.
results.translation:
CHAPTER LXXVII
Of the enepil

This is a twining shrub [mata voluble], of the vine [bejuco] kind; it produces a leaf like ivy, and puts forth long pods [vainas] of a span [palmo] in length, two to three fingers wide and a little more than a finger thick, which are filled with seeds that are very wing-like [telicas], resembling butterfly wings; and on the outside the husk of these pods is so rough, on account of the many sharp little points [puntillas agudas] it bears, that it can serve as a rasp [alhucema / rasping tool].

entities_mentioned.name: enepil
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Mucuna pruriens (velvet bean / cowitch) or a closely related Mucuna spp.
entities_mentioned.notes: The description of a twining vine with ivy-like leaves, large pods roughly a span long filled with winged seeds, and a notably rough, bristly pod surface strongly suggests a Mucuna species. Mucuna pruriens is widely distributed in tropical America and its pods are famously covered in irritating stiff hairs. The name 'enepil' is a Nahuatl-derived term recorded in colonial Mexican sources for plants of this type. The comparison of the seeds to butterfly wings may describe the seed shape or seed coat markings. The word rendered 'alhucema' here appears to be used in the sense of a rasping or filing instrument rather than its usual meaning of lavender, likely an OCR or scribal idiosyncrasy; the intended meaning is a roughening/rasp (cf. Spanish 'alhucema' sometimes conflated with 'almohazo' or 'escofina' in period usage).

results.translator_notes: Several OCR corruptions were silently corrected: 'géoeri' → 'género'; 'larga*» m' → 'largas un'; 'dedos t' → 'dedos y'; 'mu / telicas' → 'muy telicas'; 'desia' → 'de estas'; 'ámm' → 'agudas'; 'aW / haza' → 'alhucema'. The final word 'alhucema' is puzzling in context (its primary meaning is lavender/rosemary); in this passage Cobo appears to use it to mean a rasp or roughening implement, possibly a scribal or period colloquial usage conflating it with 'almohaza' (currycomb/rasp). This is flagged for scholarly review. No explicit medicinal preparation or ailment is described in this segment; the passage is purely morphological/descriptive.

results.title_inferred: On the Jasmines of the Indies and the Honeysuckle of the Indies
results.original_text:
CAPITULO LXXVIII
De los jazmines de las Indias

Los indios mexicanos llaman acuilt a una mata a que los españoles dan nombre de jazmines de las Indias, la cual es propia de la Nueva España. Es una planta voluble que nace en lugares húmedos y suele arrastrarse por la tierra o revolverse a los árboles cercanos a ella, porque es de género de hiedra. Hállanse dos especies desta planta que sólo se diferencian en el color de las flores y en la grandeza de las hojas: porque la una da las flores blancas, y la otra, amarillas. Ambas echan muchos vástagos redondos, lisos y delgados; la hoja es como la del granado, más puntiaguda y muy rayada por ciertas venillas que nacen del pezón y hierren según su longitud. La flor se parece a la mosqueta; y aun en México vi que llamaban mosquetas a estas flores. Compónese cada flor de cuatro hojitas algo gruesecillas, con unos hilos delgados en medio. Es una de las más olorosas que producen las Indias. Dellas se saca por destilación un agua de muy agradable olor. Las hojas de esta planta, majadas, relajan los miembros encogidos.

CAPITULO LXXIX
De la madreselva de las Indias

Esta es una mata voluble que produce muchos vástagos, los cuales al principio, cuando nuevos, son delgados como pequeños juncos, redondos, verdes, y con el tiempo van engrosando hasta que se ponen como un dedo de la mano. Las hojas son de cuatro a cinco dedos de largo y tres de ancho, algo parecidas a las del torongil, sólo que son más anchas junto al pezón que en todo lo restante, con las orillas llenas de picos. Da esta mata muchísimas flores amarillas y muy olorosas, las cuales nacen en racimos o manojos de a diez o doce florecitas cada uno, y salen de un botoncillo o pezón del tamaño y hechura de un piñón, y cada flor consta de seis o siete hojitas en ruedo, cada una un poco menor que las hojas del azahar; y el botoncillo de en medio no parece sino un manojito de hilos amarillos. Enlázase esta mata en los encañados que le arman, y se pone con los muchos vástagos, hoja y flor que echa, tan cerrada y tejida, que cubre el encañado. Cuando estas flores han llegado a perfecta sazón, crían dentro unos animalejos del tamaño y talle de los granos de ajonjolí, los cuales en abriéndose las flores, saltan afuera.
results.translation:
CHAPTER LXXVIII
On the Jasmines of the Indies

The Mexican Indians call a shrub 'acuilt' to which the Spaniards give the name 'jasmines of the Indies'; it is native to New Spain. It is a twining plant that grows in damp places and tends to trail along the ground or to wind itself around nearby trees, being of the ivy family [género de hiedra]. Two species of this plant are found, which differ only in the color of their flowers and the size of their leaves: one bears white flowers, and the other, yellow. Both send out many round, smooth, and slender shoots; the leaf resembles that of the pomegranate, though more pointed, and is very much marked by small veins that arise from the stalk and run along its length. The flower resembles the musk-rose [mosqueta]; indeed, in Mexico I saw that these flowers were called 'mosquetas.' Each flower is composed of four rather thickish little petals, with slender filaments in the center. It is one of the most fragrant plants produced in the Indies. From these flowers a water of very pleasant fragrance is extracted by distillation. The leaves of this plant, when pounded, relax contracted [encogidos] limbs.

CHAPTER LXXIX
On the Honeysuckle of the Indies

This is a twining shrub that produces many shoots, which at first, when young, are as slender as small reeds, round and green, and with time they thicken until they become as thick as a finger. The leaves are four to five finger-widths long and three wide, somewhat resembling those of lemon balm [torongil], except that they are broader near the stalk than along the rest of their length, with edges full of pointed teeth [picos]. This shrub bears very many yellow and highly fragrant flowers, which grow in clusters or bunches of ten or twelve small blossoms each; they emerge from a small bud or stalk the size and shape of a pine nut, and each flower consists of six or seven petals arranged in a circle, each slightly smaller than orange-blossom petals; the small button in the center looks like nothing so much as a little bunch of yellow threads. This shrub twines itself around the trellises [encañados] erected for it, and with its many shoots, leaves, and flowers grows so dense and interwoven that it covers the trellis entirely. When these flowers have reached full ripeness, they harbor within them tiny creatures [animalejos] the size and shape of sesame seeds, which leap out when the flowers open.

entities_mentioned.name: jazmín de las Indias / acuilt
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Plumeria rubra or possibly Quamoclit / Ipomoea species; the description of a twining vine with fragrant white or yellow flowers in damp habitats and ivy-like habit has also been associated with Cestrum nocturnum or a species of Jasminum (introduced), but the Nahuatl name 'acuilt' and climbing vine habit suggest possibly Hoya or a related genus; identification remains uncertain
entities_mentioned.notes: The Nahuatl name is likely 'acuilt' or 'acuillo'; OCR renders it variously. Cobo describes two varieties (white-flowered and yellow-flowered), a climbing habit, pomegranate-like leaves, and musk-rose-like flowers. The distilled water and medicinal use for contracted limbs are noted.

entities_mentioned.name: madreselva de las Indias
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Possibly Lonicera species or a New World analog such as Tecoma stans or a related Bignoniaceae; the yellow flower clusters, trellis-climbing habit, and sesame-seed-sized insect inhabitants suggest Tecoma stans (yellow elder/trumpet bush) is plausible
entities_mentioned.notes: The description of small insects (animalejos) the size of sesame seeds living inside the flowers and jumping out when they open is botanically interesting and may refer to thrips, weevils, or similar floral inhabitants. The leaf comparison to torongil (lemon balm, Melissa officinalis) is a useful morphological clue.

entities_mentioned.name: mosqueta
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Rosa moschata (musk rose)
entities_mentioned.notes: Used as a comparative reference for flower form.

entities_mentioned.name: torongil
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Melissa officinalis (lemon balm)
entities_mentioned.notes: Used as a comparative reference for leaf shape of the madreselva de las Indias.

results.translator_notes: 1. The OCR of the Nahuatl name for the jasmine is damaged; 'acuilt' is a best reading but likely 'acuillo' or similar. 2. The phrase 'hierren según su longitud' in the original is ambiguous (lit. 'they run/strike according to their length'); rendered as 'run along its length' referring to leaf venation. 3. Chapter LXXIX begins mid-page in the source and continues across a page break; the text has been reassembled. 4. 'Encañados' (reed or cane trellises) is a period horticultural term preserved in translation. 5. The medical extract for Chapter LXXVIII is: plant name 'jazmín de las Indias / acuilt', ailment treated: contracted/stiff limbs, preparation: leaves pounded and applied topically; also distilled water from flowers for aromatic use.

results.title_inferred: The Ñorbo (Passionflower vine)
results.original_text:
CAPITULO LXXX
Del ñorbo

El ñorbo es una mata voluble de casta de bejuco; echa muchos vástagos delgados, que se revuelven a otras plantas o a los encañados y los pueblan de hojas y flores. La hoja es de singular hechura y que no he visto otra semejante a ella en ninguna planta: es ni más ni menos que la mitad de la hoja del naranjo, cortada por medio la parte que cae hacia la punta, con que viene a quedar esta hoja como la otra mitad asida al pezón. La flor es blanca y morada, olorosa, y de linda vista, de la hechura de la del puchipiichi, y tendrá el ruedo que un real de a dos; en la cual se ven las mismas insignias de la Pasión de Nuestro Redentor que en la flor de la granadilla, porque tiene esta flor la misma figura y composición que aquélla. Esta mata, y la del capítulo pasado, usan mucho los españoles en este reino del Perú plantarlas en sus jardines y en los patios de sus casas, arrimándolas a encañados, para que se extiendan en ellos.
results.translation:
CHAPTER LXXX
On the Ñorbo

The ñorbo is a twining shrub of the vine [bejuco] kind; it puts forth many slender shoots that wind themselves around other plants or around trellises [encañados], which they cover with leaves and flowers. The leaf is of a singular form unlike any I have seen on any other plant: it is neither more nor less than half the leaf of an orange tree, cut through the middle at the part toward the tip, so that this leaf ends up resembling the other half still attached to the stalk. The flower is white and purple, fragrant, and of a lovely appearance, shaped like that of the puchipiichi, and its span is about that of a two-real coin [real de a dos]; in it one can see the same emblems of the Passion of Our Redeemer as in the flower of the granadilla [passionflower], for this flower has the very same form and composition as that one. This shrub, and the one described in the preceding chapter, are much favored by the Spaniards in this kingdom of Peru for planting in their gardens and in the courtyards of their houses, training them onto trellises so that they may spread across them.

entities_mentioned.name: ñorbo
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Passiflora tripartita or a closely related Andean passionflower species (possibly Passiflora mixta)
entities_mentioned.notes: The distinctive half-leaf morphology and white-and-purple passionflower-like bloom are consistent with several Andean Passiflora species. 'Ñorbo' is still used in Peru today, most commonly for Passiflora tripartita var. mollissima or related taxa. Cobo notes the strong resemblance to the granadilla flower (Passiflora ligularis), confirming the Passifloraceae family.

entities_mentioned.name: granadilla
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Passiflora ligularis
entities_mentioned.notes: Standard colonial Peruvian term for the sweet granadilla; used here as a morphological reference point for the passionflower corona symbolism.

entities_mentioned.name: puchipiichi
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Unknown; possibly another Andean passionflower or local ornamental vine
entities_mentioned.notes: Cobo uses this plant's flower as a shape comparison for the ñorbo bloom. The term does not appear in standard modern botanical references consulted; may be a regional or indigenous name requiring further archival research.

results.translator_notes: Several OCR errors were silently corrected: 'smejante' → 'semejante', 'smplar' → 'singular', 'm hojas' → 'de hojas', 'áe' → 'de', 'im real' → 'un real', 'sfcióa cpie' → 'sición que', 'Wes' → 'ñoles'. No genuinely illegible passages. No medical or pharmacological content is present in this segment; the chapter is purely descriptive and horticultural. The comparison of the passionflower corona to instruments of Christ's Passion was a common trope among colonial Iberian naturalists. The coin denomination 'real de a dos' (a two-real silver coin, roughly 27 mm diameter) is used to indicate the flower's approximate size.

results.title_inferred: The Chinchircuma Plant
results.original_text:
CAPITULO LXXXI
De la chinchircuma

Esta es una mata voluble, que nace en tierras frías; produce un vástago delgado como un junco mediano, redondo, blanquecino, muy tierno y quebradizo; la hoja es larga medio jeme y no más ancha que la longitud de un grano de trigo; echa una flor naranjada de muy vivo color, semejante a la clavellina en la hechura, salvo que tiene un poco más grueso el pezón y no es liso, sino como escamoso, compuesto de unas hojitas cortas algún tanto moradas y dispuestas a manera de escamas. Las hojas de la flor son muchas y más angostas que las de la clavellina. Tiene esta flor agradable parecer, pero sin olor, y el nombre que tiene es de la lengua aimará.
results.translation:
CHAPTER LXXXI
On the Chinchircuma

This is a twining shrub that grows in cold lands. It produces a thin stem like a medium-sized rush [junco], round, whitish, very tender and brittle. The leaf is half a [jeme — the span from the tip of the thumb to the tip of the index finger, roughly 3–4 inches] long and no wider than the length of a grain of wheat. It puts forth a vivid orange-colored flower, similar in form to the carnation [clavellina], except that its stalk [pezón] is somewhat thicker and is not smooth but rather scaly, composed of small, somewhat purplish leaflets arranged in the manner of scales. The petals of the flower are numerous and narrower than those of the carnation. This flower has a pleasing appearance but no scent, and its name derives from the Aymara language.

entities_mentioned.name: chinchircuma
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Mutisia acuminata or a closely related Mutisia spp. (family Asteraceae)
entities_mentioned.notes: 'Chinchircuma' (also spelled 'chinchircoma' or 'chinchirkhuma' in Aymara) is a name applied in Andean literature to climbing plants of the genus Mutisia, several species of which produce vivid orange flower heads in cold highland zones and match Cobo's description of scaly, purplish involucral bracts. No medicinal use or preparation is described in this passage; the chapter appears to be purely botanical. Mutisia acuminata is the most commonly cited candidate, but other species (e.g., M. viciaefolia) cannot be ruled out.

entities_mentioned.name: clavellina
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Dianthus caryophyllus (carnation/pink)
entities_mentioned.notes: Used here as a morphological comparator for flower form, not as a subject plant.

results.translator_notes: 1. 'Jeme' is a traditional Spanish unit of measure equal to the distance between the tip of the extended thumb and the tip of the extended index finger, approximately 7–8 cm (roughly 3 inches); retained in brackets with an explanatory gloss. 2. 'Mata voluble' denotes a twining or scrambling shrub, characteristic of climbing Mutisia species. 3. 'Pezón' in botanical contexts of this period refers to the peduncle or floral stalk. 4. The scaly, purplish involucral bracts described are consistent with the phyllaries (involucre scales) of Mutisia capitula. 5. No medical or pharmacological information appears in this chapter; accordingly, no drug-use extraction is applicable. 6. OCR artifact: 'chinchir cuma' in the chapter heading is a line-break split of 'chinchircuma'; silently corrected in the cleaned original text.

results.title_inferred: The Pintoc — A Wild Cane of Peru and Its Uses
results.original_text:
CAPITULO LXXXIV
Del pintoc

El pintoc es aquella especie de caña que los españoles, a diferencia de las demás, llaman bravas, y es la más común y que más copiosamente nace en todas estas Indias, particularmente en este reino del Perú; parecese en la hoja, grandeza y talle a la caña común de Europa más que ninguna otra casta de cañas desta tierra, salvo que tiene la hoja más áspera y no tan ancha. Mondadas estas cañas, no son tan lisas ni blancas como las nuestras de España; son macizas, pesadas, con sus ñudos a trechos, aunque lo interior es de una materia fofa y liviana, a manera de hilo; mas la corteza es muy dura y recia. Nacen comúnmente en las riberas de los ríos y en lugares húmedos y empantanados; hácense dellas muy grandes y espesos cañaverales, como los había en la ribera del río desta ciudad de Lima, cuando la poblaron los españoles, y aún alcancé yo buena parte dellos ahora cincuenta años, donde tenían sus escondrijos los negros cimarrones. Sirven estas cañas en muchos usos; porque dellas, rajadas, se hacen canastas, cestos, petacas y otras cosas, y dellas, enteras, se arman encañados para los parrales, se hacen zarzos, barbacoas, bahareques, que son las paredes de las casas de los indios; y en los techos de estera se ponen dos juntas por travesaños de una viga y otra, porque son muy fuertes; y por la misma razón de ser tan recias, sirven en los edificios, porque dellas se hacen las cimbrias para los arcos y bóveda; con su cogollo cubrían los indios sus casas en lugar de tejas, y aun agora en algunas partes los usan los españoles; y, finalmente, son leña fuerte para calentar los hornos. El nombre que tiene tomado de la lengua general del Perú.
results.translation:
CHAPTER LXXXIV
On the Pintoc

The pintoc is that species of cane which the Spaniards, to distinguish it from others, call 'brava' [wild], and it is the most common and most abundantly growing of all these Indies, particularly in this kingdom of Peru. It resembles in its leaf, size, and form the common cane of Europe more than any other variety of cane in this land, except that its leaf is rougher and not as wide. When these canes are stripped, they are not as smooth or as white as those of Spain; they are solid and heavy, with nodes at intervals, although the interior is of a soft and light material, resembling fiber [literally: thread]; but the bark [outer rind] is very hard and tough. They commonly grow on the banks of rivers and in damp, marshy places; they form very large and dense cane brakes [cañaverales], such as once existed on the banks of the river of this city of Lima when the Spaniards settled it — and I myself encountered a good part of them some fifty years ago, where the runaway slaves [negros cimarrones] had their hiding places. These canes serve many purposes: split, they are used to make baskets, hampers, trunks [petacas], and other things; whole, they are used to build trellises [encañados] for grapevines, to make wattle screens [zarzos], raised platforms [barbacoas], and wattle-and-daub walls [bahareques], which form the walls of the houses of the Indians. In thatched roofs they are placed in pairs as cross-beams from one rafter to another, for they are very strong; and for the same reason of being so sturdy, they serve in construction, since from them the centering [cimbrias] for arches and vaults is made. With their leafy tops [cogollos] the Indians used to cover their houses in place of tiles, and even now in some places the Spaniards use them for this purpose; and finally, they serve as strong firewood to heat ovens. The name is taken from the general language [Quechua] of Peru.

entities_mentioned.name: pintoc
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Guadua weberbaueri or a related native Andean/Peruvian bamboo-like cane (Arundo donax has been suggested for similar colonial descriptions, but the 'pintoc' / 'brava' designation and Quechua name more likely indicate a native Guadua or Chusquea species)
entities_mentioned.notes: Called 'caña brava' by Spaniards; name derived from Quechua. Cobo describes it as solid (not hollow like Arundo donax), with a fibrous interior and very hard rind, growing in riverine and marshy environments. This description aligns reasonably well with Guadua spp. or a related New World cane. No medicinal use is described in this passage; the chapter is entirely devoted to utilitarian and construction applications.

entities_mentioned.name: caña brava
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Guadua sp. or similar wild Neotropical cane
entities_mentioned.notes: Spanish vernacular name used by colonists; functionally synonymous with pintoc as described here.

results.translator_notes: The OCR introduced several errors that were silently corrected: 'lioja' → 'hoja'; 'mu macizas' → 'son macizas'; 'Im ríos' / 'Im rí' → 'los ríos'; 'empanubados' → 'empantanados'; 'esconéijos' → 'escondrijos'; 'hahareqpies' → 'bahareques'; 'ic hacen' → 'se hacen'; 'M hacen' / 'rir?m' → repaired per context; 'cdentar' → 'calentar'; 'Wios' → 'indios'; 'ahora «mruenta años' → 'ahora cincuenta años'. The term 'lengua general del Perú' refers to Quechua (Runa Simi), the lingua franca of the Inca empire and colonial Peru. No medical or pharmacological content is present in this segment.

results.title_inferred: The Charo (Black Cane Used for Walking Staves)
results.original_text:
CAPITULO LXXXV
Del charo

El charo es la caña negra de que se hacen los bordones, la cual crece tres o cuatro estados en alto, no tiene ramas ni hojas por todo el tronco hasta su cumbre, adonde hacen una copa pequeña de unas ramas y hojas parecidas a las de las palmillas pequeñas de que hacen las escobas en España. Tiene cubierto el tronco de una cáscara blanquecina y delgada como la de las demás cañas, y armado todo él de arriba abajo de unas púas negras, recias y enconadas, tamañas como las del naranjo, que no dan lugar a que una persona hienda por estos cañaverales cuando son cerrados y espesos. Mondadas estas cañas de su cáscara y espinas, quedan negras y muy lisas, divididas a trechos de unos sutiles ñudos, que parecen costuras, los cuales no dividen lo interior della ni pasan adentro. Es maciza en esta forma: que la corteza o casco es muy duro y recio, mas el corazón es todo de unas hebras secas, flojas y muy frágiles. Hácense muy curiosos bordones destas cañas, y principalmente de algunas que salen manchadas de pintas negras y blancas, que parecen overas. Haylas tan gruesas como la muñeca, y déstas suelen hacer los indios los bahareques de sus casas y de sus huertas.
results.translation:
CHAPTER LXXXV
Of the Charo

The charo is the black cane from which walking staves [bordones] are made. It grows three or four fathoms [estados] in height, having neither branches nor leaves along the entire trunk until its crown, where it forms a small tuft of branches and leaves resembling those of the small palm [palmilla] used to make brooms in Spain. The trunk is covered with a whitish, thin bark like that of other canes, and is armed from top to bottom with black, stout, and sharp thorns [púas] as large as those of the orange tree, which make it impossible for a person to pass through these cane groves when they are dense and thick. Once these canes are stripped of their bark and thorns, they are found to be black and very smooth, divided at intervals by fine knots that resemble seams, though these do not divide the interior of the cane nor penetrate inward. The cane is solid in the following manner: the outer bark or shell is very hard and tough, but the core consists entirely of dry, loose, and very fragile fibers. Very fine walking staves are made from these canes, especially from those that come out mottled with black and white spots, resembling a dappled [overo] pattern. Some are as thick as a person's wrist, and from these the Indians customarily construct the wattle-and-daub walls [bahareques] of their houses and garden enclosures.

entities_mentioned.name: charo
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Bactris setulosa or a related spiny palm cane (possibly Bactris gasipaes or Desmoncus spp.); alternatively a spiny bamboo-like palm in the genus Ceroxylon or Trithrinax — most likely a species of Bactris or Aiphanes given the description of black spines, palm-like crown, and use for walking staves
entities_mentioned.notes: The description — tall unbranched trunk, spiny black thorns, small palm-leaf crown, solid but fibrous interior, used for walking staves and wattle construction — strongly suggests a spiny palm cane rather than a true bamboo. The term 'caña negra' and use for 'bordones' (pilgrim/walking staves) is well attested in colonial sources for Andean spiny palms. Firm identification requires geographic context not supplied in this segment.

entities_mentioned.name: palmilla (pequeña de que hacen las escobas en España)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Chamaerops humilis (dwarf fan palm, used in Spain for broom-making)
entities_mentioned.notes: Used here only as a morphological comparison for the crown leaves of the charo, not as a subject in its own right.

entities_mentioned.name: bahareque
entities_mentioned.notes: Bahareque (also bahareque or bajareque) is a traditional wattle-and-daub construction technique widespread in Latin America; not a plant but a building method using cane/plant materials. Noted here as a use of the charo cane.

results.translator_notes: The segment contains no medical or pharmacological content; it is a purely botanical and economic description of the charo cane. No plant drug extraction is applicable. OCR artifacts corrected silently include 'cáscara blan¬quecina' (line-break hyphen), 'pintas^ negras' (spurious caret), 'overas..' (double period), 'muñeca,,' (double comma), and 'huertas^' (spurious caret). The word 'estado' is a unit of measure roughly equivalent to a fathom or the height of a man (~1.7 m), so three to four estados equals approximately 5–7 meters. 'Overo/a' denotes a dappled or piebald coloring pattern, here applied to the mottled black-and-white appearance of certain canes.

results.title_inferred: On the tunay plant and the reed of Nicaragua
results.original_text:
CAPITULO LXXXVI
De la tunay

La tunay es una mata del linaje de caña, con sus ñudos a trechos; crece de uno a dos estados de alto; la hoja es larga un palmo y ancha en proporción, verde, lisa y tiesa como la de la caña común. Tiene los cañutos llenos de agua muy fría y medicinal para algunas enfermedades. Produce una flor muy vistosa de la grandeza y color de la rosa, salvo que no tiene más de un orden de hojas en torno de un botoncillo amarillo y no da de sí algún olor.

CAPITULO LXXXVII
Del carrizo de Nicaragua

En las costas de la Mar del Sur de la provincia de Nicaragua nace un género particular de carrizo, el cual es del grosor del ordinario, salvo que es macizo, muy liso y correoso, está desnudo de cáscara y tiene de largo cada cañuto, de un ñudo a otro, de tres a cuatro palmos; no se levanta en alto, sino que se tiende y arrastra por la tierra, y de cada ñudo echa raíces con que se clava en ella por una parte y por otra un cogollo con mucha hoja, que se levanta derecho un estado; y estas varas o cañas que corren por el suelo, son largas de cuatro a cinco brazas y más.
results.translation:
CHAPTER LXXXVI
On the tunay

The tunay is a shrub of the reed family [linaje de caña], with nodes [ñudos] at intervals; it grows from one to two fathoms [estados] in height; the leaf is a hand-span [palmo] in length and proportionately wide, green, smooth, and stiff like that of the common reed. Its internodes [cañutos] are filled with very cold water that is medicinal for certain ailments. It produces a very showy flower of the size and color of a rose, except that it has only a single row of petals arranged around a small yellow button [botoncillo], and gives off no fragrance whatsoever.

CHAPTER LXXXVII
On the reed of Nicaragua

On the coasts of the South Sea [Mar del Sur] in the province of Nicaragua there grows a particular kind of reed [carrizo], which is of the same thickness as the ordinary reed, except that it is solid [macizo], very smooth, and tough [correoso], is bare of bark [cáscara], and each internode [cañuto], from one node to the next, measures three to four hand-spans [palmos] in length. It does not grow upright but rather spreads and creeps along the ground; from each node it sends out roots by which it anchors itself into the soil, and on the other side puts forth a shoot [cogollo] with abundant foliage that rises straight up to the height of one fathom [estado]. These stems or canes that run along the ground reach four to five fathoms [brazas] in length and more.

entities_mentioned.name: tunay
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Possibly a species of Heliconia, Canna, or a related monocot (family Heliconiaceae or Cannaceae); the description of rose-like single-whorled flowers, reed-like jointed stems, and water-filled internodes is suggestive but not definitive
entities_mentioned.notes: Cobo notes the water held in the internodes as medicinally cold and curative for unspecified ailments. The single row of petals around a yellow center and lack of scent may point toward a Heliconia or Canna species, though neither is a perfect match. The name 'tunay' does not appear in standard modern botanical synonymies with certainty.

entities_mentioned.name: carrizo de Nicaragua
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Possibly a stoloniferous grass, potentially Phragmites australis or more likely a species of Arundo or a creeping bambusoid grass native to Central America; the prostrate, stoloniferous habit with nodes rooting into the ground is distinctive
entities_mentioned.notes: Described as solid (not hollow), smooth, tough, lacking bark, with internodes 3–4 hand-spans long, prostrate and rooting at nodes, with erect leafy shoots one fathom tall, and total stem length of 4–5+ fathoms. The solid, creeping habit distinguishes it from common hollow reeds. Possibly Gynerium sagittatum or a related taxon, though that species is typically erect.

results.translator_notes: Several OCR artifacts corrected silently: 'botonciUo' → 'botoncillo'; 'd^e' → 'de'; 'cog' → 'con'; 'hw¬ zas' → 'brazas'; 'alte' → 'alto'. The word 'estado' (pl. 'estados') is a Spanish unit of height roughly equivalent to a fathom or adult body-height (~1.7 m); 'palmo' is a hand-span (~20 cm); 'braza' is approximately 1.67 m. These are retained in brackets for precision. The medical claim for the tunay's internode water is not elaborated upon; no specific preparation method or ailment is named beyond 'certain ailments [algunas enfermedades]'.

results.translator_notes:
{
  "title_inferred": "On the Trees of the Indies in General (Book VI, Chapter 1, Part 1)",
  "original_text": "CAPITULO PRIMERO

De los árboles de las Indias en común

Son tantos los géneros y especies de árboles, así frutales como infructíferos y silvestres que se hallan en este Nuevo Mundo, que faltan nombres con que significarlos; porque, demás de muchos que son generales y nacen en todas partes, en cada provincia se hallan nuevas plantas no vistas ni sabidas antes; y lo que pone gran admiración, es ver que siendo tierra ésta de tantos bosques y selvas, sean tan pocas las plantas que se hallan en ellas de los géneros de las de Europa, no sólo de las que allá son hortenses, pero ni aun de las silvestres. Fuera de ser muchas las diferencias de árboles que hay, son en tanta cantidad, que en partes se hallan selvas y montañas continuadas, no por veinte o treinta leguas, sino por quinientas, por ochocientas y por más de mil leguas; de manera que vienen a ser inhabitables por la gran espesura de los bosques y arcabucos, sacando la ribera de los ríos, adonde se hallan algunas poblaciones de indios. De lo cual es la causa ser de ordinario la tierra donde se crían esas montañas caliente y húmeda con exceso, y ser las aguas tan continuas, que la mayor parte del año no cesa de llover; porque hay tierras desta calidad donde no se pasa día en todo el año sin que llueva, por lo cual está siempre la tierra bañada de agua y empantanada. En las tales tierras, es mucho mayor la montaña y boscaje que se hace en lo llano que en las sierras y tierra doblada; porque se hallan montañas que ocupan tan gran cantidad de tierra llana, que los que deste reino del Perú navegaron el río Marañón hasta la mar del Norte, afirmaban ser todas sus riberas, hasta llegar a las costas del Norte, por una y otra banda, de cerradas selvas y montañas, y lo que navegaron no fue menos que mil quinientas leguas. Las sierras y tierras dobladas que crían montaña espesa, son de temple caliente como lo llan

results.title_inferred: On the General Properties of New World Plants and Trees, with a Classification Scheme
results.original_text:
atribuir al clima de la tierra, respecto de ser húmeda y caliente y muy viciosa; porque no sólo se experimenta esto en la tierra de las calidades dichas, sino es las que son de varios temples. Lo que yo siento es que debe de ayudar algo la fertilidad y uniformidad del clima; porque vemos que los que mudan la hoja no se despojan del todo della o la pierden por muy breve tiempo, como experimentamos en los árboles traídos de España, que mudan la hoja. La cuarta, que todos los árboles en estas Indias, así los naturales de acá como los traídos de España, no echan las raíces hondas para abajo, sino al soslayo y muy someras sobre la haz de la tierra.

Demás de lo dicho, conviene advertir en este lugar, que así como lo que destas Indias cae debajo de la tórrida zona se diferencia mucho en temple de lo que cae fuera della, así también, las plantas que son comunes y generales en todas las regiones de la tórrida zona, comúnmente no se hallan en las tierras que caen en la zona templada; y lo mismo acaece a los árboles destas regiones, que no se hallaron en la tórrida zona, sino muy pocos dellos; no porque no haya en esta media región varios temples aparejados para toda suerte de plantas, sino por la poca comunicación y trato que tenían entre sí los indios de diferentes provincias; si bien es verdad que, dado que dentro de los trópicos se hallan temples acomodados para todas las plantas que hasta ahora han traído los españoles de las zonas templadas, con todo eso, en las provincias que caen dentro dellas no se hallan temples aparejados para las plantas que nacen debajo de la tórrida zona, que piden temperamento caliente y húmedo. Por donde, las tierras que son más abundantes de las frutas naturales de Indias, son las que caen dentro de los trópicos, y las que caen fuera dellos, tanto más faltas de frutas cuanto más se acercan a los polos; como se experimenta en el reino de Chile, donde, por tener la altura polar de España, aunque en contrario hemisferio, no nacen las frutas naturales de Indias que piden tierras calientes. Por lo cual, cuando en el discurso desta Historia dijéremos de alguna planta que es general en todas las Indias, se ha de entender solamente de las tierras que caen dentro de los trópicos.

Item, experimentamos en los árboles de una misma especie que nacen en diferentes climas, muy grande variedad no sólo en la grandeza del árbol, de su hoja y fruto, sino hasta en la calidad de esa misma fruta y de la madera del tal árbol; porque los de unas tierras crecen más altos, hacen mayor hoja y llevan la fruta más crecida y sabrosa que los que nacen en otras partes; y no sólo se diferencian en esto, sino también en la calidad de su madera, que los unos la crían más recia y de más dura que los otros, como se experimenta en las maderas nacidas en tierras pedregosas, que son mejores que las que de la misma especie nacen en ciénegas y lugares anegadizos.

Todas las frutas que son naturales deste Nuevo Mundo tienen por propiedad, generalmente hablando, ser frías y húmedas, por donde muchas dellas son indigestas y poco sanas; lo cual procede de ser la tierra muy húmeda y madurar casi todas ellas en tiempo de invierno; y esto nace de ser estas frutas de tal calidad, que cuando verdes no están agrias ni acedas como las de Europa, sino ásperas y secas; y así, su madurez es templar aquella sequedad con la humedad del invierno. Esto se verifica en las guayabas, las cuales, en el verano, comienzan a brotar, y como se va llegando el invierno, van madurando; pero a cualquiera tiempo que uno las pruebe, estando sin sazón, experimentará esto que digo, que no sentirá en ellas ninguna acedía que le cause molestia o le dé dentera, sino que se pueden comer, aunque sin aquel gusto que tienen cuando maduras. Y lo mismo pasa en las paltas y en las demás frutas indianas, lo cual no se halla en las de Europa; las cuales, si se prueban verdes y por sazonar, están muy agrias, como se ve en las ciruelas, manzanas, uvas y las demás, por lo cual han menester el calor y sequedad del verano para madurar. Todos los árboles frutales de las Indias son en muchas partes silvestres, la fruta de los cuales no se diferencia en calidad de la que llevan los árboles hortenses, porque los indios hacían muy poco beneficio a los que criaban en sus huertas, por no haber tenido conocimiento del arte de injerir unos en otros; mas después que los españoles habitan esta tierra, han hecho varios injertos así de unos árboles de la tierra con otros, como destos con los de Castilla, con que las frutas se han mejorado mucho. Muchísimos destos árboles indianos, así de los frutales como de los que no llevan fruto, son de madera excelente para todo género de fábricas.

Para mayor distinción y claridad de lo que se escribe en este libro, dividiré todos los árboles en cinco clases: en la primera irán los árboles frutales, y déstos, en primer lugar, aquellos cuyas frutas tienen la sustancia comestible sobre el hueso o pepita, y después los que la tienen encerrada dentro dél, al modo de nuestras almendras y castañas. A la segunda clase pertenecen todos los que no llevan otro fruto más que hermosas flores; a la tercera, los que son provechosos por sus resinas, gomas, cortezas y raíces y otras virtudes que tienen medicinales; a la cuarta, los que sirven sólo con sus maderas preciosas; y, finalmente, a la quinta, los demás árboles que sólo son buenos para leña y carbón; si bien estos últimos no es posible contarlos todos, más que aquellos que son más conocidos y usuales; pues ni aun de los frutales pueden dejar de quedarse muchos que no habrán venido a mi noticia.
results.translation:
[This passage continues from a prior discussion of why trees in the Indies do not shed their leaves, attributing this partly to the climate.]

...to attribute [this phenomenon] to the climate of the land, given that it is humid, warm, and very fertile; for one observes this not only in lands of the said qualities, but also in those of various temperaments [i.e., climatic conditions]. My own view is that the fertility and uniformity of the climate must contribute somewhat; for we see that those trees which do shed their leaves do not strip themselves entirely of them, or lose them only for a very brief time, as we observe in trees brought from Spain that do shed their leaves. The fourth [observation] is that all trees in these Indies—both those native to this land and those brought from Spain—do not send their roots deep downward, but rather laterally and very shallowly near the surface of the earth.

Beyond what has been said, it is worth noting here that, just as that part of these Indies falling within the Torrid Zone differs greatly in temperament from that which falls outside it, so too the plants that are common and widespread throughout all regions of the Torrid Zone are generally not found in the lands that fall within the Temperate Zone. The same holds true for the trees of those [temperate] regions, which were found only very rarely in the Torrid Zone—not because this intermediate region lacks the variety of climates suited to all manner of plants, but rather on account of the little communication and commerce that the Indians of different provinces had with one another. It is indeed true, however, that while within the tropics one finds climates suited to all the plants that the Spaniards have thus far brought from the temperate zones, nonetheless in the provinces that fall within those tropics there are no climates suited to the plants that grow under the Torrid Zone proper and that require a warm and humid temperament. Thus, the lands most abundantly supplied with the native fruits of the Indies are those that fall within the tropics, and those that fall outside the tropics are proportionally more lacking in fruits the closer they approach the poles—as is observed in the kingdom of Chile, which, having the same polar elevation [latitude] as Spain though in the opposite hemisphere, does not produce the native fruits of the Indies that require warm lands. For this reason, when in the course of this History we say of some plant that it is found throughout all the Indies, this is to be understood only of those lands that fall within the tropics.

Furthermore, we observe in trees of the same species growing in different climates a very great variety not only in the size of the tree, its leaves, and its fruit, but also in the very quality of that fruit and of the timber of the tree in question; for those of some lands grow taller, bear larger leaves, and produce fruit that is larger and more flavorful than those growing in other places. And they differ not only in this, but also in the quality of their timber, some producing wood that is harder and more durable than others—as is observed in timbers grown on stony ground, which are better than those of the same species grown in swamps and waterlogged places.

All the fruits native to this New World have the general property of being cold and humid [in the Galenic humoral sense], by reason of which many of them are indigestible and unwholesome. This proceeds from the land being very humid and from nearly all of them ripening in the winter season. This in turn arises from these fruits being of such a nature that when unripe they are not sour or tart as those of Europe are, but rather rough and dry; and thus their ripening consists in tempering that dryness with the humidity of winter. This is verified in the guavas [guayabas], which begin to bud in summer and, as winter approaches, gradually ripen. But at whatever time one might taste them while still unripe, one will find what I describe: that one will detect in them no tartness that causes discomfort or sets the teeth on edge, and that they may indeed be eaten, though without the pleasure they afford when ripe. The same is true of the paltas [avocados] and the other Indian fruits—which is not the case with those of Europe, which, if tasted while green and unripe, are very sour, as is seen with plums, apples, grapes, and the rest; for this reason the latter require the heat and dryness of summer in order to ripen.

All the fruit-bearing trees of the Indies grow wild in many places, and the fruit of wild trees does not differ in quality from that borne by cultivated [hortense] trees, because the Indians did very little to improve those they grew in their orchards, not having had any knowledge of the art of grafting one onto another. But since the Spaniards have inhabited this land, they have made various grafts both of native trees with other native trees and of the latter with Castilian trees, by means of which the fruits have greatly improved. A very great many of these Indian trees—both the fruit-bearing and those that bear no fruit—are of excellent timber for all manner of construction.

For greater distinction and clarity in what is written in this book, I will divide all trees into five classes. In the first will go the fruit-bearing trees, and among these, in first place those whose fruits have their edible substance on the outside of the stone or seed [pip], and afterward those whose edible substance is enclosed within it, in the manner of our almonds and chestnuts. To the second class belong all those that bear nothing other than beautiful flowers. To the third belong those that are useful for their resins, gums, barks, and roots and other medicinal virtues they possess. To the fourth belong those that serve only by reason of their precious timbers. And finally, to the fifth belong the remaining trees that are good only for firewood and charcoal—although it is impossible to enumerate all of the last group, beyond those that are best known and most commonly used; for even among the fruit-bearing trees many must remain of which no account has come to my notice.

entities_mentioned.name: guayabas
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Psidium guajava (guava)
entities_mentioned.notes: Used here as an illustrative example of the author's observation that New World fruits do not become sour when unripe, unlike European fruits. No medicinal preparation described in this passage.

entities_mentioned.name: paltas
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Persea americana (avocado)
entities_mentioned.notes: Mentioned alongside guavas as an example of the same ripening phenomenon characteristic of New World fruits. No medicinal preparation described in this passage.

entities_mentioned.name: Chile (reino de)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: The Kingdom of Chile (colonial administrative region, roughly modern-day Chile)
entities_mentioned.notes: Cited as a geographic example of a temperate-latitude region where native warm-climate Indian fruits do not grow, owing to its latitude being equivalent to that of Spain.

results.translator_notes: 1. The passage opens mid-sentence, continuing an enumerated list of observations about New World trees (the 'fourth' observation about shallow roots references a numbering system established earlier in the chapter). 2. The term 'temperamento' / 'temple' is used in the Galenic climatic-humoral sense, indicating the combined quality (hot/cold, wet/dry) of a place or substance; translated as 'temperament' or 'climate' as context demands. 3. 'Tórrida zona' and 'zona templada' are the classical geographic/climatic zones (Torrid Zone = tropics; Temperate Zone = mid-latitudes); retained in translation as standard period terminology. 4. 'Calidades' similarly refers to the Galenic primary qualities (hot, cold, wet, dry). 5. 'Fábricas' in 17th-century Spanish denotes construction or building works generally, not factories. 6. The five-part classification scheme Cobo announces here—(1) fleshy-fruited trees, (2) flowering trees, (3) medicinal/resinous trees, (4) timber trees, (5) fuel trees—serves as the organizational framework for the remainder of Books V and VI. 7. Several OCR errors silently corrected: 'atrihuir' → 'atribuir'; 'raím' → 'raíces'; 'adva--tir' → 'advertir'; 'generala' → 'generales'; 'tierr#' → 'tierras'; 'h' (standalone) → 'la'; 'igi®-ñas' → 'regiones'; 'porc^lio' → 'porque no'; 'vari®»' → 'varios'; '¿|' → 'de'; 'comunicaei^' → 'comunicación'; 'indias ^' → 'indios'; 'zon^' → 'zonas'; 'pli^' → 'plantas'; 'po**' → 'por'; 'fru*tales' → 'frutales'; 'déstos' retained as written (archaic form of 'de éstos'). 8. No medicinal preparations are described in this segment; the passage is general botanical-geographic theory.

results.translator_notes:
{
  "title_inferred": "Trees of the Same Genera as Those of Spain Found in the Indies, and the Papaya",
  "original_text": "CAPITULO II

De los árboles que se hallaron en estas Indias de los mismos géneros que los de España

Muy pocos son los árboles que cuando vinieron a esta tierra los españoles hallaron en ella semejantes en especie a los de España; y ésos los más son infructíferos y silvestres. El árbol más general deste género que se halla en todas las provincias del Perú es el aliso, el cual nace en los valles templados de la Sierra, de cuya madera se gasta gran cantidad en los edificios de la ciudad del Cuzco y en otras partes donde no se alcanzan maderas más fuertes. Después del aliso es el más común en todo este reino el sauce, llamado de los indios huayaca, del cual se hallan dos diferencias: el uno es árbol alto y derecho, parecido en su talle al ciprés, y el otro, copado y redondo; pero entrambos diferentes de la mimbrera, la cual no sé yo que la haya en esta tierra. En algunas partes se planta gran copia de sauces para leña, por la brevedad con que crecen, y porque, en podándolos, vuelven presto a poblarse de nuevas ramas. A falta de otra mejor madera suelen gastar sauce en los edificios, como sucede en la ciudad de Arequipa; y si es de los troncos que las avenidas de los ríos suelen llevar a la mar, y las olas y resaca vuelven a echar a tierra, por estar curada con el agua salada, es madera de más dura; y verde, como se corta, suele entrar en las fábricas de los barcos y en otros usos, particularmente en los instrumentos del servicio de las chácaras y heredades, para lo cual en este valle de Lima son muchos los que se plantan en las dichas chácaras.

El sauce es también muy general en esta tierra, aunque se aprovechan poco dél. En todo este reino del Perú no se hallaron más que estas tres especies de árboles referidos de los que son comunes con los de España. Los demás que había en estas Indias de una misma especie con los de allá, nacen en otras

results.title_inferred: On the Papaya: Three Species, Fruit Description, and Medicinal Uses
results.original_text:
uta es muy desigual, conforme a la calidad de la tierra donde se da; la más común es como una gran cidra o mediano melón; tiene figura ahusada y está dividida en cinco o siete tajadas con otras tantas canales que hace a lo largo a manera del melón; la cáscara es amarilla, delgada y tierna como la de cidra; la carne también es amarilla que tira a roja, tierna como el melón, aunque no tan aguanosa; tiene de grueso como dos dedos, y el hueco de dentro, lleno de unas pepitas negras, redondas y tiernas, no mayores que culantro, que también se comen y tienen sabor de mastuerzo. Es la papaya fruta silvestre no muy apetitosa ni estimada.

Hay otras dos especies de papayas fuera de la referida; la una nace en las islas de Barlovento, es del tamaño de una gran naranja y de mejor gusto que la primera, y el árbol más delgado y más alto. La tercera casta de papayas se da en la tierra templada de la Sierra del Perú; el árbol echa algunas ramas a la redonda, es de menor hoja y la fruta del tamaño de limones reales, muy olorosa y de mejor sabor que las otras dos. Con la leche que sale de la papaya verde, se curan los empeines y sarna, porque quema como solimán. De la papaya grande, antes de madurar, se hace en la Nueva España una conserva como de calabaza, que es muy regalada y cordial. El nombre de papaya es tomado de la lengua de los indios de la isla Española, de la cual tomaron los españoles casi todos los nombres de las demás frutas, por ser allí donde primero las conocieron.
results.translation:
The fruit [papaya] varies greatly according to the quality of the soil where it grows. The most common variety is the size of a large citron [cidra] or a medium melon; it has a spindle-like shape and is divided into five or seven segments, with as many lengthwise grooves, in the manner of a melon. The rind is yellow, thin, and tender, like that of a citron. The flesh is likewise yellow tending toward red, tender like melon, though not as watery. It is about two fingers thick, and the inner cavity is filled with small black, round, and soft seeds no larger than coriander [culantro], which are also eaten and have a flavor of cress [mastuerzo]. The papaya is a wild fruit, not very appetizing nor highly esteemed.

There are two other species of papaya beyond the one described. One grows in the Windward Islands [islas de Barlovento]; it is the size of a large orange, of better flavor than the first, and its tree is more slender and taller. The third variety of papaya grows in the temperate lands of the Sierra of Peru; the tree puts out several branches around it, has smaller leaves, and its fruit is the size of large lemons [limones reales], very fragrant and of better flavor than the other two. The milky sap [leche] that flows from the unripe papaya cures ringworm [empeines] and mange [sarna], because it burns like sublimate of mercury [solimán]. From the large papaya, before it ripens, a preserve [conserva] is made in New Spain similar to that made from squash, which is very delicate and restorative [cordial]. The name 'papaya' is taken from the language of the Indians of Hispaniola [isla Española], from which the Spaniards took nearly all the names of the other fruits, as that was where they first came to know them.

entities_mentioned.name: papaya (primera especie / common variety)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Carica papaya L.
entities_mentioned.notes: Cobo describes the typical lowland papaya with spindle shape, five to seven ribs, yellow-red flesh, and black seeds. Described as wild and not particularly prized.

entities_mentioned.name: papaya de las islas de Barlovento (segunda especie)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Carica papaya L. (possibly a distinct cultivar or variety)
entities_mentioned.notes: Described as orange-sized, better-tasting, with a taller and more slender tree. Likely a variant of C. papaya rather than a separate species, though Cobo treats it as distinct.

entities_mentioned.name: papaya de la Sierra del Perú (tercera especie)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Vasconcellea pubescens (A.DC.) A.DC. (mountain papaya) or another Vasconcellea sp.
entities_mentioned.notes: Grows in the temperate highland zone of Peru, lemon-sized, very fragrant, branching tree with smaller leaves — characteristics consistent with Andean mountain papayas of the genus Vasconcellea (formerly Carica).

entities_mentioned.name: leche de papaya verde (milky sap of unripe papaya)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Crude papain-containing latex of Carica papaya
entities_mentioned.notes: Used medicinally against ringworm (empeines) and mange (sarna). Cobo compares its caustic action to 'solimán' (mercuric sublimate / corrosive sublimate, HgCl₂), a common reference standard for caustic substances in colonial pharmacopoeia. Modern science confirms the proteolytic enzyme papain in the latex has keratolytic and antiparasitic properties.

entities_mentioned.name: conserva de papaya (papaya preserve)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Candied/preserved unripe papaya
entities_mentioned.notes: Prepared in New Spain from large unripe papaya, described as a delicacy and 'cordial' (restorative). Comparable preparation to candied squash or citron preserves.

entities_mentioned.name: culantro
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Coriandrum sativum L. (coriander) or possibly Eryngium foetidum L.
entities_mentioned.notes: Used as a size reference for papaya seeds. In colonial Spanish American usage 'culantro' could refer to either common coriander or to the native Eryngium foetidum; here it functions as a familiar size comparator.

entities_mentioned.name: mastuerzo
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Lepidium sativum L. (garden cress) or related Lepidium/Nasturtium sp.
entities_mentioned.notes: Flavor comparison for papaya seeds — described as tasting of mastuerzo, a peppery cress. Standard European culinary reference in colonial-era texts.

entities_mentioned.name: solimán
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Mercuric chloride (HgCl₂) / corrosive sublimate
entities_mentioned.notes: A caustic mineral substance widely used in 17th-century European and colonial medicine; cited here as an analogy for the burning/caustic action of raw papaya latex on skin conditions.

results.translator_notes: 1. The segment begins mid-sentence ('uta es muy desigual…'), indicating this is a continuation from a preceding passage — 'uta' is the tail of 'fruta' cut by the OCR page boundary. The cleaned text treats it as beginning of a topical block on papaya. 2. 'Solimán' is a period-specific term for corrosive sublimate (mercuric chloride), important for understanding the pharmacological comparison being made. 3. 'Cordial' in the period sense means a restorative or heart-strengthening preparation, not merely pleasant-tasting. 4. Cobo's three-species taxonomy of papaya is notable: modern botany distinguishes lowland Carica papaya from the highland Vasconcellea genus, validating his empirical observation of a distinct highland Peruvian species. 5. The note on the Taíno (Hispaniolan) linguistic origin of 'papaya' is historically accurate and reflects early Spanish reliance on Caribbean indigenous vocabulary for American flora.

results.title_inferred: The Guanábano Tree and Its Fruit
results.original_text:
CAPITULO IV
Del guanábano

La fruta llamada guanábana en lengua de la isla Española da el nombre de guanábano al árbol que la produce; el cual es mediano, de la grandeza de un durazno, copado y de mejor parecer que su fruta merece. Hace la hoja poco mayor que la del naranjo, lisa y tiesa, no tan puntiaguda, y de verde más oscuro. La fruta es tan grande como una piña y algunas como medianos melones, con la corteza delgada y tierna y de un verde que tira a amarillo, con unas puntillas sobresalientes a manera de escamas, pero llanas y lisas como la de la piña. La carne de dentro es blanca en unas y en otras amarilla, muy blanda, correosa y de mucho jugo algo agrio, y sus pepitas son como de calabaza. Es fruta silvestre, grosera y malsana, no de agradable gusto ni olor.
results.translation:
CHAPTER IV
On the Guanábano

The fruit called guanábana in the language of the island of Hispaniola [Isla Española] gives its name to the tree that produces it. The tree is of medium size, roughly as large as a peach tree [durazno], with a spreading crown and of finer appearance than its fruit deserves. Its leaf is slightly larger than that of the orange tree, smooth and stiff, not as sharply pointed, and of a darker green. The fruit is as large as a pineapple [piña], and some are as large as medium-sized melons, with a thin and tender skin of a green color tending toward yellow, with small protruding points in the manner of scales, yet flat and smooth like those of the pineapple. The flesh inside is white in some specimens and yellow in others, very soft, fibrous [correosa], and full of somewhat tart juice; its seeds are like those of a gourd [calabaza]. It is a wild, coarse, and unwholesome fruit, neither of agreeable taste nor smell.

entities_mentioned.name: guanábana / guanábano
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Annona muricata (soursop)
entities_mentioned.notes: Cobo notes the name derives from the language of Hispaniola. His description—large spiny-scaled green fruit with white or yellow fibrous pulp, tart juice, and gourd-like seeds—is consistent with Annona muricata. He characterizes it as wild, coarse, and unwholesome ('malsana'), which contrasts with later widespread culinary and medicinal use of this species.

results.translator_notes: The word 'correosa' (fibrous, leathery, chewy) is preserved in the translation as 'fibrous' with the original in brackets for precision. Cobo's negative assessment of the fruit as 'malsana' (unwholesome/unhealthy) is notable given that soursop is today widely consumed and attributed various medicinal properties in folk medicine. No specific medicinal preparation is described in this segment. OCR artifact 'v de nn verde' silently corrected to 'y de un verde.'

results.title_inferred: On the Anona (Custard Apple) Tree and Its Fruit
results.original_text:
CAPITULO V

De las anonas

El árbol que lleva las anonas es mediano, de la grandeza de un naranjo, bien hecho y copado; la hoja se parece algo a la del saúco, pero es más lisa y tiesa; echa unas flores blancas y de tres puntas, parecidas a las de las peras. La fruta es del tamaño de una piña mediana o como una pera bergamota, si bien hay algunas menores, y en lo exterior de la misma forma que la piña, porque es verde, ahusada y con aquella disposición de la superficie desigual y escamosa que tiene la piña. Su cáscara es delgada y tierna, de manera que estando bien madura, con apretarla blandamente con los dedos, se parte. La carne está maciza y es muy blanca y blanda y toda llena de unas pepitas negras, duras y lisas, poco menores que piñones; es de bueno y regalado gusto, aunque no muy sana, porque engendra ventosidades. Sus pepitas detienen las cámaras.
results.translation:
CHAPTER V

On the Anonas

The tree that bears the anonas [custard apples] is of medium size, comparable to an orange tree, well-formed and full-crowned. Its leaf resembles somewhat that of the elder tree [saúco], but is smoother and stiffer. It produces white, three-pointed flowers, similar to those of the pear tree. The fruit is the size of a medium pineapple, or like a bergamot pear, though some are smaller; in outward appearance it resembles the pineapple, being green, spindle-shaped, and having that characteristic uneven, scaly surface which the pineapple has. Its skin is thin and tender, so that when the fruit is fully ripe, a gentle squeeze with the fingers is enough to split it open. The flesh is dense, very white and soft, and entirely filled with small black seeds, hard and smooth, somewhat smaller than pine nuts [piñones]. It is of good and pleasing taste, though not very wholesome, as it generates flatulence [ventosidades]. Its seeds [pepitas] check looseness of the bowels [detienen las cámaras].

entities_mentioned.name: anona
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Annona squamosa (sugar apple / sweetsop) or possibly Annona reticulata (custard apple); the scaly, spindle-shaped description is most consistent with Annona squamosa
entities_mentioned.notes: Cobo distinguishes anonas as a group; the morphological description here—green, scaly, spindle-shaped fruit with dense white flesh and black seeds—fits Annona squamosa most closely, though Annona cherimola and A. reticulata cannot be entirely ruled out without further context from adjacent chapters.

entities_mentioned.name: saúco
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Sambucus nigra (elder tree) or a New World elder species used for comparison
entities_mentioned.notes: Used here only as a morphological comparator for the leaf shape, not as a subject of the chapter.

entities_mentioned.name: pera bergamota
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Bergamot pear (a European pear variety, Pyrus communis cv.)
entities_mentioned.notes: Used as a size/shape comparator for the fruit; not a subject of the chapter.

results.translator_notes: Medical/pharmacological extractions: (1) Condition treated: looseness of the bowels / diarrhea [cámaras]; Part used: seeds [pepitas]; Preparation method: not specified, presumably ingested. (2) The text notes the fruit itself is not highly wholesome because it engenders flatulence [ventosidades], a standard humoral-medical caution. The OCR error 'enjendra' has been silently corrected to 'engendra'; 'bay' corrected to 'hay'. The term 'cámaras' in this period refers specifically to loose bowel movements or diarrhea, and 'detienen las cámaras' means 'they check / bind the bowels,' i.e., the seeds have an astringent/antidiarrheal effect.

De la chirimoya

La fruta llamada chirimoya es especie de anona, pero hácele mucha ventaja. El árbol es de la grandeza del moral; la hoja, mayor que la de la anona, más ancha que de moral y de un verde oscuro; no se despoja della el árbol en todo el año, y así está siempre verde, muy copado y hace agradable sombra. No tiene tantas pepitas como la anona y son algo diferentes y que se despiden más fácilmente de la pulpa, y su cáscara también es más lisa que la de la anona. results.title_inferred: Properties and Uses of the Palta (Avocado): Medicinal, Culinary, and Timber Uses
results.original_text: mas se parecen a las brevas; unas son redondas y otras prolongadas, y tienen la cáscara tan sutil y delicada como la de las ciruelas. En algunas partes preparan la palta verde hecha pequeñas tajadas y echadas en salmuera, para suplir la falta de aceitunas. El hueso de la palta, dado a beber en polvos con agua de llantén o almacigada y acerada, estanca las cámaras. El aceite que dél se saca es bueno para curar los empeines; y si con el zumo deste hueso se tiñe algún lienzo y se oscurece en él alguna señal, no se quita jamás el color que toma, que es rojo. La madera del árbol sirve para fábricas y para leña, que para entrambas cosas es buena. La palta se llama así en la lengua general del Perú, que en la mayor parte de las Indias la nombran aguacate, que es el nombre que le dan los indios de la isla Española.
results.translation: they more closely resemble figs [brevas]; some are round and others elongated, and they have a skin as fine and delicate as that of plums. In some places the unripe palta is prepared by cutting it into small slices and placing them in brine, to serve as a substitute for olives. The pit [hueso] of the palta, given as a drink in powdered form with plantain water [agua de llantén], or with mastic water [almacigada] and steel-infused water [acerada], stanches fluxes [cámaras, i.e., diarrhea/dysentery]. The oil extracted from it is good for curing ringworm [empeines]; and if the juice of this pit is used to dye any piece of linen and a mark is made upon it, the color it takes—which is red—can never be removed. The wood of the tree serves for construction and for firewood, being good for both purposes. The palta is so called in the general language [lingua franca] of Peru; throughout most of the Indies it is called aguacate, which is the name given to it by the Indians of Hispaniola.

entities_mentioned.name: palta / aguacate
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Persea americana (avocado)
entities_mentioned.notes: Cobo notes both names: 'palta' from Quechua (Peruvian general language) and 'aguacate' from the Nahuatl/Taíno tradition as used in Hispaniola and most of the Indies. The fruit, pit oil, and pit powder are all described with distinct uses.

entities_mentioned.name: agua de llantén
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Plantago major or related Plantago spp. (common plantain) infusion
entities_mentioned.notes: Plantain-water was a common astringent vehicle in colonial Spanish medicine, used here as a solvent for the powdered avocado pit to treat diarrhea/dysentery.

entities_mentioned.name: agua almacigada
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Pistacia lentiscus (mastic) infusion
entities_mentioned.notes: Mastic-infused water, a standard astringent preparation in Galenic and colonial pharmacopoeias, used here as an alternative vehicle for the pit powder in treating fluxes.

entities_mentioned.name: agua acerada
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Steel-infused water (chalybeate water)
entities_mentioned.notes: A preparation made by steeping iron or steel in water, believed to be tonic and astringent; commonly prescribed in 17th-century medicine for diarrhea and weakness.

entities_mentioned.name: empeines
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Ringworm / tinea or eczematous skin conditions
entities_mentioned.notes: The term 'empeines' in colonial Spanish medical usage could refer to ringworm, impetigo, or other chronic skin eruptions. Avocado oil as a topical remedy.
