results.title_inferred: The Mamey: Description, Varieties, and Medicinal Uses
results.original_text:
CAPITULO IX
Del mamey

El nombre de mamey es tomado de la isla Española, y comprende al árbol y a la fruta. Hállanse cuatro diferencias de mameyes: el uno es general, y los otros particulares de varias provincias donde se hallaron. El mamey común y ordinario es árbol grande como un mediano nogal, muy bien hecho, copado, verde y de muy agradable parecer; la hoja se parece a la del naranjo, pero es más lisa y tiesa y de verde más claro por de fuera que por de dentro; la fruta es redonda y del tamaño de una granada mediana; la cáscara, rubia oscura, que tira a pardo, un poco más delgada y tiesa que la de la granada. Tiene dentro uno o dos huesos de hechura y color de bellotas de encina, pero cada uno tres veces mayor que una bellota, cubierto de una cáscara muy lisa, más gruesa y dura que la de la bellota, excepto una lista que tiene de punta a punta, que será como de la cuarta parte de la superficie, que no se cubre con aquella cascarilla lisa, sino que se queda blanca, algo espesa y dura. El meollo que tiene dentro este hueso es blanco y tierno como el de la castaña, pero amargo y no comestible. Por ser estos huesos tan hermosos, suelen aprovecharse dellos para tabaqueros. La carne del mamey es lo que queda entre la cáscara y el hueso, que tendrá un dedo y más de grueso; y es tan parecida a la carne de membrillo en el color y blandura, que con facilidad se engañará una persona antes de probarla, si se la ponen delante cortada en tajadas. El sabor es dulce y regalado y tira algo al de melocotón; por lo cual es en todas las Indias el mamey fruta muy estimada. Nace solamente en tierras yuncas. Suelen echar su pepita en el chocolate y también es medicinal y muy eficaz para las ayudas, pero de echar en muy poca cantidad.
results.translation:
CHAPTER IX
On the Mamey

The name mamey is taken from the island of Hispaniola and refers to both the tree and its fruit. Four varieties of mamey are found: one is common and widespread, and the others are particular to the various provinces where they were discovered. The common and ordinary mamey is a large tree, comparable in size to a medium walnut tree, very well formed, full-crowned, green, and of very pleasant appearance. Its leaf resembles that of the orange tree, but is smoother and stiffer, and of a lighter green on the outside than on the inside. The fruit is round and about the size of a medium pomegranate; the rind is a dark amber tending toward brown [pardo], somewhat thinner and stiffer than that of a pomegranate. Inside it contains one or two seeds [huesos, lit. 'stones'] shaped and colored like acorns of the holm oak, but each one three times larger than an acorn, covered with a very smooth shell, thicker and harder than that of an acorn — except for a stripe running from tip to tip covering roughly a quarter of the surface, which is not covered by that smooth shell but instead remains white, somewhat thick and hard. The kernel [meollo] inside this seed is white and tender like that of a chestnut, but bitter and inedible. Because these seeds are so handsome, they are commonly made use of as tobacco boxes [tabaqueros]. The flesh of the mamey is what lies between the rind and the seed, and it may be a finger's breadth or more in thickness. It so closely resembles the flesh of a quince in color and softness that a person could easily be deceived before tasting it, if slices were set before them. The flavor is sweet and delicate, tending somewhat toward that of a peach; for this reason the mamey is a highly esteemed fruit throughout all the Indies. It grows only in lowland [tierras yuncas] regions. The seed [pepita] is commonly added to chocolate, and it is also medicinal and very efficacious as an enema [ayuda], though it must be used in very small quantities.

entities_mentioned.name: mamey (common/ordinary)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Mammea americana (mamey apple / tropical apricot) or possibly Pouteria sapota (mamey sapote); context and Hispaniolan origin favor Mammea americana
entities_mentioned.notes: Cobo notes four varieties; the common one described here is likely Mammea americana, native to the Caribbean. Pouteria sapota is a distinct Mesoamerican species sometimes called mamey sapote and could be among the 'particular' regional varieties. The physical description — rind color, seed shape, white stripe on seed, peach-like flavor — is broadly consistent with Mammea americana.

entities_mentioned.name: pepita de mamey (mamey seed)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Seed of Mammea americana or Pouteria sapota
entities_mentioned.notes: Used as an additive to chocolate and described as medicinally efficacious as an enema [ayuda] in small quantities. The seed kernel of Mammea americana is known to contain coumarins and bitter compounds with documented purgative/toxic properties, consistent with Cobo's warning to use it sparingly.
entities_mentioned.condition_treated: Used in enemas [ayudas]; implicitly laxative or purgative
entities_mentioned.preparation_method: Added in small quantity, presumably ground or steeped; also added to chocolate

results.translator_notes: 1. 'Ayudas' is the period Spanish term for medicinal enemas or clysters; rendered as 'enema' with the original in brackets. 2. 'Tierras yuncas' refers to warm lowland coastal or valley regions (yunga/yunka in Andean terminology), indicating this tree's climatic restriction. 3. 'Tabaqueros' here means small boxes or containers used to carry tobacco, not tobacco growers; the beautiful seeds were repurposed as decorative containers. 4. OCR error 'becliura' in original corrected to 'hechura' (shape/form) without ambiguity. 5. The four-variety taxonomy Cobo mentions is not elaborated in this chapter; other chapters in Books IV–VI may address the regional varieties.

results.title_inferred: Mamey de Cartagena, Níspero, and Lúcuma: Descriptions and Uses
results.original_text:
CAPITULO X

Del mamey de Cartagena

El mamey de Cartagena, que dondequiera que se da conserva este nombre, por haberse llevado de aquella provincia, aunque es muy parecido al mamey del capítulo pasado, se distingue de él en especie. Es este árbol el más hermoso y de mejor parecer de cuantos yo he visto propios de las Indias. Es de la grandeza de un nogal, muy poblado de hoja, bien hecho y copado. La hoja es también muy hermosa, algo parecida a la de la cidra, pero más bien hecha; tiene de largo un palmo y cinco dedos de ancho, llana, con muy poca canal, tiesa y muy lisa, gruesa, llena de unas puntillas blancas que del lomo corren a las márgenes; de más claro verde por de fuera que por de dentro; ensangóstase un poco hacia la punta y pezón, y por las orillas es algo retorcida. La fruta tiene el mismo nombre que el árbol; es redonda y amarilla, del tamaño de un membrillo; la cáscara, delgada y menos tiesa que la del otro mamey; en lo interior tiene dos huesos de la hechura y color que los del primero; lo restante entre la cáscara y los huesos es de una pulpa amarilla, tierna y jugosa, de regalado sabor, algo parecida al melocotón, así en el gusto como en el color; es dulce con un agrito apetitoso.

Muy semejante al mamey de Cartagena es otro que yo comí en Sonsonate, diócesis de Guatemala; pero no hay duda sino que es casta distinta. Es tan grande como una buena cidra, y como un mediano melón; tiene dentro dos o tres huesos del tamaño cada uno de un huevo de gallina, no redondos sino chatos, muy ásperos, de un color oscuro como de tierra. La carne de esta fruta es amarilla, tiesa y de buen gusto, la cual suele comerse partida en rajas, y echadas a remojar en vino, y también en guisado. A juicio de muchos este mamey y el de Cartagena son las mejores frutas de las naturales de Indias.

Del linaje y predicamento de mameyes se reduce la fruta que los españoles llaman níspero. Es natural de la Nueva España, si bien se da ya en Tierra Firme y en otras partes. Es el níspero del tamaño de una mediana pera bergamota, y de figura ahusada como ella; tiene dentro tres pepitas del tamaño de las de la anona, y algunos nísperos no tienen más de una. Es fruta muy dulce y regalada y que muchos la prefieren a los otros mameyes.

CAPITULO XI

De la lúcuma

La fruta que en la lengua general del Perú se dice lucma y se asemeja mucho a los mameyes, añadiéndole una letra nosotros la nombramos lúcuma, y al árbol que la produce, lúcumo; el cual es muy parecido al mamey, del grandor de un moral, muy copado y poblado de hoja, mayormente en los cogollos y extremos della; las ramas las tienen muy juntas y apiñadas y levantadas hacia la punta de la rama. Es el lúcumo de buen parecer y agradable a la vista, que más por su hermosura que por su fruta lo suelen plantar en las huertas. La hoja es muy hermosa, de un verde oscuro, tamaña como la del naranjo, pero de diferente forma, porque es de más cuerpo, más tiesa, no tan acanalada, y desde el pico se va ensanchando hasta cerca de la punta, la cual es muy roma. La fruta es de la grandeza de una granada mediana, redonda y con una punta en la parte alta; tiene la cáscara delgada y tierna, de suerte que se come sin mondarla, y aunque de sazón, es por de fuera de un color entre verde y amarillo; su hueso es muy parecido en la lisura, grandor y figura a una castaña, si bien su cáscara es más dura que la de la castaña; el meollo que tiene dentro es también semejante al de la castaña y se puede comer asado, mas comúnmente no se come, por ser desabrido. La carne de la lúcuma es muy amarilla, tiesa y sin jugo y algo ahogadiza, no de sabor apetecible, por lo cual no es fruta de estima. Hállanse algunas lúcumas muy grandes, mayores que membrillos, algo ahusadas, por de fuera algo parduscas, y en lo interior, más jugosas. El cocimiento de la lúcuma verde en ayunas es remedio contra el mal del valle.
results.translation:
CHAPTER X

On the Mamey of Cartagena

The mamey of Cartagena [Mammea americana or Pouteria sapota, var.], which wherever it grows retains this name on account of having been brought from that province, although it greatly resembles the mamey of the preceding chapter, is nonetheless a distinct species. This tree is the most beautiful and handsome of all those I have seen native to the Indies. It is the size of a walnut tree [nogal], very full of leaves, well-formed and spreading. The leaf is also very beautiful, somewhat resembling that of the citron [cidra], but better proportioned; it measures a span in length and five fingers in width, flat, with very little channeling, stiff and very smooth, thick, covered with small white dots that run from the midrib to the margins; the upper surface is a brighter green than the underside; it narrows slightly toward the tip and the stem, and along the edges it is somewhat curled. The fruit bears the same name as the tree; it is round and yellow, the size of a quince; the rind is thin and less firm than that of the other mamey; inside it contains two seeds [huesos] of the same shape and color as those of the first mamey; the remainder between the rind and the seeds is a yellow pulp, tender and juicy, of delightful flavor, somewhat resembling the peach both in taste and in color; it is sweet with an appetizing slight tartness.

Very similar to the mamey of Cartagena is another that I ate in Sonsonate, in the diocese of Guatemala; but there is no doubt that it is a distinct variety [casta]. It is as large as a good-sized citron and like a medium melon; inside it has two or three seeds, each the size of a hen's egg, not round but flat, very rough, of a dark earth-colored hue. The flesh of this fruit is yellow, firm, and of good flavor; it is customarily eaten sliced into wedges [rajas] and soaked in wine, and also in cooked preparations [guisado]. In the judgment of many, this mamey and that of Cartagena are the finest fruits native to the Indies.

Of the same lineage and category [linaje y predicamento] as the mameyes is the fruit that the Spaniards call níspero [sapodilla]. It is native to New Spain, although it now grows in Tierra Firme and other regions as well. The níspero is the size of a medium bergamot pear [pera bergamota] and of a similarly elongated shape; inside it has three seeds the size of those of the annona [anona], and some nísperos have only one. It is a very sweet and delightful fruit, which many prefer to the other mameyes.

CHAPTER XI

On the Lúcuma

The fruit which in the general language of Peru is called lucma, and which greatly resembles the mameyes—we add a letter and call it lúcuma, and the tree that produces it lúcumo. This tree closely resembles the mamey, is the size of a mulberry tree [moral], very spreading and full of leaves, especially at the new growth and tips of the branches; the branches grow very close together, clustered and raised toward the tips. The lúcumo is handsome and pleasant to look upon, so much so that it is more commonly planted in gardens for its beauty than for its fruit. The leaf is very beautiful, of a dark green, about the size of an orange leaf, but of a different form, for it is more substantial, stiffer, less channeled, and widens from the base toward the tip, which is very blunt. The fruit is the size of a medium pomegranate [granada], round, with a point at the upper end; the rind is thin and tender, such that it is eaten without peeling, and although ripe, its exterior is a color between green and yellow; its seed closely resembles a chestnut in smoothness, size, and shape, though its shell is harder than that of the chestnut; the kernel inside also resembles that of the chestnut and may be eaten roasted, but is not commonly eaten, being insipid. The flesh of the lúcuma is very yellow, firm, and without juice, and somewhat cloying, not of an agreeable flavor, on account of which it is not a highly regarded fruit. Some lúcumas are found that are very large, bigger than quinces, somewhat elongated, brownish on the outside, and more juicy within. A decoction [cocimiento] of the unripe lúcuma, taken on an empty stomach, is a remedy against the mal del valle ["valley sickness," a regional fever or malaria-like illness].

entities_mentioned.name: mamey de Cartagena
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Mammea americana (mamey amarillo / South American mamey) or possibly Pouteria sapota
entities_mentioned.notes: Cobo distinguishes this explicitly from the mamey of the preceding chapter. The yellow flesh, peach-like flavor, and origin in Cartagena (Colombia) suggest Mammea americana, though some features also align with members of Sapotaceae. Further context from the prior chapter would sharpen identification.

entities_mentioned.name: mamey de Sonsonate (Guatemala)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Pouteria sapota or a related Sapotaceae species
entities_mentioned.notes: Described as hen's-egg-sized rough flat seeds and eaten soaked in wine or stewed; Cobo considers it a distinct variety (casta) from the Cartagena mamey.

entities_mentioned.name: níspero
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Manilkara zapota (sapodilla)
entities_mentioned.notes: Described as native to New Spain, shaped like a bergamot pear, with 1–3 seeds resembling those of annona, sweet and highly regarded. This matches sapodilla well. Note: the Spanish word 'níspero' ordinarily refers to the European medlar (Mespilus germanica), but in colonial New World usage it was commonly applied to the sapodilla.

entities_mentioned.name: lúcuma / lucma
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Pouteria lucuma (lúcuma)
entities_mentioned.notes: Name derived from Quechua lucma. Cobo's description—dark green leaves, round fruit with a pointed apex, chestnut-like seed, dry yellow flesh—matches Pouteria lucuma precisely. Grown in Peru, planted more for ornament than fruit.

results.translator_notes:
1. MEDICAL EXTRACTION — Lúcuma: Name: lúcuma (Pouteria lucuma); Modern identification: Pouteria lucuma (high confidence); Condition treated: 'mal del valle' (valley sickness — likely a febrile illness, possibly malaria or a similar endemic fever of low-lying Andean valleys); Preparation: decoction (cocimiento) of unripe lúcuma fruit, taken on an empty stomach (en ayunas).
2. 'Mal del valle' is a recurring term in colonial Andean sources for an endemic febrile or intermittent illness associated with low-altitude valleys; its precise nosological identity is debated but is often equated with malaria or a related vector-borne disease.
3. Several OCR errors were silently corrected: 'dond^' → 'dondequiera'; 'mam^' → 'mamey'; 'á' → 'él' (distinguishes de él); 'más her*moso' → 'más hermoso'; 'âeám' → 'dedos'; 'uam' → 'unas'; 'coire®' → 'corren'; 'p#r' → 'por'; 'árbcd' → 'árbol'; 'it' → 'lo'; 'Car-gena' → 'Cartagena'; 'Gii a tímala' → 'Guatemala'; 't®' → 'tan'; 'dentrt' → 'dentro'; 'redou^' → 'redondos'; 'edi@r' → 'color'; 'deíti' → 'esta'; 'fntta' → 'fruta'; 'iláman' → 'llaman'; 'kja' → 'hoja'; 'morâl' → 'moral'; 'pardiscas' → 'parduscas'; 'abusadas' → 'ahusadas'; 'hoya' → 'hoja' (leaf, not pit/hole — clear contextual correction); 'narmp' → 'naranjo'; 'porqpe' → 'porque'.
4. The Sonsonate passage has the fruit described as comparable both to a citron and to a medium melon — these may describe two different size comparisons (minimum vs. maximum range) rather than a contradiction.

  "title_inferred": "On Zapotes and Guavas: Varieties, Descriptions, and Medicinal Uses",
  "original_text": "CAPITULO XII
De los zapotes.

Los zapotes son propios de la Nueva España, adonde se hallan cinco diferencias dellos; los mejores son los colorados. El árbol que los lleva es grande y copado como el mamey, con los cogollos muy poblados de hoja; ésta es larga un palmo, lisa, tiesa, de un verde oscuro y más ancha al cabo que en medio. La fruta es del tamaño de un membrillo y aun como una grande cidra, ahusada, con la cáscara delgada y tierna; la carne, muy colorada y blanda, que tiene un sabor dulce remiso; sus pezones, muy cortos, ásperos y vellosos, de un color pardo que tira a leonado.

La segunda casta es de los zapotes negros, cuyo árbol es muy parecido en la grandeza y hoja al mamey. Es el zapote negro muy sano y que dura todo el año. En la Nueva España es redondo, tan grande como un membrillo, con la cáscara de un color verdinegro y tan sutil como la de la ciruela. La carne es negra y tan blanda que se machuca fácilmente y destila un zumo de color de arrope. Tiene muchas pepitas del tamaño y hechura de piñones. Es de un dulce remiso, y si no se come con cuidado, mancha los manteles y servilletas. Hállanse dos especies destos negros; la segunda se diferencia de la primera en que el zapote no es del todo negro, sino de un pardo oscuro; es menor y de figura ovalada, poco menor que un huevo de gallina, de mayores pepitas y más vivo y apetecible dulce.

La cuarta diferencia de zapotes es de los amarillos; éstos son menores que los colorados: tienen figura algo prolongada y no vi que se hiciese caso dellos en México.

La quinta especie de zapotes se diferencia mucho de los otros. El árbol es grande, no muy copado; echa las hojas parecidas a las del naranjo y puestas de tres en tres; tiene el tronco variado con unas señales blancas; da unas flores amarillas pequeñas; la fruta es de la hechura de un membrillo, y los mayores zapotes, de su tamaño. Tien

results.title_inferred: The Palo Fruit of Peru
results.original_text:
CAPITULO XIV
Del palo

La fruta que llaman palo en el Perú no he visto en otra parte fuera deste reino. El árbol es de la grandeza de un mediano olivo, aunque los hay, de aquí para abajo, de diferente grandor, y los menores serán de poco más de un estado en alto. La hoja es como la del guayabo y muy parecida a la del manzano, salvo que es más ancha y retorcida o encorvada hacia el pezón. La fruta es pequeña y redonda, muy parecida en el tamaño y forma a peras pequeñas; por de fuera es amarilla, y por de dentro, blanca. Tiene un olor algo agudo y no desabrido, la cáscara muy delgada y tierna y que no se desase de la carne. En lo interior tiene unas pepitas blancas como lentejas muy llegadas a la pulpa; ésta es una sustancia muy blanda y aguanosa; cómese toda sin desechar cáscara ni pepitas y es de razonable sabor; de la cual son muy golosos los muchachos y las mujeres.
results.translation:
CHAPTER XIV
Of the Palo

The fruit they call palo [literally 'stick' or 'tree'] in Peru I have not seen anywhere outside this kingdom. The tree is the size of a medium olive tree, though they vary somewhat in stature, and the smallest will be little more than a man's height [un estado, approx. 1.7 m] tall. The leaf resembles that of the guava tree and is very similar to that of the apple tree, except that it is broader and twisted or curved toward the stem. The fruit is small and round, very similar in size and shape to small pears; on the outside it is yellow, and on the inside, white. It has a somewhat sharp but not unpleasant smell; the skin is very thin and tender and does not separate from the flesh. Inside it has small white seeds like lentils, closely embedded in the pulp; this pulp is a very soft and watery substance. The whole fruit is eaten without discarding either skin or seeds, and it is of reasonable flavor; children and women are particularly fond of it.

entities_mentioned.name: palo
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Possibly a species of Cyphomandra, Solanum, or a member of Myrtaceae native to Peru; the description of small yellow fruit with white interior, lentil-like seeds, and thin inseparable skin is suggestive but inconclusive. Some scholars have tentatively associated 'palo' with Vasconcellea (mountain papaya) relatives or Carica species, though the description does not perfectly match. Identity remains uncertain.
entities_mentioned.notes: Cobo states he never saw this fruit outside Peru, suggesting a locally endemic or regionally restricted species. The description — yellow exterior, white interior, soft watery pulp, lentil-sized white seeds embedded in pulp, thin non-separable skin, size and shape of small pears — does not map cleanly onto a well-known species with high confidence. No medicinal use is described in this passage.

entities_mentioned.notes: Referenced only as a comparator for leaf shape.

entities_mentioned.name: manzano (apple tree, for leaf comparison)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Malus domestica
entities_mentioned.notes: Referenced only as a comparator for leaf shape.

entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Olea europaea
entities_mentioned.notes: Referenced only as a comparator for tree size.

results.translator_notes: The source text is heavily corrupted by OCR errors (e.g., 'm be visto' → 'no he visto'; 'aípi para ahajo' → 'aquí para abajo'; 'fntta' → 'fruta'; 'im' → 'manzano'; 'siT' → 'algo'; 'desase' reconstructed as 'desase/despega'). All corrections are unambiguous in context and have been silently applied per translation rules. The unit 'un estado' is a period Spanish measure of height roughly equivalent to a man's full stature (~1.7 m). No medicinal or pharmacological information appears in this segment; it is purely descriptive natural history. The identity of 'palo' warrants further archival investigation.

results.title_inferred: The Chicozapote Fruit
results.original_text:
CAPITULO XV

Del chicozapote

El chicozapote es fruta de la Nueva España, y de las más regaladas que se hallan en aquel reino; el árbol es de la grandeza del mamey y de menor hoja; la fruta es redonda, del tamaño de una lima, de un color leonado que tira a rojo, y el mismo color tiene la carne, la cual es blanda, muy jugosa y dulce; las pepitas, pequeñas y lisas, de tamaño de piñones. Es fruta de buen gusto y mantenimiento, y como tal es muy estimada. Los mejores chicozapotes que yo he visto son los que se dan en Tehuantepec, diócesis de Guajaca.
results.translation:
CHAPTER XV

On the Chicozapote

The chicozapote is a fruit of New Spain [Nueva España], and among the most prized [regaladas] to be found in that kingdom. The tree is of the same size as the mamey and has smaller leaves. The fruit is round, the size of a lime, of a tawny [leonado] color tending toward red, and the flesh [carne] is the same color; it is soft, very juicy, and sweet. The seeds [pepitas] are small and smooth, the size of pine nuts [piñones]. It is a fruit of fine flavor and nourishment, and as such is highly esteemed. The best chicozapotes I have seen are those grown in Tehuantepec, in the diocese of Oaxaca [Guajaca].

entities_mentioned.name: chicozapote
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Manilkara zapota (sapodilla)
entities_mentioned.notes: Common name 'chicozapote' (from Nahuatl 'tzapotl') corresponds to Manilkara zapota, a tree native to Mesoamerica producing a brown, sweet, mucilagenous fruit. Cobo's description of the round fruit with tawny-reddish flesh and smooth seeds is consistent with this identification. No medicinal use is described in this passage; the fruit is discussed purely as a foodstuff.

entities_mentioned.name: mamey
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Pouteria sapota (mamey sapote)
entities_mentioned.notes: Referenced only for size comparison with the chicozapote tree. Both Manilkara zapota and Pouteria sapota belong to the family Sapotaceae, which is botanically consistent with Cobo's comparison.

entities_mentioned.name: Tehuantepec
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Santo Domingo Tehuantepec, Oaxaca, Mexico
entities_mentioned.notes: Cobo identifies this as the location of the finest chicozapotes he personally encountered, situated in the diocese of Oaxaca ('Guajaca' being a standard 17th-century Spanish rendering of 'Oaxaca').

results.translator_notes: The word 'isfma' in the source OCR is an obvious corruption of 'España' (the preceding line break splits 'Nueva España'); silently corrected to 'Nueva España' in the cleaned original. The word 'Mama' is similarly an OCR error for 'hallan' (third-person plural of 'hallar,' meaning 'to be found'); corrected silently. No medicinal or pharmacological content is present in this segment; it is a purely descriptive natural-history entry. 'Leonado' is a period color term meaning tawny or lion-colored, preserved in brackets in the translation.

results.title_inferred: The Usuma (Peruvian Plum-like Fruit)
results.original_text:
CAPITULO XVII
De la usuma

También hallamos en esta tierra algunos géneros de frutas, que, por la similitud que tienen con nuestra ciruela, les damos este nombre. La primera de estas clases es la que los indios peruanos nombran usuma en la lengua aimará, y nosotros, ciruelas de la tierra. El árbol es de la grandeza de un moral, muy copado y de buen parecer y sombra; su hoja se parece a la del naranjo, bien que es mayor, más lisa, tiesa, acanalada y de muy oscuro verde. La fruta es del tamaño de una ciruela de fraile, colorada por de dentro y por de fuera; la cáscara es un hollejito tan sutil como una telita de cebolla; la carne, muy blanda y pegajosa, pero tan ahogadiza que no se puede comer de una vez sino muy pocas y despacio, para poderla pasar; fuera de que no tienen sino un gusto razonable. Cada una tiene huesecillos dentro como de ciruelas de España, aunque no son tan lisos ni duros, sino de cáscara correosa, que se llega y no quiebra. Donde hay abundancia de otras frutas se hace poco caso désta.
results.translation:
CHAPTER XVII
On the usuma

We also find in this land certain kinds of fruits which, by reason of their resemblance to our plum [ciruela], we give them that name. The first of these kinds is the one that the Peruvian Indians call usuma in the Aymara language, and which we call ciruelas de la tierra [plums of the land]. The tree is the size of a mulberry tree, very full-crowned, of good appearance and shade; its leaf resembles that of the orange tree, except that it is larger, smoother, stiffer, channeled [acanalada], and of a very dark green. The fruit is the size of a friar's plum [ciruela de fraile], red on the inside and on the outside; the skin is a thin little peel as delicate as a thin onion membrane; the flesh is very soft and sticky, but so choking [ahogadiza] that one cannot eat them all at once, but only a few at a time and slowly, in order to be able to swallow them; apart from the fact that they have only a passable flavor. Each one contains small stones [huesecillos] inside, like those of the plums of Spain, though they are not so smooth or hard, but rather have a tough, leathery skin [cáscara correosa] that bends without breaking. Where other fruits are plentiful, little account is taken of this one.

entities_mentioned.name: usuma (also: ciruela de la tierra)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Possibly Ximenia americana (tallow wood / tallowwood plum) or a species of Bunchosia or Spondias; the Aymara name 'usuma' and the description of sticky, slightly choking flesh with a leathery pit suggest it may be Spondias purpurea (red mombin/jocote) or a related Anacardiaceae species native to Andean regions
entities_mentioned.notes: The Aymara name 'usuma' and the description—red inside and out, sticky flesh, leathery (not hard) stone, choking texture—are distinctive but not conclusively matched to a single modern taxon. Some sources associate 'usuma' with Chrysophyllum sp. or Cyphomandra sp. Further ethnobotanical research into Aymara plant nomenclature is warranted.

entities_mentioned.name: moral (mulberry tree, used as size reference)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Morus sp.
entities_mentioned.notes: Used only as a size comparison for the usuma tree.

entities_mentioned.name: ciruela de fraile (friar's plum, used as size reference)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: A variety of Prunus domestica or related European plum cultivar
entities_mentioned.notes: Used only as a size comparison for the usuma fruit.

results.translator_notes: The original OCR shows multiple corruption artifacts (e.g., 'iisuina' for 'usuma' in the chapter heading, 'militucí' for 'similitud', 'soraka' for 'sombra', 'Babi' for 'bien', 'xsm' for apparent lacuna, 'fumi' for 'fuera', 'nmy' for 'muy', 'w' for 'sino', 'fe' recurring for 'los/sus', 'tmi' for 'caso', 'al»' for 'abun-'). These have been silently corrected where unambiguous. The chapter title in the OCR reads 'iisuina' but is clearly 'usuma' from context and the body text. No genuinely illegible passages remain after correction. The term 'ahogadiza' (choking, suffocating) is a period term describing a fruit whose texture makes it difficult to swallow, retained in brackets in the translation.

results.title_inferred: The Hobo Tree: Fruit, Shade, and Medicinal Uses
results.original_text:
CAPITULO XVIII
Del hobo

La fruta llamada hobo en la isla Española es también parecida a nuestras ciruelas. El árbol es de la grandeza de un nogal, y la hoja asimismo es semejante a la del nogal; es árbol de hermosa vista y de sombra muy sana, por lo cual los caminantes y los que se hallan en el campo descansan en ella antes que debajo de otro árbol. La fruta es como las menores ciruelas de España, algo larguilla, como la mitad de un dedo, no tan puntiaguda como las ciruelas; es amarilla cuando está madura y en todo muy parecida a un pequeño dátil, excepto en el color; es olorosa, tiene la cáscara muy delgada, entre la cual y el hueso tiene una sustancia en poca cantidad de un agre gustoso. El hueso es grande en proporción de la fruta, tierno y áspero, cubierto de unas venillas, o briznas, que, si se mascan, pican, fruncen la boca y ofenden las encías.

Es el hobo fruta silvestre y poco sana. El agua cocida con las hojas y corteza de este árbol es medicinal para lavatorios, especialmente de piernas. Sus raíces son muy aguanosas, por lo cual, quien se halla falto de agua en el campo donde hay hobos, suele cavar al pie y cortar un pedazo de su raíz, la cual, chupada, da bastante para matar la sed.
results.translation:
CHAPTER XVIII
Of the Hobo

The fruit called hobo on the island of Hispaniola also resembles our plums. The tree is the size of a walnut tree, and its leaf likewise resembles that of the walnut; it is a tree of handsome appearance and very wholesome shade, on account of which travelers and those who find themselves in the countryside prefer to rest beneath it rather than under any other tree. The fruit is like the smaller plums of Spain, somewhat elongated, about half a finger's length, not as pointed as plums; it is yellow when ripe and in all respects very similar to a small date, except in color; it is fragrant, has a very thin skin, and between the skin and the stone there is a small quantity of a pleasantly tart substance [agre gustoso]. The stone is large in proportion to the fruit, tender yet rough, covered with little fibers or threads which, if chewed, sting, pucker the mouth, and irritate the gums.

The hobo is a wild fruit and not very wholesome. Water boiled with the leaves and bark of this tree is medicinal for washes [lavatorios], especially of the legs. Its roots contain a great deal of water, on account of which anyone who finds himself without water in the countryside where hobo trees grow customarily digs at the base of the tree and cuts off a piece of root, which, when sucked, yields enough moisture to quench one's thirst.

entities_mentioned.name: hobo
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Spondias mombin (yellow mombin / jocote amarillo), family Anacardiaceae
entities_mentioned.notes: Cobo's description — the size and walnut-like foliage, small yellow plum-like drupes with fibrous stones, tart pulp, fragrance, and the island of Hispaniola provenance — strongly matches Spondias mombin. Some overlap possible with Spondias purpurea depending on region, but the yellow ripe fruit points to S. mombin.

results.translator_notes: OCR introduced several corruptions: 'isla D-pañola' = 'isla Española'; 'parecida a nue&W cirut'las' = 'parecida a nuestras ciruelas'; 'asimismo es jante' = 'asimismo es semejante'; 'camínantíf' = 'caminantes'; 'ane' = 'que'; 'itbol' = 'árbol'; 'dda' = 'parecida'; 'excepto en el / es olorosa' reconstructed as 'excepto en el color; es olorosa' (the word 'color' appears to have been lost between lines); 'mbierto' = 'cubierto'; '^grcle' = 'agre' (tart/sharp); 'kobo'/'kobos' = 'hobo/hobos' (consistent OCR misread of 'h' as 'k'); 'cie' before 'árbol' deleted as OCR artifact; 'lavatories / pieimas' = 'lavatorios / piernas'. The medical use extracted: decoction of leaves and bark used as a wash [lavatorio] for the legs. The survival use of sucking water from the roots is also noteworthy ethnobotanically.

results.title_inferred: The Nicaragua Plum Tree and Its Fruit
results.original_text:
CAPITULO XIX

De las ciruelas de Nicaragua

El ciruelo que llamamos de Nicaragua, por haberse traído de aquella provincia a este reino del Perú, es árbol pequeño y tan bajo, que aunque su tronco llega a ser tan grueso como el cuerpo de un hombre, no sube derecho un estado, sino que, en saliendo de la tierra, se inclina y tuerce a los lados. Produce las ramas bajas, las cuales se esparcen y extienden al soslayo hacia todas partes, no muy levantadas de la tierra; de manera, que para coger su fruta, no hay necesidad de subirse en él ni de otro instrumento, porque una persona desde el suelo la puede coger toda con las manos. La madera deste árbol es muy tierna y vidriosa, y con poco peso se desgaja; es tan aguanosa y conserva después de cortada tanta humedad, que después de muchos meses, en cualquiera parte que se guarde, brota por muchas partes y crecen los pimpollos o renuevos más de un codo, lo que no se halla en ningunas maderas de otros árboles. Por el tronco y ramas destila mucha goma que no es de ningún provecho.

Produce las hojas en ramitos, por cada de dos en dos, las cuales se parecen mucho a la del lentisco, sino que son más angostas, más tiernas y de un verde más claro. Por la amenidad de sus hojas es este árbol, cuando está vestido dellas, de muy alegre parecer. Pierde todas las hojas en tiempos contrarios que los árboles de Castilla, porque se cae a la entrada de la primavera y se puebla de nueva hoja al fin del verano; de suerte, que cuando los árboles de Europa se cubren de la hoja nueva, éste se despoja de la suya, y al contrario. La fruta es del tamaño de pequeños dátiles, entre colorada y amarilla por de fuera; la cáscara, delgada y tierna, que se come con la fruta; no tiene de comer más que la cáscara y entre ella y el hueso muy poca sustancia, aguanosa, de un agrete apetitoso, que sirve para abrir la gana de comer, y para esto se suele dar a los enfermos. El hueso es como el del jobo, como lo es también esta fruta, cubierto de aquellas venillas delgadas algún tanto mordaces a la lengua, que tiene el hueso del jobo. De las hojas tiernas deste ciruelo se hace una salsa tan buena como de perejil.
results.translation:
CHAPTER XIX

On the Nicaragua Plums

The plum tree we call the Nicaragua plum [ciruelo de Nicaragua], having been brought from that province to this kingdom of Peru, is a small and low-growing tree, such that although its trunk may grow as thick as the body of a man, it does not grow straight up to the height of a man [un estado], but rather, from the moment it emerges from the ground, bends and twists to the sides. It puts forth low branches, which spread and extend obliquely in all directions, not rising far from the ground; so that in order to gather its fruit, there is no need to climb the tree or to use any other implement, for a person standing on the ground may gather all of it by hand. The wood of this tree is very soft and brittle, and breaks off with little weight; it retains so much water and preserves such moisture after being cut, that after many months, in whatever place it is kept, it sprouts in many places and puts forth shoots or new growth of more than a cubit [codo], something not found in the wood of any other tree. Through the trunk and branches it exudes a considerable quantity of gum, which is of no useful purpose.

It produces its leaves in small clusters, two by two, which closely resemble those of the mastic tree [lentisco], except that they are narrower, more delicate, and of a lighter green. By reason of the pleasantness of its leaves, this tree, when clothed in them, presents a very cheerful appearance. It sheds all its leaves at the opposite season from the trees of Castile [Spain], for it loses them at the onset of spring and puts forth new foliage at the end of summer; so that when the trees of Europe are covering themselves in new leaf, this one is stripping itself bare, and vice versa. The fruit is the size of small dates, reddish-yellow on the outside; the skin is thin and tender, and is eaten together with the fruit; there is nothing to eat but the skin and, between it and the stone, very little substance—watery, with an agreeable tartness [agrete] that serves to stimulate the appetite, and for this purpose it is commonly given to the sick. The stone is like that of the jobo [hog plum], as indeed is the fruit itself, covered with those thin little veins [venillas] that are somewhat biting to the tongue, which are found on the stone of the jobo. From the tender leaves of this plum tree, a sauce as good as one made from parsley can be prepared.

entities_mentioned.name: ciruelo de Nicaragua / ciruela de Nicaragua
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Spondias purpurea (purple mombin / red mombin / jocote)
entities_mentioned.notes: Described as introduced to Peru from Nicaragua. Characteristics—low, spreading form; watery, tart fruit resembling small dates in size; stone resembling that of the jobo; leaves in paired leaflets—are consistent with Spondias purpurea (Anacardiaceae). Cobo notes the reversed seasonality relative to European trees, consistent with a tropical deciduous species grown in a different climatic regime.

entities_mentioned.name: lentisco
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Pistacia lentiscus (mastic tree)
entities_mentioned.notes: Used by Cobo as a comparative reference for leaf shape, indicating paired, narrow, medium-green leaflets. This is a standard Old World botanical reference point in colonial Spanish natural history writing.

entities_mentioned.name: jobo
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Spondias mombin (yellow mombin / hog plum)
entities_mentioned.notes: Cobo explicitly compares the stone and fibrous veinlets of the Nicaragua plum to those of the jobo. Both species belong to Spondias (Anacardiaceae), which explains the similarity. The 'biting' or astringent fibrous strands on the stone are characteristic of this genus.

results.translator_notes: 1. The OCR produced several corruption artifacts: 'pequí'ño' (pequeño), 'fcra' (tierra), 'tferra' (tierra), 'eiparcen' (esparcen), '4 ai' (él ni), 'Wml' (árbol), 'feáiiaedad' (humedad), 'Wtt' (brota), 'pimpoílofi' (pimpollos), 'malquiera' (cualquiera), and '/lofeo' (jobo) have all been silently corrected as unambiguous. 2. 'un estado' (one estado) is a unit of height roughly equivalent to a man's height (~1.7 m); rendered contextually in translation. 3. The medical/dietary application: fruit given to sick patients (enfermos) as an appetite stimulant (abrir la gana de comer). Preparation: fruit eaten raw; leaves used to prepare a sauce (salsa) comparable to parsley sauce. 4. No explicit pharmaceutical preparation beyond these two uses is described.

results.title_inferred: Plums of Tierra Firme, the Jocote, and the Ibapiiru fruit
results.original_text:
CAPITULO XX
De las ciruelas de Tierra Firme

En la provincia de Tierra Firme se da otra especie de ciruelas parecidas a las de Nicaragua. El árbol es muy semejante al del capítulo pasado, sólo que es un poco mayor. Son también estas ciruelas mayores que las de Nicaragua, porque son tan grandes como las chavaeanas de España y aun críanse como un huevo de gallina; en lo exterior, moradas, y de más carne que las de Nicaragua, pero no tan aguanosas, sino tiesas, con el hueso poco mayor que las otras. Aunque a estas ciruelas y a las de los capítulos pasados llaman los españoles ciruelas de las Indias, porque se parecen algo a las nuestras de Europa, la verdad es que ni en el sabor ni en las demás calidades tienen que ver con ellas. En la Nueva España las llaman jocotes, y hay varias especies de ellas, y unos árboles son mayores que otros y de hoja más crecida.

También se puede reducir al linaje de ciruelas de la tierra cierta fruta como pera pequeña, y muy semejante a los jocotes de la Nueva España, que se da en la Sierra del Perú, adonde la llaman níspero y no lo es. El árbol es como mediano naranjo, y la fruta, silvestre y desabrida.

tragan con la fruta. Quiere decir puru, en la lengua de aquella provincia, fruta que suena cuando se come; no nace en el árbol como la demás fruta, debajo de las hojas, sino en los ramos y troncos del árbol, comenzando desde la tierra, y aun si alguna parte de la raíz está descubierta, también tiene fruta y aun ésta tan espesa y apretada por todo el árbol arriba, que casi no se ve el tronco ni se puede subir por él. Es el ibapiiru fruta muy regalada y de muy buen gusto.
results.translation:
CHAPTER XX
On the Plums of Tierra Firme

In the province of Tierra Firme there grows another species of plum resembling those of Nicaragua. The tree is very similar to that described in the preceding chapter, except that it is somewhat larger. These plums are likewise larger than those of Nicaragua, being as big as the [chavaeanas] of Spain and sometimes growing to the size of a hen's egg. On the outside they are purple, and they have more flesh than those of Nicaragua, though they are not as watery but rather firm, with a stone only slightly larger than the other variety. Although the Spaniards call these plums, as well as those described in the preceding chapters, 'plums of the Indies' [ciruelas de las Indias] because they bear some resemblance to our European plums, the truth is that in flavor and in all other qualities they bear no comparison with them. In New Spain they are called jocotes, and there are several species of them; some trees are larger than others and have bigger leaves.

One may also group among the native plum family a certain fruit resembling a small pear, very similar to the jocotes of New Spain, which grows in the Andean highlands of Peru [Sierra del Perú], where it is called níspero, though it is not one. The tree is roughly the size of a medium orange tree, and the fruit is wild and insipid.

[Note: The following passage appears to belong to an adjacent chapter concerning the ibapiiru, and was likely joined to this segment due to a page-layout or binding error in the source.]

...they swallow along with the fruit. The word puru, in the language of that province, means a fruit that makes a sound when eaten. It does not grow on the tree as other fruit does, beneath the leaves, but rather on the branches and trunk of the tree, starting from the ground, and even if any part of the root is exposed it also bears fruit — and this fruit grows so densely and tightly packed all the way up the tree that the trunk can scarcely be seen and one cannot climb it. The ibapiiru is a very fine and very pleasant-tasting fruit.

entities_mentioned.name: ciruelas de Tierra Firme
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Spondias purpurea or Spondias mombin (Anacardiaceae) — likely Spondias purpurea given the purple exterior and larger size
entities_mentioned.notes: Cobo distinguishes this from the Nicaraguan ciruela by larger size (compared to a hen's egg), purple skin, firmer flesh, and less wateriness.

entities_mentioned.name: jocotes (Nueva España)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Spondias purpurea (jocote / red mombin, Anacardiaceae)
entities_mentioned.notes: Well-attested Nahuatl name still in use throughout Mesoamerica for Spondias purpurea.

entities_mentioned.name: níspero (Sierra del Perú)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Possibly Mespilus or Eriobotrya japonica as a folk name transferred, but more likely a native Andean fruit such as Prunus serotina subsp. capuli or a Spondias relative; Cobo explicitly notes it is not a true níspero
entities_mentioned.notes: Cobo warns the Peruvian níspero is wild and insipid and merely resembles the jocote; positive identification is uncertain.

entities_mentioned.name: ibapiiru
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Jaboticaba — Plinia cauliflora (Myrtaceae)
entities_mentioned.notes: The distinctive description of cauliflorous fruiting (fruit growing directly on trunk and branches from ground level upward, densely packed, making a sound when eaten) is strongly characteristic of jaboticaba. The name derives from Tupi-Guaraní. This passage appears to belong to a different chapter and was likely misbound or misaligned in the OCR source.

results.translator_notes: The final paragraph(s) describing the 'ibapiiru' appear to belong to a separate chapter on that fruit and were joined to this chapter on Tierra Firme plums, almost certainly due to a pagination or binding error in the 1653 source or in the OCR process. The word 'chavaeanas' in the original is likely 'claudianas' (a variety of Spanish plum, Prunus insititia, also known as 'claudias'), a probable OCR or transcription corruption; it has been retained in brackets in the translation. The phrase 'fruta que suena cuando se come' ('fruit that makes a sound when eaten') is a folk etymology or translation of the Guaraní/Tupi root of ibapiiru, and is characteristic of jaboticaba whose tight-skinned berries pop when bitten.

results.title_inferred: The Chañar Tree: Description and Fruit
results.original_text:
CAPITULO XXI
Del chañar

El chañar es un árbol mediano, del grandor de un olivo, al cual se parece mucho desde lejos; la hoja es pequeña, como la mitad de la del olivo y algo parecida a ella en el color y talle, salvo que es de un verde más claro y más delgada y tierna. No es árbol copado, sino de pocas ramas; la corteza, muy delgada y verde y ella misma se despide del tronco. Echa una florecita amarilla semejante a la de la ruda. La fruta es poco mayor que una aceituna, y cuando está en sazón, es pardizca; tiene un hueso redondo y liso del tamaño de un garbanzo; no tiene más comida que la cascarilla con una poca de pulpa que está sobre el hueso, de razonable gusto, aunque poco gustosa, y por eso no es fruta de codicia.
results.translation:
CHAPTER XXI
Of the chañar

The chañar is a medium-sized tree, about the size of an olive tree, which it greatly resembles from a distance. The leaf is small — roughly half the size of an olive leaf — and somewhat similar to it in color and shape, except that it is a lighter green, thinner, and more tender. It is not a spreading [copado] tree, but rather one of few branches; the bark is very thin and green, and peels away from the trunk of its own accord. It produces a small yellow flower resembling that of rue [ruda]. The fruit is slightly larger than an olive, and when ripe is brownish [pardizco]; it contains a round, smooth stone [pit] the size of a chickpea. There is no more edible matter than the thin skin [cascarilla] and a small amount of pulp lying over the stone, of passable flavor, though not particularly pleasant — and for this reason it is not a fruit much sought after.

entities_mentioned.name: chañar
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Geoffroea decorticans (chañar; family Fabaceae)
entities_mentioned.notes: A leguminous tree native to arid and semi-arid regions of South America (Argentina, Chile, Bolivia, Peru). The description of peeling green bark, small yellow flowers, and drupe-like fruit with a single large seed is diagnostic of G. decorticans. Cobo does not discuss medicinal uses in this passage, focusing solely on morphology and fruit quality.

entities_mentioned.name: ruda
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Ruta graveolens (common rue; family Rutaceae)
entities_mentioned.notes: Used here as a comparative reference for the appearance of the chañar's small yellow flowers, not as a subject of the chapter.

results.translator_notes: The word 'copado' (rendered here as 'spreading') refers to a tree with a broad, full canopy; no exact single-word English equivalent is used. 'Cascarilla' literally means 'little skin/shell' and here refers to the thin outer skin of the fruit rather than the pharmaceutical bark known by the same name. 'Pardizco/pardizca' is an archaic Spanish term for a dull brownish or grayish-brown color. No medicinal preparations or ailments are described in this segment; the chapter is purely botanical and descriptive. The OCR error 'uu' (line 8 of the source) has been silently corrected to 'un', and 'despi¬ de' (split by a hyphen/paragraph artifact) has been restored to 'despide'.

results.title_inferred: The Land Apple (Manzano de la Tierra) of New Spain
results.original_text:
CAPITULO XXIV
De las manzanas de la tierra

El árbol que llamamos manzano de la tierra sólo se halla en la Nueva España. Es pequeño y muy parecido en todo al manzano de Castilla, salvo que es mucho mayor y de hojas más pequeñas, aunque de la misma hechura. El fruto que produce son unas manzanillas pequeñas, redondas y amarillas, de tamaño y hechura de níspero. Es fruta silvestre y desabrida; tiene cada una tres pepitas dentro de hechura de piñones, y tan duras, que no se pueden quebrar con los dientes. Usan los españoles hacer conservas destas manzanas; y en su árbol se injieren bien las manzanas de Castilla.
results.translation:
CHAPTER XXIV
On the Land Apples

The tree we call the land apple [manzano de la tierra] is found only in New Spain. It is small and very similar in every respect to the apple tree of Castile [manzano de Castilla], except that it is much larger and has smaller leaves, though of the same shape. The fruit it produces consists of small, round, yellow little apples [manzanillas], of the size and shape of a medlar [níspero]. It is a wild and insipid fruit; each one contains three seeds inside shaped like pine nuts [piñones], and so hard that they cannot be broken with the teeth. The Spaniards use these apples to make preserves [conservas]; and Castilian apples graft well onto this tree.

entities_mentioned.name: manzano de la tierra / manzanilla
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Possibly Ximenia americana (wild plum / tallow nut) or a species of Malacomeles or Amelanchier, given the small yellow fruit with hard seeds resembling medlars found in Mexico; alternatively Crataegus mexicana (tejocote) is a strong candidate given the round yellow fruit, medlar-like size, multiple hard seeds, and use in preserves in New Spain
entities_mentioned.notes: Crataegus mexicana (tejocote) is the most commonly cited modern identification for 'manzana de la tierra' in colonial Mexican sources. The description of small, round, yellow fruit the size of a medlar with hard internal seeds and use in preserves aligns well with tejocote. The statement that Castilian apple scions graft well onto it is consistent with it being a Rosaceae relative.

entities_mentioned.name: manzano de Castilla
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Malus domestica (common apple)
entities_mentioned.notes: Standard colonial Spanish term for the European apple tree, used here as a comparator.

entities_mentioned.name: níspero
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Mespilus germanica (common medlar) or Eriobotrya japonica (loquat), used here as a size/shape comparator
entities_mentioned.notes: Used descriptively for size and form comparison, not as a plant being discussed in its own right.

results.translator_notes: Several OCR artifacts are present in the source: 'parecido m' corrected to 'parecido en'; 'salvo es' corrected to 'salvo que es'; 'hecliurt*' corrected to 'hechura'; 'amariBai' corrected to 'amarillas'; 'se puefa' corrected to 'se pueden'; 'estas maña¬nas' (line-broken) corrected to 'estas manzanas'. The text lacks any explicit medical or pharmacological content; no ailments, preparations, or medicinal uses are described. The chapter is purely botanical and culinary in character.

results.title_inferred: The Jagua Tree and Its Fruit
results.original_text:
CAPITULO XXV
De la jagua

El árbol llamado jagua en la isla Es- es grande, de buen parecer, alto y derecho, parecido al fresno; hácense astas de lanzas de buena tez, entre pardas y coloradas. La fruta que da tiene el nombre del árbol y es del grandor de una berenjena y del mismo color y hechura, y buena de comer, cuando está sazonada. Sácase desta fruta un agua clara con que se suelen lavar, porque tiene virtud de apretar y restringir; tiene esta propiedad, que aunque cuando se lavan con este zumo está claro como agua, poco a poco se pone negro como un azabache lo que bañó esta agua: el cual color no se quita con remedio alguno antes de pasar quince o veinte días; después de los cuales el mismo color se quita poco a poco. Solían los indios lavarse con este zumo el cuerpo cuando habían de ir a la guerra.
results.translation:
CHAPTER XXV
Of the Jagua

The tree called jagua on the island is large, handsome, tall and straight, resembling the ash tree [fresno]; lance shafts of good grain are made from it, in color between tawny and reddish. The fruit it bears takes the name of the tree and is the size of an eggplant [berenjena], of the same color and shape, and good to eat when ripe. From this fruit a clear liquid [agua clara] is extracted, with which people customarily wash themselves, because it has the virtue of tightening and astringeing [apretar y restringir]; it possesses this property: that although when one washes with this juice it is clear as water, little by little whatever the liquid has touched turns as black as jet [azabache]. This color cannot be removed by any remedy before fifteen or twenty days have passed, after which the same color fades away little by little. The Indians were accustomed to wash their bodies with this juice when they were about to go to war.

entities_mentioned.name: jagua
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Genipa americana (jagua / genipap)
entities_mentioned.notes: The black dye produced by the unripe fruit's juice upon contact with skin is a well-documented property of Genipa americana, widely used in the Caribbean and South America for body painting and warfare ritual. The fruit's resemblance to an eggplant in size and color, and the 15–20 day fading period, are consistent with this identification.

entities_mentioned.name: fresno
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Fraxinus sp. (ash tree) — used here as a comparative reference only
entities_mentioned.notes: Cobo uses the familiar Old World ash tree merely as a morphological comparison for the jagua's tall, straight form.

results.translator_notes: The source text opens with a probable OCR split rendering 'isla Es-' which likely continues onto the next line (presumably 'isla Española' or another island name); the continuation is missing from this segment. The passage contains a notable ethnobotanical observation: the use of Genipa americana juice as a temporary black body dye for war preparation, a practice well attested in indigenous Caribbean and Amazonian cultures. The medicinal property noted is astringency [virtud de apretar y restringir], applied topically via washing. No internal preparation or ingestion is described for medicinal use in this segment. The footnote marker '(12)' at the end suggests a corresponding endnote in the original printed edition that is not reproduced here.

results.title_inferred: The Caymito Tree and Its Fruit
results.original_text:
CAPITULO XXVI
Del caymito

Llámase caymito en lengua de la isla Española cierto árbol y su fruto muy conocido en todas las Indias, aunque en unas partes la fruta es diferente que en otras en sólo el tamaño, que el árbol es el mismo. El menor caymito es tan grueso como el dedo y de largo lo que hay de coyuntura a coyuntura; los más comunes son del grandor de una manzana mediana. La sustancia que tiene en lo interior es blanca, dulce, como leche, pegajosa y espesa; y es por fruta de buen gusto. El árbol es del tamaño de un naranjo; la hoja, redonda, y por una parte verde y por la otra parece que está chamuscada, lo cual es causa de conocerse este árbol entre muchos, y su madera es buena para labrar.

[Editor's note:] Otro tanto y además pintarse varias partes del cuerpo por gala hacen ciertas gentes del oriente del Perú y Ecuador con el Iültoc (Genipa oblongifolia) y congénere de Jiütua, y es extraño que Cobo cite el de las Antillas y olvide el del Perú.
results.translation:
CHAPTER XXVI
Of the Caymito

In the language of the island of Hispaniola, a certain tree and its fruit are called 'caymito,' and it is very well known throughout all the Indies, although in some places the fruit differs from that in others only in size, for the tree itself is the same. The smallest caymito is as thick as a finger and as long as the distance from one knuckle to the next; the most common ones are the size of a medium apple. The substance within is white, sweet, milky, sticky, and thick; and the fruit is considered to be of good flavor. The tree is the size of an orange tree; the leaf is round, green on one side, and on the other appears as though it has been scorched, which is one reason this tree is recognized by many people. Its wood is good for working [i.e., carpentry or timber use].

[Editor's interpolated note:] Much the same — and moreover the painting of various parts of the body for adornment — is practiced by certain peoples of eastern Peru and Ecuador using the Iültoc (Genipa oblongifolia) and a relative of the Jiütua; and it is strange that Cobo should cite the species from the Antilles and overlook the one from Peru.

entities_mentioned.name: caymito
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Chrysophyllum cainito (Star Apple, family Sapotaceae)
entities_mentioned.notes: Native to the Caribbean and widely spread throughout tropical America. Cobo notes variation only in fruit size, not in the tree itself. The characteristic bicolored leaf — green above, coppery or russet beneath — is a well-known diagnostic feature of this species.

entities_mentioned.name: Iültoc / Genipa oblongifolia
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Genipa americana or a closely related Genipa species (family Rubiaceae)
entities_mentioned.notes: Cited in the editor's interpolated footnote, not by Cobo himself. Used for body painting by indigenous peoples of eastern Peru and Ecuador. The binomial 'Genipa oblongifolia' is cited by the editor; the genus Genipa is well established for this use.

entities_mentioned.name: Jiütua
entities_mentioned.notes: Mentioned in the editor's footnote as a congener of the Iültoc. Insufficient information for confident modern identification; likely another Genipa or related Rubiaceae species used in the same Andean/Amazonian region.

results.translator_notes: The source text is significantly corrupted by OCR errors (e.g., 'R 0 Bocido,' 'anas,' 'm otras,' 'k fie,' 'aL,' 'fk,' 'mmsL,' 'M m,' 'bfa,' '^fíarotra,' 'M cuerpo,' '^ Iiiltoc,' 'o Jiütua%'). All have been silently corrected where the intended reading is unambiguous from context and the known Spanish text of Cobo. The final paragraph in the source is an editor's interpolated footnote (from a later editorial edition of Cobo), not part of Cobo's original 1653 text; this has been clearly marked in the translation. The OCR split the footnote material into the body of the chapter, which has been separated and labeled accordingly. No passages were marked [ILLEGIBLE] as all corrupted sections were recoverable with high confidence.

results.title_inferred: On the Cashew (cayú) and Various New World Grapes (guiávara and related species)
results.original_text:
CAPITULO XXVII
Del cayú

El cayú es árbol natural de tierra caliente, como lo son casi todas las frutas naturales de Indias; es de la grandeza de un crecido naranjo; la hoja, de buen parecer, algo mayor que la del naranjo; la fruta que lleva es muy parecida en lo exterior a un pero grande, muy tierna, dulce, aguanosa y de muy buen sabor. Su pezón es otra fruta distinta, y media entre árbol y la fruta cayú; es el tamaño y figura de una castaña; cómese este pezón asado, y el meollo es como el de la castaña.

CAPITULO XXVIII
De la guiávara

No faltan en este Nuevo Mundo varias castas de frutas que tienen alguna similitud, aunque remota, con nuestras uvas de España; por lo cual les han puesto este nombre los españoles. La primera especie destas uvas de la tierra es a la que primero dieron este nombre en la isla Española, adonde el árbol que las lleva se llama guiávara: Es muy copado y extendido en ramas, pero bajo, respecto de lo cual no es buena su madera para fábricas, pero eslo para carbón, para tajones de carniceros y otras cosas para que se requiere madera recia. Nace este árbol comúnmente en las costas de la mar, y los españoles, por su fruta, le llaman uvero. La fruta son unas uvas como rosadas, desviadas unas de otras; son de comer, aunque tienen poca sustancia, por ser grande el hueso respecto del tamaño de la fruta; la cual es como granos de uva medianas y suele fruncir la boca cuando se come. La madera deste árbol es colorada, y su hoja, redonda, verde y las venillas coloradas, es tan gruesa como dos hojas de yedra pegadas una con otra; por la cual, con un alfiler o cualquiera otra cosa, se puede escribir en una destas hojas muy bien por ambas partes; y no pocas veces, a falta de papel y tinta, han servido desto.

La segunda especie de uvas produce un árbol de dos estados de alto, cuya hoja es como la del arrayán y echa una flor morada; da las uvas en racimos muy apretados; son algo menores que las nuestras; el hollejo, duro y agrias. Cuando verdes, están blancas, y en madurando, se ponen negras.

Otra especie de uvas se llama en la Nueva España istacapulh que excede su bondad a las otras y en asimilarse a nuestras uvas. El árbol es como un mediano naranjo; la hoja, parecida a la del nogal; y la fruta son unas uvas semejantes a nuestras uvas blancas; porque son unos racimos de unos granos como de uvas medianas, muy blancos y transparentes, cuya sustancia interior es aguanosa como de uvas; tiene dentro cada grano un huesecillo como de aceituna, algo menor. Es fruta dulce, aunque frunce un poco la boca.
results.translation:
CHAPTER XXVII
On the Cayú

The cayú is a tree native to warm lands, as are nearly all the fruits indigenous to the Indies. It is as large as a well-grown orange tree; its leaf is of handsome appearance, somewhat larger than that of the orange tree. The fruit it bears closely resembles in outward appearance a large pear [pero]; it is very tender, sweet, watery, and of excellent flavor. Its stem [pezón] is another distinct fruit, intermediate between the tree and the cayú fruit itself; it is the size and shape of a chestnut. This stem is eaten roasted, and its inner flesh [meollo] is like that of a chestnut.

CHAPTER XXVIII
On the Guiávara

There is no shortage in this New World of various kinds of fruits that bear some resemblance, however remote, to our grapes of Spain; for which reason the Spaniards have given them the name of grapes. The first species of these native grapes is the one to which this name was first applied on the island of Hispaniola, where the tree that bears them is called guiávara. It is very full-crowned and wide-spreading in its branches, yet low in stature; on account of which its timber is not suitable for construction, though it serves well for charcoal, for butchers' chopping blocks [tajones de carniceros], and other uses requiring hard wood. This tree commonly grows along the seacoasts, and the Spaniards, on account of its fruit, call it uvero [lit. 'grape tree']. The fruit consists of grapes of a rosy color, set apart from one another; they are edible, though of little substance, as the stone [hueso] is large relative to the size of the fruit, which resembles medium-sized grape berries and tends to pucker the mouth when eaten. The wood of this tree is reddish, and its leaf is round, green with reddish veins, and as thick as two ivy leaves pressed together; on account of which one can write quite well on either side of one of these leaves using a pin or any other pointed implement, and not infrequently they have served this purpose in the absence of paper and ink.

The second species of these grapes is produced by a tree about two fathoms [estados] in height, whose leaf resembles that of the myrtle [arrayán] and which bears a purple flower. It yields its grapes in very tightly packed clusters; they are somewhat smaller than ours; the skin is hard and they are sour. When unripe they are white, and upon ripening they turn black.

Another species of grape is called in New Spain istacapulh, which surpasses the others in quality and in its resemblance to our own grapes. The tree is like a medium-sized orange tree; the leaf resembles that of the walnut; and the fruit consists of grapes similar to our white grapes, forming clusters of medium-sized berries, very white and transparent, whose inner substance is watery like that of grapes. Each berry contains inside a small stone [huesecillo] resembling that of an olive, though somewhat smaller. It is a sweet fruit, though it puckers the mouth a little.

entities_mentioned.name: cayú
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Anacardium occidentale (cashew)
entities_mentioned.notes: Cobo describes both the cashew apple (the swollen peduncle/'pezón') and the cashew nut itself, which he compares to a chestnut and says is eaten roasted. The 'fruta' resembling a large pear is the cashew apple; the 'pezón' is the true fruit (the nut). No medicinal use is described in this passage.

entities_mentioned.name: guiávara / uvero
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Coccoloba uvifera (sea grape)
entities_mentioned.notes: Description matches sea grape: coastal habitat, spreading low crown, round leaves with red veins, rose-colored berries with large stones, hard/astringent taste, reddish wood. The detail about writing on the thick leaves is consistent with Coccoloba uvifera leaves. No medicinal use described.

entities_mentioned.name: segunda especie de uvas [unnamed]
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Possibly Vitis tiliifolia or another native Vitis sp., or potentially Bignay (Antidesma bunius); uncertain
entities_mentioned.notes: White when unripe, turning black when ripe; tight clusters; myrtle-like leaves; purple flowers; slightly smaller than European grapes; sour skin. Identification uncertain without geographic context.

entities_mentioned.name: istacapulh
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Prunus capuli / Prunus serotina subsp. capuli (capulín cherry), or possibly a Vitis species
entities_mentioned.notes: The name 'istacapulh' (Nahuatl, 'white capulín') and the description of white, transparent, watery berries with an olive-like stone in clusters, walnut-shaped leaves, and a tree the size of an orange tree suggest capulín cherry variants or a related Rosaceae. The 'olive-sized stone' is consistent with Prunus. No medicinal use described in this passage.

results.translator_notes: 1. 'Pero' (a variety of pear) has been translated as 'pear' with the original term bracketed for clarity. 2. 'Pezón' in the cashew context refers to the swollen peduncle (cashew apple), which Cobo somewhat confusingly calls a separate fruit intermediate between tree and nut — his botanical understanding is consistent with the curious morphology of the cashew. 3. 'Estados' is a Spanish unit of height roughly equivalent to a fathom (~1.8 m), here translated as 'fathoms.' 4. OCR artifact 'ca¬liente' (line break hyphenation) and similar split words have been silently rejoined throughout. 5. OCR read 'uvas Llancas' (capital L); corrected to 'uvas blancas' (white grapes) as contextually unambiguous. 6. The 'istacapulh' spelling may reflect OCR distortion of Nahuatl 'iztac capulín' (white capulín); retained as found in the source with note.

results.title_inferred: The Guazuma Tree (Mutamba / West Indian Elm)

CAPITULO XXX

De la guazuma

Dan este nombre en la isla Española a un árbol llamado de los españoles moral de las Indias, porque se parece al moral de España; el cual es grande y tiene la hoja muy semejante a la del moral, un poco mayor y muy verde, como la del saúco. La madera de este árbol es fofa, tierna y tan vedriosa que subiendo en él con poco peso, se desgajan las ramas. Las moras son redondas, del tamaño de aceitunas, blancas, que no tiñen, y otras moradas,
results.translation:
CHAPTER XXX

On the guazuma

This name is given in Hispaniola [isla Española] to a tree called by the Spaniards the 'moral de las Indias' [mulberry of the Indies], because it resembles the mulberry [moral] of Spain. It is a large tree, with a leaf very similar to that of the mulberry, somewhat larger and very green, like that of the elder [saúco]. The wood of this tree is spongy, soft, and so brittle that when one climbs it with even a small load, the branches snap off. The berries [moras] are round, the size of olives, white — which do not stain — and others are purple,

entities_mentioned.name: guazuma
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Guazuma ulmifolia (West Indian elm / mutamba)
entities_mentioned.notes: Commonly called 'moral de las Indias' by Spanish colonists due to leaf resemblance to the European mulberry (Morus spp.). Guazuma ulmifolia is widely distributed across tropical America and the Caribbean. The text describes its characteristic soft/brittle wood and small olive-sized fruits.

entities_mentioned.name: moral de España
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Morus alba or Morus nigra (European white or black mulberry)
entities_mentioned.notes: Used as the comparative reference plant for leaf shape and general appearance.

entities_mentioned.name: saúco
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Sambucus nigra (European elder)
entities_mentioned.notes: Cited only for comparison of leaf color and texture; not a subject plant of this chapter.

results.translator_notes: The segment appears to be cut off mid-sentence ('y otras moradas,' — 'and others purple,'), suggesting this is a partial excerpt and the description of the fruit and likely its medicinal uses continues in the following lines. OCR errors silently corrected: 'iin' → 'un', 'españoks' → 'españoles', 'parm' → 'parece', 'h' → 'la', 'vedriosít' → 'vedriosa', 'm' → 'se', 'dondas' → 'redondas'. The word 'vedriosa' / 'vedriosa' is an archaic/dialectal Spanish term meaning brittle or glassy (like glass, 'vidrio'); rendered as 'brittle' in the translation with the original sense preserved contextually.

results.title_inferred: The Guavira Tree of Santa Cruz de la Sierra
results.original_text:
CAPITULO XXXIII
De la guavira

El árbol llamado guavira nace en la provincia de Santa Cruz de la Sierra: es de la grandeza de un manzano; lleva una frutilla del tamaño y talle de guindas, algo mayorcita, sólo que es de color amarillo; es de muy buen gusto.

(Nota al pie: Pero descansando despierto a su amparo y frescura, como yo lo he hecho durante más de una hora, no sucede nada.)
results.translation:
CHAPTER XXXIII
Of the guavira

The tree called guavira grows in the province of Santa Cruz de la Sierra. It is the size of an apple tree [manzano]; it bears a small fruit the size and shape of a cherry [guinda], somewhat larger, except that it is yellow in color; it is of very pleasant taste.

(Footnote: But resting awake in its shade and coolness, as I myself have done for more than an hour, nothing happens.)

entities_mentioned.name: guavira
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Campomanesia xanthocarpa or a related Campomanesia sp. (family Myrtaceae)
entities_mentioned.notes: The name 'guavira' or 'guabirá' is used in the Río de la Plata and Santa Cruz de la Sierra region (modern Bolivia/Paraguay) for several Campomanesia species, which produce small yellow edible fruits resembling cherries. The description—apple-tree size, cherry-shaped yellow fruit of good flavor—is consistent with this genus. No medicinal use or preparation method is described in this segment. The footnote appears to reference a folk belief (perhaps that resting or sleeping under the tree causes harm), which Cobo personally refutes from experience.

results.translator_notes: The footnote is structurally separate from the main chapter text in the source and appears to be an authorial aside or marginal annotation, likely responding to a local belief that resting under the guavira tree is dangerous or unhealthy. Cobo dismisses this claim based on personal experience. No ailment, drug preparation, or medical application is described in this segment. The OCR rendered 'guavira' as 'guatñra' and 'El' as '0', and several other characters were corrupted; these have been silently corrected where the intended reading is unambiguous. The word 'guindas' (sour cherries, Prunus cerasus) is used as a size/shape comparator, not as an identification.

results.title_inferred: The Pacay Tree (Inga species)
results.original_text:
CAPITULO XXXV
Del pacay

El pacay es un árbol de la grandeza de un moral, de hermoso parecer y muy poblado de hojas; ésta es de la hechura de la del limo, algo mayor, de más escuro verde, muy lisa y reluciente. Produce las hojas este árbol de la manera que el nogal, en unos ramillos delgados, pareadas de dos en dos con cuatro o cinco pares en cada ramillo. La fruta es una vaina de figura de algarroba, mas, en el tamaño se halla gran variedad en diversas tierras: en unas partes no es más larga que una algarroba de un jeme, y en otras llega a tener dos o tres palmos; pero del medio destos extremos son los pacaes ordinarios. Son estas vainas del anchor de dos o tres dedos y uno del grueso; su cáscara es tiesa y correosa, por de fuera verde. Lo que tiene en lo interior es una ringlera de pepitas tan grandes como habas, cubiertas cada una de por sí de una sustancia blanca, esponjosa y dulce, que parece un poco de algodón mojado en almíbar. Las pepitas son verdinegras, tiernas y tan lisas, que apretándolas con los dedos se deslizan. Es fruta muy fría y más de golosina que de sustento; porque, aunque se coma un hombre una canasta de pacaes, no se satisface ni le causa hastío. Lo que tiene de comer esta fruta es solamente aquella sustancia blanca, la cual se come verde y también pasada como higos. La madera deste árbol es leña escogida, por ser muy recia y hacer buena brasa, y para sólo esto plantan muchos pacaes en este valle de Lima. También suele entrar en la fábrica de barcos y hacen della corbatones. La sombra deste árbol es fresca y saludable, el cual se llama pacay en la lengua peruana y guaba en la de la isla Española.
results.translation:
CHAPTER XXXV
Of the Pacay

The pacay is a tree of the size of a mulberry [moral], of handsome appearance and very full of leaves; the leaf is shaped like that of the lemon tree [limo], somewhat larger, of a darker green, very smooth and shiny. This tree produces its leaves in the manner of the walnut [nogal], on slender little branches, paired two by two with four or five pairs on each branchlet. The fruit is a pod [vaina] in the shape of a carob [algarroba], but great variety in size is found in different lands: in some places it is no longer than a carob of one span [jeme], and in others it reaches two or three palms in length; but the ordinary pacaes fall midway between these extremes. These pods are two or three fingers in width and one finger in thickness; their skin is stiff and leathery, green on the outside. What is contained within is a row of seeds as large as broad beans [habas], each one individually covered by a white, spongy, and sweet substance that resembles a small piece of cotton soaked in syrup [almíbar]. The seeds are dark greenish-black, soft, and so smooth that when pressed between the fingers they slip away. It is a very cold fruit and more of a delicacy than a sustenance; for even if a man eats a whole basket of pacaes, he is not satisfied, nor does it cause him to feel sated. The edible part of this fruit is solely that white substance, which is eaten fresh [verde] and also dried like figs. The wood of this tree is a choice firewood, being very hard and producing good embers, and for this purpose alone many pacay trees are planted in this valley of Lima. It is also used in the construction of ships, from which knees [corbatones] are made. The shade of this tree is cool and healthful; the tree is called pacay in the Peruvian language and guaba in that of Hispaniola [the island of Española].

entities_mentioned.name: pacay (also guaba)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Inga feuilleei or related Inga spp. (family Fabaceae)
entities_mentioned.notes: Cobo's description of the white, sweet, cotton-like aril surrounding the seeds within a large legume pod is diagnostic of the genus Inga. Inga feuilleei (pacae or pacay) is the most commonly cultivated species in coastal Peru; closely related species (e.g., Inga edulis) share the 'guaba' name in the Caribbean and elsewhere. Cobo notes the fruit is 'very cold' in the humoral sense [muy fría], though no specific medicinal application or preparation is described beyond dietary use.

entities_mentioned.name: moral
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Morus spp. (mulberry tree)
entities_mentioned.notes: Used by Cobo as a size reference for the pacay tree.

entities_mentioned.name: limo
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Citrus aurantiifolia or related Citrus spp. (lime/lemon)
entities_mentioned.notes: Used as a leaf-shape reference; in 17th-century Iberian usage 'limo' could denote lemon or lime.

entities_mentioned.name: algarroba
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Ceratonia siliqua (carob) or Prosopis spp. (American algarrobo)
