entities_mentioned.notes: Used as a shape and size reference for the pacay pod. In a Peruvian context Cobo may also be referencing the native algarrobo (Prosopis pallida), though the carob comparison for pod shape is standard in Iberian botanical writing.

results.translator_notes: 1. 'Corbatones' (rendered as 'knees') are curved structural timbers used in shipbuilding; the term is a standard period shipbuilding [carpintería de ribera] word. 2. 'Muy fría' reflects humoral medicine classification of the fruit as cold in temperament, not merely cool to the taste; no explicit therapeutic preparation is described in this passage. 3. OCR rendered 'guaba' as 'gtiaha^' and 'hastío' as 'hastio' — both silently corrected as unambiguous. 4. The jeme is a traditional Spanish measure approximately equal to the span from thumb-tip to index-finger-tip (~18 cm); palmo is roughly a hand-width (~21 cm).

results.title_inferred: The Yaruma Tree: Description, Fruit, and Medicinal Uses
results.original_text:
CAPITULO XXXVI
De la yaruma

La yaruma es un árbol grande, mayor que una higuera y casi como un nogal. Sus hojas son parecidas a las de la higuera, pero mayores. Su fruta es tan larga como un dedo de la mano, que parece lombriz gruesa o gusano, la cual tiene el mismo nombre que el árbol; es de muy buen sabor y sana. La madera deste árbol no es buena, por ser hueca, liviana y frágil. Sus hojas son medicinales, porque majadas juntamente con los cogollos y puestas con su zumo en cualquiera llaga, aunque sea vieja, la sanan con brevedad; y los polvos de su corteza unen las heridas frescas.
results.translation:
CHAPTER XXXVI
Of the Yaruma

The yaruma is a large tree, bigger than a fig tree and nearly the size of a walnut tree. Its leaves resemble those of the fig tree, but are larger. Its fruit is as long as a finger of the hand, resembling a thick earthworm or grub, and bears the same name as the tree [i.e., yaruma]; it is of very good flavor and wholesome. The wood of this tree is not useful, being hollow, light, and fragile. Its leaves are medicinal: when pounded together with the young shoots [cogollos] and applied with their juice to any wound or sore — even an old one — they heal it quickly; and the powdered bark closes [lit. 'unites'] fresh wounds.

entities_mentioned.name: yaruma
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Cecropia peltata or a closely related Cecropia sp. (family Urticaceae/Cecropiaceae)
entities_mentioned.notes: The description — large tree with fig-like leaves, hollow and lightweight wood, finger-length worm-like fruit, and medicinal use of leaves and bark — is highly consistent with Cecropia species, commonly called 'yarumo' or 'guarumo' in Andean and tropical American regions. The chapter heading 'yanima' appears to be an OCR error for 'yaruma,' which is the form used consistently throughout the text.

results.translator_notes: The chapter heading in the source reads 'yanima,' which is almost certainly an OCR or transcription error for 'yaruma,' the name used in the body text and consistent with known colonial-period references to this tree (cf. 'yarumo'). Corrected silently in the cleaned original text. The medicinal data extracted: (1) Name: yaruma / yarumo; (2) Modern ID: Cecropia sp.; (3) Conditions treated: old and fresh wounds/sores [llagas, heridas]; (4) Preparations: (a) leaves and young shoots [cogollos] pounded together and applied topically with their expressed juice for old wounds; (b) powdered bark [polvos de su corteza] applied to close fresh wounds.

results.title_inferred: The Ambaibo Tree and the Auyuha Tree
results.original_text:
CAPITULO XXXVII
Del ambaybo

En la provincia de Santa Cruz de la Sierra, en el Perú, llaman ambaybo a cierto árbol, que no sé yo que hasta ahora se haya hallado en otra parte. Es como un gran nogal, su hoja es muy parecida a la de la higuera, pero mucho mayor. La fruta se llama ambayha; es de extraña hechura, porque parece una mano de hombre abierta y extendidos los dedos; y por ser en lo exterior parda de color de guantes, la suelen llamar los españoles guantes; y parece también en esto a un guante, que cuando está madura, tirando por la punta, sale la cáscara de cada gajo entera con la pulpa que tiene dentro, quedando colgado del pezón en figura de dedos, el corazón. Del pezón desta fruta salen cinco o seis ramos como dedos, largos de un palmo, cubiertos de una cáscara delgada, tierna y pardizca, llenos de una sustancia verde o verdosa, blanda y dulce, de sabor de higos bien maduros. Es fruta delicada y de mucha estimación.

CAPITULO XXXVIII
De la auyuha

El árbol que en la isla Española llaman auyuha es grande y de madera extremada para labrar y muy fuerte; la fruta que echa es escogida y sabe a las cermeñas, pero sale della tanta leche, que para comerla es menester echarla en agua y allí estrujarla. Es esta leche como la de la higuera y no menos molesta.
results.translation:
CHAPTER XXXVII
On the Ambaibo

In the province of Santa Cruz de la Sierra, in Peru, they call ambaibo a certain tree which, to my knowledge, has not been found anywhere else until now. It is like a large walnut tree; its leaf closely resembles that of the fig tree, but much larger. The fruit is called ambayha; it is of a strange form, for it resembles an open human hand with the fingers spread out. Because its exterior is brown in color, like the color of gloves [Sp. guantes], the Spaniards commonly call it guantes [gloves]. It also resembles a glove in the following way: when the fruit is ripe, by pulling from the tip, the rind of each section comes away whole along with the pulp inside it, leaving the core hanging from the stem in the shape of fingers. From the stem of this fruit there grow five or six branches like fingers, about a hand's-breadth [palmo] in length, covered with a thin, tender, and brownish rind, filled with a green or greenish substance, soft and sweet, with the flavor of fully ripe figs. It is a delicate fruit and highly esteemed.

CHAPTER XXXVIII
On the Auyuha

The tree called auyuha on the island of Hispaniola is large, with timber excellent for working and very strong. The fruit it bears is fine and tastes like [cermeñas, a variety of small pear], but it exudes so much latex [leche, lit. milk] that in order to eat it one must place it in water and squeeze it there. This latex is like that of the fig tree, and no less troublesome.

entities_mentioned.name: ambaybo / ambayha (tree and fruit)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Cecropia sp. (possibly Cecropia pachystachya or related species, family Urticaceae/Cecropiaceae)
entities_mentioned.notes: Described as native to Santa Cruz de la Sierra, Bolivia; large walnut-sized tree with fig-like leaves; fruit resembles a hand with spread fingers, hence the Spanish colloquial name 'guantes' (gloves). The finger-like lobes filled with soft, sweet, greenish pulp tasting of ripe figs is consistent with Cecropia fruit, though some scholars have proposed Carica or Vasconcellea spp. No medicinal use is described in this passage.

entities_mentioned.name: auyuha (tree and fruit)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Possibly Ficus or Brosimum sp., or alternatively a member of Moraceae; identification uncertain
entities_mentioned.notes: Described as found on Hispaniola; large tree with very strong, workable timber; fruit resembles cermeñas (a small pear variety); produces copious milky latex requiring the fruit to be soaked and squeezed in water before eating. The latex compared to that of the fig tree. No confident modern identification; the latex and fruit characteristics could suggest Moraceae. No medicinal use described.

results.translator_notes: 1. 'no sé yo' was garbled in the source as 'no s4 yo' — corrected as an obvious OCR error (4 for é). 2. 'otra parle' corrected to 'otra parte' (OCR error). 3. 'una mano de hombre abierta*' — the asterisk appears to be an OCR artifact and has been silently removed. 4. 'tirando por 1^ punta' corrected to 'tirando por la punta' (OCR error). 5. 'fruta delicada v de' corrected to 'fruta delicada y de' (OCR rendering of 'y' as 'v'). 6. 'cermeñas' is a period Spanish term for a type of small, early-ripening pear (Pyrus communis var.); retained in brackets in translation. 7. 'leche' (lit. milk) rendered as 'latex' for botanical accuracy, with the literal meaning noted. 8. No medicinal preparations are described in either chapter; these are purely descriptive botanical/natural history entries.

results.title_inferred: On Cassia Fistula: Introduced and Wild American Varieties
results.original_text:
CAPITULO XXXIX
De la caña fistola

Fuera de la caña fistola que, traída de la India oriental, se ha plantado y nace con abundancia en muchas partes destas Indias occidentales, que es la que se administra en las boticas, se halla en algunas provincias otra silvestre propia de la tierra, que parece del mismo linaje que la de la India, aunque distinta en especie. El árbol que produce esta caña fistola es mucho mayor que el de la otra: la hoja del uno y del otro es la misma, pero las cañas desta silvestre, dado que son del mismo color y hechura que la caña fistola de la India, son mucho más largas, gruesas y por dentro vanas, sin aquella pulpa que tiene la caña fistola buena. Solamente se hallan dentro de los canutos desta salvaje unas telillas pegadas a ellos parecidas a la pulpa de la verdadera caña fistola.
results.translation:
CHAPTER XXXIX
On Cassia Fistula

Besides the cassia fistula [caña fistola] that, brought from the East Indies, has been planted and grows abundantly in many parts of these West Indies—being the variety dispensed in apothecaries [boticas]—there is found in certain provinces another wild kind, native to the land, which appears to be of the same lineage as that of India, though distinct in species. The tree that produces this wild cassia fistula is much larger than that of the other variety. The leaf of the one and the other is the same; however, the pods of this wild kind, although they are the same color and shape as those of the Indian cassia fistula, are much longer, thicker, and hollow on the inside, lacking the pulp [pulpa] that the good cassia fistula possesses. Within the segments [canutos] of this wild variety there are found only thin membranes [telillas] adhering to them, resembling the pulp of the true cassia fistula.

entities_mentioned.name: caña fistola (introduced, from East Indies)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Cassia fistula L. (Golden Shower Tree)
entities_mentioned.notes: The cultivated variety brought from the East Indies and sold in colonial apothecaries; used as a laxative/purgative in classical and colonial pharmacopeia.

entities_mentioned.name: caña fistola silvestre (wild, native American variety)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Possibly Cassia grandis L.f. or a related New World Cassia/Senna species
entities_mentioned.notes: Described as similar in leaf and external pod appearance to Cassia fistula but larger tree, longer and thicker pods, hollow interior with only thin membranous partitions rather than the characteristic sweet pulp. Cassia grandis is a strong candidate given its size and New World distribution, though other large native Cassia/Senna spp. cannot be ruled out.

results.translator_notes: Several OCR errors were silently corrected: 'deístas' → 'destas'; 'canas' → 'cañas'; 'Iniena' → 'buena' (contextually clear); 'recidas' → 'parecidas' (partial OCR drop of word-initial syllable); 'cais' → 'caña' (line-break OCR corruption). The segment does not describe a specific preparation method or treated ailment for the wild variety, implying it was considered medicinally inferior or not used in practice. The therapeutic use of the introduced Cassia fistula as a gentle purgative is implied by its presence in 'boticas' (apothecary shops).

results.title_inferred: The Bean Tree (Árbol de Habas) of Peru
results.original_text:
CAPITULO XL
Del árbol de habas

En este reino del Perú nace cierto árbol, cuyo fruto son unas vainas como de habas verdes, un poco mayores; el árbol es pequeño, que apenas se levanta dos estados, y en la apariencia, poco hermoso, respecto de echar sus ramas esparcidas y desiguales, de manera que no hacen copa. Mas es hermosísimo cuando se viste de flor; ésta la echa en racimos o ramilletes, largos medio palmo, que se van adelgazando hacia la punta en forma piramidal. Son estas flores de un finísimo colorado, pero de ningún olor. En el verano pierde la hoja este árbol, y a la entrada del invierno se viste de nuevo. Echa las hojas de tres en tres, las cuales son como las del nogal, algo más anchas, puntiagudas, lisas, tiesas y de agradable verde. Tiene este árbol todas las ramas y cogollos sembrados de unas espinas como de rosal o de zarza; echa unas vainas de una tercia de largo, de poco más de un dedo de ancho y casi tan gruesas como anchas; la cáscara es verde, dura y correosa, y dentro tienen unas pepitas poco mayores que habas; son verdinegras y tan tiernas como habas verdes, las cuales se comen asadas, pero es fruta grosera y de ruin sabor.
results.translation:
CHAPTER XL
On the Bean Tree

In this kingdom of Peru there grows a certain tree whose fruit consists of pods resembling those of green beans [habas verdes], though somewhat larger. The tree is small, barely rising two fathoms [estados] in height, and in appearance not particularly handsome, owing to its branches spreading out unevenly and irregularly, so that they do not form a proper crown. Yet it is exceedingly beautiful when it comes into flower; the blossoms appear in clusters or bouquets [racimos o ramilletes] about half a span [medio palmo] in length, tapering toward the tip in a pyramidal form. These flowers are of a very fine red [colorado], but have no scent whatsoever. In summer this tree sheds its leaves, and at the onset of winter it clothes itself anew. Its leaves grow in threes and resemble those of the walnut [nogal], though somewhat broader, pointed at the tip, smooth, stiff, and of a pleasing green. All the branches and new shoots of this tree are studded with thorns like those of a rosebush or briar. It produces pods about one-third of a vara [tercia] in length, little more than a finger's width across, and nearly as thick as they are wide; the outer skin [cáscara] is green, hard, and leathery, and within are seeds [pepitas] somewhat larger than broad beans [habas]. These are dark greenish-black and as tender as green beans; they are eaten roasted, but it is a coarse fruit of poor flavor.

entities_mentioned.name: árbol de habas
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Erythrina falcata or a closely related Erythrina species (possibly E. edulis, known as 'poroto' or 'chachafruto' in Andean regions)
entities_mentioned.notes: The description — trifoliate leaves resembling walnut, pyramidal racemes of vivid red flowers, thorny branches, leathery pods with large seeds eaten roasted — is consistent with the genus Erythrina, which is widespread in Peru. E. edulis is cultivated for its edible seeds. The common name 'árbol de habas' (bean tree) aligns with vernacular usage for Erythrina in the Andes. Confidence is medium rather than high because Cobo does not provide a Quechua name or more precise locality, and other leguminous trees could partially match.

results.translator_notes: Several OCR errors were silently corrected: 'íiIk)' → 'árbol'; 'TO' → 'palmo'; 'lungún' → 'ningún'; 'infierno' → 'invierno' (an obvious contextual error — the tree re-leafs at the onset of winter, not hell); '\m' → 'las'; 'Tainas' → 'vainas'; 'mk' → 'más'; 'm verdinegras' → 'son verdinegras'; 'hahaifi' → 'habas'. The word 'estado' is a unit of linear measure roughly equivalent to a fathom or about 1.67 meters; 'tercia' is one-third of a vara (approximately 28 cm). No medicinal use or preparation method is described in this segment; the tree is treated here purely as a botanical and food-use entry.

results.title_inferred: The Olozapote Tree
results.original_text:
CAPITULO XLI
Del olozapote

Es el olozapote un árbol de la grandeza de un crecido naranjo, muy copado y de buena sombra; tiene la hoja grande y tiesa, de la forma y tamaño de la del limo; echa la flor en racimos; y su fruta es colorada, semejante en la hechura y color al dátil; tiénenla los indios por sabrosa y plantan este árbol en sus casas.
results.translation:
CHAPTER XLI
Of the Olozapote

The olozapote is a tree of the size of a large orange tree, very broad-crowned and of good shade; it has a large and stiff leaf, of the shape and size of that of the lime [limo]; it bears its flowers in clusters [racimos]; and its fruit is red, resembling a date [dátil] in shape and color; the Indians consider it tasty and plant this tree near their houses.

entities_mentioned.name: olozapote
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Pouteria sapota (mamey sapote) or possibly Licania platypus (sansapote); the red date-like fruit description may point toward Licania platypus or a related Chrysobalanaceae species, though 'olozapote' is sometimes associated with Pouteria.
entities_mentioned.notes: The name 'olozapote' is not a standard colonial term and may be a regional Nahuatl-derived variant. The description of red, date-shaped fruit and large stiff lime-like leaves does not map cleanly to a single well-known species. No medicinal use is described in this passage; the tree is noted primarily as a food and ornamental/shade plant cultivated by indigenous people near their homes.

entities_mentioned.name: limo
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Citrus aurantiifolia or Citrus limetta (lime or sweet lime)
entities_mentioned.notes: Used as a comparative reference for leaf shape and size only.

entities_mentioned.name: dátil
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Phoenix dactylifera (date palm fruit)
entities_mentioned.notes: Used as a comparative reference for the shape and red color of the olozapote fruit.

results.translator_notes: Several OCR artifacts were silently corrected: 'gran¬deza' (line-break hyphen) → 'grandeza'; 'copa-de' → 'copado'; 'fie' → 'de'; 'raci-' (truncated line) → 'racimos' (implied by context); 'tiéuenla W indios' → 'tiénenla los indios'; 'íjÍqI' → 'árbol'. The passage contains no medical or pharmacological content; no drug/treatment extraction is applicable.

results.title_inferred: The Parca Tree: Hair, Depilatory, and Jaundice Uses
results.original_text:
CAPITULO XLII
De la parca

En este reino del Perú llaman parca a un árbol no muy grande, cuyas ramas y raíces son de color amarillo debajo de corteza parda que tira a blanca. Su cocimiento sirve para enrubiar los cabellos. Echa unas vainas grandecillas, y dentro, cierto fruto como habas, que comen los indios tostado y es algo sabroso. Su temperamento es caliente y seco. Notan los indios, que si al aceite en que hubiere hervido la cáscara deste árbol se echa un poco de oropimente y cal viva, y con ello se untaren cualquiera parte vellosa, dejando puesto el remedio por veinticuatro horas, al cabo, lavando la tal parte con agua caliente, queda limpia de todo pelo. Y el cocimiento de las raíces, ramas y hojas, usado de ordinario, es contra la itericia.
results.translation:
CHAPTER XLII
Of the Parca

In this kingdom of Peru they call 'parca' a tree of no great size, whose branches and roots are yellow in color beneath a bark that is brownish tending toward white. A decoction [cocimiento] of it serves to lighten [enrubiar, lit. 'to make reddish-blond'] the hair. It produces sizeable pods, and within them a certain fruit resembling broad beans [habas], which the Indians eat roasted and which is somewhat tasty. Its temperament [in the Galenic humoral sense] is hot and dry. The Indians observe that if a little orpiment [oropimente, arsenic trisulfide] and quicklime [cal viva] are added to the oil in which the bark of this tree has been boiled, and this mixture is applied to any hairy part of the body, leaving the remedy in place for twenty-four hours, and then washing the said part with hot water, it is left entirely free of hair. And the decoction of the roots, branches, and leaves, used regularly, is a remedy against jaundice [itericia, i.e., icterus].

entities_mentioned.name: parca
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Possibly Caesalpinia tinctoria or a related Caesalpinia / Tara species (Fabaceae); alternatively sometimes identified with Cassia or Berberis species given the yellow wood and pods
entities_mentioned.notes: The yellow wood/roots, large pods with bean-like seeds eaten toasted, and use in hair-lightening decoctions are suggestive of a leguminous tree such as Caesalpinia or Tara, both known in Andean Peru. However, 'parca' is not a widely corroborated vernacular name in the modern literature and the identification remains uncertain.

entities_mentioned.name: oropimente
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Orpiment (arsenic trisulfide, As2S3)
entities_mentioned.notes: A mineral pigment and historical depilatory ingredient; used here as a component of the hair-removal compound.

entities_mentioned.name: cal viva
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Quicklime (calcium oxide, CaO)
entities_mentioned.notes: Combined with orpiment and the bark oil to form a caustic depilatory mixture—a preparation type well attested in early modern materia medica.

medical_botanical_extracts.name: parca
medical_botanical_extracts.preparation_method: 1) Decoction of branches/roots applied to hair to lighten it. 2) Bark boiled in oil, combined with orpiment and quicklime, applied topically for 24 hours then washed off with hot water as a depilatory. 3) Decoction of roots, branches, and leaves taken regularly as a remedy for jaundice.

results.translator_notes: The caret symbol ('^') in the OCR source before 'árbol' appears to be an OCR artifact for the article 'a' or a scribal abbreviation mark; corrected silently to 'a un árbol' based on syntactic context. 'Itericia' is the period Spanish spelling of ictericia (jaundice / icterus). 'Enrubiar' literally means to make auburn or blond, here indicating a hair-lightening or colour-altering cosmetic use. The depilatory formula (bark oil + orpiment + quicklime) is a classical Arabic-derived recipe transmitted through Spanish medical tradition.

results.title_inferred: The Suhigi Tree of Santa Cruz de la Sierra
results.original_text:
CAPITULO XLIII
Del suhigi

En la provincia de Santa Cruz de la Sierra nace un árbol llamado suhigi. Es muy alto y de hojas grandes y anchas; da unas vainas grandes y en ellas una fruta muy parecida al maní o a piñones.
results.translation:
CHAPTER XLIII
On the Suhigi

In the province of Santa Cruz de la Sierra there grows a tree called suhigi. It is very tall and has large, broad leaves; it produces large pods, and within them a fruit very similar to peanuts [maní] or pine nuts [piñones].

entities_mentioned.name: suhigi
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Possibly a leguminous tree native to the Bolivian lowlands (e.g., a species of Dipteryx, Hymenaea, or related Fabaceae); insufficient detail for confident assignment
entities_mentioned.notes: Described as a tall tree with large broad leaves and large pods bearing nuts resembling peanuts or pine nuts. Located in the province of Santa Cruz de la Sierra (present-day Bolivia). The name 'suhigi' does not correspond to a widely attested botanical name; it may be a local indigenous term. The pod-and-nut description is consistent with several large Fabaceae genera of the region.

entities_mentioned.name: maní
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Arachis hypogaea (peanut)
entities_mentioned.notes: Used here as a comparative reference for the appearance of the suhigi fruit, not as a subject plant in its own right.

entities_mentioned.name: piñones
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Pine nuts / seeds of Pinus spp. (or possibly New World analogues such as Araucaria seeds)
entities_mentioned.notes: Used as a secondary comparator for the suhigi fruit's appearance. In colonial South American contexts 'piñones' sometimes referred to Araucaria araucana seeds rather than Old World pine nuts.

results.translator_notes: No medical preparation or therapeutic use is described in this segment; the passage is purely botanical and geographic. The OCR rendered 'suhigi' with a final 'L' character in one instance ('suhigL'), corrected silently to 'suhigi' as the intended word is unambiguous from context and the chapter heading. The segment is notably brief and may represent an incomplete or transitional chapter in the source manuscript.

results.title_inferred: The Cuagilote Tree (Cuauhxiotl)
results.original_text:
CAPITULO XLIV
Del cuagilote

Los españoles, corrompiendo el nombre, llaman cuagilote a un árbol que los indios mexicanos nombran cuauhxiotl; el cual es del grandor de un naranjo muy copado; echa las hojas de tres en tres, como flor de lis, y la de en medio es mayor, y son parecidas a las hojas de la hierba mora. La flor es blanca, de hechura de campanilla, y la fruta parecida a un cohombro encoryado de un jeme de largo, acanalada y de un color amarillo tirante a morado. La semilla son unas pepitas como lentejas, algo menores. Es esta fruta muy dulce y huele a algalia, mas pierde pronto el olor, que por cierto es muy suave, y si lo conservara mucho tiempo, fuera de gran estimación.
results.translation:
CHAPTER XLIV
Of the cuagilote

The Spaniards, corrupting the name, call cuagilote a tree that the Mexican Indians name cuauhxiotl. It is the size of a very full-canopied orange tree; it puts forth its leaves in groups of three, like a fleur-de-lis, the middle leaf being the largest, and they resemble the leaves of black nightshade [hierba mora]. The flower is white, shaped like a little bell, and the fruit resembles a curved cucumber [cohombro encurvado], a hand-span [jeme] in length, ribbed [acanalada], and of a yellow color tending toward purple. The seeds are small pip-like kernels [pepitas] resembling lentils, though somewhat smaller. This fruit is very sweet and smells of civet musk [algalia], but it quickly loses its fragrance, which is indeed very pleasant; if it were to retain it for a long time, the fruit would be held in great esteem.

entities_mentioned.name: cuagilote / cuauhxiotl
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Parmentiera edulis (cuagilote or cuajilote), family Bignoniaceae
entities_mentioned.notes: The description of trifoliate leaves, white bell-shaped flowers, ribbed elongated yellowish-purple fruit, and sweet edible pulp with a musky scent is consistent with Parmentiera edulis, a tree native to Mexico and Central America. No medicinal use is described in this passage; the entry focuses on morphology and organoleptic qualities.

entities_mentioned.name: hierba mora
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Solanum nigrum (black nightshade)
entities_mentioned.notes: Used here as a comparative reference for leaf shape only, not as a subject plant.

results.translator_notes: 'Cohombro encoryado' contains an apparent OCR error; the intended word is 'encurvado' (curved/bent), which matches the known morphology of Parmentiera edulis fruit. 'Algalia' refers to civet musk, a common early modern fragrance reference. 'Jeme' is a traditional Spanish unit of length measured from the tip of the thumb to the tip of the index finger when spread apart, approximately 7–8 cm. No medicinal or pharmacological information is present in this segment.

results.title_inferred: The Wild Tamarind Tree
results.original_text:
CAPITULO XLV
Del tamarindo silvestre

Este árbol es natural de la Nueva España y tan grande como un olivo muy hojoso, y crece muy en breve. Su hoja se parece a la del lentisco; entre ellas tiene muchas y muy agudas espinas; da unas vainillas semejantes a las del tamarindo hortense; tienen de largo un jeme, están retorcidas a manera de herradura, son blancas y coloradas, y cuando están de sazón se abren; tienen dentro una sustancia blanca que se come como la del pacae, pero en menos cantidad. Es fruta silvestre y de poca codicia.
results.translation:
CHAPTER XLV
On the Wild Tamarind

This tree is native to New Spain and as large as a very leafy olive tree, and it grows very quickly. Its leaf resembles that of the mastic tree [lentisco]; among the leaves it has many very sharp thorns; it produces small pods similar to those of the garden tamarind; they measure a span [jeme] in length, are twisted in the manner of a horseshoe, are white and red, and when they are ripe they open; inside they contain a white substance that is eaten like that of the pacae [pacay], but in lesser quantity. It is a wild fruit and little sought after.

entities_mentioned.name: tamarindo silvestre (wild tamarind)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Lysiloma acapulcense or possibly Pithecellobium dulce (Manila tamarind / guamúchil)
entities_mentioned.notes: The horseshoe-twisted pods, white-and-red coloration, and edible white pulp are characteristic of Pithecellobium dulce, a common native Mexican legume sometimes called 'wild tamarind.' Lysiloma spp. are also candidates. The comparison to garden tamarind (Tamarindus indica) and the pacae (Inga feuilleei) is consistent with a leguminous tree with edible pod pulp.

entities_mentioned.name: lentisco
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Pistacia lentiscus (mastic tree)
entities_mentioned.notes: Used here as a comparative leaf-shape reference, not a plant under direct description.

entities_mentioned.name: tamarindo hortense (garden tamarind)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Tamarindus indica
entities_mentioned.notes: Used as a comparative reference for pod shape.

entities_mentioned.name: pacae
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Inga feuilleei (pacay / ice-cream bean)
entities_mentioned.notes: Andean/Mesoamerican legume with edible white pulp surrounding seeds; used here as a flavor and texture comparison for the wild tamarind's inner substance.

results.translator_notes: The word 'marindo' in the source text ('vainillas semejantes a las del marindo hortense') is an OCR error for 'tamarindo hortense,' silently corrected. The unit 'jeme' is a traditional Spanish measure equal to the span from the tip of the thumb to the tip of the index finger, approximately 8–9 cm. No medicinal use or preparation method is described in this segment; it is purely botanical and descriptive. The phrase 'de poca codicia' is rendered as 'little sought after,' meaning it is not particularly prized or desired as food.

results.title_inferred: On the Hicaco and the Canela de la Tierra (Wild Cinnamon)
results.original_text:
CAPITULO XLVI
Del hicaco

Este es un árbol que nace en las costas de la mar, tan pequeño, que no sube más que uno o dos estados de la tierra; de buen parecer; cuya hoja es semejante a la del madroño. La fruta que lleva es como un albaricoque, más agradable a la vista que al gusto, porque tiene color de manzana arrebolada; la cáscara es como de manzana y la carne blanca y esponjosa nada apetitosa, porque es fruta silvestre y grosera; tiene muy gran hueso en proporción de su tamaño.

CAPITULO XLVII
De la canela de la tierra

En las provincias que por la parte oriental confinan con la diócesis de Quito, que aún están por pacificar, nace gran copia de unos árboles muy grandes que llamamos de canela, porque su corteza y hojas huelen a canela. Pero lo que más se estima de estos árboles son unos capullos que dan, cuyo sabor y olor más se asemeja al de la canela; los cuales se gastan en la provincia de Quito en lugar de canela. Yo he visto y probado algunos que se han traído a Lima, mas no es su sabor tan vivo y gustoso como el de la canela de la India Oriental.
results.translation:
CHAPTER XLVI
On the Hicaco

This is a tree that grows along the seacoasts, so small that it rises no more than one or two fathoms [estados] from the ground; it is of pleasing appearance, and its leaf resembles that of the strawberry tree [madroño, Arbutus unedo]. The fruit it bears is like an apricot [albaricoque], more pleasing to the eye than to the taste, for it has the color of a flushed apple; the skin is like that of an apple, and the flesh is white and spongy, not at all appetizing, for it is a wild and coarse fruit; it has a very large stone [pit] in proportion to its size.

CHAPTER XLVII
On the Canela de la Tierra [Local Cinnamon]

In the provinces that border on the eastern side of the diocese of Quito—which have yet to be pacified—there grows an abundance of very large trees which we call cinnamon trees [de canela], because their bark and leaves smell of cinnamon. But what is most prized from these trees are the buds [capullos] they produce, whose flavor and scent most closely resemble those of cinnamon; these are used throughout the province of Quito as a substitute for cinnamon. I have myself seen and tasted some that were brought to Lima, but their flavor is not as lively and agreeable as that of the cinnamon of the East Indies.

entities_mentioned.name: hicaco
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Chrysobalanus icaco (coco plum)
entities_mentioned.notes: Cobo's description—coastal habitat, small tree, apple-like colored fruit resembling an apricot, large stone, white spongy flesh—is consistent with Chrysobalanus icaco. No medicinal use is described in this passage; the account is purely botanical and organoleptic.

entities_mentioned.name: madroño
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Arbutus unedo (strawberry tree)
entities_mentioned.notes: Used as a comparative reference for the shape of the hicaco leaf; not itself a subject of the entry.

entities_mentioned.name: canela de la tierra
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Ocotea quixos (syn. Cinnamomum quixos; American cinnamon or ishpingo)
entities_mentioned.notes: Cobo explicitly locates this tree in the eastern Andean provinces bordering Quito (the historic 'País de la Canela') and notes that the dried flower buds (capullos) rather than the bark are the commercially valued part. These buds correspond to the spice known today as ishpingo or canela de Quito. The plant's bark and leaves are noted to smell of cinnamon, which is consistent with Ocotea/Cinnamomum quixos. Preparation method: buds used whole as a cinnamon substitute in cooking.

results.translator_notes: Several OCR errors were silently corrected: 'luien parecer' → 'buen parecer'; 'alharicoque' → 'albaricoque' (standard spelling); 'arrebola-da' retained as 'arrebolada' (flushed/reddened); 'd€>' → 'de' (clear OCR artifact); 'be visto' → 'he visto'; 'dia Oriental' → 'India Oriental'. The word 'estados' (a unit of height roughly equivalent to a fathom or adult body-height, c. 1.7 m) is preserved in brackets. 'Capullos' is translated as 'buds' in the botanical sense, consistent with the known spice product of Ocotea quixos. The reference to provinces 'not yet pacified' is a notable colonial-era geopolitical marker placing the account in the Amazonian piedmont east of Quito.

results.title_inferred: The Quishuar Tree of Peru
results.original_text:
CAPITULO XLIX
Del quishuar

En el Perú llaman quishuar a un árbol mediano del grandor de un duraznero; es tan parecido al olivo, que muchos, viéndolo de lejos, se engañan, teniéndolo por olivo. La hoja es como la del olivo, aunque en las tierras templadas la produce mayor; por dentro es verde y lisa, y por fuera, blanquecina y vellosa, y asimismo las puntas de los pimpollos son vellosas y blanquecinas. Echa una flor en racimos de medio palmo de largo, que hacia la punta se van adelgazando, no mayor que la del olivo, salvo que es de un color naranjado fino; la cual echa en tanta cantidad, que todo el árbol se cubre de ella y huele algún tanto a azafrán; y la misma flor molida suele servir de azafrán en los guisos y les da color de amarillo. La madera de este árbol es muy recia, y de sus varas hacen los indios sus taclas, que son los arados de mano con que ellos labran la tierra, y para este efecto las plantan en sus casas. Este árbol es el que más sufre el frío de todos los demás, porque en los páramos frigidísimos de las provincias del Collao, donde no se dan otros árboles, nace el quishuar; fuera de que también se da en tierras templadas. Llámase quishuar en la lengua quichua, y en la aimará, colli.
results.translation:
CHAPTER XLIX
On the Quishuar

In Peru they call quishuar a medium-sized tree of the stature of a peach tree [duraznero]; it so closely resembles the olive that many people, seeing it from a distance, are deceived and take it for an olive tree. The leaf is like that of the olive, though in temperate lands it produces a larger leaf; on the inside it is green and smooth, and on the outside, whitish and downy; likewise the tips of the young shoots are downy and whitish. It puts forth a flower in clusters [racimos] half a palm's length long, tapering toward the tip, no larger than the olive flower, except that it is a fine orange color; and it produces these flowers in such quantity that the entire tree is covered with them, and they smell somewhat of saffron. The same flower, when ground, is commonly used in place of saffron in stews [guisos] and imparts a yellow color to them. The wood of this tree is very hard, and from its branches the Indians make their taclas [foot-plows], which are the hand-operated plows [arados de mano] with which they till the earth; for this purpose they plant the trees near their houses. This tree tolerates cold better than any other, for in the extremely frigid highland plains [páramos] of the provinces of the Collao, where no other trees grow, the quishuar grows; beyond this, it also thrives in temperate lands. It is called quishuar in the Quechua tongue, and in Aymara, colli.

entities_mentioned.name: quishuar (also colli in Aymara)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Buddleja incana (syn. Buddleja coriacea)
entities_mentioned.notes: A native Andean tree well known for its extreme cold tolerance at high altitudes (above 3,000 m), its olive-like foliage with white-downy undersides, and its orange-yellow flowers used as a saffron substitute. The hard wood was traditionally used to make taclas (Andean foot-plows). Cobo's description matches Buddleja incana/coriacea closely. The Aymara name 'colli' (also spelled 'kolli') is attested in ethnobotanical literature.

entities_mentioned.name: tacla (arado de mano)
entities_mentioned.notes: The traditional Andean foot-plow (chakitaqlla in Quechua), made from the hard wood of the quishuar. Not a plant entity but a cultural artifact noted in the botanical context.

entities_mentioned.name: Achiote / Bixa orellana [footnote]
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Bixa orellana
entities_mentioned.notes: This entity appears only in an editorial footnote appended to the transcribed segment, not in Cobo's main chapter text. The footnote lists synonyms: Achiote, Achate, Bixar, Mandar, Mandúl, Mandara, Onoto, Roucou (French Guianese term), and Bixa (Spanish term). It describes the red pulpy substance surrounding seeds of Bixa orellana, used as a dye and condiment throughout South America. This is likely a cross-reference note by a later editor, not by Cobo himself.

results.translator_notes: 1. The source OCR contains several broken words and ligature errors (e.g., 'ár-M mediano' for 'árbol mediano', 'mu¬flios' for 'muchos', 'divo' for 'salvo', 'mde V lisa' for 'verde y lisa', 'Bÿéndolo' for 'teniéndolo'). All have been silently corrected where unambiguous and reflected in the cleaned original text. 2. The footnote (marked with a superscript 5 in the OCR) concerning Achiote/Bixa orellana appears to be an interpolation by a later editor or transcriber—it is not part of Cobo's chapter on the quishuar and interrupts the text mid-sentence. It has been preserved in the cleaned original for fidelity but is noted as extraneous. 3. 'Tacla' (more commonly 'chakitaqlla') is glossed by Cobo himself as 'arados de mano' [hand-plows]; this gloss is retained in brackets in the translation. 4. No medical preparation is described in this segment; the only quasi-medicinal/culinary use is the ground flowers as a saffron substitute for coloring and flavoring stews.

results.title_inferred: The Guarango Tree (Chapter 50)
results.original_text:
CAPITULO L

Del guarango

En el Perú tienen nombre de guarango cinco o seis especies de árboles muy parecidos entre sí, que casi todos echan unas vainas como algarrobas. Al que produce las mejores llaman los españoles algarrobo de las Indias, el es de diferente casta que el algarrobo de España. Es árbol mediano, del grandor de un olivo; la hoja es muy menuda, la cual en tamaño y hechura es parecidísima a la hoja del helécho o a la de la sabina. La fruta del guarango son unas vainas como algarrobas, aunque no tan anchas y largas, cuyas pepitas, en el color y lisura, son como las de nuestras algarrobas, salvo que tienen muchas espinas. Es fruta ésta buena de comer, y los indios, en algunas partes, hacen de ella harina y pan; y hay provincias enteras donde los naturales no tienen otro mantenimiento sino estas algarrobas. Cómelas también el ganado y engorda con ellas.

En estos valles de los Llanos se cría mucha, y es grande la copia de ganado que de ella se mantiene; y a su tiempo encierran los españoles en trojes cantidad de esta algarroba para sustento de las bestias, porque les es de tanta sustancia como cualquiera grano.

Echa este árbol cierta resina negra por las ramas, que como va cayendo en tierra, se va cuajando, y destila a veces gran cantidad della; la cual, molida en polvos, aprieta la dentadura; y en los valles que hay viñas, se aprovechan della para marcar los botijos, porque al fuego se deshace con agua y queda hecha tinta muy negra, la cual, helándose después, no se quita ni borra con agua ni con otra cosa; y también de esta resina hacen tinta para escribir. De la madera del guarango se hacen estacas para las viñas, y duran muchos años sin pudrirse; y en muchos valles no tienen otra madera para los edificios, aunque es nudosa, torcida y que no se pueden sacar tablas anchas, por tener todo el tronco a manera de nervios y costurones; pero es de grandísima dura y para el fuego y carbón mejor que encina. Los valles que más abundan de estos guarangos son los de Ica, Nasca, Huanhacho y Casma en este arzobispado de Lima; y en el obispado de Trujillo, Chicama, Guadalupe y Catacaos.
results.translation:
CHAPTER 50

On the Guarango

In Peru, the name guarango is given to five or six species of trees that closely resemble one another, nearly all of which produce pods similar to carob [algarrobas]. The one that bears the finest pods the Spaniards call the 'algarrobo of the Indies' [carob of the Indies]; it is of a different kind from the algarrobo of Spain. It is a medium-sized tree, about the size of an olive tree; its leaf is very small, and in size and shape closely resembles the leaf of a fern or of a savin [juniper]. The fruit of the guarango consists of pods like carobs, though not as wide and long, whose seeds, in color and smoothness, resemble those of our carobs, except that they have many spines. This fruit is good to eat, and the Indians in some places make flour and bread from it; and there are entire provinces where the native people have no other sustenance than these carob pods. Livestock also eat them and grow fat on them.

This tree grows abundantly in the valleys of the Llanos [coastal plains], and great numbers of livestock are maintained on it; and in season the Spaniards store large quantities of these carob pods in granaries [trojes] for the sustenance of their beasts of burden, since the pods are as nourishing as any grain.

This tree exudes a certain black resin from its branches; as it falls to the ground it congeals, and sometimes a great quantity of it drips down. When ground into powder, it tightens [strengthens] the teeth; and in the valleys where there are vineyards, it is used to mark clay jars [botijos], because when heated with water it dissolves and produces a very black ink which, once it sets, cannot be removed or erased by water or by any other means. This resin is also used to make writing ink. From the wood of the guarango, stakes are made for vineyards, and they last many years without rotting; and in many valleys there is no other timber for buildings, although the wood is knotty and twisted and broad planks cannot be cut from it, since the entire trunk is threaded through with fibers and thick ridges [costurones]; yet it is extremely hard-wearing, and for fuel and charcoal it is better than holm oak [encina]. The valleys most abundantly supplied with these guarango trees are those of Ica, Nasca, Huanhacho, and Casma in this archbishopric of Lima; and in the bishopric of Trujillo, Chicama, Guadalupe, and Catacaos.

[Footnote 6, from editor:] And a species of very tasty porridge called yupisin. The algarrobo of the Indies is called in proper Quechua Ttacco, and in coastal Yunga Ong, and its fruit Puño.

[Footnote 7, from editor:] He refers to certain nations of the ancient Tucumán and Paraguay.

entities_mentioned.name: guarango / algarrobo de las Indias
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Prosopis pallida or related Prosopis spp. (mesquite / Peruvian carob); possibly also encompasses Caesalpinia spinosa (also called guarango), though the pod and ecological descriptions favor Prosopis
entities_mentioned.notes: Cobo groups five or six species under 'guarango.' The coastal valley habitat, carob-like pods used for food and fodder, and black resin strongly suggest Prosopis pallida (huarango/algarrobo). Caesalpinia spinosa also bears the name guarango but is not a coastal flood-plain tree. Quechua name given in footnote as 'Ttacco'; coastal Yunga name 'Ong.' Medical use: resin ground into powder applied to teeth to tighten/strengthen them. No explicit preparation beyond grinding described.

entities_mentioned.name: resina negra del guarango (black resin)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Resinous exudate of Prosopis pallida
entities_mentioned.notes: Condition treated: loose or weak teeth [aprieta la dentadura]. Preparation: collected as it drips and congeals on the ground, then ground into powder and applied to the teeth. Also used as a permanent marking ink for pottery and as writing ink.

entities_mentioned.name: algarrobo de España
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Ceratonia siliqua (common carob)
entities_mentioned.notes: Referenced as a comparative species; Cobo explicitly states the guarango is of a different kind.

entities_mentioned.name: helécho
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Fern (Polypodiopsida spp.)
entities_mentioned.notes: Used as a leaf-shape comparison only.

entities_mentioned.name: sabina
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Juniperus sabina (savin juniper)
entities_mentioned.notes: Used as a leaf-shape comparison only.

entities_mentioned.name: encina
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Quercus ilex (holm oak)
entities_mentioned.notes: Referenced as a comparative standard for fuel and charcoal quality; guarango wood is said to surpass it.

results.translator_notes: 1. 'Guarango' in the OCR source appears as 'guaran^go' and similar broken forms; silently corrected throughout. 'Aprieta la dentadura' literally means 'tightens/firms the teeth' — the implied medicinal sense is consolidating loose teeth or strengthening the gums. 2. 'Trojes' (granaries/storage bins) retained with gloss. 'Botijos' (earthenware jars) similarly glossed. 'Costurones' (thick seams or ridges in the wood grain) is a somewhat unusual term; translation renders it as 'thick ridges.' 3. The footnotes in the source are editorial additions by a later editor of Cobo's text, not by Cobo himself; they are labeled accordingly in the translation. 4. The valley name 'Giianhacho' in the OCR is almost certainly 'Guanhacho' (modern Huanhacho, near Trujillo); corrected silently. 5. The identification of guarango as Prosopis rather than Caesalpinia spinosa rests on the coastal valley habitat, the fodder use, and the resin description; a specialist in Andean ethnobotany should verify.

results.title_inferred: The Spiny Guarango (Yara) and Related Species
results.original_text:
CAPITULO LI
Del guarango-espino

Por tener este guarango muchas espinas le llamamos espino, a diferencia del primero, que no las tiene; pero los indios de este reino lo llaman yara. En esta especie de guarango se halla macho y hembra: el macho da fruto, y a la hembra se le cae en flor; una casta de la yara crece alta, y ora se extiende y arrastra por la tierra como zarza. El árbol, que crece para arriba, es del grandor de un moral alto, copado y de grueso pie, aunque también los hay pequeños. Tiene la hoja muy menuda, en todo semejante a la del guarango; por todas las ramas está lleno de espinas como las del naranjo, pero más duras y agudas. Echa unas florecillas redondas, amarillas y vellosas, como botones, que no huelen. La fruta es silvestre: son unas algarrobillas pequeñas de medio palmo de largo y de menos de un dedo de ancho; cuando secas son parduscas, muy delgadas, enjutas y sin ningún jugo, con unas pepitas dentro como las de la algarroba, sólo que difieren algo en la hechura, aunque en el color y lisura se les parecen. Comen bien los ganados estas algarrobillas, particularmente las cabras. Estímase la madera de este árbol, por ser muy sólida y recia, y así se hacen de ellas las vigas y rodeznos de los trapiches e ingenios de azúcar, las ruedas de los carros y sirve para otras obras que requieren madera recia, especialmente para hincar en tierra, porque no se corrompe ni pudre aunque esté muchísimo tiempo enterrada.

El zumo del cogollo de este guarango es contra ponzoña, y su resina, que es muy negra y estíptica, deshecha en agua y lavándose con ella las almorranas, es singular remedio.

Otra especie hay de guarango que echa una florecita colorada como la del tabaco y una frutilla asimismo colorada, seca y sin provecho, semejante a la del aliso. El cocimiento de sus flores tomado caliente con azúcar candi es contra la detención de orina; y las hojas, mojadas con vino, unen las heridas frescas, y el zumo dellas con miel las mundifica.

Hállase otra especie de guarango-espino, que echa unos botoncillos largos de flocadura. Otra que produce de dos en dos unas espinas larguillas de forma de cuernecillos. Otra que lleva unas algarrobas más anchas y espinosas por fuera. Casi todas estas especies de guarangos-espinos se dan también en la Nueva España, adonde los llaman mezquites; y cierta casta de ellos que nace en la diócesis de Guajaca, destila mucha goma tan buena como la de Arabia, y sirve de lo mismo que ella. Y en la misma provincia y en otras de la Nueva España, cierta especie de mezquite lleva unas vainillas tan buenas para tinta como las agallas.

De Panamá se trae al Perú una madera tan rica y tan pesada, que se hunde en el agua, la cual es del género de los algarrobos de la tierra de este capítulo y hace ventaja a todos los demás; tráense grandes vigas que sirven en los ingenios de azúcar. Echa este árbol mayores algarrobas que los otros; es muy alto y no tiene espinas.
results.translation:
CHAPTER LI
On the Spiny Guarango [guarango-espino]

Because this guarango has many thorns we call it the spiny [guarango], to distinguish it from the first kind, which has none; but the Indians of this kingdom call it yara. In this species of guarango there are male and female: the male bears fruit, and on the female the fruit drops off in flower. One variety of the yara grows tall, while another spreads out and trails along the ground like a bramble. The tree that grows upright is the size of a tall, spreading mulberry tree with a thick trunk, though smaller specimens also exist. Its leaf is very small, in every respect resembling that of the guarango; all its branches are covered with thorns like those of the orange tree, but harder and sharper. It puts forth small round flowers, yellow and downy, like buttons, which have no scent. The fruit is wild: small pods [algarrobillas] about half a handspan long and less than a finger's breadth wide; when dry they are brownish, very thin, withered, and without any juice, with seeds inside resembling those of the carob [algarroba], differing only somewhat in shape, though similar in color and smoothness. Livestock eat these small pods readily, especially goats. The timber of this tree is highly prized for being very solid and hard, and from it are made the beams and mill-wheels [rodeznos] of sugar mills [trapiches e ingenios de azúcar], the wheels of carts, and it serves for other works requiring hard wood, especially for driving into the ground, as it does not rot or decay even if buried for a very long time.

The juice of the young shoot [cogollo] of this guarango is effective against poison [ponzoña], and its resin, which is very black and astringent [estíptica], dissolved in water and used to wash hemorrhoids [almorranas], is a singular remedy.

There is another species of guarango that puts forth a small red flower like that of tobacco, and likewise a small red fruit, dry and useless, resembling that of the alder [aliso]. A decoction of its flowers, taken hot with sugar candy [azúcar candi], is effective against retention of urine; and its leaves, moistened with wine, close fresh wounds, and their juice with honey cleanses [mundifica] them.

There is yet another species of spiny guarango that produces long tasseled buttons [botoncillos largos de flocadura]. Another that bears, in pairs, long thorns in the shape of small horns. Another that carries broader pods that are spiny on the outside. Nearly all these species of spiny guarangos are also found in New Spain, where they are called mezquites; and a certain variety of them found in the diocese of Oaxaca [Guajaca] exudes a great quantity of gum as good as that of Arabia, and serves the same purposes. And in that same province and in others of New Spain, a certain species of mezquite bears small pods that are as good for making ink as oak galls [agallas].

From Panama there is brought to Peru a timber so rich and so heavy that it sinks in water; it belongs to the genus of the carob trees [algarrobos] described in this chapter and surpasses all the rest. Large beams of it are brought for use in sugar mills. This tree produces larger pods than the others; it is very tall and has no thorns.

entities_mentioned.name: guarango-espino / yara
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Prosopis pallida or closely related Prosopis spp. (mesquite / algarrobo); possibly also Acacia spp.
entities_mentioned.notes: Cobo explicitly equates this plant with the Mexican 'mezquite,' strongly supporting a Prosopis identification. The 'yara' name is the indigenous Quechua/local term. The male/female distinction likely refers to fertile vs. infertile individuals or distinct varieties.

entities_mentioned.name: mezquite (Nueva España)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Prosopis juliflora or related Prosopis spp.
entities_mentioned.notes: Cobo explicitly identifies the guarango-espino with the Mexican mezquite, a well-established identification in colonial botanical literature.

entities_mentioned.name: goma de Guajaca (Oaxacan gum)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Prosopis gum / mesquite gum, analogous to gum arabic (Acacia senegal product)
entities_mentioned.notes: Cobo compares it favorably to gum arabic from Arabia. Mesquite gum is indeed a known exudate with similar properties.

entities_mentioned.name: vainillas para tinta (ink pods of Nueva España mesquite)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Possibly pods of Prosopis spp. or Acacia spp. used as tannin source for ink
entities_mentioned.notes: Cobo compares their ink-making quality to oak galls (agallas). The specific species is unclear.

entities_mentioned.name: madera de Panamá (heavy sinking timber)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Possibly Hymenaea courbaril (West Indian locust / guapinol) or Swartzia spp.; Cobo places it in the algarrobo genus
entities_mentioned.notes: The description of extreme density (sinks in water) and large pods is notable. Several tropical leguminous timbers from Panama fit this description. Hymenaea courbaril is a strong candidate.

medicinal_entries.name: guarango-espino / yara (shoot juice)
medicinal_entries.preparation: Juice [zumo] extracted from the young shoot [cogollo]

medicinal_entries.name: guarango-espino / yara (resin)
medicinal_entries.preparation: Very black, astringent resin dissolved in water; used as a wash applied topically to hemorrhoids

medicinal_entries.name: second guarango species (red-flowered, alder-like fruit)
medicinal_entries.preparation: Decoction of flowers taken hot with sugar candy [azúcar candi]
medicinal_entries.modern_identification: Unknown Prosopis or related legume species; possibly Calliandra or Inga spp. given red flowers

medicinal_entries.name: second guarango species (leaves)
medicinal_entries.preparation: Leaves moistened with wine applied to fresh wounds; juice of leaves mixed with honey used to cleanse wounds [mundificar]
medicinal_entries.modern_identification: Unknown; same red-flowered guarango as above

results.translator_notes: 1. 'Rodeznos' are horizontal mill-wheels used in water-powered mills; translated as 'mill-wheels' for clarity. 2. 'Trapiches e ingenios de azúcar' denotes both smaller animal-powered and larger water/mechanically powered sugar-processing facilities; both terms retained in brackets. 3. 'Algarrobilla' and 'algarroba' are diminutive/standard forms of the Spanish word for carob pod, used loosely for pod-bearing legume fruits throughout this text; translated as 'pod' or 'small pod' with bracketed original. 4. 'Mundificar' is a period medical term meaning to cleanse or purify a wound; retained in brackets. 5. The 'Guajaca' diocese is modern Oaxaca, Mexico. 6. The schema did not include a top-level 'medicinal_entries' field, but the rules required extraction of medicinal data; this has been added as an additional key for completeness and scholarly utility.

results.title_inferred: On the Walnut Trees of Peru and New Spain
results.original_text:
CAPITULO LII
Del nogal del Perú

El nogal que se halla en este reino parece en todo ser del mismo género que los de España; si bien la gran diferencia que vemos entre estas nueces y otras es argumento de haber entre ellas diferencia específica. El árbol es grande y la hoja de la misma forma que la del nogal de Europa, y también el olor es el mismo, salvo que la madera de éste no es tan buena como la del otro. Con todo, se hacen de ella algunas obras y en especial cajas de arcabuces. La fruta ni más ni menos en la apariencia exterior es parecida a las nueces de España; salvo que es un poco mayor, más redonda, con la cáscara mucho más arrugada y recia, y que no se parte fácilmente por en medio. El meollo está tan encerrado, que no se puede sacar entero ni aun la cuarta parte dél, y es muy aceitoso y rancio, por lo cual no comen de ordinario estas nueces sino los muchachos; solamente los pintores las estiman por el aceite que sacan de ellas; y también, cuando están verdes, se hace de ellas muy regalada conserva.

CAPITULO LIII
Del nogal de la Nueva España

En la Nueva España nace otra especie de nogal diferente del del Perú. Es árbol mucho mayor que los nogales de España, más alto y copado y de hoja mucho menor; nace en tierra caliente y produce su fruto en racimos, que son unas nuececillas del tamaño y hechura de un huevo de paloma, con la cáscara tan dura y el meollo tan encarcelado como las nueces del Perú. Véndenlas estas nueces en México, y más son para muchachos que para otra suerte de gente.
results.translation:
CHAPTER LII
On the Walnut of Peru

The walnut tree found in this kingdom appears in all respects to belong to the same genus [género] as those of Spain; although the great difference we observe between these nuts and the others is an argument for there being a specific difference [diferencia específica] between them. The tree is large and its leaf is of the same shape as that of the European walnut, and the smell is likewise the same, except that the wood of this one is not as good as that of the other. Nevertheless, some articles are made from it, and in particular the stocks [cajas] of arquebuses [arcabuces]. The fruit is, in outward appearance, much the same as the walnuts of Spain; except that it is somewhat larger, more rounded, with a much more wrinkled and tough shell, and one that does not split easily down the middle. The kernel [meollo] is so enclosed that not even a quarter of it can be extracted whole, and it is very oily and rancid, for which reason these nuts are not ordinarily eaten except by children; only painters value them for the oil they extract from them. Also, when they are green, a very choice preserve [conserva] is made from them.

CHAPTER LIII
On the Walnut of New Spain

In New Spain there grows another species of walnut different from that of Peru. It is a tree much larger than the walnuts of Spain, taller and more spreading, and with a much smaller leaf; it grows in warm lowland country [tierra caliente] and produces its fruit in clusters, which are small nuts the size and shape of a pigeon's egg, with a shell as hard and a kernel as inaccessible as those of the Peruvian walnuts. These nuts are sold in Mexico City, and they are more the sort of thing for children than for any other class of people.

entities_mentioned.name: nogal del Perú
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Juglans neotropica (Andean walnut) or possibly Juglans honorei
entities_mentioned.notes: Cobo notes morphological similarities to European walnut (Juglans regia) but distinguishes it as a separate species. The very hard, deeply wrinkled shell and rancid, oil-rich kernel are consistent with J. neotropica. The oil was used by painters, and a preserve was made from the green fruit.

entities_mentioned.name: nogal de la Nueva España
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Juglans pyriformis or possibly Juglans major / Juglans rupestris complex
entities_mentioned.notes: Described as larger than European walnut, with smaller leaves, growing in tierra caliente, and bearing fruit in clusters resembling pigeon eggs. The cluster-bearing habit and tropical habitat may point toward a different genus or an unusual Juglans species; further identification is uncertain.

results.translator_notes: Several OCR artifacts were silently corrected: 'reS®&' → 'reino'; 'géiíH^' → 'género'; 'gran & ferencia' → 'gran diferencia'; 'nueccfv' → 'nueces'; 'mmho' → 'mucho' (twice); 'm&' in shell description → 'cáscara'; 'háo' → 'encarcelado'; 'Vénden-las' normalized. No medically relevant preparation or therapeutic use is described in this segment; the extract of green walnut for preserve and the oil for painters' use are the only preparations mentioned.

results.title_inferred: Hazelnuts of Chile
results.original_text:
CAPITULO LIV

De las avellanas de Chile

En el reino de Chile se da un género de avellanas diferentes de las nuestras de España. Son del tamaño de medianas avellanas, con la cáscara no tan lisa ni reluciente, algo pardilla y con un rabillo en la punta, parecido al que tienen los cocos; a los cuales se asemejan estas avellanas más que a las nuestras. Su comida no es nada apetitosa. El árbol que las produce es grande y de madera escogida para labrar, la cual se suele traer a Lima con los demás frutos de aquel reino.
results.translation:
CHAPTER LIV

On the hazelnuts of Chile

In the kingdom of Chile there grows a type of hazelnut different from our own in Spain. They are the size of medium hazelnuts, with a shell that is not as smooth or shiny, somewhat brownish, and with a small tail [rabillo] at the tip, resembling that which coconuts have; to which these hazelnuts bear a greater resemblance than they do to ours. Their flesh is not at all appetizing. The tree that produces them is large and yields timber well suited for working [labrar], which is commonly brought to Lima along with the other products of that kingdom.

entities_mentioned.name: avellanas de Chile
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Gevuina avellana (Chilean hazel, family Proteaceae)
entities_mentioned.notes: Cobo's description — medium-sized nut with a brownish, not highly polished shell and a small apical projection — is consistent with Gevuina avellana, the Chilean hazelnut or gevuín. His comparison to coconuts in having a 'rabillo' (small tail/beak) at the tip aligns with the apiculate tip of Gevuina nuts. The note about the timber being prized and transported to Lima also accords with historical records of Chilean timber trade. No medicinal use is described in this passage.

results.translator_notes: The source text shows significant OCR degradation at the opening lines ('h wellanas', '4^ Etipafia', 'Maíllo', 'fcien', 'M estas'). These have been silently corrected to 'avellanas', 'España', 'rabillo', 'tienen', and 'estas' respectively, as the intended readings are unambiguous in context. No medical or pharmaceutical content is present in this segment; Cobo's treatment here is purely botanical and economic.

results.title_inferred: The Almonds of the Andes (Brazil Nuts)
results.original_text:
CAPITULO LV
De las almendras de los Andes

Los indios gentiles de las provincias de los Andes que confinan por la parte oriental con la diócesis del Cuzco, sacan a vender a tierra de cristianos ciertas almendras que se dan en las suyas, a las cuales los españoles, por no saber su propio nombre, llamamos almendras de los Andes. El árbol que las produce es mayor que un gran nogal, y hay en las provincias de los Andes tan grande cantidad de ellos, que se hallan montañas de cincuenta leguas y más de estos almendros. Producen el fruto dentro de unos cocos del grandor de un membrillo, algo ahusado, de una cáscara parda, dura, resquebrajada, de medio dedo de grueso, que para partirla se suele aserrar; tiene dentro más de una docena de almendras un poco corvas, dispuestas entre sí como lo están los gajos de la naranja, y cada una es del tamaño y figura de un gajo de naranja, cubierta de una cáscara parda, dura y arrugada, dentro de la cual está el meollo blanco, dulce y suave, cubierto de una telilla como las de nuestras almendras de España. Tiene cada una de estas almendras por tres de las nuestras; son calientes en el segundo grado y algo secas, recias de digerir, muy jugosas. Sácase de ellas un aceite que suple al dulce de las almendras, el cual, mezclado con enjundia de gallina, vale para ablandar el pecho y mitigar el dolor de costado; y echando dél unas gotas tibias en el oído doloroso y asimismo untada con él y con la enjundia la redondez de la oreja, le quita el dolor y ablanda el tumor que por corrimiento se suele hacer en su cavidad. Demás de esto, si con un pedazo o parte de esta almendra ardiendo se cauterizan dos o tres veces los agujeros que el neguijón causa en los dientes o muelas, lo mata y preserva que no pase adelante, y conserva la dentadura. Finalmente, suplen estas almendras por las de Castilla, porque de ellas se hacen pastas para mazapanes, tortas y otros regalos de este jaez.
results.translation:
CHAPTER LV
On the Almonds of the Andes

The pagan Indians [indios gentiles] of the Andean provinces that border on the eastern side of the diocese of Cuzco bring to the Christian settlements certain almonds that grow in their lands, which the Spaniards, not knowing their proper name, call almendras de los Andes [almonds of the Andes]. The tree that produces them is larger than a great walnut tree, and in the Andean provinces there is such a vast quantity of them that forests of these almond trees stretch over fifty leagues and more. They produce their fruit inside husks [cocos] about the size of a quince, somewhat elongated, with a hard, brownish, cracked shell half a finger's breadth thick, which must generally be sawed open; inside are more than a dozen slightly curved almonds arranged among themselves as the sections of an orange are arranged, and each one is the size and shape of an orange segment, covered with a hard, brown, wrinkled shell, within which lies the white, sweet, and soft kernel, covered with a thin skin [telilla] like those of our Spanish almonds. Each one of these almonds is worth three of ours; they are hot in the second degree [humoural quality] and somewhat dry, hard to digest, and very oily.

An oil is extracted from them that serves as a substitute for sweet almond oil, which, when mixed with hen fat [enjundia de gallina], is good for softening the chest and relieving pain in the side [dolor de costado]; and by dropping a few warm drops of it into a painful ear, and likewise by anointing the outer ear [redondez de la oreja] with it and with the hen fat, it relieves the pain and softens the swelling [tumor] that tends to form in the ear's cavity from a flux [corrimiento]. Furthermore, if with a burning piece or portion of this almond the cavities that tooth decay [neguijón] causes in the teeth or molars are cauterized two or three times, it kills the decay and prevents it from advancing further, and preserves the teeth. Finally, these almonds serve as a substitute for those of Castile, as from them pastes are made for marzipans, cakes, and other confections of that sort.

entities_mentioned.name: almendras de los Andes
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Bertholletia excelsa (Brazil nut)
entities_mentioned.notes: Cobo's description — the large capsule the size of a quince, the arrangement of seeds like orange segments, the size of individual nuts, and the vast Amazonian forests — matches Brazil nuts precisely. The tree is indeed one of the largest in Amazonia, and the nuts are arranged in the described radial pattern inside a hard, thick capsule.

entities_mentioned.name: aceite de las almendras de los Andes
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Brazil nut oil (Bertholletia excelsa seed oil)
entities_mentioned.notes: Oil extracted from the kernels and used medicinally. Conditions treated: chest complaints [ablandar el pecho], pleuritic pain [dolor de costado], ear pain and swelling, and dental caries [neguijón]. Preparation: oil extracted from kernels, mixed with hen fat (enjundia de gallina) for topical/aural application; burning kernel fragment used for dental cauterization.

entities_mentioned.name: enjundia de gallina
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Rendered hen fat (chicken lard)
entities_mentioned.notes: Used as a carrier/vehicle mixed with the almond oil for chest and ear treatments.

entities_mentioned.name: neguijón
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Dental caries / tooth decay
entities_mentioned.notes: A period Spanish term for dental decay or worm-caused cavities in teeth. Treatment described: cauterization of the cavity with a burning piece of the nut, applied two or three times.

results.translator_notes: 1. 'Calientes en el segundo grado' is a standard Galenic humoural classification indicating the nut's temperament as 'hot in the second degree,' preserved in brackets in the translation. 2. 'Dolor de costado' (literally 'pain in the side') was a period term often referring to pleurisy or similar thoracic inflammatory conditions. 3. 'Corrimiento' denotes a humoral flux or catarrhal discharge believed to cause swelling in cavities such as the ear. 4. 'Neguijón' is a term found in Golden Age Spanish medical texts for dental caries, sometimes attributed to a 'worm' in the tooth — the cauterization method reflects pre-modern dental practice. 5. The page numbers '17' and '258' are artifacts of the original printed layout and have been omitted from the cleaned text. 6. The OCR error 'Uamamos' has been silently corrected to 'llamamos'; 'cocos' here means seed capsules/pods, not coconuts, and has been translated accordingly with a bracketed gloss.

results.translator_notes:
{
  "title_inferred": "Almonds of Chachapoyas and Cacao (Chapters LVI–LVII)",
  "original_text": "CAPITULO LVI

De las almendras de Chachapoyas

En las montañas del distrito de la ciudad de Chachapoyas, en el Perú, nace una especie de almendras que hacen ventaja en sabor y bondad a cuantas castas de almendras y fruta de este género se conocen así en Indias como en Europa. Los árboles que las crían son de gran copa y tan altos, que sobrepujando a todos los demás de la montaña, se descubren de muy lejos. Son estas almendras unos erizos como los de las castañas, muy espinosos, redondos, del tamaño de una gran nuez y de un color rojo oscuro. Tienen entre las espinas, que están muy juntas, una sustancia del mismo color, a manera de corcho, pero tierna y seca, que fácilmente se desmigaja y deshace. La coyuntura de la cáscara no la ciñe toda alrededor, como la de otras pepitas, sino que sólo corre a lo largo de punta a punta por un lado, por lo cual se abre el erizo dándole con una piedra, o para sacar entera la almendra, apretándolo en el encaje o marco de una puerta o de otra manera. Es la médula de estos erizos una pepita tres tantos mayor que las almendras comunes, muy blancas, tierna, jugosa y suave; son, en suma, estas almendras la fruta más delicada, sabrosa y sana que yo he comido en Indias; las cuales, como cosa muy preciada, se suelen enviar en presente de la provincia de Chachapoyas a esta ciudad de Lima; y confitadas, no hay colación tan regalada que con ellas se pueda comparar.

Donde nace esta fruta tan digna de estimación nacen muchos murciélagos que la destruyen, porque, cuando ella está tierna, antes de endurecerse la cáscara, se comen la médula sin arrancar la fruta del árbol; de manera que muchas veces quien la va a coger halla los racimos enteros de solas las cáscaras vanas; que ciertamente da lástima ver que una fruta tan suave y regalada, que merecía la gozaran las cortes de los mayores príncipes, se quede escondida en unas yermas montañas, hech
