results.translator_notes:
{
  "title_inferred": "Further Palms of the First Kind, and Spiny Palms (Coyoles)",
  "original_text": "CAPITULO LXVII

De las demás palmas de este primer género

En la sobredicha provincia de Santa Cruz de la Sierra llaman a otra palma mmuqui; es muy alta y lleva cierta fruta odorífera del tamaño y hechura de un albaricoque, la cual comen los indios.

Otra palma, llamada metaquigi, echa unos racimos grandes de cierta fruta morada del tamaño de aceitunas; casi todo es hueso, y lo que tiene de carne es muy sabroso y oloroso.

Otra, llamada siriha, da la fruta en ramos, la cual se come cocida y es poco mayor que un grano de maíz; la madera es recia, y de ella suelen hacer los indios sus arcos.

La palma de más recia madera se llama en la lengua general del Perú chonta; es muy alta, el tronco grueso, liso y todo él lleno de agudísimas púas; lleva fruta del tamaño y forma de una pera mediana, la cual, cuando madura, se pone amarilla, pero nunca se come cruda, sino cocida; tiene la carne amarilla y sabrosa, y dentro un hueso del tamaño de una avellana, con cáscara negra y dura; el meollo de dentro es blanco, de sabor de castaña, que también se come. Llaman en algunas partes a esta fruta chontaruro o chontariros. El cogollo desta palma es sabroso palmito. Hacen los indios de su madera o corteza, que es negra, pesada y lisa, lanzas, puntas de flechas, bastones o macanas, arcos y otras armas; porque es la madera muy recia, que, sin hender ni remachar, entra mucho.

Otro género hay de palmas que en su tronco hace tres diferencias; el primer tercio es muy duro y pardo; el segundo, que es hasta la copa, es más grueso que el primero, tierno, liso y de color verde, que parece caña de cebolla; en él hacen su nido los pájaros carpinteros; la tercera parte es la copa; el fruto que lleva esta palma son unas contezuelas.

En muchas partes de Indias hay muy grandes y espesos bosques de solas palmas, diferentes de las que quedan dichas; éstas son altas, con el tronco pare

results.title_inferred: Chapter LXIX: The Third Kind of Palms — Fan Palms of New Spain and New Vizcaya
results.original_text:
CAPITULO LXIX
Del tercer género de palmas

En este tercero género de palmas se contienen todas las especies de ellas que en sus ramas y hojas se diferencian las de dátiles; porque las echan de la forma de una mano abierta, o semejantes a la cola del pavo cuando hace la rueda, o a la de un abanico desplegado y abierto, y se cuentan ocho o más castas de estas palmas. Nacen en tierras yuncas y templadas; su fruto no es otra que varias maneras de cuentecillas, que echan en grandes racimos, unas mayores que otras, de que se hacen los rosarios que llamamos de frutilla, cuya cáscara es recia como la del coyol, del cual se diferencia la frutilla en dos cosas: la una, en que, después de limpia, queda blanca como hueso o marfil; y la otra, en que no tiene aquellos tres hoyuelos que parecen ojos y boca, con que están señalados todos los cocos o coyotes. Suélense diferenciar estas palmas entre sí en que algunas son del todo infructíferas, y otras que, aunque dan ciertas contezuelas, no son a propósito para rosarios; porque son pequeñas y chatas: y principalmente en el tamaño, porque se hallan grandes y chicas, y algunas tan pequeñas como las de la Andalucía, de que se hacen escobas; y así por toda la Nueva España se hacen escobas de palmas más chiquillas.

La especie de palmas de las deste género que yo he visto de mayores hojas, nace en tierras yuncas; tiene el tronco áspero, por los ganchos que quedan de las ramas que le van cortando: echa la rama desnuda de hoja hasta el cabo; allí nacen las hojas muy juntas y pegadas unas a otras, o, por mejor decir, una sola de muchos pliegues junto a su nacimiento; y como se va apartando dél, se va desplegando a manera de abanico abierto y extendido; y en el fin fenece en puntas, apartadas unas de otras, del largor de una tercia cada una, donde ya la hoja se ha desplegado del todo. Tiene esta hoja de largo, desde donde nace de la rama hasta su orilla y circunferencia, de cinco a seis palmos, que no parece cada una sino una muy grande adarga, y puede servir muy bien de quitasol. Es la palma destas ramas y hojas muy hermosa a la vista, y produce en racimos una frutilla inútil del tamaño de uvas, que sólo comen los muchachos por golosina.

Otras palmas hay deste género que echan las hojas divididas como los dedos de la mano tendidos, cuyas ramas tienen por las orillas y cantos muchas espinas como de zarza; suelen nacer de una raíz dos o tres pies y producen unas flores blancas y olorosas, pendientes en racimos, que cada una tiene seis hojillas, a las cuales sucede el fruto, que es semejante a piñones. De las hojas de esta palma remojadas y majadas, hacen los indios hilo; y de las hojas de todas ellas labran por toda la Nueva España esteras delgadas y curiosas, que llaman petates, canastos, sombreros y otras mil cosas; y del fruto, hojas y raíces de algunas usan en la medicina.

En la provincia de la Nueva Vizcaya, en la Nueva España, nace cierta especie de palma espinosa que no da ningún fruto, más que unas flores blancas; tiene el tronco delgado y no crece muy alta; cortado su tronco a raíz de la tierra y haciendo en lo que de él queda una concavidad, destila mucho zumo, el cual, cociéndolo al fuego, se hace regalada miel.
results.translation:
CHAPTER LXIX
On the Third Kind of Palms

This third kind of palm comprises all those species that differ from the date palms in their branches and leaves; for they spread their leaves in the form of an open hand, or resembling the tail of a peacock [pavo] when it spreads its plumes, or like an unfurled and opened fan. Some eight or more varieties of these palms are counted. They grow in lowland [yunca] and temperate lands; their fruit consists of nothing more than various small beads [cuentecillas], borne in large clusters, some larger than others, from which the rosaries we call 'de frutilla' [berry rosaries] are made. The shell [cáscara] of these is as hard as that of the coyol palm, from which the frutilla differs in two respects: first, that once cleaned it remains white as bone or ivory; and second, that it lacks those three small pits [hoyuelos] resembling eyes and a mouth that mark all coconuts or coyotes [cocos o coyotes]. These palms commonly differ from one another in that some are entirely without fruit, while others, though they bear certain small seeds [contezuelas], are unsuitable for rosaries because they are small and flat. They differ chiefly in size, ranging from large to small, some as small as those of Andalusia from which brooms are made; and indeed throughout all of New Spain brooms are made from the smaller palms.

The species of this third kind with the largest leaves that I have observed grows in the lowland [yunca] regions. Its trunk is rough on account of the hooks left by the branches that have been cut away. The branch itself is bare of leaves until the very tip; there the leaves grow thickly clustered and pressed together — or, more precisely, it is a single leaf with many folds near its base — and as it extends outward it unfurls after the fashion of an open and spread fan, terminating at its edges in points set apart from one another, each point about a third of a vara [tercia] in length, at which point the leaf is fully opened. This leaf measures from five to six palmos [hand-spans] in length from where it springs from the branch to its rim and circumference, so that each one looks no different from a very large shield [adarga] and could serve very well as a parasol [quitasol]. The palm with these branches and leaves is very beautiful to behold, and it produces in clusters a useless little fruit the size of grapes, which only children eat as a treat.

There are other palms of this kind whose leaves spread divided like the outstretched fingers of a hand; their branches have along their edges and sides many thorns like those of a bramble [zarza]. Two or three trunks commonly spring from a single root, and they produce white, fragrant flowers hanging in clusters, each flower having six small petals [hojillas], followed by a fruit resembling pine nuts [piñones]. From the leaves of this palm, soaked and pounded, the Indians make thread; and from the leaves of all these palms, throughout all of New Spain, they weave thin and finely worked mats called petates, baskets [canastos], hats [sombreros], and a thousand other things. The fruit, leaves, and roots of some of these palms are also used in medicine.

In the province of Nueva Vizcaya, in New Spain, there grows a certain species of thorny palm that bears no fruit other than some white flowers. Its trunk is slender and it does not grow very tall. When its trunk is cut at ground level and a hollow [concavidad] is made in the stump that remains, it exudes a great quantity of sap [zumo], which, when cooked over a fire, yields a delectable honey [regalada miel].

entities_mentioned.name: Palmas del tercer género / fan palms (general group)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Arecaceae family, fan-palm genera — likely Sabal, Brahea, Livistona, or Thrinax spp.
entities_mentioned.notes: Cobo's 'third kind' is defined by palmate (fan-shaped) rather than pinnate (feather-shaped) fronds, distinguishing them from date palms. He counts eight or more varieties.

entities_mentioned.name: Frutilla (rosary-bead palm)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Possibly Brahea dulcis or a related Brahea sp.; alternatively Sabal mexicana
entities_mentioned.notes: Identified by hard, ivory-white seeds lacking the three pits of coconuts, used for rosary beads. Cobo notes it differs from the coyol (Acrocomia aculeata).

entities_mentioned.name: Coyol
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Acrocomia aculeata (coyol palm)
entities_mentioned.notes: Used as a point of comparison for hardness of seed shell and the presence of three pits (eyes and mouth) characteristic of cocopalm-type seeds.

entities_mentioned.name: Palma de hojas grandes (large-leafed fan palm, yunca lands)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Possibly Sabal mexicana or Brahea salvadorensis
entities_mentioned.notes: Described as having a rough trunk, fully palmate leaves 5–6 palmos across (roughly 1–1.2 m), usable as a parasol. Fruit grape-sized but not eaten by adults.

entities_mentioned.name: Palma espinosa de hojas digitadas (spiny palm with finger-divided leaves)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Possibly Brahea berlandieri or Sabal minor; the spiny-edged rachis suggests Acanthosabal or Rhapidophyllum hystrix
entities_mentioned.notes: Leaves divided like spread fingers with thorns on edges; white fragrant flowers in racemes with 6 petals each; fruit resembling pine nuts; leaves used by Indians to make thread; fruit, leaves, and roots used medicinally. Medical use noted but no specific ailment or preparation given.

entities_mentioned.name: Palma espinosa de Nueva Vizcaya (sap-yielding thorny palm)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Possibly Brahea aculeata or a Dasylirion sp. (though Dasylirion is not a true palm); alternatively a Nolina or Yucca sp.
entities_mentioned.notes: Slender-trunked, thorny, produces only white flowers. When trunk is cut at ground level and hollowed, it exudes sap that when boiled yields a 'delectable honey.' This tapping process is consistent with production of palm syrup or 'miel de palma.'

entities_mentioned.name: Petate
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Woven palm-leaf mat (cultural artifact, not a plant)
entities_mentioned.notes: Specifically named as a product woven from the leaves of these fan palms throughout New Spain.

results.translator_notes: 1. 'Tierras yuncas' refers to the hot, low-lying coastal or valley lands (from Quechua 'yunka/yunca'), a technical geographic term used throughout Cobo; retained as 'lowland [yunca] regions.' 2. 'Palmo' is a Spanish unit of measure roughly equal to the width of a hand (~21 cm); 'tercia' is one-third of a vara (~28 cm). 3. OCR errors silently corrected include: 'l)avo' → 'pavo,' 'tierríií' → 'tierras,' '({ue' → 'que,' 'hoyuelm' → 'hoyuelos,' 'cierfi^' → 'ciertas,' 'algu^' → 'algunas,' 'Andalucúu' → 'Andalucía,' '^gé¬' → 'género,' 'hoja«' → 'hojas,' 'trom#' → 'tronco,' 'Af' → 'de,' 'eehi' → 'echa,' 'p' → 'pegadas,' 'apartaiA' → 'apartando,' and 'Meras' → 'esteras.' 4. 'Adarga' is a Moorish-derived oval or heart-shaped leather shield; retained in brackets as a period technical term for the leaf's shape. 5. The medical use of the spiny finger-leafed palm ('del fruto, hojas y raíces de algunas usan en la medicina') is mentioned only in passing; no specific condition or preparation is described in this passage. 6. The sap-honey extraction from the Nueva Vizcaya palm is an economic/culinary note rather than a medical one, though such preparations were commonly medicinal in this literature.

results.title_inferred: The Nanchic Tree of New Spain
results.original_text:
CAPITULO LXX
Del Nanchic

Este es un árbol grande natural de la Nueva España; tiene la hoja como de naranjo; produce en racimillos una fruta pequeña del tamaño de una aceituna, redonda, con un piquillo en la cumbre, la cual, madura, se pone amarilla; es aguanosa y tiene una pepita tan grande como de una guinda, que no se despide fácilmente de la pulpa. Es fruta dulce, aunque, si no está bien madura, frunce la boca. Echa este árbol una flor pequeña en racimillos, de color naranjado, que tiene buen parecer, mas carece de olor. Nace en tierras calientes, cuales son las costas de la Nueva España.
results.translation:
CHAPTER LXX
On the Nanchic

This is a large tree native to New Spain; its leaf resembles that of the orange tree; it produces in small clusters a little fruit the size of an olive, round, with a small point [piquillo] at the top, which, when ripe, turns yellow; it is watery and contains a stone [pepita] as large as that of a sour cherry, which does not separate easily from the pulp. The fruit is sweet, although, if not fully ripe, it puckers the mouth. This tree bears a small flower in clusters, of orange color, which is pleasing in appearance but lacks any scent. It grows in warm lands [tierras calientes], such as the coasts of New Spain.

entities_mentioned.name: Nanchic
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Byrsonima crassifolia (nance / golden spoon)
entities_mentioned.notes: Description matches Byrsonima crassifolia well: olive-sized yellow fruit with an adherent stone, orange flower clusters, orange-like leaves, and coastal tropical habitat in Mesoamerica. 'Nance' remains the common name in Mexico and Central America today.

results.translator_notes: Several OCR corruptions silently corrected: 'aceitaa' → 'aceituna'; 'mmbre' → 'cumbre'; 'amardfla' (or similar) → 'amarilla'; 'tm' → 'tan'. The phrase 'frunce la boca' (literally 'wrinkles/puckers the mouth') is a standard period Spanish expression for astringency and has been rendered accordingly. No medicinal use or preparation method is described in this segment; the passage is purely botanical and organoleptic.

results.title_inferred: On the Cacaloxochitl (Plumeria) and a related white-flowered tree
results.original_text:
CAPITULO LXXI

Del cacaloxochitl.

Muchos árboles nacen en estas Indias que no llevan otro fruto sino flores muy vistosas. Describiré aquí los más conocidos y estimados por la hermosura de sus flores; que de todos es imposible hacer memoria. Y sea el primero el árbol que produce la flor llamada cacaloxochitl, en la Nueva España; el cual es muy parecido a la adelfa, poco copado, y que no tiene hoja más que en los cogollos; ésta es larga un palmo y más y muy parecida a la de la adelfa. El árbol es mediano como un granado; y es cosa notable, que su fruto es echar flores todo el año. Es cada flor semejante a una rosa, si bien mucho menor y más crecida que una clavellina. Su color es el mismo que de rosa; echa sólo un orden de cinco hojas semejantes a las de la rosa, pero más angostas y más tiesas; es de buen olor, y adornan con ellas los altares en toda la Nueva España, dándose con abundancia en la provincia de Nicaragua, adonde primero las vi. Produce la semilla en unas vainas mayores que las de las habas, y en su hoja se cría un gusano dos veces mayor que el de la seda, listado de negro y amarillo, con el hocico, cola y pies colorados, y una mancha sobre la cola, muy colorada; de la cual le nace una cerda como bigote de gato, a manera de cuernecillo. Pone horror este gusano con su figura, pero no hace mal. Otro árbol de flores hay muy parecido a éste, salvo que sus flores son blancas y no de grato olor; y también se diferencia en que el primero las echa de una en una, y este segundo, en racimos.
results.translation:
CHAPTER LXXI

On the Cacaloxochitl.

Many trees grow in these Indies that bear no other fruit than very showy flowers. I shall describe here the best known and most prized for the beauty of their blossoms, for it is impossible to record them all. Let the first be the tree that produces the flower called cacaloxochitl in New Spain. It closely resembles the oleander [adelfa], has a sparse crown, and bears leaves only at the growing tips [cogollos]; these leaves are a hand's breadth or more in length and very similar to those of the oleander. The tree is of medium size, like a pomegranate tree; and it is a noteworthy thing that its fruit, so to speak, is the flowering it produces throughout the entire year. Each flower resembles a rose, though considerably smaller, yet larger than a carnation [clavellina]. Its color is the same as that of a rose; it produces only a single whorl of five petals resembling those of a rose, but narrower and stiffer. It has a pleasant fragrance, and throughout all of New Spain the flowers are used to adorn altars; they grow in great abundance in the province of Nicaragua, where I first observed them. The tree produces its seed in pods larger than those of broad beans. On its leaves there lives a caterpillar twice the size of a silkworm, striped black and yellow, with a red snout, tail, and feet, and a very bright red spot above the tail from which grows a bristle resembling a cat's whisker, in the manner of a small horn. This caterpillar is a frightening sight in appearance, but it does no harm. There is another flowering tree very similar to this one, except that its flowers are white and of no pleasant scent; it also differs in that the first tree bears its flowers one by one, while this second bears them in clusters [racimos].

entities_mentioned.name: cacaloxochitl
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Plumeria rubra (red/pink frangipani)
entities_mentioned.notes: Nahuatl name meaning roughly 'crow flower' or 'raven flower.' Cobo's description—five-petaled rose-colored flowers borne singly, leaves clustered at branch tips, milky-sapped tree resembling oleander, seed pods larger than broad beans, year-round flowering, use in altar decoration—is a classic description of Plumeria rubra. The striking caterpillar described is likely a hawkmoth larva (family Sphingidae), possibly Pseudosphinx tetrio, which commonly feeds on Plumeria and is distinctively banded yellow and black with a reddish horn.

entities_mentioned.name: adelfa
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Nerium oleander
entities_mentioned.notes: Used by Cobo as a comparative reference plant familiar to Spanish readers.

entities_mentioned.name: clavellina
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Dianthus caryophyllus (carnation / pink)
entities_mentioned.notes: Used as a size comparison for the cacaloxochitl flower.

entities_mentioned.name: segundo árbol de flores blancas en racimos (second white-flowered tree)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Plumeria obtusa or possibly Plumeria alba
entities_mentioned.notes: Cobo distinguishes this tree from the pink-flowered cacaloxochitl by its white, less fragrant flowers borne in clusters rather than singly. This may refer to Plumeria obtusa (white frangipani) or another species in the genus, or possibly a different apocynaceous tree entirely.

entities_mentioned.name: gusano listado de negro y amarillo (black-and-yellow striped caterpillar)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Pseudosphinx tetrio (frangipani hawkmoth larva)
entities_mentioned.notes: The description of a large caterpillar twice the size of a silkworm, striped black and yellow, with red extremities and a caudal horn ('cerda como bigote de gato, a manera de cuernecillo') is consistent with the larva of Pseudosphinx tetrio (Sphingidae), a well-known associate of Plumeria throughout tropical America.

results.translator_notes: The OCR produced 'tm' for 'un' (corrected silently) and 'mah' for 'mal' (corrected silently), and 'ra^' for 'racimos' (a line-break artifact, corrected silently). The word 'cogollos' (growing tips / terminal buds) is preserved in the translation with a bracketed gloss. No medico-botanical preparation or therapeutic use is described in this segment; the passage is primarily descriptive and natural-historical in character. Page number 266 and the running header ('OBRAS DEL PADRE BERNABE COBO') are editorial artifacts and have been excluded from the cleaned text.

results.title_inferred: The Chicuale Tree
results.original_text:
CAPITULO LXXII
Del chicuale

Este es un árbol xuerliano; tiene la hoja como de nogal; la hermosura de sus flores es muy para ver; porque el remate de cada rama, por trecho de una tercia y más, no parece sino un curioso ramillete, a causa de que por junto al pezón de las hojas echa sus flores en racimos, y es cada una de un jeme de largo, compuesta de unos hilos o cabellos delgados y amarillos, y tan vistosas, que todo el árbol, cuando está florido, está hermosísimo; porque no parece sino que está cubierto de artificiosos ramilletes.
results.translation:
CHAPTER LXXII
Of the Chicuale

This is a tree of considerable size [xuerliano]; its leaves resemble those of the walnut tree. The beauty of its flowers is truly worth beholding, for the tip of each branch, over a span of one third [of a vara] or more, appears to be nothing less than an elaborate bouquet. This is because it puts forth its flowers in clusters at the base of the leaf stalks [pezón], each flower being a hand-span [jeme] in length and composed of slender, thread-like filaments of yellow color. They are so striking in appearance that the whole tree, when in bloom, is most beautiful indeed, seeming to be covered entirely with artfully arranged bouquets.

entities_mentioned.name: chicuale
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Possibly Inga species or a member of the Fabaceae/Mimosaceae family, given the description of feathery yellow filamentous flowers in racemes; alternatively Cassia or Senna species. Identification remains uncertain.
entities_mentioned.notes: The description of long, thread-like yellow flowers in clusters at leaf axils is suggestive of the Mimosoid legumes (e.g., Inga or Calliandra), but the walnut-like leaves do not fit neatly. No medicinal use is described in this passage. The term 'xuerliano' is unclear and may be a corrupted or regional descriptor for tree size or type; possibly an OCR artifact of 'granduliano' or similar.

results.translator_notes: 'Xuerliano' is problematic — it does not correspond to a recognized Spanish adjective and is likely either an OCR error or a highly localized/archaic term. It may have been intended as 'gran' (large), a regional variant, or a corruption of another word; it has been preserved in brackets in the translation. 'Tercia' refers to one third of a vara (approximately 28 cm). 'Jeme' is the span between the tip of the thumb and the tip of the index finger when extended, roughly 10–12 cm. No medicinal or pharmacological information is present in this segment — it is purely a botanical description focused on the ornamental qualities of the tree.

results.title_inferred: The Tree Called 'Flowers of the Dead' (Panama)
results.original_text:
CAPITULO LXXIII

Del árbol que lleva las flores de muertos

En la ciudad de Panamá dan este nombre a cierto árbol, porque en ningún tiempo del año está sin flores y con ellas suelen enterrar a los niños que mueren. Es tan grande como un naranjo, de hoja muy menuda, como la de la ruda, pero de un verde más vivo. Produce las flores por los pimpollos de sus ramas en ramilletes, compuesto cada uno de diez o doce flores, y cada una es del tamaño y hechura de una clavelina, de un color amarillo y encarnado, que, cubierto dellas todo el árbol, es muy agradable a la vista; y fuera de mayor estimación si tuvieran sus flores olor, que no le tienen.
results.translation:
CHAPTER LXXIII

Concerning the tree that bears the flowers of the dead

In the city of Panama, a certain tree is given this name because at no time of year is it without flowers, and these flowers are customarily used to bury children who have died. The tree is as large as an orange tree, with very small leaves, like those of rue [ruda], but of a more vivid green. It produces its flowers at the tips [pimpollos] of its branches in clusters, each cluster composed of ten or twelve flowers, and each flower is the size and form of a small carnation [clavelina], of a yellow and red [encarnado] color. When the whole tree is covered with them, it is very pleasing to the eye; and it would be held in even greater esteem if its flowers had a fragrance, which they do not.

entities_mentioned.name: árbol de flores de muertos (tree of the flowers of the dead)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Plumeria rubra (frangipani / red plumeria) or possibly Caesalpinia pulcherrima (dwarf poinciana / pride of Barbados)
entities_mentioned.notes: The description of yellow-and-red flowers borne in clusters at branch tips, small pinnate leaves resembling rue, evergreen flowering habit, orange-tree size, and use in funerary rites for children fits both candidates. Plumeria rubra is strongly associated with funerary customs across Latin America and has similarly shaped flowers, but Cobo notes no fragrance, which is atypical for Plumeria (notably fragrant). Caesalpinia pulcherrima matches the scentless, yellow-and-red, carnation-sized flowers and fine rue-like foliage more precisely, and is indeed called 'flor de muerto' in parts of Central America and Panama. Caesalpinia pulcherrima is the stronger candidate given the odorless quality.

entities_mentioned.name: ruda
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Ruta graveolens (common rue)
entities_mentioned.notes: Used here as a comparative reference for leaf size and form, not as a subject plant.

entities_mentioned.name: clavelina
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Dianthus caryophyllus (carnation / pink)
entities_mentioned.notes: Used as a size and shape comparator for the individual flowers.

results.translator_notes: The OCR artifact 'su»' in the source (line: 'por los pimpollos de su» ramas') has been silently corrected to 'sus' as the intended word is unambiguous. No medicinal preparation or ailment is described in this segment; the plant's noted use is strictly funerary (burial of deceased children). The absence of fragrance is explicitly remarked upon by Cobo as a deficiency, which is a useful diagnostic detail for modern identification.

results.title_inferred: The Ocot Tree and Its Ornamental Flowers
results.original_text:
CAPITULO LXXIV
Del ocot

El ocot es un árbol grande y grueso; la hoja, del tamaño de la del nogal, es un poco más ancha. Nace en tierras yuncas y su fruto son solas flores. Produce antes de la flor un botón como una avellana, y déste nace una punta larga medio jeme, de hechura y grosor de la bellota; la cual se abre en cinco hojas delgadas y correosas y dentro dellas nace un plumaje hermosísimo que es un manojito de hilos delgados de medio jeme de largo; los cuales desde el pezón hasta la mitad son de un color rojo fino, y de allí hasta el cabo blancos, y se remata cada hilo en un botoncito como grano de anís. Cuando esta flor se abre y se esparcen por la cumbre sus hojitas o hilos, hacen la forma de una borla de doctor o de un plumaje de los que se suelen hacer de vidrios. Echa el árbol tanta cantidad destas flores, que todas sus ramas se rematan en racimos dellas; y por ser poca la hoja que tiene, y quedar cubierto destos plumajes o borlas, viene a ponerse de los más hermosos árboles que yo he visto, y que por su belleza se pudiera plantar en los jardines reales.
results.translation:
CHAPTER LXXIV
Of the Ocot

The ocot is a large and stout tree; its leaf, roughly the size of a walnut leaf, is somewhat broader. It grows in the lowland [yunca] regions, and its fruit consists solely of flowers. Before the flower appears, it produces a bud the size of a hazelnut, from which grows a spike about half a [jeme, a hand's-breadth measure] in length, in shape and thickness resembling an acorn. This spike opens into five thin, leathery leaflets, and from within them emerges a most beautiful plumage: a little bundle of slender filaments about half a jeme in length. These filaments are, from the base [pezón] to the midpoint, a fine crimson color, and from there to the tip they are white, with each filament terminating in a tiny knob like a grain of anise. When this flower opens and its little leaflets or filaments spread out at the top, they take on the form of a doctoral tassel, or of the kind of plume that is commonly fashioned from glass [filigree]. The tree bears such a great quantity of these flowers that all of its branches end in clusters of them; and because it has relatively few leaves and is covered over by these plumes or tassels, it becomes one of the most beautiful trees I have ever seen, and one that by its beauty alone would deserve a place in royal gardens.

entities_mentioned.name: ocot
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Possibly Calliandra or Inga species (Fabaceae/Leguminosae), or alternatively a species of Combretum or Barnadesia; the description of showy, tassel-like staminal filaments bicolored red and white is strongly suggestive of Calliandra trinervia or a related mimosoid legume native to Andean yunca valleys
entities_mentioned.notes: The term 'ocot' does not correspond to a widely attested Quechua or Nahuatl botanical name in standard references. The yunca habitat (Andean inter-Andean low valleys) and the dramatic pom-pom or tassel-like flower morphology with red-and-white bicolored filaments and five leathery tepals point toward a mimosoid legume, but a confident species-level identification is not possible from this description alone. No medicinal use is described in this passage.

entities_mentioned.name: tierras yuncas
entities_mentioned.notes: Yunca (also yunga) refers to the warm, low-lying valleys on the flanks of the Andes, a recognized ecological/geographic zone in colonial Peruvian literature.

results.translator_notes: Several OCR artifacts were silently corrected: 'ocof' → 'ocot' (chapter title makes intent unambiguous); 'hermosígi®^' → 'hermosísimo'; 'dentro' reconstructed from 'dentr®'; 'cubierto' reconstructed from 'cobierto' (period spelling variant also acceptable). The unit 'jeme' is a Spanish linear measure equal to roughly 9–10 cm (the span from the tip of the thumb to the tip of the index finger when extended). No medicinal or pharmaceutical content appears in this segment; it is purely descriptive and aesthetic. The passage contains no extractable drug/preparation data.

results.title_inferred: The Yoloxochitl (Heartflower Tree)
results.original_text:
CAPITULO LXXV
Del yoloxochitl

Yoloxochitl, en lengua mexicana, quiere decir flor de corazón, porque tiene hechura de corazón; y es de tal disposición y forma, que más parece, antes de abrirse, algún género de fruta puntiaguda, que flor; porque es del tamaño y talle de una pera bergamota y se compone de unas hojas grandes, gruesas y tiesas, como pencas de alcachofas, que unas están apretadas sobre otras; y el abrirse esta flor es apartarse un poco por las puntas las hojas exteriores que ciñen toda la flor, las cuales son de un verde claro que tira a blanco. El árbol que lleva estas flores es de la grandeza de un naranjo, copado y de buen parecer, con la hoja tan grande como la del cidro, y la flor da de sí un olor muy agudo y grato.
results.translation:
CHAPTER LXXV
On the Yoloxochitl

Yoloxochitl, in the Mexican tongue [Nahuatl], means 'heart-flower,' because it has the shape of a heart. Its form and appearance are such that, before it opens, it looks more like some kind of pointed fruit than a flower; for it is the size and figure of a bergamot pear [pera bergamota], and is composed of large, thick, and stiff leaves [petals], like the fleshy leaves of artichokes, pressed tightly one upon another. The opening of this flower consists in the outer leaves that encircle the whole bloom spreading apart slightly at their tips; these outer leaves are of a pale green that tends toward white. The tree that bears these flowers is the size of an orange tree, full-crowned and of handsome appearance, with leaves as large as those of the citron [cidro], and the flower gives off a very sharp and pleasing fragrance.

entities_mentioned.name: Yoloxochitl
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Talauma mexicana (syn. Magnolia mexicana), Mexican magnolia
entities_mentioned.notes: The Nahuatl name 'yoloxochitl' (heart-flower) is well attested for Magnolia mexicana. The description of large, stiff, artichoke-like petals forming a pear-shaped bud, a fragrant bloom, and a tree the size of an orange tree with large leaves are all consistent with this identification. No medicinal use is described in this segment.

entities_mentioned.name: pera bergamota
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Bergamot pear (Pyrus communis var. bergamotta or similar Old World cultivar)
entities_mentioned.notes: Used here as a size and shape comparison for the unopened flower bud, not as a plant under discussion.

entities_mentioned.name: alcachofa
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Cynara cardunculus var. scolymus (artichoke)
entities_mentioned.notes: Used as a comparison for the texture and arrangement of the petals ('pencas de alcachofas'), not a plant under discussion.

entities_mentioned.name: cidro
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Citrus medica (citron)
entities_mentioned.notes: Used as a comparison for the size of the yoloxochitl's leaves, not a plant under discussion.

results.title_inferred: Various Flowering Trees of the Americas
results.original_text:
CAPITULO LXXVII

De los demás árboles de flores

Entre las muchas especies de árboles que llevan flores, una es la de cierto árbol llamado sicahani, el cual es pequeño, copado y que produce la hoja poco más larga que la de arrayán, muy fina y áspera, y una florecita colorada, de color de azafrán, que huele bien; y tiene una frutilla del mismo color y del tamaño de ciruelas pequeñas en que nace la flor.

Llaman en la Nueva España flor de la oreja a cierta flor, porque es de figura de una oreja de hombre, la cual echan en el chocolate. El árbol que la produce es mediano, tiene las hojas largas y angostas, de color verde oscuro, pendientes de un pezoncillo marchito. La flor huele bien, y sus hojas, por la parte interior, son purpúreas, y por la exterior, verdes. Bebida esta flor en chocolate, es buena para la asma y para confortar el estómago resfriado.

Otro árbol de flores tiene la hoja como de durazno; echa una flor blanca del tamaño de la clavellina, con dos órdenes de hojitas encrespadas. Tiene buen olor, mas no tan suave como la de arriba.

El árbol llamado tensixochitl echa la hoja parecida a la del lentisco; da una florecita colorada al modo de la del granado, pero menor y de más cortas hojas, con unos hilillos delgados del mismo color. Esta flor no tiene ningún olor, mas es de buen parecer; nace en racimos apiñados; y el árbol echa unas vainillas como algarrobas.

Otro árbol se halla que tiene por cogollos unas grandes flores, cada una con muchas hojas alrededor tan largas y más anchas que de sauce; son de un color rojo muy encendido, con unos botoncillos amarillos en medio; hace cada cogollo una rosa de una tercia de diámetro con las hojas ralas. El árbol tiene la hoja semejante a la del moral, y partida, destila leche.

Otro árbol que tiene la hoja semejante a la del naranjo, poco menor, echa en racimos unas florecitas como las vainillas del rábano, algo menores, de un color purpúreo muy fino, que se pone muy vistoso el árbol. A la flor suceden unos granillos negros del tamaño y forma de escaramujos. Con la hoja y cogollo de este árbol tiñen de negro.

El árbol llamado fasta, en el Perú, es del grandor de un mediano olivo, copado, de mucha hoja, más pequeña que la del arrayán; vístese de unas florecitas blancas menores que azahar; nacen estos árboles en los valles de la Sierra, y su madera es extremada para carbón.
results.translation:
CHAPTER LXXVII

On the Remaining Flowering Trees

Among the many species of trees that bear flowers, one is a certain tree called sicahani, which is small, bushy, and produces a leaf slightly longer than that of the myrtle [arrayán], very fine and rough, and a small reddish flower, saffron-colored, that smells pleasant; it also bears a small fruit of the same color and of the size of small plums, at the point where the flower emerges.

In New Spain, a certain flower is called the 'ear flower' [flor de la oreja] because it is shaped like a human ear; this flower is added to chocolate. The tree that produces it is of medium size, with long, narrow leaves of a dark green color, hanging from a withered little stem. The flower smells pleasant, and its leaves are purple on the inner side and green on the outer side. This flower, drunk in chocolate, is good for asthma and for comforting a cold stomach [estómago resfriado].

Another flowering tree has leaves resembling those of the peach tree; it produces a white flower the size of a pink [clavellina], with two rows of curled petals. It has a good scent, though not as gentle as the one described above.

The tree called tensixochitl bears leaves resembling those of the mastic tree [lentisco]; it produces a small reddish flower in the manner of the pomegranate flower, though smaller and with shorter petals, with slender little threads [hilillos] of the same color. This flower has no fragrance, but is pleasant in appearance; it grows in dense clusters; and the tree produces small pods resembling carob pods.

Another tree is found whose new shoots [cogollos] bear large flowers, each with many surrounding petals as long as and wider than those of the willow; they are of a very vivid red color, with small yellow buttons in the center; each shoot forms a bloom about one-third of a vara [approximately 28 cm] in diameter, with sparse petals. The tree has a leaf similar to that of the mulberry, and when cut, it exudes a milky sap.

Another tree, whose leaf resembles that of the orange tree, though somewhat smaller, produces in clusters small flowers resembling radish seed-pods, slightly smaller, of a very fine purplish color, which makes the tree very striking in appearance. After the flowers come small black seeds of the size and shape of dog-rose hips [escaramujos]. The leaf and new shoots of this tree are used to dye cloth black.

The tree called fasta, in Peru, is the size of a medium olive tree, bushy and leafy, with leaves smaller than those of the myrtle; it clothes itself in small white flowers smaller than orange blossoms [azahar]. These trees grow in the mountain valleys [valles de la Sierra], and their wood is excellent for making charcoal.

entities_mentioned.name: sicahani
entities_mentioned.notes: Small bushy tree with saffron-colored flowers and small plum-sized fruit; likely Andean. No confident modern identification.

entities_mentioned.name: flor de la oreja (ear flower)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Cymbopetalum penduliflorum (Annonaceae)
entities_mentioned.notes: Added to chocolate in New Spain; ear-shaped flower with purple inner leaves and green outer leaves. Condition treated: asthma and cold stomach. Preparation: drunk in chocolate. The common identification of 'flor de la oreja' in colonial Mexican sources is Cymbopetalum penduliflorum, though other Annonaceae have been proposed.

entities_mentioned.name: tensixochitl (also given as 'tensiixochitl' in source, corrected to 'tensixochitl')
entities_mentioned.notes: Nahuatl name; leaves resemble mastic (lentisco); red flowers without fragrance; pods resembling carob. Possibly a Caesalpiniaceae or Mimosaceae species, but insufficient detail for confident identification.

entities_mentioned.name: unnamed tree with large red flowers, milky sap, mulberry-like leaves
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Possibly Malvaviscus arboreus or a Bombacaceae/Malvaceae species
entities_mentioned.notes: Large vivid-red flowers with yellow centers, milky latex when cut, mulberry-like leaves. The description is suggestive of a Malvaceae or possibly Erythrina, but not conclusive.

entities_mentioned.name: unnamed tree used for black dye, orange-like leaves, purple flowers, black hip-like seeds
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Possibly Monnina species (Polygalaceae) or Berberis species
entities_mentioned.notes: Used to dye cloth black from leaves and shoots. Purple flowers in racemes; black seeds like dog-rose hips. Black dyeing plants in the Andes include several candidates.

entities_mentioned.name: fasta (Peru)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Possibly Escallonia resinosa or Buddleja species
entities_mentioned.notes: Medium olive-sized bushy tree of Andean mountain valleys; small myrtle-like leaves; small white flowers; excellent for charcoal. 'Fasta' may correspond to a Peruvian vernacular name. Some sources associate this description with Escallonia or related Andean shrubs.

results.translator_notes: Several OCR errors were silently corrected: 'fte' → 'que', 'i^hol' → 'árbol', 'ijaeño' → 'pequeño', 'Aíra' → 'fina', 'tíeae' → 'tiene', 'tolano' → 'tamaño', 'Wf' → 'nace', 'poduce' → 'produce', 'echa una flor hlan-^' → 'echa una flor blanca', '<«deneí' → 'órdenes', '%in' → 'buen', 'tensüxochitl/tensiixochitl' normalized to 'tensixochitl', 'parecida a la del lentisco; da una / florecita colorada al modo de la del / pero menor' — the word between 'del' and 'pero' is OCR-lost; from botanical context (red flower resembling a pomegranate) 'granado' has been inferred and inserted, marked implicitly in translation. 'encedido' corrected to 'encendido'. The 'tercia' measurement refers to one-third of a vara (the Spanish yard), approximately 28 cm.

results.title_inferred: On the Molle Tree: Properties, Resin, and Medicinal Uses
results.original_text:
CAPITULO LXXVIII
Del molla

Los árboles que hallamos en estas Indias de saludables gomas y resina, son sin número; sólo trataré aquí de los más conocidos; y sea el primero el que llaman molla en este reino del Perú, que es un árbol muy conocido y que nace en tierras templadas y calientes. Es de la grandeza de un olivo, y de aquí para abajo se halla de diferente tamaño; es agradable a la vista, de un verde claro y su hoja parecida a la del lentisco, algo más luenga, más angosta y más delgada.

Echa una frutilla en racimos colorada, del tamaño de la del saúco, de la cual suelen hacer chicha los indios, y es tan fuerte, que embriaga más que la que se hace de maíz y de otras semillas, y la tienen los indios por la más preciosa y regalada. En este linaje de árboles hay macho y hembra; el macho da el fruto que queda dicho, y la hembra, aunque lo produce, no se logra, porque se queda en ciernes.

Es el molle árbol incorruptible, de un olor aromático, y de temperamento caliente, y con mucha estipticidad, muy estimado de los indios por sus maravillosos efectos. Dándole algunas cuchilladas así en el tronco como en las ramas, destila una resina algo blanca y olorosa, la cual servía para embalsamar y conservar sin corrupción los cuerpos de los reyes Incas, cuando los ponían en sus guacas o sepulcros. Vale esta resina para infinitas cosas; porque es una purga extremada y noble para la flema y melancolía, sin bascas ni pesadumbre. Echada en un poco de vino en remojo hasta tanto que se vuelva como leche, que será en veinticuatro horas, dada con un poco de azúcar, purga suavemente el agua y flema de los hidrópicos; y si en el estómago halla flemas, las suele echar por vómito, si el estómago se inclina a ello. Puesta en el sieso una calilla de esta resina mezclada con acíbar y sebo de macho, mata las lombrices.

Para curar el mal del valle, he visto aplicar el molle de esta manera: de sus cortezas y resina se hace un cocimiento hasta que el agua quede colorada: de esta agua, tibia, se ha de beber una escudilla en ayunas y otra por la tarde, y del mismo cocimiento se endian ayudas; y de la corteza seca al sol se hacen polvos, los cuales se echan también en aquella parte donde está el mal. Con este medicamento se curan los indios del valle de Ica, adonde es muy ordinario este mal; y yo he visto curas maravillosas de enfermos ya desahuciados.

Aprovechan los polvos de esta resina para mundificar y encerrar toda llaga con suavidad y blandura. Cocidas, las hojas del molle tienen facultad, dando baño, de deshinchar las piernas de los hidrópicos y gotosos. Las hojas majadas con facilidad cierran las heridas frescas y las desecan y sanan; y el vino estíptico que se hace de sus cortezas y hojas, junto con rosas, resina de Zípa, millo o alumbre, deseca y conforta llagas y cría fuerte cicatriz.

El emplasto de las uvillas de este árbol, majadas, aplicado caliente sobre el estómago flaco, lo conforta, da gana de comer y repara los vómitos; y aplicado asimismo por todo el vientre, quita las cámaras. Lo mismo hace el mosto dellas, si con él se echan ayudas con azúcar. Finalmente, con las hojas verdes deste árbol se defienden los que trabajan en las viñas, de los mosquitos, poniéndose en la cabeza una guirnalda dellas, porque deben huir de su olor.
results.translation:
CHAPTER LXXVIII
On the Molle

The trees we find in these Indies that yield healthful gums and resins are beyond number; I shall treat here only of the best known, and the first shall be the one called molle [also molle] in this kingdom of Peru, a well-known tree that grows in temperate and warm lands. It is as large as an olive tree, and smaller below that size; it is pleasant to the eye, of a light green, and its leaf resembles that of the mastic tree [lentisco], though somewhat longer, narrower, and thinner.

It bears a small reddish fruit in clusters, the size of elderberries, from which the Indians customarily make chicha [a fermented beverage], and it is so strong that it intoxicates more than that made from maize or other seeds; the Indians hold it to be the most precious and refined of all. In this kind of tree there are male and female; the male bears the fruit described above, and the female, though it produces fruit as well, does not bring it to maturity, for it remains in blossom.

The molle is an incorruptible tree, of an aromatic smell and a hot temperament [in the Galenic sense], and of great astringency [estipticidad], highly esteemed by the Indians for its marvellous effects. By making several cuts in the trunk and branches alike, it exudes a somewhat whitish and fragrant resin, which was used to embalm and preserve without corruption the bodies of the Inca kings when they were placed in their guacas [huacas] or sepulchres. This resin is of use for countless things, for it is an excellent and refined purge for phlegm and melancholy [in the humoral sense], without nausea or discomfort. Steeped in a little wine until it turns milky — which takes twenty-four hours — and given with a little sugar, it gently purges the water and phlegm of dropsical patients [hidrópicos]; and if it encounters phlegm in the stomach, it tends to expel it by vomiting, should the stomach be disposed to that. A small suppository [calilla] of this resin mixed with aloes [acíbar] and tallow from a male goat, inserted into the rectum [sieso], kills intestinal worms.

To cure the mal del valle [lit. 'disease of the valley,' likely Chagas disease or a locally endemic illness of the Ica valley], I have seen the molle applied in the following manner: a decoction is made of its bark and resin until the water turns red; of this water, lukewarm, a bowlful is to be drunk on an empty stomach and another in the afternoon, and enemas [ayudas] are likewise administered from the same decoction; and from the bark dried in the sun, powders are made, which are also applied to the affected part. By means of this remedy, the Indians of the valley of Ica — where this disease is very common — are cured, and I have myself witnessed marvellous cures of patients who had already been given up as hopeless.

The powders of this resin are also useful for cleansing and closing all manner of wounds gently and softly. The leaves of the molle, when boiled and used as a bath, have the power to reduce the swollen legs of dropsical patients and those with gout. The leaves, bruised, readily close fresh wounds and dry and heal them; and the astringent wine [vino estíptico] made from its bark and leaves, together with roses, resin of Zipa [identity uncertain; possibly a local resinous plant], millet or alum, dries and comforts wounds and forms a firm scar.

A poultice [emplasto] of the small berries of this tree, bruised and applied warm to a weak stomach, comforts it, stimulates appetite, and checks vomiting; and applied likewise over the entire abdomen, it stops diarrhoea [cámaras]. The must [mosto] of the berries, used as an enema with sugar, does the same. Finally, those who work in the vineyards protect themselves from mosquitoes by wearing a garland of the green leaves of this tree upon their heads, since mosquitoes apparently flee from their smell.

entities_mentioned.name: molle / molla
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Schinus molle (Peruvian pepper tree / false pepper)
entities_mentioned.notes: One of the most commonly described Andean medicinal trees in colonial sources. The description of leaf form, red berried clusters, aromatic resin, and Peruvian provenance is unambiguous. Used by Incas for embalming.

entities_mentioned.name: chicha de molle
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Fermented beverage made from Schinus molle berries
entities_mentioned.notes: Described as stronger than maize chicha; prepared from the red berries of the molle tree.

entities_mentioned.name: mal del valle
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Possibly Chagas disease (Trypanosoma cruzi infection) or another endemic illness of the coastal valleys of Peru; some scholars associate it with leishmaniasis or a form of syphilis
entities_mentioned.notes: Cobo reports this disease as very common in the valley of Ica. The identification remains debated in the literature. The symptoms are not described in detail here, limiting confident identification.

entities_mentioned.name: acíbar
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Aloe vera or related Aloe species (inspissated aloe juice/latex)
entities_mentioned.notes: Standard term in colonial Spanish pharmacopeia for dried aloe latex used as a laxative and in suppositories.

entities_mentioned.name: resina de Zípa
entities_mentioned.notes: Possibly a resin associated with the Zipa (the ruler of the Muisca people of the Bogotá savanna), or a plant named after that region. Identity not established with confidence. Could be a variant spelling or OCR artifact of another known resin.

entities_mentioned.name: lentisco
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Pistacia lentiscus (mastic tree)
entities_mentioned.notes: Used by Cobo as a comparative reference for the molle leaf shape, not as a medicinal plant under discussion here.

entities_mentioned.name: saúco
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Sambucus nigra or a related elderberry species
entities_mentioned.notes: Used as a size comparison for the molle berry.

results.translator_notes: 1. 'Molla' in the chapter heading is a variant spelling (possibly OCR) of 'molle,' corrected silently in the cleaned text and translation. 2. 'Resina algo blanca' was rendered in the source OCR as 'resina algo Manca' — the capital M is an obvious OCR error for 'b'; corrected to 'blanca.' 3. 'Calilla' (small suppository or pessary inserted rectally) is a period medical term preserved in brackets. 4. 'Mal del valle' is left in Spanish with a bracketed note, as it is a specific colonial disease designation whose modern correlate is uncertain. 5. 'Millo' in the phrase 'millo o alumbre' most likely refers to millet used as an astringent powder or possibly a local variant term; alumbre is alum. 6. 'Guacas' is glossed as 'huacas' (the more standard modern spelling) in the translation. 7. The final passage about vineyard workers using molle garlands to repel mosquitoes is ethnobotanically notable. 8. Preparation methods identified: (a) resin soaked in wine 24 hours → oral purgative with sugar; (b) resin + aloes + tallow → rectal suppository for worms; (c) bark + resin decoction → oral drink and enema for mal del valle; (d) sun-dried bark → powder applied topically; (e) boiled leaves → bath for dropsy/gout; (f) bruised leaves → poultice for fresh wounds; (g) bark + leaves + roses + Zipa resin + alum → astringent wine for wound healing; (h) bruised berries → warm poultice for stomach and diarrhea; (i) berry must → enema with sugar.

results.title_inferred: On the cauchuc (rubber tree) of Peru and Nicaragua
results.original_text:
CAPITULO LXXIX
Del cauchuc

En este reino del Perú llaman cauchuc a un licor resinoso que en la Nueva España nombran ule, el cual es bien conocido en todas las Indias. El árbol es muy semejante a un nogal, si bien no es tan grande; echa las ramas muy largas, muy pobladas de hojas pareadas de dos en dos, como las del nogal, salvo que cada una destas hojas es larga dos palmos y ancha un jeme; es de verde oscuro y están ramas y hojas cubiertas de un corto vello. Produce una fruta silvestre, amarga y desabrida, como manzanas, que por golosina suelen comer los muchachos. La madera del árbol es aguanosa; la corteza del tronco lo es mucho más, cuyo cocimiento cura las cámaras de sangre.

Sajado el tronco deste árbol, destila una goma tan líquida y blanca como leche, y en tanta cantidad, que sale un hilo della como brota la sangre de la vena en una sangría; vase volviendo poco a poco amarilla y últimamente negra, pero con esta diferencia, que si se deja en una vasija, se está líquida mucho tiempo, y en untando con ella cualquiera cosa, en breve se cuaja y se vuelve negra. Y para experimentar esta leve mudanza, hice una vez picar un árbol, estando en la provincia de Nicaragua, y con el licor que corría me fui lavando las manos, y aunque quedaron luego blancas, dentro de una hora se había ya helado y vuelto negro, de manera que parecían estar las manos con guantes negros. Sirve esta resina para calzar botas, antiparas y otras cosas, porque resiste al agua; y los que caminan por Nicaragua y otras tierras calientes, donde la hierba crecida suele mojar con su rocío a los caminantes, suelen hacer unas grandes medias de lienzo que les lleguen hasta la cintura, y calzadas por de fuera con esta resina, quedan como enceradas y defienden muy bien del agua. En cuajándose esta resina, queda negra, liviana como corcho y correosa como un nervio. Si la encienden, arde como cera, y hecha de ella una pelota, da mayor bote que pelota de viento; y echando un pedacillo de ella en las pelotas de cuero, les hace dar muy levantados botes; y así usan de ella para jugar a la pelota. Y, finalmente, es muy útil para muchas medicinas. Aplícase para curar cámaras de sangre, para provocar la orina y cura otros males.
results.translation:
CHAPTER LXXIX
On the cauchuc

In this kingdom of Peru they call cauchuc a resinous liquid which in New Spain is called ule [hule], and which is well known throughout all the Indies. The tree is very similar to a walnut tree, though not as large; it puts forth very long branches, thickly set with leaves arranged in pairs, two by two, like those of the walnut, except that each of these leaves is two palms [roughly 16 inches] long and one hand-span [jeme, roughly 7 inches] wide; they are dark green, and both branches and leaves are covered with a short down. It produces a wild fruit, bitter and unpleasant in taste, resembling apples, which children are wont to eat as a treat. The wood of the tree is watery; the bark of the trunk is even more so, and a decoction [cocimiento] of it cures bloody flux [cámaras de sangre, i.e., dysentery].

When the trunk of this tree is incised [sajado], it exudes a gum as liquid and white as milk, and in such quantity that it flows in a stream just as blood flows from a vein during a bloodletting [sangría]; it turns little by little yellow and ultimately black, but with this distinction: if left in a vessel it remains liquid for a long time, whereas if it is applied to any surface, it quickly congeals and turns black. And in order to witness this swift transformation for myself, I once had a tree tapped while I was in the province of Nicaragua, and I washed my hands with the liquid as it ran out; and though they remained white at first, within an hour it had already solidified and turned black, so that my hands appeared to be wearing black gloves. This resin serves to waterproof boots, gaiters [antiparas], and other things, because it resists water; and those who travel through Nicaragua and other hot lands, where the tall grass tends to soak travelers with its dew, are accustomed to making large linen stockings reaching up to the waist, and once coated on the outside with this resin, they become as though waxed and protect very well against water. Once it congeals, this resin turns black, light as cork, and tough and pliable as a sinew [nervio]. If it is set alight, it burns like wax; and made into a ball, it bounces higher than an inflated ball [pelota de viento]; and by inserting a small piece of it inside leather balls, it makes them bounce very high; and so they use it for playing ball games. And, finally, it is very useful for many medicines. It is applied to cure bloody flux [cámaras de sangre], to promote urination, and it cures various other ailments.

entities_mentioned.name: cauchuc (also: ule / hule in New Spain)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Hevea brasiliensis (Pará rubber tree) or a closely related Castilla elastica (Panama rubber tree); given the Nicaragua reference, Castilla elastica is plausible, though Hevea spp. are the primary source of the name 'cauchuc'
entities_mentioned.notes: Cobo provides the earliest substantial Spanish-language description of natural rubber, noting both its physical properties (elasticity, waterproofing, flammability) and medicinal uses. The name 'cauchuc' derives from Quechua; 'ule/hule' is from Nahuatl. The description of pinnate paired leaves and walnut-like appearance, combined with the Nicaragua provenance, is more consistent with Castilla elastica than with Hevea brasiliensis, which is Amazonian. Conditions treated: bloody dysentery (cámaras de sangre) via bark decoction; the resin also applied for dysentery, to promote urination, and unspecified other ailments. Preparation: (1) bark decoction (cocimiento de la corteza) for dysentery; (2) raw latex applied topically or internally for the same and other conditions.

results.translator_notes: The OCR introduced numerous errors throughout (e.g., 'cm chuc', 'Niie* va España', 'hm conocido', 'árkl', 'mm largas', 'pareada', 'salv#', 'frati', 'ro»', '^', 'daí?', 'troni¬co', 'cociraieEtíi^', 'desti¬la', 'com', 'sa', '«', 'k', 'fe¬ve', 'm ir*', 'f®', 'quedárí^', '^', '»', 'ñ »', 'guiar', 'páian', 'guiadas', 'jauT', 'mienden', '¿anfrc', '#tro?'); all have been silently corrected where the intended reading was unambiguous from context and surrounding text. The unit 'jeme' (a hand-span measured from thumb tip to index finger tip, roughly 7 inches / 18 cm) has been glossed in the translation. 'Antiparas' are leggings or gaiters worn over footwear as protection. 'Pelota de viento' refers to an air-filled or bladder ball. The first-person observation places Cobo in Nicaragua, providing a useful autobiographical data point for scholars.

results.title_inferred: The Yoyote Tree: Description, Fruit, and Toxic Properties
results.original_text:
CAPITULO LXXX
Del yoyote

El yoyote es un árbol mediano, que nace en tierras yuncas; su hoja es larga un jeme, y más angosta que la del lisa, tiesa y gruesa, y tiene los cogollos muy poblados de ella, por lo cual suelen adornar las iglesias con sus ramas. Produce una fruta del tamaño de pera cermeña, algo chata, que tiene tres picos en igual distancia: uno en la cumbre, enfrente del corazón, y los dos a los lados. Debajo de la cáscara verde y tierna tiene una pepita encerrada en dura como la de la almendra; es esta pepita ponzoñosa y también lo es la cáscara exterior; y se ha visto comérsela conejos caseros y morir luego. Basta esta pepita sobre la muela podrida, la deshace.
results.translation:
CHAPTER LXXX
On the Yoyote

The yoyote is a medium-sized tree that grows in the lowland [yunca] regions. Its leaf is a hand-span [jeme] in length, narrower than that of the lisa, stiff and thick; its shoots are very densely covered with leaves, for which reason its branches are often used to decorate churches. It produces a fruit the size of a Saint John's pear [pera cermeña], somewhat flattened, bearing three points at equal intervals: one at the top, opposite the core [corazón], and one on each side. Beneath the green and tender outer rind lies a seed enclosed in a shell as hard as that of an almond. This seed is poisonous, as is the outer rind; domestic rabbits have been observed to eat it and die shortly thereafter. Merely placing this seed upon a rotten molar is sufficient to dissolve it [i.e., destroy the decayed tooth].

entities_mentioned.name: yoyote
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Thevetia thevetioides or Thevetia peruviana (Be-still tree / yellow oleander, family Apocynaceae); possibly also Cascabela spp.
entities_mentioned.notes: The description of a medium tree native to lowland (yunca) regions, with a three-angled fruit, hard inner seed, and potent toxicity (lethal to rabbits) is consistent with Thevetia/Cascabela species. Some scholars associate 'yoyote' with Thevetia thevetioides, native to Mexico and parts of South America. The dental application of the toxic seed to destroy a rotten tooth is a notable recorded use.

entities_mentioned.name: pera cermeña
entities_mentioned.notes: A variety of small, early-ripening pear used here as a size reference; not a plant being discussed in its own right.

results.translator_notes: Several OCR errors silently corrected: 'm jeme' → 'un jeme' (a standard Spanish hand-span measure, approx. 20 cm); 'h- pera' → 'de pera'; 'h cumbre' → 'la cumbre'; 'ihñ lados' → 'a los lados'; 'x^czón' → 'corazón'; '5 tierna' → 'y tierna'; '«ísta' → 'esta'; 'h ciscara' → 'la cáscara'; '5 ba visto' → 'se ha visto'; 'Berla' → 'comérsela'; 'IWsta' → 'Basta'; 'Aida' → 'podrida'. The dental use (applying the toxic seed to a decayed tooth to dissolve it) constitutes a medicinal/dental application. Yunca (also spelled 'yunga') refers to the warm, low-altitude valleys of the Andean foothills, a recognized geographic/ecological term in colonial Peruvian sources.

results.title_inferred: On the Quina-Quina Tree: Properties, Uses, and Preparations
results.original_text:
CAPITULO LXXXI
De la quina-quina

Llaman en el Perú a un árbol grande y hermoso como un membrillo o un olivo; la hoja, del tamaño y talle que la del limón ceutí; el tronco es algo colorado, resinoso y aromático. Es árbol caliente en el segundo grado, estíptico y seco y de suave olor. Echa unas pepitas por semilla del tamaño de almendras, de color amarillo y de sustancia oleaginosa, que con fragancia huelen amigablemente; son asimismo calientes y estípticas en el segundo grado, y secas en más del primero. Sajando el tronco y ramas destila una resina olorosa, que se congela tanto, que se muele en polvos y queda de color negro claro, la cual es caliente y más seca que las pepitas. Nace este árbol en la tierra caliente de la provincia de los Charcas, en el Perú. Si con su corteza se limpia de ordinario la dentadura, la aprieta y conforta; y el palo, raspado y cocido con polipodio, hojas de sen y anís, y el cocimiento tomado en ayunas algunas mañanas, desopila el estómago, hígado y bazo, mundifica y limpia la vejiga. Las hojas, majadas y puestas sobre las heridas frescas, las desecan y juntan, y el cocimiento dellas con salmuera, hojas de chilca y molle deshincha las piernas gotosas. De las pepitas de este árbol se hace un aceite maravilloso para toda herida fresca, el cual se usa mucho en Potosí y hácese desta manera: majadas cuatro onzas déstas pepitas, se echan en la cuarta parte de un cuartillo de vino añejo por espacio de dos horas, y luego se echa todo en dos libras y media de aceite, y a fuego manso cuece hasta que se consume el vino; y quitado del fuego y frío, se cuela y se vuelve a la olla o cazo, y se añade una libra de trementina común, y con ella da un hervor no más; y apartado del fuego, se le echan de polvos de incienso y de mirra, de cada cosa onza y media, sutilmente molidos, y se menean para que se incorporen; y tapado el vaso, se guarda; y hace maravillosos efectos.

Demás desto, sahumándose así con las pepitas como con la resina, se quitan los dolores de cabeza. Las pepitas tostadas y tomadas con vino son contra el dolor de ijada y ventosidades, y majadas, mezcladas con polvos de la resina y todo ello cocido con vino con un poco de la resina de molle, incienso y miel de abejas, aprovecha el cocimiento, después de colado, contra las llagas sucias y cavernosas, porque mundifica y deseca con suavidad. La resina, sutilmente molida y hervida con aceite común o con tocino o manteca, junta las heridas frescas; y el polvo, echado sobre la herida, atrae cuanta humedad tiene y la deseca.
results.translation:
CHAPTER LXXXI
On the Quina-Quina

In Peru they give this name to a large and handsome tree resembling a quince or an olive; its leaf is the size and shape of the Ceuti [Moroccan] lemon leaf; the trunk is somewhat reddish, resinous, and aromatic. The tree is [humorally] hot in the second degree, astringent [estíptico], dry, and of a pleasant smell. It bears small seeds [pepitas] the size of almonds, yellow in color and of an oily substance, which smell agreeably fragrant; these too are hot and astringent in the second degree, and dry beyond the first. When the trunk and branches are scored [sajando], they exude a fragrant resin that congeals so thoroughly it can be ground to powder, at which point it becomes a pale black color; this resin is hot and drier than the seeds. This tree grows in the warm lowlands [tierra caliente] of the province of Charcas, in Peru.

If one regularly cleans the teeth with its bark, it tightens and strengthens them. The wood, scraped and boiled together with polypody [fern], senna leaves, and anise, and the decoction taken on an empty stomach for several mornings, unblocks [desopila] the stomach, liver, and spleen, and cleanses and purifies the bladder. The leaves, pounded and applied to fresh wounds, dry and close them; and a decoction of the leaves with brine, leaves of chilca, and molle reduces the swelling of gouty legs.

From the seeds of this tree a marvelous oil is made for all fresh wounds, which is widely used in Potosí. It is prepared as follows: four ounces of these seeds are pounded and steeped in a quarter of a quartillo [a small liquid measure] of aged wine for two hours; this mixture is then added to two and a half pounds of oil and cooked over a gentle fire until the wine has been consumed. Removed from the heat and allowed to cool, it is strained and returned to the pot or pan, and one pound of common turpentine is added, brought to a single boil only; then, removed from the fire, one and a half ounces each of powdered frankincense and myrrh, finely ground, are added and stirred in so as to blend together. The vessel is sealed and stored; it produces marvelous effects.

Furthermore, fumigation [sahumándose] with both the seeds and the resin relieves headaches. The toasted seeds taken with wine are effective against flank pain [dolor de ijada] and flatulence [ventosidades]. Pounded and mixed with powdered resin, the whole boiled in wine with a little molle resin, frankincense, and honey, the resulting decoction—once strained—is beneficial against foul and cavernous sores [llagas sucias y cavernosas], because it cleanses and dries them gently. The resin, finely ground and boiled with common oil or with lard or butter, closes fresh wounds; and the powder, applied directly to a wound, draws out all its moisture and dries it.

entities_mentioned.name: quina-quina
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Myroxylon peruiferum or Myroxylon balsamum var. pereirae (balsam of Peru / quina-quina); alternatively sometimes identified as Myroxylon toluiferum. Some scholars also associate the name with Chenopodium quinoa or Lomatia species, but the resinous, balsamic description strongly suggests Myroxylon.
entities_mentioned.notes: The 'quina-quina' name was applied to multiple Andean plants; the aromatic resin, almond-sized oily seeds, and warm-dry humoral classification are consistent with a Myroxylon species (family Fabaceae). Not to be confused with quina / Cinchona (the antimalarial bark), which is a distinct plant. The province of Charcas (modern Bolivia) is within the natural range of Myroxylon.

entities_mentioned.name: polipodio (polypody)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Polypodium vulgare or a related Polypodium sp. (family Polypodiaceae)
entities_mentioned.notes: A common Old World fern used as a mild laxative and hepatic remedy in Galenic medicine; likely imported or locally substituted.

entities_mentioned.name: sen (senna leaves)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Senna alexandrina (Cassia senna), family Fabaceae
entities_mentioned.notes: Standard laxative/purgative in 17th-century Galenic pharmacy; almost certainly refers to the imported Old World drug.

entities_mentioned.name: chilca
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Baccharis latifolia (or related Baccharis spp.), family Asteraceae
entities_mentioned.notes: Widely used Andean shrub with anti-inflammatory and vulnerary properties; used here in a decoction for gouty swelling.

entities_mentioned.name: molle
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Schinus molle (Peruvian pepper tree), family Anacardiaceae
entities_mentioned.notes: The resin of molle is mentioned both in the decoction for wounds and alongside quina-quina preparations; S. molle resin was a staple of Andean colonial medicine.

entities_mentioned.name: trementina (turpentine)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Oleoresin of Pinus spp. or related conifers
entities_mentioned.notes: Standard ingredient in wound oils in European and colonial pharmacopoeias.

entities_mentioned.name: incienso (frankincense)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Boswellia sacra resin (or generic 'incense' resin)
entities_mentioned.notes: Used as a desiccant and antiseptic additive in wound preparations.

entities_mentioned.name: mirra (myrrh)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Commiphora myrrha resin, family Burseraceae
entities_mentioned.notes: Standard vulnerary and antiseptic resin in European and colonial formularies.

medical_extractions.preparation: Regular rubbing of teeth with the bark

medical_extractions.condition_treated: Obstruction of stomach, liver, spleen; bladder impurities
medical_extractions.preparation: Wood scraped and boiled with polypody, senna leaves, and anise; decoction taken fasting for several mornings
Leaves pounded and applied as a poultice
Decoction of the leaves with brine, chilca leaves, and molle applied externally

Oil prepared by pounding 4 oz of seeds, macerating in aged wine for 2 hours, cooking in 2.5 lbs of oil over gentle heat until wine evaporates, straining, returning to vessel, adding 1 lb turpentine and bringing to one boil, then incorporating 1.5 oz each of powdered frankincense and myrrh off the heat. Stored in a sealed vessel.
Fumigation (smoked inhalation) with seeds or resin

medical_extractions.plant_name: quina-quina seeds + resin + molle resin + frankincense + honey
medical_extractions.preparation: Pounded seeds mixed with powdered resin, boiled in wine with molle resin, frankincense, and honey; strained decoction applied to wounds
Resin finely ground and boiled with common oil, lard, or butter; applied to close wounds. Alternatively, pure powdered resin applied directly to draw out moisture and dry the wound.

results.translator_notes: 1. The OCR rendered the chapter heading and opening lines fragmentarily ('Déla q ii i n a ^ q u i n a', 'irW grande y hermoso como un me- / olivo'); these have been reconstructed as 'quina-quina' and 'membrillo o un olivo' (quince or olive) based on context and standard botanical descriptions of this tree in other colonial sources. The word before 'olivo' is partially illegible but 'membrillo' (quince) is a plausible reading given the tree's described size and appearance. 2. 'Cuarta parte de un cuartillo' is a very small volume (~15–20 ml by period measures); the recipe proportions are unusual but reproduced faithfully. 3. The humoral characterizations ('hot in the second degree,' 'dry,' 'astringent') follow standard Galenic scholastic medicine as applied by colonial naturalists. 4. 'Desopila' (unblocks obstructions) is a key term in colonial Galenic pathology referring to clearing blockages of the visceral organs. 5. The medical extraction fields have been added as an extension of the schema to capture the dense pharmacological content; they do not replace any required schema fields.

results.title_inferred: The Tecoma Haca Tree and Its Medicinal Resin
results.original_text:
CAPITULO LXXXIV
De la tecoma haca

Este es un árbol que tiene las hojas redondas, y aserradas; lleva un fruto redondo, pequeño y rojo lleno de simientes semejantes a las pepitas del durazno. Es planta mordaz y odorífera, cortando este árbol mana dél una goma como la del copal, muy provechosa, que la suelen usar en lugar de la mirra y en la cura de muchas enfermedades, y principalmente corrige las destemplanzas frías y conforta el estómago. Nace este árbol en la tierra caliente de la Nueva España.
results.translation:
CHAPTER LXXXIV
Of the Tecoma Haca

This is a tree with round, serrated leaves; it bears a small, round, red fruit full of seeds resembling the pits of the peach [durazno]. It is a pungent and fragrant plant; when this tree is cut, a gum [resin] flows from it similar to that of copal, which is very beneficial and is commonly used in place of myrrh in the treatment of many ailments. It principally corrects cold imbalances [destemplanzas frías] and fortifies the stomach. This tree grows in the hot lands [tierra caliente] of New Spain.

entities_mentioned.name: tecoma haca
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Possibly Bursera species (e.g., Bursera copallifera or related copal-producing Bursera sp.), or potentially a Tecomella / Tecoma relative; the resin description strongly suggests a Burseraceae member
entities_mentioned.notes: The name 'tecoma haca' does not correspond to a well-attested Nahuatl or botanical epithet in standard references. The description of round serrated leaves, small red fruit with peach-pit-like seeds, and a copal-like aromatic resin used medicinally in place of myrrh is consistent with a Bursera species, but positive identification is uncertain.

entities_mentioned.name: copal
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Bursera spp. or Protium copal (Burseraceae)
entities_mentioned.notes: Copal resin, widely attested in Mesoamerican sources; used here as a comparison for the tecoma haca resin.

entities_mentioned.name: mirra (myrrh)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Commiphora myrrha (Burseraceae)
entities_mentioned.notes: Referenced as a therapeutic standard against which the tecoma haca resin is compared; indicates the resin was used as a local substitute for the imported Old World drug.

entities_mentioned.name: durazno
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Prunus persica (peach)
entities_mentioned.notes: Used as a morphological reference point for the fruit seeds; not itself a subject of this chapter.

results.translator_notes: Several OCR errors were silently corrected: 'rfilondas' → 'redondas'; 'jtiíondo' → 'redondo'; 'iiientes' → 'simientes'; 'lurízno' → 'durazno'; 'viajando' → 'cortando' (contextually unambiguous — cutting the tree causes resin to flow); '333 como' → 'goma como'; '>|iie' → 'que'; 'destemplan-%u' → 'destemplanzas'; 'la España' → 'la Nueva España' (standard Cobo usage for New Spain). The humoral term 'destemplanzas frías' (cold intemperances/imbalances) reflects Galenic medicine and has been rendered with the bracketed original. The preparation method is implicit: the resin is harvested by cutting the tree and collected for use as an aromatic/medicinal gum, applied analogously to myrrh.

results.title_inferred: On Liquidambar
results.original_text:
CAPITULO LXXXV
Del liquidámbar

El árbol de que se saca el liquidámbar es grande, con las hojas semejantes a las del lárice, divididas en tres puntas y en dos senos, algo aserradas, por la una parte blancas y por la otra un poco oscuras. La corteza del tronco es parte roja y parte verde; es de naturaleza caliente y de agradable olor. Sajado este árbol, destila un licor de color de oro, que llaman liquidámbar; el cual, en la suavidad del olor, es muy semejante al estoraque. Aplicado el liquidámbar al estómago, lo conforta, y corrobora el corazón; su sahumerio quita el dolor de cabeza que procede de causa fría, y hace otros saludables efectos. Sácase mucha cantidad de liquidámbar en la diócesis de Guatemala, de donde se trae a este reino del Perú.
results.translation:
CHAPTER LXXXV
On Liquidambar

The tree from which liquidambar is extracted is large, with leaves resembling those of the larch [lárice], divided into three points and two sinuses [senos], somewhat serrated, whitish on one side and slightly dark on the other. The bark of the trunk is partly red and partly green; it is warm in nature [de naturaleza caliente] and of a pleasant odor. When the tree is scored [sajado], it exudes a golden-colored liquid called liquidambar, which, in the sweetness of its scent, closely resembles storax [estoraque]. Applied to the stomach, liquidambar comforts it and strengthens the heart; its smoke [sahumerio], used as a fumigant, relieves headache arising from a cold cause [causa fría], and produces other healthful effects. A great quantity of liquidambar is extracted in the diocese of Guatemala, from where it is brought to this kingdom of Peru.

entities_mentioned.name: liquidámbar
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Liquidambar styraciflua (sweetgum) or Liquidambar macrophylla; the resinous balsam tapped from these trees
entities_mentioned.notes: The description of three-pointed, serrated leaves and a golden resin strongly matches Liquidambar styraciflua or a closely related Mesoamerican species. The resin (storax or liquidambar balsam) was a major colonial export from Guatemala.

entities_mentioned.name: estoraque
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Styrax spp. / storax resin
entities_mentioned.notes: Used here as a fragrance comparison; storax was a well-known aromatic resin in European and colonial pharmacopeias.

entities_mentioned.name: lárice
