entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Larix spp. (larch)
entities_mentioned.notes: Used as a morphological reference point for leaf shape, familiar to a European readership.

results.translator_notes: Multiple OCR errors silently corrected: 'liquidám-hsr' → 'liquidámbar'; 'rdm' → 'naturaleza'; '1^0' → 'Sajado'; 'fial' → 'cual'; 'fjsita' → 'quita'; 'Sácase' reconstructed from 'Sácase'; 'Mmhar' → 'dámbar'. The Galenic humoral framing ('warm in nature,' 'cold cause') is preserved in brackets. The preparation methods described are: (1) topical application to the stomach, (2) fumigation/inhalation of smoke (sahumerio). Ailments treated: stomach weakness, cardiac debility, headache of cold humoral origin.

results.title_inferred: On the Caraña Tree and Its Resin
results.original_text:
CAPITULO LXXXVI
De la caraña

El árbol que destila la caraña es grande, tiene el tronco liso, rojo, resplandeciente y oloroso; las hojas, como las del olivo y puestas en forma de [cruz]. Es árbol odorífero, agudo, mordiente al gusto y algo astringente. Nace en las provincias de la Mixteca y México en la Nueva España. La goma que mana de este árbol se llama caraña, la cual tiene las mismas facultades que la tecomahaca, pero con mayor eficacia. Bebido el polvo de su tronco o corteza, conforta el estómago y el corazón y corrige cualquier destemplanza fría.
results.translation:
CHAPTER LXXXVI
On the Caraña

The tree that yields caraña is large, with a smooth, reddish, lustrous, and fragrant trunk; its leaves resemble those of the olive and are arranged in the form of a [cross]. It is a fragrant tree, sharp and biting to the taste, and somewhat astringent. It grows in the provinces of the Mixteca and Mexico in New Spain. The gum [resin] that flows from this tree is called caraña; it possesses the same properties as tecomahaca [tacamahaca], but with greater efficacy. When the powder of its trunk or bark is drunk, it strengthens the stomach and the heart, and corrects any cold distemperature [imbalance of cold humoral quality].

entities_mentioned.name: caraña
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Bursera copallifera or Protium copal (family Burseraceae); some sources associate caraña resin with Trattinnickia spp. or related Burseraceae
entities_mentioned.notes: Caraña is a resinous gum from a Burseraceae tree native to Mesoamerica. The description of a smooth, reddish, shining, fragrant trunk with olive-like leaves is consistent with several Bursera or Protium species. The resin was used medicinally and as incense.

entities_mentioned.name: tecomahaca (tecomohaca in source)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Populus balsamifera or Bursera fagaroides; 'tacamahaca' is a colonial term applied to various balsamic resins, often from Bursera spp.
entities_mentioned.notes: Cobo compares caraña favorably to tecomahaca (tacamahaca), a well-known colonial drug resin. The OCR renders this as 'tecomohaca'; corrected to 'tecomahaca' (also spelled tacamahaca).

medical_extraction.condition_treated: Cold distemperature [humoral cold imbalance]; weakness of the stomach and heart
medical_extraction.preparation_method: Powder of the trunk or bark, taken as a drink (dissolved or mixed in liquid)
medical_extraction.properties_noted: Sharp, biting, slightly astringent; aromatic; stomachic and cardiotonic; shares properties with tecomahaca but is considered more efficacious

results.translator_notes: Several OCR artifacts corrected: 'ande' → 'grande'; 'l^andedente' → 'resplandeciente'; 'lai' → 'las'; 'destemplan¬za fría' reconstructed as 'destemplanza fría'. A word or phrase appears lost between 'en forma de' and 'Es árbol' — likely 'cruz' (cross), describing the leaf arrangement, supplied in brackets based on botanical convention in Cobo's other descriptions. 'Misteca y Me-' reconstructed as 'Mixteca y México'. The term 'destemplanza fría' is a Galenic humoral concept rendered as 'cold distemperature.' 'Tecomohaca' in the OCR is corrected to 'tecomahaca' (tacamahaca).

results.title_inferred: Dragon's Blood and Guaiacum (Chapters LXXXVII–LXXXVIII)
results.original_text:
CAPITULO LXXXVII
De la sangre de drago

El árbol que mana la sangre de drago se dice en la lengua mexicana ezpuahuilt, que es tanto como decir árbol que mana sangre, por ser de ese color el licor que produce. Es árbol grande; tiene las hojas anchas y esquinadas. Nace en muchas partes de las Indias, y su licor es muy conocido; el cual fortifica los dientes y hace los mismos efectos que la sangre de drago de que se usa en España. Aprovéchanse della los pintores, y la mejor que se saca en las Indias, es la de la provincia de Cartagena.

CAPITULO LXXXVIII
Del guayacán

El guayacan, que también nombran palosanto, es un árbol mediano; tiene el tronco espinoso y la parte más gruesa roja, siendo la demás corteza cenicienta; las hojas poco mayores que la de la ruda; las flores, azules; el palo es amarillo y el corazón azul oscuro. Es muy grande la virtud deste palo para curar el mal de buhas, y bien conocida y en todas partes.

Otra especie de guayacán hay semejante en todo a éste, salvo que el palo deste segundo es todo de un color blanco que tira a pardo, y es de mayor virtud que el primero; y la madera de ambos tan recia que hacen della los carpinteros los mazos y cabos de sus herramientas, como los demás instrumentos para que se requiere madera muy fuerte; y se ha tomado ya por proverbio para encarecer la fortaleza de alguno, el decir que es duro como un guayacán.
results.translation:
CHAPTER LXXXVII
Of Dragon's Blood

The tree that yields dragon's blood is called in the Mexican tongue ezpuahuilt, which is to say 'tree that flows blood,' because the liquor it produces is of that color. It is a large tree with broad, angular leaves. It grows in many parts of the Indies and its liquor is very well known; this liquor strengthens the teeth and produces the same effects as the dragon's blood [sangre de drago] used in Spain. Painters also make use of it. The finest quality obtained in the Indies comes from the province of Cartagena.

CHAPTER LXXXVIII
Of Guaiacum

The guaiacum [guayacán], which is also called holy wood [palo santo], is a medium-sized tree. Its trunk is thorny, and the thicker inner portion is red, while the outer bark is ash-gray. Its leaves are somewhat larger than those of rue; its flowers are blue; the wood is yellow, and the heartwood is dark blue. The virtue of this wood for curing the French disease [mal de buhas, i.e., syphilis] is very great and widely known everywhere.

There is another species of guaiacum similar to the first in every respect, except that the wood of this second kind is uniformly white tending toward brown, and it is of greater medicinal virtue than the first. The timber of both species is so extremely hard that carpenters use it to make mallets, tool handles, and other implements requiring very strong wood; and it has already become a proverbial expression to describe someone's toughness by saying he is as hard as a guaiacum.

entities_mentioned.name: sangre de drago / ezpuahuilt
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Croton draco or related Croton spp. (Mexican dragon's blood); possibly also Dracaena or Pterocarpus spp. in broader colonial usage
entities_mentioned.notes: Cobo specifies the Nahuatl name 'ezpuahuilt' (from 'eztli' = blood, 'cuahuitl' = tree). The resin was used medicinally to strengthen teeth and gums, and as a pigment by painters. He notes the best quality came from Cartagena. Multiple unrelated taxa were called 'sangre de drago' across the Americas.

entities_mentioned.name: guayacán / palosanto (first species)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Guaiacum officinale or Guaiacum sanctum (Zygophyllaceae)
entities_mentioned.notes: Classic description matches Guaiacum: ash-gray bark, red inner wood, yellow outer heartwood, dark-blue true heartwood, blue flowers. Used to treat 'mal de buhas' (syphilis). Extremely dense timber used for mallets and tool handles.

entities_mentioned.name: guayacán (second species, whitish-brown wood)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Possibly Guaiacum sanctum or a related Zygophyllaceae species; alternatively Bulnesia sarmientoi or another dense-wooded tropical species
entities_mentioned.notes: Cobo describes this second species as having uniformly white-to-brownish wood and claims it is of greater medicinal virtue than the first. Precise identification is uncertain without further botanical detail.

results.translator_notes: 1. 'Ezpuahuilt' is rendered as Cobo spells it; the Nahuatl etymology he offers ('tree that flows blood') is consistent with 'eztli' (blood) + 'cuahuitl' (tree), though the exact orthography varies in sources. 2. 'Mal de buhas' is the standard 16th–17th century Spanish term for syphilis (morbus gallicus / French disease); translated accordingly. 3. 'Palosanto' (holy wood) is a direct translation of the Latin 'lignum sanctum,' the name under which guaiacum wood was celebrated in European medicine as a treatment for syphilis. 4. The chapter number LXXXVIII appears as 'LXXXVm' in the OCR source; silently corrected. 5. 'Esquinadas' (angular/cornered leaves) retained in translation as 'angular.'

results.title_inferred: The Vilca Tree: Description, Medicinal Uses, and Timber
results.original_text:
CAPITULO LXXXIX

De la vilca

En el Perú llaman vilca a un árbol de la grandeza de un olivo, de hoja menuda, muy parecida a la del guarango, de verde más oscuro. Es árbol copado y de buen parecer. Produce unas vainas enjutas, como algarrobas, de una tercia de largo y dos dedos de ancho, y en ellas una pepita del tamaño y delgadeza de medio real, la cáscara lisa, de un color leonado oscuro y muy delgada. La sustancia que tienen dentro estas pepitas es amarilla y amarga como la acíbar. Estímanla mucho los indios, por ser medicinales. Con ellas se curan de algunas enfermedades, como de calenturas, cámaras de sangre y del mal del valle, tomando esta purga en su bebida ordinaria, que es la chicha. Tienen virtud laxativa con que evacúan la cólera por vómitos, y también la melancolía. El conocimiento de estas habilillas bebido con miel, limpia el pecho y el estómago y provoca la orina. Y según afirman los indios hace fecundas las mujeres. El árbol es muy estimado por su madera, que es recia, y a esa causa se labran de ella muchas cosas que requieren madera fuerte.
results.translation:
CHAPTER LXXXIX

On the Vilca

In Peru, the name vilca is given to a tree of the size of an olive tree, with small leaves very similar to those of the guarango [Caesalpinia spinosa], but of a darker green. It is a broad-crowned, handsome tree. It produces dry pods, like carob pods, one third of a vara in length and two fingers in width, and within them a seed [pepita] the size and thinness of half a real [a small silver coin], with a smooth husk of a dark tawny color and very thin. The substance inside these seeds is yellow and bitter like aloe [acíbar]. The Indians hold them in great esteem on account of their medicinal properties. With them they treat certain illnesses, such as fevers, bloody fluxes [dysentery], and the 'mal del valle' [a regional disease, possibly leishmaniasis or a malarial condition endemic to certain valleys], taking this purge dissolved in their ordinary drink, which is chicha. The seeds have a laxative virtue by which they evacuate bile [cólera] through vomiting, and also melancholy [black bile]. A decoction [conocimiento] of these small seeds [habilillas] drunk with honey cleanses the chest and stomach and promotes urination. And according to what the Indians affirm, it renders women fertile. The tree is greatly prized for its timber, which is hard, and for this reason many things requiring strong wood are fashioned from it.

entities_mentioned.name: vilca
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Anadenanthera colubrina (vilca/cebil) or possibly Anadenanthera peregrina; some sources also identify Peruvian 'vilca' with this genus
entities_mentioned.notes: The description of dry pod-like fruits with flat, coin-sized seeds and bitter yellow inner substance is consistent with Anadenanthera colubrina, a large leguminous tree known in the Andes. The seeds were historically used medicinally and as a hallucinogenic snuff, though Cobo emphasizes purgative and medicinal uses here.

entities_mentioned.name: guarango
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Caesalpinia spinosa (tara/guarango)
entities_mentioned.notes: Used by Cobo as a reference point for leaf shape comparison.

entities_mentioned.name: acíbar
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Aloe vera or related Aloe spp. (the bitter resinous juice)
entities_mentioned.notes: Standard period term for aloe latex/juice, used here as a comparator for bitterness and color of the vilca seed interior.

entities_mentioned.name: chicha
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Fermented maize beer (Zea mays)
entities_mentioned.notes: The vehicle in which the purgative preparation was administered.

entities_mentioned.name: mal del valle
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Possibly Carrión's disease (bartonellosis), cutaneous leishmaniasis (uta), or a malaria-like valley fever; identification uncertain
entities_mentioned.notes: A regionally specific ailment referenced frequently in colonial Peruvian sources. The exact disease identity is debated among historians of medicine.

results.translator_notes: 1. 'Vilca' in the source is rendered with OCR error 'vika' and 'tñica'; both silently corrected to 'vilca' as contextually unambiguous. 2. 'Acíbar' appears as 'alcihar' in the OCR; corrected. 3. 'Conocimiento' in the medical sense here likely means 'decoction' or 'preparation made known/recognized' — a somewhat unusual usage; it may alternatively be an OCR or scribal corruption of 'cocimiento' (decoction), which is highly plausible and would be the standard term. Flagged but not silently corrected in the original_text. 4. 'Habilillas' appears to be a diminutive colloquial form for the seeds/pods ('haba' = bean), rendered as 'small seeds.' 5. 'Cámaras de sangre' is a period term for bloody diarrhea/dysentery. 6. The laxative action 'evacuating bile through vomiting' reflects humoral medical theory standard to the period.

results.title_inferred: On the espingo plant and its medicinal pods
results.original_text:
CAPITULO XC

Del espingo

Los indios gentiles de las provincias de los Andes, en el Perú, suelen sacar a los pueblos de su frontera unas vainillas como algarrobas, de color leonado oscuro, cuya sustancia, cuajada, es como sangre de drago, aunque reluciente y tirante a negra, y de suave y profundo olor. Produce estas vainas un árbol que nace en aquella tierra, llamado espingo. Sacan los indios bárbaros estas vainas como cosa preciosa, para rescatar con ella cuchillos, tijeras y otras menudencias, que ellos precian mucho; lo cual alcanzan con facilidad de los españoles, por ser tenidas por muy medicinales estas vainas; porque, con sus polvos, tomados en ayunas, en agua o vino aguado, se curan las camaras de sangre, y tomados por el mismo orden o en alguno de los lamedores de rosas secas o de arrayán, son contra el flujo de sangre de vena rota en el pecho, y contra el que sale por la vía de la orina.
results.translation:
CHAPTER XC

On the Espingo

The pagan Indians of the Andean provinces of Peru are accustomed to bringing out to the frontier towns certain small pods resembling carob [algarroba], of a dark tawny color, whose coagulated substance is like dragon's blood [sangre de drago], though lustrous and tending toward black, with a gentle and deep fragrance. These pods are produced by a tree that grows in that land, called the espingo. These barbarous Indians bring out the pods as a precious commodity, trading them for knives, scissors, and other small items that they value greatly; this they easily obtain from the Spaniards, for these pods are held to be highly medicinal. Specifically, their powders, taken on an empty stomach in water or diluted wine, cure bloody dysentery [camaras de sangre]; and taken in the same manner, or mixed into one of the electuaries [lamedores] of dried roses or myrtle [arrayán], they are effective against the flow of blood from a ruptured vein in the chest, and against blood that passes by way of the urine.

entities_mentioned.name: espingo
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Talauma espintana or Nectandra sp. (various candidates discussed in literature; most frequently associated with Talauma ovata or related Magnoliaceae/Lauraceae species from Amazonian Peru)
entities_mentioned.notes: Espingo remains botanically contested. Some authorities link it to Talauma (Magnoliaceae); others to Lauraceae genera. The aromatic, resinous pods described are consistent with both families. The trade of the pods from Amazonian frontier peoples to highland Spanish settlements is well-documented in colonial sources.

entities_mentioned.name: sangre de drago
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Croton lechleri (or related Croton spp.); dragon's blood resin
entities_mentioned.notes: Used here as a visual comparator for the coagulated, dark reddish-black substance of the espingo pod, not as a separate medicinal ingredient.

entities_mentioned.name: arrayán
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Myrtus communis or a local Andean Myrtaceae species (e.g., Luma apiculata)
entities_mentioned.notes: Used as a base for a lickable electuary [lamedores] combined with espingo powder.

entities_mentioned.name: algarroba
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Prosopis pallida or Ceratonia siliqua (used as a morphological reference for pod shape)
entities_mentioned.notes: Cited only as a shape comparison for the espingo pods, not as a medicinal ingredient.

results.translator_notes: Several OCR corruptions were silently corrected: 'cama¬ ras' → 'camaras'; 'mbmfi' → 'mismo'; 'df' → 'de'; 'ri' → 'el'; 'vb' → 'vía'. The term 'lamedores' (literally 'lickables') refers to thick syrupy or honey-based electuaries administered by licking, a standard Galenic pharmaceutical form; rendered as 'electuaries' with the original term noted. 'Camaras de sangre' denotes bloody flux or dysentery. The phrase 'flujo de sangre de vena rota en el pecho' suggests hemoptysis (coughing blood from a ruptured vessel), consistent with the period's understanding of pulmonary hemorrhage.

results.title_inferred: The Guayroro Tree and Its Seeds
results.original_text:
CAPITULO XCI

Del guayroro

El guayroro es un árbol muy grande, que se halla en las provincias de los Andes, en el Perú. Echa unas vainas o canutos al modo de los de la cañafístola, en cuya cavidad se hallan unas pepitas más redondas, aunque llanas y lisas, de color de un fino coral; tiene cada una, una mancha negra que la hermosea. Estiman mucho estas pepitas los indios, afirmando valer contra el mal de corazón y melancolías, tomando de sus polvos en vino o agua de azahar poco más de media drama [sic]. Demás de esto, dicen que traída una sarta de ellas al cuello que caiga sobre el pecho, aprovechan contra las tristezas de corazón, y que confortan la vista y cerebro.
results.translation:
CHAPTER XCI

On the Guayroro

The guayroro is a very large tree, found in the provinces of the Andes, in Peru. It produces pods or tubes in the manner of those of the cassia fistula [cañafístola], in whose cavity are found seeds that are rather round, though flat and smooth, the color of fine coral; each one bears a black spot that adorns it. The Indians hold these seeds in high esteem, asserting that they are effective against heart ailments and melancholy, the powder being taken in wine or orange-blossom water in a dose of just over half a dram [sic]. Beyond this, they say that wearing a string of them around the neck so that it rests upon the chest is beneficial against sorrows of the heart, and that they strengthen the sight and the brain.

entities_mentioned.name: guayroro
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Possibly Ormosia coccinea or a related Ormosia species (family Fabaceae); the coral-red seeds with a black spot are highly characteristic of this genus
entities_mentioned.notes: Seeds of Ormosia species are famously coral-red with a black hilum spot, matching Cobo's description precisely. The plant also produces legume pods. Found in Andean/Amazonian regions of Peru, consistent with the stated range. Sometimes called 'huayruro' in modern Peru, where the seeds are still worn as amulets and attributed protective and medicinal properties.

entities_mentioned.name: cañafístola (cassia fistula)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Cassia fistula (Golden Shower Tree, family Fabaceae)
entities_mentioned.notes: Used here as a morphological reference point for the pod form of the guayroro, not as a subject of medical discussion in this passage.

entities_mentioned.name: agua de azahar (orange-blossom water)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Distilled water of Citrus aurantium blossoms
entities_mentioned.notes: Common pharmaceutical vehicle in early modern Spanish medicine, used here as an alternative to wine for administering powdered seed.

results.translator_notes: 1. 'Cañafístola' in the source appears as 'anfistola' — almost certainly an OCR corruption; silently corrected. 2. 'Tristezas' in the source appears as 'tritezas' — obvious OCR error; silently corrected. 3. The modern Peruvian/Quechua name for this plant is 'huayruro,' a direct cognate of Cobo's 'guayroro,' and it remains a popular amulet seed today, suggesting strong cultural continuity. 4. The '[sic]' appears in the original, apparently Cobo's own qualification of the dosage figure. 5. The dual therapeutic mode described — internal (powdered seeds in liquid) and external/talismanic (worn as a necklace) — is characteristic of early modern Andean medical syncretism. PLANT EXTRACTION SUMMARY — Name: guayroro (huayruro); Modern ID: Ormosia coccinea or related sp. (tentative); Ailment treated: mal de corazón (heart ailment), melancolía (melancholy), tristezas de corazón (heart sorrow), weakness of sight and brain; Preparation: (1) seeds ground to powder, administered in wine or orange-blossom water at a dose slightly over half a dram; (2) seeds strung on a cord and worn as a necklace resting on the chest (talismanic use).

results.title_inferred: The tree called caá (Paraguayan herb / yerba mate)
results.original_text:
CAPITULO XCII

Del árbol llamado caá

En la provincia del Paraguay se llama caá un árbol grande que echa la hoja parecida a la del zumaque. Hállase este árbol solamente en la tierra de los indios gentiles y de guerra, y ellos salen a vender la hoja seca a los españoles, los cuales, como no han visto el árbol sino la hoja, la llaman comúnmente hierba del Paraguay, siendo, como es, hoja de árbol. Toman los indios paraguayos esta hierba, y a su imitación los españoles de aquella provincia, y aun de otras bien distantes, pues la vi yo tomar en México; y tómanla de esta manera: echan un puño de ella en una grande olla de agua, y después que ha hervido, beben de esta agua tibia la mayor cantidad que pueden; y como la hoja es amarga y vomitiva, y ayúdala la mucha agua caliente que se bebe, lanzan al punto cuanto tienen en el estómago. Sirve esta hierba, tomada por este orden y cuando la necesidad lo requiere y no con el vicio que acostumbran algunos, para revelar los humores de los extremos, como de las piernas hinchadas o gotosas; limpia el estómago de las flemas, quita la jaqueca, y es contra la ijada, abre las vías y facilita el menstruo y la orina. Vi una vez a un religioso que estaba reventando de detención de orina, y en tomando esta hierba, orinó con gran facilidad, pero primero echó el agua clara como la había bebido y a lo último la orina, porque no bebió para provocar a vómito.
results.translation:
CHAPTER XCII

Of the tree called caá

In the province of Paraguay, a large tree that puts forth leaves resembling those of the sumac [zumaque] is called caá. This tree is found only in the lands of the pagan and warring Indians, who go out to sell its dried leaves to the Spaniards. The Spaniards, having seen only the leaf and not the tree itself, commonly call it 'herb of Paraguay' [hierba del Paraguay], though it is, properly speaking, a tree leaf. The Paraguayan Indians take this herb, and after their example so do the Spaniards of that province, and even of others quite distant — for I myself saw it taken in Mexico. They take it in the following manner: they throw a handful of it into a large pot of water, and after it has boiled, they drink as large a quantity of this warm water as they can; and since the leaf is bitter and emetic, aided by the great quantity of hot water drunk, they promptly cast up everything they have in their stomachs. This herb, taken in this fashion and when necessity requires it — and not out of the habitual indulgence to which some are accustomed — serves to draw down the humors from the extremities, such as swollen or gouty legs; it cleanses the stomach of phlegms [flemas], relieves headache [jaqueca], and acts against colic [ijada]; it opens the passages and promotes menstruation and urination. I once witnessed a friar who was near bursting from urinary retention, and upon taking this herb he urinated with great ease — though first he expelled the water as clear as he had drunk it, and only at the end passed urine, because he had not drunk enough to provoke vomiting.

entities_mentioned.name: caá / hierba del Paraguay
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Ilex paraguariensis (yerba mate)
entities_mentioned.notes: Cobo's description — large tree, sumac-like leaves, found in Paraguay, used as a boiled infusion with emetic and diuretic properties — matches yerba mate almost certainly. The preparation described here is an emetic/purgative use rather than the social sipping common today.

entities_mentioned.name: zumaque
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Rhus coriaria or related Rhus spp. (sumac)
entities_mentioned.notes: Used here only as a morphological comparator for the leaf shape of caá.

conditions_treated: Swollen or gouty legs (drawing down humors from the extremities)
plant_drug_extraction.preparation_method: A handful of dried leaves boiled in a large pot of water; the warm decoction is drunk in as large a quantity as possible, inducing vomiting. When taken in lesser quantity (insufficient to provoke vomiting), it acts as a diuretic.

results.translator_notes: Several OCR corruptions were silently corrected: 'bnja' → 'hoja', 'es|g' → 'este', 'h-dios' → 'indios', 'sa»' → 'salen', 'española,' → 'españoles', 'árW j' → 'árbol', 'comúnmeuls j' → 'comúnmente', 'como m, j' → 'como es', 'imitaciii j' → 'imitación', 'tómarda' → 'tómanla', 'waá j' → 'una', 'despriés' → 'después', 'j' line fragments are column-break artifacts. The footnote marker '(12)' at the end of the source has been omitted from the translation as it refers to an editorial apparatus not present in this segment. The term 'revelar los humores' is rendered 'draw down the humors' following standard humoral-medicine usage; 'revelar' here likely means 'reveler / to move/discharge' rather than modern Spanish 'to reveal'. 'Ijada' (flank/colic pain) is a period medical term preserved with gloss.

results.title_inferred: The Tarco Tree: Properties and Medicinal Uses
results.original_text:
CAPITULO XCIII
Del tarco

Tarco llaman en el Perú a un árbol mediano, cuyas flores son entre azules y moradas, y así ellas como el árbol son de temperatura caliente y seco en el segundo grado. Tienen las flores cierta parte laxativa con que purgan la flema y melancolía, según dicen los indios; hacen de ellas una conserva que obra el mismo efecto de purgar. Demás de esto, los polvos, así de las hojas como de la corteza del árbol, tienen facultad de secar toda llaga húmeda y desecan y consumen las almorranas, y su cocimiento, con un poco de linaza, las ablanda. Y el cocimiento de la corteza, hojas y flores, con un poco de alumbre, mundifica, deseca y encarna las úlceras.
results.translation:
CHAPTER XCIII
Of the Tarco

In Peru, the name tarco is given to a medium-sized tree whose flowers are between blue and purple; both the flowers and the tree itself are of a hot and dry temperament [complexion] in the second degree. The flowers possess a certain laxative quality by which they purge [phlegm] and melancholy [black bile], according to the Indians; a preserve [conserva] is made from them that produces the same purgative effect. Beyond this, the powders made from both the leaves and the bark of the tree have the property of drying any moist wound [llaga húmeda], and they dry up and consume hemorrhoids [almorranas]; a decoction of the same, prepared with a little linseed [linaza], softens them. And a decoction of the bark, leaves, and flowers, prepared with a little alum [alumbre], cleanses, dries, and promotes the granulation [encarna] of ulcers.

entities_mentioned.name: tarco
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Jacaranda mimosifolia or a closely related Jacaranda species
entities_mentioned.notes: The tarco is a well-known Andean common name applied to Jacaranda species, most commonly J. mimosifolia (blue jacaranda), which bears blue-violet flowers consistent with Cobo's description. Some sources also apply the name to J. cuspidifolia. Positive identification as a specific species requires further botanical corroboration.

entities_mentioned.name: linaza (linseed / flaxseed)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Linum usitatissimum
entities_mentioned.notes: Used here as an adjuvant in a decoction to soften hemorrhoids; consistent with its well-documented emollient properties.

entities_mentioned.name: alumbre (alum)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Potassium alum (KAl(SO₄)₂·12H₂O)
entities_mentioned.notes: Alum was a standard astringent and desiccant in colonial and early modern pharmacy, used here to enhance the cleansing and wound-healing decoction.

results.translator_notes: The source text as provided contains a large interpolated editorial footnote (marked by the OCR with symbols such as tl2, mmm, áfe, etc.) that does not belong to Cobo's original Chapter XCIII. This footnote appears to be an editorial annotation by a later editor discussing the history of yerba mate (caá/yerba del Paraguay) consumption in South America and Francisco Solano's legendary role in its introduction as a beverage. It is entirely extraneous to the tarco chapter and has been excluded from the cleaned original text and translation, as it is clearly not Cobo's prose. The relevant botanical chapter text has been reconstructed from the portions of the segment that are clearly Cobo's. The humoral terminology ('hot and dry in the second degree,' 'phlegm,' 'melancholy') reflects standard Galenic medical theory as applied in colonial Spanish natural history. 'Encarna' (literally 'incarnates') is a technical surgical/medical term meaning to stimulate the growth of granulation tissue in a wound, i.e., to promote healing and re-epithelialization of ulcers.

results.title_inferred: The Guandur tree and its medicinal use for kidney and bladder stones
results.original_text:
CAPITULO XCVI
Del guandur

Guandur llaman los indios peruanos a cierto árbol que produce una frutilla colorada del mismo nombre y del tamaño de la cochinilla de la Nueva España. Sirven estos granos a los indios para pintarse el rostro cuando van de camino o en sus fiestas. Tienen propiedad de quebrar la piedra de la vejiga y riñones, dando de sus polvos como media dracma con unos granos de salitre, todo deshecho en zumo de lima o limón y bebido caliente.
results.translation:
CHAPTER XCVI
On the Guandur

The Peruvian Indians give the name guandur to a certain tree that produces a small red fruit of the same name, the size of the cochineal insect of New Spain. These berries serve the Indians as a face paint when traveling or during their festivals. They have the property of breaking up stones in the bladder and kidneys, administering about half a drachm [dracma] of their powder together with a few grains of saltpeter [salitre], the whole dissolved in the juice of a lime or lemon and drunk warm.

entities_mentioned.name: guandur
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Possibly Monnina polystachya or a related Polygalaceae species; alternatively sometimes tentatively linked to species of Coriaria or Elaeocarpus in Andean flora. Identification remains uncertain.
entities_mentioned.notes: Described as a tree bearing small red fruits the size of cochineal insects, used as body paint and as a lithontriptic remedy. The small red berry matching cochineal size (~2–3 mm) limits candidates. No firmly established modern botanical identification found in standard references.

entities_mentioned.name: cochinilla de la Nueva España
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Dactylopius coccus (cochineal insect)
entities_mentioned.notes: Used here solely as a size reference for the guandur berry, not as a medicinal ingredient.

entities_mentioned.name: salitre
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Potassium nitrate (saltpeter, KNO3)
entities_mentioned.notes: Used as a component of the lithontriptic preparation; saltpeter was commonly employed in colonial-era medicine as a diuretic and solvent for urinary calculi.

entities_mentioned.name: zumo de lima o limón
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Juice of Citrus aurantiifolia (lime) or Citrus limon (lemon)
entities_mentioned.notes: Serves as the liquid vehicle for dissolving the powdered guandur and saltpeter; the acidic medium would have been considered pharmacologically active in humoral terms.

results.translator_notes: The source text reads 'media drama' which is a clear OCR/scribal error for 'media dracma' (half a drachm, approximately 1.77 g in apothecary measure); corrected silently in the cleaned original and flagged here. The preparation constitutes a classic colonial lithontriptic (stone-breaking) remedy: powdered plant material combined with a diuretic salt, dissolved in citric acid, and administered hot — a formula consistent with humoral and empirical practices of the period. The phrase 'cuando van de camino' ('when traveling') suggests the face-paint use was protective or apotropaic as well as cosmetic.

results.title_inferred: The María Tree and Its Medicinal Oil
results.original_text:
CAPITULO XCV
Del árbol llamado María

El árbol llamado María en estas Indias es muy alto, derecho, con la hoja grande y redonda; produce unos granillos inútiles como uvas; es admirable para árboles o mástiles de navios, porque su pie es muy derecho y cimbrea y no quiebra; su madera es roja y no tan buena para tablas, porque se raja fácilmente. Sajando este árbol, destila una resina tan medicinal como conocida en todas partes con nombre de aceite de María, con el cual se curan las heridas con admirables sucesos. Confésé yo una vez a un hombre a quien acababan de dar una puñalada en el pecho, y me dijo el cirujano que era herida mortal, porque salía por ella la respiración; curóla con este aceite, y a pocos días se levantó bueno y sano. Tráese de Tierra Firme a este reino del Perú mucha cantidad de este aceite, y no hay botica donde no se halle, ni aun casa ni chácara donde no lo tengan de respeto para los casos que suelen ofrecerse.
results.translation:
CHAPTER XCV
Of the tree called María

The tree called María in these Indies is very tall and straight, with large, round leaves; it produces small, useless berries resembling grapes. It is admirable for the yards or masts of ships, because its trunk is very straight, bends [cimbrea] without breaking; its wood is red and not so well suited for planks, as it splits easily. When this tree is scored [sajando], it exudes a resin as medicinal as it is universally known, under the name of oil of María [aceite de María], with which wounds are healed with remarkable results. I myself once heard the confession of a man who had just been stabbed in the chest, and the surgeon told me the wound was mortal, because breath was escaping through it; he treated it with this oil, and within a few days the man rose up well and sound. Large quantities of this oil are brought from Tierra Firme [the mainland, i.e., present-day northern South America] to this kingdom of Peru, and there is no apothecary's shop [botica] where it cannot be found, nor even a house or small farm [chácara] that does not keep some in reserve for the cases that commonly arise.

entities_mentioned.name: árbol María / aceite de María
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Calophyllum brasiliense or Calophyllum mariae (Calophyllaceae); the resin is commonly identified as 'aceite de María' or 'Santa María oil,' derived from trees of the genus Calophyllum
entities_mentioned.notes: The description of a tall, straight tree with large round leaves, red wood that splits easily, and a medicinally prized resin tapped by scoring the bark is consistent with Calophyllum brasiliense (syn. C. rekoi), widely distributed in tropical mainland South America and Central America ('Tierra Firme'). The oil was a well-known colonial-era vulnerary [wound-healing remedy]. Some authorities also associate 'aceite de María' with Mammea americana (Calophyllaceae), a related genus, but Calophyllum is the stronger candidate given the bark-tapping description.

entities_mentioned.name: Tierra Firme
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: The Spanish colonial term for the mainland of northern South America and parts of Central America (roughly modern Colombia, Venezuela, Panama)
entities_mentioned.notes: Used here as the source region from which the oil was commercially exported to Peru.

entities_mentioned.name: chácara
entities_mentioned.notes: Colonial Andean term (from Quechua 'chakra') for a small farm or rural holding; retained in brackets as period vocabulary.

results.translator_notes: The OCR produced 'granillos inútiles' (small useless berries), 'cimbrea' (flexes/bends, a nautical/carpentry term preserved in brackets), and 'Confésé' (clearly 'Confésé' = 'Confesé,' i.e., 'I heard the confession of' — Cobo writes as a Jesuit priest, not a physician). The page break mid-sentence ('salía por ella la [page] respiración') has been silently rejoined. The anecdote of the penetrating chest wound is a first-person testimonial of particular medical-historical interest, suggesting the oil was used as a topical sealant and vulnerary for pneumothorax-like injuries. The phrase 'de respeto' means 'in reserve' or 'kept as a standby,' a common colonial domestic usage.

results.title_inferred: The Tree of Immortality and its medicinal use for urinary retention
results.original_text:
CAPITULO XCVI
Del árbol de la inmortalidad

Este nombre dan en la nueva España a un árbol grande de que se hacen bordones y vasos al torno en que beber, por la virtud que comunican al agua, que es ésta: En hinchendo de agua un vaso de estos, en menos de una hora la tiñe de azul, la cual agua, bebida, aprovecha contra la retención de orina; por lo cual, los que padecen este mal, suelen beber en vasos de esta madera; y el mismo efecto de teñir el agua hace una raja de este árbol echada en ella. La madera de este árbol es muy buena para labrar, de un color morado y linda tez, y así es tenida y contada entre las más preciosas de esta tierra.
results.translation:
CHAPTER XCVI
On the Tree of Immortality

This name is given in New Spain to a large tree from which walking staffs [bordones] and turned drinking vessels are made, on account of the virtue they impart to water, which is as follows: upon filling one of these vessels with water, within less than an hour it dyes it blue; this water, when drunk, is beneficial against retention of urine. For this reason, those who suffer from this ailment customarily drink from vessels made of this wood. The same effect of dyeing the water is also produced by a split piece [raja] of this tree placed in the water. The wood of this tree is very fine for working [labrar], of a purple color and a beautiful grain, and is therefore held and counted among the most precious timbers of this land.

entities_mentioned.name: Árbol de la inmortalidad (Tree of Immortality)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Possibly Peltogyne spp. (purpleheart) or alternatively Eysenhardtia polystachya (kidneywood / palo dulce), the latter being strongly associated with New Spain, urinary ailments, and the famous blue fluorescence it imparts to water
entities_mentioned.notes: The distinctive blue coloration imparted to water is a well-documented property of Eysenhardtia polystachya (kidneywood), which produces a blue fluorescence due to the compound 'matlaline.' This species was historically used in New Spain for urinary complaints. The purple color of the wood and its fine workable quality are also consistent with this identification, though purpleheart (Peltogyne spp.) could account for the purple wood color. The blue-water fluorescence phenomenon is most convincingly linked to Eysenhardtia polystachya, making it the most probable candidate.

results.translator_notes: The OCR rendering 'apro- cha' is silently corrected to 'aprovecha.' The asterisk-like character after 'ésta' in the original appears to be a typographic flourish or OCR artifact for a colon or period and is rendered as a colon. The medical preparation described is essentially a vessel-infusion or chip-infusion (placing a split piece of the wood in water), with the colored water then drunk to treat urinary retention [dysuria / stranguria]. This passage is notable in the history of chemistry: the blue fluorescence of Eysenhardtia polystachya wood-water infusions is considered the earliest recorded observation of fluorescence, predating Sir George Gabriel Stokes's formal description by two centuries.

results.title_inferred: The Fever Tree (Cinchona) and a Dysentery Tree of Barbacoas
results.original_text:
CAPITULO XCVII
Del árbol de calenturas

En los términos de la ciudad de Loja, diócesis de Quito, nace cierta casta de árboles grandes, que tienen la corteza como de canela, un poco más gruesa, y muy amarga; la cual, molida en polvos, se da a los que tienen calenturas y con sólo este remedio se quitan. Háuse de tomar estos polvos en cantidad del peso de dos reales en vino o en cualquiera otro licor poco antes que dé el frío. Son ya tan conocidos y estimados estos polvos, no sólo en todas las Indias, sino en Europa, con instancia los envían a pedir a Roma.

(Nota 13: Principalmente en los montes de Uritofinga, Loxa o Viñocenca, luma o pico de loro, en quichua; Malacatos y Cajamarca. Las primeras especies que se descubrieron y usaron son la Cinchona macrocalyx y la C. pubescens.)

CAPITULO XCVIII
Del árbol que quita las cámaras

En la provincia de los Barbacoas, diócesis de Quito, que ha pocos años que la poblaron españoles, nace un género de árboles muy crecidos, cuya corteza es parda y casi tan gruesa como un dedo. Los polvos de esta corteza, bebidos en vino o en otro licor en cantidad de dos reales, quitan las cámaras. Ha poco tiempo que salió a luz esta planta y se conoció su virtud.
results.translation:
CHAPTER XCVII
Of the Fever Tree

In the territory of the city of Loja, diocese of Quito, there grows a certain kind of large tree whose bark resembles cinnamon bark, somewhat thicker, and very bitter. This bark, ground into powder, is given to those suffering from fevers, and by this remedy alone the fevers are cured. The powder should be taken in a quantity equivalent to the weight of two reales [a small silver coin unit, roughly 6–7 grams], in wine or in any other liquid, shortly before the onset of the chills. These powders are now so well known and esteemed not only throughout the Indies but in Europe as well, that they are urgently requested and sent for even in Rome.

(Footnote 13: Principally in the mountains of Uritofinga, Loxa, or Viñocenca — called luma or pico de loro [parrot's beak] in Quechua — and in Malacatos and Cajamarca. The first species discovered and used are Cinchona macrocalyx and C. pubescens.)

CHAPTER XCVIII
Of the Tree that Cures Dysentery [cámaras]

In the province of Barbacoas, diocese of Quito, which was settled by Spaniards only a few years ago, there grows a kind of very large tree whose bark is brown and nearly as thick as a finger. The powder of this bark, drunk in wine or another liquid in a quantity of two reales' weight, cures dysentery [cámaras]. It is only recently that this plant came to light and its virtue became known.

entities_mentioned.name: árbol de calenturas / polvos de corteza de Loja (Cinchona bark / Jesuit's Bark / Quinine bark)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Cinchona macrocalyx and Cinchona pubescens (as identified in footnote 13); genus Cinchona, family Rubiaceae
entities_mentioned.notes: This is the famous cinchona or quinine bark, source of quinine, used to treat malaria (here called 'calenturas' — fevers with chills). The footnote explicitly names C. macrocalyx and C. pubescens. The locality near Loja (Ecuador) is historically the site of the first documented medicinal use of cinchona. The dosage of 'two reales' weight (~6–7 g) in wine before the chill phase is consistent with other 17th-century accounts. The reference to demand from Rome alludes to the bark's promotion by Jesuit missionaries, hence its common European name 'Jesuit's bark.' Preparation: bark ground to powder, taken in wine or another liquid shortly before fever chill onset.

entities_mentioned.name: árbol que quita las cámaras (tree of Barbacoas, bark for dysentery)
entities_mentioned.notes: Described as a large tree with thick brown bark from the province of Barbacoas (modern Nariño, Colombia / Esmeraldas, Ecuador border region). Used to treat 'cámaras' (dysentery, bloody flux). Cobo notes it was newly discovered at time of writing. Insufficient detail to identify species with confidence; possibly a member of Rubiaceae or another tannin-rich bark-bearing tree. Preparation: bark ground to powder, taken in wine or other liquid at a dose of two reales' weight.

results.translator_notes:
1. The footnote (note 13) in the source appears corrupted by OCR, particularly 'LViíozenca' and 'nana o pico de loro'; I have interpreted these as 'Viñocenca' and 'luma o pico de loro' based on known historical toponymy and Quechua plant names in the Loja region. 'Uritofinga' may also be a corrupt rendering of a local toponym.
2. 'Cámaras' is a period Spanish medical term for dysentery or bloody diarrhea (from Latin camera, used in the sense of a purging of the bowels); rendered as 'dysentery' with the original in brackets.
3. The weight 'dos reales' refers to a monetary-weight unit (the silver real), approximately 3–3.5 grams each, totaling roughly 6–7 grams of bark powder per dose.
4. The reference to Rome sending for these powders reflects the early 1640s–1650s dissemination of cinchona bark via Jesuit networks, consistent with Cobo's 1653 writing date.
5. Chapter numbering: XCVII and XCVIII are rendered as 97 and 98.

results.title_inferred: The Sogue Tree (Peruvian Willow): Uses and Preparations
results.original_text:
CAPITULO XCIX
Del sogue

Al árbol que los indios del Perú llaman sogue, los españoles le dan nombre de sauce, porque se le parece. Sus hojas, corteza y flores son estípticas; el cocimiento de las hojas con vino aprovecha para llagas de pierna, y cocido con leche y con ella dado baño, quita los dolores y los nudos de la gota. Y el cocimiento de las dichas hojas, mezclado con zumo de granadas y aceite, quita el dolor de los oídos, echando en ellos unas gotas tibias. Y el cocimiento hecho con un poco de alumbre de hojas, corteza y flores conforta la dentadura y es contra los sabañones, dando baño con él. Los polvos de las hojas valen contra las quemaduras; y la simiente en polvo tomada con la flor de rosas secas o de arrayán, estanca la sangre que del pecho o estómago sale por vena rota.
results.translation:
CHAPTER XCIX
On the Sogue

The tree that the Indians of Peru call sogue is given the name sauce [willow] by the Spaniards, because it resembles that tree. Its leaves, bark, and flowers are astringent [estípticas]; a decoction of the leaves with wine is beneficial for leg sores, and when boiled with milk and used as a bath, it relieves pains and the nodules of gout. A decoction of the said leaves, mixed with pomegranate juice and oil, relieves earache when a few warm drops are instilled into the ears. A decoction made with a little alum [alumbre] of the leaves, bark, and flowers strengthens the teeth and gums, and is used against chilblains [sabañones] when applied as a bath. The powdered leaves are effective against burns; and the seed in powder form, taken with the flower of dried roses or of myrtle [arrayán], stanches blood coming from the chest or stomach through a ruptured vein.

[Footnote translated: Llano y Zapata (loc. cit.) calls quina Lacanna Perida (a name he encountered for the first time and whose etymology he does not know), and divides the medicinal history of this celebrated febrifuge into five epochs, each named after the particular denomination the drug bore during that period, namely: 1st, Powders of the Countess [Polvos de la Condesa], during the government of her husband the Count of Chinchón in Peru and after her return to Spain in 1639, where they made known the antidote to the chuchú [ague] and the endemic fevers of Peru; 2nd, Powders of the Jesuit Fathers [Polvos de los Padres Jesuitas], from 1649; 3rd, Powders of Cardinal de Lugo [Polvos del Cardenal de Lugo], for having propagated its use, especially in Rome; 4th, Cascarilla, Bark of Peru [Corteza del Perú], and Powders, from the time Talbot began to use it in France and England; 5th and last, a period of decline, due to the discredit which excessive use brought upon this drug. Various accounts, more or less reliable, circulate regarding the discovery and first trials of this medicine against fevers; what is certain and established is that the first person to use cascarilla in powder form to cut short fevers was Juan de Vega, physician to the aforementioned viceroy, on the occasion of the vicereine suffering from very stubborn fevers, in the year 1638.]

entities_mentioned.name: sogue / sauce
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Salix humboldtiana (Humboldt's willow, Peruvian willow)
entities_mentioned.notes: Called 'sauce' (willow) by Spaniards due to its resemblance to European willows. The astringent properties described are consistent with willow bark's known tannin and salicylate content.

entities_mentioned.name: arrayán
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Myrtus communis or a local Myrtaceae species (myrtle)
entities_mentioned.notes: Used in powdered form with dried rose flowers to stanch internal bleeding. 'Arrayán' in Peru may refer to a local myrtle-family species rather than the European Myrtus communis.

entities_mentioned.name: quina / cascarilla / Corteza del Perú
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Cinchona officinalis or related Cinchona spp. (cinchona bark, source of quinine)
entities_mentioned.notes: This identification appears only in the editorial footnote, which traces the naming history of cinchona bark from 'Polvos de la Condesa' to 'Cascarilla.' The footnote credits Juan de Vega as the first to use it in powdered form against fevers in 1638.

entities_mentioned.name: granadas
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Punica granatum (pomegranate)
entities_mentioned.notes: Juice used in the ear-drop preparation for earache.

results.translator_notes: The segment as it appears in the OCR source is interrupted by a lengthy editorial footnote (footnote 14) concerning the history of cinchona/quina, which belongs to a different chapter context (likely cap. XCVIII on quina). This footnote has been translated separately and bracketed. The main chapter text on sogue has been reconstructed by silently joining the text that appears before and after the footnote interruption, as the intended continuity is unambiguous. The word 'roquila' in the OCR ('aceite ro- / quila') was corrected to 'y aceite' with 'quita' beginning the next clause; 'gimiente' was corrected to 'simiente' (seed); 'éüotadiira' was corrected to 'dentadura' (teeth/gums); 'laüfdor' was corrected to 'la flor' (the flower). The medical properties ascribed—astringency, anti-inflammatory, haemostatic—are consistent with willow-family phytochemistry.

results.title_inferred: On the Siaya: Yellow Aromatic Powders from the Andes
results.original_text:
CAPITULO C

De la siaya

Llaman siaya en el Perú a ciertos polvos amarillos que los indios gentiles de las provincias de los Andes sacan en unos canutos a vender a los españoles; son de un árbol no conocido de nosotros, a quien damos nombre de los polvos; los cuales son calientes en el segundo grado, de suave y agradable olor y muy medicinales. Solían los reyes Incas aprovecharse de ellos para guardar y conservar sus vestidos ricos sin polilla ni otra cosa que los dañase y para que tomasen buen olor. Ultra de esto, dicen de ellos los indios, que trayéndolos sobre el corazón, quitan toda flaqueza dél; y su cocimiento, tomado en la boca, da buen olor y deshace el malo.
results.translation:
CHAPTER C

On the Siaya

In Peru, the name siaya is given to certain yellow powders that the pagan [gentile] Indians of the provinces of the Andes bring out in small tubes [canutos] to sell to the Spaniards. They come from a tree unknown to us, to which we give the name of 'the powders.' These powders are hot in the second degree [of the Galenic system], of a mild and pleasant scent, and very medicinal. The Inca kings were accustomed to make use of them to preserve and keep their fine garments free from moths and anything else that might damage them, and to impart a pleasant fragrance to them. Beyond this, the Indians say of them that, when worn over the heart, they remove all weakness thereof; and a decoction [cocimiento] of them, held in the mouth, gives good breath and dispels bad breath.

entities_mentioned.name: siaya
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Possibly a resinous or aromatic bark powder from an Andean tree, tentatively associated with species of Myrosylon, Ocotea, or related aromatic Lauraceae or Leguminosae; some scholars have suggested a connection with 'palo de bálsamo' or similar taxa, but no firm identification is established
entities_mentioned.notes: Described as yellow powder sold in small cane tubes (canutos) by Andean indigenous traders. Cobo himself acknowledges the source tree is unidentified. The descriptor 'caliente en el segundo grado' reflects Galenic humoral classification. Used as a moth repellent, cardiac tonic (worn on the body), and oral deodorant (decoction as mouthwash).

results.translator_notes: Several words in the source text were garbled by OCR (e.g., 'Uaraan' for 'Llaman', 'pol-m' for 'polvos', 'ele / potros' for 'de nosotros', 'pdvos' for 'polvos', '^^do' for 'segundo', '¿r' for 'olor', 'jp^dar' for 'guardar', 'sb' for 'sin', 'j' for 'y', 'tiayéndolos' for 'trayéndolos', 'tera' for 'deshace', 'reyes' reconstructed from 'm reyes'). All corrections are unambiguous in context. The 'canutos' (small tubes or hollow cane containers) is a period-specific term preserved for archival accuracy. Galenic thermal grading ('caliente en el segundo grado') is a standard 17th-century medical qualifier indicating moderate heat in humoral medicine.

results.title_inferred: On the Siga Tree and Its Resin
results.original_text:
CAPITULO CI
De la siga

La siga es cierto árbol que nace en el reino del Perú, del grandor de un [naranjo], que echa unas flores amarillas. Herido este árbol, destila una resina que los españoles llaman de Santa Cruz, por traerse de la provincia de Santa Cruz de la Sierra. Tiene esta resina el olor del árbol y es caliente en el grado tercero con vigor y fuerza de tal, y algo olorosa. Aprovecha, aplicada en forma de emplasto, contra todo dolor de causa fría, en cualquiera parte que esté, y contra el de ijada; y aplicada en la misma forma con polvos de hierba buena, de almáciga, de aromático rosado sobre el estómago flaco por vómitos, relajación o fría intemperie, quita el dolor, lo conforta y da fuerza para que dijiera. Demás de esto, aprovecha también contra los ahitos e indigestiones; y mezcladas tres partes della con una de sebo de capado y otra de cera que llaman de Nicaragua o amarilla, es contra los dolores de ciática y resuelve con toda energía cualquier tumor; y aplicada con unto sin sal y azafrán, ablanda los tumores o postemas.
results.translation:
CHAPTER CI
On the Siga

The siga is a certain tree that grows in the kingdom of Peru, of the size of an [orange tree], and bears yellow flowers. When this tree is wounded, it exudes a resin that the Spaniards call [resin] of Santa Cruz, because it is brought from the province of Santa Cruz de la Sierra. This resin carries the scent of the tree; it is hot in the third degree [of the classical humoral scale], with vigour and force befitting that grade, and is somewhat fragrant. Applied in the form of a plaster, it is beneficial against all pain arising from a cold cause, in whatever part of the body it may be located, and against pain of the flank [colic/kidney pain]. Applied in the same manner together with powders of spearmint, mastic, and aromatic rose [compound] upon a stomach weakened by vomiting, relaxation [of the stomach], or cold intemperate disposition, it removes the pain, comforts the organ, and gives it strength to digest. Beyond this, it is also useful against surfeit and indigestion. Mixed in a proportion of three parts of this resin to one part of the tallow of a castrated animal and one part of the wax they call Nicaragua wax or yellow wax, it is [effective] against the pains of sciatica and resolves with great energy any tumour. Applied with unsalted lard and saffron, it softens tumours or abscesses [postemas].

entities_mentioned.name: siga
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Possibly Elaphrium or Bursera sp., or a related resin-producing Burseraceae tree native to the Andean/Santa Cruz region; some scholars link it to Myroxylon or related genera
entities_mentioned.notes: The resin is described as hot in the third degree and aromatic, consistent with various balsamic or resinous Burseraceae or Fabaceae trees of the region. Precise identification remains uncertain.

entities_mentioned.name: resina de Santa Cruz
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Resin of the siga tree, named for its trade origin in Santa Cruz de la Sierra (present-day Bolivia)
entities_mentioned.notes: A commercial resin traded under this regional name; not definitively identified with a single modern botanical species.

entities_mentioned.name: hierba buena
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Mentha spicata (spearmint) or Mentha sp.
entities_mentioned.notes: Standard colonial Spanish term for spearmint or a related mint.

entities_mentioned.name: almáciga
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Pistacia lentiscus (mastic resin)
entities_mentioned.notes: Classic Old World medicinal resin commonly referenced in colonial Materia Medica texts.

entities_mentioned.name: aromático rosado
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Aromatic rose compound (Rosa sp.-based powder/electuary)
entities_mentioned.notes: A standard galenic preparation involving rose petals and aromatics; precise formulation varied by apothecary tradition.

entities_mentioned.name: sebo de capado
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Tallow rendered from a castrated animal (likely sheep or pig)
entities_mentioned.notes: Capado refers to a castrated animal; the tallow was preferred in pharmacy for its supposed purity and consistency.

entities_mentioned.name: cera de Nicaragua / cera amarilla
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Yellow wax, possibly a native beeswax or plant-derived wax from Central America
entities_mentioned.notes: Referred to as 'Nicaragua wax' or 'yellow wax'; likely a beeswax or vegetable wax traded from Nicaragua, used as an excipient in plasters.

entities_mentioned.name: azafrán
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Crocus sativus (saffron)
entities_mentioned.notes: Used here as an ingredient in a topical preparation to soften tumours and abscesses.

results.translator_notes: Several lines in the source text are affected by OCR errors, including caret symbols and broken words (e.g., '^ leino', '%indo', 'di jifera', 'pdia tercero'). These have been silently corrected to the most contextually plausible readings: 'el reino', 'Herido', 'dijiera' (digiera), and 'grado tercero' respectively. The word following 'grandor de un' is missing or illegible in the source; 'naranjo' (orange tree) has been supplied in brackets as a plausible reading consistent with Cobo's common size comparisons elsewhere, but this is speculative. 'Dolor de ijada' refers to flank or kidney/colic pain in period usage. 'Postemas' are abscesses or suppurating tumours. The therapeutic rationale throughout reflects classical Galenic humoral medicine (hot/cold qualities, degrees of temperament).

results.title_inferred: The Tipa Tree: Description, Properties, and Medicinal Uses
results.original_text:
CAPITULO CII

De la tipa

Los indios naturales de la provincia de los Charcas, en el Perú, llaman tipa a un árbol muy grande y hermoso, copado, de hojas verdes todo el año; algunos son tan crecidos como nogales. La hoja es del tamaño y hechura que la del lentisco, algún tanto mayor y blanquecina. Echa unas vainillas del largor de un dedo, delgadas y enjutas, salvo que junto al pezón son gruesecillas como si tuvieran dentro un garbanzo. Cada una de estas vainillas parece en la figura y talle a una ala de mariposa. El tronco es colorado y pesado, y así él como su corteza, ramas y hojas son muy estípticos y de complexión caliente; y principalmente la resina que destila el árbol, sajando su tronco y ramas, a la cual llaman sangre de tipa o sangre de drago del Perú, y es muy estimada por sus buenos efectos. Limpiando de ordinario los dientes con la corteza de este árbol, los conforta y encarna, y lo mismo hace si se enjuagan a menudo con el cocimiento de la corteza y resina; y si con este cocimiento echan ayudas, estanca todo género de cámaras, y lavando con él las almorranas estanca el flujo de sangre que suele haber en ellas. El cocimiento de sus hojas y corteza, bebido de ordinario, estanca el flujo de sangre así del pecho como del estómago y el que sale por la vía de la orina; y añadiendo al cocimiento bastante sal, y lavando con él las piernas hinchadas, las seca y deshincha, y conforta las de los gotosos; mayormente si para este efecto se añaden al dicho cocimiento hojas de molle, ramas de pinco-pinco y un poco de vino. Allende de lo dicho, el cocimiento de la resina con agua de cebada o de llantén con unas gotas de vinagre, un poco de alumbre y azúcar, lo que basta a endulzar el cocimiento, es maravilloso gargarismo para inflamaciones de la garganta; y principalmente cuando la campanilla está relajada o alargada. Demás de esto, habiendo llagas en la garganta, aprovecha el referido gargarismo con adición de miel de abejas, con que las mundifica y limpia. Finalmente, las hojas de este árbol, majadas y puestas sobre las heridas frescas, las unen con presteza.
results.translation:
CHAPTER CII

On the Tipa

The native Indians of the province of Charcas, in Peru, call tipa a very large and beautiful tree, broad-crowned, with green leaves throughout the year; some specimens grow as large as walnut trees. The leaf is similar in size and shape to that of the mastic tree [lentisco], somewhat larger and whitish. It produces small pods about a finger's length, slender and dry, except that near the stem they are somewhat thicker, as if they contained a chickpea inside. Each of these little pods resembles in shape and form the wing of a butterfly. The trunk is reddish and heavy, and both the trunk and its bark, branches, and leaves are highly astringent [estípticos] and of a hot complexion [complexión caliente]; and chiefly the resin that the tree exudes when its trunk and branches are scored, which they call sangre de tipa [blood of tipa] or sangre de drago [dragon's blood] of Peru, and it is highly prized for its beneficial effects. Cleaning the teeth regularly with the bark of this tree strengthens and restores them [conforta y encarna], and the same effect is achieved by rinsing frequently with a decoction of the bark and resin; and if enemas [ayudas] are administered with this decoction, it arrests all manner of fluxes of the bowels [cámaras], and washing hemorrhoids [almorranas] with it stops the flow of blood that typically occurs in them. A decoction of its leaves and bark, taken regularly as a drink, arrests bleeding from the chest as well as from the stomach, and that which passes by way of the urine; and by adding sufficient salt to the decoction and washing swollen legs with it, it dries and reduces the swelling, and brings relief to those suffering from gout; particularly if for this purpose leaves of molle, branches of pinco-pinco, and a little wine are added to the said decoction. Beyond what has been said, a decoction of the resin made with barley water or plantain water, with a few drops of vinegar, a little alum, and enough sugar to sweeten the decoction, makes a marvelous gargle [gargarismo] for inflammations of the throat; and especially when the uvula [campanilla] is relaxed or elongated. Furthermore, when there are sores in the throat, the aforementioned gargle with the addition of honey from bees is beneficial, as it cleanses and purifies them. Finally, the leaves of this tree, pounded and applied to fresh wounds, close them quickly.

entities_mentioned.name: tipa
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Tipuana tipu (Benth.) Kuntze (family Fabaceae)
entities_mentioned.notes: Commonly known as the tipa tree or rosewood of the Pampas; native to northwestern Argentina and Bolivia (the former Charcas region). The pod description—slender, dry, winged like a butterfly—matches the samaroid legume fruit of T. tipu. Still used in folk medicine in the region.

entities_mentioned.name: sangre de tipa / sangre de drago del Perú
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Resin of Tipuana tipu; distinct from the better-known sangre de drago (Croton lechleri)
entities_mentioned.notes: Cobo equates this resin with 'sangre de drago del Perú,' a name more commonly applied to Croton lechleri resin, but here clearly refers to the resin of the tipa tree. The two were apparently conflated or used interchangeably in colonial practice.

entities_mentioned.name: molle
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Schinus molle L. (Peruvian pepper tree, family Anacardiaceae)
entities_mentioned.notes: Frequently mentioned by Cobo and other colonial authors as a medicinal plant of the Andes.

entities_mentioned.name: pinco-pinco (pincú-pinco)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Ephedra americana Humb. & Bonpl. ex Willd. or a related Ephedra sp.
entities_mentioned.notes: Andean shrub used medicinally; the name pinco-pinco or pingo-pingo is commonly associated with Ephedra species in Peruvian and Bolivian folk medicine.

entities_mentioned.name: lentisco
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Pistacia lentiscus L. (mastic tree)
entities_mentioned.notes: Used here as a leaf-shape comparison; a plant well known to Spanish readers.

entities_mentioned.name: llantén
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Plantago major L. or related Plantago sp. (plantain)
entities_mentioned.notes: Used as a base for the gargle decoction.

results.translator_notes: 1. 'Complexión caliente' reflects Galenic humoral theory, indicating the plant's hot temperament. 2. 'Ayudas' is a period term for enemas/clysters. 3. 'Cámaras' refers to diarrhea or dysentery (literally 'chambers,' i.e., of the bowels). 4. 'Conforta y encarna' for teeth/gums likely means 'strengthens and promotes tissue regeneration.' 5. 'Campanilla relajada o alargada' (relaxed or elongated uvula) is a classical clinical presentation treated with astringent gargles. 6. OCR errors corrected silently: 'Uaman' → 'llaman' (twice), 'blángiiecina' → 'blanquecina', 'ralle' → 'talle', 'pincú-pinco' standardized to 'pinco-pinco', 'endulzar' restored from apparent OCR ambiguity. 7. Preparation methods extracted: (a) rubbing teeth with bark; (b) rinsing/gargling with bark-and-resin decoction; (c) enemas with bark-and-resin decoction; (d) topical wash for hemorrhoids; (e) drinking decoction of leaves and bark for internal bleeding and urinary bleeding; (f) decoction with salt for washing swollen/gouty legs, with added molle leaves, pinco-pinco branches, and wine; (g) gargle of resin decocted with barley or plantain water, vinegar, alum, and sugar for throat inflammation and elongated uvula; (h) same gargle with honey added for throat sores; (i) poultice of pounded leaves applied to fresh wounds.

results.title_inferred: The Incense Tree of Quito
results.original_text:
CAPITULO CIII
Del incienso de Quito

En la provincia de Quito, en el Perú, se halla un árbol que destila una resina parecida al incienso, por la cual la llaman los españoles árbol de incienso. Es pequeño, de hasta dos estados de alto; sus ramas son cortas, de suerte que no se copa, sino que viene a ser ahusado; y su hoja es muy parecida a la del olivo. Por las ramas destila una resina en granitos pequeños y redondos, que tienen olor de incienso, y el mismo árbol huele a lo mismo, de manera que cuando pasa alguna persona por junto a él, le parece, según el olor que percibe, que allí cerca se quema incienso.
results.translation:
CHAPTER CIII
On the Incense Tree of Quito

In the province of Quito, in Peru, there is found a tree that exudes a resin resembling frankincense [incienso], on account of which the Spaniards call it the incense tree [árbol de incienso]. It is small, reaching up to two estados [approximately 10–11 feet] in height; its branches are short, so that it does not spread into a broad crown but rather tapers to a point [comes to be spindle-shaped]. Its leaf closely resembles that of the olive. Along the branches it exudes a resin in small, round granules that smell of frankincense, and the tree itself smells the same, such that when anyone passes near it, the scent they perceive makes them think that frankincense is being burned somewhere close by.

entities_mentioned.name: árbol de incienso (incense tree of Quito)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Clusia cf. rosea or Bursera graveolens, though most likely a species of Protium (family Burseraceae) native to the Andean region of Ecuador
entities_mentioned.notes: The description—small tree, spindle shape, olive-like leaves, small round resinous granules with a frankincense-like scent—is broadly consistent with several resin-producing Burseraceae, particularly Protium species or possibly Bursera graveolens ('palo santo'), which is found in the wider Andean/Pacific region. Without more detail, a firm species-level identification is not possible. No medicinal use is described in this passage; the tree is noted solely for its aromatic resin.

results.translator_notes: The original OCR text contains several clear errors: 'l^s' (corrected to 'Es'), 'rainas soii' (corrected to 'ramas son'), and 'qiie' (corrected to 'que'). The unit 'estado' (pl. 'estados') is a traditional Spanish measure of height roughly equivalent to the height of a man, approximately 5–5.5 feet, so 'dos estados' is roughly 10–11 feet. The word 'ahusado' (spindle-shaped or tapered) has been preserved in the translation with a bracketed gloss. No medical or pharmacological information is present in this segment.

results.title_inferred: The Pepper of Tabasco
results.original_text:
CAPITULO CIV
De la pimienta de Tabasco

El árbol que da esta pimienta nace en la provincia de Tabasco, en la Nueva España; es grande, con las hojas como las del naranjo; la flor roja, a manera de la del granado, que huele como azahar, y también las hojas del árbol son olorosas. Echa la fruta en racimos, la cual es redonda; al principio está verde, luego se pone leonada, y últimamente inclina a color negro; es de buen olor y mordaz al gusto; puede suplir a falta de la pimienta: conforta el estómago y el corazón y hace otros buenos efectos.
results.translation:
CHAPTER CIV
On the Pepper of Tabasco

The tree that yields this pepper grows in the province of Tabasco, in New Spain. It is large, with leaves like those of the orange tree; its flower is red, resembling that of the pomegranate, and it smells like orange blossom; likewise, the leaves of the tree are fragrant. It bears its fruit in clusters; the fruit is round — at first it is green, then it turns tawny [leonado], and finally it tends toward black. It is of pleasant aroma and sharp [mordaz] to the taste. It can serve as a substitute when pepper is lacking. It fortifies the stomach and the heart, and produces other beneficial effects.

entities_mentioned.name: pimienta de Tabasco
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Pimenta dioica (allspice / Jamaica pepper)
entities_mentioned.notes: The description — large tree, orange-like leaves, red pomegranate-like flowers smelling of orange blossom, round berries borne in clusters that ripen from green through tawny to near-black, aromatic and pungent flavor, grown in the Tabasco region of New Spain — strongly matches Pimenta dioica (L.) Merr. (Myrtaceae), commonly known as allspice. The name 'pimienta de Tabasco' was one of several vernacular designations used by Spanish colonial writers for allspice. Medical uses recorded: stomachic and cardiac tonic ('conforta el estómago y el corazón'). No specific preparation method described; context implies use as a spice/condiment analogous to black pepper.

results.translator_notes: OCR errors silently corrected: 'Tabmco' → 'Tabasco' (chapter heading); 'priu. cipio' → 'principio' (line break artifact); 'guíto' → 'gusto'; 'j' at line end → 'y' (common OCR confusion with Spanish long-s / abbreviation artifacts). The term 'leonado' (tawny, lion-colored) is retained in brackets in the translation as it is a precise period color term. 'Mordaz' (biting, sharp, pungent) similarly preserved. The comparison of the flower's scent to 'azahar' (orange blossom) is a notable organoleptic detail consistent with allspice's eugenol-rich aroma profile.

results.title_inferred: On Copal: the tree, its resin, and its medicinal uses
results.original_text:
CAPITULO CV
Del copal

El árbol del copal es grande; sus hojas son parecidas a las de la encina, sólo que son más largas; su fruto es redondo y que tira a rojo; tiene el mismo sabor que la goma que mana del árbol, la cual unas veces destila espontáneamente y otras sajando el árbol. Este licor cuajado es blanco, transparente, bueno para sahumerios, con que se quita el dolor de cabeza, y es remedio para todas las enfermedades que nacen de causa fría; y lo mismo hacen cuantas diferencias hay de copal, que son muchas; porque los indios de la Nueva España dan este nombre a cualquiera árbol que echa de sí goma olorosa; y los españoles dan este nombre a todos los que manan la goma blanca, que son varias especies de ellos.
results.translation:
CHAPTER CV
On Copal

The copal tree is large; its leaves resemble those of the holm oak [encina], except that they are longer; its fruit is round and tends toward red; it has the same flavor as the gum that flows from the tree, which sometimes exudes spontaneously and at other times by making incisions [sajando] in the tree. This congealed resin [licor cuajado] is white and transparent, good for fumigations [sahumerios], by means of which headache is relieved, and it is a remedy for all ailments arising from a cold cause [causa fría]; and all the various kinds of copal, of which there are many, do the same. For the Indians of New Spain give this name to any tree that produces an aromatic gum; and the Spaniards give this name to all those that yield a white gum, which belong to various species.

entities_mentioned.name: copal
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Bursera spp. (most likely Bursera copallifera or related species; the term broadly covers multiple resin-bearing trees in Mesoamerica, including some Protium and Dacryodes spp.)
entities_mentioned.notes: Cobo explicitly notes that 'copal' was a generic Nahuatl term applied by indigenous peoples of New Spain to any aromatic gum-producing tree, and by Spaniards to those yielding white resin specifically. This makes precise species identification difficult. The description of leaves resembling holm oak (but longer) and reddish round fruit is consistent with Bursera spp. Medicinal use: treats headache and ailments of 'cold cause' via fumigation. Preparation: the resin is collected either by spontaneous exudation or by incising the bark, then burned as incense/fumigant.

entities_mentioned.name: encina
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Quercus ilex (holm oak)
entities_mentioned.notes: Used here only as a morphological comparator for leaf shape, not as a medicinal entity.

results.translator_notes: The source text contains several clear OCR artifacts: 'susEo.' for 'sus ho-' (hojas), 'mE-nxo' for 'mismo', 'ei^poii*táneamente' for 'espontáneamente', 'árM' for 'árbol', 'mb»' for 'mismo', 'cop4' for 'copal', 'gom' for 'goma', 'nos-bre' for 'nombre', and 'goraablts}-ca' for 'goma blanca'. All corrections are treated as unambiguous and silently applied in the cleaned original text. The humoral medical concept of 'causa fría' (cold cause) is preserved in brackets in the translation as it is a technical term of Galenic medicine as practiced in colonial contexts.

results.title_inferred: The Espigua Tree (Soapberry): Description and Medicinal Use
results.original_text:
CAPITULO CVII
De la espigua

Este es un árbol de la grandeza de un moral, alto y copado, de mucha hoja, la cual es semejante a la del naranjo; echa estas hojas como el nogal, en varillas que nacen de la rama con dos hileras de hojas pareadas a los lados. Su fruto son unas bolillas o granos a la manera de las que lleva el árbol del paraíso; son del tamaño de uvas gruesas, con una cascarilla verde por encima, que cuando se seca queda amarilla y arrugada. Debajo de esta cascarilla está un grano negro, redondo, del tamaño dicho, tan liso, resplandeciente y duro, que dallos se hacen rosarios. Llaman los españoles jaboncillo a estos granos, porque cuando están cubiertos de su cascarilla, lavando con ellos cualquiera ropa, el agua hace espuma como si se lavara con jabón; aunque no hacen el efecto de limpiar la ropa como el jabón. Estos granos de la espigua, verdes o maduros, bien majados y deshechos en agua caliente, son extremado remedio en ayudas para curar el mal del valle.
results.translation:
CHAPTER CVII
Of the Espigua

This is a tree of the size of a mulberry tree, tall and spreading, with abundant foliage, which resembles that of the orange tree; it puts forth its leaves as the walnut does, on small stems growing from the branch with two rows of paired leaves on either side. Its fruit consists of small balls or berries in the manner of those borne by the chinaberry tree [árbol del paraíso]; they are the size of large grapes, with a thin green rind on the outside, which when it dries turns yellow and wrinkled. Beneath this rind is a black, round seed, of the size mentioned, so smooth, lustrous, and hard that rosaries are made from them. The Spaniards call these seeds 'jaboncillo' [little soap], because when they are covered in their rind, washing any garment with them causes the water to foam as if washed with soap — though they do not have the actual cleansing effect of soap.
