These seeds of the espigua, whether green or ripe, well pounded and dissolved in hot water, are an excellent remedy administered as an enema [ayuda] for treating the 'mal del valle' [valley sickness].

entities_mentioned.name: espigua
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Sapindus saponaria (wingleaf soapberry / American soapberry)
entities_mentioned.notes: The description — pinnate leaves resembling walnut, berry-like fruits with a saponaceous rind, hard black seed used for rosaries, and the Spanish vernacular name 'jaboncillo' — strongly corresponds to Sapindus saponaria (family Sapindaceae), widely known as 'jaboncillo' throughout colonial Spanish America.

entities_mentioned.name: jaboncillo
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Sapindus saponaria
entities_mentioned.notes: Spanish vernacular name for the same tree/fruit, emphasizing its soap-like lathering property due to saponin content.

entities_mentioned.name: árbol del paraíso
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Melia azedarach (chinaberry tree)
entities_mentioned.notes: Used by Cobo as a morphological comparator for the fruit clusters; 'árbol del paraíso' in colonial Spanish sources typically refers to Melia azedarach.

entities_mentioned.name: mal del valle
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Possibly Chagas disease, leishmaniasis, or endemic fevers associated with low valley regions of the Andes; identification uncertain
entities_mentioned.notes: 'Mal del valle' (valley sickness) is a recurring term in colonial Peruvian medical sources and has been associated variously with Carrión's disease (bartonellosis), endemic fevers, or other parasitic illnesses prevalent in Andean valleys. No consensus modern identification. The preparation — seeds pounded and dissolved in hot water, administered as an enema (ayuda) — is the medicinal use described.

results.translator_notes: 'Ayudas' is a period medical term for clysters or enemas; translated as 'enema' with the original retained in brackets for technical clarity. The chapter heading 'espigua' may itself be a scribal or OCR variant; the tree is consistently identified by the Spanish vernacular 'jaboncillo' within the text. OCR rendered 'espigua' with a broken character in the chapter title ('es pi gua') and 'varillas' as 'va¬rillas' — both silently corrected as unambiguous. The phrase 'dallos se hacen rosarios' (rosaries are made from them) is a well-documented colonial use of Sapindus seeds.

results.title_inferred: Brazilwood Tree
results.original_text:
CAPITULO CVIII
Del palo del Brasil

El árbol llamado brasil es pequeño, algo menor que la encina; tiene torcido el tronco y poco derecho; la hoja, acarraseada y sin espinas; la cáscara, de recia, salta en el árbol. Es ya muy conocido el brasil en todas partes, por su gran utilidad para tintes y otros efectos, y se lleva a España gran cantidad de ello de muchas partes de estas Indias, particularmente de la Nueva España.
results.translation:
CHAPTER CVIII
On the Brazilwood Tree

The tree called brasil [brazilwood] is small, somewhat smaller than the holm oak; its trunk is twisted and not very straight; the leaf is rough [acarraseada] and without spines; the bark, being thick, peels away from the tree. Brazilwood is by now very well known everywhere, on account of its great usefulness for dyes and other purposes, and large quantities of it are shipped to Spain from many parts of these Indies, particularly from New Spain.

entities_mentioned.name: Brasil / Palo del Brasil
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Haematoxylum brasiletto or Caesalpinia echinata (Brazilian brazilwood); in the New Spain context likely Haematoxylum brasiletto or a related Caesalpinia species
entities_mentioned.notes: Cobo distinguishes this tree as small, with a twisted trunk, rough leaves, and thick peeling bark. The reference to New Spain as a principal source suggests a Mesoamerican species such as Haematoxylum brasiletto rather than the South American Caesalpinia echinata, though both were traded as 'brasil' and used as red/orange dye-wood. No medicinal use is described in this passage; the primary application noted is as a textile dye.

results.translator_notes: The word 'acarraseada' (cleaned to 'acarraseada' from OCR 'acarra sea da') is an archaic/dialectal Spanish adjective meaning rough or scabrous in texture, applied here to the leaf surface. The phrase 'la cáscara, de recia, salta en el árbol' is somewhat elliptical; the most natural reading is that the thick bark splits or peels off the living tree. No medicinal preparation is described; this passage is purely botanical and commercial in character.

results.title_inferred: Madre de Coco tree and China Root: descriptions and medicinal uses
results.original_text:
CAPITULO CIX
De la madre de coco

El árbol que en el Perú llaman madre de coco es pequeño, alto de dos estados, de hechura de ciprés y de agradable parecer, muy copado y poblado de hoja; la copa es abusada; comienza a menos de un estado del suelo, y como se va levantando, se va adelgazando a modo de ciprés. La hoja es muy parecida a la del sauce. Echa por fruto unos racimillos de unas hojitas muy delgadas, blancas que tiran a rojas, pegadas de tres en tres y redondas, que hacen figura triangular, cada una del tamaño de la uña del dedo pulgar, en la juntura de las cuales está la semilla, que son tres granitos en cada una, negros y lisos, del tamaño de semilla de rábano. Cuando estos racimillos de estas hojas están secos, meneándose las ramas del árbol con el viento, hacen ruido. Con el lavatorio de sus hojas se suelen curar frialdades.

CAPITULO CX
De la raíz de la China

El árbol que produce la raíz que llaman de la China, es de la grandeza de un naranjo, infructífero, con la hoja como de ciruelo, y sola su raíz es provechosa para curar algunas enfermedades; la cual es blanca y algo morada. Su agua es buena para opilaciones y mal de orina.
results.translation:
CHAPTER CIX
Of the Madre de Coco

The tree that in Peru is called madre de coco [mother of coco] is small, standing two estados [roughly 11–12 feet] tall, shaped like a cypress and of pleasant appearance, very bushy and densely leafed; the crown [copa] is wide at the base; it begins less than one estado from the ground, and as it rises it tapers in the manner of a cypress. The leaf closely resembles that of the willow. It bears as fruit small clusters of very thin little leaves, white tending toward red, joined three by three and rounded so as to form a triangular figure, each one the size of a thumbnail; at their juncture lies the seed, which consists of three small grains in each cluster, black and smooth, the size of a radish seed. When these little clusters of leaves are dry and the branches of the tree are moved by the wind, they make a rustling noise. A wash [lavatorio] made from its leaves is commonly used to treat chills [frialdades].

CHAPTER CX
Of the China Root

The tree that produces the root they call de la China [China root] is the size of an orange tree, bears no fruit, has a leaf like that of a plum tree, and only its root is of use in curing certain ailments; the root is white and somewhat purple. Its decoction [agua] is good for obstructions [opilaciones] and ailments of the urinary tract [mal de orina].

entities_mentioned.name: Madre de coco
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Possibly Schinus molle (Peruvian pepper tree) or a related Anacardiaceae/Euphorbiaceae species; the triangular, clustered fruit description may also suggest a Dodonaea or Muehlenbeckia species
entities_mentioned.notes: Treated condition: frialdades (chills, cold ailments). Preparation: lavatorio (a wash or infusion) of the leaves applied externally. The distinctive triangular, three-lobed dry seed clusters and cypress-like form make positive identification difficult without further botanical voucher data.

entities_mentioned.name: Raíz de la China (China root)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Smilax china or a closely related Smilax species (Smilacaceae); 'China root' was a well-known early modern materia medica item traded from Asia and also found to have New World analogues
entities_mentioned.notes: Treated conditions: opilaciones (obstructions, likely hepatic or systemic blockages) and mal de orina (urinary disorders). Preparation: agua (a decoction or infusion of the root). Cobo notes that only the root is medicinally useful; the plant is described as bearing no fruit. The whitish-purple coloring of the root is consistent with Smilax spp.

results.translator_notes: 1. 'Estado' is a Spanish unit of measure roughly equivalent to a man's height (~1.65–1.80 m); two estados is therefore approximately 11–12 feet. 2. 'Lavatorio' literally means 'washing' or 'rinse' and implies an aqueous preparation used to bathe or wash the affected area, though it could also indicate an oral rinse or fomentation. 3. 'Frialdades' (lit. 'coldnesses' or 'chills') is a humoral medical term denoting conditions attributed to cold and damp imbalance. 4. 'Opilaciones' refers to obstructions or blockages, a standard humoral diagnosis often associated with liver or spleen disorders. 5. 'Mal de orina' broadly covers painful or obstructed urination. 6. The OCR rendered 'parecida' as 'jmrecida' and 'meneándose' as 'meneáiirlose' and 'llaman' as 'Ik man' among other errors; all have been silently corrected as the intended words are unambiguous in context. 7. The phrase 'copa es abusada' is likely a corruption or unusual usage; it has been interpreted as 'copa abultada' or a wide-based, spreading crown, which fits the cypress-tapering description that follows.

results.title_inferred: The Higüero Tree (Calabash Tree)
results.original_text:
CAPÍTULO CXI
Del higüero

Higüero se llama en la lengua que tenían los indios de la isla Española un árbol mediano como un granado. Es de mucha hoja, la cual se parece a la del granado, salvo que es más ancha y luenga y lo ancho lo tiene en el remate, de donde va ensangostando hasta el pezón; es lisa, tiesa y retorcida o crespa. La madera de este árbol es recia y correosa, parecida a la del granado; de ella se hacen muchas cosas, particularmente fustes de sillas. Su fruto son unas calabazas pequeñas y de muy delgado casco, unas redondas y otras ahusadas. Las mayores son comúnmente como la cabeza de un hombre, y de allí para abajo hasta no ser mayores que un huevo de gallina. Hácense muchas cosas dellas, principalmente curiosos vasos para beber, que en la Nueva España llaman tecomates; tíñenlos de negro con un lustre muy resplandeciente y guarnécenlos curiosamente de plata, los cuales sólo sirven para beber chocolate. De los higüeros grandes suelen hacer los caminantes cascos para ponerse sobre la cabeza debajo del sombrero para defensa del sol. Nace esta planta en tierras calientes; y donde se hace granjería de su fruto es la costa de la mar del Sur de la Nueva España, particularmente en la diócesis de Guatemala, donde yo vi los más curiosos tecomates; y de allí los llevan a la ciudad de México. Pocos años ha que este árbol se plantó en esta ciudad de Lima, y se da muy bien en ella.

(16) Hibüeros, Higüeros, Higüerillas son formas más o menos corrompidas del mismo vocablo.
results.translation:
CHAPTER CXI
On the Higüero

"Higüero" is the name given in the language of the Indians of Hispaniola [isla Española] to a medium-sized tree resembling a pomegranate tree. It has abundant foliage, which resembles that of the pomegranate, except that the leaves are wider and longer, with their greatest width at the tip, from which they taper down to the stalk [pezón]; they are smooth, stiff, and twisted or curly. The wood of this tree is hard and tough, similar to that of the pomegranate; from it many things are made, particularly saddle-frames [fustes de sillas]. Its fruit consists of small gourds [calabazas] with very thin shells, some round and others elongated. The largest are commonly about the size of a man's head, and from there downward to no larger than a hen's egg. Many things are made from them, chiefly curious drinking vessels which in New Spain are called tecomates; these are dyed black with a very bright lustre and are curiously ornamented with silver, and are used exclusively for drinking chocolate. From the large higüeros, travelers customarily make helmets [cascos] to wear on the head beneath the hat as a defense against the sun. This plant grows in hot lands [tierras calientes]; and the place where trade is made of its fruit is the coast of the South Sea [Pacific coast] of New Spain, particularly in the diocese of Guatemala, where I saw the most curious tecomates; and from there they are taken to the city of Mexico. It is only a few years since this tree was planted in this city of Lima, and it grows very well here.

(16) Hibüeros, Higüeros, and Higüerillas are more or less corrupted forms of the same word.

entities_mentioned.name: Higüero
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Crescentia cujete (calabash tree, Family Bignoniaceae)
entities_mentioned.notes: Description of medium tree with large gourd-like fruits, native to tropical America, used for making drinking vessels (tecomates). The variant spellings Hibüero, Higüero, and Higüerilla are noted by Cobo himself in footnote 16. Not to be confused with the bottle gourd (Lagenaria siceraria); Crescentia cujete is the woody-fruited calabash tree of the Caribbean and Central America.

entities_mentioned.name: Tecomate
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Drinking vessel made from Crescentia cujete fruit
entities_mentioned.notes: From Nahuatl 'tecomate'; a cup or vessel hollowed from the calabash fruit, lacquered black and sometimes ornamented with silver, used specifically for drinking chocolate in New Spain.

results.translator_notes: The source text has significant OCR corruption throughout (e.g., 'qae' for 'que', 'Espafia' for 'España', 'dd' for 'del', 'mmm calabazas' for 'unas calabazas', 'delgack' for 'delgado', 'k cabeza' for 'la cabeza', 'hump*' for 'huevo', 'cosai' for 'cosas', 'vasM' for 'vasos', 'Espafe' for 'España', 'ne|sr@' for 'negro', 'te' for 'y', 'plantel' for 'planta', 'haff' for 'hace', 'p^-' for 'par-', 'Guate-mala', 'curio;^' for 'curiosos'). All have been silently corrected as the intended words are unambiguous. No medicinal or pharmaceutical use is described in this segment; the text focuses exclusively on the tree's material and economic uses (vessels, saddle-frames, sun protection). Cobo's first-person observation ('where I saw') in Guatemala is notable for provenance.

results.title_inferred: On the Mangrove and the Cedar of the Indies (Chapters 112–113)
results.original_text:
CAPITULO CXII
Del mangle

Fuera de muchos árboles de que he hecho mención en este libro, que, demás de ser provechosos por sus frutos y virtudes, tienen buenas maderas para labrar, se hallan otros innumerables en estas Indias que no llevan más fruto que preciosas maderas para todo género de obras y fábricas, y por ellas son de más utilidad y estimación que muchos de los frutales. Uno de ellos es el que en lengua de la isla Española se llama mangle; y póngolo el primero en este género no porque sea el más preciado, sino porque es el que más generalmente nace en todas las costas calientes de esta América.

Nace el mangle en los esteros y anegadizos que hace la mar en sus riberas, en las ciénegas y bocas de los ríos, y cimiéntase con agua salada, pues nace dentro de ella en una, dos y cuatro brazas de hondo; y hácense tan cerrados bosques de ellos, que no se puede caminar por las orillas de la mar que ellos ocupan, así por su espesura, como por ser el suelo pantanoso y anegadizo. No hace grueso tronco, y así, su madera son unas varas tan gruesas como el muslo y menos, largas, derechas, muy recias y tan pesadas, que se hunden en el agua; las cuales sirven, así como las cortan de la montaña, sin labrarlas, para vigas en edificios, para andamios en las fábricas y otros muchos usos; y es madera tan recia, que quiebra las barrenas al barrenarla y dura muchos años. Tiene el mangle una propiedad entre otras, y es que las más de las ramas que echa se vuelven a la tierra y prenden en ella, de las cuales nacen otros mangles. La hoja es como de peral, más luenga, gruesa y de verde muy claro. Echa este árbol unos canutos de [texto ilegible], con una médula muy salvaje y sin gusto. En la Nueva España se halla otra especie de mangle, que es de madera blanca y más blanda.

CAPITULO CXIII
Del cedro de las Indias

El árbol que los españoles llaman cedro en esta tierra, difiere en especie del cedro que nos describen los antiguos; pero hanle dado este nombre por su preciosa madera. Es árbol muy grande, mayor que un nogal, copado y de agradable parecer; echa las hojas como el nogal, pareadas de dos en dos, unas frontero de otras; son un poco menores que las del nogal, más acanaladas y de verde más oscuro. Produce unas bellotas poco mayores que las de la encina y más romas, las cuales, en secándose, se abren en cuatro partes y dentro no tienen más que un hueso duro, y entre él y la cáscara la semilla, la cual es del tamaño de una uva y más delgada que el papel, y es muy parecida a la telilla de la cebolla; pero por una parte es un poquito más gruesa, que es donde tiene la pepita, que es como de melón. Nacen estos árboles en todas las tierras calientes y templadas de este Nuevo Mundo en gran cantidad; aunque con esta diferencia: que la madera del cedro de tierra templada es blanca, y la de la tierra caliente, roja, cual es la que se trae a este reino del Perú, de Tierra Firme, Nicaragua y otras partes; y ésta, mucho mejor que la blanca, es amarga al gusto y olorosa. Lábranse del cedro casi todas las cosas curiosas y de dura que se hacen en esta tierra, como son retablos para los templos, santos de bulto, artesones, bufetes, arcas y otras mil cosas, y hasta naves enteras se fabrican de sólo cedro y salen muy ligeras. Y dos galeras que el virrey conde de Chinchón hizo labrar en el puerto del Callao, son de madera de cedro traída de Tierra Firme. Es esta madera, como afirman los carpinteros, la más suave de labrar que se halla y muy aparejada para hacer en ella cualesquiera labores y figuras. Hállanse a veces cedros tan gruesos, que he visto yo viga que tenía diez palmos de ancho, de la cual se podían sacar muchas tablas de este ancho.

De poco tiempo a esta parte se traen a esta ciudad de Lima grandes tablones de cedro del puerto de las Barbacoas, diócesis de Quito; y con esta madera acabamos de labrar el retablo de nuestra iglesia, que es el mayor de este reino; y me afirmó el maestro que lo labró, que era este cedro de las Barbacoas mejor que todo lo demás que llevaba el retablo, que se trajo de Tierra Firme. También del puerto de Nicoya, diócesis de Nicaragua, traen navíos cargados de vigas de cedro tan grandes como de cualquiera otra madera, y es muy estimado. Yo he visto venderse estas vigas en el puerto del Callao a cuarenta pesos y a menos cada una; eran labradas en cuadro y tenían veinticinco pies de largo y dos de ancho. Llámase el cedro, en la lengua general del Perú, sibis.
results.translation:
CHAPTER CXII
On the Mangrove

Beyond the many trees I have mentioned in this book, which are useful both for their fruits and virtues and also yield good timber for working, there are found in these Indies countless others that bear no fruit worth noting but do provide precious wood for all manner of works and construction; for this reason they are of greater utility and esteem than many fruit-bearing trees. One of them is what in the language of the island of Hispaniola is called mangle [mangrove]; I place it first in this category not because it is the most prized, but because it grows most commonly along all the warm coastlands of this America.

The mangrove grows in the tidal channels [esteros] and inundated areas formed by the sea along its shores, in the marshes and mouths of rivers, and it takes root in salt water, growing within it at depths of one, two, and four fathoms. Such dense thickets are formed by these trees that one cannot walk along the shores they occupy, both on account of their density and because the ground is marshy and waterlogged. It does not grow a thick trunk; thus its timber consists of poles as thick as a man's thigh and less, long, straight, very tough, and so heavy that they sink in water. These are used just as they are cut from the forest, without any further working, as beams in buildings, as scaffolding in construction works, and for many other purposes. The wood is so hard that it breaks augers when drilled and lasts for many years. The mangrove has one particular property among others: most of the branches it puts out turn back toward the ground and take root in it, from which new mangrove trees spring up. The leaf resembles that of a pear tree, but longer, thicker, and of a very light green. This tree produces certain tube-like growths of [ILLEGIBLE: brief OCR corruption, possibly a species name or descriptive term], with a very coarse pith and no taste. In New Spain there is found another species of mangrove, which has white and softer wood.

CHAPTER CXIII
On the Cedar of the Indies

The tree that the Spaniards call cedar [cedro] in this land differs in species from the cedar described by the ancients, but it has been given this name on account of its precious wood. It is a very large tree, bigger than a walnut tree, broad-crowned and of pleasing appearance. Its leaves resemble those of the walnut, arranged in pairs facing one another; they are somewhat smaller than walnut leaves, more channeled [acanaladas], and of a darker green. It produces acorn-like fruits slightly larger than those of the holm oak and more blunt-tipped, which upon drying open into four parts; inside they contain nothing but a hard stone, and between the stone and the husk lies the seed, which is the size of a grape and thinner than paper, very similar to the thin skin of an onion, though slightly thicker on one side where the kernel [pepita] lies, resembling that of a melon. These trees grow in great abundance throughout the warm and temperate lands of this New World, with the following difference: the wood of cedar from temperate land is white, while that from warm land is red — such as the timber brought to this kingdom of Peru from Tierra Firme [the Spanish Main], Nicaragua, and other regions. This red variety, far superior to the white, is bitter to the taste and aromatic. From cedar are made nearly all the fine and durable objects crafted in this land, such as altarpieces [retablos] for churches, carved religious statues [santos de bulto], coffered ceilings [artesones], writing desks [bufetes], chests [arcas], and a thousand other things; indeed, entire ships are built of cedar alone and they come out very light. The two galleys that the Viceroy the Count of Chinchón had built in the port of Callao were made of cedar timber brought from Tierra Firme. This wood, as carpenters attest, is the easiest to work of any found here and is very well suited to carving of all kinds of designs and figures. Cedars are sometimes found so thick that I myself have seen a beam ten hand-spans [palmos] wide, from which many planks of that width could be cut.

In recent times, large planks of cedar have been brought to this city of Lima from the port of Barbacoas, diocese of Quito; and with this wood we have just finished building the altarpiece of our church, which is the largest in this kingdom. The master craftsman who built it assured me that this cedar from Barbacoas was superior to all the other timber used in the altarpiece, which had been brought from Tierra Firme. Likewise from the port of Nicoya, diocese of Nicaragua, ships arrive laden with cedar beams as large as any other timber, and it is highly esteemed. I have seen these beams sold at the port of Callao for forty pesos or less apiece; they were squared [labradas en cuadro] and measured twenty-five feet in length and two feet in width. Cedar is called, in the general language [lingua franca] of Peru, sibis.

entities_mentioned.name: mangle
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Rhizophora mangle (Red Mangrove) and related Rhizophoraceae species
entities_mentioned.notes: Cobo notes a second, white-wooded species in New Spain, possibly Laguncularia racemosa (White Mangrove) or Avicennia germinans. The text emphasizes the characteristic prop-root reproduction, salt-water habitat, and extreme density/weight of the wood. No medicinal use is described; utility is purely structural.

entities_mentioned.name: cedro de las Indias / sibis (Quechua name)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Cedrela odorata (Spanish Cedar / West Indian Cedar), family Meliaceae
entities_mentioned.notes: Cobo's description — pinnate leaves in opposite pairs, capsule fruits splitting into four valves, papery winged seed, aromatic and bitter red heartwood from warm lowlands vs. white wood from temperate zones — matches Cedrela odorata closely. The Quechua name 'sibis' is recorded here. No medicinal preparation is described; the chapter focuses entirely on timber quality and commercial use. The red heartwood variety is noted as superior and is explicitly imported to Peru from Tierra Firme and Nicaragua.

entities_mentioned.name: cedro de tierra templada (white cedar variety)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Possibly Cedrela montana or another Cedrela sp. from cooler Andean elevations
entities_mentioned.notes: Cobo distinguishes a white-wooded cedar from temperate altitudes from the superior red-wooded lowland variety. Cedrela montana is a plausible candidate for the higher-elevation form.

results.translator_notes: 1. The phrase 'unos canutos de retño-/í.«fío/«' in the mangrove chapter is clearly corrupted in the OCR source; it has been marked [ILLEGIBLE] in the cleaned text and rendered descriptively in the translation. The word may be a species-specific term or a further physical description of the propagule tubes. 2. 'Lengua general del Perú' refers to Quechua (Runasimi), the administrative and trade language of the former Inca Empire, standardized under Spanish colonial rule. 3. The Count of Chinchón mentioned is Luis Jerónimo de Cabrera, 4th Count of Chinchón, Viceroy of Peru 1629–1639, also known for his (wife's) association with quinine/cinchona bark. 4. 'Palmo' is a Spanish unit of measure roughly equivalent to 8–9 inches (ca. 20–23 cm); 'braza' is approximately 1.67 meters. 5. No medical or pharmaceutical content appears in either chapter; both are purely botanical-commercial descriptions of timber trees.

results.title_inferred: The Caobana (Mahogany) Tree of Hispaniola
results.original_text:
CAPITULO CXIV
De la caobana

En la lengua de la isla Española se llama caobana un árbol que parece de género de cedro, pero de más fina madera. Es tan grande que se sacan de él vigas de a setenta y a ochenta pies de largo, y tan grueso, que se asierran tablones de dos varas de ancho, que son ocho palmos. Su madera es muy preciosa, recia, más densa y de mejor tez que el cedro, de buen olor y colorada, más encendida que el cedro. De esta madera tenemos labrados los cajones de la sacristía de nuestra casa profesa de México, y son muy vistosos. Hay muchos de estos árboles en las islas de Barlovento y en otras partes de la Tierra Firme.
results.translation:
CHAPTER CXIV
On the Caobana

In the language of the island of Hispaniola, a tree is called caobana that appears to be of the cedar family [género de cedro], but with finer timber. It is so large that beams of seventy and eighty feet in length are obtained from it, and so thick that planks two varas wide—that is, eight palms [roughly 66 inches]—can be sawn from it. Its timber is very precious, strong, denser and of finer grain [mejor tez] than cedar, of good fragrance, and reddish in color, more vivid [más encendida] than cedar. From this wood we have had crafted the sacristy cabinets [cajones de la sacristía] of our professed house [casa profesa] in Mexico City, and they are very handsome. There are many of these trees on the Windward Islands [islas de Barlovento] and in other parts of the mainland [Tierra Firme].

entities_mentioned.name: caobana
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Swietenia mahagoni or Swietenia macrophylla (West Indian / American Mahogany)
entities_mentioned.notes: The name 'caobana' from the Taíno language of Hispaniola is the direct etymological root of the modern Spanish word 'caoba' (mahogany). Cobo's description of the reddish, dense, fragrant timber far superior to cedar and yielding very wide planks is consistent with mahogany. The Windward Islands (Barlovento) location further supports this identification. No medicinal use is described in this segment; the passage focuses entirely on the timber's physical properties and commercial/decorative value.

entities_mentioned.name: cedro
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Cedrela odorata (Spanish Cedar / tropical cedar)
entities_mentioned.notes: Used here as a comparative reference point for wood quality, color, and fragrance, not as the subject of the chapter.

results.translator_notes: No medical or pharmaceutical content is present in this segment; it is a purely descriptive timber entry. The 'casa profesa de México' refers to the Jesuit Professed House in Mexico City, providing a datable material-culture reference point. The measurement 'dos varas de ancho, que son ocho palmos' reflects Cobo's own gloss equating 2 varas to 8 palmos (1 vara ≈ 4 palmos, consistent with the Castilian vara of roughly 33 inches). The OCR artifact 'árhol' has been silently corrected to 'árbol'; 'preciosa' was rendered as 'preciosa' (corrected from 'precío¬sa' linebreak artifact).

results.title_inferred: Four Timber Trees of Warm and Andean Regions: Guachapelí, Harca, Cacha Cacha, and Soto
results.original_text:
CAPITULO CXV

Del guachapelí

El guachapelí es un árbol que sólo nace en tierras calientes; no crece muy alto, porque no sube derecho, sino que echa el tronco torcido y encorvado y muchas y muy gruesas ramas. Su hoja es pequeña como la de la alfalfa. Es árbol silvestre infructífero, pero de muy buena madera para labrar, de la cual se hacen comúnmente los corvatones y costillas de los navíos; y para que salgan a propósito cortan el tronco una o dos varas de la tierra, y de aquel lazo y otro tanto de su raíz hacen un corvatón. Es madera la de este árbol muy recia, correosa y que no raja, por lo cual hacen della los motones y poleas de los navíos y otras muchas cosas que piden hacerse de madera fuerte. Y es providencia de Dios que haga este árbol el tronco torcido y encorvado, porque de esa suerte se pueden hacer de su madera ruedas para ingenios de azúcar y otros menesteres, para que no hay otra madera más a propósito. Echadas sus astillas en agua, la tiñen colorada y ponen amarga.

CAPITULO CXVI

De la harca

La harca es un árbol muy parecido en su grandeza y hoja a la vilca; da unas vainillas como algarrobas, delgadas y con ciertas pepitas dentro; son menores estas vainillas que las de la vilca, de la cual se diferencia la harca en dos cosas: la primera en ser mayor que ella, y la segunda, en no perder la hoja en todo el año. Su madera es muy recia y el corazón negro como ébano, por lo cual se hacen de él algunas cosas como son cruces y otras semejantes, que parecen de ébano. Llámase harca este árbol en la lengua general del Perú, y nace en las provincias de los Charcas.

CAPITULO CXVII

De la cacha cacha

En las mismas provincias del arzobispado de los Charcas nace la cacha cacha, que es un árbol muy semejante a la harca en su grandeza y hojas. Lleva unas vainillas de cáscara gruesa, inútiles, de más particular hechura que las otras, porque son como nueces algo chatas y más propiamente como almejas de la mar. No tienen dentro más que unas pepitas secas. Su madera es fuerte y que nunca cría carcoma, y dura mucho tiempo. Llámase así este árbol en la lengua peruana.

CAPITULO CXVIII

Del soto

También el soto es natural de las provincias de los Charcas. Es un árbol muy parecido a la tipa en la hoja y apariencia, salvo que crece más que ella y más derecho. La hoja es poco menor que la de la tipa y de verde más oscuro. Echa unas vainillas menores que las de la tipa y algo coloradas. Con la hoja de este árbol curten los cordobanes en algunas partes, y son por eso muy buenos. Su madera es por extremo pesada y recia; de la cual son casi todos los mazos de los ingenios de plata de Potosí y de Oruro; y por llevarse de lejos esta madera, cuesta allí muy cara.
results.translation:
CHAPTER CXV

On the Guachapelí

The guachapelí is a tree that grows only in warm lowland regions [tierras calientes]. It does not grow very tall, because it does not rise straight up; rather, it sends out its trunk twisted and curved, with many very thick branches. Its leaf is small, like that of alfalfa. It is a wild tree that bears no fruit, but yields very good timber for working. From this timber, the knees [corvatones] and ribs of ships are commonly made. In order to obtain pieces suitable for this purpose, the trunk is cut one or two varas [a vara being roughly 33 inches] above the ground, and from that junction together with an equal length of the root, a ship's knee is fashioned. The wood of this tree is very hard, tough, and does not split, which is why blocks [motones] and pulleys for ships are made from it, as well as many other things requiring strong timber. It is by the providence of God that this tree grows its trunk twisted and curved, because in that form wheels for sugar mills [ingenios de azúcar] and other implements can be made from its wood, for which no other timber is more suitable. When its chips are placed in water, they dye the water red and make it bitter.

CHAPTER CXVI

On the Harca

The harca is a tree very similar in size and leaf to the vilca. It produces small pods like carob beans [algarrobas], slender and containing certain seeds inside. These pods are smaller than those of the vilca, from which the harca differs in two respects: first, in being larger than the vilca; and second, in not losing its leaves at any time of year [i.e., it is evergreen]. Its wood is very hard and its heartwood black as ebony, on account of which various articles such as crosses and similar objects are made from it, which appear to be of ebony. This tree is called harca in the general language [Quechua] of Peru, and it grows in the provinces of Charcas.

CHAPTER CXVII

On the Cacha Cacha

In the same provinces of the archbishopric of Charcas grows the cacha cacha, a tree very similar to the harca in size and leaves. It bears pods with a thick husk, useless [as food], and of a more peculiar shape than the others, being like somewhat flattened nuts, and more precisely like sea clams [almejas de la mar]. They contain inside nothing but dry seeds. Its wood is strong, never becomes worm-eaten [carcoma], and lasts a very long time. This tree is so called in the Peruvian language [Quechua].

CHAPTER CXVIII

On the Soto

The soto likewise is native to the provinces of Charcas. It is a tree very similar to the tipa in leaf and appearance, except that it grows larger than the tipa and more upright. The leaf is slightly smaller than that of the tipa and of a darker green. It produces pods smaller than those of the tipa and somewhat reddish. In some places the leaves of this tree are used to tan cordovan hides [cordobanes], which are very good for this reason. Its wood is extremely heavy and hard; from it are made almost all the stamping mallets [mazos] of the silver processing mills [ingenios de plata] of Potosí and Oruro. Because this timber must be transported from afar, it is very expensive there.

entities_mentioned.name: guachapelí
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Pseudosamanea guachapele (syn. Albizia guachapele), a leguminous tree of the family Fabaceae, native to tropical lowland America
entities_mentioned.notes: Prized shipbuilding timber; chips dye water red and impart bitterness. No medicinal use described here, only industrial/naval use.

entities_mentioned.name: harca
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Possibly Prosopis ferox or a related Prosopis / Anadenanthera species native to the Charcas (modern Bolivia) region; the black heartwood resembling ebony is a distinguishing feature
entities_mentioned.notes: Described as evergreen, larger than the vilca, with black heartwood. Cobo compares it to vilca (Anadenanthera colubrina). Some sources tentatively equate harca with Prosopis ferox. No medicinal use described.

entities_mentioned.name: cacha cacha
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Unknown; possibly a Prosopis or Acacia species from the Charcas region of Bolivia
entities_mentioned.notes: Notable for clam-shaped pods and durable, worm-resistant wood. No modern botanical identification firmly established.

entities_mentioned.name: soto
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Possibly Myroxylon peruiferum or Tipuana tipu (given comparison to tipa); alternatively Astronium urundeuva or a related dense-wooded Andean species. Some sources identify soto as Schinopsis haenkeana (Anacardiaceae)
entities_mentioned.notes: Used to tan hides (leaves) and for heavy-duty mill hammers at Potosí and Oruro. Compared to tipa (Tipuana tipu). No medicinal use described.

entities_mentioned.name: vilca
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Anadenanthera colubrina, a leguminous tree of the Andes
entities_mentioned.notes: Referenced as a comparator for harca; well-known Andean tree.

entities_mentioned.name: tipa
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Tipuana tipu (Fabaceae), the tipa or rosewood tree, native to Bolivia and northwestern Argentina
entities_mentioned.notes: Referenced as a comparator for soto.

results.translator_notes: 1. 'Corvatones' (OCR: 'corhatones') are the curved structural knee-timbers used in wooden shipbuilding; rendered as 'knees' in English naval terminology. 2. 'Motones' are wooden blocks (pulleys) used in rigging. 3. 'Ingenios de azúcar' and 'ingenios de plata' refer to sugar-processing mills and silver-ore stamping mills respectively — important colonial industrial installations. 4. 'Cordobanes' are high-quality tanned leather hides (originally associated with Córdoba, Spain). 5. 'Lengua general del Perú' refers to Quechua, the lingua franca of the Inca Empire and colonial Andes. 6. OCR errors silently corrected include: 'corhatones'→'corvatones', 'harcoma'→'carcoma', 'arzotópado'→'arzobispado', 'fsr'→'parecen', 'im'→'dos', 'Lbrt'→'Lleva', 'lazo'→'lazo' (retained as 'junction/node'), 'Rai¬gan'→'salgan', 'pone'→'ponen'. 7. The phrase 'inútiles, de [más] particular hechura' — 'más' supplied from context as the OCR rendered it ambiguously; bracketed in the cleaned original. 8. No medical preparations or ailment treatments are described in any of these four chapters; all four trees are discussed exclusively for their timber or industrial properties, with the sole exception of the guachapelí's chips producing colored, bitter water (no therapeutic use ascribed).

results.title_inferred: The Copey Tree of Hispaniola
results.original_text:
CAPITULO CXIX

Del copey

En la isla Española nombran copey a cierto árbol mediano, de buena madera para labrar; la hoja es redonda y algo más gruesa que la del ubero, y aun se escribe muy bien en ella. Servían estas hojas a los soldados, al principio de la conquista de aquella isla, de naipes, dibujando en ellas todas las figuras de la baraja.
results.translation:
CHAPTER CXIX

On the Copey

On the island of Hispaniola they call 'copey' a certain medium-sized tree, with good timber for working; its leaf is round and somewhat thicker than that of the sea-grape [ubero], and one can write quite well upon it. These leaves served the soldiers, at the beginning of the conquest of that island, as playing cards [naipes], with all the figures of the card deck [baraja] drawn upon them.

entities_mentioned.name: copey
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Clusia rosea (autograph tree / copey)
entities_mentioned.notes: Clusia rosea is well-attested in Caribbean sources under the name 'copey'; its large, stiff, waxy leaves are indeed capable of being written or drawn upon, consistent with Cobo's account of soldiers using them as playing cards.

entities_mentioned.name: ubero
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Coccoloba uvifera (sea-grape)
entities_mentioned.notes: The ubero or sea-grape is a common Caribbean coastal tree used as a size and texture reference for the copey leaf here.

No medical or pharmacological content is present in this segment; it is purely botanical and ethnographic. No ailments, preparations, or treatments are described.

results.translator_notes:
{
  "title_inferred": "Timber Trees of Peru and the Americas: Chapters CXX–CXXIII",
  "original_text": "CAPITULO CXX

Del Hoque

El Hoque es un árbol que nace en la [sierra] del Perú, de madera recia y [dura]. Echa sus ramas largas y derechas, de las cuales se hacen bordones, que salen muy pesados, nudosos y recios, y varas para otros usos para que se requiere madera fuerte.

CAPITULO CXXI

Del canacaspi

El canacaspi nace en tierras yuncas. Es árbol muy grande; produce las ramas y hojas muy semejantes a las del guarango-espino, salvo que no tiene espinas. De este género de árboles hacen los indios en la costa de la Nueva España las bateas y canoas, porque la madera es muy a propósito para ello.

CAPITULO CXXII

Del tuco

El tuco es un árbol silvestre tan grande como un nogal; su hoja se parece a la del álamo; lleva por fruto unas bolillas o agallas secas parecidas al fruto de aliso. Su madera es colorada, muy recia y preciada. El primer navichuelo que navegó la laguna de Chucuito el año de 1616, se fabricó todo de madera de tuco, sin que se entremetiese otra alguna, porque hay gran copia de estos árboles en la provincia de la Recaja [Larecaja], ocho leguas distante de las riberas de dicha laguna, donde se cortó la madera.

CAPITULO CXXIII

De los demás árboles de buena madera

Fuera nunca acabar querer yo aquí dar cuenta de todas las diferencias de árboles de madera buena para labrar que cría esta tierra; sólo hago mención en los capítulos pasados y en éste de los árboles más conocidos, cuyas maderas son las que comúnmente se gastan en los pueblos más principales de estas Indias.

La madera más preciosa que conocemos es el ébano que se trae de la India Oriental; éste nace también en muchas partes de la América y se labran de él las mismas cosas que del otro; del cual sólo difiere en que no es igualmente tan negro, porque a trechos suele tener unas listas pardas, sin embargo de lo cual parece ser de la misma especie que el de la India. El que

results.title_inferred: Timber Trade and Wood Varieties Supplying Lima
results.original_text:
un árbol que llaman aliso, muy buena de labrar; y otra que dicen de peral, no de los árboles que llevan peras.

Por donde siento que no debe de haber ciudad en lo descubierto que goce de tanta abundancia y variedad de maderas fuertes y preciosas como esta ciudad de Lima, por la comodidad de su puerto; adonde entran al año muchos navíos cargados de sola madera, unos de las costas de Nueva España, Guatemala, Nicaragua y Costa Rica, y otros del reino de Tierra Firme, de Chile y de los puertos de este mismo reino, como son Guayaquil, Bahía de Caracúes, las Barbacoas; y de los valles de este arzobispado, como son Guambacho, Casma y otros se trae la madera de espino, que corresponde acá a la madera de encina de España, y aún se tiene por más recia que la de encina. Y todos los días del año se ocupan muchas birretas en acarrear madera del Callao a Lima, y hay muchos hombres que tienen esta granjería.
results.translation:
a tree they call aliso [alder], very good for working; and another they call peral [pear-wood], not from the trees that bear pears.

I therefore believe there cannot be a city in the known world that enjoys such abundance and variety of strong and precious timbers as this city of Lima, owing to the convenience of its port; where every year many ships arrive loaded with timber alone — some from the coasts of New Spain, Guatemala, Nicaragua, and Costa Rica, and others from the kingdom of Tierra Firme, from Chile, and from ports within this same kingdom [of Peru], such as Guayaquil, Bahía de Caracúes, and Las Barbacoas. And from the valleys of this archbishopric, such as Guambacho and Casma and others, espino [thorn-wood] is brought, which here corresponds to the oak timber of Spain, and is even considered harder than oak. And every day of the year many small carts [birretas] are occupied in hauling timber from Callao to Lima, and there are many men who make their livelihood from this trade.

entities_mentioned.name: aliso
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Alnus acuminata or related Alnus spp. (Andean alder)
entities_mentioned.notes: Cobo notes it is 'very good for working,' consistent with alder's use as a workable timber.

entities_mentioned.name: peral (de madera)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Unknown; possibly a local hardwood with pear-like grain; not Pyrus spp.
entities_mentioned.notes: Cobo explicitly clarifies this is not the fruit-bearing pear tree but a timber species named for resemblance to pear-wood.

entities_mentioned.name: espino
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Possibly Acacia macracantha or another thorny hardwood native to coastal Peru
entities_mentioned.notes: Cobo equates espino with Spanish encina (holm oak, Quercus ilex) in hardness and utility, and claims it surpasses it. Several spiny hardwoods from Andean coastal valleys could match this description.

entities_mentioned.name: encina
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Quercus ilex (holm oak, Spain)
entities_mentioned.notes: Used here as a comparative benchmark for timber hardness, not as a species present in Peru.

entities_mentioned.name: Lima

entities_mentioned.notes: Described as receiving timber by sea from across the Spanish colonial world.

entities_mentioned.name: Callao
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: El Callao, port of Lima, Peru
entities_mentioned.notes: The timber landing port from which daily cartage to Lima was undertaken.

entities_mentioned.name: Guayaquil
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Guayaquil, Ecuador
entities_mentioned.notes: Named as a timber-supplying port, consistent with its historical role as a major shipbuilding and timber center.

entities_mentioned.name: Bahía de Caracúes
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Bahía de Caráquez, Ecuador
entities_mentioned.notes: Coastal port in present-day Ecuador, a known timber source in the colonial period.

entities_mentioned.name: Barbacoas
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Las Barbacoas, coastal region near present-day Ecuador/Colombia border
entities_mentioned.notes: A timber-producing coastal region mentioned among Pacific supply ports.

2. This segment contains no medical or pharmacological content; it is a passage on timber commerce and urban supply chains for Lima.

results.title_inferred: The Balsa Tree and Its Uses
results.original_text:
CAPITULO CXXIV
Del palo de balsa

Si unas maderas se estiman por ser raras y pesadas, no por eso se desechan las blandas y livianas, porque para diferentes usos se requieren de contrarias cualidades; tal es la que llamamos en estas Indias palo de balsa, porque hacen de ella buenas balsas. El árbol es grande, y en algunas partes crece más que los mayores nogales; su hoja se parece a la del laurel; no echa fruto, sino unas agallas o botoncillos como los de la vara. Su madera es tan blanda y fofa, que cuando se corta este árbol, a cada golpe se entra toda la hacha por el tronco. De sus maderos, después de secos, se hacen en muchas partes de este reino balsas para navegar la mar y por los ríos; las cuales, por ser de madera tan liviana, son muy ligeras. Suple esta madera a falta de corcho, que no hay en esta tierra sino lo que se trae de España. La corteza de este árbol es gruesa como dos dedos, y sacándola entera de arriba abajo, hacen de ella, después de mojada, fuertes sogas. También esta corteza, golpeándola bien, sirve a los indios y negros de vestido, porque queda delgada y a manera de red; y para que cubra bien el cuerpo, le dan dos o tres dobleces.
results.translation:
CHAPTER CXXIV
Of the Balsa Tree [palo de balsa]

If some timbers are prized for being rare and heavy, the soft and lightweight ones are not on that account to be rejected, since different uses require contrary qualities. Such is the wood we call in these Indies the balsa tree [palo de balsa], because good rafts [balsas] are made from it. The tree is large, and in some places grows taller than the biggest walnut trees; its leaf resembles that of the laurel; it bears no fruit, but only galls or little buds like those of the wand [vara]. Its timber is so soft and spongy that when this tree is cut, with every stroke the axe sinks entirely into the trunk. From its timbers, once dried, rafts are made in many parts of this realm for sailing the sea and navigating the rivers; which, being of so light a wood, are very buoyant. This timber serves as a substitute for cork, there being none in this land except what is brought from Spain. The bark of this tree is as thick as two fingers, and when stripped off whole from top to bottom, it is used—after being soaked in water—to make strong ropes. Furthermore, this bark, when well beaten, serves the Indians and Africans [negros] as clothing, for it becomes thin and takes on the form of a net; and to cover the body adequately, two or three layers are folded together.

entities_mentioned.name: palo de balsa
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Ochroma pyramidale (balsa tree, family Malvaceae)
entities_mentioned.notes: Cobo's description of extreme lightness, large size, laurel-like leaves, and use for rafts and ropes is consistent with Ochroma pyramidale, the balsa tree native to tropical America. The use of beaten bark as clothing aligns with known practices for bark-cloth production from this species.

results.translator_notes: The OCR produced several corrupted words in the original (e.g., 'ikras' for 'raras', 'Wandas' for 'blandas', 'ttja' for 'hoja', 'fmlo' for 'fruto', 'UaiMla' for 'blanda', 'a cada golpe se entra toda la ha- / «"ia' for 'hacha'). All have been silently corrected in the cleaned original text, as the intended readings are unambiguous from context. The term 'negros' has been rendered as 'Africans' in brackets to acknowledge the historical reference while flagging it for archival sensitivity. No medical or pharmaceutical content is present in this segment; extraction of drug/preparation data is therefore not applicable.

results.title_inferred: The Chahuar Tree and Its Bark Uses
results.original_text:
CAPITULO CXXV
Del chahuar

El chahuar parece tener algún parentesco con el palo de balsa. Es un árbol derecho, muy copado, no más grueso que el muslo, de madera muy liviana, la hoja poco mayor que la del naranjo y más delgada. Tiene la corteza gruesa y correosa, de la cual se hacen las sogas para encestar la coca, alpargatas y cuerdas de arcabuz.
results.translation:
CHAPTER CXXV
On the chahuar

The chahuar appears to bear some kinship to the balsa tree [palo de balsa]. It is an upright tree, very full-crowned, no thicker than a thigh, with very lightweight wood; its leaf is somewhat larger than that of the orange tree and thinner. It has a thick and leathery bark, from which are made the ropes used for basketing [encesting] coca, sandals [alpargatas], and musket [arcabuz] cords.

entities_mentioned.name: chahuar
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Possibly Furcraea andina or a related Furcraea/Agave species, or alternatively a species of Eriotheca or Chorisia (Malvaceae/Bombacaceae) given the balsa comparison; identification remains uncertain
entities_mentioned.notes: Cobo's comparison to balsa (Ochroma pyramidale) suggests lightweight wood; the thick, leathery bark used for cordage, sandals, and rope suggests a fibrous-bark tree. 'Chahuar' is also sometimes applied in Andean sources to Furcraea andina (a large monocot), but Cobo's description of a tree with a trunk and orange-like leaves does not fit Furcraea well. May be an unresolved species.

entities_mentioned.name: palo de balsa
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Ochroma pyramidale (Cav. ex Lam.) Urb. (Malvaceae)
entities_mentioned.notes: Referenced only as a comparative species for wood lightness.

results.translator_notes: No medicinal or pharmaceutical use is described in this passage; it is purely botanical and material-culture in focus. 'Encestar la coca' refers to packing or basketing coca leaves for transport. 'Cuerdas de arcabuz' are the cords or slow-matches used with matchlock firearms [arcabuces]. 'Alpargatas' are rope-soled sandals of Iberian origin, here evidently also produced locally from this bark. The OCR hyphen artifact in 'paren¬tesco' has been silently corrected to 'parentesco'.

results.title_inferred: The Ceiba Tree
results.original_text:
CAPITULO CXXVI
De la ceyba

En la lengua de la isla Española se llama ceyba un árbol de los mayores que se hallan en estas Indias. Es de buena sombra y echa muchas y muy extendidas ramas; su fruto son unas vainas como manzanas redondas llenas de cierta lana delgada y blanca como algodón, las cuales, después de secas, se abren y se lleva el aire la lana; entre la cual hay unos granillos o pepitas, que es la semilla. Echa la hoja en un pezón como de hoja de parra, y ella es semejante a la hoja del cáñamo, de seis a siete puntas, en forma de estrella; su madera es blanda y no sólida. Suélense plantar estos árboles en las plazas de pueblos de indios, por su gran hermosura, y aun de algunos de españoles; pues conocí yo una en Panamá delante del convento de San Francisco, que permaneció allí muchos años.
results.translation:
CHAPTER CXXVI
Of the Ceiba

In the language of the island of Hispaniola, a tree called the ceiba is one of the largest to be found in these Indies. It provides good shade and puts forth many widely spreading branches. Its fruit consists of pods like round apples, filled with a fine, white wool resembling cotton; once dried, these pods open and the wind carries off the wool, among which are small grains or seeds [pepitas], which constitute the seed proper. It puts forth its leaves on a stalk [pezón] like that of a grape-vine leaf, and the leaf itself resembles that of hemp [cáñamo], having six to seven points in the form of a star. Its wood is soft and not solid. These trees are commonly planted in the main squares [plazas] of Indian towns on account of their great beauty, and even in some Spanish towns as well; for I myself knew one in Panama, in front of the convent of San Francisco, where it stood for many years.

entities_mentioned.name: ceyba (ceiba)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Ceiba pentandra (L.) Gaertn. — kapok tree
entities_mentioned.notes: Cobo's description closely matches Ceiba pentandra: large size, wide-spreading branches, star-shaped palmate leaves (5–9 leaflets resembling hemp leaves), and pods producing fine white cottony fiber (kapok) surrounding small seeds. The soft, non-dense wood is also characteristic. The name derives from the Taíno language of Hispaniola. No medicinal use is described in this passage; it is treated primarily as an ornamental and shade tree.

results.translator_notes: OCR errors silently corrected: 'xm' → 'un' (standard OCR misread of 'un'); 'herinosura' → 'hermosura'; 'aUí' → 'allí'. No medicinal preparation or therapeutic use is described in this segment; the chapter is purely botanical and descriptive. The reference to the ceiba in Panama before the Convent of San Francisco is a firsthand observation by Cobo and has potential value for historical urban botany.

results.title_inferred: The Capirona Tree and Its Petrifying Properties
results.original_text:
CAPITULO CXXVII
De la capirona

En la provincia del Paraguay y en otras tierras calientes de este reino del Perú nace cierto árbol, que en la gobernación de Yaguarsongo, diócesis de Quito, llaman capirona, el cual es de madera recia, y de tan particular naturaleza, que metido en el agua por tiempo, se convierte en piedra; y como nazca comúnmente en las orillas de los ríos, las ramas que caen dentro del agua, quedando parte fuera de ella, tienen de piedra aquella parte que cubrió el agua, y lo demás se queda palo como lo era antes; y es tan recia, dura y densa esta piedra, que sirve de pedernal y herida con el eslabón, despide muchas centellas.
results.translation:
CHAPTER CXXVII
Of the Capirona

In the province of Paraguay and in other warm lands of this kingdom of Peru there grows a certain tree, which in the governorship of Yaguarsongo, diocese of Quito, they call capirona. It is of hard wood and of so particular a nature that, when submerged in water for a period of time, it turns to stone. And since it commonly grows along the banks of rivers, the branches that fall into the water, with part remaining above the surface, have turned to stone in that portion covered by the water, while the rest remains wood as it was before. This stone is so hard, dense, and tough that it serves as a flint [pedernal], and when struck with a steel [eslabón], it gives off many sparks.

entities_mentioned.name: capirona
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Calycophyllum spruceanum (Rubiaceae) or possibly Capirona decorticans; both are Amazonian hardwood trees known by this common name
entities_mentioned.notes: The 'petrification' described is likely a folk observation of silicification or extreme mineral deposition in submerged wood, a real phenomenon in certain dense-wooded riparian species. The geographic attribution to Yaguarsongo (a region in present-day southeastern Ecuador/northeastern Peru) is consistent with the known range of Calycophyllum spruceanum. No medicinal use is described in this segment; the focus is entirely on the wood's physical properties.

results.translator_notes: This segment contains no pharmacological or medical content; it is a natural-history description of an unusual physical property attributed to the capirona tree. 'Pedernal' (flint) and 'eslabón' (fire-steel/striker) are period technical terms for fire-starting equipment and have been retained in brackets in the translation. The claim that the wood literally converts to stone when submerged likely reflects either genuine silicification of submerged wood or an exaggerated folk account of the wood's extreme density and hardness when waterlogged. OCR artifact: 'go¬bernación' (hyphenated across line) silently corrected to 'gobernación'.

results.title_inferred: The Quínua Tree: Description, Habitat, and Uses
results.original_text:
CAPITULO CXXVIII
De la quínua

La quínua es un árbol del tamaño de un olivo y de ahí para abajo hasta no crecer más de un estado; tiene las ramas y tronco rojos, con la corteza muy delgada, que con facilidad se despide; echa las hojas de tres en tres, las cuales son poco más cortas que las del olivo, por dentro verdes y por fuera blanquecinas, las puntas botas y aserradas sutilmente por toda su redondez. Produce unas florecillas sin olor ni apariencia; son verdes y no mayores que las del olivo. Es árbol tan fuerte en resistir el rigor del frío y heladas como el quishuar; y así, sólo estas dos castas de árboles nacen en los rigorosos páramos del Perú, especialmente en las provincias del Collao. Hacen de la quínua muy buen carbón, que es bien necesario donde tanto frío hace.
results.translation:
CHAPTER CXXVIII
Of the Quínua

The quínua is a tree of the size of an olive tree, ranging downward to no more than one [estado, roughly one man's height] in growth. Its branches and trunk are red, with a very thin bark that peels away easily. It puts forth its leaves in groups of three; these leaves are somewhat shorter than those of the olive tree, green on the inner [upper] surface and whitish on the outer [lower] surface, with blunt tips and finely serrated all around their edges. It produces small flowers without scent or notable appearance; they are green and no larger than those of the olive tree. This tree is as hardy in withstanding the rigors of cold and frost as the quishuar [Buddleja incana], and thus these two kinds of trees alone grow in the harsh high moorlands [páramos] of Peru, especially in the provinces of the Collao. Very good charcoal is made from the quínua, which is greatly needed in so cold a region.

entities_mentioned.name: quínua (árbol)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Polylepis species (likely Polylepis racemosa or a closely related Andean species; family Rosaceae)
entities_mentioned.notes: Not to be confused with the grain crop quinoa (Chenopodium quinoa). Cobo's description—reddish, papery-barked trunk, trifoliate leaves, small green flowers, extreme cold tolerance in Andean páramos—matches Polylepis very well. These trees are characteristic of high-altitude Andean zones and are historically used for charcoal production.

entities_mentioned.name: quishuar
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Buddleja incana (Scrophulariaceae / Buddlejaceae)
entities_mentioned.notes: Cobo compares the quínua's frost resistance to that of the quishuar, a well-known high-altitude Andean tree. The quishuar is commonly identified as Buddleja incana, though some sources suggest Buddleja coriacea. Both species are native to the Andean puna and share the same cold-hardy habitat.

entities_mentioned.name: Collao (provinces of)
entities_mentioned.notes: The Collao (Qullasuyhu region) refers to the high-altitude altiplano area around Lake Titicaca, encompassing parts of modern Peru and Bolivia. Geographic rather than botanical entity; noted as the primary habitat of the quínua tree.

results.translator_notes: 1. The OCR rendered 'quínua' variously as 'qtiínua' and 'qiiínua'; all instances have been silently normalized to 'quínua' in the cleaned original. Similarly, 'no mayore*s' (OCR artifact) has been corrected to 'no mayores,' and 'CoUao' to 'Collao.' 2. The word 'estado' (a unit of measure roughly equivalent to a man's height, ~1.7 m) has been preserved in brackets in the translation as it is a period technical term. 3. No medicinal use or preparation method is described in this segment; the tree's primary practical use mentioned is as a source of charcoal in cold high-altitude regions. 4. The description of leaves ('de tres en tres'—in groups of three) is consistent with Polylepis, which has pinnately compound leaves often appearing in clusters.

results.title_inferred: On the Palo Santo: Two Trees of That Name, and Conclusion of the Trees Treatise
results.original_text:
CAPITULO CXXIX

Del palo santo

Los españoles habitadores de la provincia de Santa Cruz de la Sierra han puesto este nombre a cierto árbol que nace en aquella tierra no porque sea santo, sino en sentido contrario, huyen todos de llegarse a él, por el daño que han experimentado los que incautamente se le han arrimado. Es del tamaño de un manzano pequeño, tiene las hojas grandes como cidro, pocas ramas y el tronco no más grueso que el muslo. Todo este árbol está hueco desde el tronco hasta las más delgadas ramas y lleno de unas hormigas bermejas y larguillas, tan ponzoñosas, que de su picadura suelen dar calenturas y siempre duele muchísimo (18). Como las hormigas están escondidas dentro del árbol, no se echan de ver, que es la causa de que no se guarden los que no saben el secreto; pero en tocando a una hoja, salen de presto por todas partes tantas de estas hormigas que pone admiración, y se arrojan sobre la persona que tocó el árbol, si con tiempo no se aparta, y la martirizan a picaduras. Es de tal calidad este arbolillo, que debajo de él no nace hierba alguna.

En este reino del Perú llaman también palo santo a otro árbol que nace en la Sierra en mucha cantidad; tiene un olor parecido al del incienso, mas es tan vehemente, que los que caminan por donde hay de estos árboles, lo perciben a gran distancia. Su madera es amarilla, blanda y fofa, y tan resinosa que arde como tea; ofende tanto su olor, por ser tan profundo y penetrante, que no se puede tener en un aposento ninguna pequeña rajita, porque causa dolor de cabeza.

Con esto doy fin al tratado de los árboles, porque, querer describirlos todos, ni hay quien pueda contarlos, ni aun paciencia para leerlos; basta que he hecho mención en este libro de más de doscientas especies de ellos naturales de esta tierra y no conocidos en España, de los cuales, la mitad, son fructíferos, y algunos de frutas tan sabrosas y regaladas, que en Europa fueran muy preciadas.

(18) Esta hormiga es la Myrmica [hormiga del palo santo y hormiga nombrada por el árbol en que habita].
results.translation:
CHAPTER CXXIX

On the Palo Santo [Holy Wood]

The Spanish inhabitants of the province of Santa Cruz de la Sierra have given this name to a certain tree that grows in that land, not because it is holy [santo], but rather in the opposite sense: everyone avoids approaching it, on account of the harm experienced by those who have incautiously drawn near to it. It is the size of a small apple tree, has large leaves like those of the citron [cidro], few branches, and a trunk no thicker than a man's thigh. The entire tree is hollow from the trunk to its thinnest branches, and filled with certain reddish, elongated ants so venomous that their sting commonly brings on fevers and always causes very great pain (18). Because the ants are hidden inside the tree they cannot be seen, which is the reason those who do not know the secret take no precautions; but upon touching a single leaf, so many of these ants pour out immediately from every part that it is astonishing, and they fling themselves upon the person who touched the tree if he does not withdraw in time, tormenting him with their stings. This little tree is of such a nature that no herb whatsoever grows beneath it.

In this kingdom of Peru the name palo santo is also given to another tree that grows in great abundance in the Sierra. It has a scent resembling that of incense, but so intense that those who travel through areas where these trees grow perceive it from a great distance. Its wood is yellow, soft, and spongy, and so resinous that it burns like a torch [tea]; its odor is so offensive, being so deep and penetrating, that not even a small splinter of it can be kept in a room without causing headache.

With this I bring to a close the treatise on trees, for to wish to describe them all is beyond the counting of any man, nor would there be patience enough to read them all. It suffices that I have mentioned in this book more than two hundred species of them native to this land and unknown in Spain, of which half are fruit-bearing, and some bear fruits so flavorful and delicate that they would be greatly prized in Europe.

(18) This ant is the Myrmica [the ant of the palo santo, named after the tree in which it lives].

entities_mentioned.name: Palo santo (first, Santa Cruz de la Sierra)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Possibly a species in the genus Triplaris (e.g., Triplaris americana, known as 'tachi' or 'palo santo'), famous for housing colonies of stinging ants (Pseudomyrmex triplarinus) in hollow stems
entities_mentioned.notes: Cobo describes a hollow-stemmed tree harboring aggressive, venomous reddish ants whose sting causes fever and severe pain; no vegetation grows beneath it. This matches well with Triplaris spp. and their obligate ant symbionts. The footnote identifies the ant as 'Myrmica,' though modern taxonomy would place the relevant ant in genus Pseudomyrmex.

entities_mentioned.name: Palo santo (second, Sierra del Perú)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Likely Bursera graveolens or possibly Dacryodes peruviana; the incense-like, highly penetrating resinous odor and yellow, soft, torch-burning wood are consistent with Bursera graveolens, widely known as 'palo santo' in Andean regions
entities_mentioned.notes: Described as abundant in the Sierra, with incense-like scent perceptible from a great distance, yellow soft spongy wood, highly resinous, burning like a torch, and causing headache when kept indoors due to its intense penetrating odor. Bursera graveolens is the most commonly identified 'palo santo' of the Peruvian/Ecuadorian coast and sierra today.

entities_mentioned.name: Myrmica (ant, footnote 18)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Pseudomyrmex triplarinus or related Pseudomyrmex sp. (historically misassigned to genus Myrmica)
entities_mentioned.notes: The footnote label appears to be a later editorial annotation. The ants described — reddish, elongated, living inside hollow Triplaris-type trees, highly venomous — correspond to Pseudomyrmex triplarinus in modern taxonomy, not Myrmica.

results.translator_notes:
1. The footnote (18) is heavily damaged/OCR-corrupted; the text '[hormiga cl<t>del palo santo y hormiga por el árbol en que hab[ita]' has been reconstructed as '[hormiga del palo santo y hormiga nombrada por el árbol en que habita]' — marked accordingly. The genus name 'Myrmica' appears to be a later editorial interpolation, not Cobo's own text.
2. 'Tea' in the original means a resinous pine torch, a common period usage; rendered as 'torch' with the original in brackets preserved in translation for clarity.
3. 'Cidro' refers to the citron (Citrus medica), used here as a leaf-size comparator.
4. No medical preparation is described in this passage; the health effects mentioned (fever, severe pain from ant stings; headache from wood odor) are observational rather than therapeutic. No ailment-treatment extraction is applicable.
5. The closing paragraph is Cobo's authorial conclusion to his entire trees section (Books IV–VI), noting he has described over 200 species native to the Americas unknown in Spain.

results.title_inferred: Refutation of the theory that Solomon's fleets introduced Old World plants and animals to the New World
results.original_text:
ara caminar y con que más fácilmente trajinasen desde las minas a los puertos los ricos metales que de tan lejanas tierras venían a buscar, ya que de las otras cosas no se curasen?

Ni es conforme a razón el intentar satisfacer a todas estas dificultades con decir que durante aquel comercio y navegación trujeron a esta tierra las mismas cosas que han traído los españoles, mas que, andando el tiempo, vinieron a extinguirse y acabarse. Porque las plantas y animales traídos por los nuestros, han cundido tanto por este Nuevo Mundo, que en muchas partes nacen tan copiosamente sin industria ni beneficio humano, que cuando en quererlas acabar y consumir pusieran los hombres toda su inteligencia y conato, no salieron con ello; cuanto más que no la ponen sino en conservarlas y aumentarlas, llevados del interés y utilidad que de las tales cosas les resulta. En conclusión, no vemos alguna diferencia de una región a otra en carecer de todos estos indicios, que es otro argumento bien fuerte; porque no hay duda sino que en las partes que aquellas flotas más frecuentaron con su contratación, hubieran quedado algunas señales y memorias que en las otras; porque no pudiera dejar de pegárseles algo a los naturales dellas de la comunicación y trato con los hebreos, y no pasa así, sino que todos son unos, así los de tierras ricas como los de poblaciones; y los marítimos como los que habitan la tierra adentro. Y la misma fuerza tienen estos argumentos contra los que presumen responder que cuando las flotas de Salomón navegaban a esta tierra, no estaba ella poblada de otras gentes, sino que entonces la comenzaron a poblar los hebreos, que si conceden que ya de antes estaba poblada de indios.
results.translation:
...for traveling, and so that they might more easily transport from the mines to the ports the rich metals which they came from such distant lands to seek, caring nothing for other things?

Nor is it reasonable to attempt to resolve all these difficulties by saying that during that trade and navigation they brought to this land the same things that the Spaniards have brought, but that, as time passed, these came to be extinguished and wiped out. For the plants and animals brought by our people have spread so widely throughout this New World that in many places they grow so abundantly without human industry or cultivation, that even if men were to apply all their intelligence and effort to eradicating and destroying them, they would not succeed; all the more so since men apply themselves not to destroying but to preserving and increasing them, driven by the interest and utility they derive from such things. In conclusion, we see no difference whatsoever from one region to another in the absence of all these signs, which is itself a very strong argument; for there is no doubt that in those parts most frequently visited by those fleets in their trade, some traces and memories would have remained, more so than in others; for it could not have failed that something of the communication and dealings with the Hebrews would have rubbed off on the native peoples of those lands. Yet this is not the case; rather, all the natives are alike — those of rich lands as well as those of populous ones, and the coastal peoples as well as those who dwell inland. And these arguments carry the same force against those who presume to answer that when Solomon's fleets navigated to this land, it was not yet populated by other peoples, but that it was the Hebrews who then began to settle it — unless they concede that it was already populated by Indians beforehand.

entities_mentioned.name: Salomón (Solomon)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Biblical King Solomon of Israel
entities_mentioned.notes: Cobo is engaging with the then-current hypothesis that Solomon's biblical fleet (the fleet of Ophir/Tarshish) had reached the Americas; he systematically refutes this claim on botanical, zoological, and ethnographic grounds.

entities_mentioned.name: hebreos (Hebrews)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Ancient Israelites / Jewish people
entities_mentioned.notes: Referenced as the proposed original settlers or traders who might have introduced Old World biota to the Americas; Cobo disputes this.

entities_mentioned.name: plantas y animales traídos por los nuestros (plants and animals brought by the Spaniards)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Various Old World species introduced to the Americas during the Spanish colonial period
entities_mentioned.notes: Cobo uses the rapid naturalization of Spanish-introduced species as evidence that any pre-Columbian introduction would have left comparable traces; no specific species named in this passage.

results.translator_notes: This segment is part of Cobo's larger ethnographic and historical argument against pre-Columbian contact between the Old World and the Americas, specifically the 'Ophir hypothesis.' The passage contains no botanical or pharmacological content; the schema fields for plant/drug/medical data are therefore not applicable here. The opening fragment ('ara caminar y con...') is the tail end of a preceding sentence, likely cut off by the segment boundary. OCR rendered 'conafo' as 'conato' (effort/endeavor) — corrected silently. The word 'qne' in the final clause is a clear OCR error for 'que' — corrected silently.
