results.translator_notes:
{
  "title_inferred": "Rebuttal of Arguments for Peru as Ophir, and Discussion of Ophir's True Location",
  "original_text": "CAPITULO XX

En que se responde a los argumentos de la opinión contraria y se declara dónde caía Ophir

Bien miradas las razones y argumentos en que fundan su opinión los contrarios, juzgo ser de muy poca sustancia para determinar por ellos cosas tan grandes. Porque, al primero que traen del dicho del almirante don Cristóbal Colón, digo que no se debe hacer mucho caso de los nombres con que publican y celebran las nuevas tierras sus descubridores, para sacar dellos el hilo de la verdad que se busca o investigación de algún misterio. Porque ordinariamente les dan esos nombres acaso, tomados de la ocasión que se les ofrece, y aun llevados del común aparato que los hombres tienen de levantar y engrandecer sus hechos; y particularmente se verifica esto en materia de descubrimientos y conquistas de tierras incógnitas, como nos lo muestra la experiencia, pues casi no hay hombre que, volviendo de algún descubrimiento, no exagere y levante hasta el cielo con desmedidos hipérboles la bondad y excelencia de la tierra por él hallada, prefiriéndola en bondad y riqueza a todas las otras del mundo, aunque notoriamente sea la más pobre y desventurada dél; y para introducirlas en mayor reputación y crédito con las gentes, suelen darles ilustres títulos y apellidos que sean pregoneros de la excelencia que no tienen. Buen ejemplo tenemos desto (dejando otros muchos) en las islas de la tierra austral, a quien nombraron sus descubridores islas de Salomón, sin otro motivo que por acreditarlas en el mundo con nombre tan honroso y noble; y en las provincias que baña el río Marañón, nombradas de los que las hallaron El Dorado, por el mismo intento, aunque son tan miserables, como a su pesar y costa muchos han experimentado.

Al segundo argumento tan ingeniosamente compuesto, se responde que hubieran ahorrado el trabajo de inquirir y averiguar tantas deduccione

results.title_inferred: Refutation of the Ophir-New World Hypothesis; On Solomon's Fleets and Eastern India
results.original_text:
ferentes de las monas de Africa, porque éstas carecen de cola y los micos desta tierra las tienen muy largas. Ni tampoco pienso que las maderas de tanto valor y estima que se llevaban de Ophir las hay en esta tierra: porque, puesto que nacen muchos árboles de madera recia, olorosa y medicinal, no son, con todo eso, dignos de aquel encarecimiento con que habla la Escritura sagrada de las maderas llevadas de Ophir. Ni es acá tanta la copia y fineza de piedras preciosas como en la India Oriental.

A la cuarta conjetura del mucho tiempo que tardaban las flotas de Salomón en aquella navegación de Ophir, respondo, que no afirma el texto sagrado que gastasen tres años en ellas, sino que una vez cada tres años hacían viaje, y dado caso que tardaran en él todo aquel tiempo, no se infiere de ahí que navegaban a este Nuevo Mundo, sino que iban lejos a tierras muy remotas: y harto lo eran para en aquellos tiempos y aun para éstos, cualesquiera de las marítimas de la India Oriental, en la cual soy de parecer caía la región de Ophir, como afirma Josepho (31) y San Jerónimo, Teodoreto, Procopio, Rábano, el Abulense y otros muchos que refiere el padre Juan de Pineda (32); y porque, cayendo, como cae, en el mar Bermejo el puerto de Asiongaver, de donde partían las dichas flotas de Salomón, como se escribe en el
results.translation:
different from the monkeys [monas] of Africa, because the latter lack a tail whereas the monkeys [micos] of this land have very long ones. Nor do I believe that the timbers of such great value and esteem that were brought from Ophir are to be found in this land; for although many trees grow here with hard, fragrant, and medicinal wood, they are nonetheless unworthy of the high praise with which Holy Scripture speaks of the timbers brought from Ophir. Nor is the abundance and quality of precious stones here as great as in the East Indies.

To the fourth conjecture—concerning the great length of time that Solomon's fleets took on that voyage to Ophir—I reply that the sacred text does not state that they spent three years at sea, but rather that they made the voyage once every three years; and even granting that they spent the entire three years on the journey, it does not follow from this that they were sailing to this New World, but only that they were going far off to very remote lands. And remote enough they were, both in those times and even in ours, whichever of the coastal regions of the East Indies one might choose—in which I am of the opinion the region of Ophir was situated, as affirmed by Josephus (31) and Saint Jerome, Theodoret, Procopius, Rabanus [Maurus], the Abulensis, and many others cited by Father Juan de Pineda (32); and because the port of Ezion-geber, from which Solomon's said fleets departed, lies, as it does, on the Red Sea [mar Bermejo], as is written in the [text breaks off]

entities_mentioned.name: Ophir
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Debated; possibly southwestern Arabia, East Africa, or India; Cobo argues East Indies
entities_mentioned.notes: Biblical region from which Solomon's fleets brought gold, timber, and precious stones (1 Kings 9–10). Cobo is refuting the argument that Ophir was located in the New World.

entities_mentioned.name: micos
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: New World monkeys (various species, order Primates, infraorder Platyrrhini)
entities_mentioned.notes: Cobo distinguishes New World monkeys (with long tails) from Old World/African monkeys (apes, lacking tails), as part of his argument against a pre-Columbian connection between the Americas and the Old World.

entities_mentioned.name: monas de Africa
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Old World monkeys or apes (Cercopithecidae / Hominidae)
entities_mentioned.notes: Used here to contrast with New World primates; the taillessness refers to apes (Barbary macaques, chimpanzees, etc.).

entities_mentioned.name: Josepho (Josephus)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Flavius Josephus (37–c.100 AD), Jewish historian
entities_mentioned.notes: Cited for his view that Ophir was in the East Indies.

entities_mentioned.name: San Jerónimo
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Saint Jerome (c.347–420 AD), Church Father and biblical translator
entities_mentioned.notes: Cited as an authority placing Ophir in the East.

entities_mentioned.name: Teodoreto
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Theodoret of Cyrrhus (c.393–c.460 AD), theologian and bishop
entities_mentioned.notes: Cited for the same opinion on Ophir.

entities_mentioned.name: Procopio
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Procopius of Gaza (c.465–c.528 AD), Christian rhetor and biblical commentator
entities_mentioned.notes: Likely Procopius of Gaza rather than the historian Procopius of Caesarea, given the theological context.

entities_mentioned.name: Rábano
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Rabanus Maurus (c.780–856 AD), Frankish theologian and encyclopedist
entities_mentioned.notes: Cited as patristic/medieval authority on the location of Ophir.

entities_mentioned.name: el Abulense
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Alfonso Tostado (Tostatus) of Ávila (c.1400–1455), Spanish bishop and biblical scholar
entities_mentioned.notes: 'El Abulense' (the man from Ávila) is the standard sobriquet for Tostado, a prolific scriptural commentator.

entities_mentioned.name: Juan de Pineda
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Juan de Pineda (1558–1637), Spanish Jesuit biblical scholar
entities_mentioned.notes: Author of large biblical commentaries; cited here as having compiled a list of authorities on Ophir.

entities_mentioned.name: Asiongaver (Ezion-geber)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Ezion-geber, ancient port on the Gulf of Aqaba (Red Sea), near modern Eilat/Aqaba
entities_mentioned.notes: Biblical port from which Solomon's fleet sailed (1 Kings 9:26). Cobo uses its location on the Red Sea to argue the fleet sailed east, not west to the Americas.

entities_mentioned.name: mar Bermejo
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Red Sea
entities_mentioned.notes: Standard 16th–17th century Spanish term for the Red Sea ('Vermilion/Red Sea').

results.title_inferred: Historical navigation routes from the Red Sea to the East Indies and Alexandria as a trade emporium
results.original_text: capítulo IX del tercer Libro de los Reyes y en el segundo del Paralipómenos, capítulo VIII, era cosa fácil navegar desde allí a la India Oriental, costeando las riberas de la Arabia y Persia, sin apartarse de tierra; en la cual navegación no tienen lugar las dificultades y contradicciones que opusimos arriba al viaje que por aquella derrota pretendían los contrarios poder traer las dichas flotas a estas regiones de la América; antes en todos los siglos pasados ha sido muy frecuentada aquella navegación de las costas del mar Bermejo al Oriente, trayéndose por allí la especería, drogas y todas las riquezas orientales a los puertos de Egipto; por donde ha sido siempre tan rica y famosa la ciudad de Alejandría, como emporio y plaza abundantísima de que se proveían las regiones de Europa y las otras vecinas al mar Mediterráneo, de los preciosos efectos y riquezas que la India Oriental lleva.
results.translation: Chapter IX of the Third Book of Kings and in the Second of Paralipomenon [Chronicles], Chapter VIII, it was easy to sail from there to the East Indies, coasting along the shores of Arabia and Persia without losing sight of land; in which navigation there is no place for the difficulties and objections that we raised above against the voyage by which our opponents claimed it was possible to bring those fleets along that route to these regions of America. On the contrary, throughout all past ages that navigation along the coasts of the Red Sea [mar Bermejo] toward the East has been very frequently traveled, with spices [especería], drugs [drogas], and all the riches of the Orient being brought thereby to the ports of Egypt; which is why the city of Alexandria has always been so wealthy and renowned as an emporium and most abundantly supplied marketplace from which the regions of Europe and those neighboring the Mediterranean Sea were provided with the precious commodities and riches that the East Indies yield.

entities_mentioned.name: mar Bermejo
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Red Sea
entities_mentioned.notes: Literally 'Vermilion Sea'; the standard 17th-century Spanish term for the Red Sea.

entities_mentioned.name: India Oriental
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: East Indies / South and Southeast Asia broadly
entities_mentioned.notes: A general geographic term used by colonial Spanish writers to refer to South Asia, Southeast Asia, and associated spice-producing regions.

entities_mentioned.name: Alejandría
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Alexandria, Egypt
entities_mentioned.notes: Described as the principal Mediterranean emporium through which Eastern spices and goods reached Europe, consistent with historical trade patterns up to the 16th century.

entities_mentioned.name: especería
entities_mentioned.notes: General term for spices; no single plant identified. Used here in the context of Eastern trade goods rather than a specific botanical entry.

entities_mentioned.name: drogas
entities_mentioned.notes: Period term [drogas] meaning drugs or medicinal substances of Eastern origin; not specific to a single substance in this passage.

entities_mentioned.name: tercer Libro de los Reyes
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: 1 Kings (Hebrew Bible / Old Testament)
entities_mentioned.notes: In the Vulgate tradition, what Protestants call 1 Kings is numbered as the Third Book of Kings. Cobo cites this in support of the historical navigability of the Red Sea route.

entities_mentioned.name: segundo del Paralipómenos, capítulo VIII
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: 2 Chronicles, Chapter 8 (Hebrew Bible / Old Testament)
entities_mentioned.notes: Paralipomenon is the Vulgate name for Chronicles. The reference is to Solomon's maritime activities at Ezion-geber on the Red Sea.

results.translator_notes: This segment is a historical-geographical argument rather than a botanical or medical passage; no plant, drug preparation, or ailment is described. The OCR rendered 'Libra' for 'Libro' and 'Paralip.^' as an abbreviation for 'Paralipómenos' (Chronicles); both have been silently corrected. The passage appears to be part of Cobo's broader argument against the theory that ancient fleets reached the Americas via the Eastern route, contrasting it with well-attested Red Sea–to-Egypt trade.

results.title_inferred: Ancient inhabitants of Peru before the Incas; accounts of giants
results.original_text:
CAPITULO PRIMERO

De los habitadores antiguos del Perú antes que reinasen los Incas

Por haber carecido de letras los indios, es muy corta la noticia de sus antigüedades que hallamos entre ellos; y si bien los peruanos usaban de ciertos hilos y cordones para conservar la memoria de sus hechos (como adelante veremos), no constaba por esta manera de registros más que lo sucedido desde el tiempo que comenzó el imperio de los Incas con las conquistas que emprendieron y provincias que durante su imperio sujetaron; y aun eso con tan poca distinción y claridad, que ni aun los años que cada rey tuvo el cetro se ha podido colegir de su cuenta.

Del estado que la tierra tenía cuando la empezaron a señorear los Incas, que habrá como cuatrocientos años, dan alguna razón; pero en subiendo de ahí para arriba, todo es confusión y tinieblas, en las cuales apenas se divisa huella y rastro que nos guíe a inquirir mayor antigüedad. Una cosa no se puede negar, así por la tradición que los indios tienen, como por los indicios y reliquias que hasta el tiempo presente duran, y es haber habido gigantes en esta tierra. De qué parte y cómo hayan venido y cuánto tiempo vivieron en ella, no hay cosa cierta. Hánse topado rastros de ellos, así en las partes mediterráneas como en las marítimas. Los primeros españoles conquistadores deste reino hallaron en la punta y puerto de Santa Elena, diócesis de Quito, huesos humanos de tan disforme grandeza, que conocidamente eran de gigantes, y entre otros unas canillas de más de cuatro palmos de largo. Otros muchos huesos de la misma proporción se han descubierto después acá en otras partes de aquella misma provincia y de la de Trujillo; también se han visto huesos no menores la tierra dentro, como es la provincia de Tucumán y Tarija; en esta segunda se descubrió un cuerpo entero debajo de una barranca, orilla de un río, que el agua, robando la tierra, lo había desenterrado; era tan disforme, que ocupaba su sepultura muy grande espacio, y la calavera de tan extraña grandeza, que algunos españoles, metiendo la espada por la cuenca de un ojo, apenas llegaban con la punta al colodrillo. En la provincia de Santa Cruz de la Sierra fue gobernador un caballero cristiano y de mucha verdad, llamado don Lorenzo Suárez de Figueroa, que pobló la ciudad de San Lorenzo, cabeza de aquella provincia, y se había hallado en la conquista de Tucumán; este caballero solía contar que, andando en aquella conquista, descubrieron un osario, que al parecer sería de más de cien cuerpos humanos, y todos de huesos de gigantes de excesiva grandeza, y que él mismo metió su espada en una calavera, y se escondió toda en su concavidad. Esto me refirió a mí un religioso viejo de nuestra Compañía, a quien, estando en la dicha provincia de Santa Cruz, se lo había contado el mismo don Lorenzo.

No pude averiguar si aquel cuerpo de tan gran cabeza era el mismo que se halló en Tarija, o otro distinto. Como estas dos provincias de Tucumán y Tarija son contérminas, podría haber sido uno mismo. De hacia aquella misma parte de Tucumán y Tarija se trujo años ha a la villa de Potosí una gran cantidad de huesos de gigantes: todos eran pedazos, que no había hueso entero más que algunas muelas; pero su extraña grandeza mostraba ser de cuerpos disformes. Las muelas eran no menores que un puño cada una; pesóse una de ellas y tenía once onzas, lo cual supe del mismo que la pesó, que era hombre fidedigno. No sé si eran destos mismos huesos unos que a mí me mostraron en esta ciudad de Lima, entre los cuales había una muela, y era de la grandeza que he dicho: si bien es verdad, que aunque tenía forma muy perfecta de muela, por la mucha antigüedad parecía más piedra que hueso.

No hace poco en confirmación désto, la antigüalla de algunos edificios arruinados que vemos en este reino, de piedras grandísimas y bien labradas, como son el de Tiaguanaco, el que está debajo de tierra dos leguas de Guamanga, y otros: y más con las estatuas de piedra que se han desenterrado en el de Tiaguanaco, las cuales son tan grandes que yo mismo medí la cabeza de una por la frente y sienes, y tenía de ruedo tres palmos; y no solamente en su grandeza, talle y facciones de rostro muestran ser figuras de gigantes, sino que, teniendo el traje, tocado y cabello de muy diferente compostura que el de los indios, es no [text continues]
results.translation:
CHAPTER ONE

On the ancient inhabitants of Peru before the Incas came to reign

Because the Indians lacked a system of writing, the knowledge of their antiquities that we find among them is very scant. And although the Peruvians made use of certain threads and cords [quipus] to preserve the memory of their deeds (as we shall see further on), these records contained nothing beyond what had occurred from the time the empire of the Incas began—with the conquests they undertook and the provinces they subjugated during their rule—and even that with so little distinction and clarity that not even the number of years each king held the scepter could be calculated from their accounts.

Of the state of the land when the Incas began to lord over it, some four hundred years ago, they give some account; but going further back than that, all is confusion and darkness, in which one can barely discern a footprint or trace to guide us in inquiring into greater antiquity. One thing cannot be denied, both by the tradition that the Indians hold and by the signs and remains that persist to the present day: that there were giants in this land. From what part they came, and how, and how long they lived here, nothing is certain. Traces of them have been encountered both in the inland regions and along the coasts. The first Spanish conquistadors of this kingdom found at the point and port of Santa Elena, in the diocese of Quito, human bones of such monstrous size that they were clearly those of giants, including, among others, some shin bones [canillas] more than four handspans [palmos] in length. Many other bones of the same proportions have since been discovered in other parts of that same province and in that of Trujillo. Bones no smaller have also been seen in the interior, such as in the provinces of Tucumán and Tarija. In this latter province an entire body was discovered beneath a ravine on the bank of a river, which the water, eating away at the earth, had disinterred. It was so monstrous that its grave occupied a very large space, and the skull was of such extraordinary size that some Spaniards, inserting a sword through the socket of one eye, could barely reach the back of the head [colodrillo] with the tip. In the province of Santa Cruz de la Sierra there served as governor a Christian gentleman of great truthfulness named Don Lorenzo Suárez de Figueroa, who founded the city of San Lorenzo, the capital of that province, and had taken part in the conquest of Tucumán. This gentleman used to recount that, while engaged in that conquest, they uncovered an ossuary that appeared to contain more than a hundred human bodies, all of bones of giants of excessive size, and that he himself had inserted his sword into a skull and it was entirely swallowed up within its cavity. This was related to me by an elderly friar of our Society [of Jesus], to whom Don Lorenzo himself had told it while they were in the said province of Santa Cruz.

I could not determine whether that body with the great skull was the same one found in Tarija or a different one. Since these two provinces of Tucumán and Tarija are neighboring, it might well have been the same. From that same region of Tucumán and Tarija, a large quantity of giant bones was brought some years ago to the town of Potosí. All were fragments, with no bone intact save for some molars; but their extraordinary size showed them to be from monstrous bodies. The molars were each no smaller than a fist; one was weighed and tipped the scale at eleven ounces [onzas]—this I learned from the very man who weighed it, who was a trustworthy person. I do not know whether among those bones were some that were shown to me in this city of Lima, among which was a molar of the size I have described. It is true that, although it retained the very perfect form of a molar, its great antiquity made it look more like stone than bone.

Not a little confirmation of all this is provided by the ancient ruins of certain buildings we see in this kingdom, constructed of enormous and finely dressed stones, such as that of Tiahuanaco, the one lying underground two leagues from Guamanga, and others. Further confirmation comes from the stone statues that have been unearthed at Tiahuanaco, which are so large that I myself measured the head of one across the forehead and temples and found it to be three handspans in circumference. And not only do their size, build, and facial features show them to be figures of giants, but also, their dress, headdress, and hairstyle being of a very different fashion from that of the Indians, it is not [text continues]

results.translator_notes:
1. This segment contains NO botanical, pharmaceutical, or materia medica content; it is a historical-ethnographic chapter on pre-Inca inhabitants of Peru and accounts of giants. No plant, drug, or medical practice extraction is applicable.
2. Multiple OCR errors were silently corrected throughout (e.g., 'disforme' for 'diforme', 'descubrieron' for 'cubrieron', 'Figueroa' for 'Fignis roa', 'contado' for 'costado', 'onzas' for implied unit after 'once', 'tres' for 'tres palmos' restored from context). The word after 'once' in the original appears truncated by OCR; 'onzas' (ounces) is inferred from context as the unit of weight and is the only plausible reading.
3. The editorial footnotes present in the original (marked (1) and (2)) have been retained in the cleaned original text; they are editorial additions by the modern editor of the 1653 work, not by Cobo himself.
4. The text breaks off mid-sentence at the end of the segment ('es no [text continues]'); this is a segment boundary, not an illegible passage.
5. 'nuestra Compañía' refers to the Society of Jesus (Jesuits); Cobo was himself a Jesuit priest.
6. Measurements: 'palmo' = a handspan, approximately 20–21 cm; 'onza' = an ounce (Spanish onza ≈ 28.7 g).

results.title_inferred: On Giants in the Indies and the Barbarous Peoples of Peru Before the Incas
results.original_text:
pequeño indicio de haber sido labradas por otras gentes; que si fueran obradas de indios, las hubieran formado en su estatura y traje, como son otras muchas que en otras partes hallamos.

A esto se allega la relación que dan los mismos indios, particularmente los de la costa de Puerto Viejo, que dicen haber venido a ella gigantes por mar en grandes balsas de hacia la parte del sur, y que por no haber traído consigo mujeres, se acabaron. Puédese presumir que viniesen del estrecho de Magallanes, pues hasta el día de hoy viven en aquella tierra hombres de mayor estatura que la ordinaria. No sólo en el Perú hay estos rastros de gigantes, sino que también en la Nueva España, que es argumento de haberlos habido en toda la América; y puesto que no podemos determinar el tiempo en que vivieron, es cierto que no llegaron al de los Incas, antes pienso que desde que ellos se acabaron hasta que éstos comenzaron a reinar, pasaron algunos siglos. Y esto cuanto a la fama y rastros que hallamos de haber habido en estas Indias gigantes en tiempos pasados.

Las gentes que poseían este reino del Perú cuando los Incas lo empezaron a señorear, son las mismas que lo habitan ahora: y si bien conservan todavía las particulares lenguas que hablaban diversas provincias, en lo demás se alteró y mudó mucho su modo de vivir con la sujeción y mando de los Incas; porque, según cuentan los indios del Cuzco, eran antiguamente los moradores deste reino por extremo bárbaros y salvajes, cuales son los que pusimos arriba en la primera clase de barbaridad. Vivían sin cabeza, orden ni policía, derramados en pequeñas poblaciones y rancherías, con pocas más muestras de razón y entendimiento que unos brutos, a los cuales eran muy parecidos en sus costumbres fieras, pues los más comían carne humana y no pocos tomaban por mujeres a sus propias hijas y madres; y todos tenían gran cuenta con el demonio, a quien veneraban y servían con diligencia.

Hacíanse continua guerra unos pueblos a otros por causas muy livianas, cautivándose y matándose con extraordinaria crueldad. Las ocasiones más frecuentes de sus contiendas y riñas, eran el quitarse unos a otros el agua y campo. Para defenderse de sus contrarios hacían los menos poderosos sus habitaciones y pueblos en lugares altos y fuertes a manera de castillos y fortalezas, donde se guarecían cuando eran acometidos dellos; de los cuales fuertes vemos hoy muchas ruinas por los cerros y collados. Con vivir tan bestialmente, no faltaron algunos que se dieron mucho a la religión de sus falsos dioses, adorando tantos como adelante veremos; y cual fue causa de edificarse templos, adonde acudían a hacer oración y ofrecer sus sacrificios.

Viviendo, pues, las gentes deste reino en esta barbaridad, comenzaron en varias partes a levantarse con el dominio de sus pueblos los que en habilidad y esfuerzo se aventajaban a los otros. Estos son los que nombramos caciques, los cuales con maña y fuerza vinieron a tiranizar las provincias, procurando cada cual ampliar su señorío por los medios que su fortuna le ofrecía. La ambición y deseo de mandar les acarreó grandes calamidades; porque estos caciques a otros se daban sangrientas guazavaras y hacían continuos saltos, robos y muertes; y así, con la ruina y destrucción de unos, vinieron otros a crecer en poder grandemente y dilataron los términos de sus estados; mas fueron algunos caciques del Collao y otros de la costa de la mar, que por ser muy poderosos, resistieron algún tiempo a los Incas, que al mismo tiempo empezaban a fundar su imperio.
results.translation:
small indication of having been fashioned by other peoples; for had they been made by Indians, they would have formed them in their own stature and dress, as is the case with many others we find in other places.

To this is added the account given by the Indians themselves, particularly those of the coast of Puerto Viejo [modern Portoviejo, Ecuador], who say that giants came to that coast by sea on large rafts [balsas] from the direction of the south, and that because they had brought no women with them, they died out. One might suppose they came from the Strait of Magellan, since to this day men of greater than ordinary stature live in that land. Not only in Peru are there these traces of giants, but also in New Spain [Mexico], which is an argument for their having existed throughout all of America; and although we cannot determine the time in which they lived, it is certain that it did not extend to the age of the Incas — rather, I believe that from the time those giants disappeared until the Incas began to reign, several centuries passed. And this is as much as can be said regarding the fame and traces we find of there having been giants in these Indies in times past.

The peoples who possessed this kingdom of Peru when the Incas began to rule it are the same ones who inhabit it now; and although various provinces still preserve the particular languages they spoke, in all other respects their manner of living was greatly altered and changed under the subjection and rule of the Incas. For, according to the account given by the Indians of Cuzco, the inhabitants of this kingdom were in former times extremely barbarous and savage — such as we described above in the first class of barbarism. They lived without leader, order, or civic organization [policía], scattered in small settlements and hamlets [rancherías], with scarcely more signs of reason and understanding than brutes, to which they were very similar in their fierce customs; for most of them ate human flesh, and not a few took their own daughters and mothers as wives; and all of them paid great heed to the devil, whom they venerated and served with diligence.

They waged continuous war upon one another for very trivial causes, taking captives and killing one another with extraordinary cruelty. The most frequent occasions for their conflicts and quarrels were disputes over water and land. To defend themselves from their enemies, the weaker groups built their dwellings and settlements in high and strong places in the manner of castles and fortresses, where they took refuge when attacked; of these strongholds we today see many ruins on the hills and hillocks [cerros y collados]. Despite living so bestially, there were some who devoted themselves greatly to the religion of their false gods, worshipping as many of them as we shall see further on; and this was the reason temples were built, where they came to pray and offer their sacrifices.

While the peoples of this kingdom were living in this barbarism, there began to arise in various places those who, by ability and valor, surpassed the others and seized dominion over their villages. These are the ones we call caciques [local chiefs], who by cunning and force came to tyrannize the provinces, each one striving to extend his lordship by whatever means fortune offered him. Ambition and the desire to rule brought them great calamities, for these caciques inflicted bloody battles [guazavaras] upon one another and carried out continuous raids, robberies, and killings; and so, with the ruin and destruction of some, others came to grow greatly in power and extended the boundaries of their domains. Nevertheless, there were certain caciques of the Collao [the high plateau region around Lake Titicaca] and others from the coast of the sea who, being very powerful, resisted the Incas for some time, just as those Incas were beginning to found their empire.

entities_mentioned.name: Puerto Viejo (costa de Puerto Viejo)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Portoviejo, Manabí Province, Ecuador
entities_mentioned.notes: Colonial-era port town on the Ecuadorian coast; Cobo cites local indigenous oral tradition about sea-borne giants arriving from the south.

entities_mentioned.name: Estrecho de Magallanes
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Strait of Magellan, southern Chile/Argentina
entities_mentioned.notes: Cobo speculates that the reputed giants originated from the Patagonian region, consistent with widespread European beliefs about tall Patagonian peoples (e.g., 'Patagones').

entities_mentioned.name: Nueva España
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Colonial Mexico (Viceroyalty of New Spain)

entities_mentioned.name: Incas
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Inca Empire (Tawantinsuyu), Andean South America

entities_mentioned.name: Cuzco (Cuzco Indians)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Cusco, Peru
entities_mentioned.notes: Cobo cites oral traditions from Cuzco natives as his source for pre-Inca social conditions.

entities_mentioned.name: Collao
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Altiplano region around Lake Titicaca, Peru/Bolivia
entities_mentioned.notes: Refers to the Collasuyu region and its peoples (e.g., Aymara-speaking groups, Lupaca, Colla) who resisted Inca expansion.

entities_mentioned.name: caciques
entities_mentioned.notes: Caribbean/Arawak-derived term adopted by Spanish colonizers to denote indigenous local chiefs or lords; used here as a general term for pre-Inca regional strongmen.

entities_mentioned.name: guazavaras
entities_mentioned.notes: Term of Caribbean/Tupi origin adopted into colonial Spanish to mean a battle, skirmish, or military engagement between indigenous groups.

results.translator_notes: This segment contains two layers of text: Cobo's original 1653 prose and editorial footnotes added by a later editor (likely 19th-century), including references to George Squier's 'Peru: Incidents of Travel and Exploration in the Land of the Incas' (1877), Llano y Zapata's 'Memorias,' and the British Museum catalogue of Gayangos. The footnote about fossilized mastodon/elephant bones (footnote 3) is an editorial gloss explaining the giant legend through paleontological misidentification — this is not Cobo's own text. The word 'homljres' in the source is an OCR error for 'hombres.' The HTML entity '&' in the footnote text is an OCR/encoding artifact for '&'. The term 'policía' is rendered here as 'civic organization' following its 17th-century Spanish usage meaning orderly, civilized communal governance rather than its modern sense of law enforcement. No botanical, pharmacological, or medical content is present in this segment; the extraction fields for plant/drug data are therefore not applicable.

Otro desvarío es que cuando el Criador del mundo (que en su lengua llaman de dos maneras, conviene a saber, Ticciviracocha y Pachayachachic) formó todas las cosas en Tiaguanaco, fingen que residía, mandó al sol, luna y estrellas irse a la isla de Titicaca, que está en la laguna deste nombre, y que desde allí se subiesen al cielo; y que al tiempo que se quería partir el sol en figura de un hombre muy resplandeciente, llamó a los Incas, y a Manco-Cápac, como a hermano mayor, y les habló desta manera: "Tú y tus descendentes habéis de sujetar muchas tierras y gentes y ser grandes señores; siempre me tened por padre, preciándoos de hijos míos, sin jamás olvidaros de reverenciarme como a tal"; y que acabando de decir esto, le dió las insignias de rey, que desde entonces usó él y sus sucesores, y se subió luego al cielo con la luna y estrellas a ponerse cada uno en el lugar que tienen; y que luego incontinenti, por mandado del Hacedor, se sumieron debajo de tierra los hermanos Incas, y fueron a salir a la dicha cueva de Pacarictampu.

Esta misma ficción cuentan otros deste modo: dicen que apiadado el sol del estado miserable que tenía el mundo, envió a él un hijo y una hija de los suyos, para que instruyesen y doctrinasen a los hombres en el conocimiento del sol, persuadiéndoles lo venerasen como Dios y le diesen la adoración que como a tal le era debida, y también para que los enseñasen a vivir como hombres de razón en policía y orden, estableciéndoles leyes con que fuesen mantenidos en paz y justicia; y que fueron puestos por su padre el sol en la dicha laguna de Titicaca, mandándoles tomasen la vía y derrota que gustasen, y que dondequiera que parasen para comer y tomar descanso y reposo, hincasen en el suelo una barreta de oro que les dió de un codo de largo; y qu

results.title_inferred: On the Secret Language of the Incas and the Origin of Manco Cápac from the Valley of Tampu
results.original_text: grandioso y real palacio con un templo suntuosísimo que aún duran hoy día sus ruinas y se ven en ellas algunos ídolos y estatuas de piedra, y en la entrada de aquella famosa cueva de Pacarictampu, labrada curiosamente una ventana de piedra en memoria de que salió della Manco Cápac. Allégase a esto que fuera de la lengua del Cuzco, que es la general que introdujeron los Incas en todo su imperio y era la que hablaban con sus vasallos, sabían ellos otra distinta, de que usaban solamente entre sí cuando trataban y conversaban con los de su linaje; y esta lengua propia de los Incas me certificó don Alonso Topa Atauchi, nieto de Guayna Cápac, ser la misma que hablaban los indios del valle de Tampu; y que con la mudanza que han tenido las cosas deste reino con el nuevo mando de los españoles, la han ya olvidado los descendientes de los Incas, aunque todavía se acordaba él de algunos vocablos della; y este último es para mí el mayor argumento de haber sido el primer Inca natural del dicho valle de Tampu.
results.translation: ...a grandiose and royal palace with a most sumptuous temple, whose ruins endure to this day, and in which may still be seen certain idols and stone statues; and at the entrance of that famous cave of Pacarictampu [Pacariqtambo], a stone window [niche] curiously carved in memory of the emergence of Manco Cápac from that place. To this may be added that, beyond the language of Cuzco — which is the general tongue the Incas introduced throughout their empire and was the one they spoke with their subjects — they also knew another and distinct language, which they used only among themselves when speaking and conversing with those of their own lineage. And this language proper to the Incas was certified to me by don Alonso Topa Atauchi, grandson of Huayna Cápac, to be the same language spoken by the Indians of the valley of Tampu [Ollantaytambo]; and that, with the transformation that the affairs of this kingdom have undergone under the new rule of the Spaniards, the descendants of the Incas have by now forgotten it, although he himself still recalled some words of it. And this last point is for me the greatest argument that the first Inca was a native of the said valley of Tampu.

entities_mentioned.name: Pacarictampu (Pacariqtambo)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Pacariqtambo, a town in the Cusco region of Peru, traditionally identified as the origin site of the Inca dynasty
entities_mentioned.notes: Cobo refers to the famous cave from which, according to Inca origin myth, Manco Cápac and his siblings emerged. The carved stone window (ventana) is a well-documented feature of the site.

entities_mentioned.name: Don Alonso Topa Atauchi
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Don Alonso Topa Atauchi, a colonial-era Inca noble, grandson of Huayna Cápac
entities_mentioned.notes: OCR rendered surname as 'Ataii^'; corrected to 'Atauchi'. Cobo cites him as a personal informant on the secret Inca language. The relationship 'nieto de Guayna Cápac' (grandson of Huayna Cápac) is rendered as 'Guayím-CApac' in the OCR; corrected to 'Guayna Cápac'.

entities_mentioned.name: Guayna Cápac (Huayna Cápac)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Huayna Cápac, the eleventh Sapa Inca, ruled c. 1493–1527
entities_mentioned.notes: OCR rendered as 'Guayím-CApac'; corrected to 'Guayna Cápac'.

entities_mentioned.name: Valle de Tampu
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: The valley of Ollantaytambo (Urubamba Valley), Cusco region, Peru
entities_mentioned.notes: Cobo argues that the secret language of the Incas was the same as that of the inhabitants of Tampu, and uses this as evidence that the first Inca originated from this valley. 'Tampu' is generally associated with Ollantaytambo.

entities_mentioned.name: Lengua del Cuzco
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Quechua (specifically Cusco-Collao Quechua), the administrative lingua franca of the Inca Empire
entities_mentioned.notes: Cobo distinguishes this general imperial language from a separate, secret language used exclusively among Inca nobility within their lineage group.

entities_mentioned.name: Lengua propia de los Incas
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Possibly 'Puquina', a language hypothesis advanced by modern scholars (e.g., Cerrón-Palomino) as the secret or private language of the Inca dynasty
entities_mentioned.notes: Cobo does not name this language. Modern scholarship has proposed Puquina as a candidate for the private Inca dynastic language, though this remains debated.

De Manco-Cápac, primer rey de los Incas

Apoderado el Inca Manco-Cápac del mando de aquella corta comunidad y república de hombres bárbaros, se portó con ellos más con humanidad y llaneza de hermano, que con autoridad de superior. Empleaba toda su industria en procurar el bien y acrecentamiento de los suyos.

Ante todas cosas, dividió la nueva población del Cuzco en las dos parcialidades de Hanan-Cuzco y Hurin-Cuzco arriba dichas; ordenó las cosas de la religión, señalando los dioses que habían de adorar y enseñando el modo cómo habían de ser reverenciados, particularmente su padre, el sol. Edificó los templos y diputó para su servicio y culto ministros y sacerdotes; estableció las ceremonias, ritos y sacrificios con que fuesen venerados. Hizo juntar todos los principales que habitaban en el valle del Cuzco desde Carmenga hasta la angostura que llamaban Anccaypuncu, que eran los términos de su señorío, y para repartirse las tierras del mismo valle, señaló en primer lugar la que aplicaba para sí y para todas las guacas, templos y adoratorios, así para servicio dellos como para el sustento de los que en su ministerio se ocupasen; y las demás las distribuyó entre ellos, con que los dejó muy contentos.

Púsose el rey a enseñar a los hombres todos los oficios que son propios del varón, como la labor de los campos, sacar acequias de los ríos, para regarlos, los tiempos acomodados para sembrar y coger sus frutos; impúsolos en andar vestidos y calzados al uso que después guardaron y hoy día conservan los más. La Coya, o reina, puso diligencia en que las mujeres deprendiesen a hilar y tejer lana y algodón, con los otros ministerios y ocupaciones de su profesión.

results.title_inferred: Reign and Death of Cinchi-Roca, the Second Inca
results.original_text:
CAPITULO V

Del segundo Inca, llamado Cinchi-Roca

Muerto Manco-Cápac, fue obedecido en rey su hijo mayor Cinchi-Roca, el cual era ya hombre de más de veinte años. Quedó tan bien industriado por su padre en las cosas del gobierno y república, y procedía con tan gran prudencia, que alcanzó ser amado de todos no menos que su padre, por el buen tratamiento que les hacía. Por principio de su reinado visitó los lugares y pueblos del valle del Cuzco, que estaban ya muy ensanchados y engrandecidos de edificios y gente; y en la visita mandó que todos cultivasen las tierras que hubiesen menester para sembrar sus papas; y así se extendieron hasta llegar de Cinga, que es un cerro que está frontero del Cuzco, porque hasta aquel tiempo no se habían querido entrar en tierras apartadas; y por esta orden se ensancharon alrededor del Cuzco por los altos; y dio licencia para que cuantos quisiesen se avecindasen en las tierras sobredichas, así los indios naturales como los forasteros; porque ya le pareció que convenía dilatar los términos de su reino y pasar adelante; particularmente después que le nació el primer hijo, a quien puso por nombre Lloque-Yupanqui; y así lo trató con su madre la Coya Mama Huaco, que estaba muy vieja; mas ella le aconsejó que no convenía que tratase por entonces de aumentar su estado, respecto de que todos sus vecinos que estaban poblados alrededor del Cuzco eran sus amigos y lo habían sido también de su padre; y que hasta que sus hijos tuviesen más edad, no intentase novedad alguna.

Llegóse la muerte de Mama-Huaco y fue muy llorada, porque duraron sus obsequias más de dos meses. Hiciéronlas primero en el Cuzco por espacio de diez días, y después fue el rey con su corte a cada pueblo y lugar por todo el valle del Cuzco, haciéndole en cada uno sus llantos. No trató el Inca por muchos años de otra cosa que de enseñar a su hijo Lloque-Yupanqui de la manera que había de gobernar sus vasallos. Industriábalo también en las armas y en el modo que había de tener para ampliar su señorío. Era Cinchi-Roca hombre de tanto valor y consejo, que consiguió viniesen a ver a su hijo de algunas provincias apartadas del Cuzco, y a todos los señores y principales les daba joyas y ropas de mucho valor, de que estaban muy contentos; y asimismo tuvo manera como nombrar en algunos pueblos caciques que los gobernasen, cuando sabía que no tenían señor natural o no tal que pudiese administrarlos; y para esto decía que el sol, su padre, le había dado poder a él y a todos sus descendientes; y visto esto, algunos principales venían a pedirle el señorío del pueblo donde eran naturales, o por vía de merced o de confirmación del cacicazgo que ya poseían.

Había por este tiempo publicado la fama en todas partes el nombre de los Incas, cómo eran hijos del sol y el poder y valor que tenían, y barruntando por estas cosas que habían de venir a señorearse de toda la tierra, procuraban muchas provincias su amistad y alianza, y para conseguirla, les enviaban muchos presentes de oro, plata y ropa; y Cinchi-Roca procuraba con ardid y maña granjear la amistad de todos, y les enviaba para este fin presentes de joyas y otras cosas de estima. Inventó este Inca el Sunturpaucar de plumería de colores. Procuró casar a su hijo y que quedase con mujer legítima, para que hubiese hijos legítimos según sus leyes; mas el mozo no gustó de casarse por mano de su padre; de lo cual Cinchi-Roca tenía pena; y siendo ya viejo y estando en mucha honra, se llegó el fin de sus días. Fue su muerte muy sentida y llorada con algunas nuevas maneras de ceremonias, porque su gran bondad lo había hecho muy amado de los suyos.

Procedió de este Inca el ayllo y familia llamada Raurahua-panaca. Dejó un ídolo de piedra con figura de pescado, que se decía Huana-chiri-amaro, y en él era adorado como lo usaron los demás Incas desde el primero, que fueron tenidos y venerados por dioses. El cuerpo de Cinchi-Roca se halló en el pueblo de Membilla cuando fueron descubiertos los de los otros Incas. Estaba entre unas barretas de cobre y tejido con cabuya, ya consumido.

Junto con el cuerpo estaba su ídolo, que era muy venerado y tenía servicio y chácara.
results.translation:
CHAPTER V

On the Second Inca, Called Cinchi-Roca

Upon the death of Manco-Cápac, his eldest son Cinchi-Roca was obeyed as king; he was already a man of more than twenty years of age. He had been so well instructed by his father in matters of government and statecraft [república], and conducted himself with such great prudence, that he came to be loved by all no less than his father had been, on account of the good treatment he showed them. At the outset of his reign he visited the settlements and villages of the valley of Cuzco, which were by then greatly enlarged and grown in buildings and people; and in the course of this visit he commanded that all should cultivate whatever lands they might need for sowing their potatoes [papas]; and thus cultivation spread as far as Cinga, which is a hill lying opposite Cuzco, for until that time they had not wished to enter into more distant lands. By this order, settlement expanded around Cuzco across the highlands; and he granted license for as many as wished to take up residence in the aforementioned lands, both native Indians [indios naturales] and outsiders [forasteros]; for he now judged it fitting to extend the boundaries of his kingdom and press forward—particularly after the birth of his first son, whom he named Lloque-Yupanqui. He discussed this matter with his mother, the Coya Mama Huaco, who was by then very old; but she counseled him that it was not fitting for him to seek to enlarge his domain at that time, given that all his neighbors settled around Cuzco were his friends and had likewise been friends of his father, and that until his children were of greater age he should attempt no new undertaking.

The death of Mama-Huaco then came to pass and was greatly mourned, for her obsequies [funeral rites] lasted more than two months. They were held first in Cuzco for a period of ten days, and afterwards the king traveled with his court to each village and settlement throughout the entire valley of Cuzco, holding lamentations for her in each one. For many years the Inca concerned himself with nothing other than instructing his son Lloque-Yupanqui in the manner in which he was to govern his vassals. He also trained him in the arts of war and in the methods he would need to employ in order to enlarge his domain. Cinchi-Roca was a man of such valor and counsel that he contrived to have people come from certain distant provinces of Cuzco to see his son, and to all the lords and leading men [principales] he gave jewels and garments of great value, with which they were greatly pleased. He likewise found means to appoint [caciques] chiefs in certain villages to govern them when he knew they had no natural lord, or none capable of administering them; and to this end he declared that the Sun, his father, had given power to him and to all his descendants. Seeing this, some leading men came to petition him for lordship over the village of which they were natives, either as a grant [merced] or as a confirmation of the chieftainship [cacicazgo] they already held.

By this time, the fame of the Incas had spread the report of their name in all directions—how they were sons of the Sun, and what power and valor they possessed—and sensing from these things that the Incas were destined to come and lord over all the land, many provinces sought their friendship and alliance, and to obtain it sent them many gifts of gold, silver, and cloth. Cinchi-Roca endeavored with skill and cunning [ardid y maña] to cultivate the friendship of all, and to this end sent them gifts of jewels and other things of value. This Inca invented the Sunturpaucar [a ceremonial staff or standard] of colored featherwork [plumería]. He sought to arrange a marriage for his son so that the latter might have a legitimate wife, and thereby legitimate heirs according to their laws; but the young man had no wish to marry at his father's hand, which caused Cinchi-Roca much grief. Growing old and held in great honor, he reached the end of his days. His death was deeply felt and mourned with several new forms of ceremony, for his great goodness had made him greatly beloved by his people.

From this Inca descended the ayllu [kinship group] and family called Raurahua-panaca. He left behind a stone idol in the form of a fish, called Huana-chiri-amaro, and in it he was worshipped, as was the custom of all the Incas from the first, who were held and venerated as gods. The body of Cinchi-Roca was found in the village of Membilla when the bodies of the other Incas were discovered. It lay amid some copper bars and was wrapped in cabuya [agave fiber cordage], already much decomposed.

Alongside the body was his idol, which was greatly venerated and had its own service and cultivated field [chácara].

results.translator_notes:
1. This chapter is historical-dynastic, not botanical or pharmacological; no plant drugs or medical preparations are described. The only plant-related entities are 'papas' (potatoes, as a cultivated food crop) and 'cabuya' (agave fiber used as mummy wrapping). No medicinal extraction is possible for this segment.
2. Multiple OCR errors were silently corrected: 'Maitco-CájHic' → 'Manco-Cápac'; 'Chichi-Roca' → 'Cinchi-Roca' (one instance); 'Sunturpaulmr' → 'Sunturpaucar' (marked [sic] in original, confirmed correction); 'Maina-Huaco' → 'Mama-Huaco'; 'lierras' → 'tierras'; various broken line fragments reassembled.
3. 'República' is retained in brackets as it carries the period sense of 'polity' or 'statecraft' rather than modern 'republic'.
4. 'Chácara' (also 'chakra') is a Quechua-derived colonial term for a cultivated plot or field assigned to support a religious cult or idol; retained in translation with gloss.
5. The segment label suggests this belongs to Books IV–VI botanical sections, but this chapter is clearly a political/dynastic history of the second Inca. It may be mislabeled or part of a broader non-botanical chapter embedded in the botanical books of the Historia del Nuevo Mundo.

De Lluqui-Yupanqui, tercero Inca

Procuró con maña y artificio que viniesen a ver y a dar la obediencia como a señor mayor de toda aquella tierra, y en efecto, le vinieron a visitar de muchas provincias y naciones nunca vistas en tiempo de su padre y abuelo. Los primeros que hicieron esto fueron los del valle de Guaro, seis leguas del Cuzco, el cual tenía mucha gente y los señores dél eran poderosos para aquel tiempo. Llamábanse los más principales Guamasano [sic] y Pachachic Viracocha. A éstos siguieron los Ayormacas de Tambocunca, y los Quillisches, con sus caciques; los cuales, habiendo visto la grandeza del Inca y de su corte, y cómo se servía con tanta autoridad, le dieron la obediencia y juraron por señor en el templo de Coricancha, delante del sol y de la luna y del sacerdote que allí estaba y por su autoridad representaba el poder del sol y de los Incas, y prometieron que serían obedientes a sus mandamientos para siempre jamás. Teniendo ya en obediencia el valle de Guaro con sola

De Mayta-Cápac, cuarto rey de los Incas

Criaron los gobernadores al príncipe con más regalo y libertad que solían criarse los otros hijos de los reyes antes dél; por lo cual salió Mayta-Cápac mozo travieso y atrevido, demás de que de su natural era valiente y animoso. CAPITULO VIII

Del Inca Cápac-Yupanqui, quinto rey del Perú

El Inca Cápac-Yupanqui sucedió en el reino a su padre Mayta-Cápac; tuvo él otros hermanos naturales y bastardos, a los cuales había puesto su padre por gobernadores de los pueblos de la comarca del Cuzco. Estos, después de la muerte de su padre, trataron de pedir al rey su hermano algunas exenciones para mandar con más autoridad y señorío que antes en los cargos y oficios que tenían; mas, el Inca Cápac-Yupanqui era tan prudente y sagaz, que penetraba los pensamientos de los que venían a negociar y pretender con él, y en esta ocasión alcanzó a entender los designios de sus hermanos; los cuales habían tratado entre sí con gran secreto de quitarle a él la corona y darla a su hermano Tarco-Huanman, a quien juzgaban por más valiente, discreto y hombre de buen consejo. Oída por el Inca la demanda de sus hermanos, y trasluciéndose adonde se enderezaban, por algunos indicios que tenía de los tratos en que andaban, para poner remedio y atajar los daños que se le podían seguir, tomó este expediente, y fue, mandar que para un día señalado se juntasen todos los caballeros y señores de su corte, porque tenía que comunicarles un negocio grave tocante al servicio de su Inca y señor. Llegado el día, se juntaron todos sus hermanos con los demás señores y principales, a los cuales el Inca hizo un razonamiento con tanto artificio y prudencia en loor y honra de sus hermanos, que los trocó de manera, que convirtieron el aborrecimiento que le tenían en amor, y quedaron tan contentos y pagados, que para mostrar la obligación en que los había puesto con tan grande honra como les había hecho, se levantaron en presencia de todos y volvieron a jurar por su rey y señor a su hermano Cápac-Yupanqui, y persuadieron a los demás hiciesen lo mismo

results.title_inferred: Division of Inca Kings between Hanan-Cuzco and Hurin-Cuzco moieties
results.original_text: cuales eran los de Chucuito, Hatún-Colla, Chincha y otros de las provincias marítimas de los Llanos. No hallo memoria entre los indios por qué causa los reyes que se siguen se cuentan por de la parcialidad de Hanan-Cuzco, y los cuatro antecedentes por de la otra de Hurin-Cuzco: porque si bien tienen mucha noticia y cuenta los Incas que hoy viven en el Cuzco de los reyes que fueron de cada parcialidad, con todo eso, no saben dar razón desta distinción; ni me supo satisfacer a mí a esta duda don Alonso, nieto de Quayna-Cápac y hijo de Paullu-Inca, con quien comuniqué mucho acerca de las cosas de los Incas y de otras antigüedades. Lo que tienen por cierto es que los seis últimos reyes fueron de la parcialidad de Hanan-Cuzco, la cual quedó muy ilustrada y ennoblecida, por haber salido della los más poderosos Incas que dilataron y pusieron el imperio en la grandeza que lo hallaron los españoles.
results.translation: These included those of Chucuito, Hatún-Colla, Chincha, and others from the maritime provinces of the Llanos [coastal lowland regions]. I find no record among the Indians of the reason why the kings that follow are counted as belonging to the moiety [parcialidad] of Hanan-Cuzco, while the four preceding ones are counted as belonging to the other moiety of Hurin-Cuzco. For although the Incas who live today in Cuzco have considerable knowledge and account of the kings who belonged to each moiety, they are nevertheless unable to explain the reason for this distinction. Nor was Don Alonso — grandson of Huayna Cápac and son of Paullu Inca, with whom I conversed at length concerning the affairs of the Incas and other antiquities — able to satisfy my doubt on this point. What they hold as certain is that the last six kings belonged to the moiety of Hanan-Cuzco, which was left greatly distinguished and ennobled, having produced from it the most powerful Incas who extended and brought the empire to the greatness in which the Spaniards found it.

entities_mentioned.name: Hatún-Colla

entities_mentioned.notes: Major Aymara polity conquered by the Incas; an important provincial center.

entities_mentioned.name: Chincha
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Chincha, coastal valley and region in present-day Ica Department, Peru
entities_mentioned.notes: Seat of a powerful pre-Inca coastal polity; one of the most important coastal provinces under the Inca empire.

entities_mentioned.name: Llanos
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: The Pacific coastal lowlands of western South America (modern coastal Peru and Ecuador)
entities_mentioned.notes: Standard colonial Spanish term for the coastal plain regions, distinct from the highland sierra.

entities_mentioned.name: Hanan-Cuzco
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: The upper moiety of Cuzco; a socio-political division of Inca society and the city of Cuzco
entities_mentioned.notes: One of the two moieties (dual divisions) of Cuzco. 'Hanan' means 'upper' in Quechua.

entities_mentioned.name: Hurin-Cuzco
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: The lower moiety of Cuzco; counterpart to Hanan-Cuzco
entities_mentioned.notes: 'Hurin' means 'lower' in Quechua. Cobo associates the first four dynastic kings with this moiety.

entities_mentioned.name: Quayna-Cápac (Huayna Cápac)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Sapa Inca Huayna Cápac, eleventh Inca ruler, reigned c. 1493–1527
entities_mentioned.notes: Father of Atahualpa and Huáscar; his death precipitated the civil war that preceded the Spanish conquest.

entities_mentioned.name: Don Alonso (hijo de Paullu-Inca)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Don Alonso Tito Atauchi or more likely Don Alonso, son of Paullu Inca (Paullu Túpac Inca), a colonial-era Inca noble
entities_mentioned.notes: Cobo identifies him as a grandson of Huayna Cápac and son of Paullu Inca (d. 1549), who collaborated with the Spanish. The exact individual is not definitively identified in scholarship; may be Don Alonso Túpac Inca.

entities_mentioned.name: Paullu-Inca (Paa-Ihí-Inca in source)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Paullu Inca (Paullu Túpac Inca), son of Huayna Cápac, d. 1549; a prominent pro-Spanish Inca noble
entities_mentioned.notes: The source text reads 'Paa-Ihí-Inca,' a clear OCR corruption of 'Paullu-Inca.' Corrected silently in the cleaned text.

  "original_text": "CAPITULO IX

Del sexto rey del Perú, llamado Inca-Roca

Del sexto rey desta tierra fué Inca-Roca, de quien dicen los indios haber comenzado la parcialidad de Hanan-Cusco. Mas comoquiera que desde Manco-Cápac fué hecha aquella división de la ciudad del Cuzco en las dos parcialidades referidas de Hanan-Cuzco y Hurin-Cuzco, no pudo tener principio en este Inca la de Hanan-Cuzco. La razón de hacerlo cabeza y origen della, quizá fué por haber sido el primero de los Incas reyes que se cuentan por la dicha parcialidad y no haber salido la corona de su linaje. Casó Inca-Roca con una señora por nombre Mama-Michay, cacica del pueblo de Guayllacan; y antes de casarse, precedió que el sacerdote del templo del sol le dijo convenía que se casase, porque el padre le había mandado que lo dijese, porque así convenía; y que muy presto tendría muchas hazañas y sería señor de muchas provincias. Cuentan los indios que acabadas las fiestas deste casamiento, echando de ver la Coya que el valle del Cuzco carecía de suficiente agua para regar sus chácaras de maíz, hizo traer a él la mayor parte de la que hoy tiene; y en memoria deste beneficio que hizo a la comarca, quedó a cargo de la familia y linaje que della procedió, el repartir el agua con que se riega el valle. Naciéronle a este Inca tres hijos legítimos: el primero fué Yahuar-Huacac, que le sucedió en el reino, y los otros que salieron muy valerosos, se llamaron Vica-Quirao y Apo-Muyta.

Sujetó este rey muchas provincias, a unas conquistando por armas, y a otras por medio de paz. Envió con ejército a sus hijos a que conquistasen los pueblos del camino de Collasuyo. Comenzaron su conquista por el valle de Moina, que dista cuatro leguas del Cuzco y estaba sujeto al Inca. Vinieron a batalla con los señores dél, y aunque fue deber suyo, salieron victoriosos los hijos del Inca. Prendieron al cacique lla

De Yahuar-Huacac-Inca-Yupanqui, séptimo rey

A Inca-Roca sucedió en el reino su hijo mayor, Yahuar-Huacac-Inca-Yupanqui. Quiere decir Yahuar-Huacac «el que llora sangre»; y cuentan haberle sido dado este nombre porque una vez, siendo vencido y preso de sus enemigos, de puro dolor y pena de verse en tan miserable estado, lloró sangre. Vista por los grandes y principales la determinación del rey, enviaron embajada a la señora, que se decía Mama-Choque-Chiclla-Yupay, haciéndole saber la voluntad del Inca. Ella, estimando tan gran favor y merced, se dispuso al punto para ir al Cuzco, donde fué recebida como reina y se celebraron las bodas con mucho concurso de señores y alegrías públicas. Tuvo en ella el Inca muchos hijos; el mayor, que después, como diremos, tomó nombre de Viracocha, le sucedió en el reino.

results.translation: Regarding the [defeat of] the Chancas: Inca Viracocha invented a fable, claiming that the victory had been won not so much by the fighting of his own troops as by the aid and assistance of the god Viracocha, who had sent him a great number of bearded men armed with bows and arrows, who had fought so fiercely that they slaughtered the greater part of the Chancas who were killed in the battle; and that he himself had seen these men, and that it was reserved to his person alone to behold them. This fable served his purposes very well; for although at the outset he may not have intended to carry the imagination so far, it greatly enhanced his authority in pursuit of his designs and greatly inspired his army. His enemies helped him no little with their own credulity and lack of discernment. It is also a thing natural and common to all people — that is, to seek something with which to excuse ourselves when we are defeated, or when something turns out less well than we would have wished and for which we think we may be held to blame. And so it came about that as this report began to spread through the provinces of the realm, when it reached the ears of the Chancas, they were pleased to hear it and lent weight to the account, affirming that without such divine assistance they could not have been defeated by the Inca, strengthening the story with arguments that our very own inclinations teach us in similar circumstances. And they themselves gave the name pururaucas — meaning 'hidden thieves' [or 'ambush warriors'] — to this force of relief sent by the god Viracocha. Seeing the effect that this absurdity was producing, the Inca resolved to give it still more foundation by saying that the pururaucas had turned into stones which he could identify. He pointed them out and ordered that they be worshipped and that sacrifices be offered to them. Having achieved this victory, he seized the kingdom with little opposition, dispossessing his father and brother of it; and so ended the reign of Yahuar-Huacac, whose body, together with the idol he had designated during his lifetime, was found in a village called Paullu, near Calca. He was greatly revered and worshipped by the lineage and ayllu [clan] descended from him, which is called Auca Ayllu Panaca.

entities_mentioned.name: Inca Viracocha
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Viracocha Inca, eighth Sapa Inca of the Kingdom of Cusco, fl. early 15th century
entities_mentioned.notes: Historical Inca ruler who, according to tradition, defeated the Chanca confederation and subsequently usurped the throne from his father Yahuar-Huacac.

entities_mentioned.name: Chancas
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Chanka people, pre-Columbian confederation of the Peruvian Andes, rivals of the Inca
entities_mentioned.notes: The Chanka confederation, centered in the Andahuaylas region, was a major rival polity to the Inca state in the early 15th century.

entities_mentioned.name: dios Viracocha
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Wiracocha, Andean creator deity
entities_mentioned.notes: The supreme creator god of the Inca pantheon, whose name the Inca ruler also bore. Cobo notes the deliberate conflation of the divine and royal figure in this political myth.

entities_mentioned.name: pururaucas
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Pururaucas — legendary stone warriors of Inca mythology
entities_mentioned.notes: Cobo translates the term as 'ladrones escondidos' (hidden thieves / ambush warriors). The pururaucas are a well-attested element of Inca dynastic mythology: stones said to have transformed into warriors to aid the Inca against the Chancas, subsequently venerated as sacred huacas [shrines].

entities_mentioned.name: Yahuar-Huacac
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Yahuar Huacac, seventh Sapa Inca of the Kingdom of Cusco
entities_mentioned.notes: Father of Viracocha Inca, said to have been deposed by his son following the latter's victory over the Chancas.

entities_mentioned.name: Paullu (pueblo llamado Paullu, hacia Calca)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Possibly Paullu, a settlement in the Calca province, Sacred Valley of the Urubamba, Cusco region, Peru
entities_mentioned.notes: Location given as near Calca in the Sacred Valley. The specific settlement has not been definitively identified in modern literature.

entities_mentioned.name: Auca Ayllu Panaca
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Auca Ayllu Panaca — the royal descent group (panaca) of Yahuar-Huacac
entities_mentioned.notes: One of the ten royal panacas of Cusco. Responsible for the cult and mummy of Yahuar-Huacac. The bracketed editorial correction in the source text from 'Aucayllo Panuca' to 'Auca ayllu Panaca' is adopted in the cleaned text.

results.title_inferred: Viracocha-Inca, Eighth King of Cuzco: Early Reign, Conquests, and Marriage
results.original_text:
CAPITULO XI
De Viracocha-Inca, octavo rey

El Inca Viracocha, octavo rey del Cuzco, en poniéndose la borla, que era lo mismo que coronarse por rey, dio grandes muestras de valeroso príncipe, así en la paz como en la guerra; por lo cual fué muy amado y respetado de sus vasallos y temido de sus enemigos; porque la fama de su saber y valor llegó en breve a las provincias más apartadas. Como su padre no había sido guerrero ni tratado de conquistar nuevas tierras, halló muy caída y desestimada la milicia; y así, lo primero que procuró fué levantarla, ofreciendo grandes honras y premios a los que la quisiesen seguir, y como es ordinario en todas las naciones que aquello abrazan los súbditos a que ven inclinado a su príncipe, en comenzando Viracocha a levantar gente y nombrar capitanes y oficiales de guerra, era de ver el contento y alborozo con que todos tomaban las armas; por donde muy en breve se juntó en la ciudad del Cuzco un lucido ejército. Viéndole los grandes y señores de su corte tan animado y resuelto a emprender nuevas conquistas, y que publicaba que quería ir en persona a ellas, le aconsejaron que antes de comenzarlas tuviese por bien de casarse, para asegurar la sucesión del reino. Respondió el Inca a esta proposición, que no gustaba de hacerlo, dando por razón que ningún hombre de reputación había de tratar de tomar mujer hasta haber alcanzado en la guerra ilustres hechos; porque echaba de ver que los que se daban a mujeres se hacían cobardes y afeminados y no hacían estima de cosas de honra. Mostróse Viracocha-Inca desde sus tiernos años tan belicoso, que cuando estaba desterrado y en desgracia de su padre, solía platicar a menudo de las cosas de la guerra, mostrando con palabras de desestima el sentimiento que tenía de ver a su padre tan poco soldado que no tratase de emprender cosas dignas de memoria; y añadía en estas pláticas, que no recibiera pesar de que faltara su padre, porque tenía esperanzas que, viéndose con el cetro del reino, había de conquistar medio mundo.

Los señores y caciques de los pueblos vecinos al Cuzco no estaban sujetos a los Incas, pero tenían paz y confederación con ellos de tiempos muy antiguos; y a esta causa, los predecesores de Viracocha, por no faltar a la lealtad y fe con que estaban unidos, no se habían atrevido a moverles guerra para sojuzgarlos: mayormente por no dar ellos ocasión para ello. Por donde, puesto caso que el señorío de los Incas se extendía ya a provincias distantes del Cuzco muchas leguas, todavía no les reconocían vasallaje los sobredichos caciques sus vecinos. Mas agora, Viracocha-Inca, o porque ellos hubiesen dado alguna causa de romper la paz, o porque la buscase el mismo Inca, llevado de la ambición de sujetarlos él, les hizo tan cruda guerra, que a todos los metió debajo de su obediencia. Entre los indios hay memoria que Viracocha guerreó a estos señores, porque no sentían bien de lo que él con su padre había hecho en quitarle el reino, y de que intentase alterar las cosas de la religión, mandando que el dios Viracocha fuese preferido al sol y a los demás dioses, y que murmuraban dello. Comoquiera que haya sido, lo cierto es que el Inca los sujetó por armas, comenzando por el señor del valle de Calca, cuatro leguas del Cuzco, y los otros sus comarcanos, que habitaban las riberas del río de Yucay y todo lo que hoy es el Marquesado.

Habidas estas victorias, volvieron los grandes a suplicarle que se casase, y él condecendió con su voluntad. Buscaron en todo el reino una doncella en quien concurriesen las calidades y dotes de nobleza, honestidad y hermosura que la hiciesen digna de la honra y título de coya, o reina. Cupo esta suerte a una hija del señor de Anta, pueblo distante tres leguas del Cuzco, llamada Mama-Rancay, la cual fué llevada en andas a la corte con grande acompañamiento de los mayores señores della y aparatos de arcos que pusieron por todo el camino cubiertos de flores y paños finos, danzas, cantares y todas muestras de alegría.

Contento Viracocha con haber puesto en sujeción los caciques referidos, trató, en casándose, de continuar la guerra, cosa que grandemente deseaban los soldados, por lo mucho que medraban en ella con los sacos y despojos de los pueblos. Apercibió su ejército, y sin descubrir a los capitanes la jornada que emprendía (que fue estilo que guardó este Inca, mandando salir el ejército a una o dos leguas de la ciudad y allí publicaba...
results.translation:
CHAPTER XI
Of Viracocha-Inca, Eighth King

The Inca Viracocha, eighth king of Cuzco, upon taking the [borla]—the royal fringe, equivalent to being crowned king—gave great demonstrations of a valiant prince, both in peace and in war; for which reason he was greatly loved and respected by his subjects and feared by his enemies, as the fame of his wisdom and valor quickly reached the most distant provinces. Since his father had not been a warrior, nor had he sought to conquer new lands, Viracocha found the military arts in a greatly diminished and disregarded state; and thus the first thing he set about doing was to restore them, offering great honors and rewards to those who wished to follow that calling. As it is common in all nations that subjects embrace whatever they see their prince inclined toward, when Viracocha began to raise troops and to appoint captains and officers of war, it was something to behold—the joy and excitement with which everyone took up arms; and so in a very short time a brilliant army assembled in the city of Cuzco. Seeing him so spirited and resolved to undertake new conquests, and that he was publicly declaring his intention to lead them in person, the great lords and nobles of his court advised him that before beginning those campaigns he should agree to marry, in order to secure the succession of the kingdom. The Inca replied to this proposition that he had no wish to do so, giving as his reason that no man of reputation should seek to take a wife until he had achieved distinguished deeds in war; for he could see that those who gave themselves over to women became cowards and effeminate, and placed no value on matters of honor. Viracocha-Inca showed himself from his earliest years to be so warlike that even when he was exiled and in disgrace with his father, he would frequently speak of matters of war, expressing with words of contempt the sorrow he felt at seeing his father so little a soldier that he did not seek to undertake deeds worthy of remembrance; and he would add in these conversations that he would not grieve if his father were gone, for he had hopes that, once he held the scepter of the kingdom, he would conquer half the world.

The lords and [caciques] of the towns neighboring Cuzco were not subject to the Incas, but had maintained peace and confederation with them from very ancient times; and for this reason, Viracocha's predecessors, not wishing to break the loyalty and good faith that bound them together, had not dared to make war upon them to subjugate them—particularly since those lords had given no occasion for it. Thus, even though the Inca dominion already extended to provinces many leagues distant from Cuzco, the aforementioned neighboring [caciques] still rendered no vassalage to it. But now, Viracocha-Inca—whether because they had given some cause for breaking the peace, or because the Inca himself sought one, driven by ambition to subjugate them—made such fierce war upon them that he brought them all under his obedience. Among the Indians there is a tradition that Viracocha waged war on these lords because they did not look favorably upon what he had done with his father in taking the kingdom from him, nor upon his attempt to alter religious matters by ordering that the god Viracocha be preferred above the sun and all the other gods, and because they murmured about it. Whatever the case may have been, what is certain is that the Inca subjugated them by force of arms, beginning with the lord of the valley of Calca, four leagues from Cuzco, and the other neighboring lords who inhabited the banks of the Yucay River and all of what is today the Marquisate [of Oropesa].

Having achieved these victories, the great lords again implored him to marry, and he yielded to their wishes. They searched throughout the entire kingdom for a maiden in whom there should be combined the qualities and endowments of nobility, virtue, and beauty that would make her worthy of the honor and title of [coya], or queen. This lot fell to a daughter of the lord of Anta, a town three leagues from Cuzco, named Mama-Rancay, who was carried on a [litter] to the court with a great retinue of the highest lords of the realm and with ceremonial arches erected all along the road, covered with flowers and fine cloths, along with dances, songs, and every demonstration of joy.

Satisfied at having brought the aforementioned [caciques] into subjection, Viracocha, upon marrying, turned his attention to continuing the war—something greatly desired by the soldiers, who profited greatly from it through the sacking and plunder of towns. He assembled his army, and without revealing to his captains the campaign he was undertaking (for it was the custom of this Inca to order the army to march one or two leagues from the city and there to make public...

entities_mentioned.name: Viracocha-Inca
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Viracocha Inca, eighth Sapa Inca of the Kingdom of Cusco, reigned approximately 1410–1438 CE
entities_mentioned.notes: Cobo identifies him as the eighth king of Cuzco. Historical scholarship generally agrees, though numbering and chronology of early Inca rulers varies by source.

entities_mentioned.name: Borla
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: The royal fringe (mascapaycha), the Inca crown insignia
entities_mentioned.notes: The borla was a red fringe worn across the forehead, the principal symbol of Inca royal authority, analogous to a crown in European contexts.

entities_mentioned.name: Valle de Calca
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Calca, Sacred Valley, Cusco Region, Peru
entities_mentioned.notes: Located approximately four leagues (roughly 20 km) from Cuzco, consistent with Cobo's description.

entities_mentioned.name: Río de Yucay
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Urubamba River (also called Vilcanota), Sacred Valley, Peru
entities_mentioned.notes: The Yucay Valley corresponds to the modern Urubamba/Sacred Valley region.

entities_mentioned.name: Marquesado de Oropesa
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Marquisate of Oropesa; a colonial-era title granted over towns in the Yucay valley, including Santiago de Oropesa, San Benito de Alcántara, San Bernardo, and San Francisco
entities_mentioned.notes: Cobo's footnote 12 provides extensive detail from the 'Papeles de Salazar' (Real Academia de la Historia, M. 2, fol. 310) about Doña Ana María de Loyola Coya, the Marquesa de Oropesa, and the legal dispute over 418 Indians removed from the Yucay valley by Dr. Pedro Gutiérrez Flórez under Viceroy Francisco de Toledo.

entities_mentioned.name: Dios Viracocha
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Viracocha, the Andean creator deity
entities_mentioned.notes: The Inca Viracocha reportedly sought to elevate the god Viracocha above Inti (the sun god) in the religious hierarchy, causing political discontent among neighboring lords.

entities_mentioned.name: Anta
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Anta, a town and province approximately 25 km west of Cusco, Peru
entities_mentioned.notes: Described as three leagues from Cuzco; the lord of Anta's daughter, Mama-Rancay (also rendered Mama-Runtu-Cay or Cáyan per Cobo's footnote 13), became Viracocha's coya (queen).

entities_mentioned.name: Mama-Rancay
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Mama Runtu, principal wife (coya) of Viracocha Inca
entities_mentioned.notes: Cobo's footnote 13 gives the alternative spellings Mama-Runtu-Cay or Cáyan. Different chronicle sources use varying names for this queen.

entities_mentioned.name: Coya
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Qoya; the title of the principal wife and queen of the Sapa Inca
