results.title_inferred: Inca Policy of Resettlement: The Mitimae System
results.original_text:
CAPITULO XXIII

Cómo disponían los Incas las tierras que de nuevo adquirían, plantando en ellos indios extraños, que llamaban mitimaes, y las diferencias que había dellos

Todo el imperio de los Incas, aunque tan extendido y compuesto de tantas y tan diversas naciones, era una sola república, regida por unas mismas leyes, fueros y costumbres, y observantes de una misma religión, ritos y ceremonias, puesto caso que cada una de esas provincias, antes de ser reducidas a su obediencia, tenía sus fueros y modo de vivir y gobernarse diferente; y por esta unión y uniformidad que en todas partes se guardaba, se ha de entender que lo que aquí dijéremos que introducían en las naciones que sujetaban, ese mismo modo de gobierno era el que se guardaba en su corte y en lo que de antes señoreaban.

Lo primero que hacían estos reyes en ganando una provincia, era sacar della seis o siete mil familias (más o menos, como les parecía, atento a la capacidad y disposición que hallaban) y llevarlas a otras partes de las provincias quietas y pacíficas, repartiéndolas por diversos pueblos; y en trueque dellas, metían otra tanta gente, que hacían salir de los lugares adonde asentaban los primeros, o de la parte que les parecía, y entre ellos muchos orejones de la sangre real. Estos tales que se avecindaban en las nuevas tierras, eran llamados mitimaes, que es tanto como decir hombres advenedizos y extraños a diferencia de los naturales; el cual nombre comprehendía no menos a los vasallos nuevos que a los antiguos que con ellos se trocaban, pues los unos y los otros pasaban de sus propias tierras a las extrañas; y el día de hoy lo entendemos desta suerte, llamando mitimaes a todos los advenedizos que están poblados en todas las provincias deste reino. Atendíase en esta transmigración a que los que se trasladaban, así de los recién conquistados como de los otros, no se mudasen a cualesquier tierras, así a poco más o menos, sino a las que fuesen del mismo temple y calidades o muy conformes a las que dejaban y en que se habían criado. Por manera que a los que eran naturales de tierra fría, los pasaban a tierras frías, y a los de tierras calientes, a tierras calientes; para que desta suerte sintiesen menos la mudanza de su naturaleza y se hallasen más sanos en las nuevas tierras, sin ser probados dellas, como lo fueran si los llevaran a las de contrario temperamento que las suyas. A los que así mudaba el Inca, los sacaba de la obediencia de sus caciques antiguos, mandándoles estuviesen sujetos a los de las tierras donde los plantaba; en las cuales mandaba dar a los unos y a los otros mitimaes sitios para labrar sus casas y tierras en que hiciesen sus chácaras y sementeras, y que quedasen por moradores perpetuos de los pueblos en que los ponía, guardando los usos y modo de vivir de los naturales dellos, salvo que se quedaban con el traje, divisas y señales de los de su nación y provincia; lo cual han conservado hasta el tiempo presente, en que por las cosas dichas conocemos en cada pueblo los que son naturales dél o mitimaes.

Introdujo el Inca esta mudanza de vecindad, para tener con más facilidad quieto y seguro su señorío; porque, considerando que la ciudad del Cuzco, cabeza dél, donde tenía su corte y residencia, estaba tan distante de las últimas provincias, en que había muchas naciones de gentes bárbaras y belicosas, juzgó que no podría de otro modo mantenerlas en paz y obediencia; y como era éste el fin principal a que se ordenaba este medio, mandaba que la mayor parte de los mitimaes que hacía ir a los pueblos recién sujetados, asentasen en las cabeceras de provincias, para que fuesen como guarnición y presidio, no por vía de sueldo ni por tiempo limitado, sino perpetuamente ellos y sus descendientes; y como a gente de guerra les daba algunos privilegios con que pareciesen más nobles, y les ordenaba que estuviesen siempre muy obedientes a cuanto sus capitanes y gobernadores les mandasen. Con esta traza e industria, si los naturales se rebelaban, estando estos mitimaes a devoción de los gobernadores, eran luego reducidos a la obediencia del Inca; y si los mitimaes tumultuaban y levantaban algún motín, eran reprimidos y castigados por los naturales; y así, mediante esta disposición de hacer estuviese avecindada la mayor parte de su gente los unos en tierras de los otros, tenía el Rey seguros sus estados que no se le rebelasen, y el trato y comercio de unas provincias con otras era más frecuente y toda la tierra más bien proveída de lo que era necesario. Otrosí, con esta traslación de sus vasallos de unas partes a otras, tiraban los Incas a que hubiese en todo su imperio similitud y conformidad en las cosas de la religión y gobierno político, y todas las naciones dél aprendiesen y hablasen la lengua del Cuzco, que por esta vía vino a ser general en todo el Perú; porque, con esta mudanza de domicilios, los nuevamente conquistados, que eran trasladados adentro del reino, aprendían todo esto con brevedad y sin pesadumbre ni apremio, y los vasallos antiguos que se avecindaban por mitimaes en lo que de nuevo se pacificaba, la enseñaban a los naturales, en que se ponía gran cuidado y eran compelidos a ello; porque a todos obligaban los Incas a recebir su lengua, leyes y religión, con todas las opiniones tocantes a estas cosas que estaban establecidas en el Cuzco, quitándoles los usos y ritos que antes tenían, o del todo o en parte, y haciéndoles recebir los suyos; y para con más efecto introducir y asentar estas cosas, ultra de la trasmutación de hombres dicha, hacían quitar a la provincia que conquistaban el ídolo principal que te[nían...]
results.translation:
CHAPTER XXIII

How the Incas Organized Newly Acquired Lands by Settling Them with Foreign Indians Called Mitimaes, and the Distinctions Among Them

The entire Inca empire, extensive as it was and composed of so many diverse nations, constituted a single commonwealth [república], governed by the same laws, statutes [fueros], and customs, and observing the same religion, rites, and ceremonies — notwithstanding that each of its provinces, before being brought under Inca obedience, had its own statutes and its own distinct manner of living and governing itself. By reason of this unity and uniformity maintained everywhere, it should be understood that whatever we here describe as having been introduced among the nations they subjected was the same mode of government that was observed at their court and in the territories they had ruled from earlier times.

The first thing these kings did upon conquering a province was to remove from it six or seven thousand families — more or fewer, as they saw fit, having regard to the capacity and condition they found there — and transfer them to other parts of the peaceful and pacified provinces, distributing them among various towns. In exchange for these, they brought in an equal number of people drawn from the places where the first group had settled, or from whatever part seemed appropriate, and among them many orejones ["big-ears," members] of the royal blood. Those who thus took up residence in the new lands were called mitimaes [mitimaes], a term meaning, in effect, men who are newcomers and strangers, as distinguished from the native inhabitants [naturales]. This name applied no less to the newly conquered subjects than to the old vassals who were exchanged with them, since both groups were moving from their own lands to foreign ones. To this day we understand the term in this sense, calling mitimaes all newcomers who are settled throughout the provinces of this kingdom. Care was taken in this resettlement that those who were transferred — whether recently conquered or otherwise — should not be moved to just any lands indiscriminately, but specifically to lands of the same climate [temple] and character, or very similar to those they were leaving and in which they had been raised. Thus those who were natives of cold lands were moved to cold lands, and those from warm lands to warm lands, so that they might feel the change from their native conditions as little as possible and remain in better health in the new lands, without being harmed by them, as they would have been had they been taken to regions of a contrary temperament to their own. Those whom the Inca thus relocated were removed from the authority of their former caciques [local lords] and ordered to be subject to the lords of the lands where they were planted. In these lands, he commanded that plots be given to both groups of mitimaes on which to build their houses and fields [chácaras and sementeras] in which to grow their crops, and that they should remain as permanent inhabitants [moradores perpetuos] of the towns where they were placed, observing the customs and way of life of the local inhabitants — except that they retained their own dress, insignia [divisas], and identifying marks of their own nation and province. This they have preserved to the present day, by which signs we can identify in each town those who are native to it and those who are mitimaes.

The Inca introduced this resettlement policy in order to maintain his dominion more easily in peace and security. Recognizing that the city of Cuzco, the capital, where he held his court and resided, was so distant from the outermost provinces — which contained many nations of barbarous and warlike peoples — he judged that he could not otherwise keep them in peace and obedience. Since this was the principal end to which this measure was directed, he ordered that the greater part of the mitimaes sent to newly subjected towns should settle in the provincial capitals [cabeceras de provincias], so that they would serve as a garrison [guarnición] and garrison force [presidio] — not for pay, nor for a fixed term, but perpetually, they and their descendants. As soldiers [gente de guerra], he granted them certain privileges that made them appear more distinguished, and he ordered that they should at all times be fully obedient to whatever their captains and governors commanded. By this scheme and contrivance, if the native inhabitants rebelled, these mitimaes, being devoted to the governors, would promptly reduce them to obedience to the Inca; and if the mitimaes rioted or stirred up some uprising, they were suppressed and punished by the native inhabitants. Thus, through this arrangement of having the greater part of his people settled in one another's lands, the King kept his dominions secure against rebellion, and the trade and commerce between provinces became more frequent, and all the land was better supplied with what was necessary.

Furthermore, by thus transferring his vassals from place to place, the Incas aimed to bring about throughout their empire a similarity and uniformity in matters of religion and political government, and to have all the nations within it learn and speak the language of Cuzco, which by this means came to be universal throughout Peru. For through this change of domiciles, the newly conquered — who were transferred into the interior of the kingdom — learned all these things quickly and without hardship or compulsion; while the old vassals who settled as mitimaes in the newly pacified regions taught it to the native inhabitants, and great care was taken in this and they were compelled to it. For the Incas obliged all to receive their language, laws, and religion, together with all the beliefs pertaining to these matters as established in Cuzco, removing from them the customs and rites they had previously held, either entirely or in part, and compelling them to adopt their own. And in order to introduce and establish these things more effectively, in addition to the aforementioned transfer of peoples, they caused the principal idol [ídolo principal] of each conquered province to be removed… [text breaks off]

entities_mentioned.name: mitimaes (also spelled mitimaes, mitinmaes)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Quechua: mitmaqkuna — Inca-era state-resettled colonists
entities_mentioned.notes: Cobo notes in a marginal gloss (likely an editor's footnote in the source) that his definition is 'not entirely exact,' and that they were also called 'llactayos.' The term refers to forcibly or administratively relocated populations used for political, military, and cultural integration purposes throughout Tawantinsuyu.

entities_mentioned.name: orejones
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Quechua: 'big-ears' — Inca nobility distinguished by large ear spools
entities_mentioned.notes: Term used by Spanish chroniclers for members of the Inca royal and noble class, recognizable by large ear ornaments.

entities_mentioned.name: caciques
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Local indigenous lords / curacas
entities_mentioned.notes: Arawak-derived term adopted by Spanish administrators to denote indigenous local leaders; equivalent to Quechua 'curaca' in Andean context.

entities_mentioned.name: chácaras / sementeras
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Agricultural plots / cultivated fields
entities_mentioned.notes: Chácaras (from Quechua 'chakra') are small cultivated plots; sementeras refers to sown fields or plantings generally.

entities_mentioned.name: Cuzco (Cusco)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Capital city of the Inca Empire, present-day Cusco, Peru

entities_mentioned.name: lengua del Cuzco
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Quechua (specifically the imperial Quechua variety, 'Runasimi')
entities_mentioned.notes: The administrative and prestige language imposed by the Incas across their empire; became the lingua franca of the Andes.

results.translator_notes:
1. This segment is entirely administrative/ethnographic in content and contains no botanical, pharmacological, or medical material; therefore no plant/drug/ailment extraction is applicable.
2. The bracketed footnote in the source ('Que llamaban llactayús. La definición de mitimaes que da el padre Cobo no es del todo exacta.') appears to be an editorial annotation added by a later editor of the Historia, not Cobo's own text; it has been preserved as a note here rather than integrated into the main translation.
3. The phrase 'Por mancipa que' in the source is an OCR corruption of 'Por manera que' ('in such a way that'), corrected silently.
4. The text ends mid-sentence ('el ídolo principal que te—') indicating the segment is cut off; this is flagged in both the cleaned original and translation.
5. 'Fueros' is retained in brackets in the translation as it denotes a specific legal concept (customary rights/statutes) without a single precise English equivalent.
6. 'Temple' (temperament/climate) is translated as 'climate' in context but flagged as a period humoral-geography term.

results.title_inferred: The Inca System of Mitimaes: Resettlement Policies, Colonial Redefinition, and Agricultural Colonies
results.original_text:
nían, y ponerlo en el Cuzco con el mismo aparato y culto que solía tener en ella, y que de todo eso le proveyesen sus naturales, ni más ni menos que cuando tenían en su provincia el dicho ídolo y huaca; y por razón desto, residían siempre en aquella ciudad y corte indios de todas las provincias del reino, ocupados en la guarda y ministerio de sus ídolos, adonde aprendían los usos y costumbres de los cortesanos; y como se mudaban por sus mitas y turnos, vueltos a su patria, guardaban y enseñaban a los suyos cuanto habían visto y aprendido en la corte.

No se reparaba, en estas mudanzas de mitimaes, en la distancia que había de sus tierras adonde los mandaban ir, aunque fuese muy grande; antes sucedía no pocas veces trasplantarlos de un extremo a otro del reino, otras trescientas y a cuatrocientas leguas más o menos, como al Príncipe se le antojaba; por donde vemos hoy en las provincias del Collao mitimaes naturales y originarios de las de Chinchaysuyu, y en éstas muchos indios de aquélla. Ello es cosa averiguada que estaban tan mezclados y revueltos los de distintas provincias, que apenas hay valle o pueblo en todo el Perú adonde no haya algún aylla y parcialidad de mitimaes.

A dos cosas principalmente tenía consideración el Inca en el mudar a sus súbditos: la primera (como queda dicho), a que no pasasen a temple contrario a el de su naturaleza, y la otra, a que las provincias todas de su Imperio estuviesen bien pobladas y bastecidas de mantenimientos y de todo lo necesario para la vida humana; y a este fin, metía gente de fuera en las poco pobladas, y de las que tenían más de la que se podía sustentar cómodamente, sacaba colonias que poblasen aquéllas; y éstos que por mandado del rey dejaban su patria y sujeción de sus caciques y se avecindaban en tierras extrañas, dando juntamente la obediencia a los caciques déllas, son los que propiamente se decían mitimaes en tiempo de los Incas. Pero después que los españoles ocuparon esta tierra, se ha extendido este nombre a otros que antiguamente no eran mitimaes propiamente, conviene a saber, a los indios que por orden de sus caciques y con licencia suya o del Inca, estaban de asiento fuera de sus pueblos y provincias, en distritos de otros caciques, aunque no sujetos a ellos, sino a los de la provincia de donde habían salido o eran oriundos. Para declaración desto, se ha de presuponer una costumbre antigua desta gente, y es que cuando alguna provincia era estéril de comidas, señaladamente de su pan, que era el maíz, y aparejada para otros aprovechamientos, cuales son todas las del Collao, que por su gran frialdad no llevan maíz ni otras semillas ni frutas de tierra templada, pero son abundantísimas de pastos y muy a propósito para criar ganados, y producen papas, de que hacen chuño, que les sirve de pan, y otras algunas raíces, a los habitantes, pues, de las tales provincias tenía el Inca señaladas tierras en los valles calientes de la costa de la mar, que les caen a un lado, y en esotra bajada de la serranía hacia los Andes, en los valles templados que por allí hay, en que sembrasen las cosas de que se carecía en sus pueblos; y por estar estos valles distantes de su tierra a veinte, a treinta más leguas, y no poder acudir a sembrarlos de comunidad, como lo hacían en lo restante del reino, tenían cuidado los caciques de dar a sus tiempos gente que lo hiciese, los cuales, cogida su cosecha, se tornaban con ella a sus pueblos. Fuera déstos, había, por orden del Inca, salida de cada pueblo, cierta cantidad de indios con sus mujeres y casas, para residir de asiento con sus hijos y descendientes, como residían, en los dichos valles, para entender en la guarda y beneficio de las chácaras de sus caciques y comunidades. Estos, aunque vivían en tierra ajena, eran sujetos a sus caciques y no a los de la tierra donde residían; pero entrados los españoles en este reino, al tiempo que la primera vez se visitó la tierra para repartirla y encomendarla en los pobladores, a estos indios que se hallaron en los valles dichos puestos en ellos por sus caciques para el efecto dicho, los contaron y repartieron con los naturales del distrito en que estaban, sacándolos de la obediencia de sus caciques antiguos y sujetándolos a aquellos en cuya tierra y jurisdicción habitaban; y consiguientemente los dieron en encomienda al mismo que repartieron el tal distrito y no al encomendero del cacicazgo de donde ellos eran naturales. A todos éstos, pues, que por el modo dicho se habían quedado en las tierras en que los hallamos, damos también agora nombre de mitimaes, sin diferencia de los primeros que sólo lo solían ser en tiempo de los Incas, y no esotros.
results.translation:
they had [the idol brought], and placed it in Cuzco with the same ceremony and worship it had customarily received there, and that their own native people should provide for all of this in no different manner than when they had kept the said idol and huaca [sacred object or place] in their own province. For this reason, there always resided in that city and court Indians from every province of the kingdom, occupied in the keeping and ministry of their idols, where they learned the customs and usages of the court; and as they rotated through their mitas [obligatory labor turns] and shifts, upon returning to their homeland they preserved and taught their own people all that they had seen and learned at court.

In these transfers of mitimaes [resettled colonists], no account was taken of the distance between their own lands and the place to which they were ordered to go, however great it might be; on the contrary, it happened not infrequently that they were transplanted from one end of the kingdom to the other, sometimes three or four hundred leagues more or less, as it pleased the Prince. This is why we see today in the provinces of the Collao mitimaes who are natives and originals of those of Chinchaysuyu, and in those provinces many Indians from the Collao. It is an established fact that the people of different provinces were so intermingled and intermixed that there is scarcely a valley or town in all of Peru where there is not some aylla [kin group or community unit] and faction of mitimaes.

The Inca had two principal considerations in mind when relocating his subjects: the first (as has already been said) was that they should not be moved to a climate contrary to their native one, and the other was that all the provinces of his Empire should be well populated and supplied with provisions and everything necessary for human life. To this end, he would bring people from outside into sparsely populated areas, and from those regions that had more people than could comfortably be sustained, he would draw colonies to populate those others. And these people who, by the king's command, left their homeland and submission to their caciques [chiefs] and settled in foreign lands, giving their obedience to the caciques of those places, are those who in the time of the Incas were properly called mitimaes. But since the Spaniards occupied this land, this name has been extended to others who in former times were not properly mitimaes — namely, to those Indians who, by order of their caciques and with their permission or that of the Inca, resided permanently outside their own towns and provinces, in the districts of other caciques, though not subject to those caciques but rather to those of the province from which they had come or were native. To explain this, one must presuppose an ancient custom of these people: when a province was barren with respect to foodstuffs, most notably with respect to their bread, which was maize — and yet well suited to other uses, as are all those of the Collao, which on account of their great cold do not bear maize, nor other seeds or fruits of temperate land, but are exceedingly rich in pastures and very suitable for raising livestock, and produce potatoes [papas] from which they make chuño [freeze-dried potato], which serves them as bread, along with certain other roots — to the inhabitants of such provinces, the Inca had designated lands in the warm valleys of the seacoast that lie on one side of their territory, and on the other slope of the mountain range toward the Andes, in the temperate valleys found there, where they might sow the things that were lacking in their own towns. And because these valleys lay twenty to thirty or more leagues from their lands, and they could not attend to sowing them communally as they did in the rest of the kingdom, the caciques took care to send people at the appropriate times to do so; and these, once they had gathered their harvest, returned with it to their towns. Beyond these, there were, by order of the Inca, a certain number of Indians sent out from each town with their wives and households to reside permanently with their children and descendants — as they indeed did — in the said valleys, in order to attend to the maintenance and improvement of the chácaras [agricultural plots or fields] of their caciques and communities. These people, although they lived in foreign land, were subject to their own caciques and not to those of the land where they resided. But once the Spaniards had entered this kingdom, at the time the land was first surveyed in order to be divided and granted in encomienda [labor and tribute grant] to the settlers, those Indians who were found in the said valleys, having been placed there by their caciques for the aforementioned purpose, were counted and distributed together with the native inhabitants of the district in which they were found, being removed from the obedience of their former caciques and subjected to those in whose land and jurisdiction they dwelt; and consequently they were given in encomienda to the same grantee to whom the said district was assigned, and not to the encomendero of the chiefdom from which they were native. All of these, therefore, who in the manner described had remained in the lands where we find them, are now also called mitimaes, without distinction from the former, who alone were properly so called in the time of the Incas, and not the latter.

entities_mentioned.name: mitimaes
entities_mentioned.notes: Quechua term (also spelled 'mitmaq') referring to Inca-era resettled colonists; Cobo discusses both the proper Inca-period meaning and the broader colonial Spanish usage of the term.

entities_mentioned.name: huaca
entities_mentioned.notes: Andean sacred object, place, or entity; Quechua 'wak'a'. Not a plant or drug; included as a cultural/religious entity mentioned in the text.

entities_mentioned.name: aylla
entities_mentioned.notes: Quechua 'ayllu'; the fundamental Andean social and kinship unit. Spelled 'aylla' by Cobo.

entities_mentioned.name: mita
entities_mentioned.notes: Quechua 'mit'a'; system of rotating obligatory labor service under the Incas, later adapted by the Spanish colonial administration.

entities_mentioned.name: Collao
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Altiplano region around Lake Titicaca, modern Peru/Bolivia
entities_mentioned.notes: High-altitude plateau region, described as too cold for maize but suitable for livestock and potato cultivation.

entities_mentioned.name: Chinchaysuyu
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Northwestern quarter (suyu) of the Inca Empire, roughly northern Peru and Ecuador
entities_mentioned.notes: One of the four suyus (quarters) of Tawantinsuyu.

entities_mentioned.name: papas
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Solanum tuberosum (potato)
entities_mentioned.notes: Described as a staple crop of the Collao region; used to make chuño.

entities_mentioned.name: chuño
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Freeze-dried potato; traditional Andean preserved food product
entities_mentioned.notes: Described as serving the inhabitants of the Collao as bread ('pan'); made from papas by freeze-drying at high altitude.

entities_mentioned.name: maíz
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Zea mays (maize/corn)
entities_mentioned.notes: Described as the primary bread-grain staple, absent from the cold Collao region but cultivated in warm coastal and Andean valleys.

entities_mentioned.name: chácaras
entities_mentioned.notes: From Quechua 'chakra'; agricultural plots or cultivated fields belonging to caciques and communities.

entities_mentioned.name: cacique
entities_mentioned.notes: Term of Caribbean origin adopted broadly in Spanish colonial usage to denote indigenous chiefs or lords.

entities_mentioned.name: encomienda
entities_mentioned.notes: Spanish colonial institution granting an encomendero rights to indigenous labor and tribute from a designated population.

entities_mentioned.name: Cuzco
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Cusco, Peru
entities_mentioned.notes: Capital city of the Inca Empire.

results.translator_notes: This segment contains no botanical or medical content; it is entirely an ethnographic and administrative account of the Inca mitmaq (mitimaes) resettlement system and its colonial-era reinterpretation. No plant-drug-ailment-preparation extraction is applicable here. Several OCR errors were silently corrected throughout (e.g., 'qne' → 'que', 'minb. 5 terio' → 'ministerio', 'mudan?;^' → 'mudanzas', 'mitimaes^' → 'mitimaes', 'halik' → 'había', 'antoj^' → 'antojaba', 'natural^' → 'naturales', 'estalm' → 'estaban', 'halk' → 'halla', 'wí?.' → 'mitimaes', 'v.ú 1 ¥' → 'consideración', 'diebo' → 'dicho', 'aatinianiente' → 'antiguamente', and various other broken words). The passage on the Collao mentions papas and chuño in an agricultural-economic context rather than a medical one, though these items appear elsewhere in the Materia Medica sections of the work. The term 'mitas' (labor turns/rotations) is distinct from 'mitimaes' (resettled colonists), a distinction Cobo is careful to maintain. The page header 'HISTORIA DEL NUEVO MUNDO 112' and catch-word artifacts have been excluded from the cleaned original text.

results.translator_notes:
{
  "title_inferred": "Inca Policy of Resettlement, Census-Taking, and Division of Subjects into Moieties",
  "original_text": "CAPITULO XXIV

Que los Incas reducían a pueblos las gentes que sujetaban, y el orden que en ellos ponían

Ya queda dicho arriba cómo los indios peruanos, antes de ser gobernados por los reyes Incas, no tenían pueblos concertados con traza y forma de nuestros pueblos, sino que vivían en caseríos de poca vecindad, puestos los más por los cerros y collados, para defenderse de los asaltos que unos a otros se daban. Digo ahora, que en poniendo el Inca debajo de su dominio una provincia, obligaba a sus moradores a que dejasen sus habitaciones antiguas y bajasen de los lugares altos y fragosos a otros más a propósito que les eran señalados, y en ellos hiciesen su asiento y viviesen en comunidad sujetos a los superiores que les ponía. Verdad es que, aunque damos nombre de pueblos a estos asientos o rancherías en que eran reducidos los vasallos del Inca, sólo merecen este nombre comparados con los caseríos en que antes vivían; que, a la verdad, ellos eran de ordinario tan pequeños y mal trazados (sacando las cabeceras de provincias, que solían ser mayores y más bien formadas) que no tenían que ver con las más humildes aldeas nuestras.

Luego, para saber el número de gente que había en cada provincia, así de los naturales como de los mitimaes, nobles y plebeyos, mandaba se contasen todos por sus edades, órdenes y estados, haciendo catálogo aparte de los tributarios y de los que no lo eran, de los indios, mujeres y viejos. En el número de tributarios entraban solamente los varones de la gente común desde veinticinco hasta cincuenta años, poco más o menos; porque, como entre ellos no se contaban por años las edades ni sabía ninguno los que tenía, sólo por el sujeto y disposición de cada uno se gobernaban para este efecto, poniendo en la minuta tanto número de muchachos, tanto de mancebos y tanto de varones; y por el mismo estilo las mujeres, que

results.title_inferred: Distinguishing Marks and Headdresses of Andean Nations Under Inca Rule
results.original_text:
en sus pueblos sin poder salir déllos ni andar vagueando o hacer viajes por tierras extrañas sin licencia de sus caciques.

Tenían los de cada nación y provincia hombres y mujeres, sus señales y divisas por donde eran conocidos, y no podían andar sin ellas ni trocarlas con las de otra nación, so graves penas. Estas traían en el vestido con diferentes listas y colores; y los hombres otra en la cabeza más señalada, diferenciándose cada nación en el tocado; porque, así como todos andaban con cabello largo, unos lo traían cortado por bajo de las orejas y otros muy largo; unos trenzado y otros suelto, y los más vendado o ceñido con diversas suertes de ligaduras. Los Cañares, que eran los naturales de Tumibamba, se ponían en las cabezas una corona redonda de palo a manera de aro de cedazo o ruedo de cajeta de conserva. Los de Cajamarca traían cogido el cabello con una honda; y sus vecinos, con unos cordones delgados de lana colorada con muchas vueltas. Los de Guaylas unos rodetes en las cabezas, que ellos llamaban pillos, y con hondas muy blancas alrededor. Los de Bombón, unas toquillas pintadas de amarillo y colorado alrededor de las cabezas. Los de Jauja, unas fajas coloradas del ancho de una mano. Los de Huaylas liaban la cabeza con unas cuerdas de lana que les venían a caer por debajo de la barba. Los del Cuzco y todos los del linaje de los Incas, usaban de cierta ligadura llamada llanto, que era una cinta de lana del ancho de un dedo con algunas vueltas. Los Collas traían unos bonetes de lana justos pero ahusados, porque así se amoldaban las cabezas; y sus mujeres unos bonetes puntiagudos al talle de capillas de frailes; sacando estos Collas, el tocado de todas las otras naciones era en forma de guirnalda, y los más de cordones y ramales con muchas vueltas formados como una madeja de hilo del tamaño de la cabeza; y se diferenciaban en que unos eran de lana, otros de cabuya (que es su cáñamo), unos más delgados que otros, y unos de un color y otros de otro; con otras diversidades que hacían se diferenciasen los de cada provincia; las cuales divisas son pocos los que ya las usan, porque todos van entrando en el uso de nuestros sombreros.

Eran tan conocidos por estas señales, que en viendo cualquiera indio, o viniendo a la presencia del Inca, echaba de ver de qué nación y provincia era; y no hay duda sino que fue ingeniosa invención ésta para distinguirse unos de otros, porque, siendo casi innumerables las naciones diversas que se congregaban a cualquiera llamamiento general del rey, y siendo, como son, todos los indios desbarbados, de un color, aspecto y facciones, y usando de una misma lengua y traje, fuera imposible poderse distinguir de otra suerte los de cada nación. Dejado aparte, que, cuando iban a la guerra era muy de ver un ejército numeroso compuesto de tanta variedad de gentes como marchaba, llevando su lugar distinto los de cada nación, repartidos en varios tercios y escuadrones, que con estas divisas campeaba de lejos la variedad; y eran fácilmente conocidos de su general y de los demás oficiales del campo; y en la batalla no se podía ocultar la nación que más valor mostraba.
results.translation:
in their towns without being able to leave them or wander about or make journeys through foreign lands without the permission of their [caciques] [local lords].

The men and women of each nation and province had their own marks and [divisas] [insignia] by which they were known, and they were not permitted to go about without them nor to exchange them for those of another nation, under severe penalties. These they wore on their clothing in different stripes and colors; and the men wore another mark more prominently on the head, with each nation distinguished by its headdress [tocado]. For although all of them went about with long hair, some wore it cut below the ears and others very long; some braided and others loose, and most bound or wrapped with various kinds of ties [ligaduras]. The Cañares, who were the native people of Tumibamba, placed on their heads a round wooden crown in the manner of a sieve-hoop or the rim of a jar of preserves. Those of Cajamarca gathered their hair with a sling [honda]; and their neighbors with thin cords of red wool wound many times around. Those of Guaylas wore circular pads [rodetes] on their heads, which they called pillos, with very white slings wrapped around them. Those of Bombón wore head-cloths [toquillas] painted in yellow and red around their heads. Those of Jauja wore red bands [fajas] the width of a hand. Those of Huaylas bound their heads with wool cords that came down beneath their chins. Those of Cuzco and all of the lineage of the Incas used a certain binding called llanto [mourning-band], which was a wool ribbon the width of a finger wound several times around. The Collas wore tight but tapering wool bonnets [bonetes], because they molded their heads to that shape from infancy; and their women wore pointed bonnets resembling the hoods of friars. Setting aside these Collas, the headdress of all the other nations was in the form of a garland, most of them made of cords and strands wound many times and formed like a skein of thread fitted to the size of the head; and they differed in that some were of wool, others of cabuya (which is their hemp [cáñamo]), some thinner than others, and some of one color and others of another; with other variations that distinguished those of each province from one another. Very few people still use these [divisas] today, for everyone is gradually adopting the use of our [Spanish] hats.

They were so well identified by these marks that upon seeing any Indian, or when one came into the presence of the Inca, it was immediately apparent from which nation and province he came. There is no doubt that this was an ingenious invention for distinguishing one from another, because, given that the diverse nations that gathered at any general summons of the king were almost innumerable, and given that all Indians are beardless, of one color, appearance, and features, and use the same language and dress, it would have been impossible to distinguish those of each nation by any other means. Beyond this, when they went to war it was a remarkable sight to see a great army composed of such a variety of peoples marching together, each nation keeping its distinct place, divided into various tercios [infantry regiments] and squadrons, so that from a distance the variety of insignia displayed the diversity of the force; and they were easily recognized by their general and the other officers of the field; and in battle it was impossible to conceal which nation showed the greatest valor.

entities_mentioned.name: Cañares / Tumibamba
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Cañari people of the Cuenca basin, Ecuador; Tumibamba = modern Cuenca
entities_mentioned.notes: Described as wearing a round wooden crown resembling a sieve-hoop or the rim of a preserve jar.

entities_mentioned.name: Cajamarca
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Cajamarca region, northern highland Peru
entities_mentioned.notes: Headdress: hair gathered with a sling (honda).

entities_mentioned.name: Guaylas / Huaylas
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Huaylas (Huailas) people, Callejón de Huaylas, Ancash, Peru
entities_mentioned.notes: Headdress: circular pads called pillos with white slings; also described separately as binding head with wool cords reaching under the chin.

entities_mentioned.name: Bombón
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Pumpu / Bombón plateau, Junín region, Peru
entities_mentioned.notes: Headdress: toquillas painted yellow and red.

entities_mentioned.name: Jauja
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Jauja, Junín region, Peru
entities_mentioned.notes: Headdress: red bands the width of a hand.

entities_mentioned.name: Cuzco / Incas
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Cusco, Peru; Inca ruling lineage
entities_mentioned.notes: Headdress: ligadura called llanto, a wool ribbon the width of a finger wound several times.

entities_mentioned.name: Collas
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Colla (Aymara) people of the Lake Titicaca basin, Peru/Bolivia
entities_mentioned.notes: Headdress: tight tapering wool bonnets shaped by cranial deformation; women wore pointed bonnets resembling friars' hoods.

entities_mentioned.name: cabuya
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Furcraea andina or Agave americana (Andean agave/maguey fiber plant)
entities_mentioned.notes: Cobo glosses it as 'su cáñamo' (their hemp), indicating it was used as a fiber for making cords and textiles.

entities_mentioned.name: llanto
entities_mentioned.notes: Inca head-binding; the word llanto normally means 'weeping/mourning' in Spanish, but here Cobo uses it as a Quechua-derived or specialized term for the Inca headband. Possibly a mishearing or variant of 'llauto' (the standard Quechua term for the Inca royal headband), which is the more commonly attested form in the literature.

results.translator_notes:
1. This segment contains no botanical or medical content; it is an ethnographic description of Andean social control mechanisms and the system of provincial insignia (divisas) under the Inca state. No plant/drug extraction is applicable.
2. The word 'llanto' as used for the Inca headband almost certainly represents 'llauto' (Quechua: llawthu), the well-documented Inca diadem. The OCR or manuscript may have rendered it as 'llanto'; I have preserved Cobo's spelling but flagged it in the entities.
3. 'Tercios' is a specific Spanish military term for infantry regiments; translated accordingly.
4. Several OCR artifacts in the source (e.g., 'k cabeza', 'ñ ceñido', 'fe de lana', ampersand entities '&') have been silently corrected to their evident originals in the cleaned text.
5. The passage's two mentions of 'Guaylas/Huaylas' appear to describe the same group with two distinct headdress details (pillos with white slings, and wool cords under the chin); this may reflect two sub-groups or a textual redundancy in the original.

results.translator_notes:
{
  "title_inferred": "Inca Provincial Governance: Governors, Caciques, and Administrative Hierarchy",
  "original_text": "CAPITULO XXV

De los gobernadores, caciques y demás superiores en quienes repartían los Incas el gobierno de sus estados

En lo que toca a los gobernadores, magistrados y toda suerte de ministros de justicia, es muy poca la diversidad que hallamos en el gobierno de los Incas, porque, cuantos tenían alguna jurisdicción conocían en sus distritos de todo género de causas y delitos que no excediesen su potestad; de manera, que no se diferenciaban los gobernadores y jueces por las materias de que les era concedido juzgar o calidad de su jurisdicción sino por la cantidad y medida della, según a cada uno se la daba el rey. Unos la tenían muy corta y limitada, y otros muy amplia y cumplida; de modo, que para dar una regla general, basta decir, que como todos los vasallos se contaban y se les ponían superiores a ciertos números, aquellos tenían más amplia potestad que regían mayor número de indios. Comenzando, pues, de los más moderados y de menos autoridad, digo, que a cada diez indios tributarios o vecinos tenía puesto el Inca un superior que cuidaba de los nueve; y en cada cinco decurias déstas, otro que tenía cuenta con cincuenta; otro gobernaba una centuria, que constaba de las dos decurias de a cincuenta; en cada cinco centurias, o cada quinientos, había otro; y dos superiores de a quinientos con sus súbditos, reconocían a un milenario, que mandaba a mil; diez déstos estaban sujetos a otro más principal, que tenía debajo de su obediencia diez mil, que hacían una gobernación llamada hunu. Tomaban el nombre estos superiores del número de sus decurias: el que tenía cuenta con diez, se decía Chunca camayoc; el de quinientos, Pachacamayoc; el de mil, Huaranca; y el de diez mil, Hunu; y todos los que gobernaban de ciento para arriba, se decían comúnmente curacas. Sobre todos éstos ponía el Inca en cada provincia un gobernador o virrey, el cual era pe

results.title_inferred: Inca System of Cacique Succession and Administrative Hierarchy
results.original_text:
ando a unos de lo que tenían, y añadiendo a otros; pero si alguno incurría en caso por el que mereciese ser privado del señorío que poseía, era echado dél y encomendábanlo a otro de la misma provincia, deudo del primero, si lo había. En la sucesión de los cacicazgos y señoríos guardaba el Inca este orden: que si el hijo mayor era hábil y capaz para el cacicazgo de su padre, lo nombraba en él y le daba el duho, que era una silleta baja o banquillo en que los caciques se asentaban para tomar la posesión del cacicazgo, y después solos ellos usaban este asiento; y si el mayor no fuera hábil y lo era el segundo, se daba a éste; y faltando hijos hábiles y de edad sucedía el hermano del difunto, y tenía el señorío mientras vivía; pero muerto éste, no le sucedía sino el sobrino que dejó de heredar por falta de edad competente: y cuando en todos los hijos de tal cacique no había ninguno suficiente para ello, ni dejaba el difunto hermanos, se nombraba por cacique la segunda persona del señorío, teniendo las partes y requisitos para serlo. Esto se guardaba en el nombramiento y sucesión de todos los curacas y caciques; los cuales proveía el Inca o el tocrícuc por especial misión suya; excepto los pachaccamayos de a cien indios y los de a menos, porque a éstos nombraban los guarancas o caciques de a mil súbditos a quienes eran sujetos, con consentimiento y aprobación de los caciques más principales; y no les podían quitar el oficio mientras vivían, si no era por delito grave, y entonces sucedían sus hijos como los demás. Los de a cincuenta y de a diez elegían los caciques y los quitaban cuando hacían mal su oficio. Y no se heredaban.

Llamamos a estos señores caciques, porque con este nombre los comenzaron a llamar en la isla Española, que en las dos lenguas generales deste reino se nombran curacas en la quichua, y maycos en la aimará. Tenían todos estos superiores y mandones limitada jurisdicción, los menores más que los mayores, y los que más amplia la tenían, que eran los hinus, no podían dar pena de muerte; solamente conocían de los agravios pequeños y culpas livianas. Tenían cuidado de repartir las tierras de labor a sus súbditos, señalando a cada indio la cantidad que les bastaba; y asimismo el agua que había de tomar para regar sus chácaras y sementeras, si era tierra de regadío. Item, hacían sacar oro y plata los que tenían minas en sus distritos; una vez al año juntar en la plaza los mancebos y mujeres casaderas, y daban así a ellas como a ellos a escoger con quien querían casarse, y la que el hinu les daba recebían los indios por mujeres. Los decuriones inferiores tenían cuidado de ver cómo vivían los que estaban a su cargo, y de acusarlos cuando cometían algún delito. Otrosí, cuidaban de avisar de las necesidades que tenían a quien había de remediarlas; de contar los que nacían y morían; los mancebos y mujeres que llegaban a edad de casarse; los viejos y contrahechos que se hallaban en sus decurias, para que en todo se diese orden como fuesen remediadas sus necesidades. De todas estas cosas daban cuenta estos decuriones a sus inmediatos superiores, y éstos la iban dando a sus mayores por sus grados hasta el Inca, que por esta forma sabía cada año los indios de todas edades que había en cada provincia para las contribuciones y mitas con que podían acudir para la guerra, obras públicas y demás ministerios en que solía ocuparlos.
results.translation:
—reducing what some held and adding to what others held; but if any one fell into a case by which he deserved to be deprived of the lordship [señorío] he possessed, he was removed from it, and it was entrusted to another person of the same province, a kinsman of the first, if one existed.

In the succession of chieftainships [cacicazgos] and lordships, the Inca observed the following order: if the eldest son was able and capable of assuming his father's chieftainship, he was appointed to it and was given the duho [a low stool or small bench upon which the caciques seated themselves to take possession of the chieftainship, and which thereafter only they used]; and if the eldest was not capable but the second son was, it was given to the latter; and if there were no able sons of suitable age, the brother of the deceased succeeded and held the lordship for his lifetime; but upon his death, he was not succeeded by his own heirs, but rather by the nephew who had previously been passed over for want of sufficient age. And when none of the deceased cacique's sons was found adequate, nor did the deceased leave brothers, the second-ranking person of the lordship was named cacique, provided he had the qualities and requirements for the office. This was observed in the appointment and succession of all curacas [kuraka] and caciques, whom the Inca or the tocrícuc [tucuyricuc, Inca provincial inspector] appointed by special commission; except for the pachaccamayos [pachaka kamayuq, overseers of one hundred] of one hundred Indians and those of fewer, for these were appointed by the guarancas [waranka, leaders of one thousand] or caciques of one thousand subjects to whom they were subordinate, with the consent and approval of the more principal caciques; and they could not be removed from office during their lifetime, unless for a serious crime, in which case their sons succeeded as in the other cases. Those overseers of fifty and of ten were chosen by the caciques and were removed when they performed their duties poorly, and these offices were not inherited.

We call these lords caciques because they began to be called by this name on the island of Hispaniola [La Española]; in the two general languages of this kingdom they are called curacas in Quechua [quichua], and maycos in Aymara [aimará]. All these superiors and commanders had limited jurisdiction: the lesser ones more so than the greater, and those with the broadest authority—who were the hinus [hunu, overseers of ten thousand]—could not impose the death penalty; they dealt only with minor grievances and light offenses. They took care to distribute the cultivable lands to their subjects, assigning to each Indian the amount sufficient for his needs; and likewise the water to be used for irrigating their plots [chácaras] and fields [sementeras], if it was irrigated land. Furthermore, those who had mines in their districts made gold and silver extracted from them; once a year they gathered in the plaza the young men and marriageable women, and allowed both the women and the men to choose whom they wished to marry, and whatever woman the hinu gave them, the Indians accepted as wives. The inferior decurions [decuriones] took care to observe how those under their charge lived, and to denounce them when they committed any offense. Furthermore, they took care to inform those responsible of the needs that existed so that remedies could be provided; to count those who were born and those who died; the young men and women who reached marriageable age; the old and the disabled found within their decuries [decurias], so that in all things an arrangement might be made to remedy their needs. Of all these matters the decurions gave account to their immediate superiors, and these in turn reported to their own superiors by degrees up to the Inca himself, who in this manner knew each year the Indians of all ages in every province, in order to assess the contributions and labor drafts [mitas] with which they could serve in war, public works, and the other duties in which he was accustomed to employ them.

entities_mentioned.name: duho
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Low ceremonial stool used by Caribbean and Andean leaders for investiture rituals
entities_mentioned.notes: A term of Taíno origin widely adopted in colonial Spanish; here specifically refers to the stool used by Andean caciques during formal assumption of office.

entities_mentioned.name: cacique
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Term of Taíno origin applied to indigenous leaders throughout Spanish colonial America
entities_mentioned.notes: Cobo explicitly notes this word originated on Hispaniola and that the Quechua equivalent is 'curaca' and the Aymara equivalent is 'mayco.'

entities_mentioned.name: curaca / kuraka
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Andean hereditary or appointed local lord (Quechua term)
entities_mentioned.notes: Quechua term for the same office called 'cacique' in general colonial Spanish usage.

entities_mentioned.name: mayco
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Aymara term for a local lord or chief
entities_mentioned.notes: Aymara-language equivalent of curaca/cacique.

entities_mentioned.name: tocrícuc / tucuyricuc
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Inca imperial inspector or provincial governor ('he who sees all')
entities_mentioned.notes: High Inca administrative official responsible for overseeing provincial governance and appointments.

entities_mentioned.name: pachaccamayo / pachaka kamayuq
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Overseer responsible for one hundred tributary households in the Inca decimal administrative system
entities_mentioned.notes: Part of the Inca decimal hierarchy of administrative units.

entities_mentioned.name: guaranca / waranka
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Overseer or lord of one thousand households in the Inca decimal administrative system
entities_mentioned.notes: Quechua term for the unit of one thousand in Inca administrative organization.

entities_mentioned.name: hinu / hunu
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Overseer of ten thousand households in the Inca decimal administrative system
entities_mentioned.notes: Cobo describes the hinu as having the broadest local jurisdiction but still unable to impose capital punishment.

entities_mentioned.name: mita
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Obligatory labor draft system under the Inca and later colonial Spanish administration
entities_mentioned.notes: Rotational labor obligation owed by subject communities to the Inca state for public works, military service, and other duties.

entities_mentioned.name: chácaras
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Small cultivated plots or agricultural parcels (from Quechua 'chakra')
entities_mentioned.notes: Quechua-derived term for individual family agricultural plots; still in widespread use in Andean Spanish today.

entities_mentioned.name: isla Española / Hispaniola
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Island of Hispaniola (modern Haiti and Dominican Republic)
entities_mentioned.notes: Cobo notes this is where the term 'cacique' was first adopted by Spaniards, reflecting Taíno linguistic influence on colonial administrative vocabulary.

  "original_text": "CAPITULO XXVI

De las leyes y castigos con que los Incas gobernaban su reino

Como los indios carecían de letras, no tenían leyes escritas, mas conservaban las que habían establecido sus reyes por tradición y con el uso y observancia en que estaban. Las mas principales y de que más memoria había entre ellos pondré aquí.

Donde se hallaba presente el Inca, él solo era el juez ante quien se pedían todos los agravios hechos, y donde no, hacían justicia sus gobernadores y caciques, según a quien tocaba conocer del caso, vista su calidad.

A el que mataba a traición, luego incontinente le quitaban la vida pública y afrentosamente, aunque fuese hombre de calidad y el muerto de muy desigual fortuna.

results.title_inferred: Inca Tribute System: Exemptions, Labor Tax, Salaries, and Land Division
results.original_text:
Los orejones, los gobernadores, capitanes, caciques y jueces del Inca con sus hijos; los cuales todos, no siendo exentos de las contribuciones que pagaba la gente común, mas tiraban gajes de su rey y eran contentados con el tributo de servicio personal que a ellos daban los muchos pecheros; puesto caso que no por eso se excusaban de mostrar el vasallaje y reconocimiento debido a su rey con algunos dones y presentes que a tiempos le hacían; mas, como éstos eran voluntarios y sin tasa ni apremio, aunque puestos en uso y costumbre, no entraban en cuenta de tributos; como no vemos que en España tributan los nobles y grandes porque sirven al rey con sus personas y haciendas en las guerras y gobernaciones. Allende desto, el trabajo que éstos ponían en servicio del Inca y administración de sus oficios, que era bien pesado, equivalía con ventajas al servicio y tributos de la gente vulgar.

Empadronábanse por pecheros todos los demás del reino, hasta los hilacatas y mandones de cien indios para abajo; de manera, que los que tenían mando sobre cien indios y de ahí para arriba, eran libres de pechar; y los que tenían debajo de sí cincuenta y menos, eran contados con los pecheros, y como tales trabajaban corporalmente con sus manos en las obras y oficios que los otros. En el número de tributarios entraban los aucacamayos, que quiere decir gente hábil para la guerra o hombre de armas tomar; que comúnmente eran desde veinticinco a cincuenta años, poco más o menos, o en casándose, aunque no llegasen a los veinticinco; y un vecino con su casa e hijo de menor edad se contaba por un tributario. Los nobles y principales que eran exentos de pecho, como queda dicho, nunca trabajaban corporalmente en obras mecánicas; servían en oficios y cargos honrosos, por los cuales llevaban sus salarios competentes y se les seguía mucha honra; y aunque no estuviesen ocupados en cargos públicos, eran honrados como pedía su calidad. Con éstos entraban gozando de la exención de tributar las mujeres todas de cualquiera edad, estado y calidad, aunque fuesen viudas; ítem, todos los ciegos, cojos, mancos y enfermos que estaban impedidos para el trabajo; en suma, no se empadronaban por tributarios sino los varones de la gente común que pudiesen actualmente trabajar en los ejercicios a que acudían los pueblos por vía de comunidad.

Todo el tributo que pagaban era servicio personal, trabajando corporalmente en las obras y ocupaciones que contaré en los capítulos que se siguen. En lugar de tributo trabajaban los oficiales en servicio del Inca, de la religión o de sus caciques, cada uno en el oficio que sabía, como en labrar ropa, oro y plata, en sacar estos metales de las minas y beneficiarlos; en hacer vasos de barro y de madera, y en los demás oficios; en todo lo cual entendían todo el tiempo que les cabía. Mientras se ocupaban en cumplir sus tasas y tributos con estos oficios y trabajos, así los oficiales y artífices, como las comunidades de los pueblos y mitayos, eran sustentados a costa del dueño en cuya hacienda trabajaban o a quien servían, aunque fuese la hacienda del Inca o de la Religión; y de la misma hacienda se les daban también las herramientas con los demás instrumentos y aparejos necesarios; de suerte, que no ponían ellos de su casa más que el trabajo de sus manos.

La paga y salarios que el Inca daba a sus ministros, gobernadores y caciques, por el trabajo de los oficios que servían, no era en cantidad determinada y cierta de alguna cosa en especie, como en oro, plata, etcétera, sino en servicio personal de los súbditos que en sus distritos y debajo de su mando tenían; señalándoles tanto número de mitayos, cuanto bastase para el servicio de sus personas y de las puertas adentro de sus casas, a cada uno conforme a su calidad y para sustentar la autoridad de su oficio; y lo ordinario era a razón de uno por ciento de los sujetos que gobernaban. De forma que al curaca de cien pecheros, se le daba un criado o mitayo; al de quinientos, cinco; al de mil, diez, y en esta proporción a los demás. Fuera destos criados que se les daban para su acompañamiento y servicio de dentro de sus casas, las comunidades de sus pueblos acudían a labrarles sus chácaras, edificarles sus casas, guardarles el ganado, si lo tenían, con lo demás en que, por orden del Inca, estaban obligados a servirlos; y este servicio de sus súbditos era en lugar de salarios.

En lo que toca a gratificar méritos, tampoco faltaba cuidado. A quien más acostumbraban premiar los Incas, era a los capitanes que se habían señalado en la guerra, y a quien le hacía algún particular servicio; a éstos remuneraba el rey con darles joyas de valor, vestidos preciosos, y lo más ordinario y que los vasallos más estimaban, con algunas doncellas de las que se recogían de tributo, con ganado y tierras de labor que tuviesen en propiedad de ellos y sus herederos.

CAPITULO XXVIII

De la partición que hacía el Inca de las tierras de labor, y de la hacienda y rentas que en ellas tenían él y la Religión

En asentando el Inca un pueblo, o reduciéndolo a su obediencia, amojonaba sus términos y dividía los campos y tierras de pan llevar de su distrito en tres partes, por esta forma: una parte aplicada a la Religión y culto de sus falsos dioses; otra tomaba para sí, y la tercera dejaba para la comunidad del dicho pueblo. No se ha podido averiguar si estas partes en cada pueblo y provincia eran iguales; porque consta no haber sido hecha esta división por igual en muchas partes, sino en cada tierra conforme a su disposición y calidad de gente de ella. En unas provincias era mayor la parte diputada a la Religión; en otras, la que pertenecía al Inca, y en algunas la porción de la comunidad excedía a cualquiera de las otras dos.
results.translation:
The orejones [lit. 'big-ears'; the Inca nobility], the governors, captains, caciques [local chiefs], and judges of the Inca along with their sons — all of these, while not exempt from the contributions paid by common people, nonetheless drew wages from their king and were satisfied by the tribute of personal service rendered to them by the many tributary commoners [pecheros]. This notwithstanding, they were not thereby excused from showing the vassalage and due recognition owed to their king by means of certain gifts and presents they made to him from time to time; but since these were voluntary, without set rate or compulsion — though established by custom and usage — they did not count as tribute, just as we do not see the nobles and grandees of Spain paying tribute, since they serve the king with their persons and estates in wars and in governance. Beyond this, the labor that these men devoted to the service of the Inca and to the administration of their offices, which was very burdensome, was more than equivalent to the service and tributes of the common people.

All others in the kingdom were registered as tributary commoners [pecheros], down to the hilacatas [minor Andean community leaders] and overseers of fewer than one hundred Indians. Thus those who held command over one hundred or more Indians were free of tribute obligations; while those who had fewer than fifty under them were counted among the commoners and, as such, worked bodily with their hands at the same works and trades as the others. Included in the number of tributaries were the aucacamayos [lit. 'capable warriors'; soldiers fit for war], generally men between twenty-five and fifty years of age, more or less, or from the time of marriage even if they had not yet reached twenty-five; and a householder together with his household and minor-age son counted as a single tributary. The nobles and prominent men who were exempt from tribute, as has been said, never worked bodily at mechanical trades; they served in honorable offices and positions, for which they received fitting salaries and considerable honor; and even when not occupied in public office, they were honored as befitted their rank. Joining them in exemption from tribute were all women of any age, condition, or quality whatsoever, even widows; likewise all the blind, lame, one-armed, and sick who were impeded from labor. In sum, only the males of the common people who were actually able to work at the communal tasks to which the towns were obligated were registered as tributaries.

All the tribute they paid was personal service, working bodily at the works and occupations I shall describe in the chapters that follow. In lieu of tribute, craftsmen worked in the service of the Inca, of the [state] religion, or of their caciques, each in the trade he knew — such as weaving cloth, working gold and silver, extracting these metals from the mines and refining them [beneficiarlos], making vessels of clay and wood, and other trades — at all of which they devoted whatever time fell to their lot. While they were occupied fulfilling their quotas [tasas] and tributes through these trades and labors, both the craftsmen and artisans and the village communities and mitayos [those performing mit'a rotational labor service] were sustained at the expense of the lord on whose estate they worked or whom they served, even if that estate belonged to the Inca or to the [state] Religion; from that same estate they were also furnished with the tools and all other instruments and necessary equipment, so that they contributed from their own households nothing beyond the labor of their hands.

The pay and salaries that the Inca gave to his ministers, governors, and caciques for the work of the offices they filled were not a fixed and certain quantity of any specific good, such as gold, silver, and the like, but rather consisted in the personal service of the subjects within their districts and under their command, assigning them a number of mitayos sufficient for the service of their persons and the interior of their households, each according to his rank and to the demands of maintaining the authority of his office; the customary rate was one for every hundred subjects governed. Thus a curaca [Andean lord] over one hundred tributary commoners was given one servant or mitayo; one over five hundred received five; one over a thousand, ten; and so on in the same proportion for the rest. Beyond these servants given for their personal attendance and household service, the communities of their towns would also come to till their chácaras [cultivated plots], build their houses, and guard their livestock if they had any, along with whatever else the Inca's order obligated them to provide; and this service of their subjects took the place of salaries.

As for rewarding merit, care was not lacking in this regard either. Those whom the Incas most customarily rewarded were captains who had distinguished themselves in war and those who had rendered some particular service; these the king remunerated by giving them jewels of value, precious garments, and most ordinarily — and what vassals valued most — some maidens drawn from the tribute pool, along with livestock and arable lands to be held as their property and that of their heirs.

CHAPTER XXVIII

On the division the Inca made of the arable lands, and on the estate and revenues that he and the [state] Religion held in them

Whenever the Inca established a settlement or reduced it to his obedience, he marked out its boundaries and divided the fields and grain-bearing lands [tierras de pan llevar] of its district into three parts, as follows: one part was assigned to the Religion and the worship of his false gods; another he took for himself; and the third he left to the community of the said settlement. It has not been possible to determine whether these parts were equal in each town and province, for it is established that this division was not made equally in many places but rather in each territory according to its nature and the quality of its people. In some provinces the portion allotted to the Religion was greater; in others, the portion belonging to the Inca; and in some the community's share exceeded either of the other two.

entities_mentioned.name: orejones
entities_mentioned.notes: Inca hereditary nobility, so called by Spaniards because of their large ear spools; not a plant or drug.

entities_mentioned.name: hilacatas
entities_mentioned.notes: Minor Andean community leaders or overseers; from Quechua 'hilacata' (head of an ayllu subdivision).

entities_mentioned.name: aucacamayos
entities_mentioned.notes: Quechua term used by Cobo to mean men of fighting age liable for military service; from 'awqa' (enemy/warrior) + 'kamayuq' (one responsible for).

entities_mentioned.name: mitayos / mita
entities_mentioned.notes: Workers performing the mit'a, the Inca system of rotational labor tribute; from Quechua 'mit'a' (turn, season).

entities_mentioned.name: curaca
entities_mentioned.notes: Andean local lord or chief; Quechua/Aymara term for a hereditary community leader under the Inca hierarchy.

entities_mentioned.name: chácaras
entities_mentioned.notes: From Quechua 'chacra'; small cultivated agricultural plots or fields.

entities_mentioned.name: tierras de pan llevar
entities_mentioned.notes: Standard colonial Spanish expression for arable/grain-bearing agricultural lands.

results.translator_notes: This segment contains no botanical, pharmacological, or medical content. It is entirely devoted to the political economy and tribute system of the Inca Empire — specifically the hierarchy of tax exemptions, the nature of labor tribute (mit'a), salary structures for Inca officials, and the tripartite division of arable land among the state religion, the Inca, and the community. The segment label references Books IV–VI botanical sections, but this passage appears to be drawn from a different thematic portion of Cobo's Historia del Nuevo Mundo (likely Book XI or XII, which deal with Inca governance), and contains no plant, drug, or medical extraction applicable under Rule 3. Several OCR artifacts were corrected silently (e.g., 'criiales' → 'cuales', 'niiíctyos' → 'muchos', 'áo' → 'no siendo', 'contriluicio' → 'contribuciones', 'i>aga' → 'paga', 'i>odido' → 'podido', 'x>ara' → 'para', 'perteueck' → 'pertenecía'). The chapter break to Chapter XXVIII (De la partición…) appears mid-segment and has been preserved in both cleaned text and translation.

results.title_inferred: Inca Land Tenure, Communal Ownership, and Inheritance Customs
results.original_text: consta cuán absoluto señor era el Inca de cuanto sus vasallos poseían, pues a ninguno era permitido tener chácara o heredad, ni aun el dominio de un solo palmo de tierra en particular, sino por merced especial del Inca; y fuera deste título, no había otro para adquirir propiedad en bienes raíces; y solía él hacer esta merced a algunos capitanes e insignes beneméritos, en remuneración de sus servicios, como por haber hecho en la guerra alguna hazaña, haber hallado industria para alguna cosa de gran utilidad de la república, como para hacer alguna puente, acequia o camino, o por ser hijos de caciques que le hubiesen servido mucho, o por otros respectos; y la tal tierra concedida a persona particular, pasaba a sus descendientes, mas, con tal condición que ni el primer poseedor ni los sucesores la podían enajenar, trocar, partir ni disponer della por ninguna vía ni manera; salvo que uno que representaba siempre la persona del avila y linaje, como pariente mayor, la tenía en su cabeza y dividía cada año entre los deudos conforme a sus costumbres, de suerte que todos gozasen de su fruto; y repartíase por cabezas desta manera: que si un hijo del señor primero tenía seis hijos, y otro dos, cada uno, así de los hijos como de los nietos, tenía partes iguales, y tantas se hacían cuantas personas había en el linaje; en lo cual se tenía este orden, que llegado el tiempo, acudían todos a sembrarla, y los mismos que se hallaban al sembrarla, se habían de hallar al coger y repartir los frutos; y el que no se hallaba presente cuando se sembraba, no llevaba parte ni la podía dar a otro que substituyese por él; mas, aunque estuviese ausente diez o veinte años, no perdía su derecho cuando volvía; y aunque fuesen tantos los dueños que no les cupiese más que una mazorca de maíz a cada uno, guardaban esta costumbre. De manera, que por lo dicho queda entendido cómo las tierras que gozaban los indios eran de la comunidad de todo el pueblo, y que en particular tenían por suyas por merced del Rey, también los del linaje del primer dueño las poseían en común, y sin partillas, y el trabajo de beneficiarlas también era de comunidad, y el que no trabajaba en sembrar, no llevaba parte de la cosecha.
results.translation: It is evident how absolute a lord the Inca was over everything his subjects possessed, for no one was permitted to hold a chácara [small farm or plot] or estate, nor even dominion over a single span of land as private property, except by special grant [merced] of the Inca; and outside of this title, there was no other means by which to acquire ownership of real property [bienes raíces]. He was accustomed to bestow this grant upon certain captains and distinguished men of merit, in remuneration for their services — such as for having performed some feat in war, or having devised some invention of great utility to the commonwealth [república], such as the building of a bridge, irrigation channel [acequia], or road, or for being sons of caciques who had served him greatly, or for other reasons. The land thus granted to a private individual passed on to his descendants, but on the condition that neither the first possessor nor his successors could alienate, exchange, divide, or dispose of it in any way or manner whatsoever. Rather, one person who always represented the person of the lineage group [avila and linaje], as the senior kinsman [pariente mayor], held it in his name and divided it each year among the relatives according to their customs, so that all might enjoy its fruit. It was distributed per capita in the following manner: if one son of the original lord had six children and another had two, each person — both sons and grandsons alike — received equal shares, and as many shares were made as there were persons in the lineage. The order observed in this was that, when the time came, all would gather to sow it, and those same persons who were present at the sowing were required to be present at the harvest and the distribution of the fruits. Anyone who was not present at the time of sowing received no share, nor could he give his place to another to substitute for him; however, even if he had been absent ten or twenty years, he did not forfeit his right upon his return. And even if the owners were so numerous that each received no more than a single ear of maize, they observed this custom nonetheless. Thus, by what has been said, it is made clear that the lands enjoyed by the Indians belonged to the community of the entire settlement [pueblo], and that those held privately by royal grant were likewise possessed in common by the members of the lineage of the original owner, without partition, and that the labor of working them was also communal — and he who did not labor at the sowing received no part of the harvest.

entities_mentioned.name: Inca
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Sapa Inca, ruler of the Inca Empire (Tawantinsuyu)
entities_mentioned.notes: Described here in his role as ultimate sovereign over all land and property within the empire.

entities_mentioned.name: chácara
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Small agricultural plot or farm (from Quechua 'chakra')
entities_mentioned.notes: A common Andean term for a cultivated field or smallholding; still in use in Andean Spanish today.

entities_mentioned.name: acequia
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Irrigation channel or aqueduct
entities_mentioned.notes: A term of Arabic origin widely used in colonial Spanish to denote irrigation infrastructure, including the extensive Inca canal networks.

entities_mentioned.name: cacique
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Indigenous local chieftain or lord
entities_mentioned.notes: A term of Caribbean (Taíno) origin adopted broadly in colonial Spanish to denote indigenous leaders at the local or regional level.

entities_mentioned.name: avila
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Possibly Quechua 'ayllu' (lineage/kinship group)
entities_mentioned.notes: The word 'avila' as used here in the context of lineage and communal land tenure almost certainly refers to the Quechua concept of 'ayllu' — the fundamental Andean kinship and community unit. The OCR or manuscript orthography may have rendered 'ayllu' as 'avila'. Flagged for scholarly review.

entities_mentioned.name: mazorca de maíz
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Ear of maize (Zea mays)
entities_mentioned.notes: Used here rhetorically to illustrate the extreme subdivision of communal harvests; not a medicinal or botanical description in this context.

results.translator_notes: 1. 'Avila' in the original almost certainly represents 'ayllu' (Quechua: kinship/community group), a critical concept in Andean social organization. The OCR or manuscript source renders it inconsistently. Scholars should verify against manuscript witnesses. 2. The phrase 'pariente mayor' is a specific Iberian legal/social term denoting the senior male representative of a lineage who holds and administers entailed family property — Cobo is deliberately mapping Inca communal land customs onto familiar Spanish juridical concepts. 3. This segment contains no botanical or medical content; the entities_mentioned section reflects accordingly. 4. 'Bienes raíces' is a legal term meaning immovable property/real estate, preserved in translation for precision. 5. OCR errors silently corrected include: 'absohito' → 'absoluto'; 'xin' → 'un'; 'renumeración' → 'remuneración'; 'repxiblica' → 'república'; 'jiartir' → 'partir'; 'Jirarla' → 'sembrarla'; 'i>' → 'por'; 'la*' → 'las'; 'pc^r' → 'por'; 'e®' → 'en'; 'conumi-dad' / 'comuni-dad' → 'comunidad'.
