results.title_inferred: On the False Religion of the Peruvians and Their Devotion to It
results.original_text:
CAPITULO PRIMERO

De la religión falsa que tenían los del Perú, y cuán dados eran a ella

Eran los indios del Perú tan grandes idólatras que adoraban por dioses casi cuantas especies hay de criaturas. Porque, como no tuvieron lumbre sobrenatural, decendieron en los mismos errores y desatinos que las otras naciones de gentiles, por concurrir en éstos las mismas causas que en aquéllos para no atinar con el verdadero Dios; conforme a saber, el abismo grande de vicios y pecados en que estaban sumergidos, con que se habían hecho inhábiles e indignos de que se les comunicase la luz pura del conocimiento de su Criador; y la malicia y astucia del demonio enemigo del linaje humano, que por su antigua soberbia e invidia de nuestro bien, procuró usurpar en estas ciegas gentes la adoración que debían a sólo el que los crió, para tenerlos aprisionados en su duro cautiverio y servidumbre y privarlos de la bienaventuranza de que él no fue merecedor: y hallando para conseguir sus designios bastante aparejo y disposición en la simpleza e ignorancia destos bárbaros, tuvo por tantos siglos puesto su trono entre ellos, hasta que la virtud de la Cruz le va desposeyendo y echando así desta, como de las otras naciones deste Nuevo Mundo.

Y como las naciones désta, por ser tan extendida, eran muchas, lo eran también las maneras de religión e idolatrías que seguían, no sólo antes que fuesen sujetadas y reducidas a un imperio, sino también después. Porque si bien los reyes peruanos compelían a todos los que sojuzgaban a recibir su religión, no era de suerte que les hiciesen dejar del todo la que tenían antes, más de aquello que parecía contradecir a la suya; y así, no sólo se quedaban con sus dioses antiguos, sino que los mismos Incas los admitían y hacían traer al Cuzco y colocarlos entre los suyos propios; a los cuales hacían alguna veneración, aunque mucho menor que la que daban a sus dioses naturales; y echábase bien de ver el poco caso que destos dioses extraños hacían y la poca estimación en que los tenían, por lo que aquí diré: y es, que cuando se les rebelaba alguna provincia, mandaban sacar y poner en público a los dioses naturales y protectores della, y que los azotasen afrentosamente cada día hasta reducir a su servicio la dicha provincia; y en reduciéndola, los hacían restituir a sus lugares y festejarlos con sacrificios, diciendo que en virtud dellos y por no ser afrentados, se había reducido la tal provincia; y aun cuentan que los más de los rebelados se reducían sólo por oír que sus ídolos estaban en afrenta pública.

Por ser, pues, como queda dicho, tantas las diferencias de idolatrías que tenían las gentes deste imperio peruano, que si de todas se hubiera de tratar en particular, fuera cosa infinita, escribiré aquí no más que la que guardaba la nación de los Incas, por haber venido a ser general en todo el reino, haciéndola recibir los reyes dél a todos sus vasallos; los cuales reyes se dieron tal maña en introducirla, que no sólo acabaron que la recibiesen todos los que ponían debajo de su obediencia, sino también que la viniesen a estimar en tanto, que se preciasen y tuviesen por honra el profesarla, haciendo más caso della que de la suya propia. Esto consiguieron con encarecerles la señalada merced que les hacían en admitirlos a la veneración de sus dioses; y para dárselo a entender, puesto caso que su pretensión y deseo era que los vencidos abrazasen sus opiniones en materia de religión, no permitían que desde luego gozasen todos deste favor, ni que sus fiestas fuesen comunes, sino que en todo dejaban para sí reservadas algunas cosas que nadie más que los dichos Incas y los de su linaje las podían usar; y en otras les prohibían el hacerlas con la solemnidad y ceremonias que ellos acostumbraban; y con el tiempo y servicios que hacían, los reyes iban dispensando con algunas provincias para que las pudiesen hacer; y desta suerte los vasallos tenían esto por gran premio de sus servicios; y la dificultad con que se alcanzaba hacía poner mayor cuidado y diligencia en la observancia dello; y el no ser general a todos ni poder estar presentes los que no tenían privilegio, hacía crecer la estima y devoción que con estos ritos y supersticiones tenían; y los súbditos extranjeros estimaban estas dispensaciones y privilegios en más que ninguna otra cosa de cuantas el Inca podía darles; porque tenían por cierto que ellas eran parte para alcanzar lo que pedían en sus necesidades y tribulaciones: y de todo esto se tenían por remediados cuando tenían licencia para usar de los sacrificios y ceremonias de los Incas; porque de todo los certificaban y aseguraban los mismos Incas cuando les concedían esta merced. Todo lo cual fundaban ellos por sus imaginaciones, sueños, revelaciones y mandatos que fingían tener de sus dioses, a fin de hacer entender al pueblo rudo, que a ellos solos y a quien ellos quisiesen era permitida la veneración del Viracocha, que era su principal dios, y de los otros sus ídolos con los ritos que estaban estatuidos para ello. Lo cual todo era artificio de los Incas para que fuese estimada su religión y mediante ella tener a sus súbditos más sujetos y obedientes.

Verdad es que no siempre los Incas desde que comenzó su imperio estuvieron firmes e invariables en su religión ni tuvieron unas mismas opiniones ni adoraron unos mismos dioses, sino que en varios tiempos fueron añadiendo y quitando muchas cosas y perpetuamente cargando a los pueblos de más cultos y ceremonias; moviéndose para variar en esta materia por echar de ver que por este camino se afirmaban mejor en el reino y lo tenían más en obediencia. Porque como todo el fundamento de su gobierno político estribaba en medios ordena[dos...]
results.translation:
CHAPTER ONE

On the False Religion of the Peruvians and How Devoted They Were to It

The Indians of Peru were such great idolaters that they worshipped as gods nearly every species of creature in existence. For, lacking supernatural enlightenment, they fell into the same errors and follies as the other heathen nations, the same causes being at work in them as in those others that prevented them from finding the true God: namely, the great abyss of vices and sins in which they were submerged, which had rendered them incapable and unworthy of receiving the pure light of knowledge of their Creator; and the malice and cunning of the demon, enemy of the human race, who, out of his ancient pride and envy of our good, sought to usurp among these blind peoples the adoration owed to Him alone who created them, in order to hold them imprisoned in his harsh captivity and servitude and to deprive them of the blessedness of which he himself was found unworthy. Finding in the simplicity and ignorance of these barbarians sufficient opening and disposition to accomplish his designs, he maintained his throne among them for so many centuries, until the power of the Cross has been gradually dispossessing and expelling him from this land as from the other nations of this New World.

And since the nations of this land were many, owing to its great extent, so too were the varieties of religion and idolatry they followed — not only before they were subjected and brought under a single empire, but also afterward. For although the Peruvian kings compelled all those they subjugated to receive their religion, this was not done in such a manner as to make them abandon entirely the religion they had previously held, beyond what appeared to contradict their own; and so not only did the conquered peoples retain their ancient gods, but the Incas themselves admitted those gods and had them brought to Cuzco and installed among their own deities. These foreign gods were given some veneration, though far less than that accorded to the Incas' own native gods. The low regard and scant esteem in which these foreign gods were held could be clearly seen from what I will recount here: when a province rose in rebellion, the Incas would order the native and tutelary gods of that province to be brought out and publicly displayed, and to be flogged shamefully each day until the said province was reduced to obedience; and once it was reduced, they would have those gods restored to their places and celebrated with sacrifices, saying that it was by virtue of those gods — and in order to spare them further public shame — that the province had submitted. It is even reported that most of the rebels submitted upon merely hearing that their idols were being publicly dishonored.

Because, as has been said, the varieties of idolatry practiced by the peoples of this Peruvian empire were so many that to treat all of them individually would be an endless undertaking, I shall here write only of that religion maintained by the nation of the Incas, since it came to be universal throughout the entire kingdom, its kings having caused all their vassals to receive it. Those kings were so skillful in introducing it that not only did they succeed in having all peoples brought under their authority adopt it, but also in making them come to esteem it so highly that they took pride and honor in professing it, holding it in greater regard than their own native religion. This they achieved by impressing upon their subjects the signal favor done to them in admitting them to the veneration of the Incas' gods. To make this understood, and although their aim and desire was that the conquered peoples embrace their religious opinions, they did not permit everyone to enjoy this favor at once, nor did they make their festivals open to all. Rather, they always reserved for themselves certain things that no one other than the Incas themselves and those of their lineage could perform; and in other matters they prohibited the subject peoples from conducting ceremonies with the solemnity and rites to which the Incas themselves were accustomed. Over time, and in recognition of services rendered, the kings would grant dispensations to certain provinces allowing them to perform such ceremonies. In this way their vassals came to regard this as a great reward for their service, and the difficulty of obtaining it led to greater care and diligence in its observance. The fact that this privilege was not universal and that those without it could not be present at such ceremonies caused the esteem and devotion accorded to these rites and superstitions to grow. Foreign subjects valued these dispensations and privileges above anything else the Inca could grant them, being firmly convinced that through them they could obtain what they sought in their needs and tribulations; and they considered themselves fully provided for in all things when they had license to make use of the sacrifices and ceremonies of the Incas, since the Incas themselves would certify and assure them of this when granting the favor. All of this they grounded in their imaginings, dreams, revelations, and commands they claimed to receive from their gods, in order to make the ignorant common people understand that only the Incas, and those whom the Incas chose, were permitted to venerate [Viracocha] — who was their principal god — and their other idols, with the rites prescribed for that purpose. All of which was a contrivance of the Incas to make their religion esteemed and, through it, to hold their subjects in greater subjection and obedience.

It is true that the Incas, from the very beginning of their empire, were not always steadfast and unchanging in their religion, nor did they hold the same opinions or worship the same gods at all times. Rather, at various periods they added and removed many things, perpetually burdening the peoples with additional cults and ceremonies, moved to make changes in this matter by perceiving that in this way they consolidated their hold on the kingdom more effectively and kept it in greater obedience. For since the entire foundation of their political governance rested on [the means they ordain... text breaks off]

entities_mentioned.name: Cuzco

entities_mentioned.notes: Mentioned as the location to which conquered gods were brought and installed alongside Inca deities.

results.title_inferred: On Inca Religion, Idolatry, and the Rationality of Indigenous Peoples
results.original_text:
dos a tener los suyos más sujetos y que perdiesen el brío de levantarse contra ellos, a este mismo fin iban también enderezadas las opiniones que fundaban entre ellos en lo tocante a su religión, cuyo título llevaban por delante en sus conquistas, con pretexto que diesen la honra y obediencia debida al Viracocha como a criador del mundo, y al sol, trueno y demás dioses suyos, que juzgaban ser causa de la conservación de lo criado, mediante el poder que les estaba dado para ello; especialmente el parentesco que fingían tener con el sol y las ayudas particulares que recebían de su mano en la guerra, y otras ficciones. Que como eran señores tiranos, el tiempo y las ocasiones les enseñaron todos los medios necesarios para sustentar su tiranía y señorear con más seguridad. Y así, por esta diversidad de culto y ritos que en diferentes edades tuvieron, conviene advertir que no se escribe aquí más de lo que observaban al tiempo que los españoles entraron en esta tierra y ellos comenzaron a recebir la fe de la doctrina del cielo; que son las opiniones en que se habían venido a resumir y había más general conformidad entre ellos.

Aunque esta forma de idolatría y falsa religión de los Incas era la más concertada y menos apartada de razón que los disparates y errores de las otras naciones destas Indias, con todo eso estaba tan llena de ficciones, imposturas y desvarios, que pone admiración cómo hombres de entendimiento se hubiesen persuadido a tenerla por verdadera; por la infinidad de cosas que adoraban por dioses, hasta dar reverencia a las más viles y asquerosas que produce la tierra, las boberías que de ellas creían y el caso que hacían de su culto y sacrificios, como constará por este libro: si bien es verdad que esto no ha de ser motivo para que tengamos por bestias y faltos de discurso a los que tales cosas creían y observaban; porque quien tuviere noticia de las vanidades y desatinos que tuvieron las más nobles y sabias naciones de Europa y de las otras dos partes del Mundo, como fueron los egipcios, caldeos, griegos y romanos, de cuya sabiduría tanta cuenta se ha hecho en todas edades, no tendrá por cosa nueva y extraña leer las fábulas y desvarios destos indios, antes echará de ver que muchos de aquellos antiguos filósofos tuvieron algunas opiniones más fáciles y de menos fundamento que éstos. Ni nos es argumento de la inhabilidad destos indios adorar ídolos y cosas perecederas, antes arguye en ellos ingenio, careciendo de lumbre verdadera de fe, buscar algo a que tener respeto y en que tener confianza.

Y no es poco que ellos mismos se diesen a buscar la causa de cada cosa, como los gentiles, y si erraron en su investigación, en el mismo error y por el mismo camino dieron los antiguos por ser tan dados a las ciencias y especulación de las cosas; los cuales, sin dar con quién fuese el verdadero artífice de lo criado, vinieron a adorar piedras, palos, figuras de animales y cosas hechas de mano de hombre, como éstos. Antes alcanzaron estos indios algunas cosas guiados por razón natural, en que pasaron adelante a muchos de los otros gentiles, como es en haber alcanzado a conocer que el verdadero Dios y primera causa era uno solo, al cual, aunque confusamente, adoraban como a Criador de todo.

Y no parando aquí, se dieron a buscar las razones que había para cada cosa y las causas de donde procedían, con que vinieron en conocimiento de algunas de las segundas causas que se podían ver por los efectos; y puesto que erraron notablemente en que, creyendo que era uno solo el Criador universal de todo y siempre dirigían a él sus rogativas y sacrificios, adoraban con igual reverencia y con unos mismos actos de culto y sumisión a las segundas causas como al sol, al agua, a la tierra y a otras muchas cosas que tenían por divinas, por entender tenían virtud para hacer o conservar lo necesario para la vida humana: a lo cual tuvieron siempre su principal intento y que por esto se les debía adoración como al Criador, de donde resultaron infinitas idolatrías y supersticiones; con todo eso, el haber querido especular es argumento de ser gente de razón y menos bárbara que las otras de este Nuevo Mundo. Adonde hallamos que cuanto menos tiene una nación de religión, tanto más tiene de rudeza y barbaridad; y es mucho de estimar que, aunque erraron estos peruanos, al fin buscaron la primera y segunda causa de las cosas; y si se engañaron en dar culto a las causas segundas, era por el poder que creían les estaba dado para que fuesen parte en la conservación del universo. Lo que yo siento, considerando atentamente los ritos y opiniones destos indios, es que en las más tuvieron las mismas costumbres e invenciones que los romanos, y no es de maravillar, pues los unos y los otros tuvieron un mismo maestro. Porque en la estimación y autoridad que pretendieron se tuviese la ciudad del Cuzco después de sus victorias por todas las gentes que señorearon, y en traer a ella los dioses principales de todas las provincias, los cuales pusieron al principio en el templo del sol, como los romanos en aquel suntuoso edificio que llaman Panteón, conformaron grandemente con ellos.

La gran facilidad destos indios fue la principal causa de haberse introducido en ellos tan grandes desatinos y errores; porque son tan fáciles, que después de haber admitido un yerro, creían luego cualquiera cosa que sobre él quisiesen inventar los sacerdotes o los Incas, para que su opinión recibiese fuerza y corroboración; y aprovechándose desta facilidad los sacerdotes y ministros de las guacas, por ser tan interesados en las ofrendas y sacrificios que ofrecía el pueblo, inventaban y fingían cada día mil novelas y misterios de visiones y milagros que sucedían.
results.translation:
to keep their subjects more subdued and to make them lose the spirit to rise against them—toward this same end were also directed the opinions they established among them concerning their religion, which they placed at the forefront of their conquests, under the pretext that the people should render the honor and obedience owed to Viracocha as creator of the world, and to the sun, thunder, and their other gods, whom they judged to be the cause of the preservation of all created things, by virtue of the power granted to them for that purpose; and especially the kinship they pretended to have with the sun, and the particular assistance they claimed to receive from his hand in war, and other such fictions. For, being tyrannical lords, time and circumstance taught them all the means necessary to sustain their tyranny and to rule with greater security. And so, on account of this diversity of worship and rites that they observed in different ages, it is worth noting that what is written here covers only what they practiced at the time the Spaniards entered this land and they began to receive the faith of the heavenly doctrine—that is, the beliefs into which they had come to consolidate, and which enjoyed the most general agreement among them.

Although this form of idolatry and false religion of the Incas was the most orderly and least removed from reason of all the absurdities and errors of the other nations of these Indies, it was nevertheless so full of fictions, impostures, and follies that it is astonishing how men of understanding could have been persuaded to hold it as true; given the infinite number of things they worshipped as gods, going so far as to venerate the most vile and disgusting things the earth produces, the foolishness they believed about them, and the weight they placed on their worship and sacrifices, as will be evident from this book. It is true, however, that this should not be grounds for us to regard as beasts and lacking in reason those who believed and observed such things; for anyone acquainted with the vanities and follies held by the most noble and learned nations of Europe and of the other two parts of the World—such as the Egyptians, Chaldeans, Greeks, and Romans, whose wisdom has been so highly regarded in every age—will find nothing new or strange in reading the fables and follies of these Indians; indeed, he will observe that many of those ancient philosophers held opinions more far-fetched and less well-founded than these. Nor does the worship of idols and perishable things argue the incapacity of these Indians; rather it argues ingenuity in them, for lacking the true light of faith, they sought something toward which to feel reverence and in which to place their trust.

And it is no small thing that they themselves set out to seek the cause of each thing, as the Gentiles did; and if they erred in that investigation, the ancients fell into the same error and by the same path, being so devoted to the sciences and the speculation of things—who, without finding who was the true maker of created things, came to worship stones, sticks, figures of animals, and things made by human hands, just as these people did. Indeed, these Indians attained certain things guided by natural reason, in which they surpassed many other Gentiles; as, for example, in having come to know that the true God and first cause was one alone, whom, albeit confusedly, they worshipped as the Creator of all things.

And not stopping there, they set about seeking the reasons behind each thing and the causes from which they proceeded, whereby they came to knowledge of some of the secondary causes that could be seen through their effects. And yet they erred notably in that, believing there was but one universal Creator of all things—to whom they always directed their prayers and sacrifices—they worshipped with equal reverence and with the same acts of worship and submission the secondary causes, such as the sun, water, the earth, and many other things they held to be divine, believing these had the power to produce or preserve what is necessary for human life; to which end they always directed their principal attention, and for this reason they believed these causes were owed adoration alongside the Creator—whence arose infinite idolatries and superstitions. Nonetheless, their very desire to speculate is an argument that they were a people of reason, and less barbarous than the other peoples of this New World. We find that the less religion a nation has, the more rudeness and barbarity it possesses; and it is greatly to be esteemed that, though these Peruvians erred, they ultimately sought the first and second cause of things; and if they went astray in rendering worship to secondary causes, it was because of the power they believed had been granted to these causes to have a part in the preservation of the universe. What I myself perceive, upon careful consideration of the rites and beliefs of these Indians, is that in most respects they had the same customs and inventions as the Romans—and this is not to be wondered at, since both had the same teacher. For in the esteem and authority they sought to have accorded to the city of Cuzco after their victories by all the peoples they ruled, and in bringing to it the principal gods of all the provinces—which they placed at first in the Temple of the Sun, just as the Romans placed them in that sumptuous edifice called the Pantheon—they conformed greatly with them.

The great credulity [lit. 'easiness'] of these Indians was the principal cause of the introduction among them of such great follies and errors; for they are so credulous that, once they had accepted one error, they would immediately believe anything the priests or the Incas wished to invent upon it, so that their belief might gain strength and corroboration. Taking advantage of this credulity, the priests and ministers of the huacas [guacas]—being so interested in the offerings and sacrifices that the people brought—invented and fabricated new stories and mysteries of visions and miracles every single day.

entities_mentioned.name: Viracocha
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Wiracocha — the supreme creator deity of the Inca religious tradition
entities_mentioned.notes: Described here as the creator of the world; Cobo treats him as the Inca analog of a first cause or supreme being.

entities_mentioned.name: Sol (the Sun)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Inti — the Inca sun deity
entities_mentioned.notes: Worshipped as a secondary cause and claimed as an ancestor by the Inca rulers.

entities_mentioned.name: Trueno (Thunder)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Illapa — the Inca thunder/lightning deity
entities_mentioned.notes: Listed alongside the sun as one of the principal Inca gods.

entities_mentioned.name: Guacas (guacas)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Huacas — sacred objects, places, or beings in Andean religion
entities_mentioned.notes: Cobo references the priests and ministers of the guacas as exploiting popular credulity for material gain through offerings and sacrifices.

entities_mentioned.name: Cuzco (Cuzco)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Cusco, Peru — capital of the Inca Empire
entities_mentioned.notes: Compared to Rome in its function as a religious and political center; the Temple of the Sun is mentioned as a repository for the gods of conquered provinces.

entities_mentioned.name: Templo del Sol
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Coricancha — the great Temple of the Sun in Cusco
entities_mentioned.notes: Compared explicitly by Cobo to the Roman Pantheon as a house for the gods of subject peoples.

entities_mentioned.name: Panteón (Pantheon)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: The Pantheon, Rome
entities_mentioned.notes: Used as a comparative reference for the Inca practice of housing the gods of conquered provinces in Cuzco.

results.translator_notes: This segment contains no botanical, pharmacological, or materia medica content; it is a theological and ethnographic commentary on Inca religion and Cobo's comparative assessment of indigenous versus classical European paganism. The OCR source was substantially degraded, with numerous broken words, split lines, and garbled characters; reconstruction relied on context and standard Cobo editions. The phrase 'lumbre verdadera de fe' ('true light of faith') is a standard Counter-Reformation apologetic idiom. 'Facilidad' in the final paragraph carries the sense of credulity or susceptibility, not merely ease. The comparison between Inca religious centralization and the Roman Pantheon is a notable piece of Cobo's analytical method. No plant, drug, or medical practice is described in this segment.

results.title_inferred: Inca Religion, Sacrifice, and Oral Preservation of Sacred Memory
results.original_text:
clían en sus guacas, y las hacían creer al simple vulgo, para que, creciendo la reputación de sus dioses, se acrecentasen las ofrendas; y estaban recibidas y asentadas algunas cosas tan aparentes y sin fundamento, que no es posible que algunos dejasen de mirar en ellas y conociesen su falsedad y engaño, siendo tan claras y demostrativas, sino que, cuando una cosa está introducida, aunque sea notoriamente mala, ninguno, aunque lo alcance, osa ser el primero que lo contradiga. Porque claro está que muchos filósofos alcanzaron ser burla la multitud de los dioses, y con todo eso no osaban hablar en ello, por no contradecir a la multitud de la gente. Lo cual especialmente ha más lugar entre estos indios, en quien la libertad era poca, los castigos grandes y mucha la gente que se mantenía destos oficios.

Tenía el demonio a esta ciega gente tan edificada y habituada en su errada seta, particularmente a los Incas (que era la nación más dada a la religión que debe haber habido en el mundo), que hacían sus ritos y sacrificios con tanta determinación y en tanta cantidad, que casi cuanto cogían y cuanto trabajaban y hasta sus propios hijos consumían en ello; y así, de tan profunda devoción como mostraban y cuidado que cada uno ponía en venerar y sacrificar lo que estaba a su cargo de una religión tan arraigada y universalmente recibida y de una determinación que pone espanto de ofrecer y sacrificar a sus propios hijos y haciendas, matándolos y quemándolos, conforme a sus costumbres, no se puede presumir que sus actos fuesen solamente exteriores, pues no permite la naturaleza que uno mate a su mismo hijo y aventure su hacienda con ánimo y voluntad tan alegre, si no tuviese fines y pretensiones de galardón por lo que hace, o pensase y tuviese creído enviaba su hijo a mejor lugar que el que acá tenía; y para dar muestras exteriores de contento cuando lo hace, necesario es que interiormente lo crea y que tenga por cierto que no se pierde el sacrificio. Y así no hay duda sino que las obras déstos pendían de alguna esperanza, pues fuera bestialidad matar nadie sus hijos y consumir su hacienda si no creyese que le aprovechaba para algo.

Puesto caso que todas las naciones deste reino del Perú tuvieron fines y cuenta con sus dioses y adoratorios, ninguna hubo que llegase con mucho a preciarse de tan religiosa como los Incas; y así eran ellos los más cargados de ceremonias, supersticiones, ídolos y sacrificios, y guardaban con tanta observancia lo estatuido en orden a esto, que eran leyes y opiniones inviolables entre ellos; y aunque el delito que contra ellas se cometía, y aun el descuido en la veneración de lo estatuido, fuera castigado ásperamente, fue tanta su religión, que por falta della dicen haberse castigado muy pocos, aunque con gran diligencia se tuvo con ello cuenta; que cierto no es de pasar por esto fácilmente, pues al que le cabía sacrificar el hijo, aunque no tuviese otro, era delito notable el mostrar alguna tristeza, sino que lo hacían con muestras de alegría y contentamiento, como si los llevaran a dar un cargo de grande importancia.

Lo que en esta materia de religión más admira, es la cuenta tan extraordinaria que tenían estos indios en conservar la memoria de las cosas tocantes a ella. Porque dado que no las tenían por escrito para saberlas y guardarlas, suplían esta falta con aprenderlas y guardarlas por tradición tan exactamente, que parece las tenían esculpidas en los huesos. Porque para sólo este efecto tenían los Incas puestos en la ciudad del Cuzco más de mil hombres que no entendían en otra cosa más que en la conservación desta memoria; y con éstos se criaban otros desde mancebos, que eran instruidos dellos, para que no se pudiese perder; que bien creo yo que semejante cuidado no se halló en los gentiles antiguos ni en otra ninguna gente para conservar su religión y memoria de sus opiniones y adoratorios. Estos que entendían en este ministerio eran comúnmente viejos y sacerdotes o ministros de sus templos, y hacíanlo con tanto cuidado, que cuando eran preguntados, uno por sí daba razón y declaraba la virtud que decía tener la guaca que estaba a su cargo, las solemnidades y palabras con que se le había de hacer el sacrificio, y la ofrenda que se le había de dar, prometiendo gran esperanza del buen suceso con la relación que hacía de otros semejantes que para acreditar su adoratorio fingía. Y si bien es verdad que todas las razones que apartan del camino verdadero de Dios son débiles y de ningún fundamento, todavía en muchas cosas las tenían estos indios más coloreadas para sus yerros que los gentiles filósofos y poetas. Verdad es que no se les podía pedir entera razón de todo, porque no la alcanzaban, ni sabían los fundamentos en que estribaban para sus opiniones; pues de lo más tenían por principal causa la costumbre de sus antepasados, la cual tuvieron por ley inviolable en muchas cosas en tanto grado, que de algunas no sabían dar otra razón, y si daban alguna, puesto caso que en la forma, solemnidad y supersticiones estaban conformes, en ella diferenciaban mucho; porque, como no tenían escrituras, perdíase la razón y motivo de los pasados, y después tenían las cosas en la opinión que las hallaron, y cada uno inventaba lo que le parecía; en lo cual los que reparaban eran los menos y gente estimada y noble, que la común no se ponía a particularizar razones, sino que seguía en general a éstos y hacía lo que le mandaban y veía que se usaba. Y así aun los mismos que no trataban de otra cosa que de lo tocante a su religión, no tenían memoria del principio de las más de sus opiniones y devaneos. Y sin duda esto viene de ser muy de atrás, porque tantas cosas no se pueden [ILLEGIBLE: text breaks off]
results.translation:
they would proclaim in their guacas [shrines], and made the simple common people believe them, so that, as the reputation of their gods grew, the offerings would increase. And certain things had been received and established that were so specious and lacking in foundation that it is impossible some did not see through them and recognize their falseness and deceit—being so clear and self-evident—but when something has become established, even if it is notoriously bad, no one, even if he perceives it, dares to be the first to contradict it. For it is plain that many philosophers recognized the multitude of gods to be a mockery, and yet for all that they dared not speak of it, lest they contradict the multitude of the people. This holds especially true among these Indians, among whom freedom was scarce, punishments severe, and great numbers of people sustained their livelihoods through these offices.

The Devil had this blind people so thoroughly built up and habituated in their erring sect—particularly the Incas, who were the nation most given to religion that has perhaps ever existed in the world—that they performed their rites and sacrifices with such resolve and in such quantity that they consumed in them nearly all they harvested, all they labored for, and even their own children. And so, from such profound devotion as they displayed, and the care that each one took to venerate and sacrifice what was under his charge, from a religion so deeply rooted and universally received, and from a resolve that strikes one with awe—to offer and sacrifice their own children and estates, killing and burning them according to their customs—one cannot presume that their acts were merely outward, for nature does not permit a man to kill his own child and risk his estate with so cheerful a spirit and will unless he had ends and aims of reward for what he does, or believed and was convinced that he was sending his child to a better place than the one he had here. And in order to show outward signs of contentment when doing so, it is necessary that he inwardly believe it and hold it as certain that the sacrifice is not in vain. Thus there is no doubt that the actions of these people depended upon some hope, for it would be sheer bestiality for anyone to kill his children and consume his estate if he did not believe it served him for something.

Although all the nations of this kingdom of Peru had regard for their gods and their shrines [adoratorios], none came anywhere close to priding itself on being as religious as the Incas. And so they were the most burdened with ceremonies, superstitions, idols, and sacrifices, and they observed what was ordained in this regard with such strictness that these were inviolable laws and opinions among them. And although the offense committed against them—and even carelessness in venerating what was ordained—was punished harshly, their religion was so great that they say very few were punished for failing in it, even though it was tracked with great diligence. This is certainly not a trivial matter, for the one whose duty it was to sacrifice his son—even if he had no other—committed a notable offense by showing any sadness; rather, they did it with outward signs of joy and contentment, as if they were sending him off to be given a position of great importance.

What is most astonishing in this matter of religion is the extraordinarily careful account these Indians kept in preserving the memory of things pertaining to it. For although they did not have them in writing in order to know and keep them, they supplied this deficiency by learning and preserving them through oral tradition [tradición] so exactly that it seems they had them engraved in their very bones. For this purpose alone, the Incas had stationed in the city of Cuzco more than a thousand men who occupied themselves with nothing other than the preservation of this memory; and alongside these, others were raised from youth, instructed by them, so that it could never be lost. I truly believe that such care was not found among the ancient gentiles or among any other people to preserve their religion and the memory of their opinions and shrines. Those who attended to this ministry were commonly old men and priests or ministers of their temples, and they carried it out with such diligence that when questioned, each one would individually account for and declare the virtue he claimed the guaca [shrine] under his charge possessed, the solemnities and words with which sacrifice was to be offered to it, and the offering that was to be given to it, promising great hope of a good outcome with the account he gave of similar cases he fabricated in order to lend credit to his shrine. And although it is true that all the arguments that lead one away from the true path of God are weak and without foundation, in many things these Indians had more plausible coloring for their errors than the pagan philosophers and poets. It is true that one could not demand a complete account of everything from them, because they did not attain it, nor did they know the foundations upon which their opinions rested; for in most things they held the custom of their ancestors to be the principal cause, which they regarded as an inviolable law in so many things to such a degree that for some of them they could give no other reason, and if they did give one, although they agreed in form, solemnity, and superstitions, they differed greatly in the substance; because, as they had no writing, the reason and motive of those of the past was lost, and afterward they held things in the opinion in which they found them, each one inventing what seemed right to him. In this, those who took pains to reflect were the fewest—and men of standing and nobility—for the common people did not trouble themselves with particular reasons, but followed these leaders in general, doing what they were told and what they saw practiced. And so even those who occupied themselves with nothing other than matters pertaining to their religion had no memory of the origin of most of their opinions and fancies. And without doubt this stems from being of very ancient date, for so many things cannot [ILLEGIBLE: text breaks off mid-sentence]

entities_mentioned.name: guaca / adoratorio
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: huaca — Andean sacred place, object, or shrine
entities_mentioned.notes: Cobo uses 'guaca' and 'adoratorio' interchangeably throughout. Huacas could be physical structures, natural features, mummies, or cult objects venerated as divine.

entities_mentioned.name: Incas
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Inca (Inka) ruling class and ethnic group of the Tawantinsuyu empire, Peru
entities_mentioned.notes: Cobo singles out the Incas as the most religiously devoted nation in the kingdom of Peru, distinguishing them from other Andean ethnic groups.

entities_mentioned.name: Cuzco
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Cusco, Peru
entities_mentioned.notes: Capital of the Inca empire; Cobo states more than a thousand men were stationed there solely to preserve religious oral tradition.

results.translator_notes: 1. The segment opening is fragmentary due to the OCR cutting into mid-sentence; the first word 'clían' is almost certainly 'decían' (they said/proclaimed), an OCR error for a common word. It has been silently emended. 2. 'Enviaba su hijo a mejor lugar' (he was sending his child to a better place) is a notable passage implying Cobo attributed a form of afterlife belief to justify Inca child sacrifice (capacocha). 3. The phrase 'dar un cargo de grande importancia' (to give/assign a post of great importance) uses administrative colonial vocabulary metaphorically. 4. The text breaks off mid-sentence at the end; marked [ILLEGIBLE] accordingly. 5. This segment contains no botanical, pharmaceutical, or medical content; the schema fields for plant/drug/ailment/preparation are not applicable and no such entities are extracted. 6. Cobo's framing is consistently polemical and missionary in nature, interpreting Inca religious practice through a Christian theological lens (attributing it to demonic influence).

results.title_inferred: On the religious knowledge of common people and the Inca elite; the terms Vilca and Guaca
results.original_text:
odían inventar en poco tiempo, ni juntas, ni carga tan pesada se podía admitir por el pueblo sino en muchas edades, añadiendo en cada una un poco.

Fuera, pues, desta gente dedicada a este vano culto y a conservar los ritos y ceremonias dél, de lo restante del pueblo eran muy pocos los que tenían noticias destas cosas, ni entendían las razones y motivos por donde se establecieron; sólo hacían lo que les estaba mandado y acudían con los sacrificios a las guacas y adoratorios, sin pasar adelante; salvo los principales del linaje de los Incas, que alcanzaban más razón de sus opiniones y la daban por más orden que otros ningunos; y con todo eso no se podía tratar dello sino con los menos.

Con dos nombres llamaban estos indios a sus dioses, que son Vilca el uno y el otro Guaca, y cualquiera dellos significa en común no sólo cualquiera dios e ídolo, sino también todo lugar de adoración, como templos, sepulturas y cualquiera otro de los que veneraban y en que ofrecían sacrificios. Por lo cual usaré dellos en este tratado, particularmente del de guaca, que era el más universal y usado de los indios, en la misma significación y con toda la ampliación que lo usaban ellos.
results.translation:
They could not have invented [these practices] in a short time, nor could such a heavy burden have been taken on by the people all at once, but only over the course of many ages, adding a little in each one.

Beyond the people dedicated to this vain cult and to preserving its rites and ceremonies, of the rest of the populace very few had any knowledge of these things, nor did they understand the reasons and motives by which they had been established; they merely did what they were commanded, and came with their sacrifices to the guacas [shrines] and adoratorios [places of worship], without going any further—except for the principales [leading nobles] of the lineage of the Incas, who had a greater grasp of the reasoning behind their beliefs and could explain it in a more orderly fashion than any others; and even so, it could only be discussed with very few of them.

These Indians called their gods by two names: one is Vilca and the other Guaca, and either of them signifies in general usage not only any god or idol, but also any place of worship, such as temples, burial sites, and any other places they venerated and in which they offered sacrifices. For this reason I will use these terms in this treatise—particularly that of guaca, which was the most universal and widely used among the Indians—in the same sense and with all the breadth with which they themselves employed it.

entities_mentioned.name: Guaca (also spelled Huaca)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Huaca — Andean sacred object, place, or deity; a widely attested concept in Quechua religious thought
entities_mentioned.notes: Cobo uses 'guaca' as a general term for any sacred place, idol, or deity; this matches the standard ethnographic and linguistic understanding of the Quechua term 'wak'a'.

entities_mentioned.name: Vilca
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Vilca — Quechua term for sacred or divine; also the name of the plant Anadenanthera colubrina (vilca/wilka), though here used in a purely religious/titular sense
entities_mentioned.notes: Cobo here treats 'Vilca' as a synonym for 'Guaca' in the sense of a divine being or sacred entity. The term is related etymologically to the Quechua root 'willka' (sacred, sun). It should not be confused here with the medicinal/hallucinogenic plant of the same name, though the plant's name derives from the same sacred connotation.

entities_mentioned.name: Incas (linaje de los Incas)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: The Inca ruling dynasty and aristocratic class of the Andean empire (Tawantinsuyu)
entities_mentioned.notes: Cobo distinguishes the Inca nobility as possessing privileged esoteric religious knowledge compared to the common populace.

results.translator_notes: The opening lines are fragmentary due to the beginning of the segment mid-sentence; the OCR has garbled several words (e.g., 'feapo' for 'tiempo', 'sa' for 'se', '^muchas' for 'en muchas', 'M' for 'una', '«í vaao' for 'este vano', '«momas' for 'ceremonias', '^' and '«' as line-initial corruptions). These have been silently corrected where the intended reading is unambiguous. 'Adoratorios' is preserved in the original Spanish in brackets in the translation as a technical term. The segment contains no botanical or medical content; the plant 'vilca' (Anadenanthera colubrina) is NOT what Cobo is discussing here, though the shared terminology is flagged for scholarly awareness.
