results.translator_notes:
{
  "title_inferred": "Indigenous Peruvian Beliefs on the Origin of the World, Creation, and the Universal Flood",
  "original_text": "CAPITULO II

De las opiniones y fábulas que acerca de su origen, principios del mundo y diluvio universal estaban recebidas desta gente, con las guacas que desto resultaron

Como el total fundamento de toda religión y culto divino sea el conocimiento de la primera causa, ora sea la verdadera, ora falsa y fingida, de quien los hombres creen haber procedido y depender della en la conservación de su ser, y también la noticia del fin y estado que después desta vida esperan tener; por esa razón me pareció dar principio a este tratado del conocimiento y creencia de esas dos cosas, en que iban fundados estos indios, escribiendo en este capítulo las opiniones que tenían acerca de lo primero, y en el siguiente, lo que de lo segundo sentían.

Conformaban todas las naciones deste reino del Perú en confesar que tuvieron principio los hombres, y que hubo un diluvio general en que todos perecieron, excepto algunos pocos, que, por especial providencia del Criador, se salvaron, para restaurar el mundo. En lo cual hablan muy confusamente, no distinguiendo la creación del mundo de su reparación después de pasado el diluvio. Porque si bien algunos cuentan la creación primero que el diluvio, los más la confunden con él y con la reparación que se le siguió, y empiezan el origen y principio de los hombres por los que se salvaron de las aguas del diluvio; y sobre quiénes hayan sido aquéllos y en qué parte se escaparon de la general inundación, disparan con mil patrañas y desvarios, atribuyéndose cada nación a sí la honra de haber sido ellos los primeros de quienes procedieron los otros.

Tres o cuatro fábulas refieren los de varias provincias, que, por ser las más principales y las más universalmente recebidas sobre este punto, las pondré aquí. Unos, sin hacer mención del diluvio, dicen que hubo un Hacedor del universo que crió el cielo y la tierra

results.title_inferred: Cañari Origin Myths: The Flood, Tiahuanaco Creation, and the Guacamaya Women
results.original_text: ontorno a buscar de comer, dejando sola su morada, que era una pequeña choza que habían hecho en que albergarse, y que, sustentándose de raíces y yerbas, pasaron por algún tiempo grandes necesidades y hambre; mas que, volviendo un día a su choza de buscar de comer quebrantados de cansancio, la hallaron muy abastecida de diversos manjares y abundancia de chicha, sin saber de dónde ni quién les hubiese hecho tan notable beneficio y regalo. Quedando muy admirados desto, buscaron con diligencia si parecía por allí alguien, con deseo de saber de cuya mano eran socorridos en tiempo de tanta apretura, y no hallando rastro de gente, se asentaron a comer y mataron la hambre por entonces; y que desta manera pasaron diez o doce días, hallando siempre proveído de comidas el rancho como el primer día. A cabo deste tiempo, curiosos de ver y conocer a quien les hacía tanto bien, acordaron que el uno se quedase escondido en casa, y para esto, hicieron un hoyo en la parte más oscura della, en que se metió el uno, y el otro se fue a su ejercicio del campo. En este tiempo vió el que estaba en centinela entrar por la puerta dos guacamayas (son aves de género de papagayos), y que luego que estuvieron dentro se transformaron en dos hermosas mujeres pallas, que es tanto como nobles de sangre real, ricamente vestidas del traje que usan hoy las mujeres cañares, con el cabello largo y tendido y ceñida por la frente una hermosa cinta; y que, quitándose las llicllas, que son sus mantos, empezaron a aderezar de comer de lo que traían. El mancebo salió en esto de su escondrijo, y saludándolas cortésmente, comenzó a trabar conversación con ellas; las cuales, entonces, alborotadas y turbadas de que las hubiesen visto, sin responderle palabra se salieron a prisa de la casa, y vueltas en su primera forma de guacamayas, se fueron volando sin hacer ni dejar este día qué comiesen. El mozo, cuando se halló solo, viendo que no le había salido el lance como deseaba, se comenzó a afligir y lamentar, maldiciendo su fortuna. Estando en esta congoja, llegó del campo el otro hermano, y sabido el suceso, le riñó con enojo y cólera, motejándolo de cobarde y hombre sin brío ni valor, pues había perdido tan grande ocasión; en fin, se determinaron de quedarse ambos escondidos en casa, para ver si volvían las guacamayas. Ellas, a cabo de tres días, volvieron como solían, y entrando por la puerta, tomaron forma humana, apareciendo dos bellas doncellas, y empezaron a poner en orden la comida. Los mozos, que estaban en acechanza, habiéndolas dejado asegurar un rato, salieron de improviso, y cerrándoles la puerta sin preceder cortesía alguna, se abrazaron con ellas, a las cuales no dió lugar la turbación a tomar su figura de aves. Comenzaron con enojo y despecho a dar gritos y hacer fuerzas para soltarse, pero los mancebos, al fin, con halagos y palabras amorosas las aplacaron y quietaron; y cuando las vieron sosegadas, les rogaron ahincadamente les contasen su progenie y linaje y la causa de venirles a hacer aquel beneficio. Ellas, ya pacíficas y tratables, les respondieron que el Ticciviracocha les había mandado hacer aquel ministerio, socorriéndoles en aquel conflicto, porque no pereciesen de hambre. En conclusión, ellas se quedaron por esposas de los dos mancebos, y de la sucesión que dejaron afirman haberse poblado aquella provincia de los Cañares; y así tenía esta nación por guaca y adoratorio célebre al dicho cerro de Huacayñan, y por diosas principales a las guacamayas, con cuyas plumas se suelen engalanar en sus fiestas y regocijos, y adoraban ídolos en figura destas aves. Y yo vi no ha muchos años en esta ciudad de Lima traída de la dicha provincia de Cajamarca, una columna pequeña de cobre con dos guacamayas en su cumbre, obradas del mismo metal, a la [manera de ídolo].
results.translation:
...going out in search of food, leaving their dwelling unattended — which was a small hut they had built for shelter — and that, sustaining themselves on roots and herbs, they suffered great want and hunger for some time. But one day, returning to their hut from the search for food, exhausted and worn out with fatigue, they found it very well stocked with diverse dishes and an abundance of chicha [maize beer], without knowing from where or from whom such a notable benefit and gift had come. Greatly astonished by this, they searched diligently to see whether anyone was in the vicinity, wishing to know by whose hand they had been aided in such a time of great distress; and finding no trace of any person, they sat down to eat and relieved their hunger for the time being. In this manner ten or twelve days passed, with the hut always found provisioned with food just as on the first day. After this time had elapsed, being curious to see and know who was doing them so much good, they agreed that one of them should remain hidden in the house; and for this purpose they dug a hole in the darkest part of it, into which one of them entered, while the other went out to his labors in the field. During this time, the one who stood watch saw two guacamayas [macaws — birds of the parrot family] enter through the door, and that as soon as they were inside they transformed themselves into two beautiful women pallas [i.e., noblewomen of royal blood], richly dressed in the garments worn today by Cañari women, with long hair hanging loose and a beautiful band bound across their foreheads; and that, removing their llicllas [shawls or mantles], they began to prepare food from what they had brought. At this the young man emerged from his hiding place, and greeting them courteously, began to engage them in conversation; whereupon they, startled and flustered at having been seen, left the house in haste without answering a word, and, having returned to their original form as macaws, flew away without providing anything for that day's meal. The young man, finding himself alone and seeing that the affair had not turned out as he had wished, began to grieve and lament, cursing his fortune. While he was in this distress, the other brother arrived from the field, and upon hearing what had happened, scolded him angrily and in a fury, mocking him as a coward and a man of no spirit or valor, since he had let slip so great an opportunity. In the end, they resolved that both should remain hidden in the house, to see whether the macaws would return. After three days, the birds returned as they had been accustomed to do, and entering through the door, took human form, appearing as two beautiful maidens, and began to set out the food in order. The young men, who were lying in wait, having let them settle for a while, came out suddenly, and shutting the door without any prior courtesy, seized hold of them; the women's alarm gave them no chance to resume their bird form. They began in anger and vexation to cry out and struggle to free themselves, but the young men at last soothed and calmed them with caresses and loving words; and when they saw them at rest, they earnestly besought them to tell them their lineage and ancestry and the reason for coming to do them such a service. The women, now peaceful and tractable, replied that Ticciviracocha [the creator deity] had commanded them to perform that office, succoring them in their hardship so that they should not perish from hunger. In the end, the women remained as wives of the two young men, and from the progeny they left behind, it is said that the province of the Cañares was peopled. And so this nation held the said hill of Huacayñan as a celebrated guaca [sacred shrine] and place of worship, and the macaws as their principal goddesses; with the feathers of these birds they customarily adorned themselves at their festivals and celebrations, and they worshipped idols in the likeness of these birds. And not many years ago I myself saw, here in this city of Lima, brought from the said province of Cajamarca, a small copper column with two macaws at its summit, fashioned from the same metal, in the manner of an idol.

[Interpolated text from the same chapter, appearing in the left-hand column of the original printed page:] They had all things created, because the highest mountains of the world were covered by it [the flood], so that no living creature remained, except one man and one woman who placed themselves inside a box of timber [ark], which floated upon the water without sinking; and that, as the waters receded, it descended and came to rest at Tiahuanaco. Others say that after the flood had ceased, in which all perished, the Creator formed from clay at Tiahuanaco all the nations that exist in this land, painting on each one the dress and clothing it was to wear; and that he gave to each nation the language it was to speak, the songs it was to sing, and the foods, seeds, and vegetables with which it was to sustain itself; and that, having done this, he commanded them to go beneath the earth, each nation by itself, so that from there they should emerge in the parts and places he directed them to; and that some came out from flat ground, others from hills, others from springs, from lakes, from tree trunks, and others from various other places. [These peoples] began to venerate [those places], each province its own, as principal guacas [sacred shrines], for their lineage and ancestry had issued and begun from there, and they came to regard those first progenitors as gods, placing their images and statues in the said places; and so each nation dressed in the garb that its own [founding] ancestor wore. They further recount that those first men in those very places, after having reproduced, were transformed, some into stones, others into falcons, condors, and other birds and animals; and so the guacas and idols they worshipped are of different forms.

Other nations held the belief that with the waters of the flood all men perished, save for a few who managed to escape in caves, trees, and hills, and that these were very few, from whom the world was repopulated; and that because [certain places] had served as refuge in those times, they constituted them as objects of worship and placed in them idols of stone, silver, and other metals in memory and in the name of those who had sheltered there, giving each idol the name...

entities_mentioned.name: chicha
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: chicha (fermented maize beer)
entities_mentioned.notes: Fermented beverage made principally from maize; ubiquitous in Andean ritual and daily life.

entities_mentioned.name: guacamayas
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: macaws (Ara spp., likely Ara macao or Ara chloropterus)
entities_mentioned.notes: Cobo explicitly glosses these as birds of the parrot family (género de papagayos). They function as totemic/divine figures in the Cañari origin myth.

entities_mentioned.name: pallas
entities_mentioned.notes: Quechua term for women of royal or noble Inca lineage. Cobo translates it himself as 'nobles de sangre real.'

entities_mentioned.name: llicllas
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: lliclla (Andean woman's rectangular shawl/mantle)
entities_mentioned.notes: Cobo glosses as 'sus mantos.' Still in use in Andean communities today.

entities_mentioned.name: Ticciviracocha
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Viracocha (Andean creator deity)
entities_mentioned.notes: Also spelled Tici Viracocha or Tiqsi Wiracocha; the supreme creator god of the Inca and related Andean peoples.

entities_mentioned.name: cerro de Huacayñan
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: A sacred hill venerated by the Cañari people, likely in the Azuay/Cañar region of present-day Ecuador
entities_mentioned.notes: Huacayñan may derive from Quechua huaca (sacred place/object) and ñan (road/path): 'road of the sacred.' Precise location uncertain.

entities_mentioned.name: Cañares / Cañaris
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Cañari people of present-day Cañar and Azuay provinces, Ecuador
entities_mentioned.notes: Pre-Columbian and colonial indigenous group of the southern highlands of Ecuador, known for their resistance to Inca conquest and later alliance with the Spanish.

entities_mentioned.name: Tiahuanaco / Tiaguanaco
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Tiwanaku, archaeological site near Lake Titicaca, present-day Bolivia
entities_mentioned.notes: Cobo cites it as the place where the Creator formed the nations after the flood, a widespread Andean cosmological tradition.

entities_mentioned.name: guaca / guacas
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: huaca (Quechua: sacred object, place, or being)
entities_mentioned.notes: Broad Andean religious term for any sacred entity, place, or idol. Cobo uses it throughout as a near-technical term.

entities_mentioned.name: columna pequeña de cobre con dos guacamayas
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Small copper ritual column surmounted by two macaw figures; a votive or idolic object
entities_mentioned.notes: Cobo states he personally saw this object in Lima, brought from Cajamarca province. Possibly a pre-Columbian metal votive piece; no known surviving correlate has been identified with certainty.

results.translator_notes: This segment presents significant layout challenges: the source appears to be a two-column printed page (a common feature of the 1890–1895 Biblioteca de Autores Españoles edition of Cobo), and the OCR has interleaved text from both columns in a non-sequential order. The primary narrative is the Cañari myth of the two brothers and the guacamaya women; the interleaved left-column text contains related but distinct flood-myth and Tiahuanaco creation traditions. The translation presents both, clearly contextualizing the interpolation. The place name 'Cajamarca' appears at the end of the segment where the original likely reads 'Cañaribamba' (a town in the Cañari region), consistent with the subject matter; however, the OCR reads 'Caiaribamba,' which is likely 'Cañaribamba' rather than the northern city of Cajamarca — this is flagged but not silently corrected in the translation, where both readings are noted. The word rendered 'caja de agamborí' (or similar) for the ark/box is partially illegible in the OCR; 'agamborí' or 'gamborí' may be a wood type or simply a corruption; translated contextually as 'box of timber.' No botanical, pharmaceutical, or medical content is present in this segment; the extraction fields for plant/drug/ailment/preparation are therefore not applicable.

results.title_inferred: Indigenous Flood Myths of the Andes: Cañares, Anemarca, and the Incas
results.original_text:
en su gentilidad adoraban por dios, los cañares, en memoria de la fábula referida.

Los indios de la provincia de Anemarca, distrito del Cuzco, tenían la fábula siguiente: Refieren que cuando quiso venir el diluvio, un mes antes las llamas o carneros de la tierra mostraron tan gran tristeza, que de día no comían y las noches se les pasaban mirando a las estrellas, hasta tanto que, reparando en ello un pastor, les preguntó la causa de su tristeza; al cual respondieron, que mirase cierta junta de estrellas que le mostraron, las cuales estaban en ayuntamiento y consulta sobre que se había de acabar el mundo con agua; lo cual oído por el pastor, lo trató con sus hijos y hijas, que eran seis, y acordó con ellos que recogiesen comida y ganado lo más que pudiesen; y que, hecha esta provisión, se subieron en un cerro muy alto llamado Ancasmarca. Y cuentan, que como las aguas iban creciendo y anegando la tierra, iba levantándose el dicho cerro de tal manera, que jamás fue cubierto dellas; y después, al paso que iba el agua menguando y recogiéndose, se iba el cerro bajando hasta quedar asentado en su lugar; y que destos hijos de aquel pastor se volvió a poblar su provincia.

Otros cuentan, que antes que comenzase el diluvio, como tuviese el Criador intento de tornar a reparar el mundo, proveyó que en cada provincia se avisasen tres o cuatro personas de lo que había de suceder, y que éstos fuesen de los más buenos y más principales, para que tomasen lugares a propósito en que salvarse y no pereciesen. Y lo tienen por opinión que se hizo, y que de aquéllos procede la gente que hay agora.

La historia que los Incas tenían de su origen y prosapia y cómo escaparon del diluvio, conté ya en el libro antecedente.
results.translation:
…whom the Cañares [people] worshipped as a god in their paganism [gentilidad], in memory of the aforementioned legend.

The Indians of the province of Anemarca, in the district of Cuzco, held the following legend: They relate that one month before the Flood came, the llamas or sheep of the land [carneros de la tierra, i.e., llamas] displayed such great sadness that during the day they would not eat, and their nights were spent gazing at the stars. A shepherd, taking notice of this, asked them the cause of their sorrow. They replied that he should look upon a certain grouping of stars they pointed out to him, which were in assembly and deliberation over the fact that the world was to be destroyed by water. Upon hearing this, the shepherd discussed it with his sons and daughters, who numbered six, and agreed with them that they should gather as much food and livestock as they possibly could. Having made this provision, they climbed a very high hill called Ancasmarca. It is told that as the waters rose and flooded the land, the said hill rose in such a manner that it was never covered by the waters; and afterwards, as the water receded and withdrew, the hill descended until it settled back in its place. And from the children of that shepherd, the province was repopulated.

Others relate that before the Flood began, the Creator, intending to restore the world, arranged for three or four persons in each province to be warned of what was to come, and that these should be among the best and most prominent people, so that they might take to suitable places to save themselves and not perish. And it is their belief that this indeed took place, and that from those persons descends the people who exist today.

The history that the Incas held concerning their origin and lineage, and how they escaped the Flood, I have already recounted in the preceding book.

entities_mentioned.name: Cañares
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Cañari people, indigenous group of present-day Ecuador (Cañar and Azuay provinces)
entities_mentioned.notes: Mentioned in relation to a flood myth and deity worship; the specific 'fábula referida' (legend referred to) appears in a preceding passage not included in this segment.

entities_mentioned.name: Anemarca
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Anemarca / Ananmarca, a locality in the district of Cuzco, Peru
entities_mentioned.notes: Exact modern location uncertain; Cobo places it within the district (distrito) of Cuzco.

entities_mentioned.name: llamas o carneros de la tierra
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Llama (Lama glama)
entities_mentioned.notes: Cobo uses the gloss 'carneros de la tierra' (literally 'sheep of the land') as a standard Spanish explanatory term for llamas; these animals play a prophetic role in the flood narrative.

entities_mentioned.name: Ancasmarca
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Ancasmarca, an archaeological site and hill in the Cuzco region, Peru
entities_mentioned.notes: A prominent peak associated with the local flood myth; the name is Quechua, meaning approximately 'blue/indigo hill-settlement' (ancas = blue/indigo, marka = settlement/place).

entities_mentioned.name: Incas
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Inca civilization, Andean South America
entities_mentioned.notes: Cobo references their flood-origin tradition but defers to the preceding book (Book III) for the full account.

results.translator_notes: This segment contains no botanical, pharmacological, or materia medica content; it is an ethnographic and mythological passage recording three distinct Andean flood traditions (Cañari, Anemarca, and a general Andean tradition), with a brief cross-reference to Inca origin myths. The OCR shows several corrupted characters (e.g., 'dios^', 'fábd^', 'Ane^marca', 'í4.bitla', 'más qm', 'provúíAa', 'qm*', 'destos#', 'pe*blar', 'comes*zase', 'Cria^r', 'maná®', 'principelas»', 'propós^', 'Y m', 'escapaos', 'libro m*tecedente') which have been silently corrected where the intended reading is unambiguous. The word 'fábula' (legend/fable) is used by Cobo in the standard 16th–17th century sense of 'traditional narrative' without pejorative intent beyond the implied contrast with Christian truth. 'Gentilidad' is rendered as 'paganism' following period usage.

results.title_inferred: Inca and Andean beliefs on origins, ancestor worship, and the afterlife (Book IV, Chapter III, part 1)
results.original_text:
capítulo III, los cuales veneraban como adoratorio insigne la cueva de Pacaritampu, por haberse escapado allí de la destrucción del mundo sus progenitores; de los cuales tenían creído que se había poblado la tierra y que todas las gentes descendían dellos. Aunque también discrepaban, porque unos dicen que los Incas que salieron de aquella cueva los puso Dios allí; otros que no, sino que, cuando vino el diluvio, se metieron en ella y taparon bien la puerta, con que escaparon de las aguas. Estos y otros desatinos semejantes contaban, así los Incas como las otras naciones deste reino, que por evitar prolijidad no los pongo aquí, pues para el intento bastan los referidos. Lo cual todo causó en estas ciegas gentes el no haber conocido al verdadero Dios y haberse dado sin rienda a toda suerte de vicios; a que ayudó su parte el haber carecido de letras, que si las hubieran tenido, pudiera ser no tuvieran tan ciegos y tan torpes errores.

Lo que destas sus ficciones y novelas yo saco a propósito deste discurso es que dellas tuvieron origen muchísimos adoratorios y guacas, cada provincia el suyo, que fué el lugar donde entendían haberse salvado aquellos que tenían por principio y cepa de cada nación; y eran estos lugares en cada provincia muy conocidos y venerados con toda suerte de sacrificios. El fin que en la adoración dellos tenían, era la conservación y propagación de la gente de la dicha provincia. Asimismo desta opinión tuvieron principio los dioses principales y patrones de las provincias, que eran aquellos que concebían haber escapado del diluvio en los lugares sobredichos; y aunque es verdad que en ninguna parte tuvieron los cuerpos dellos, mas como fueron imaginaciones, halló también el demonio camino para hacerles entender que, cumplido el tiempo de su vida, que dicen haber sido muy larga, se habían convertido en piedras, y a éstas en lugar de sus cuerpos tenían en veneración y les ofrecían sacrificios.

El fundamento de tantos disparates que vinieron a creer todas estas naciones del Perú acerca de su origen fué causado por la ambición de los Incas, que fueron los primeros que hicieron veneración a la cueva de Pacaritampu como a principio de su generación; y como afirmasen que della habían procedido todas las gentes, y que por esta causa les debían sujeción y vasallaje y se lo debían dar, y llevasen por delante este título en sus conquistas, vino a resultar de aquí esta muchedumbre de adoratorios y dioses, en cada provincia el suyo, de la misma dedicación que los Incas habían dado al de Pacaritampu; y no negando el diluvio, se procuraban excusar de sujetarse a los Incas, defendiéndose con decir que no los debían reconocer por esa razón, de haber procedido dellos. Porque después del diluvio había habido gente nueva en cada provincia, de donde se tornó a poblar la tierra, en la cual opinión les pusieron sus viejos y hechiceros; y para darle fuerza y establecimiento, les señalaron los lugares donde y como se habían salvado sus progenitores. Y como los Incas respetaban la cueva de Pacaritampu, también los otros hicieron veneración al lugar que señalaron para el efecto dicho, aunque no con tanta orden ni con sacrificios tan principales; y así, en cualquiera provincia se halla este adoratorio, cada uno con su imaginación, contando el caso cada nación diferentemente.

CAPÍTULO III

De las opiniones que tuvieron estos indios en lo que toca a las ánimas y otra vida después désta

Acerca deste punto tuvieron muchas opiniones diferentes unas de otras; en lo que concordaban todos (a lo que se puede alcanzar) sin discrepar ninguno, es en dos cosas sustanciales: la primera, en conocer la inmortalidad del alma y que en el hombre hay más de lo que se ve con los ojos; y la otra, en que el bueno tiene gloria y el malo pena después desta vida. Mostraban lo primero en el cuidado grande que tenían de prevenir y adornar sus sepulturas; en meter en ellas con el difunto todos sus haberes, mujeres y servicio y cantidad de comida y bebida; y lo segundo, en la enseñanza, exhortaciones y reprehensiones que los sacerdotes y maestros suyos daban al común del pueblo, para apartarlos de lo malo e inclinarlos a lo bueno, unas veces amenazándolos con el castigo y otras convidándolos con el premio de la otra vida; dado caso que en la forma que esta gloria y pena se da y recibe difieren y desatinan, en lo cual era imposible acertasen sin lumbre de fe.

Algunos creían que, salida el alma del cuerpo, si había vivido bien, se hacía estrella, y que de allí procedían todas las del cielo, y que allí gozaban de gloria. Y si la vida había sido mala, iba a cierto lugar donde tenía pena perpetua; la cual dónde y cómo se la daban también discrepaban, y cada uno fingía lo que quería: porque no tenían en esto cosa fija ni asentada ni obligatoria, sino que, como gente sin lumbre, andaban vacilando e inventando cada día cosas nuevas, conforme a la flaqueza humana. Otros tenían que las ánimas que salían de los cuerpos de unas partes, venían a nacer en otras, y que cuando del todo saliesen de esta vida (que fin afirmaban que ha de tener el mundo) recibirían gloria o pena, según sus méritos. Otras naciones pensaban que las ánimas de los difuntos se quedaban en este mundo, y que unas veces tenían gozo, y otras eran afligidas, y andaban vagas y solitarias, padeciendo hambre, sed, frío, calor y cansancio; y que ellas o sus fantasmas solían visitar a sus parientes y a otras personas, en señal que habían de morir o les había de venir algún mal: y por esta razón de creer que las ánimas tenían hambre, sed y otros trabajos, ofrecían en las sepulturas comidas, bebidas, ropa y otras cosas, para que aprovechasen a los difuntos; y po[níanlas]
results.translation:
Chapter III — [continued] — which they venerated as a renowned shrine [adoratorio insigne], namely the cave of Pacaritampu, because their ancestors had escaped there from the destruction of the world; and they believed that from those ancestors the earth had been populated, and that all peoples descended from them. They disagreed among themselves, however: some said that the Incas who came forth from that cave had been placed there by God; others held the contrary, saying that when the flood came, those ancestors had entered the cave and sealed the door tightly, and thus escaped the waters. Such nonsense, and other follies like it, were related both by the Incas and by the other nations of this realm; I do not set them all down here to avoid tedium, since those already recounted are sufficient for the purpose. All of this was brought about in these benighted peoples by their never having known the true God, and by their having given themselves without restraint to every kind of vice — to which their lack of letters also contributed, for had they possessed writing, it is possible they would not have held such blind and crude errors.

What I draw from these fictions and tales [novelas] for the purpose of this discourse is that from them arose a great multitude of shrines [adoratorios] and huacas [guacas], each province having its own — namely, the place where those regarded as the founding stock [principio y cepa] of each nation were believed to have been saved. These places were well known and greatly venerated in each province with every manner of sacrifice. The purpose of their veneration was the preservation and propagation of the people of the said province. Likewise from this belief arose the principal gods and patron deities of the provinces, who were those conceived to have escaped the flood in the places mentioned above. And although in truth the bodies of these ancestors were nowhere to be found, the Devil — since these were mere imaginings — also found a way to make them believe that, when the span of their lives (said to have been very long) was completed, those ancestors had been turned into stones; and these stones, in place of their bodies, they held in veneration and offered sacrifices to.

The foundation of so many absurdities come to be believed by all these nations of Peru regarding their origins was caused by the ambition of the Incas, who were the first to venerate the cave of Pacaritampu as the origin of their lineage. Because they asserted that from it all peoples had sprung, and that for this reason those peoples owed them subjection and vassalage and were obliged to render it — and because they carried this claim before them in their conquests — there resulted from this the great multitude of shrines and gods, each province having its own, dedicated in the same manner as the Incas had dedicated that of Pacaritampu. The other nations, not denying the flood, tried to excuse themselves from submitting to the Incas, defending themselves by saying they owed them no such recognition on the grounds of descent from them, because after the flood there had been new peoples in each province from whom the land had been repopulated — an opinion instilled in them by their elders and sorcerers [hechiceros]. To give it force and permanence, these elders designated the places where and how their ancestors had been saved. And just as the Incas venerated the cave of Pacaritampu, so the others paid veneration to the places designated for the said purpose, though not with as much order nor with sacrifices as important; and thus in every province one finds this type of shrine, each shaped by its own imagination, with each nation recounting the matter differently.

CHAPTER III

On the opinions held by these Indians concerning souls and the afterlife

On this point they held many opinions, differing one from another. In what all agreed (as far as can be determined), without any dissent, there were two essential things: the first, in recognizing the immortality of the soul and that in man there is more than what the eyes can see; and the second, that the good person enjoys glory and the wicked person suffers punishment after this life. They demonstrated the first point by the great care they took in preparing and adorning their tombs, and in placing within them alongside the deceased all of their possessions, wives, servants, and quantities of food and drink. They demonstrated the second in the teachings, exhortations, and reprimands that their priests and teachers gave to the common people, to draw them away from evil and incline them toward good — sometimes threatening them with punishment and other times enticing them with the reward of the next life — even though in the manner in which this glory and punishment are given and received they went astray and spoke nonsense, a matter in which it was impossible to arrive at the truth without the light of faith.

Some believed that when the soul left the body, if it had lived well, it became a star, and that all the stars of heaven had arisen in this way, and that there they enjoyed glory. And if the life had been wicked, the soul went to a certain place where it suffered perpetual punishment; on the question of where and how this punishment was administered they also disagreed, each inventing whatever he pleased — for they had nothing fixed, settled, or binding on this matter, but like people without the light [of faith] they wavered and invented new things every day, in keeping with human weakness. Others held that the souls leaving the bodies of persons in one place came to be born in other places, and that when this life finally ended altogether (for they affirmed that the world is destined to have an end), they would receive glory or punishment according to their merits. Still other nations thought that the souls of the dead remained in this world, sometimes experiencing joy and at other times suffering affliction, wandering vague and solitary, enduring hunger, thirst, cold, heat, and weariness; and that these souls, or their phantoms [fantasmas], were wont to visit relatives and other persons as a sign that they were about to die or that some evil was about to befall them. For this reason — believing that souls suffered hunger, thirst, and other hardships — they offered food, drink, clothing, and other things at the tombs, so that the dead might benefit from them; and they placed [text breaks off]

entities_mentioned.notes: The mythological cave of origin of the Inca lineage; Cobo discusses it as the primary sacred origin shrine [adoratorio] of the Incas.

entities_mentioned.name: guacas (guacas / huacas)

entities_mentioned.name: Incas

entities_mentioned.name: hechiceros

results.title_inferred: Inca beliefs concerning the afterlife, heaven, hell, and the fate of souls
results.original_text:
Y esto tenían tan especial cuidado de hacer sus aniversarios.

Los Incas afirmaban que las ánimas de los que han sido buenos van al cielo y tienen perpetua gloria, y ésta dicen que es estar con el sol en partes de gran deleite que tiene aparejadas el Viracocha para este fin. Unos creían que en la gloria no comen, ni beben, ni duermen, ni tienen mujeres ni necesidad de lo uno ni de lo otro; pero los más tenían lo contrario, creyendo que los que iban al cielo comen y beben espléndidamente muy buenas y regaladas comidas que el Criador les tiene aparejadas y las que acá les ofrecen y queman en sus honras sus deudos y amigos; y así tenían tanto cuidado de ofrecer de comer y beber a sus muertos, mayormente a los cuerpos embalsamados de los señores, hablando con ellos como si estuvieran vivos y diciéndoles: "Cuando eras vivo solías comer y beber destos manjares y bebida: recíbalo ahora tu ánima y cómalo doquiera que estuviere." Y tenían por cierto que así pasaba, que dondequiera que las ánimas estaban recibían y comían los manjares que les ofrecían, como hacían antes que muriesen; y para que más se confirmasen en este error, a veces, y cuando por voluntad de Dios le era permitido, tomaba el demonio la figura de algún hombre principal de los ya muertos, y aparecía con su propio traje y forma, como cuando era vivo, con su atavío y acompañamiento, a sus deudos y conocidos, y les hacía entender que estaba en otro reino alegre y deleitoso de la manera que allí lo vían. Por cuyo dicho e ilusiones, teniendo los deudos por ciertas aquellas apariencias falsas, ponían más cuidado en el servicio y regalo de sus difuntos: y esperando los vivos ir en compañía dellos a gozar de aquella gloria después desta vida, templaban la tristeza que les causaba el morir; y estando para ello, encomendaban a sus parientes sus casas y familias, pidiéndoles que hiciesen y cumpliesen lo que les dejaban encargado, que ellos los volverían a ver desdel cielo.

Asimismo estaban persuadidos a que hay infierno para los malos, y que allí los atormentan los demonios, a quienes pintaban muy feos y espantables. El lugar del infierno decían estar debajo de tierra, y que es muy oscuro y apretado; y que los que allá van padecen mucha hambre y sed; y que les hacen comer carbones, culebras, sapos y otras sabandijas asquerosas, y beber agua turbia y hedionda: y que de sólo esto se mantienen las ánimas de los condenados, cuya pena dicen ser perpetua.

Acerca de los méritos que han de tener para alcanzar la gloria, y las culpas por donde son condenados, también disparan; porque, dado que convenían en que los malos se condenaban y los buenos se salvaban, no acertaban a señalar y distinguir los buenos de los malos. Porque les persuadió el demonio que los nobles y gente de calidad, aunque fuesen de costumbres depravadas, eran siempre buenos y no era posible poderse condenar; porque el infierno sólo era para los hombres bajos y sin calidad, como ladrones y gente pobre, para los hechiceros que mataban con yerbas y para otros deste género, y señaladamente para los que iban contra los mandatos del rey o decían mal del sol y de las guacas; en los cuales pecados no incurrían los del linaje de los Incas, que eran los nobles; no haciendo caso de las otras especies de pecados.

No se halló entre todos estos indios noción que tratase de la resurrección de la carne, ni por alguna vía creyese que los cuerpos han de venir a ser algo más. Y no es de maravillar, siendo artículo que no se puede comprender por razón natural; y como no entendían que hubiese más que cielo e infierno, no hacían sacrificios y sufragios por las ánimas de los difuntos, dando por razón, que si estaban en el cielo, no lo habían menester, y si en el infierno, no les aprovechaba.
results.translation:
And for this reason they took such special care to observe their anniversary rites.

The Incas affirmed that the souls of those who had been good go to heaven and enjoy perpetual glory, which they described as dwelling with the Sun in places of great delight that Viracocha [the Creator deity] had prepared for this purpose. Some believed that in glory souls neither eat, nor drink, nor sleep, nor have women, nor feel need of any such things; but most held the contrary, believing that those who went to heaven eat and drink splendidly of very fine and choice foods that the Creator has prepared for them, as well as those which their relatives and friends offer and burn in their honor here below. And so they took great care to offer food and drink to their dead, especially to the embalmed bodies of the lords [señores], speaking to them as though they were still alive, saying: "When you were alive you used to eat and drink of these foods and drink: may your soul now receive them and consume them wherever it may be." And they held it as certain that this is what happened—that wherever the souls were, they received and ate the offerings made to them, as they had done before death. And so that they might be further confirmed in this error, sometimes, when God in His will permitted it, the devil would take on the likeness of some prominent man already dead, and would appear to his relatives and acquaintances in his own dress and form, just as he had appeared in life, with his adornments and retinue, and would make them understand that he was dwelling in another realm, joyful and delightful, in the manner in which they saw him there. On account of these words and illusions, his relatives taking those false appearances as true, they redoubled their care in the service and comfort of their dead; and the living, hoping to go in the company of the departed to enjoy that glory after this life, moderated the sorrow that death caused them; and when they were at the point of death, they commended their houses and families to their relatives, asking them to do and fulfill what they charged them with, saying that they would see them again from heaven.

Likewise they were persuaded that there is a hell for the wicked, and that there the demons torment them—demons whom they depicted as very ugly and frightful. They said the place of hell was below the earth, and that it is very dark and confined; and that those who go there suffer great hunger and thirst; and that they are made to eat coals, serpents, toads, and other loathsome vermin, and to drink muddy and foul-smelling water; and that on these alone are the souls of the damned sustained, whose punishment they said is everlasting.

Regarding the merits required to attain glory, and the offenses for which one is condemned, they also went astray; for although they agreed that the wicked were condemned and the good were saved, they could not rightly identify and distinguish the good from the wicked. For the devil persuaded them that the nobles and people of quality, even if they were of depraved morals, were always good and could not possibly be condemned; because hell was only for men of low station and without quality—such as thieves and poor people—for sorcerers [hechiceros] who killed with herbs [yerbas], and for others of that kind, and especially for those who acted against the commands of the king or who spoke ill of the Sun and of the huacas [guacas; sacred shrines or idols]; which sins those of the lineage of the Incas, who were the nobles, did not commit—paying no heed to other categories of sin.

No notion was found among all these Indians concerning the resurrection of the flesh, nor did they in any way believe that bodies were to become anything more than they are. And this is not surprising, it being a point of doctrine that cannot be grasped by natural reason; and since they understood there to be nothing beyond heaven and hell, they made no sacrifices or suffrages [votive offerings] for the souls of the dead, giving as their reason that if the dead were in heaven they had no need of them, and if they were in hell, such offerings would do them no good.

entities_mentioned.name: guacas (huacas)

entities_mentioned.notes: Cobo spells the term 'guacas'; modern Quechua scholarship standardizes to 'huacas.' They could be physical sites, mummies, idols, or natural features endowed with sacred power.

entities_mentioned.name: hechiceros
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Ritual specialists / sorcerers in colonial Andean society
entities_mentioned.notes: Translated as 'sorcerers'; the term in colonial Spanish sources encompasses a range of indigenous ritual practitioners, including healers and diviners, often viewed negatively by Spanish chroniclers.

entities_mentioned.name: yerbas (hierbas)

entities_mentioned.notes: No specific plant is identified in this passage; 'yerbas' here refers generically to herbs or plant-derived substances used by sorcerers to cause death. This is a recurring category in colonial accounts of Andean folk medicine and maleficium.

results.translator_notes: This segment is theological/ethnographic rather than botanical; it contains no plant, drug, or medical preparation data warranting extraction under Rule 3, with the sole exception of the brief mention of 'yerbas' (herbs) used by sorcerers to kill—noted above as an entity but too vague to yield a specific materia medica entry. Several lines in the original OCR contained ligature artifacts (e.g., 'beb^', '>', 'diter', broken word-ends at line breaks) that have been silently normalized where the intended reading was unambiguous. The passage 'también disparan' is idiomatic 17th-century Spanish meaning 'they also go astray / talk nonsense,' rendered accordingly. The section on suffrages ('sufragios') reflects Cobo's explicit contrast between Inca mortuary practice and Catholic doctrine on prayers for the dead.

results.title_inferred: The God Viracocha: Supreme Lord and Creator in Andean Religion
results.original_text:
CAPITULO IV

Del dios Viracocha, que era tenido de los indios por supremo señor y hacedor de todo

En medio de las tinieblas espesas de la ignorancia, no dejó la luz de la razón de obrar algo en estos indios; porque, alumbrados della, vinieron a alcanzar y creer que había un Dios Criador universal de todas las cosas y Soberano Señor, y gobernador dellas; aunque fue tan corta y oscura esta noticia, que ni aun vocablo propio con que nombrarlo tuvieron; porque todos los que le daban eran metafóricos, como luego diré; por lo cual, en los Catecismos que están hechos para instruirlos en la doctrina cristiana, se pone nuestro nombre Dios y dél usamos siempre que hablamos con ellos, a causa de no haber en su lengua palabra que con propiedad le corresponda: y los mismos indios cristianos lo tienen y usan ya por tan propio como nosotros, dado que en la pronunciación difieran algo los que no son ladinos y españolados; porque como no tuvieron en su lengua D, sino que en lugar della usaban desta letra, T, así, en lugar de decir Dios, suelen pronunciar Tíos.

Item, mezclaban y envolvían en esta flaca noticia que de Dios tenían una infinidad de vanidades y errores, imaginando y atribuyéndole cosas muy ajenas e indignas de su nobilísima naturaleza. Admitían asimismo con la adoración del Supremo Señor la de otras innumerables cosas, que veneraban con igual respeto y reverencia, si bien confesaban ser los otros dioses criados y ministros del Hacedor e intercesores para con él. Y así, cuando hablaban con el Criador, diferían en las palabras, atribuyéndole el poder y mando de todo: teniendo a los otros dioses solamente por señores particulares cada uno en su jurisdicción, conforme a la advocación y patrocinio que tenían. Daban a la primera causa títulos y nombres de gran excelencia: los más honrosos y usados eran dos, ambos translativos y de grande énfasis: Viracocha el uno, y el otro, Pachayacháchic; al primero solían anteponer o posponer algunas palabras, diciendo unas veces Ticciviracocha, y otras Viracocháyacháchic. El de Ticciviracocha era tenido por misterioso, el cual, interpretado, significa "fundamento divino"; el nombre de Pachayacháchic quiere decir "Criador del mundo"; y la misma significación tiene el de Viracocháyacháchic.

Desde que se acuerdan estos indios viene la memoria del Viracocha de mano en mano, y tuvieron siempre gran veneración a este nombre, en tanto grado, que su padre de Incayupanqui se llamó Viracocha-Inca, y ningún rey antes ni después usó ponerse tal nombre: y a él (según dicen) no se lo tuvieron a bien que se lo hubiese puesto, aunque dió por razón que el mismo Viracocha se lo había mandado. Pero dado caso que este dios Viracocha fue tenido en mucha veneración antes del tiempo del dicho Inca, y le solían hacer sacrificios, con todo eso, no le tenían en más que al sol hasta su tiempo. Y la causa de haber ordenado el dicho rey que el dios Viracocha fuese preferido a todos los demás y adorado como Dios universal y Supremo Señor, fue la victoria que alcanzó este Inca de los Chancas, como queda dicho en el libro precedente.
results.translation:
CHAPTER IV

On the god Viracocha, who was regarded by the Indians as supreme lord and maker of all things

Amidst the thick darkness of ignorance, the light of reason did not cease to produce some effect in these Indians; for, illumined by it, they came to grasp and believe that there was a universal God, Creator of all things, Sovereign Lord and Governor thereof; though this knowledge was so limited and obscure that they did not even possess a proper word with which to name him. All the names they gave him were metaphorical [translaticios], as I shall presently explain. For this reason, in the catechisms [Catecismos] composed to instruct them in Christian doctrine, our word 'God' [Dios] is used, and we employ it always when speaking with them, since their language contains no word that properly corresponds to it. The Christian Indians themselves now regard and use it as their own word just as we do, although those who are not fluent [ladinos] in Spanish and accustomed to Spaniards differ somewhat in pronunciation — for since their language had no letter D, using T in its place, they tend to say 'Tios' instead of 'Dios.'

Furthermore, they mixed and wrapped within this weak notion they had of God an infinity of vanities and errors, imagining and attributing to him things utterly foreign to and unworthy of his most noble nature. They likewise admitted, alongside the worship of the Supreme Lord, the adoration of countless other things, which they venerated with equal respect and reverence — though they did confess that these other gods were created beings, ministers of the Maker and intercessors before him. Thus, when they addressed the Creator, their words differed from those used for lesser gods, attributing to him the power and dominion over all things, while regarding the other gods merely as particular lords, each within his own jurisdiction, according to his sphere of patronage and advocacy [advocación y patrocinio]. They gave to this First Cause titles and names of great excellence; the most honored and commonly used were two, both metaphorical [translativos] and of great weight: one was Viracocha, and the other Pachayacháchic. To the former they would sometimes prepend or append other words, saying at times Ticciviracocha and at other times Viracocháyacháchic. The name Ticciviracocha was considered mysterious, and when interpreted it signifies 'divine foundation'; the name Pachayacháchic means 'Creator of the world'; and Viracocháyacháchic carries the same meaning.

As far back as these Indians can remember, the memory of Viracocha has passed from hand to hand, and they have always held this name in great veneration — to such a degree that the father of Inca Yupanqui bore the name Viracocha-Inca, and no king before or after him used such a name. Indeed, it is said that it was not well received that he had taken it upon himself, although he gave as his reason that Viracocha himself had commanded him to do so. But even though the god Viracocha had been held in great veneration before the time of the said Inca, and sacrifices were regularly made to him, he was nonetheless not held in higher esteem than the Sun until the time of that ruler. The reason why that king ordered that the god Viracocha be elevated above all others and worshipped as the universal God and Supreme Lord was the victory this Inca achieved over the Chancas, as has been recounted in the preceding book.

entities_mentioned.name: Viracocha
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Wiracocha — principal deity of the Inca pantheon, creator god
entities_mentioned.notes: Also rendered as Ticciviracocha (interpreted by Cobo as 'divine foundation') and Viracocháyacháchic ('Creator of the world'). Cobo notes the name was used as a royal title by Viracocha-Inca, father of Pachacuti (Inca Yupanqui).

entities_mentioned.name: Pachayacháchic
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Quechua epithet for the creator deity, meaning 'Teacher/Creator of the world' (Pacha = world/earth/time; yachachic = one who teaches or makes)
entities_mentioned.notes: Cobo translates this as 'Criador del mundo' (Creator of the world). Modern scholars note 'yachachiq' more precisely means 'one who causes to know/teaches,' but the sense of 'maker' or 'instructor of the world' is well attested.

entities_mentioned.name: Ticciviracocha
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Tiqsi Wiracocha — an honorific compound epithet of the creator god
entities_mentioned.notes: Cobo interprets 'Tiqsi' as 'fundamento' (foundation/base), giving the compound the meaning 'divine foundation.' Modern scholarship generally supports 'tiqsi' as meaning 'foundation' or 'origin.'

entities_mentioned.name: Incayupanqui (Inca Yupanqui)
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Pachacuti Inca Yupanqui, ninth Sapa Inca, r. c. 1438–1471
entities_mentioned.notes: Cobo identifies his father as Viracocha-Inca. The victory over the Chancas (Chankas) is elsewhere described as the event that prompted elevation of Viracocha above the Sun god.

entities_mentioned.name: Viracocha-Inca
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: Viracocha Inca, eighth Sapa Inca, father of Pachacuti
entities_mentioned.notes: Cobo notes he was the only Inca ruler to bear the name of the god Viracocha, and that this was controversial.

entities_mentioned.name: los Chancas
entities_mentioned.modern_identification: The Chanka (Chanca) confederation, a rival Andean polity based in the Apurímac region
entities_mentioned.notes: Cobo references the famous battle in which Inca Yupanqui (Pachacuti) defeated the Chancas, an event traditionally used to explain the elevation of Viracocha in the Inca religious hierarchy.

results.translator_notes: This chapter is theological/ethnographic in content and contains no botanical or medical material; accordingly, no plant, drug, or medical practice extraction is applicable. The segment appears in the botanical volumes of the Historia del Nuevo Mundo but is clearly part of a prefatory religious-ethnographic section on Andean religion, likely misbound or included as contextual background by Cobo. Several OCR errors were silently corrected: 'fleí dios' → 'Del dios'; 'i» ignorancia' → 'la ignorancia'; 'la ra- / »' → 'la razón'; 'aX- / OT2ar' → 'alcanzar'; 'Cria¬ / dor' → 'Criador'; 'Sóbe¬ / lo' → 'Soberano'; 'Viracoehayachár / chic' → 'Viracocháyacháchic'; 'Vtracochayachá* / chic' → 'Viracocháyacháchic'; 'Incayupanqiii' → 'Incayupanqui'. The word 'translaticio/translativo' (rendered here as 'metaphorical') is a period term meaning a word used in a transferred or figurative sense, distinct from its literal meaning — a deliberate theological and rhetorical observation by Cobo about indigenous naming practices.
